AI.tldr: Dendritic neural networks combined with equilibrium propagation achieve improved performance on challenging datasets and deeper architectures compared to standard EP, approaching backpropagation performance.
AI.motivation: Equilibrium propagation (EP) is biologically plausible but struggles with deeper networks and challenging tasks. Dendritic networks show better performance with backpropagation, suggesting that biologically inspired architectures could enhance EP's effectiveness.
AI.method: Integration of dendritic neural networks with advanced EP framework, evaluated on MNIST, KMNIST, and FMNIST datasets with shallow and deeper architectures. Analysis of hidden state dynamics during free phase.
AI.result: Dendritic EP matches standard EP on simple tasks but significantly outperforms it on KMNIST and FMNIST, approaching dendritic networks trained with backpropagation. Shows higher activation magnitudes and more distributed hidden-state activity.
AI.conclusion: Dendritic structure enhances biologically plausible learning algorithms, especially where standard EP struggles, highlighting the importance of architectural design for biologically inspired training methods.

title: Behavioral Determinants of Deployed AI Agents in Social Networks: A Multi-Factor Study of Personality, Model, and Guardrail Specification
summary: Autonomous AI agents are increasingly deployed in open social environments, yet the relationship between their configuration specifications and their emergent social behavior remains poorly understood. We present a controlled, multi-factor empirical study in which thirteen OpenClaw agents are deployed on Moltbook -- a Reddit-like social network built for AI agents -- across three systematically varied independent variables: (1) personality specification via SOUL.md, (2) underlying LLM model backbone, and (3) operational rules and memory configuration via AGENTS.md. A default control agent provides a behavioral baseline. Over a one-week observation window spanning approximately 400 autonomous sessions per agent, we collect behavioral, linguistic, and social metrics to assess how configuration layers predict emergent social behavior. We find that personality specification is the dominant behavioral lever, producing a massive spread in response length across agents, while model backbone and operational rules drive more moderate but still meaningful effects on rhetorical style and topic engagement breadth. Our findings contribute empirical evidence to the emerging literature on deployed multi-agent social systems and offer practical guidance for designing agents intended for collaborative or monitoring tasks in real social environments.

AI.tldr: Empirical study shows personality specification in AI agents is the dominant factor influencing social behavior, while model backbone and operational rules have moderate effects on rhetorical style and topic engagement.
AI.motivation: There's poor understanding of how AI agent configuration specifications relate to their emergent social behavior in open social environments, despite increasing deployment of autonomous AI agents in such settings.
AI.method: Controlled multi-factor empirical study with 13 OpenClaw agents deployed on Moltbook (Reddit-like social network) across three systematically varied variables: personality specification (SOUL.md), LLM model backbone, and operational rules/memory configuration (AGENTS.md). Used default control agent as baseline. Observed for one week with ~400 autonomous sessions per agent, collecting behavioral, linguistic, and social metrics.
AI.result: Personality specification is the dominant behavioral lever, producing massive spread in response length across agents. Model backbone and operational rules drive more moderate but still meaningful effects on rhetorical style and topic engagement breadth.
AI.conclusion: Findings contribute empirical evidence to deployed multi-agent social systems literature and offer practical guidance for designing agents for collaborative or monitoring tasks in real social environments.

title: LLM Wardens: Mitigating Adversarial Persuasion with Third-Party Conversational Oversight
summary: LLMs are increasingly capable of persuasion, which raises the question of how to protect users against manipulation. In a preregistered user study (N=120) across four decision-making scenarios, we find that an adversarial LLM with a hidden goal succeeds in steering users' decisions 65.4% of the time. We then introduce a "warden" model: a secondary LLM that monitors the human-AI interaction trace in real time and issues non-binding, private advisories to the user when it detects manipulation. Adding a warden more than halves the adversary's success rate to 30.4%, with a much smaller (8.6 percentage points) reduction for genuine interactions. To probe the mechanism behind these results, we release COAX-Bench, a simulation benchmark spanning 14 decision-making scenarios, including hiring, voting, and file access. Across 16,212 simulated multi-agent interactions, capable adversarial LLMs achieve their hidden goals in 34.7% of cases, which warden models reduce to 12.3%. Notably, even warden models substantially weaker than the adversary they oversee provide meaningful protection, suggesting a path for scalable oversight of more capable models.

AI.tldr: Adversarial LLMs can manipulate users' decisions 65% of the time, but "warden" LLMs monitoring interactions can reduce this manipulation by more than half to 30%, with minimal impact on genuine interactions.
AI.motivation: As LLMs become more persuasive and capable of manipulation, there's a need to protect users against adversarial persuasion attempts while maintaining useful AI-human interactions.
AI.method: 1) Preregistered user study (N=120) across four decision-making scenarios to measure adversarial LLM persuasion success. 2) Introduced "warden" LLM to monitor interactions and issue private advisories about manipulation. 3) Created COAX-Bench simulation benchmark with 14 scenarios and 16,212 multi-agent interactions for mechanism probing.
AI.result: 1) Adversarial LLM succeeds 65.4% of the time in steering users' decisions. 2) Warden models reduce this to 30.4% (more than half reduction). 3) Warden only reduces genuine interactions by 8.6 percentage points. 4) In simulations, adversarial LLMs achieve goals 34.7% of the time, reduced to 12.3% by wardens. 5) Even weaker warden models provide meaningful protection against stronger adversaries.
AI.conclusion: Warden LLMs can effectively protect users from adversarial persuasion attempts with minimal disruption to legitimate interactions, suggesting a scalable oversight approach where less capable models can monitor and mitigate manipulation by more powerful AI systems.

title: Weight Pruning Amplifies Bias: A Multi-Method Study of Compressed LLMs for Edge AI
comment: 8 pages, 7 figures, 8 tables. Accepted at the 7th Annual World AIIoT Congress (AIIoT 2026). This is the author's accepted version; the version of record will appear in IEEE Xplore
summary: Weight pruning is widely advocated for deploying Large Language Models on resource-constrained IoT and edge devices, yet its impact on model fairness remains poorly understood. We conduct a controlled empirical study of three instruction-tuned models (Gemma-2-9b-it, Mistral-7B-Instruct-v0.3, Phi-3.5-mini-instruct) across three pruning methods (Random, Magnitude, Wanda) at four sparsity levels (10-70%) on 12,148 BBQ bias benchmark items with 5 random seeds, totaling 2,368,860 inference records. Our results reveal a Smart Pruning Paradox: activation-aware pruning (Wanda) preserves perplexity nearly perfectly (just 3.5% increase at 50% sparsity for Mistral-7B), yet produces the highest bias amplification, with Stereotype Reliance Score increasing 83.7% and 47-59% of previously unbiased items developing new stereotypical behaviors at 70% sparsity. Random pruning destroys language capability entirely (perplexity exceeding $10^4$ and reaching $10^8$) but produces only random-chance bias. We further show that unstructured pruning provides zero storage savings and zero inference latency reduction on real edge hardware, undermining the primary motivation for its use in IoT deployment. Of 180 dense-vs-pruned comparisons, 141 (78.3%) are significant ($p < 0.05$) with mean $|h| = 0.305$. Published quantization studies report up to 21% of responses flipping between biased and unbiased states; our pruning results show transition rates nearly three times higher (47-59%), suggesting pruning poses a categorically greater risk to alignment than quantization. These findings demonstrate that perplexity-based evaluation provides false assurance of behavioral equivalence, and that IoT deployment pipelines require bias-aware validation before deploying pruned models at the edge.

AI.tldr: TL;DR: Weight pruning for LLM deployment on edge devices creates a "Smart Pruning Paradox" - while activation-aware pruning preserves perplexity well, it dramatically amplifies bias (up to 83.7% increase), with nearly 60% of previously unbiased items developing stereotypes. Random pruning destroys language capability but doesn't create bias. Pruning offers no real storage/latency benefits on edge hardware and is more dangerous than quantization for model alignment.
AI.motivation: The motivation is to understand the impact of weight pruning on model fairness when deploying Large Language Models on resource-constrained IoT and edge devices. While pruning is widely advocated for edge deployment, its effects on model bias remain poorly understood, creating a critical gap in knowledge for responsible AI deployment at the edge.
AI.method: Conducted controlled empirical study with three instruction-tuned models (Gemma-2-9b-it, Mistral-7B-Instruct-v0.3, Phi-3.5-mini-instruct) across three pruning methods (Random, Magnitude, Wanda) at four sparsity levels (10-70%). Used 12,148 BBQ bias benchmark items with 5 random seeds, totaling 2,368,860 inference records. Analyzed perplexity, bias amplification, stereotype reliance, and compared with quantization studies.
AI.result: Revealed "Smart Pruning Paradox": Activation-aware pruning (Wanda) preserves perplexity well (only 3.5% increase at 50% sparsity) but produces highest bias amplification - Stereotype Reliance Score increased 83.7%, and 47-59% of previously unbiased items developed new stereotypical behaviors at 70% sparsity. Random pruning destroys language capability but produces only random-chance bias. Pruning provides zero storage savings and zero inference latency reduction on real edge hardware. 78.3% of comparisons significant with mean effect size 0.305. Pruning transition rates (47-59%) nearly three times higher than quantization studies (21%).
AI.conclusion: Perplexity-based evaluation provides false assurance of behavioral equivalence, and pruning poses a categorically greater risk to alignment than quantization. IoT deployment pipelines require bias-aware validation before deploying pruned models at the edge, as pruning can dramatically amplify bias despite preserving perplexity metrics.

title: Mid-Training with Self-Generated Data Improves Reinforcement Learning in Language Models
summary: The effectiveness of Reinforcement Learning (RL) in Large Language Models (LLMs) depends on the nature and diversity of the data used before and during RL. In particular, reasoning problems can often be approached in multiple ways that rely on different forms of reasoning, and exposure to only a limited range of such approaches in the training data may limit the effectiveness of RL. Motivated by this, we investigate using diverse self-generated data during mid-training as an intermediate step before RL training. Specifically, we adopt a bootstrapped data-generation framework guided by George Polya's problem-solving approaches for generating multiple variants of correct answers for each question in the training data, and then perform fine-tuning. We first provide a theoretical perspective on how mid-training on such data improves RL and explain how policy-gradient updates can incentivize combining multiple approaches. We then empirically demonstrate that RL-trained models initialized with our mid-training data achieve consistent improvements across various mathematical reasoning benchmarks and other OOD tasks like code generation and narrative reasoning. Overall, our investigative study shows that a language model learning multiple problem-solving approaches, through self-generated data helps subsequent RL.

AI.tldr: Using diverse self-generated reasoning data during mid-training improves Reinforcement Learning effectiveness in LLMs for multi-approach problems
AI.motivation: RL effectiveness in LLMs depends on data diversity; current training data often exposes models to limited reasoning approaches, limiting their ability to handle problems that can be solved via multiple paths
AI.method: Adopt bootstrapped data-generation framework guided by George Polya's problem-solving approaches to generate multiple correct answer variants for each training question, then fine-tune models on this diverse data as mid-training step before RL
AI.result: RL-trained models initialized with mid-training data achieve consistent improvements across mathematical reasoning benchmarks and OOD tasks like code generation and narrative reasoning; theoretical analysis shows how mid-training improves RL
AI.conclusion: Teaching language models multiple problem-solving approaches through self-generated diverse data enhances subsequent RL training, demonstrating the importance of exposure to varied reasoning strategies

title: Generalization Bounds of Emergent Communications for Agentic AI Networking
comment: Accepted at IEEE ISIT Workshop, Guangzhou, China, June 2026
summary: The evolution of 6G networking toward agentic AI networking (AgentNet) systems requires a shift from traditional data pipelines to task-aware, agentic AI-native communication solutions. Emergent communication, a novel communication paradigm in which autonomous agents learn their own signaling protocols through interaction, is increasingly viewed as a promising solution to address the challenges posed by existing rigid, predefined protocol-based networking architecture. However, most existing emergent communication frameworks fail to account for physical networking constraints, such as bandwidth and computational complexity, and often lack a rigorous information-theoretical foundation. To address these challenges, this paper introduces a novel emergent communication framework that facilitates collaborative task-solving among heterogeneous agents through an information-theoretic lens. We propose a novel joint loss function that unifies the optimization of decision-making functions and the learning of communication signaling. Our proposed solution is grounded on the multi-agent and multi-task distributed information bottleneck (DIB) theory, which allows the quantification of the fundamental trade-off between task-relevant information representation and computational complexity. We further provide theoretical generalization bounds of the emergent communication protocol during decentralized inference across unseen environmental states. Experimental validation on a real-world hardware prototype confirms that our proposed framework significantly improves generalization performance, compared to the state-of-the-art solutions.

AI.tldr: Proposes an information-theoretic emergent communication framework for 6G AgentNet that optimizes task-solving among heterogeneous agents while accounting for physical networking constraints, with theoretical generalization bounds and experimental validation.
AI.motivation: Traditional 6G networking architectures are rigid and predefined, while existing emergent communication frameworks ignore physical networking constraints (bandwidth, computational complexity) and lack information-theoretical foundations.
AI.method: Introduces a novel emergent communication framework with a joint loss function optimizing both decision-making and communication signaling, grounded in multi-agent multi-task distributed information bottleneck (DIB) theory.
AI.result: Provides theoretical generalization bounds for decentralized inference across unseen environmental states, and experimental validation on real-world hardware prototype shows significant improvement in generalization performance over state-of-the-art solutions.
AI.conclusion: The proposed information-theoretic emergent communication framework successfully addresses limitations of existing approaches by integrating physical networking constraints, providing theoretical foundations, and demonstrating practical improvements in 6G AgentNet systems.

title: DataArc-SynData-Toolkit: A Unified Closed-Loop Framework for Multi-Path, Multimodal, and Multilingual Data Synthesis
summary: Synthetic data has emerged as a crucial solution to the data scarcity bottleneck in large language models (LLMs), particularly for specialized domains and low-resource languages. However, the broader adoption of existing synthetic data tools is severely hindered by convoluted workflows, fragmented data standards, and limited scalability across modalities. To address these limitations, we develop DataArc-SynData-Toolkit, an open-source framework featuring: (1) a configuration-driven, end-to-end pipeline equipped with an intuitive visual interface and simplified CLI for exceptional usability; (2) a unified, quality-controllable synthesis paradigm that standardizes multi-source data generation to ensure high reusability; and (3) a highly modular architecture designed for seamless multimodal, multilingual, and multi-task adaptation. We apply the toolkit in multiple application scenarios. Experimental results demonstrate that our toolkit achieves an optimal balance between generation efficiency and data quality. By offering an end-to-end and visually interactive pipeline, DataArc-SynData-Toolkit significantly lowers the technical barrier to synthetic data generation and subsequent model training, accelerating its practical deployment in real-world applications.

AI.tldr: DataArc-SynData-Toolkit is an open-source framework that simplifies synthetic data generation for LLMs through configuration-driven pipelines, unified quality standards, and modular architecture, lowering technical barriers and accelerating deployment.
AI.motivation: Synthetic data is essential for addressing data scarcity in LLMs, especially for specialized domains and low-resource languages. However, existing synthetic data tools suffer from convoluted workflows, fragmented data standards, and limited scalability across modalities, hindering broader adoption.
AI.method: The paper develops DataArc-SynData-Toolkit featuring three key components: (1) configuration-driven end-to-end pipeline with visual interface and simplified CLI for usability, (2) unified quality-controllable synthesis paradigm standardizing multi-source data generation, and (3) highly modular architecture for seamless multimodal, multilingual, and multi-task adaptation.
AI.result: Experimental results demonstrate the toolkit achieves optimal balance between generation efficiency and data quality. The framework is applied in multiple application scenarios and significantly lowers technical barriers to synthetic data generation and model training.
AI.conclusion: DataArc-SynData-Toolkit accelerates practical deployment of synthetic data in real-world applications by providing an end-to-end, visually interactive pipeline that addresses existing limitations of synthetic data tools.

title: AI-Care: A Conversational Agentic System for Task Coordination in Alzheimer's Disease Care
summary: Individuals with Alzheimer's disease (AD) and Alzheimer's disease-related dementia (ADRD) experience memory and thinking changes that impact their ability to use digital daily management tools. For example, adding an event to a digital calendar requires multiple steps that may act as barriers to independent use for individuals with AD/ADRD. This paper presents AI-Care, a conversational agentic artificial intelligence (AI) layer built on top of a remote caregiving platform co-designed with people with AD/ADRD. AI-Care is designed to reduce the cognitive load on individuals with AD/ADRD when managing everyday tasks such as setting calendar reminders and organizing to-do lists through natural-language interaction with a voice-first chatbot. The system uses a LangGraph-based stateful orchestration approach in which each request passes through sanitization, intent classification, context loading, safety checks, deterministic slot collection, tool execution, and response composition. Safety-critical responses, particularly around medications and allergies, are grounded in caregiver-verified records rather than free-form model generation. The system does not make autonomous medical or treatment decisions. Incomplete or ambiguous requests are handled through controlled multi-turn clarification rather than silent failure or guessing. The system supports both typed and spoken input, with voice output through ElevenLabs text-to-speech. Longer responses are chunked before synthesis to avoid rushed playback. A preliminary pilot with four individuals with mild-to-moderate AD/ADRD showed that users found the system trustworthy, competent, and likable, and were able to complete the evaluated coordination tasks through conversation. We describe the design goals, system architecture, safety controls, and findings from this formative evaluation.

AI.tldr: AI-Care is a conversational AI system that helps Alzheimer's patients manage daily tasks through natural language interaction, with safety-focused design and caregiver verification for critical functions.
AI.motivation: Individuals with Alzheimer's disease (AD/ADRD) struggle with complex digital tools due to cognitive impairments. Standard digital daily management tools require multiple steps that create barriers to independent use for people with AD/ADRD.
AI.method: AI-Care uses a LangGraph-based stateful orchestration with a voice-first chatbot interface. The system processes requests through sanitization, intent classification, context loading, safety checks, deterministic slot collection, tool execution, and response composition. Safety-critical responses are grounded in caregiver-verified records. The system handles incomplete requests through controlled multi-turn clarification and supports both typed and spoken input with ElevenLabs text-to-speech output.
AI.result: A preliminary pilot with four individuals with mild-to-moderate AD/ADRD showed that users found the system trustworthy, competent, and likable, and were able to complete coordination tasks through conversation.
AI.conclusion: AI-Care demonstrates a promising approach to reducing cognitive load for individuals with AD/ADRD through conversational AI with robust safety controls, though further evaluation is needed with larger populations.

title: MIND-Skill: Quality-Guaranteed Skill Generation via Multi-Agent Induction and Deduction
summary: Large language model (LLM) powered AI agents have emerged as a promising paradigm for autonomous problem-solving, yet they continue to struggle with complex, multi-step real-world tasks that demand domain-specific procedural knowledge. Reusable agent skills, which encapsulate successful problem-solving strategies, offer a natural remedy by enabling agents to build on prior experience. However, curating such skills has largely remained a manual endeavor, requiring human experts to distill rich domain knowledge into actionable guidelines. In this work, we present $\textbf{M}$ulti-agent $\textbf{IN}$duction and $\textbf{D}$eduction for $\textbf{Skill}$s ($\textbf{MIND-Skill}$), a framework that automatically induces generalizable skills from successful trajectories with robust quality guarantees. MIND-Skill consists of an induction agent which is tasked to abstract reusable skills from successful trajectories, and a deduction agent which aims to reconstruct trajectories by following the induced skills. To guarantee the quality of the generated skills, we introduce a reconstruction loss that compares input and reconstructed trajectories, an outcome loss that enforces the correctness of the reconstructed trajectories, and a rubric loss that assesses the documentation quality and regularizes the abstraction level of the generated skills according to predefined criteria. These textual losses are jointly optimized with TextGrad, and the resulting skills are evaluated on held-out tasks unseen during optimization. Experiments on AppWorld and BFCL-v3 show that MIND-Skill consistently outperforms concurrent skill generation methods.

AI.tldr: MIND-Skill is an automatic skill generation framework for LLM-powered AI agents that induces reusable skills from successful trajectories with quality guarantees through multi-agent induction and deduction.
AI.motivation: LLM-powered AI agents struggle with complex multi-step real-world tasks requiring domain-specific procedural knowledge. While reusable agent skills that encapsulate problem-solving strategies could help, curating such skills has been largely manual, requiring human experts to distill domain knowledge.
AI.method: MIND-Skill uses a multi-agent framework with two components: 1) an induction agent that abstracts reusable skills from successful trajectories, and 2) a deduction agent that reconstructs trajectories by following induced skills. The system employs three loss functions for quality guarantees: reconstruction loss (compares input and reconstructed trajectories), outcome loss (ensures correctness of reconstructed trajectories), and rubric loss (assesses documentation quality and regularizes abstraction level). These textual losses are jointly optimized with TextGrad.
AI.result: MIND-Skill consistently outperforms concurrent skill generation methods in experiments on AppWorld and BFCL-v3 benchmarks, demonstrating effectiveness in generating skills that generalize to held-out tasks unseen during optimization.
AI.conclusion: The MIND-Skill framework successfully automates the generation of reusable agent skills with quality guarantees, addressing the manual bottleneck in skill curation and improving agent performance on complex multi-step tasks.

title: Reasoning emerges from constrained inference manifolds in large language models
summary: Reasoning in large language models is predominantly evaluated through labeled benchmarks, conflating task performance with the quality of internal inference. Here we study reasoning as an intrinsic dynamical process by examining the evolution of internal representations during inference. We find that inference-time dynamics consistently self-organize into low-dimensional manifolds embedded within high-dimensional representation spaces. we find that such geometric compression, although pervasive, is not sufficient for stable or reliable reasoning. Instead, effective reasoning dynamics emerge within a constrained structural regime characterized by three conditions: adequate representational expressivity, spontaneous manifold compression, and preservation of non-degenerate information volume within the compressed subspace. Models outside this regime exhibit characteristic pathological inference dynamics. Based on these insights, we introduce a unified, label-free diagnostic computed solely from internal dynamics. These findings suggest that reasoning in LLMs is fundamentally governed by geometric and informational constraints, offering a complementary framework to benchmark-centric assessment.

AI.tldr: LLM reasoning emerges from specific geometric constraints in internal dynamics, not just benchmark performance. Proper reasoning requires balanced representation, compression, and information preservation.
AI.motivation: Current evaluation of reasoning in LLMs focuses on labeled benchmarks, which conflate task performance with intrinsic inference quality. The authors want to study reasoning as an intrinsic dynamical process by examining internal representation evolution during inference.
AI.method: Analyze inference-time dynamics of internal representations in LLMs. Study how these dynamics self-organize into low-dimensional manifolds within high-dimensional spaces. Identify structural conditions for effective reasoning through geometric analysis of internal representations.
AI.result: Inference dynamics consistently organize into low-dimensional manifolds, but geometric compression alone is insufficient. Effective reasoning requires three conditions: adequate representational expressivity, spontaneous manifold compression, and preservation of non-degenerate information volume within compressed subspace. Models outside this regime show pathological inference dynamics.
AI.conclusion: Reasoning in LLMs is fundamentally governed by geometric and informational constraints. The authors propose a unified, label-free diagnostic based solely on internal dynamics, offering a complementary framework to benchmark-centric evaluation.

title: Latent Personality Alignment: Improving Harmlessness Without Mentioning Harms
comment: published at Trustworthy AI Workshop, ICLR 2026
summary: Current adversarial robustness methods for large language models require extensive datasets of harmful prompts (thousands to hundreds of thousands of examples), yet remain vulnerable to novel attack vectors and distributional shifts. We propose Latent Personality Alignment (LPA), a sample-efficient defense that achieves robustness by training models on abstract personality traits rather than specific harmful behaviors. Using fewer than 100 trait statements and latent adversarial training, LPA achieves comparable attack success rates to methods trained on 150k+ examples, while maintaining superior utility. Critically, LPA generalizes better to unseen attack distributions, reducing misclassification rates by 2.6x compared to baseline across six harm benchmarks -- without ever seeing harmful examples during training. Our results demonstrate that personality-based alignment offers a principled approach to building robust defenses with minimal cost.

AI.tldr: LPA is a sample-efficient adversarial defense that uses abstract personality traits (instead of harmful examples) with latent adversarial training to achieve robust alignment with <100 samples, matching performance of methods needing 150k+ harmful examples.
AI.motivation: Current adversarial robustness methods require extensive harmful prompt datasets (thousands to hundreds of thousands) and remain vulnerable to novel attacks and distributional shifts.
AI.method: Latent Personality Alignment (LPA) trains models on abstract personality traits rather than specific harmful behaviors, using fewer than 100 trait statements combined with latent adversarial training.
AI.result: LPA achieves comparable attack success rates to methods trained on 150k+ examples while maintaining utility, reduces misclassification rates by 2.6x across six harm benchmarks, and generalizes better to unseen attack distributions without ever seeing harmful examples during training.
AI.conclusion: Personality-based alignment offers a principled approach to building robust defenses with minimal cost, demonstrating superior generalization to unseen attack distributions.

title: Communicating Sound Through Natural Language
summary: Natural language is widely used to describe, prompt, and control audio systems, but rarely serves as the representation carrying audio itself. We introduce lexical acoustic coding (LAC), a framework in which pre-trained LLM sender and receiver agents transmit sound through natural language. Under fixed system prompts, the agents write their own analysis and synthesis code, communicating only through a lexical sentence, shared vocabulary, and optional symbolic music structure. The sender analyzes an input waveform into interpretable, non-learned acoustic descriptors, quantizes each with a feature-specific interval vocabulary, and verbalizes the lexical code as English. The receiver parses the sentence back into lexical-acoustic constraints and renders a waveform through closed-loop refinement. The transmitted text serves as both a rich caption and as the transport representation itself. We frame LAC as a finite-rate lossy quantizer, exposing trade-offs between vocabulary size, rate, and fidelity. Experiments on short sounds and symbolic music transfer show that plain text preserves measurable acoustic structure while remaining interpretable, editable, and native to LLM-mediated communication.

AI.tldr: LAC (Lexical Acoustic Coding) is a framework where pre-trained LLMs communicate sound through natural language, turning acoustic descriptors into English text that serves as both caption and audio representation.
AI.motivation: Natural language is commonly used to describe and control audio systems, but hasn't been used as the actual representation carrying audio itself. The paper addresses this gap by exploring how plain text can serve as both rich caption and transport representation for audio.
AI.method: Uses pre-trained LLM sender and receiver agents with fixed system prompts. The sender analyzes input waveform into interpretable acoustic descriptors, quantizes each with feature-specific vocabularies, and verbalizes into English text. The receiver parses the text back into acoustic constraints and renders waveform through closed-loop refinement. Optional symbolic music structure can be included. Framed as a finite-rate lossy quantizer.
AI.result: Experiments on short sounds and symbolic music transfer show that plain text preserves measurable acoustic structure while remaining interpretable, editable, and native to LLM-mediated communication. The transmitted text serves dual purpose as both rich caption and audio representation.
AI.conclusion: LAC demonstrates that natural language can effectively serve as a transport representation for audio, exposing trade-offs between vocabulary size, rate, and fidelity, while maintaining human interpretability and editability within LLM communication systems.

title: HoReN: Normalized Hopfield Retrieval for Large-Scale Sequential Model Editing
summary: Large language models encode vast factual knowledge that inevitably becomes outdated or incorrect after deployment, yet retraining is costly prohibitive, motivating model editing in lifelong settings that updates targeted behavior without harming the rest of the model. One line of work installs new facts by directly modifying base weights through locate-then-edit procedures, but accumulated edits progressively disrupt originally preserved knowledge, even with constraint-based projections. A complementary line leaves base weights intact and routes edits through external memory, but it faces routing challenges and its performance degrades at scale. We propose HoReN, a codebook-based parameter-preserving editor with enhanced routing built on three ideas. First, HoReN wraps a single MLP layer with a discrete key-value codebook, where each entry is interpreted simultaneously as a knowledge-memory key and a modern Hopfield stored pattern. Second, both keys and queries are projected onto the unit hypersphere so retrieval is governed by angular similarity, removing magnitude-driven mismatches between an edit prompt and its rephrasings. Third, the query is refined through damped Hopfield attractor dynamics, so paraphrases relax into the correct stored pattern's basin of attraction while unrelated queries remain undisturbed. HoReN achieves well-edited performance with consistent gains across diverse benchmarks spanning standard ZsRE, structured WikiBigEdit, and unstructured UnKE evaluations. Moreover, HoReN scales to 50K sequential edits on ZsRE with stable overall performance above 0.9, while prior editors collapse or degrade severely before reaching 10K. Our code is available at https://github.com/ha11ucin8/HoReN.

AI.tldr: HoReN is a codebook-based parameter-preserving editor for large language models that uses discrete key-value codebooks with Hopfield dynamics to enable scalable, stable model editing across thousands of sequential updates.
AI.motivation: LLM knowledge becomes outdated after deployment but retraining is prohibitively expensive. Existing editing methods have limitations: direct weight modification disrupts preserved knowledge, while external memory approaches face routing challenges and performance degradation at scale.
AI.method: HoReN wraps an MLP layer with a discrete key-value codebook where each entry serves as both knowledge-memory key and Hopfield stored pattern. It projects keys and queries onto the unit hypersphere for angular similarity matching, and refines queries through damped Hopfield attractor dynamics to handle paraphrases.
AI.result: HoReN achieves consistent gains across diverse benchmarks (ZsRE, WikiBigEdit, UnKE) and scales to 50K sequential edits on ZsRE with stable performance above 0.9, while prior editors collapse or degrade severely before reaching 10K edits.
AI.conclusion: HoReN demonstrates effective parameter-preserving editing with enhanced routing through Hopfield dynamics, enabling scalable lifelong knowledge updates in LLMs without compromising existing knowledge or suffering from routing degradation.

title: OracleTSC: Oracle-Informed Reward Hurdle and Uncertainty Regularization for Traffic Signal Control
comment: Published in Transactions on Machine Learning Research
summary: Transparent decision-making is essential for traffic signal control (TSC) systems to earn public trust. However, traditional reinforcement learning-based TSC methods function as black boxes with limited interpretability. Although large language models (LLMs) can provide natural language reasoning, reinforcement finetuning for TSC remains unstable because feedback is sparse and delayed, while most actions produce only marginal changes in congestion metrics. We introduce OracleTSC, which stabilizes LLM-based TSC through two mechanisms: (1) a reward hurdle mechanism that filters weak learning signals by subtracting a calibrated threshold from environmental rewards, and (2) uncertainty regularization that maximizes the probability of the selected response to encourage consistent decisions across sampled outputs. Experiments on the LibSignal benchmark show that OracleTSC enables a compact LLaMA3-8B model to substantially improve traffic efficiency, achieving a 75% reduction in travel time and a 67% decrease in queue length compared with the pretrained baseline while preserving interpretability through natural language explanations. OracleTSC also demonstrates strong cross-intersection generalization: a policy trained on one intersection transfers to a structurally different intersection with 17% lower travel time and 39% lower queue length without additional finetuning. These results suggest that uncertainty-aware reward shaping can improve the stability and effectiveness of reinforcement fine-tuning for TSC.

AI.tldr: OracleTSC stabilizes LLM-based traffic signal control using reward hurdles and uncertainty regularization, achieving 75% travel time reduction while maintaining interpretability through natural language explanations.
AI.motivation: Traditional reinforcement learning-based traffic signal control methods function as black boxes with limited interpretability, and reinforcement finetuning for TSC remains unstable due to sparse/delayed feedback and marginal changes in congestion metrics from most actions. LLMs can provide natural language reasoning but need stabilization.
AI.method: OracleTSC introduces two mechanisms: (1) a reward hurdle mechanism that filters weak learning signals by subtracting a calibrated threshold from environmental rewards, and (2) uncertainty regularization that maximizes the probability of the selected response to encourage consistent decisions across sampled outputs.
AI.result: On the LibSignal benchmark, OracleTSC enables a compact LLaMA3-8B model to achieve 75% reduction in travel time and 67% decrease in queue length compared with pretrained baseline. Strong cross-intersection generalization: policy trained on one intersection transfers to structurally different intersection with 17% lower travel time and 39% lower queue length without additional finetuning.
AI.conclusion: Uncertainty-aware reward shaping can improve the stability and effectiveness of reinforcement fine-tuning for traffic signal control while preserving interpretability through natural language explanations provided by LLMs.

title: Not All Turns Matter: Credit Assignment for Multi-Turn Jailbreaking
summary: Deploying LLMs in multi-turn dialogues facilitates jailbreak attacks that distribute harmful intent across seemingly benign turns. Recent training-based multi-turn jailbreak methods learn long-horizon attack strategies from interaction feedback, but often rely on coarse trajectory-level outcome signals that broadcast uniformly to every turn. However, we find that turn-level contributions in multi-turn jailbreaking are non-uniform, phase-dependent, and target-specific. Such coarse outcome supervision induces a credit assignment problem, leading to over-rewarding redundant turns in successful trajectories and under-crediting useful intermediate turns in failed ones. To address this, we propose TRACE, a turn-aware credit assignment framework for reinforcement learning (RL)-based multi-turn jailbreaking. For successful trajectories, TRACE estimates turn-level contributions via leave-one-turn-out semantic masking; for failed ones, TRACE assigns penalties based on prompt harmfulness and semantic relevance, with an additional local refusal-aware penalty. Furthermore, we reuse the attack-side credit signal for multi-turn defense alignment. Extensive experiments on open-source and closed-source targets show that TRACE achieves strong overall performance in effectiveness, transferability, and efficiency, yielding about a 25% relative improvement in attack success rate over the strongest RL baseline while also improving the safety-utility balance when reused for defense alignment.

AI.tldr: TRACE is a turn-aware credit assignment framework that improves multi-turn jailbreak attacks by properly assigning rewards/penalties to individual turns rather than using uniform trajectory-level signals, solving the credit assignment problem in RL-based attacks.
AI.motivation: Current multi-turn jailbreak methods use coarse trajectory-level outcome signals that assign uniform rewards to all turns, failing to account for non-uniform, phase-dependent, and target-specific contributions of individual turns. This causes credit assignment problems where redundant turns get over-rewarded in successful attacks while useful intermediate turns get under-credited in failed ones.
AI.method: TRACE uses turn-aware credit assignment for RL-based multi-turn jailbreaking: (1) For successful trajectories, estimates turn-level contributions via leave-one-turn-out semantic masking; (2) For failed trajectories, assigns penalties based on prompt harmfulness and semantic relevance with local refusal-aware penalty; (3) Reuses attack-side credit signals for multi-turn defense alignment.
AI.result: Extensive experiments show TRACE achieves strong overall performance in effectiveness, transferability, and efficiency, with about 25% relative improvement in attack success rate over the strongest RL baseline. It also improves the safety-utility balance when reused for defense alignment.
AI.conclusion: TRACE addresses the credit assignment problem in multi-turn jailbreaking by properly evaluating turn-level contributions, leading to more effective attacks and better defense alignment. The framework demonstrates that fine-grained, turn-aware reward design is crucial for multi-turn adversarial strategies.

title: NoiseRater: Meta-Learned Noise Valuation for Diffusion Model Training
summary: Diffusion models have achieved remarkable success across a wide range of generative tasks, yet their training paradigm largely treats injected noise as uniformly informative. In this work, we challenge this assumption and introduce NoiseRater, a meta-learning framework for instance-level noise valuation in diffusion model training. We propose a parametric noise rater that assigns importance scores to individual noise realizations conditioned on data and timestep, enabling adaptive reweighting of the training objective. The rater is trained via bilevel optimization to improve downstream validation performance after inner-loop diffusion updates. To enable efficient deployment, we further design a decoupled two-stage pipeline that transitions from soft weighting during meta-training to hard noise selection during standard training. Extensive experiments on FFHQ and ImageNet demonstrate that not all noise samples contribute equally, and that prioritizing informative noise improves both training efficiency and generation quality. Our results establish noise valuation as a complementary and previously underexplored axis for improving diffusion model training. Our code is available at: https://anonymous.4open.science/r/NoiseRater-DEB116.

AI.tldr: NoiseRater introduces instance-level noise valuation for diffusion models, using meta-learning to assign importance scores to noise samples and prioritize informative noise during training, improving efficiency and quality.
AI.motivation: Diffusion models treat all injected noise as equally informative, but this assumption may be suboptimal. Not all noise samples contribute equally to effective training, suggesting an opportunity to improve training by selectively focusing on more informative noise instances.
AI.method: Proposes NoiseRater, a meta-learning framework with a parametric noise rater that assigns importance scores to individual noise realizations conditioned on data and timestep. Uses bilevel optimization to train the rater based on downstream validation performance. Implements a decoupled two-stage pipeline: soft weighting during meta-training, then hard noise selection during standard training.
AI.result: Experiments on FFHQ and ImageNet show that not all noise samples contribute equally. Prioritizing informative noise improves both training efficiency and generation quality. Noise valuation emerges as a previously underexplored axis for improving diffusion model training.
AI.conclusion: NoiseRater successfully demonstrates that instance-level noise valuation can complement existing diffusion training approaches, offering a new dimension for optimization that improves model performance and efficiency.

title: Results and Retrospective Analysis of the CODS 2025 AssetOpsBench Challenge
summary: Competition retrospectives are useful when they explain what a leaderboard measured, how hidden evaluation changed conclusions, and which design patterns were rewarded. We revisit the CODS 2025 \assetopslive{} challenge, a privacy-aware Codabench competition on industrial multi-agent orchestration built on \assetops{}. We combine final rank sheets, a 300-submission server log, 149-team registrations, best-submission exports, the organizer winners report, the companion \assetopslive{} system paper, and verified planning-track source trees. Five results stand out. First, the public planning leaderboard saturates at 72.73\%, and richer prompts do not improve that peak. Second, hidden evaluation changes the story: public and private scores correlate moderately in planning ($r{=}0.69$) but negatively in execution ($r{=}{-}0.13$), with several 45.45\% public execution systems reaching 63.64\% on the hidden set. Third, the \tmatch{} term is numerically almost inert in the official composite -- combined on a 0--1 scale with 0--100 percentage scores, it contributes at most 0.05 points per track, and rescaling would swap the top two teams. Fourth, the competition is operationally account-based but substantively team-based: 149 registered teams reduce to 24 with non-zero public scores and 11 fully ranked, while 52.3\% of deduplicated registrations list multiple usernames. Fifth, successful execution methods mostly improve guardrails -- response selection, contamination cleanup, fallback, and context control -- rather than novel agent architectures. These findings identify which behaviors the evaluation rewarded, and motivate scale-aware composites, skill-level diagnostics, and versioned artifact release.

AI.tldr: Analysis of CODS 2025 assetopslive challenge reveals five key findings about planning-execution leaderboard disparities, evaluation methodology issues, team dynamics, and that successful execution methods focused on guardrails rather than novel architectures.
AI.motivation: To conduct a comprehensive retrospective analysis of the CODS 2025 assetopslive challenge to understand what the leaderboard actually measured, how hidden evaluation changed conclusions, and which design patterns were rewarded in this privacy-aware industrial multi-agent orchestration competition.
AI.method: Combined multiple data sources: final rank sheets, 300-submission server logs, 149-team registrations, best-submission exports, organizer winners report, companion system paper, and verified planning-track source trees to conduct a holistic retrospective analysis.
AI.result: Five key results: 1) Public planning leaderboard saturates at 72.73% with no improvement from richer prompts; 2) Hidden evaluation revealed significant discrepancies between public/private scores (r=0.69 for planning, r=-0.13 for execution); 3) TMatch term contributed minimally to composite scores (≤0.05 points/track); 4) Competition was account-based but team-driven (149→24→11 teams); 5) Successful execution methods focused on guardrails rather than novel architectures.
AI.conclusion: The competition retrospective reveals important insights about evaluation methodology, team dynamics, and system design patterns. Findings highlight the need for scale-aware composites, skill-level diagnostics, and versioned artifact releases to better measure and understand competition outcomes and system capabilities.

title: MCP-Cosmos: World Model-Augmented Agents for Complex Task Execution in MCP Environments
summary: The Model Context Protocol (MCP) has unified the interface between Large Language Models (LLMs) and external tools, yet a fundamental gap remains in how agents conceptualize the environments within which they operate. Current paradigms are bifurcated: Task-level planning often ignores execution-time dynamics, while reactive execution lacks long-horizon foresight. We present MCP-Cosmos, a framework that infuses generative World Models (WM) into the MCP ecosystem to enable predictive task automation. By unifying three disparate technologies, namely MCP, World Model, and Agent, we demonstrate that a "Bring Your Own World Model" (BYOWM) strategy allows agents to simulate state transitions and refine plans in a latent space before execution. We conducted experiments using two strategies, namely ReAct and SPIRAL with 2 planning models and 3 representative world models over 20+ MCP-Bench tasks. We observed improvements in Agent's environment interaction KPI such as tool success rate and tool parameter accuracy. The framework also offers new metrics such as Execution Quality to generate new insights about the effectiveness of world models compared to baseline.

AI.tldr: MCP-Cosmos integrates generative World Models into the MCP framework to enable predictive task automation through simulation in latent space before execution.
AI.motivation: Current approaches in LLM-agent tool interaction are bifurcated: task-level planning ignores execution-time dynamics, while reactive execution lacks long-horizon foresight, creating a fundamental gap in how agents conceptualize their operating environments.
AI.method: Unified MCP, World Model, and Agent technologies with a "Bring Your Own World Model" (BYOWM) strategy, allowing agents to simulate state transitions and refine plans in latent space. Tested with ReAct and SPIRAL strategies using 2 planning models and 3 world models on 20+ MCP-Bench tasks.
AI.result: Improved agent environment interaction KPIs (tool success rate and tool parameter accuracy). Introduced new metrics like Execution Quality to evaluate world model effectiveness compared to baselines.
AI.conclusion: MCP-Cosmos successfully bridges the planning-execution gap by enabling agents to operate with predictive foresight through world model simulation, creating a more unified and effective approach to task automation.

title: Feature Rivalry in Sparse Autoencoder Representations: A Mechanistic Study of Uncertainty-Driven Feature Competition in LLMs
summary: Sparse Autoencoders (SAEs) decompose large language model representations into interpretable features, but how these features interact under uncertainty remains poorly understood. We introduce Feature Rivalry -- negatively correlated SAE feature pairs -- and study whether rivalry serves as a mechanistic signature of model uncertainty in Gemma-2-2B using Gemma Scope SAEs. Through a controlled within-domain experiment on PopQA split by response entropy, we find that high-entropy questions produce significantly stronger feature rivalry at layers 0 and 12 relative to low-entropy questions (p=5.3x10^-26 and p=5.8x10^-5 respectively), localizing uncertainty to specific processing stages in the residual stream. We then test whether rivalry is causally upstream of model outputs via activation steering along rivalry axes -- finding that steering along the rivalry direction (vec_A - vec_B) causes more output changes than random directions at low steering multipliers across 15 of 20 rival feature pairs. Finally, a per-prompt rivalry score derived from pairwise cosine similarities of active SAE feature decoder vectors predicts answer correctness (AUROC=0.689), approaching but not matching softmax confidence (AUROC=0.808).

AI.tldr: Feature Rivalry identifies negatively correlated SAE feature pairs as a mechanistic signature of model uncertainty, showing it correlates with high-entropy questions and can steer model outputs, while a rivalry score predicts answer correctness but lags behind softmax confidence.
AI.motivation: While Sparse Autoencoders decompose LLM representations into interpretable features, understanding how these features interact under uncertainty remains an open question. The authors aim to discover mechanistic signatures of model uncertainty in SAE features.
AI.method: 1. Introduced "Feature Rivalry" - negatively correlated SAE feature pairs; 2. Conducted controlled within-domain experiments on PopQA split by response entropy using Gemma-2-2B with Gemma Scope SAEs; 3. Performed activation steering along rivalry axes to test causal relationships; 4. Derived per-prompt rivalry scores from pairwise cosine similarities.
AI.result: 1. High-entropy questions produce significantly stronger feature rivalry at layers 0 and 12 (p=5.3x10^-26 and p=5.8x10^-5); 2. Steering along rivalry directions causes more output changes than random directions for 15 of 20 rival pairs; 3. Rivalry score predicts answer correctness with AUROC=0.689, while softmax confidence has AUROC=0.808.
AI.conclusion: Feature rivalry serves as a mechanistic signature of uncertainty in LLMs, localizing uncertainty to specific processing stages, demonstrating causal influence on outputs, and providing predictive power for answer correctness, though not matching the performance of softmax confidence.

title: Human-LLM Dialogue Improves Diagnostic Accuracy in Emergency Care
summary: Clinical decision-making in emergency medicine demands rapid, accurate diagnoses under uncertainty. Despite benchmark progress, evidence for LLMs as interactive aids in live physician workflows remains sparse. MedSyn lets physicians iteratively query an LLM provided with the full clinical record while initially viewing only the chief complaint. Seven physicians (three seniors, four residents) completed baseline and AI-assisted sessions across 52 MIMIC-IV cases stratified by difficulty. Blinded evaluation showed residents' Hard-case correctness rose from 0.589 to 0.734; difficulty-standardised completely-correct rates confirmed a medium effect (Δ = 0.092; p = 0.071; d = 0.47). Automated metrics corroborated these gains: standardised any-match accuracy improved by 0.156 (p < 0.0001), and residents showed the largest F1 gain (Δ = 0.138; p < 0.0001). Dialogue analysis revealed expertise-dependent strategies (seniors asked targeted, hypothesis-driven questions; residents relied on broader queries) and cross-expertise concordance increased (Δ = 0.145; p < 0.0001). Interactive LLM support meaningfully enhances diagnostic reasoning.

AI.tldr: MedSyn is an interactive LLM system that helps emergency physicians make better diagnoses by allowing iterative queries while initially viewing only chief complaints. It significantly improves diagnostic accuracy, especially for residents on difficult cases.
AI.motivation: Clinical decision-making in emergency medicine requires rapid, accurate diagnoses under uncertainty. While LLMs show benchmark progress, there's sparse evidence for their use as interactive aids in live physician workflows. The study aims to test how LLMs can actually assist real clinical decision-making.
AI.method: MedSyn system lets physicians iteratively query an LLM provided with full clinical records while initially viewing only chief complaints. Seven physicians (3 seniors, 4 residents) completed baseline and AI-assisted sessions across 52 MIMIC-IV cases stratified by difficulty. Evaluation used blinded assessment with automated metrics and dialogue analysis.
AI.result: Residents' hard-case correctness rose from 0.589 to 0.734. Standardized completely-correct rates showed medium effect (Δ=0.092; d=0.47). Standardized any-match accuracy improved by 0.156 (p<0.0001), with residents showing largest F1 gain (Δ=0.138). Dialogue analysis revealed expertise-dependent query strategies and increased cross-expertise concordance (Δ=0.145).
AI.conclusion: Interactive LLM support meaningfully enhances diagnostic reasoning in emergency medicine. The system shows particular benefit for less experienced physicians on difficult cases, while revealing expertise-dependent interaction patterns that inform future AI clinical tool design.

title: Learning the Preferences of a Learning Agent
comment: Published at ICLR 2026, Workshop on Multi-Agent Learning and Its Opportunities in the Era of Generative AI. 9 pages main text
summary: For AI systems to be useful to humans, they must understand and act in accordance with our values and preferences. Since specifying preferences is a hard task, inverse reinforcement learning (IRL) aims to develop methods that allow for inferring preferences from observed behavior. However, IRL assumes the human to be approximately optimal. This is a big limitation in cases where the human themselves may be learning to act optimally in an environment. In this paper, we formalize the problem of learning the preferences of a learning agent: a predictor observes a learner acting online and tries to infer the underlying reward function being (initially suboptimally) optimized by the learner. We model the learner as either being no-regret, or as converging to an optimal Boltzmann policy over time. In each of these settings, we establish theoretical guarantees for various preference learning algorithms, or otherwise show that such guarantees are impossible.

AI.tldr: The paper addresses learning preferences from learning agents (not just optimal ones), developing theoretical guarantees for preference learning algorithms when agents are learning or no-regret.
AI.motivation: Inverse reinforcement learning (IRL) traditionally assumes humans are approximately optimal, but this is limiting when humans themselves are learning to act optimally in an environment. The paper aims to address this gap by formalizing preference learning from learning agents.
AI.method: Formalizes the problem of learning preferences from learning agents, modeling learners as either being no-regret or converging to an optimal Boltzmann policy over time. Analyzes theoretical guarantees for various preference learning algorithms in these settings.
AI.result: Establishes theoretical guarantees for preference learning algorithms in both settings (no-regret learners and those converging to Boltzmann policies), or shows when such guarantees are impossible.
AI.conclusion: The paper extends preference learning beyond the traditional IRL assumption of optimal behavior, providing theoretical foundations for inferring preferences from learning agents, with important implications for real-world AI systems that need to understand human preferences during the learning process.

title: A PyTorch Library of Turing-Complete Neural Networks
summary: We present a PyTorch package that compiles neural networks and their weights from Turing machine descriptions, producing models that exactly simulate the specified machine without any training. Given a transition function and a set of terminal states, the package constructs a model whose forward pass corresponds to one step of the Turing machine. Two architectures are implemented, each realizing a different theoretical result: (1) a transformer with self-attention, cross-attention, and feedforward layers based on Wei, Chen, and Ma (2021), and (2) a recurrent network based on Siegelmann and Sontag (1995) that encodes the stack in a Cantor set. We develop the constructions from first principles, showing how ReLU networks implement Boolean circuits (AND, OR, NOT, XOR gates and their composition into DNF formulas and binary adders) and how hard attention implements positional lookup on the tape. The package serves as a concrete, runnable reference for the symbolic-neural bridge, and as a foundation for future work on the stability of constructed solutions under gradient-based optimization. Code is available at https://github.com/jonrbates/turing.

AI.tldr: A PyTorch package that compiles neural networks from Turing machine descriptions, producing models that exactly simulate specified machines without training.
AI.motivation: To create a concrete, runnable reference for the symbolic-neural bridge and provide a foundation for studying the stability of constructed neural solutions under gradient-based optimization.
AI.method: The package constructs neural networks that exactly simulate Turing machines using two architectures: (1) transformer with self-attention, cross-attention, and feedforward layers based on Wei et al. (2021), and (2) recurrent network based on Siegelmann and Sontag (1995) encoding the stack in a Cantor set. The constructions use first principles showing how ReLU networks implement Boolean circuits and how hard attention implements positional lookup on the tape.
AI.result: Successfully implemented a PyTorch package that compiles neural networks from Turing machine descriptions, producing models whose forward pass corresponds to one step of the Turing machine. The package demonstrates how neural networks can exactly simulate symbolic computations without training.
AI.conclusion: The work provides a practical implementation bridging symbolic computation (Turing machines) with neural networks, serving as both a concrete reference for the symbolic-neural connection and a foundation for future research on optimization stability of constructed neural solutions.

title: Human-Inspired Memory Architecture for LLM Agents
summary: Current LLM agents lack principled mechanisms for managing persistent memory across long interaction horizons. We present a biologically-grounded memory architecture comprising six cognitive mechanisms: (1) sleep-phase consolidation, (2) interference-based forgetting, (3) engram maturation, (4) reconsolidation upon retrieval, (5) entity knowledge graphs, and (6) hybrid multi-cue retrieval. Each mechanism addresses a specific failure mode of naive memory accumulation. We introduce a synthetic calibration methodology that derives all pipeline thresholds without benchmark data exposure, eliminating a common source of evaluation leakage. We evaluate on two benchmarks. First, a VSCode issue-tracking dataset (13K issues, 120K events) where deduplication-based consolidation achieves 97.2% retention precision with 58% store reduction (+21.8 pp over baseline). Second, the LongMemEval personal-chat benchmark where we conduct the first streaming M-tier evaluation (475 sessions, ~540K unique turns). At a 200K-token context budget, our pipeline matches raw retrieval accuracy (70.1% vs. 71.2%, overlapping 95% CI) while exposing a tunable accuracy/store-size operating curve. At S-tier scale (50 sessions), dedup-based consolidation yields a +13.3 pp improvement in preference recall.

AI.tldr: A biologically-inspired memory architecture for LLM agents with six cognitive mechanisms and synthetic calibration methodology that optimizes memory management without benchmark data exposure, achieving efficient storage while maintaining retrieval accuracy.
AI.motivation: Current LLM agents lack principled mechanisms for managing persistent memory across long interaction horizons, leading to inefficient memory accumulation and retrieval problems.
AI.method: The paper presents a biologically-grounded memory architecture with six cognitive mechanisms: (1) sleep-phase consolidation, (2) interference-based forgetting, (3) engram maturation, (4) reconsolidation upon retrieval, (5) entity knowledge graphs, and (6) hybrid multi-cue retrieval. Introduces synthetic calibration methodology to derive pipeline thresholds without benchmark data exposure.
AI.result: On VSCode issue-tracking dataset: deduplication-based consolidation achieves 97.2% retention precision with 58% store reduction (+21.8 pp over baseline). On LongMemEval benchmark: at 200K-token context budget, matches raw retrieval accuracy (70.1% vs. 71.2%) while providing tunable accuracy/store-size operating curve. At S-tier scale, dedup-based consolidation yields +13.3 pp improvement in preference recall.
AI.conclusion: The proposed biologically-inspired memory architecture effectively addresses memory management failures in LLM agents through principled cognitive mechanisms and synthetic calibration, enabling efficient long-term memory with minimal accuracy degradation while eliminating evaluation leakage risks.

title: Empowering VLMs for Few-Shot Multimodal Time Series Classification via Tailored Agentic Reasoning
summary: In this paper, we propose the first VL$\underline{\textbf{M}}$ $\underline{\textbf{a}}$gentic $\underline{\textbf{r}}$easoning framework for few-$\underline{\textbf{s}}$hot multimodal $\underline{\textbf{T}}$ime $\underline{\textbf{S}}$eries $\underline{\textbf{C}}$lassification ($\textbf{MarsTSC}$), which introduces a self-evolving knowledge bank as a dynamic context iteratively refined via reflective agentic reasoning. The framework comprises three collaborative roles: i) Generator conducts reliable classification via reasoning; ii) Reflector diagnoses the root causes of reasoning errors to yield discriminative insights targeting the temporal features overlooked by Generator; iii) Modifier applies verified updates to the knowledge bank to prevent context collapse. We further introduce a test-time update strategy to enable cautious, continuous knowledge bank refinement to mitigate few-shot bias and distribution shift. Extensive experiments across 12 mainstream time series benchmarks demonstrate that $\textbf{MarsTSC}$ delivers substantial and consistent performance gains across 6 VLM backbones, outperforming both classical and foundation model-based time series baselines under few-shot conditions, while producing interpretable rationales that ground each classification decision in human-readable feature evidence.

AI.tldr: A novel VLM agentic reasoning framework called MarsTSC for few-shot multimodal time series classification that uses a self-evolving knowledge bank refined through collaborative reasoning roles.
AI.motivation: To address the challenges of few-shot multimodal time series classification, where models need to learn from limited labeled data and handle complex temporal patterns while maintaining interpretability.
AI.method: MarsTSC introduces a self-evolving knowledge bank with three collaborative roles: Generator for classification reasoning, Reflector for diagnosing reasoning errors and identifying overlooked temporal features, and Modifier for applying verified updates to the knowledge bank. Includes test-time update strategy for continuous refinement.
AI.result: Extensive experiments across 12 mainstream time series benchmarks show MarsTSC delivers substantial and consistent performance gains across 6 VLM backbones, outperforming both classical and foundation model-based baselines in few-shot conditions while producing interpretable rationales.
AI.conclusion: MarsTSC represents the first successful application of agentic reasoning for few-shot multimodal time series classification, achieving superior performance with interpretable feature evidence and effective mitigation of few-shot bias and distribution shift.

title: Temporal-Decay Shapley: A Time-Aware Data Valuation Framework for Time-Series Data
summary: With the rapid development of machine learning applications on time-series data, accurately assessing the value of training samples has become essential for data selection, noise detection, and model optimization. However, traditional data valuation methods usually assume that samples are independent and identically distributed, and thus ignore the time-varying nature of sample value in time-series data. This paper proposes an improved temporal Shapley data valuation method that enables accurate sample valuation for time-series data through a temporal decay mechanism and a multi-scale fusion strategy. Specifically, we propose three progressively enhanced temporal Shapley methods. Temporal-Decay Shapley (TDS) incorporates temporal information into Shapley value computation through exponential decay weights; the improved TDS adopts power exponential decay to better adapt to nonlinear temporal drift; and Multi-Scale Temporal-Decay Shapley (MS-TDS) constructs a multi-scale fusion mechanism that balances the value of short-term hotspot samples and long-term foundational samples through parallel multi-scale valuation and sample-level adaptive fusion. Experimental results show that the proposed methods generally outperform traditional methods in noise detection and high-value data identification tasks, with more evident advantages under most strongly temporal settings, thereby effectively improving the accuracy and robustness of data valuation.

AI.tldr: Proposes three enhanced Temporal Shapley methods (TDS, improved TDS, MS-TDS) for accurate sample valuation in time-series data by incorporating temporal decay and multi-scale fusion mechanisms.
AI.motivation: Traditional data valuation methods assume independent and identically distributed samples, ignoring the time-varying nature of sample value in time-series data, which is crucial for data selection, noise detection, and model optimization in machine learning applications.
AI.method: Three progressively enhanced temporal Shapley methods: 1) Temporal-Decay Shapley (TDS) with exponential decay weights, 2) improved TDS with power exponential decay for nonlinear temporal drift, 3) Multi-Scale Temporal-Decay Shapley (MS-TDS) with parallel multi-scale valuation and sample-level adaptive fusion to balance short-term hotspot and long-term foundational samples.
AI.result: The proposed methods generally outperform traditional methods in noise detection and high-value data identification tasks, with more evident advantages under strongly temporal settings, effectively improving accuracy and robustness of data valuation.
AI.conclusion: The improved temporal Shapley data valuation methods with temporal decay mechanisms and multi-scale fusion strategies successfully address the time-varying nature of sample value in time-series data, enhancing the practical utility of data valuation for machine learning applications.

title: Log analysis is necessary for credible evaluation of AI agents
summary: Agent benchmarks typically report only final outcomes: pass or fail. This threatens evaluation credibility in three ways. First, scores may be inflated or deflated by shortcuts and benchmark artifacts, misrepresenting capability. Second, benchmark performance may fail to predict real-world utility due to scaffold limitations and recurring failure modes. Finally, capability scores may conceal dangerous or catastrophic actions taken by the agent. We argue that log analysis -- the systematic tracking and analysis of the inputs, execution, and outputs of an AI agent -- is necessary to overcome these validity threats and promote credible agent evaluation. In this paper, we (1) present a taxonomy of threats to credible evaluation documented through log analysis, and (2) develop a set of guiding principles for log analysis. We illustrate these principles on tau-Bench Airline, revealing that pass^5 performance was under-elicited by nearly 50% and surfacing deployment failure modes invisible to outcome metrics. We conclude with pragmatic recommendations to increase uptake of log analysis, directed at diverse stakeholders including benchmark creators, model developers, independent evaluators, and deployers.

AI.tldr: Log analysis (systematic tracking of agent inputs, execution, and outputs) is necessary to overcome threats to credible agent evaluation that traditional pass/fail metrics fail to address.
AI.motivation: Traditional agent benchmarks only report final pass/fail outcomes, which threatens evaluation credibility in three ways: 1) scores can be inflated/deflated by shortcuts and artifacts, 2) benchmark performance may not predict real-world utility due to scaffold limitations, and 3) outcome scores may conceal dangerous/catastrophic actions taken by agents.
AI.method: The paper presents a taxonomy of threats to credible evaluation documented through log analysis, develops guiding principles for log analysis, and illustrates these principles on tau-Bench Airline to demonstrate real-world application.
AI.result: Log analysis revealed that pass^5 performance in tau-Bench Airline was under-elicited by nearly 50% and surfaced deployment failure modes that were invisible to traditional outcome metrics.
AI.conclusion: Log analysis is essential for credible agent evaluation. The paper provides pragmatic recommendations for various stakeholders (benchmark creators, model developers, independent evaluators, deployers) to increase adoption of log analysis practices.

title: CodeClinic: Evaluating Automation of Coding Skills for Clinical Reasoning Agents
summary: Clinical reasoning agents based on large language models (LLMs) aim to automate tasks such as intensive care unit (ICU) monitoring and patient state tracking from electronic health records (EHRs). Existing systems typically rely on manually curated clinical tools or skills for concepts such as sepsis detection and organ failure assessment. However, maintaining these tool libraries requires substantial expert effort, while zero-shot querying or code generation often produces inefficient and unreliable reasoning chains, especially under institution-specific clinical policies. We introduce CodeClinic, a benchmark built on MIMIC-IV for evaluating whether LLM agents can synthesize and compose reusable clinical skills instead of relying on fixed toolboxes. The benchmark contains two complementary tasks: longitudinal ICU surveillance and compositional information seeking. The longitudinal setting simulates monitoring patient trajectories with structured decisions every four hours across 25 findings and eight clinical families, while the compositional setting spans 63k instances across 259 tasks in nine domains and is stratified by compositional dependency depth to evaluate increasingly complex multi-step reasoning. We further propose an offline autoformalization pipeline that converts natural-language clinical guidelines into reusable and verified Python skill libraries through iterative LLM refinement. Compared with zero-shot code generation, the resulting libraries improve consistency while reducing per-query token usage by up to 40%.

AI.tldr: CodeClinic is a benchmark for evaluating LLM clinical agents' ability to synthesize reusable clinical skills from guidelines, improving over fixed toolboxes or unreliable zero-shot code generation.
AI.motivation: Existing clinical reasoning systems rely on manually curated tools or skills, which require substantial expert effort to maintain. Zero-shot querying or code generation often produces inefficient and unreliable reasoning chains, especially under institution-specific clinical policies.
AI.method: Introduced CodeClinic benchmark built on MIMIC-IV with two tasks: longitudinal ICU surveillance (monitoring patient trajectories with structured decisions every 4 hours) and compositional information seeking (63k instances across 259 tasks). Proposed offline autoformalization pipeline that converts natural-language clinical guidelines into reusable, verified Python skill libraries through iterative LLM refinement.
AI.result: The resulting skill libraries improve consistency while reducing per-query token usage by up to 40% compared with zero-shot code generation.
AI.conclusion: CodeClinic enables evaluation of whether LLM agents can synthesize and compose reusable clinical skills rather than relying on fixed toolboxes, addressing the maintenance burden of existing systems while improving efficiency and reliability.

title: Quantitative Sobolev Approximation Bounds for Neural Operators with Empirical Validation on Burgers Equation
summary: Neural operators have emerged as a powerful tool for learning mappings between infinite-dimensional function spaces. However, their approximation properties in Sobolev norms remain poorly quantified, even though these norms control both function values and derivatives and are the natural metrics for PDE well-posedness, stability, and generalization. We develop a functional-analytic framework for operator learning in Sobolev spaces and connect it to the numerical behavior of Fourier Neural Operators (FNOs) on a prototypical PDE. First, for a continuous nonlinear operator $\mathcal{G}: H^{s}(D)\to H^{t}(D')$ with $s > d/2$ and inputs restricted to a compact subset of $H^{s}(D)$, we prove that $\mathcal{G}$ can be uniformly approximated in $H^{t}$-norm by a neural operator with $\mathcal{O}(\varepsilon^{-d/s})$ trainable parameters. This yields an explicit complexity--error relation of the form $\|\mathcal{G}-\mathcal{G}_θ\|_{H^{t}} \lesssim C N^{-s/d}$. We then study the one-dimensional viscous Burgers solution operator $\mathcal{G}: u_{0}\mapsto u(\cdot,1)$ on a bounded $H^{1}$-ball and train FNOs with an $H^{1}$-loss. Across a sweep of model sizes, we obtain test $H^{1}$-errors down to $\mathcal{O}(10^{-7})$ and relative errors of order $10^{-3}$, with predictions accurately matching both solutions and spatial derivatives on held-out data.
