A log-log plot of Sobolev error versus parameter count exhibits an approximate power law $\|\mathcal{G}-\mathcal{G}_θ\|_{H^{1}} \approx C N^{-α}$ with empirical exponent $α\approx 1.4$, and long-horizon training reveals optimization instabilities in large FNOs, providing quantitative evidence that Sobolev-space approximation theory meaningfully predicts neural-operator scaling behavior.

AI.tldr: Neural operators can approximate PDE solution operators in Sobolev norms with quantifiable complexity-error relationships, demonstrated through theoretical analysis and empirical validation on Burgers equation.
AI.motivation: While neural operators are powerful for learning mappings between function spaces, their approximation properties in Sobolev norms (which control both function values and derivatives) remain poorly quantified, even though these norms are natural for PDE analysis, stability, and generalization.
AI.method: Developed a functional-analytic framework for operator learning in Sobolev spaces, proved uniform approximation bounds for continuous nonlinear operators, and empirically validated the theory by training Fourier Neural Operators (FNOs) on the one-dimensional viscous Burgers equation with H¹-loss.
AI.result: Theoretical result: for continuous nonlinear operators, a neural operator with O(ε^{-d/s}) parameters can uniformly approximate the operator in Hᵗ-norm, giving complexity-error relation ∥𝒢-𝒢_θ∥_Hᵗ ≲ C N^{-s/d}. Empirical results on Burgers equation: achieved test H¹-errors down to O(10^{-7}) with relative errors of order 10^{-3}, observed power law ∥𝒢-𝒢_θ∥_H¹ ≈ C N^{-α} with α≈1.4, and identified optimization instabilities in large FNOs.
AI.conclusion: Sobolev-space approximation theory meaningfully predicts neural-operator scaling behavior, providing both theoretical error bounds and empirical validation that neural operators can effectively approximate PDE solution operators while preserving derivative information.

title: Evaluating Developmental Cognition Capabilities of LLMs
summary:
Conversational AI is increasingly personalized around users' preferences, histories, goals, and knowledge, but much less around how users interpret and take up model outputs to construct and understand their reality. We draw on Robert Kegan's constructive-developmental theory as a complementary lens on this dimension. Existing methods for assessing developmental stage in the Keganian tradition rely either on expert interviews that do not scale or on sentence-completion instruments that are proprietary, lengthy, or invasive. To make this perspective tractable for LLM evaluation, we introduce the Developmental Sentence Completion Test (DSCT), a 20-item instrument designed to elicit developmental signal in self-administered text. Throughout, we treat the resulting labels as characterizations of stage-like structure in elicited responses, not as validated person-level developmental stage. We then ask how much of that signal can be recovered by LLMs across three elicited response regimes: simulated personas, real human respondents, and default model-generated answers.
  On simulated personas, top frontier models recover simulator-intended labels with high accuracy. On real human DSCT responses, human-LLM agreement is fair, with much stronger within-neighborhood than exact agreement. Finally, when LLMs answer DSCT prompts without persona-conditioning, their responses exhibit stable stage-like differences across model families, with larger and newer models tending to generate higher-rated text. These results suggest that stage-conditioned signal is cleaner in synthetic responses than in human-written DSCT text, and that the core constraint for stage-aware conversational AI is not classifier accuracy alone, but the availability of developmental signal from elicited text.

AI.tldr: Researchers adapt Robert Kegan's developmental psychology theory to conversational AI, creating a 20-item Developmental Sentence Completion Test (DSCT) to assess stage-like responses across LLMs, simulated personas, and human text.
AI.motivation: Conversational AI focuses on user preferences/history but neglects how users interpret and construct reality. Kegan's constructive-developmental theory offers valuable lens for this dimension, but existing assessment methods (expert interviews, proprietary tests) don't scale for LLM evaluation.
AI.method: Created Developmental Sentence Completion Test (DSCT), a 20-item self-administered instrument to elicit developmental signal in text. Tested across three response regimes: 1) simulated personas, 2) real human respondents, and 3) default model-generated answers, treating labels as characterizations of stage-like structure in responses rather than validated person-level stages.
AI.result: 1) On simulated personas: top frontier models accurately recover simulator-intended labels. 2) On human DSCT responses: fair human-LLM agreement with stronger within-neighborhood than exact agreement. 3) On unconditioned LLM answers: stable stage-like differences across model families, with larger/newer models generating higher-rated text, suggesting cleaner developmental signal in synthetic vs human text.
AI.conclusion: Stage-conditioned signal is clearer in synthetic responses than human-written DSCT text. The core constraint for stage-aware conversational AI isn't classifier accuracy alone, but the availability of developmental signal from elicited text, offering scalable approach to incorporate developmental perspectives into AI evaluation.

title: EnactToM: An Evolving Benchmark for Functional Theory of Mind in Embodied Agents
summary: Theory of Mind (ToM), the ability to track others epistemic state, makes humans efficient collaborators. AI agents need the same capacity in multi agent settings, yet existing benchmarks mostly test literal ToM by asking direct belief questions. The ability act optimally on implicit beliefs in embodied environments, called functional ToM, remains largely untested. We introduce EnactToM, an evolving benchmark of 300 embodied multi-agent tasks set in a 3D household with partial observability, private information, and constrained communication. Each task is formally verified for solvability and required epistemic depth, and new tasks are generated increase difficulty as models improve. On the hard split, all seven evaluated frontier models score 0.0% Pass^3 on functional task completion, while averaging 45.0% on literal belief probes. Manual analysis traces 93% of sampled failures to epistemic coordination breakdowns such as withheld information, ignored partner constraints, and misallocated messages, providing a concrete target for future work.

AI.tldr: EnactToM: A benchmark for testing functional Theory of Mind in embodied multi-agent 3D environments where AI agents must coordinate based on implicit beliefs rather than just answer direct belief questions.
AI.motivation: Current AI benchmarks only test literal Theory of Mind (direct belief questions), but real collaboration requires functional ToM - the ability to act optimally on implicit beliefs in embodied environments. This gap in evaluation prevents AI from being effective collaborators in multi-agent settings.
AI.method: Created EnactToM, an evolving benchmark of 300 embodied multi-agent tasks set in a 3D household environment with partial observability, private information, and constrained communication. Each task is formally verified for solvability and required epistemic depth, with difficulty automatically increasing as models improve.
AI.result: On the hard split, all seven evaluated frontier models scored 0.0% on functional task completion (Pass^3 metric), while averaging 45.0% on literal belief probes. Manual analysis showed 93% of failures were due to epistemic coordination breakdowns like withheld information, ignored partner constraints, and misallocated messages.
AI.conclusion: Current AI models lack functional Theory of Mind capabilities needed for effective embodied collaboration, despite performing moderately on literal belief questions. The benchmark reveals specific epistemic coordination failures and provides a concrete target for improving AI's collaborative reasoning in realistic multi-agent environments.

title: Communication Dynamics Neural Networks: FFT-Diagonalized Layers for Improved Hessian Conditioning at Reduced Parameter Count
summary:
Background and motivation. The Communication Dynamics (CD) framework, introduced in two earlier papers for atomic-energy prediction and field-induced superconductivity, treats each physical channel as a (2l+1)-vertex polygon whose discrete Fourier transform yields its energy spectrum. This paper applies the same circulant-spectral machinery to neural-network design.
  Layer construction. CDLinear is a block-circulant linear layer with block size B = 2l+1 and 1/B the parameter count of a dense layer of equal input/output dimensions. Three properties follow from the construction. (i) The Hessian of mean-squared loss with respect to the weights is diagonalized by the discrete Fourier transform, with eigenvalues |F[Xj](k)|^2 read directly from the input statistics (Theorem 1). (ii) Under input pre-whitening, the population Hessian condition number satisfies kappa = 1 exactly, with the empirical condition number bounded by 1+O(sqrt(B/N)) on N samples (Theorem 2). (iii) The Shannon noise rate alpha_CD = 0.0118 calibrated in the parent CD papers from the Na D-doublet specifies a transferable, non-arbitrary dropout rate.
  Empirical evaluation. A CDLinear MLP at B = 4 achieves 97.50% +/- 0.23% test accuracy with 2,380 parameters versus 98.15% +/- 0.47% for a parameter-matched dense MLP at 8,970 parameters, a 3.8x parameter reduction at 0.65% accuracy cost, within one standard deviation of the seed-to-seed spread. The CD-MLP mean Hessian condition number kappa = 1.9x10^4 is 310x smaller than the dense baseline kappa = 5.9x10^6, in quantitative agreement with Theorem 2.

AI.tldr: The paper applies Communication Dynamics (CD) framework to neural networks, creating CDLinear layers that reduce parameters by factor B while achieving near-baseline performance and dramatically improving Hessian conditioning.
AI.motivation: To leverage the circulant-spectral machinery from physics (Communication Dynamics framework) for neural network design, enabling parameter-efficient layers with theoretically guaranteed good conditioning properties.
AI.method: Introduces CDLinear - a block-circulant linear layer with block size B = 2l+1 that has 1/B the parameter count of dense layers. Uses discrete Fourier transform properties to derive theoretical guarantees about Hessian conditioning and provides a physics-calibrated dropout rate.
AI.result: CDLinear MLP with B=4 achieves 97.50% test accuracy (vs 98.15% dense baseline) with 3.8x parameter reduction. Hessian condition number improves by 310x (1.9×10^4 vs 5.9×10^6), confirming theoretical predictions.
AI.conclusion: The CD framework successfully transfers from physics to neural networks, enabling parameter-efficient architectures with provably good conditioning and physics-calibrated regularization, achieving competitive performance with dramatic parameter reduction.

title: Why Retrying Fails: Context Contamination in LLM Agent Pipelines
summary: When an LLM agent fails a multi-step tool-augmented task and retries, the failed attempt typically remains in its context window -- contaminating the next attempt and elevating the per-step error rate beyond the base level. This context-contaminated restart phenomenon is widely observed in practice yet entirely lacks formal treatment. We introduce the Context-Contaminated Restart Model (CCRM): a chain of T tool-call steps, each failing with base rate epsilon_0; after any failed attempt, the subsequent attempt operates in contaminated context with elevated error rate epsilon_1 > epsilon_0. Under this model we derive five main results. (R1) An exact closed-form formula for P(succeed in at most K attempts). (R2) A cascade-overhead theorem giving the additional attempts Delta K incurred by contamination versus the clean-restart baseline. (R3) An optimal budget-allocation theorem identifying the pipeline depth T* that maximises success probability for a fixed total budget B=KT; we prove the closed form T* = sqrt(B * log(1/(1-epsilon_1)) / log(1/(1-epsilon_0))), with K*=B/T*. (R4) An information-theoretic lower bound via Le Cam's method showing K_CCRM is tight up to O(1). (R5) A clean-restart dominance theorem quantifying the exact benefit of context-clearing before retry. We validate CCRM on real SWE-bench Verified data: the IID model overestimates pass@3 by 17.4 percentage points (98.6% vs. 81.2%), while CCRM fits with error less than 0.001, implying a cascade ratio of epsilon_1/epsilon_0 = 7.1. Monte Carlo experiments confirm all theoretical predictions.

AI.tldr: CCRM models LLM agent failures where failed attempts contaminate context, elevating error rates. Provides analytical formulas for success probability, optimal task pipeline depth, and validates with real SWE-bench data.
AI.motivation: LLM agents performing multi-step tool-augmented tasks exhibit context contamination when they fail and retry - failed attempts remain in context, elevating error rates beyond baseline. This phenomenon is widely observed but lacks formal analysis.
AI.method: Introduces the Context-Contaminated Restart Model (CCRM): T tool-call steps chain with base error rate ε₀, elevated to ε₁ > ε₀ after failed attempts. Derives five analytical results including exact success probability formulas, cascade-overhead analysis, optimal budget allocation, and information-theoretic bounds.
AI.result: Provides closed-form formulas for success probability in K attempts, cascade overhead analysis, optimal pipeline depth T* = √(B·log(1/(1-ε₁))/log(1/(1-ε₀))), and validation showing CCRM fits SWE-bench data (error < 0.001) while IID model overestimates performance by 17.4 percentage points.
AI.conclusion: CCRM accurately models context contamination in LLM agents, providing theoretical foundations for analyzing failure cascades. The model explains empirical performance gaps and offers optimal strategies for budget allocation and context management in tool-augmented LLM systems.

title: RADAR: Redundancy-Aware Diffusion for Multi-Agent Communication Structure Generation
summary: Compared with individual agents, large language model based multi-agent systems have shown great capabilities consistently across diverse tasks, including code generation, mathematical reasoning, and planning, etc. Despite their impressive performance, the effectiveness and robustness of these systems heavily rely on their communication topology, which is often fixed or generated in a single step. This restricts fine-grained structural exploration and flexible composition, resulting in excessive token utilization on simple tasks while limiting capability on complicated tasks. To mitigate this challenge, we introduce RADAR, a redundancy-aware and query-adaptive generative framework that actively reduce communication overhead. Motivated by recent progress in conditional discrete graph diffusion models, we formulate communication topology design as a step-by-step generation process, guided by the effective size of the graph. Comprehensive experiments on six benchmarks demonstrate that RADAR consistently outperforms recent baselines, achieving higher accuracy, lower token consumption, and greater robustness across diverse scenarios. Our code and data are available at https://github.com/cszhangzhen/RADAR.

AI.tldr: RADAR is a redundancy-aware, query-adaptive generative framework that dynamically designs communication topologies for multi-agent systems to reduce token consumption while improving performance across diverse tasks.
AI.motivation: Current LLM-based multi-agent systems rely on fixed or single-step communication topologies that restrict structural exploration and flexible composition, leading to excessive token usage on simple tasks and limited capability on complex tasks.
AI.method: RADAR formulates communication topology design as a step-by-step generation process guided by the effective size of the graph, leveraging conditional discrete graph diffusion models to actively reduce communication overhead.
AI.result: Comprehensive experiments on six benchmarks show RADAR consistently outperforms recent baselines with higher accuracy, lower token consumption, and greater robustness across diverse scenarios.
AI.conclusion: RADAR provides an effective solution to the communication overhead problem in multi-agent systems through adaptive topology generation, achieving better performance with fewer resources while maintaining robustness.

title: CERSA: Cumulative Energy-Retaining Subspace Adaptation for Memory-Efficient Fine-Tuning
summary: To mitigate the memory constraints associated with fine-tuning large pre-trained models, existing parameter-efficient fine-tuning (PEFT) methods, such as LoRA, rely on low-rank updates. However, such updates fail to fully capture the rank characteristics of the weight modifications observed in full-parameter fine-tuning, resulting in a performance gap. Furthermore, LoRA and other existing PEFT methods still require substantial memory to store the full set of frozen weights, limiting their efficiency in resource-constrained settings. To addres these limitations, we introduce Cumulative Energy-Retaining Subspace Adaptation (CERSA), a novel fine-tuning paradigm that leverages singular value decomposition (SVD) to retain only the principal components responsible for 90% to 95% of the spectral energy. By fine-tuning low-rank representations derived from this principal subspace, CERSA significantly reduces memory consumption. We conduct extensive evaluations of CERSA across models of varying scales and domains, including image recognition, text-to-image generation, and natural language understanding. Empirical results demonstrate that CERSA consistently outperforms state-of-the-art PEFT methods while achieving substantially lower memory requirements. The code will be publicly released.

AI.tldr: CERSA is a new parameter-efficient fine-tuning method that uses SVD to retain only the most important principal components (90-95% spectral energy) for low-rank adaptation, significantly reducing memory usage while outperforming existing PEFT methods.
AI.motivation: Existing PEFT methods like LoRA have two main limitations: 1) Their low-rank updates don't fully capture the rank characteristics of full-parameter fine-tuning, leading to performance gaps. 2) They still require storing full frozen weights, which limits efficiency in resource-constrained settings.
AI.method: CERSA uses singular value decomposition (SVD) to retain only the principal components responsible for 90-95% of the spectral energy. It then fine-tunes low-rank representations derived from this principal subspace, significantly reducing memory consumption while maintaining performance.
AI.result: Extensive evaluations across varying model scales and domains (image recognition, text-to-image generation, natural language understanding) show CERSA consistently outperforms state-of-the-art PEFT methods while achieving substantially lower memory requirements.
AI.conclusion: CERSA introduces an effective fine-tuning paradigm that addresses both performance and memory limitations of existing PEFT methods by leveraging spectral energy retention through SVD, offering a practical solution for resource-constrained settings.

title: Biological Plausibility and Representational Alignment of Feedback Alignment in Convolutional Networks
summary: The feedback alignment (FA) algorithm offers a biologically plausible alternative to backpropagation (BP) for training neural networks yet notably fails to scale to convolutional architectures. Modifications have been proposed to address this limitation, but at questionable cost to biological plausibility. In this paper, we evaluate five learning algorithms including modified FA and standard BP, applied to the same convolutional architecture with the CIFAR-10 dataset. We provide a tripartite comparative analysis focusing on biological plausibility, interpretability, and computational complexity. Our results indicate that modified FA algorithms converge on internal representations that are structurally similar to those produced by backpropagation. In particular, it appears the functional success of modified FA algorithms may be rooted in their ability to mimic the representational geometry of backpropagation, converging on similar representations despite relying on fundamentally different weight update mechanisms.

AI.tldr: Modified feedback alignment algorithms can achieve similar representational geometry to backpropagation in convolutional networks despite different weight update mechanisms, while maintaining better biological plausibility.
AI.motivation: Feedback alignment offers a biologically plausible alternative to backpropagation for neural network training, but fails to scale effectively to convolutional architectures. Existing modifications to address this scaling issue compromise biological plausibility, creating a need to better understand the trade-offs.
AI.method: Systematically evaluate five learning algorithms (including modified FA and standard BP) on the same convolutional architecture using CIFAR-10 dataset. Conduct tripartite comparative analysis focusing on biological plausibility, interpretability, and computational complexity.
AI.result: Modified FA algorithms converge on internal representations that are structurally similar to those produced by backpropagation. Their functional success appears rooted in mimicking backpropagation's representational geometry despite fundamentally different weight update mechanisms.
AI.conclusion: The ability of modified feedback alignment algorithms to achieve similar representational geometry to backpropagation while maintaining better biological plausibility suggests promising directions for biologically plausible deep learning algorithms.

title: Route by State, Recover from Trace: STAR with Failure-Aware Markov Routing for Multi-Agent Spatiotemporal Reasoning
summary: Compositional spatiotemporal reasoning often requires a system to invoke multiple heterogeneous specialists, such as geometric, temporal, topological, and trajectory agents. A central question is how such a system should route among specialists when execution does not simply succeed or fail, but fails in qualitatively different ways. Existing tool-augmented and multi-agent LLM systems typically leave this routing decision implicit in language generation, making recovery ad hoc, difficult to interpret, and hard to optimize. This paper presents STAR (Spatio-Temporal Agent Router), a failure-aware routing framework that externalizes inter-agent control as a state-conditioned transition policy over the current agent, task type, and typed execution status. At the center of STARis an agent routing matrix that combines expert-specified nominal routes with recovery transitions learned from execution traces. Because the matrix conditions on distinct failure states, the router can respond differently to malformed outputs, missing dependencies, and tool--query mismatches, rather than collapsing them into a generic retry signal. Specialists execute through a tool-grounded extract--compute--deposit protocol and write intermediate results to a shared blackboard for downstream fusion. Results prove that retaining unsuccessful traces during training enlarges the support of the routing policy on error states, enabling recovery transitions that success-only training cannot represent. Across three spatiotemporal benchmarks and eight backbone LLMs, STAR improves over multiple baselines with the clearest gains on queries whose execution deviates from the nominal routing path. Router-specific ablations and recovery analyses further show that typed failure-aware routing, rather than specialist composition alone, is a key factor for these improvements.

AI.tldr: STAR (Spatio-Temporal Agent Router) is a failure-aware routing framework for compositional spatiotemporal reasoning that learns recovery transitions from execution traces to handle different types of failures, improving performance on complex queries that deviate from nominal routing paths.
AI.motivation: Current tool-augmented and multi-agent LLM systems make routing decisions implicitly in language generation, making recovery ad hoc, difficult to interpret, and hard to optimize when dealing with compositional spatiotemporal reasoning that requires multiple heterogeneous specialists (geometric, temporal, topological, trajectory agents).
AI.method: STAR externalizes inter-agent control as a state-conditioned transition policy over current agent, task type, and typed execution status using an agent routing matrix that combines expert-specified nominal routes with recovery transitions learned from execution traces. Specialists execute via a tool-grounded extract-compute-deposit protocol writing to a shared blackboard.
AI.result: STAR improves over multiple baselines across three spatiotemporal benchmarks and eight backbone LLMs, with the clearest gains on queries whose execution deviates from nominal routing paths. Training with unsuccessful traces enables recovery transitions that success-only training cannot represent.
AI.conclusion: Typed failure-aware routing, not just specialist composition alone, is key to improvements in compositional spatiotemporal reasoning systems, enabling systematic recovery from different failure types rather than collapsing them into generic retry signals.

title: Physics-Modeled Neural Networks
summary:
We introduce \emph{Dynamical Physics-Modeled Neural Networks} (DynPMNNs), a continuous-time deep learning architecture in which each hidden layer is defined as the solution of an ordinary differential equation. Unlike classical feed-forward networks, this approach replaces static activation functions with time-evolving dynamical systems, providing a biologically inspired interpretation of hidden-layer behavior and enabling the integration of physically meaningful models. The framework is rigorously grounded in Reproducing Kernel Banach Spaces (RKBSs), allowing DynPMNNs to be characterized as finite-dimensional solutions of an abstract training problem and revealing structural connections with standard neural networks.
  We present a concrete implementation based on the FitzHugh--Nagumo model for neuronal activation, where numerical ODE solvers are embedded into the computational graph via Euler-type schemes. Both network weights and dynamical parameters are trained jointly. Through experiments on the California Housing dataset, we compare DynPMNNs with Neural ODEs (NODEs) and Closed-form Continuous-Time Networks (CfCs). Despite using fewer trainable parameters, DynPMNNs achieve competitive performance.
  These results position DynPMNNs as a principled bridge between dynamical systems and deep learning, with promising directions for further research in expressivity, stability, and physics-based modeling.

AI.tldr: DynPMNNs are continuous-time neural networks where hidden layers are ODE solutions, using dynamical systems as activation functions and connecting to physics models, achieving competitive performance with fewer parameters.
AI.motivation: The motivation is to create a biologically inspired architecture that replaces static activation functions with time-evolving dynamical systems, enabling integration of physically meaningful models and bridging dynamical systems with deep learning.
AI.method: DynPMNNs define each hidden layer as the solution of an ODE, using FitzHugh-Nagumo neuronal activation model. They use Euler-type ODE solvers embedded in computational graphs, with joint training of weights and dynamical parameters. The framework is grounded in Reproducing Kernel Banach Spaces theory.
AI.result: On California Housing dataset, DynPMNNs achieve competitive performance compared to Neural ODEs and Closed-form Continuous-Time Networks, despite using fewer trainable parameters.
AI.conclusion: DynPMNNs provide a principled bridge between dynamical systems and deep learning, with promising research directions in expressivity, stability, and physics-based modeling.

title: What Will Happen Next: Large Models-Driven Deduction for Emergency Instances
summary: Traditional simulation methods reproduce occurred emergency instances through presetting to assist people in risk assessment and emergency decision-making. However, due to the lack of randomness and diversity, existing simulation systems struggle to fully explore the potential risk as emergency instances are scarce. In contrast, Large Models (LMs) can dynamically adjust generation strategies to introduce controllable randomness, while also possessing extensive prior knowledge and cross-domain knowledge transfer capabilities. Inspired by it, we propose the LMs-driven World Line Divergence System (WLDS), which enables diversified visualization and deduction of emergency instances in different domains. WLDS leverages LMs to deduce emergency instances in various development directions, and introduces the factual calibration and logical calibration mechanism to ensure factual accuracy and logical rigor during the deduction process. The interactive module can independently select deduction directions to avoid potential hallucinations that are difficult for the system to identify. Furthermore, by introducing the visualization module, WLDS forms simulation and deduction that combine text and images, which enhances interpretability. Extensive experiments conducted on the proposed Emergency Instances Deduction (EID) benchmark dataset demonstrate that WLDS achieves high-precision and high-fidelity simulation and deduction of emergency instances in multiple specific domains. Relevant experiments further demonstrate that WLDS can generate more emergency instances deduction data for users and provide support for better decision-making in similar emergency instances in the future.

AI.tldr: A World Line Divergence System (WLDS) using Large Models to generate diverse emergency simulations with controllable randomness, overcoming limitations of traditional preset-based emergency simulation methods.
AI.motivation: Traditional emergency simulation methods rely on preset scenarios which lack randomness and diversity, making them insufficient for exploring potential risks due to scarce emergency instances. Large Models offer dynamic generation with controllable randomness and cross-domain knowledge transfer capabilities.
AI.method: WLDS uses Large Models to deduce emergency instances in various development directions, incorporates factual and logical calibration mechanisms to ensure accuracy, includes an interactive module for direction selection to avoid hallucinations, and adds a visualization module for text-image simulation.
AI.result: Extensive experiments on the Emergency Instances Deduction (EID) benchmark show WLDS achieves high-precision and high-fidelity simulation and deduction across multiple domains, generating more emergency deduction data to support better decision-making for future similar emergencies.
AI.conclusion: WLDS effectively addresses the limitations of traditional emergency simulation by leveraging Large Models' capabilities for diverse, controllable, and interpretable emergency instance deduction, providing valuable support for risk assessment and emergency decision-making.

title: PC3D: Zero-Shot Cooperation Across Variable Rosters via Personalized Context Distillation
summary: Cooperative multi-agent reinforcement learning often assumes a fixed execution team, yet many decentralized systems must operate with varying numbers of active agents during deployment. We study this setting under episodic roster variation: each episode is executed by a set of homogeneous agents, with the team size varying across episodes. Agents act only from local histories, without execution-time communication, privileged coordinators, or online retraining. Therefore, effective cooperation requires each agent to recover relevant context about the active team and adapt its behavior accordingly. To this end, we propose PC3D (Personalized Central Coordination Context Distillation), a method for training decentralized policies to recover and use personalized coordination context from local interaction histories. During training, a set-structured centralized teacher compresses the active team into coordination tokens and personalizes them into agent-specific contexts, which are distilled into decentralized policies. At execution, each agent predicts its own context from local history and adaptively uses it to condition decision-making. Across three cooperative MARL benchmarks, PC3D achieves higher returns than the evaluated baselines with both seen and unseen roster sizes, and ablations attribute these gains to both context distillation and adaptive context use.

AI.tldr: PC3D enables decentralized agents to adapt to varying team sizes by learning personalized coordination context from local histories, without requiring execution-time communication or retraining.
AI.motivation: Real-world decentralized multi-agent systems often need to operate with varying numbers of active agents across different episodes, but existing cooperative MARL typically assumes fixed team sizes. Agents must cooperate effectively despite having only local observations, no communication during execution, and no central coordinator or online retraining capabilities.
AI.method: PC3D (Personalized Central Coordination Context Distillation) trains decentralized policies using a centralized teacher that compresses the active team into coordination tokens, then personalizes them into agent-specific contexts. These contexts are distilled into decentralized policies, allowing each agent to predict its own context from local history and adaptively use it for decision-making.
AI.result: PC3D achieves higher returns than evaluated baselines across three cooperative MARL benchmarks with both seen and unseen roster sizes. Ablation studies confirm that both context distillation and adaptive context use contribute to performance gains.
AI.conclusion: PC3D successfully addresses the challenge of roster variation in decentralized multi-agent systems by enabling agents to recover and utilize personalized coordination context from local histories, demonstrating robust performance across varying team sizes without requiring execution-time coordination mechanisms.

title: Echo-LoRA: Parameter-Efficient Fine-Tuning via Cross-Layer Representation Injection
summary: Parameter-efficient fine-tuning (PEFT) has become a practical route for adapting large language models to downstream tasks, with LoRA-style methods being particularly attractive because they are inexpensive to train and easy to deploy. Most LoRA variants, however, revise the update rule within the weight space of each layer and leave the intermediate representations formed by deeper layers largely unused. We propose Echo-LoRA, a cross-layer representation injection method for parameter-efficient fine-tuning. During training, Echo-LoRA collects boundary hidden states from deeper source layers, aggregates them into a sample-level echo representation, and uses lightweight projection and gating networks to inject the resulting signal into shallow LoRA or DoRA modules. Answer-only masking, masked distillation, and stochastic routing are used to keep this auxiliary path stable and to reduce the gap between training and inference. On eight commonsense reasoning benchmarks, Echo-LoRA exceeds the reported LoRA baselines by 5.7 percentage points on average across LLaMA-7B, LLaMA2-7B, and LLaMA3-8B. Under reproduced LoRA baselines in our unified implementation, the average gain is 3.0 points; when combined with DoRA, the gain is 2.7 points. The Echo path is discarded after training, so the deployed model keeps the original low-rank LoRA/DoRA form and adds neither inference-time parameters nor inference computation.

AI.tldr: Echo-LoRA improves LoRA-style PEFT by injecting aggregated deep-layer representations into shallow modules during training, achieving significant performance gains while maintaining the same inference efficiency as standard LoRA/DoRA.
AI.motivation: Most LoRA variants only modify weight updates within each layer, leaving the valuable intermediate representations from deeper layers underutilized during fine-tuning. The authors want to leverage these deep-layer representations to improve PEFT performance while maintaining deployment efficiency.
AI.method: Echo-LoRA collects boundary hidden states from deeper source layers, aggregates them into sample-level echo representations, and uses lightweight projection and gating networks to inject this signal into shallow LoRA/DoRA modules. It employs answer-only masking, masked distillation, and stochastic routing to stabilize training and reduce train-inference gap.
AI.result: On eight commonsense reasoning benchmarks, Echo-LoRA outperforms reported LoRA baselines by 5.7 percentage points on average across LLaMA-7B, LLaMA2-7B, and LLaMA3-8B. When evaluated with reproduced baselines in unified implementation, average gain is 3.0 points, and when combined with DoRA, gain is 2.7 points.
AI.conclusion: Echo-LoRA successfully leverages deep-layer representations to significantly boost LoRA-style PEFT performance while maintaining deployment efficiency, as the echo path is discarded after training, leaving only the original LoRA/DoRA form without additional inference parameters or computation.

title: The Echo Amplifies the Knowledge: Somatic Marker Analogues in Language Models via Emotion Vector Re-Injection
summary:
Current language model memory systems store what happened but not how it felt. This distinction -- between semantic memory (knowing about a past event) and episodic memory (re-experiencing it) -- was identified by Tulving as the difference between noetic and autonoetic consciousness. Damasio demonstrated that humans with intact knowledge but absent emotional markers exhibit impaired decision-making.
  We bridge this gap for language models. Using Gemma 3 1B-IT with pretrained Gemma Scope 2 sparse autoencoders, we identify 310 emotion-exclusive features at layer 22 with psychologically valid geometry. We construct distinctive-feature emotion vectors during experience and partially re-inject them during recall, triggered by context similarity at layer 7.
  We test four conditions paralleling Damasio's framework: A (no memory), B (semantic labels), C (emotion echo), and BC (semantic + echo). For emotional orientation, the echo alone steepens the threat-safety gradient: the regression slope of threat rating on contextual similarity is 0.80 for C vs 0.56 for A ($p$=0.011, permutation test). For decisions, the echo amplifies knowledge into action: BC=80% good choices vs B=52% ($z$=+2.60, $p$<0.01), while the echo alone has no effect (C=22%, n.s.). The echo changes how the model feels independently, but changes what it does only when combined with knowledge -- replicating Damasio's core finding.
  The echo amplifies knowledge. It does not replace it.

AI.tldr: Language models can now incorporate emotional memory (episodic memory) alongside factual memory, using emotion-exclusive features that improve decision-making by amplifying knowledge into action, mirroring human cognitive processes.
AI.motivation: Current language models only store semantic memory (facts about past events) but lack episodic memory (emotional experience of events). This gap between knowing what happened and feeling how it happened impairs decision-making capabilities, as demonstrated by Damasio's research on humans with intact knowledge but absent emotional markers.
AI.method: Using Gemma 3 1B-IT with Gemma Scope 2 sparse autoencoders, researchers identified 310 emotion-exclusive features at layer 22 with psychologically valid geometry. They constructed distinctive-feature emotion vectors during experience and partially re-injected them during recall, triggered by context similarity at layer 7. Four conditions were tested paralleling Damasio's framework: A (no memory), B (semantic labels), C (emotion echo), and BC (semantic + echo).
AI.result: The emotion echo alone steepens emotional orientation (0.80 vs 0.56 slope for threat rating, p=0.011). For decisions, the echo amplifies knowledge into action: BC=80% good choices vs B=52% (z=+2.60, p<0.01), while echo alone has no effect (C=22%, n.s.). Emotion echo changes feelings independently but only changes actions when combined with knowledge.
AI.conclusion: The research successfully bridges the semantic-episodic memory gap in language models, demonstrating that emotional memory (episodic) amplifies and complements semantic memory rather than replacing it, replicating Damasio's core findings about how emotional markers transform knowledge into effective decision-making.

title: Optimal and Scalable MAPF via Multi-Marginal Optimal Transport and Schrödinger Bridges
comment: Accepted in ICML 2026 as a spotlight paper
summary: We consider anonymous multi-agent path finding (MAPF) where a set of robots is tasked to travel to a set of targets on a finite, connected graph. We show that MAPF can be cast as a special class of multi-marginal optimal transport (MMOT) problems with an underlying Markovian structure, under which the exponentially large MMOT collapses to a linear program (LP) polynomial in size. Focusing on the anonymous setting, we establish conditions under which the corresponding LP is feasible, totally unimodular, and consequently, yields min-cost, integral $(\{0,1\})$ transports that do not overlap in both space and time. To adapt the approach to large-scale problems, we cast the MAPF-MMOT in a probabilistic framework via Schrödinger bridges. Under standard assumptions, we show that the Schrödinger bridge formulation reduces to an entropic regularization of the corresponding MMOT that admits an iterative Sinkhorn-type solution. The Schrödinger bridge, being a probabilistic framework, provides a shadow (fractional) transport that we use as a template to solve a reduced LP and demonstrate that it results in near-optimal, integral transports at a significant reduction in complexity. Extensive experiments highlight the optimality and scalability of the proposed approaches.

AI.tldr: MAPF is reformulated as a multi-marginal optimal transport problem with Markovian structure, enabling polynomial-sized linear programming solutions and scalable Schrödinger bridge approximations.
AI.motivation: Anonymous multi-agent path finding (MAPF) on graphs is computationally challenging due to its combinatorial nature. The paper aims to develop efficient, scalable approaches by leveraging connections to optimal transport theory.
AI.method: 1) Cast MAPF as a special class of multi-marginal optimal transport (MMOT) with Markovian structure, reducing it to polynomial-sized linear program (LP). 2) Introduce Schrödinger bridge probabilistic framework as entropic regularization of MMOT, enabling iterative Sinkhorn-type solutions and shadow transports as templates for reduced LP.
AI.result: Established conditions for LP feasibility and total unimodularity, guaranteeing min-cost integral transports without space/time overlap. Schrödinger bridge approach provides near-optimal integral transports with significant complexity reduction. Experimental validation shows optimality and scalability.
AI.conclusion: The MAPF-MMOT framework provides theoretically grounded, computationally efficient solutions for anonymous path finding, with both exact LP and scalable probabilistic approximations.

title: Generalized Category Discovery in Federated Graph Learning
summary: Federated Graph Learning (FGL) enables collaborative learning over distributed graph data, yet existing approaches largely rely on a closed-world assumption, limiting their applicability in dynamic environments where novel categories continuously emerge. To bridge this gap, we target the practical scenario of Federated Graph Generalized Category Discovery (FGGCD), aiming to collaboratively discover novel categories across decentralized graph clients while retaining knowledge of known categories. We observe that FGGCD introduces two fundamental challenges: (1) the Neighborhood Absorption Effect, where structural fragmentation leads to biased neighborhood aggregation, causing novel nodes to be misclassified as known categories; and (2) Global Semantic Inconsistency, where the aforementioned local biases propagate to the server and are amplified by heterogeneous subgraph distributions, hindering cross-client knowledge integration. To address these issues, we propose GCD-FGL, an FGL framework for GCD that integrates a client-side Topology-Reliable Semantic Alignment and Discovery process to mitigate the neighborhood absorption effect, and a server-side Hierarchical Prototype Alignment strategy to resolve global semantic inconsistency. Extensive experiments on five real-world graph datasets demonstrate that GCD-FGL consistently outperforms state-of-the-art baselines, achieving an average absolute gain of +4.86 in HRScore.

AI.tldr: GCD-FGL is a federated graph learning framework for generalized category discovery that addresses novel category emergence in dynamic environments by mitigating neighborhood absorption effects and global semantic inconsistency.
AI.motivation: Current federated graph learning approaches rely on closed-world assumptions and cannot handle dynamic environments where new categories continuously emerge. There's a need for federated graph generalized category discovery (FGGCD) to discover novel categories across decentralized clients while preserving known category knowledge.
AI.method: Proposed GCD-FGL framework with two key components: 1) Client-side Topology-Reliable Semantic Alignment and Discovery to mitigate neighborhood absorption effect, and 2) Server-side Hierarchical Prototype Alignment strategy to resolve global semantic inconsistency caused by heterogeneous subgraph distributions.
AI.result: Extensive experiments on five real-world graph datasets show GCD-FGL consistently outperforms state-of-the-art baselines, achieving an average absolute gain of +4.86 in HRScore.
AI.conclusion: GCD-FGL effectively addresses the fundamental challenges of FGGCD, enabling practical generalized category discovery in federated graph learning environments with novel category emergence.

title: Quantile Geometry Regularization for Distributional Reinforcement Learning
summary: Quantile-based distributional reinforcement learning methods learn return distributions through sampled quantile regression, but their bootstrapped target quantiles may induce distorted or degenerate distribution estimates. We propose Robust Quantile-based Implicit Quantile Networks (RQIQN), a lightweight Wasserstein distributionally robust enhancement boosted from a quantile estimation perspective. We first reinterpret a snapshot of IQN loss as a collection of local empirical quantile estimation problems over sampled current fractions. We then robustify each local slot with a Wasserstein distributionally robust quantile estimation formulation, yielding a closed-form, fraction-dependent correction to the Bellman target. This correction directly addresses distributional degeneration: its median antisymmetry preserves the risk-neutral quantile average, while its monotonicity enlarges upper-lower quantile gaps and counteracts collapsed distributional spread. RQIQN thus regularizes quantile geometry without changing the underlying value objective or requiring additional sample set reconstruction. Finally, we empirically show that the proposed RQIQN outperforms other existing quantile-based distributional reinforcement learning algorithms in risk-sensitive navigation and Atari games.

AI.tldr: RQIQN enhances quantile-based distributional RL with Wasserstein distributional robustness to prevent degenerate distribution estimates, maintaining risk-neutral performance while improving quantile geometry through closed-form target corrections.
AI.motivation: Existing quantile-based distributional RL methods suffer from distorted or degenerate distribution estimates due to bootstrapped target quantiles, which can collapse distributional spread.
AI.method: Reinterpret IQN loss as local empirical quantile estimation problems, then robustify each with Wasserstein distributionally robust quantile estimation, yielding a closed-form fraction-dependent correction to Bellman targets.
AI.result: RQIQN outperforms existing quantile-based distributional RL algorithms in risk-sensitive navigation and Atari games, regularizing quantile geometry without changing value objectives.
AI.conclusion: Wasserstein distributionally robust enhancement effectively addresses distribution degeneration in quantile-based RL, providing a lightweight solution that preserves risk-neutral performance while improving distributional spread.

title: DiagnosticIQ: A Benchmark for LLM-Based Industrial Maintenance Action Recommendation from Symbolic Rules
summary: Monitoring complex industrial assets relies on engineer-authored symbolic rules that trigger based on sensor conditions and prompt technicians to perform corrective actions. The bottleneck is not detection but response: translating rules into maintenance steps requires asset-specific knowledge gained through years of practice. We investigate whether LLMs can serve as decision support for this rule-to-action step and introduce \ours{}, a benchmark of 6{,}690 expert-validated multiple-choice questions from 118 rule-action pairs across 16 asset types. We contribute (i) a symbolic-to-MCQA pipeline normalizing rules to Disjunctive Normal Form with embedding-based distractor sampling, (ii) five variants probing distinct failure modes (Pro, Pert, Verbose, Aug, Rationale), and (iii) a benchmark of 29 LLMs and 4 embedding baselines. A human evaluation (9 practitioners, mean 45.0\%) confirms \ours{} requires specialist knowledge beyond operational experience. Three findings stand out. The frontier has closed: the top three LLMs lie within one Macro point, with Bradley-Terry Elo placing claude-opus-4-6 30 points above the next model. Yet \ours{}\,Pro exposes brittleness, with every model losing 13--60\% relative accuracy under distractor expansion. \ours{}\,Aug exposes pattern-matching: under condition inversion, frontier models still select the original answer 49--63\% of the time. The deployment bottleneck is not capability but calibration: frontier models handle template-style fault detection but break under structural perturbation.

AI.tldr: LLMs can match human performance in translating industrial maintenance rules to corrective actions, but show brittleness under distribution shifts and pattern-matching behaviors that limit practical deployment.
AI.motivation: Industrial asset monitoring uses engineer-authored symbolic rules for fault detection, but translating these rules into actionable maintenance steps requires specialized asset knowledge that takes years to acquire. The bottleneck is not fault detection but the rule-to-action response step.
AI.method: Created MEND benchmark with 6,690 expert-validated MCQs from 118 rule-action pairs across 16 asset types. Developed symbolic-to-MCQA pipeline using Disjunctive Normal Form normalization and embedding-based distractor sampling. Five benchmark variants probe distinct failure modes: Pro (professional), Pert (perturbed), Verbose, Aug (augmented), and Rationale.
AI.result: Human practitioners achieved 45.0% mean accuracy, confirming need for specialist knowledge. Top 3 LLMs are within 1 Macro point, with Claude Opus 4-6 leading by 30 Elo points. Models lose 13-60% accuracy under distractor expansion (Pro variant) and show pattern-matching behavior, selecting original answers 49-63% of the time even when conditions are inverted (Aug variant).
AI.conclusion: LLMs can handle template-style fault detection tasks but break under structural perturbations. The deployment bottleneck is not raw capability but model calibration - while frontier models match human performance on standard tasks, they lack robustness to distribution shifts and show brittle pattern-matching behaviors.

title: Synergistic Simplex: Cooperative Runtime Assurance for Safety-Critical Autonomous Systems
summary:
Autonomous systems increasingly rely on machine-learning (ML) components for safety-critical tasks such as perception and control in autonomous vehicles (AVs). While ML enables essential capabilities, it inevitably exhibits long-tail faults that make it unsuitable for safety-critical tasks. Runtime assurance (RTA) mitigates this issue by pairing ML components with verifiable safety monitors, e.g., Control Simplex and Perception Simplex architectures. However, the limited performance of safety monitors remains a major bottleneck.
  The Synergistic Simplex (SS) architecture improves system performance by enabling bidirectional integration between ML components and safety monitors while preserving formal safety guarantees. The key innovation here is allowing safety monitors to use ML outputs, which is typically prohibited in RTA systems. We formally derive conditions under which this integration preserves safety and demonstrate the performance benefits. We present the design, analysis, and evaluation of SS for AV obstacle detection.

AI.tldr: The paper introduces Synergistic Simplex (SS) architecture, which allows safety monitors to use ML outputs in runtime assurance systems, improving performance while maintaining safety guarantees for autonomous vehicles.
AI.motivation: Machine learning components in autonomous systems exhibit long-tail faults making them unsuitable for safety-critical tasks. Existing runtime assurance (RTA) systems like Control Simplex and Perception Simplex pair ML with safety monitors, but limited monitor performance remains a major bottleneck.
AI.method: The Synergistic Simplex (SS) architecture enables bidirectional integration between ML components and safety monitors, allowing safety monitors to use ML outputs (typically prohibited in RTA systems). The authors formally derive conditions under which this integration preserves safety.
AI.result: The SS architecture demonstrates performance benefits for AV obstacle detection while preserving formal safety guarantees. The paper presents design, analysis, and evaluation showing improved system performance.
AI.conclusion: The Synergistic Simplex architecture successfully addresses the performance bottleneck of traditional RTA systems by allowing safety monitors to leverage ML outputs, providing a solution that maintains safety while improving overall system performance for autonomous vehicle applications.

title: C2L-Net: A Data-Driven Model for State-of-Charge Estimation of Lithium-Ion Batteries During Discharge
summary: Accurate state-of-charge (SOC) estimation is critical for the safe and efficient operation of lithium-ion batteries in battery management systems (BMS). Although data-driven approaches can effectively capture nonlinear battery dynamics, many existing methods rely on long historical input sequences, resulting in high computational cost and introducing padding-induced positional bias at the beginning of drive cycles. To address these limitations, we propose C2L-Net, a novel context-to-latest data-driven framework for realistic online SOC estimation using only a short historical window (20 s). Unlike existing short-receptive-field or long-history models, the proposed framework explicitly separates contextual encoding from latest-measurement updating, enabling both efficient temporal modeling and rapid adaptation to dynamic battery states. The proposed model incorporates a chunk-based feature extraction mechanism that combines Theta Attention Pooling with a Fourier-based Seasonality Basis to capture local temporal patterns while reducing sequence length. A causal context encoder, integrating a gated recurrent unit (GRU) with Causal Cosine Attention, models temporal dependencies without information leakage. Furthermore, a latest-measurement decoder, inspired by recursive filtering, updates the contextual state using the most recent measurement, enhancing responsiveness to dynamic operating conditions. Extensive experiments on a public lithium-ion battery drive-cycle dataset under multiple fixed-temperature conditions demonstrate that the proposed method achieves state-of-the-art or competitive accuracy while significantly improving computational efficiency. In particular, C2L-Net achieves up to 60 times faster inference and requires fewer parameters than recent data-driven baselines, while maintaining robust performance across unseen driving profiles.

AI.tldr: C2L-Net: A novel context-to-latest framework for efficient online SOC estimation using only 20-second historical data, achieving state-of-the-art accuracy with 60x faster inference.
AI.motivation: Existing data-driven SOC estimation methods require long historical sequences, leading to high computational costs and padding-induced positional bias at the beginning of drive cycles. There's a need for more efficient online estimation that can rapidly adapt to dynamic battery states.
AI.method: Proposes C2L-Net framework that separates contextual encoding from latest-measurement updating. Uses: 1) chunk-based feature extraction with Theta Attention Pooling and Fourier-based Seasonality Basis; 2) causal context encoder with GRU and Causal Cosine Attention; 3) latest-measurement decoder inspired by recursive filtering to update contextual state using most recent measurement.
AI.result: Achieves state-of-the-art or competitive accuracy on public lithium-ion battery drive-cycle dataset under multiple fixed-temperature conditions. Delivers up to 60 times faster inference with fewer parameters than recent baselines while maintaining robust performance across unseen driving profiles.
AI.conclusion: C2L-Net provides an efficient and accurate solution for realistic online SOC estimation by using only short historical windows, addressing computational efficiency and positional bias issues while enabling rapid adaptation to dynamic battery states.

title: ExecuTorch -- A Unified PyTorch Solution to Run AI Models On-Device
summary: Local execution of AI on edge devices is important for low latency and offline operation. However, deploying models on diverse hardware remains fragmented, often requiring model conversion or complete reimplementation outside the PyTorch ecosystem where the model was originally authored. We introduce ExecuTorch, a unified PyTorch-native deployment framework for edge AI. ExecuTorch enables seamless deployment of machine learning models across heterogeneous compute environments. It scales from embedded microcontrollers to complex system-on-chips (SoCs) with dedicated accelerators, powering devices ranging from wearables and smartphones to large compute clusters. ExecuTorch preserves PyTorch semantics while allowing customization, support for optimizations like quantization, and pluggable execution "backends". These features together enable fast experimentation, allowing researchers to validate deployment behavior entirely within PyTorch, bridging the gap between research and production.

AI.tldr: ExecuTorch is a PyTorch-native deployment framework that enables seamless deployment of AI models from embedded microcontrollers to complex SoCs, bridging the gap between research and production while preserving PyTorch semantics.
AI.motivation: Current edge AI deployment is fragmented, requiring model conversion or reimplementation outside PyTorch, which creates barriers between research development and production deployment on diverse hardware platforms.
AI.method: Developed ExecuTorch as a unified PyTorch-native deployment framework that supports heterogeneous compute environments, preserves PyTorch semantics, allows customization, supports optimizations like quantization, and provides pluggable execution backends.
AI.result: Enables seamless deployment of ML models across hardware from embedded microcontrollers to complex SoCs with accelerators, supporting devices ranging from wearables to large compute clusters while maintaining PyTorch semantics.
AI.conclusion: ExecuTorch bridges the gap between research and production by allowing fast experimentation and validation of deployment behavior entirely within PyTorch, enabling researchers to work seamlessly across the development-deployment lifecycle.

title: ReplaySCM: A Benchmark for Executable Causal Mechanism Induction from Interventions
summary: Most causal benchmarks for language models score local answers or graph structure. We introduce ReplaySCM, a 1,300 item benchmark for executable causal mechanism induction from finite interventional evidence. Each item contains binary worlds generated by a latent fully observed acyclic Boolean structural causal model (SCM). A system must output a mechanism map in a restricted Boolean DSL; the submission is parsed, checked for legality and acyclicity, and replayed on training and held-out intervention worlds. Scoring uses replay behavior rather than formula strings, so syntactically different mechanisms receive credit when they behave correctly. ReplaySCM varies the structural information disclosed to the model through Ordered, Block-order, Hidden-order, and Hidden-roots settings, and includes Alternative-SCM tasks that supply a valid reference SCM and ask for a semantically distinct alternative that fits the training worlds, together with a separating intervention and witness. Frontier LLMs infer parts of the functional-parent structure, but held-out replay drops sharply when order or root structure is hidden. We also evaluate a matched support-audit ladder: Original, Extra Worlds, and Counterexample Audit (CEx), that raises mean local predecessor-pattern coverage from 0.8949 to 0.9815 to 1.0; under the audited searches, no discovered semantic alternative remains consistent with the training worlds. The Ordered/Hidden-order gap persists under this stronger evidence. ReplaySCM complements answer-level causal reasoning and graph-discovery benchmarks by evaluating executable replay generalization from finite interventional evidence, without claiming unique identification of the latent SCM.

AI.tldr: ReplaySCM is a 1,300-item benchmark for evaluating causal mechanism induction from finite interventional evidence, using executable Boolean structural causal models and scoring based on replay behavior rather than formula strings.
AI.motivation: Most existing causal benchmarks focus on scoring local answers or graph structure, but there's a need for evaluating executable causal mechanism induction from finite interventional evidence. Current approaches don't adequately assess how well models can infer functional mechanisms that can be replayed on new interventions.
AI.method: The benchmark generates binary worlds from latent acyclic Boolean structural causal models (SCMs). Systems must output mechanism maps in a restricted Boolean DSL, which are then parsed, checked for legality/acyclicity, and replayed on training and held-out intervention worlds. It uses four settings with varying structural information disclosure (Ordered, Block-order, Hidden-order, Hidden-roots) and includes Alternative-SCM tasks. The method also implements a matched support-audit ladder with Original, Extra Worlds, and Counterexample Audit levels.
AI.result: Frontier LLMs can infer parts of functional-parent structure, but held-out replay performance drops sharply when order or root structure is hidden. The audit ladder significantly improves local predecessor-pattern coverage (from 0.8949 to 0.9815 to 1.0). Under audited searches, no discovered semantic alternatives remain consistent with training worlds. The Ordered/Hidden-order performance gap persists even with stronger evidence.
AI.conclusion: ReplaySCM provides a valuable complement to existing causal reasoning benchmarks by focusing on executable replay generalization from finite interventional evidence, without requiring unique identification of latent SCMs. The benchmark reveals limitations in current LLMs' ability to infer causal mechanisms when structural information is partially hidden.

title: Iterative Critique-and-Routing Controller for Multi-Agent Systems with Heterogeneous LLMs
summary: Multi-agent large language model (LLM) systems often rely on a controller to coordinate a pool of heterogeneous models, yet existing controllers are typically limited to one-shot routing: they select a model once and return its output directly. Such routing-only designs provide no mechanism to critique intermediate drafts or support iterative refinement. To address this limitation, we propose a critique-and-routing controller that casts multi-agent coordination as a sequential decision problem. At each turn, the controller evaluates the current draft, decides whether to stop or continue, and, if needed, selects the next agent for further refinement. We formulate this process as a finite-horizon Markov Decision Process (MDP) with explicit agent-utilization constraints, design a composite reward for controller decisions across turns, and optimize the controller via policy gradients under a Lagrangian-relaxed objective. Extensive experiments across multiple heterogeneous multi-agent systems and seven reasoning benchmarks show that our method consistently outperforms state-of-the-art baselines and substantially narrows the gap to the strongest agent, while using it for fewer than 25% of total calls.

AI.tldr: A critique-and-routing controller for multi-agent LLM systems that enables iterative refinement through sequential decision making, outperforming one-shot routing approaches while reducing usage of the strongest agent.
AI.motivation: Existing multi-agent LLM controllers only perform one-shot routing—they select a model once and return its output directly, lacking mechanisms for iterative refinement or critique of intermediate drafts.
AI.method: Propose a critique-and-routing controller that frames multi-agent coordination as a sequential decision problem using a finite-horizon Markov Decision Process (MDP). The controller evaluates drafts, decides to stop or continue, and selects next agents with agent-utilization constraints. Uses composite rewards and optimizes via policy gradients with Lagrangian-relaxed objective.
AI.result: Consistently outperforms state-of-the-art baselines across multiple heterogeneous multi-agent systems and seven reasoning benchmarks. Substantially narrows the gap to the strongest agent while using it for fewer than 25% of total calls.
AI.conclusion: Critique-and-routing controllers that support iterative refinement through sequential decision making are more effective than one-shot routing approaches for multi-agent LLM coordination, enabling better performance with more efficient utilization of computational resources.

title: FairHealth: An Open-Source Python Library for Trustworthy Healthcare AI in Low-Resource Settings
summary: We present FairHealth, an open-source Python library that provides a unified, modular framework for trustworthy machine learning in healthcare applications, with particular focus on low-resource and low-income country (LMIC) settings such as Bangladesh. FairHealth addresses four critical gaps in existing healthcare AI toolkits: (1) the absence of integrated fairness auditing for biosignals and clinical tabular data; (2) the lack of privacy-preserving federated learning tools compatible with standard ML workflows; (3) missing explainability tools tailored for low-bandwidth clinical decision support; and (4) no existing toolkit covering Global South healthcare datasets. Built from five peer-reviewed research contributions, FairHealth provides six modules covering federated learning with homomorphic encryption (fairhealth.federated), intersectional fairness metrics (fairhealth.fairness), hybrid fuzzy-SHAP explainability (fairhealth.explain), multilingual dengue triage (fairhealth.lowresource), equitable disaster aid allocation (fairhealth.equity), and public dataset loaders (fairhealth.datasets). All datasets used are publicly available without institutional data use agreements. FairHealth is installable via pip install fairhealth(PyPI: pypi.org/project/fairhealth/) and available at https://github.com/Farjana-Yesmin/fairhealth.

AI.tldr: FairHealth is an open-source Python library for trustworthy ML in healthcare, specifically designed for low-resource settings, addressing gaps in fairness auditing, federated learning, explainability, and Global South healthcare datasets.
AI.motivation: The paper aims to address critical gaps in existing healthcare AI toolkits, particularly for low-resource and low-income country settings. Existing tools lack integrated fairness auditing for healthcare data, privacy-preserving federated learning compatible with standard workflows, explainability tools for low-bandwidth clinical settings, and coverage of Global South healthcare datasets.
AI.method: The authors developed an open-source Python library built from five peer-reviewed research contributions. It provides six modules: federated learning with homomorphic encryption, intersectional fairness metrics, hybrid fuzzy-SHAP explainability, multilingual dengue triage, equitable disaster aid allocation, and public dataset loaders. All datasets are publicly available without institutional data use agreements.
AI.result: The paper presents FairHealth as a complete, installable Python library (pip install fairhealth) that provides a unified framework for trustworthy ML in healthcare. It specifically addresses the needs of low-resource settings with modules covering fairness, privacy, explainability, and practical healthcare applications for the Global South.
AI.conclusion: FairHealth fills important gaps in healthcare AI toolkits by providing an open-source, modular framework that addresses fairness, privacy, and explainability concerns specifically for low-resource healthcare settings, making trustworthy ML more accessible for Global South applications.

title: Reconciling Consistency-Based Diagnosis with Actual-Causality-Based Explanations
summary: We establish, from the point of view of Explainable AI (XAI), connections between Consistency-Based Diagnosis (CBD), on one side, and Actual Causality and Causal Responsibility, on the other. CBD has received little attention from the XAI community. Connections between these two areas could have a fruitful impact on XAI and Explainable Data Management.

AI.tldr: The paper establishes connections between Consistency-Based Diagnosis (CBD) and concepts from Actual Causality and Causal Responsibility, highlighting their potential impact on Explainable AI and Explainable Data Management.
AI.motivation: CBD has received little attention from the XAI community, and the authors want to bridge this gap by exploring connections between CBD and causal reasoning concepts to potentially enrich both XAI and Explainable Data Management.
AI.method: The paper establishes connections from the perspective of Explainable AI between Consistency-Based Diagnosis on one side, and Actual Causality and Causal Responsibility on the other side.
AI.result: The authors establish connections between these two areas, suggesting these connections could have a fruitful impact on both XAI and Explainable Data Management fields.
AI.conclusion: By connecting CBD with causal reasoning concepts like Actual Causality and Causal Responsibility, the paper opens up new research directions that could significantly benefit XAI and Explainable Data Management communities.

title: Weakly Supervised Concept Learning for Object-centric Visual Reasoning
summary: Neurosymbolic systems promise to combine deep neural network's (DNN) processing of raw sensor inputs with few-shot performance of symbolic artificial intelligence. Two-stage approaches explicitly decouple DNN based perception from subsequent rule based reasoning. This avoids optimization and interpretability issues of end to end differentiable approaches, but requires costly labels for the perception output. This paper introduces an efficient weak supervision scheme for the perception stage to ground its output symbols for logical induction in object-centric reasoning tasks. It combines a slot-based architecture for object-centricity with a Variational Autoencoder (VAE) for self-supervision, competing with concept guidance on latent dimensions for human interpretable grounding. The resulting predictions are translated into symbolic background knowledge for reasoning frameworks, such as Inductive Logic Programming (ILP), Decision Trees, and Bayesian Networks. Our extensive empirical evaluation on synthetic and real world datasets shows that our approach can discover complex, abstract rules for object centric reasoning whilst reducing supervision to as little as 1% of labels, and being robust even under substantial domain shift. Notably, at 1% supervision it even outperforms state of the art foundation model baselines in domain generalization

AI.tldr: A neurosymbolic approach that weakly supervises perception using object-centric slot-based VAE architecture, enabling logical induction with minimal (as little as 1%) labeled data while maintaining domain robustness.
AI.motivation: Neurosymbolic systems need to combine DNN perception with symbolic reasoning, but two-stage approaches require costly labels for perception output. The goal is to develop efficient weak supervision for perception grounding while maintaining interpretability and reducing labeling burden.
AI.method: Uses slot-based architecture for object-centric perception combined with a Variational Autoencoder for self-supervision. Applies concept guidance on latent dimensions for human interpretable grounding. Converts predictions into symbolic background knowledge for reasoning frameworks including Inductive Logic Programming, Decision Trees, and Bayesian Networks.
AI.result: Can discover complex, abstract rules for object-centric reasoning with only 1% supervision labels. Outperforms state-of-the-art foundation model baselines in domain generalization at 1% supervision. Shows robustness under substantial domain shift on synthetic and real-world datasets.
AI.conclusion: This weakly supervised neurosymbolic approach successfully combines efficient perception grounding with symbolic reasoning, dramatically reducing labeling requirements while maintaining or exceeding performance compared to current methods, offering a practical solution for real-world neurosymbolic applications.

title: SkillMaster: Toward Autonomous Skill Mastery in LLM Agents
summary: Skills provide an effective mechanism for improving LLM agents on complex tasks, yet in existing agent frameworks, their creation, refinement, and selection are typically governed by external teachers, hand-designed rules, or auxiliary modules. As a result, skills remain external resources to be invoked, rather than capabilities that agents can develop, adapt, and internalize through experience. To endow LLM agents with autonomous skill mastery, we propose SkillMaster, a training framework that teaches agents to create new skills, refine existing skills, and select accumulated skills during task solving. This capability is achieved through three key designs. First, we train agents through trajectory-informed skill review, teaching agents to propose, update, or retain skills based on evidence from completed episodes. Second, each candidate skill edit is designed to be evaluated by its counterfactual utility on related probe tasks, providing a direct learning signal for training skill-editing decisions. Third, we introduce DualAdv-GRPO, which separately estimates advantages for task-solving actions and skill-editing decisions, stabilizing joint training across task solving and skill management. Experiments on ALFWorld and WebShop show that SkillMaster improves the overall success rate over state-of-the-art baselines by 8.8% and 9.3%, respectively, achieving the best performance among all compared methods. Further analysis reveals a marked shift in agent capability: agents trained with SkillMaster can identify skill failures, refine procedural knowledge from trajectory evidence, and transfer improvements to future tasks with limited skill-bank edits. Overall, SkillMaster moves LLM agents beyond mere skill use toward self-improving agents capable of developing, adapting, and applying their own skill repertoires.

AI.tldr: SkillMaster is a framework for training LLM agents to autonomously create, refine, and select skills through experience, moving beyond external skill management toward self-improving agents.
AI.motivation: Current LLM agent frameworks treat skills as external resources managed by teachers, rules, or auxiliary modules, preventing agents from developing, adapting, and internalizing skills through experience. Agents cannot autonomously master skills.
AI.method: Three key designs: 1) Trajectory-informed skill review where agents propose/update/retain skills based on completed episodes, 2) Counterfactual utility evaluation on related probe tasks for skill edits, 3) DualAdv-GRPO that separately estimates advantages for task-solving actions and skill-editing decisions to stabilize joint training.
AI.result: Improved overall success rates over SOTA baselines by 8.8% on ALFWorld and 9.3% on WebShop. Agents show ability to identify skill failures, refine procedural knowledge from trajectories, and transfer improvements to future tasks with limited edits.
AI.conclusion: SkillMaster enables LLM agents to move beyond mere skill use toward self-improving agents capable of autonomously developing, adapting, and applying their own skill repertoires, representing a shift in agent capability.

title: Beyond Penalization: Diffusion-based Out-of-Distribution Detection and Selective Regularization in Offline Reinforcement Learning
summary: Offline reinforcement learning (RL) faces a critical challenge of overestimating the value of out-of-distribution (OOD) actions. Existing methods mitigate this issue by penalizing unseen samples, yet they fail to accurately identify OOD actions and may suppress beneficial exploration beyond the behavioral support. Although several methods have been proposed to differentiate OOD samples with distinct properties, they typically rely on restrictive assumptions about the data distribution and remain limited in discrimination ability. To address this problem, we propose DOSER (Diffusion-based OOD Detection and Selective Regularization), a novel framework that goes beyond uniform penalization. DOSER trains two diffusion models to capture the behavior policy and state distribution, using single-step denoising reconstruction error as a reliable OOD indicator. During policy optimization, it further distinguishes between beneficial and detrimental OOD actions by evaluating predicted transitions, selectively suppressing risky actions while encouraging exploration of high-potential ones. Theoretically, we prove that DOSER is a $γ$-contraction and therefore admits a unique fixed point with bounded value estimates. We further provide an asymptotic performance guarantee relative to the optimal policy under model approximation and OOD detection errors.
