Across extensive offline RL benchmarks, DOSER consistently attains superior performance to prior methods, especially on suboptimal datasets.

AI.tldr: DOSER: A diffusion-based offline RL framework that selectively regularizes OOD actions using denoising reconstruction error to distinguish beneficial vs detrimental exploration beyond behavioral support.
AI.motivation: Offline RL suffers from overestimating out-of-distribution (OOD) actions. Current methods either uniformly penalize unseen samples or make restrictive assumptions about data distribution, failing to accurately identify OOD actions and potentially suppressing beneficial exploration.
AI.method: Proposes DOSER framework with two diffusion models capturing behavior policy and state distribution. Uses single-step denoising reconstruction error as OOD indicator. During policy optimization, distinguishes between beneficial and detrimental OOD actions by evaluating predicted transitions, selectively suppressing risky actions while encouraging high-potential exploration.
AI.result: Theoretically proves DOSER is a γ-contraction with unique fixed point and bounded value estimates. Provides asymptotic performance guarantee relative to optimal policy under approximation errors. Empirically achieves superior performance across offline RL benchmarks, especially on suboptimal datasets.
AI.conclusion: DOSER effectively addresses OOD overestimation in offline RL through selective regularization based on diffusion model reconstruction error, enabling better discrimination between beneficial and detrimental out-of-distribution actions while maintaining theoretical guarantees.

title: MBP-KT: Learning Global Collaborative Information from Meta-Behavioral Pattern for Enhanced Knowledge Tracing
summary: The emerging collaborative information-based knowledge tracing (KT) has been a promising way to enhance modeling of learners' knowledge states. The core idea is to extract the collaborative information from interaction sequences of other learners to assist the prediction on the target one. Despite effectiveness, existing methods are built on the raw interaction sequences with tailored modules, which inevitably limits their capacity in deeply capturing learning behavioral patterns and generalization. To this end, we propose a general meta-behavioral pattern-aware framework (MBP-KT) for KT. Specifically, MBP-KT introduces a novel meta-behavioral sequence construction to transform the raw interaction sequences into the combinations of different meta-behavioral patterns. In this way, the learning behavioral patterns of learners can be effectively preserved. Then, MBP-KT develops a parameter-free module to extract the global collaborative representations from the constructed meta-behavioral sequences. Moreover, MBP-KT provides general injection strategies to introduce the extracted global collaborative information into various downstream KT models, ensuring the universality of the collaborative information. Extensive results on real-world datasets demonstrate that MBP-KT can consistently boosts the performance of a wide range of KT models.

AI.tldr: MBP-KT is a meta-behavioral pattern-aware framework that transforms raw interaction sequences into meta-behavioral patterns to better capture learning behaviors and improve knowledge tracing models through collaborative information.
AI.motivation: Existing collaborative KT methods use raw interaction sequences with specialized modules, which limits their ability to deeply capture learning behavioral patterns and generalization capabilities.
AI.method: MBP-KT transforms raw interaction sequences into combinations of meta-behavioral patterns, extracts global collaborative representations using a parameter-free module, and provides general injection strategies to integrate collaborative information into various downstream KT models.
AI.result: Extensive experiments on real-world datasets demonstrate that MBP-KT consistently boosts the performance of a wide range of KT models.
AI.conclusion: MBP-KT offers a general framework that effectively preserves learning behavioral patterns and enhances various KT models through collaborative information, providing universality and improved performance.

title: Learngene Search Across Multiple Datasets for Building Variable-Sized Models
summary: Deep learning methods are widely used under diverse resource constraints, resulting in models of varying sizes, such as the Vision Transformer (ViT) series. Deploying these models typically requires costly pretraining and finetuning. The Learngene paradigm addresses this issue by extracting transferable components, called learngenes, from a pretrained ancestry model (Ans-Net) to initialize variable-sized descendant models (Des-Nets).Existing learngene extraction methods rely on a single dataset, limiting downstream performance. To address this limitation, we propose Learngene Search Across Multiple Datasets for Building Variable-Sized Models (LSAMD). LSAMD expands the Ans-Net into a searchable super Ans-Net with dataset-specific blocks and dataset adapters (DADs). During training, LSAMD searches for an optimal architecture path for each dataset. The base blocks most frequently selected across datasets are extracted as learngenes for initializing Des-Nets.Experiments on multiple datasets show that LSAMD achieves performance comparable to pretrain-finetune methods while significantly reducing storage and training costs.

AI.tldr: Learngene Search Across Multiple Datasets (LSAMD) enables efficient transfer learning by extracting common architectural components from a super-network to initialize variable-sized descendant models, reducing pretraining/finetuning costs.
AI.motivation: Existing learngene extraction methods rely on single datasets, limiting downstream performance. There's a need for a method that leverages multiple datasets to extract more transferable components while maintaining efficiency.
AI.method: LSAMD expands a pretrained ancestry model into a searchable super-network with dataset-specific blocks and adapters. It searches for optimal architecture paths for each dataset, then extracts the most frequently selected base blocks across datasets as learngenes to initialize descendant models of varying sizes.
AI.result: Experiments on multiple datasets show LSAMD achieves performance comparable to pretrain-finetune methods while significantly reducing storage and training costs.
AI.conclusion: LSAMD provides an effective approach for building variable-sized models efficiently by leveraging multiple datasets to extract transferable learngenes, offering a practical alternative to costly pretraining and finetuning.

title: RewardHarness: Self-Evolving Agentic Post-Training
summary: Evaluating instruction-guided image edits requires rewards that reflect subtle human preferences, yet current reward models typically depend on large-scale preference annotation and additional model training. This creates a data-efficiency gap: humans can often infer the target evaluation criteria from only a few examples, while models are usually trained on hundreds of thousands of comparisons. We present RewardHarness, a self-evolving agentic reward framework that reframes reward modeling as context evolution rather than weight optimization. Instead of learning from large-scale annotations, RewardHarness aligns with human preferences by iteratively evolving a library of tools and skills from as few as 100 preference demonstrations. Given a source image, candidate edited images, and an editing instruction, an Orchestrator selects the most relevant subset of tools and skills from the maintained library, and a frozen Sub-Agent uses them to construct a reasoning chain that produces a preference judgment. By comparing predicted judgments with ground-truth preferences and analyzing successes and failures in the reasoning process, the Orchestrator automatically refines its library of tools and skills without additional human annotation. Using only 0.05% of the EditReward preference data, RewardHarness achieves 47.4% average accuracy on image-editing evaluation benchmarks, surpassing GPT-5 by 5.3 points. When used as a reward signal for GRPO fine-tuning, RL-tuned models achieve 3.52 on ImgEdit-Bench. Project page: https://rewardharness.com.

AI.tldr: RewardHarness: A self-evolving agentic reward framework that learns from minimal human preference data (just 100 examples) to evaluate instruction-guided image edits, outperforming models trained on much larger datasets.
AI.motivation: Current reward models for evaluating image edits require extensive preference annotations and model training, creating a data-efficiency gap. Humans can infer evaluation criteria from few examples, while models need massive datasets.
AI.method: RewardHarness reframes reward modeling as context evolution rather than weight optimization. An Orchestrator selects relevant tools/skills from an evolving library, and a frozen Sub-Agent constructs reasoning chains for preference judgments. The system automatically refines its library by analyzing successes/failures without additional human annotation.
AI.result: Using only 0.05% of EditReward preference data, RewardHarness achieves 47.4% average accuracy on image-editing benchmarks, surpassing GPT-5 by 5.3 points. When used for GRPO fine-tuning, RL-tuned models achieve 3.52 on ImgEdit-Bench.
AI.conclusion: RewardHarness demonstrates that self-evolving agentic frameworks can achieve effective preference alignment with minimal data, addressing the data-efficiency gap in reward modeling for image editing evaluation.

title: LLMs with in-context learning for Algorithmic Theoretical Physics
summary: There is an increasing number of algorithmic computations in theoretical physics. These, while conceptually simple, can nevertheless be time-consuming and contain subtleties that should not be overlooked. Given the recent improvement of Large Language Models (LLM), it is natural to investigate whether LLMs equipped with a computer algebra system (CAS) runtime and sufficiently informative context can reliably carry out these algorithmic tasks. In this work, we interface Claude with Maple, and apply this framework to cosmological perturbations in modified theories of gravity. We demonstrate the current capabilities of this approach, the typical failures, and how the same can be improved. We find that a frontier LLM supplied with worked examples is able to solve most test problems.

AI.tldr: LLMs combined with computer algebra systems can reliably perform algorithmic physics computations when provided with worked examples, though improvements are still needed.
AI.motivation: Increasing algorithmic computations in theoretical physics are time-consuming and contain subtle complexities. Recent LLM advancements suggest they could assist if equipped with computer algebra systems and proper context.
AI.method: Interface Claude LLM with Maple computer algebra system, apply to cosmological perturbations in modified gravity theories, and provide worked examples as context for the LLM.
AI.result: Frontier LLMs with worked examples can solve most test problems in cosmological perturbations, though typical failures still occur and improvements are identified.
AI.conclusion: LLMs equipped with CAS and sufficient contextual information show promise for automating algorithmic physics tasks, but current limitations require further refinement of the approach.

title: AgentPSO: Evolving Agent Reasoning Skill via Multi-agent Particle Swarm Optimization
summary: Multi-agent reasoning has shown promise for improving the problem-solving ability of large language models by allowing multiple agents to explore diverse reasoning paths. However, most existing multi-agent methods rely on inference-time debate or aggregation, which can be vulnerable to incorrect peer influence and biased consensus. Moreover, the agents themselves remain static, as their underlying reasoning skills do not evolve across tasks. In this paper, we introduce AgentPSO, a particle-swarm-inspired framework for evolving multi-agent reasoning skills. AgentPSO treats each agent as a particle-like reasoner whose state is a natural-language skill and whose velocity is a semantic update direction, iteratively moving agents toward stronger skill states to improve both individual and collective reasoning performance. Across training iterations, each agent updates its skill by combining its previous velocity, personal-best skill, global-best skill, and a self-reflective direction derived from peer reasoning trajectories. This enables agents to learn reusable reasoning behaviors from both their own experiences and the strongest skills discovered by the population, without updating the parameters of the backbone language model. Experiments on mathematical and general reasoning benchmarks show that AgentPSO improves over static single-agent skills and test-time-only multi-agent reasoning baselines. The evolved skills further transfer across benchmarks and to another backbone model, suggesting that AgentPSO captures reusable reasoning procedures rather than merely optimizing benchmark-specific prompts. Code is open-sourced at https://github.com/HYUNMIN-HWANG/AgentPSO/.

AI.tldr: AgentPSO: A particle-swarm-inspired framework that evolves multi-agent reasoning skills through iterative semantic updates, enabling agents to learn reusable reasoning behaviors without modifying the underlying language model parameters.
AI.motivation: Existing multi-agent reasoning methods rely on inference-time debate or aggregation, which can be vulnerable to incorrect peer influence and biased consensus. Additionally, agents remain static with fixed reasoning skills that don't evolve across tasks, limiting their problem-solving capabilities.
AI.method: AgentPSO treats each agent as a particle-like reasoner where the state is a natural-language skill and velocity is a semantic update direction. Agents iteratively update their skills by combining: previous velocity, personal-best skill, global-best skill from the population, and self-reflective direction from peer reasoning trajectories. This enables skill evolution without updating backbone language model parameters.
AI.result: Experiments on mathematical and general reasoning benchmarks show AgentPSO improves over static single-agent skills and test-time-only multi-agent reasoning baselines. The evolved skills transfer across benchmarks and to another backbone model, demonstrating they capture reusable reasoning procedures rather than benchmark-specific prompt optimization.
AI.conclusion: AgentPSO successfully evolves multi-agent reasoning skills through particle-swarm-inspired optimization, enabling both individual and collective performance improvement. The framework captures reusable reasoning behaviors that transfer across tasks and models, offering a promising approach for enhancing language model reasoning capabilities without parameter updates.

title: Retrieval Mechanisms Surpass Long-Context Scaling in Time Series Forecasting
summary: Time Series Foundation Models (TSFMs) have borrowed the long context paradigm from natural language processing under the premise that feeding more history into the model improves forecast quality. But in stochastic domains, distant history is often just high-frequency noise, not signal. Hence, the proposed work tests whether this premise actually holds by running continuous context architectures (PatchTST included) through the ETTh1 benchmark. The obtained results contradict the premise: an inverse scaling law shows up clearly, with forecasting error rising as context gets longer. A 3,000-step window causes performance to drop by over 68%, evidence that attention mechanisms are poor at ignoring irrelevant historical volatility. Retrieval-Augmented Forecasting (RAFT) is evaluated as an alternative. RAFT achieves a mean squared error (MSE) of 0.379 with a fixed 720-step window and selective retrieval, outperforming both long-context configurations and zero-shot foundation models (Chronos, Moirai) despite requiring far less computation. In addition, the retrieval step injects only the most relevant historical segments as dynamic exogenous variables, which gives the model a context-informed inductive bias it cannot build on its own from raw sequences. Therefore, foundation models going forward need to shift architecturally toward selective retrieval.

AI.tldr: Longer context windows in time series foundation models actually hurt performance due to attention mechanisms' inability to ignore irrelevant historical noise, while selective retrieval-based approaches like RAFT outperform both long-context models and zero-shot foundation models with less computation.
AI.motivation: Time Series Foundation Models have adopted the long context paradigm from NLP under the assumption that more historical data improves forecasts, but this premise may not hold in stochastic domains where distant history is often high-frequency noise rather than signal.
AI.method: Tested continuous context architectures (including PatchTST) on the ETTh1 benchmark to examine whether longer context actually improves performance, and evaluated Retrieval-Augmented Forecasting (RAFT) as an alternative approach that uses selective retrieval of relevant historical segments.
AI.result: Found an inverse scaling law where forecasting error increases as context gets longer, with a 3,000-step window causing over 68% performance drop. RAFT achieved MSE of 0.379 with a fixed 720-step window and selective retrieval, outperforming both long-context configurations and zero-shot foundation models (Chronos, Moirai) with far less computation.
AI.conclusion: Foundation models need to shift architecturally toward selective retrieval approaches, as attention mechanisms are poor at ignoring irrelevant historical volatility, and retrieval-based methods can provide context-informed inductive bias that models cannot build from raw sequences alone.

title: When Can Human-AI Teams Outperform Individuals? Tight Bounds with Impossibility Guarantees
comment: 8 pages, 2 figures, 7 tables. Accepted at CogSci 2026
summary: Human-AI teams fail to outperform their best member in 70% of studies, yet no theory specifies when complementarity is achievable. We derive tight bounds for the broad class of confidence-based aggregation rules by integrating signal detection theory with information-theoretic analysis, yielding four results: (1) a complementarity theorem (teams outperform individuals iff error correlation $ρ_{HM} < ρ^*$, with $ρ^* \approx a$ in the symmetric near-chance regime); (2) minimax bounds showing gains scale as $Θ(\sqrt{Δd})$ with metacognitive sensitivity difference; (3) an impossibility result proving no confidence-based aggregation rule achieves complementarity when $ρ_{HM} \geq ρ^*$; and (4) multi-class generalization $ρ^*_K \approx ρ^*/\sqrt{K-1}$. Predictions match observed team accuracy ($R = 0.94$ on ImageNet-16H, $R = 0.91$ on CIFAR-10H) and the multi-class threshold scaling holds on human data ($R = 0.93$, $K = 16$), with robustness under non-Gaussian distributions. The framework explains why complementarity is rare and provides actionable design formulas; results apply to aggregation, not to interactive deliberation that generates novel answers.

AI.tldr: Human-AI teams rarely beat their best member, but this paper provides a theoretical framework showing when complementarity is possible through confidence-based aggregation rules, with precise mathematical bounds and empirical validation.
AI.motivation: Human-AI teams underperform their best member in 70% of studies, yet there's no theoretical framework explaining when complementarity (team outperforming individuals) is achievable. The lack of predictive theory limits effective team design.
AI.method: Integrates signal detection theory with information-theoretic analysis to derive tight bounds for confidence-based aggregation rules. Uses mathematical derivation to establish theoretical bounds and validates predictions with empirical data from ImageNet-16H and CIFAR-10H datasets.
AI.result: Four key results: (1) Complementarity theorem with threshold correlation ρ*; (2) Minimax bounds showing gains scale with metacognitive sensitivity; (3) Impossibility result when ρ_HM ≥ ρ*; (4) Multi-class generalization. Predictions match empirical data with high correlation (R = 0.94, 0.91) and multi-class scaling holds (R = 0.93).
AI.conclusion: The framework explains why complementarity is rare, provides actionable design formulas, and establishes precise conditions for when human-AI teams can outperform individuals. Results apply to aggregation (not interactive deliberation) and show robustness under non-Gaussian distributions.

title: Deep Dreams Are Made of This: Visualizing Monosemantic Features in Diffusion Models
summary: This paper proposes latent visualization by optimization (LVO), a mechanistic interpretability technique that extends feature visualization by optimization - originally developed for convolutional neural networks - to latent diffusion models. LVO employs sparse autoencoders (SAEs) to disentangle polysemantic layer representations into monosemantic features. Key contributions include latent-space optimization, time-step activity analysis, schedule-matched noise injection, prior initialization through feature steering, and suitable regularization strategies. We demonstrate the method on Stable Diffusion 1.5 fine-tuned on the Style50 dataset, showing that SAE features produce clear visualizations of recognizable concepts - including diagonal compositions, human figures, roses, cables, and waterfall foam - that correlate with dataset examples, while the baseline without disentanglement produces less coherent results. We further show that regularization techniques from pixel-space feature visualization transfer to the latent domain, though they require different configurations for the raw-layer and SAE variants. Compared to dataset examples and steering, LVO provides complementary insights by directly revealing what activates a feature rather than its downstream effects.

AI.tldr: LVO extends feature visualization to latent diffusion models using sparse autoencoders for disentangled feature visualization, revealing what directly activates features rather than just showing downstream effects.
AI.motivation: To apply mechanistic interpretability techniques like feature visualization to latent diffusion models, which previously lacked methods for direct feature visualization in latent space, and to overcome the challenge of polysemantic (mixed-concept) representations in standard layer activations.
AI.method: Latent-space optimization with sparse autoencoders to disentangle polysemantic features into monosemantic ones, plus techniques including time-step activity analysis, schedule-matched noise injection, prior initialization via feature steering, and specialized regularization strategies.
AI.result: SAE features produce clear visualizations of recognizable concepts (diagonal compositions, human figures, roses, cables, waterfall foam) that correlate with dataset examples, while baseline without disentanglement gives less coherent results; regularization techniques from pixel-space visualization transfer to latent domain with different configurations.
AI.conclusion: LVO provides complementary insights to dataset examples and steering by directly revealing what activates a feature rather than just showing downstream effects, establishing a framework for mechanistic interpretability in latent diffusion models.

title: Bias by Necessity: Impossibility Theorems for Sequential Processing with Convergent AI and Human Validation
comment: 6 pages, 3 figures, 5 tables. Accepted to CogSci 2026
summary: Are certain cognitive biases mathematically inevitable consequences of sequential information processing? We prove that primacy effects, anchoring, and order-dependence are architecturally necessary in autoregressive language models due to causal masking constraints. Our three impossibility theorems establish: (1) primacy bias arises from asymmetric attention accumulation; (2) anchoring emerges from sequential conditioning with provable information bounds; and (3) exact debiasing by permutation marginalization requires factorial-time computation, with Monte Carlo approximation feasible at constant per-tolerance overhead. We validate these bounds across 12 frontier LLMs ($R^2 = 0.89$; $Δ$BIC $= 16.6$ vs. next-best alternative). We then derive quantitative predictions from the framework and test them in two pre-registered human experiments ($N = 464$ analyzed). Study 1 confirms anchor position modulates anchoring magnitude ($d = 0.52$, BF$_{10} = 847$). Study 2 shows working memory load amplifies primacy bias ($d = 0.41$, BF$_{10} = 156$), with WM capacity predicting bias reduction ($r = -.38$). These convergent findings reframe cognitive biases as resource-rational responses to sequential processing.

AI.tldr: The paper proves that cognitive biases like primacy effects and anchoring are mathematically inevitable in autoregressive language models due to causal masking constraints, and validates these predictions in both AI models and human experiments.
AI.motivation: To determine whether cognitive biases are mathematically inevitable consequences of sequential information processing, specifically examining if these biases emerge necessarily from the architectural constraints of autoregressive language models.
AI.method: Theoretical proof of three impossibility theorems, validation across 12 frontier LLMs, and two pre-registered human experiments with N=464 participants analyzing anchor position effects and working memory load impacts.
AI.result: Primacy effects, anchoring, and order-dependence are architecturally necessary in autoregressive models. Validation shows strong fit (R²=0.89, ΔBIC=16.6). Human experiments confirm anchor position modulates anchoring magnitude (d=0.52, BF₁₀=847) and working memory load amplifies primacy bias (d=0.41, BF₁₀=156).
AI.conclusion: Cognitive biases are reframed as resource-rational responses to sequential processing constraints rather than irrational errors, with mathematical necessity established through both AI model proofs and human behavioral evidence.

title: NoisyCoconut: Counterfactual Consensus via Latent Space Reasoning
summary: This paper presents NoisyCoconut, a novel inference-time method that enhances large language model (LLM) reliability by manipulating internal representations. Unlike fine-tuning methods that require extensive retraining, NoisyCoconut operates directly on model representations during inference and requires no retraining. Rather than training models to reason in latent space, we inject controlled noise into latent trajectories to generate diverse reasoning paths. Agreement among these paths provides a confidence signal, enabling models to abstain when uncertain. We demonstrate that this approach achieves effective coverage-accuracy tradeoffs across multiple reasoning benchmarks without requiring access to training data or modification of model parameters. This approach provides a practical pathway to improving the reliability of LLM outputs while maintaining compatibility with existing models. Our experiments show that unanimous agreement among noise-perturbed paths reduces error rates from 40-70% to below 15%, enabling models to exceed 95% accuracy on mathematical reasoning tasks through selective abstention.

AI.tldr: NoisyCoconut is an inference-time method that improves LLM reliability by injecting controlled noise into latent representations to generate diverse reasoning paths, using agreement among paths as confidence signal for selective abstention without retraining.
AI.motivation: Current methods for improving LLM reliability often require extensive retraining or fine-tuning, which is resource-intensive and may not be compatible with existing models. There's a need for practical methods that can enhance reliability during inference without modifying model parameters or requiring training data access.
AI.method: During inference, NoisyCoconut injects controlled noise into the latent trajectories of LLMs to generate multiple diverse reasoning paths. It doesn't require retraining or fine-tuning. The method works by comparing the outputs from these noise-perturbed paths and using the level of agreement as a confidence signal to decide when the model should abstain from answering.
AI.result: The method achieves effective coverage-accuracy tradeoffs across multiple reasoning benchmarks. Unanimous agreement among noise-perturbed paths reduces error rates from 40-70% to below 15%. Through selective abstention based on confidence signals, models can exceed 95% accuracy on mathematical reasoning tasks.
AI.conclusion: NoisyCoconut provides a practical pathway to improve LLM reliability that maintains compatibility with existing models, operates at inference time without retraining, and enables effective confidence estimation through noise-induced reasoning diversity.

title: Done, But Not Sure: Disentangling World Completion from Self-Termination in Embodied Agents
summary: Standard embodied evaluations do not independently score whether an agent correctly commits to task completion at episode closure, a capacity we call terminal commitment. Behaviorally distinct failures--never completing the task, completing it but failing to stop, and reporting success without sufficient evidence--collapse into the same benchmark failure. We introduce VIGIL, an evaluation framework that makes terminal commitment independently measurable. Under VIGIL's default protocol, agents observe only egocentric RGB, receive no action-success signals, and must end each episode with a semantic report checked deterministically against hidden world state. This yields two separate scores: world-state completion (W) and benchmark success (B), where B additionally requires a correct terminal report. This decoupling makes four outcome categories distinguishable: missed execution, post-attainment drift, unsupported commitment, and verified success. Across 20 models on 1,000 frozen episodes, systems with comparable W differ by up to 19.7 pp in B: one model converts achieved states into correct reports, while another with near-identical execution drifts past the goal without closing. An action-feedback intervention further tests the separation: execution-oriented signals improve W broadly, yet commitment failures persist in models that do not already ground terminal reports in the achieved state. VIGIL provides a protocol that makes terminal commitment independently visible and scorable.

AI.tldr: VIGIL introduces a new evaluation framework that separately scores task execution completion from terminal commitment (correctly recognizing and reporting task completion), revealing distinct failure modes that standard benchmarks collapse together.
AI.motivation: Standard embodied AI evaluations fail to independently assess whether agents correctly commit to task completion at episode closure. Different behavioral failures (never completing tasks, completing but failing to stop, reporting success without evidence) are all treated as the same benchmark failure, obscuring important distinctions in agent capabilities.
AI.method: VIGIL uses a protocol where agents only observe egocentric RGB, receive no action-success signals, and must end each episode with a semantic report checked deterministically against hidden world state. This yields two separate scores: world-state completion (W) measuring actual task execution, and benchmark success (B) requiring correct terminal reports. The framework distinguishes four outcome categories: missed execution, post-attainment drift, unsupported commitment, and verified success.
AI.result: Across 20 models on 1,000 frozen episodes, systems with comparable world-state completion scores differed by up to 19.7 percentage points in benchmark success. Some models successfully converted achieved states into correct reports, while others with near-identical execution drifted past goals without proper closure. Action-feedback interventions improved execution broadly but commitment failures persisted in models that didn't already ground terminal reports in the achieved state.
AI.conclusion: VIGIL provides a protocol that makes terminal commitment independently visible and scorable, revealing important distinctions in agent capabilities that standard evaluations overlook. The framework enables better understanding of how well agents recognize and report task completion, which is crucial for reliable embodied AI systems.

title: A Simulated Federated Analysis of MS-Induced Brain Lesions
comment: Accepted for publication at The 39th IEEE International Symposium on Computer-Based Medical Systems
summary: Federated techniques such as federated learning and federated analysis have emerged as a powerful paradigm for enabling multi-center research on sensitive clinical data while preserving patient privacy. In this study, we introduce a simulation framework that emulates a real-world federated research project focused on the analysis of multiple sclerosis (MS) patient data. The project comprises two components: an image segmentation task and a clinical data analysis task, where federated variants of survival analysis and Principal Component Analysis (PCA) are employed. To capture the complexity and heterogeneity of real clinical datasets, we construct a federation of high-fidelity synthetic cohorts designed to mirror MS-related clinical and demographic characteristics, while the imaging component leverages publicly available real-world datasets. Our simulation replicates key elements of authentic federated workflows, including distributed data governance, site-specific preprocessing, model training across isolated nodes, and the secure aggregation of analytical outputs. This framework provides a realistic testbed for developing, evaluating, and benchmarking federated learning methods in the context of MS research.

AI.tldr: A simulation framework for federated MS research that combines synthetic clinical data with real imaging data to test federated learning and analysis methods.
AI.motivation: To create a realistic testbed for federated research on sensitive clinical data (specifically multiple sclerosis) while preserving patient privacy, addressing the need for frameworks that capture real-world complexities of federated workflows.
AI.method: Developed a simulation framework with two components: (1) image segmentation task using real-world datasets, and (2) clinical data analysis using federated survival analysis and PCA. Created high-fidelity synthetic cohorts mirroring MS characteristics, and replicated authentic federated workflows including distributed governance, site-specific preprocessing, and secure aggregation.
AI.result: A comprehensive simulation framework that realistically emulates federated MS research projects, providing a testbed for developing and evaluating federated learning methods in clinical research contexts.
AI.conclusion: The framework enables realistic testing and benchmarking of federated techniques for MS research, addressing both clinical and imaging data analysis while maintaining privacy and capturing real-world workflow complexities.

title: Value-Decomposed Reinforcement Learning Framework for Taxiway Routing with Hierarchical Conflict-Aware Observations
summary: Taxiway routing and on-surface conflict avoidance are coupled safety-critical decision problems in airport surface operations. Existing planning and optimization methods are often limited by online computational cost, while reinforcement learning methods may struggle to represent downstream traffic conflicts and balance multiple objectives. This paper presents Conflict-aware Taxiway Routing (CaTR), a reinforcement learning framework for real-time multi-aircraft taxiway routing. CaTR constructs a grid-based airport surface environment with action masking, introduces a hierarchical foresight traffic representation to encode current and downstream conflict-related traffic conditions, and adopts a value-decomposed reinforcement learning strategy to prioritize sparse but safety-critical objectives. Experiments are conducted on a realistic environment based on Changsha Huanghua International Airport under multiple traffic density levels. Results show that CaTR achieves better safety--efficiency trade-offs than representative planning, optimization, and reinforcement learning baselines while maintaining practical runtime.

AI.tldr: A reinforcement learning framework called CaTR for real-time multi-aircraft taxiway routing that uses hierarchical foresight traffic representation and value-decomposition to handle safety-critical conflicts while maintaining computational efficiency.
AI.motivation: Existing methods for taxiway routing and conflict avoidance are limited: planning/optimization methods have high computational costs for real-time use, while reinforcement learning struggles to represent downstream traffic conflicts and balance multiple safety/efficiency objectives.
AI.method: CaTR constructs a grid-based airport surface environment with action masking, introduces hierarchical foresight traffic representation to encode current and downstream conflict conditions, and uses value-decomposed reinforcement learning to prioritize sparse but safety-critical objectives.
AI.result: Experiments on Changsha Huanghua International Airport under multiple traffic density levels show CaTR achieves better safety-efficiency trade-offs than planning, optimization, and RL baselines while maintaining practical runtime.
AI.conclusion: CaTR provides an effective reinforcement learning framework for real-time multi-aircraft taxiway routing that addresses computational and safety-representation limitations of existing approaches through hierarchical foresight representation and value-decomposition techniques.

title: Social Determinants of Health and Fentanyl Overdose Mortality Across US Counties: An XGBoost and SHAP Analysis Identifying Silent Risk Counties and Treatment Deserts
summary:
Background: Fentanyl overdose deaths are still increasing across the U.S. We do not fully understand which county-level social and structural conditions lead to higher overdose death rates. Social determinants of health, including disability, treatment access, and behavioral health issues, may help identify vulnerable counties before deaths become severe. No earlier study has used explainable machine learning with SHAP attribution on 2022 CDC WONDER data to study treatment access gaps and silent risk counties.
  Methods: We combined data from four government sources for 975 U.S. counties, including CDC WONDER (2022) overdose mortality data, CDC Social Vulnerability Index (SVI), CDC PLACES health behavior data, and Area Health Resources Files. An XGBoost model was used to predict overdose mortality risk using Standardized Mortality Ratio (SMR). Five-fold cross-validation was used to test model accuracy, and SHAP values were used to show which factors increase or decrease risk.
  Results: XGBoost outperformed all tested models (Spearman rho=0.67, R2=0.457, MAE=0.409, high-risk recall=71.1%). Top predictors were disability rate, hypertension, smoking, and lack of vehicle access. Treatment desert counties had 52.6% higher overdose mortality (SMR 1.786 vs 1.170; p<0.0001). K-means identified 143 silent risk counties. Overdose deaths were spatially clustered (Moran's I=0.505, p=0.001) with 75 hotspots and 136 coldspots. Suppressed counties were 58.2% of WONDER counties, mostly rural (72%) and treatment deserts (65%).
  Conclusions: County-level SDOH factors predict overdose deaths, especially disability, treatment access, and behavioral health burden. MOUD expansion should prioritize treatment desert counties, and silent risk counties need early intervention before mortality worsens.

AI.tldr: Using explainable machine learning on 2022 CDC data, the study finds that county-level social determinants (disability, treatment access, behavioral health) predict fentanyl overdose deaths, identifying treatment desert counties and silent risk clusters that need targeted interventions.
AI.motivation: Despite increasing fentanyl overdose deaths across the U.S., there's limited understanding of which county-level social and structural conditions lead to higher overdose death rates. Social determinants of health including disability, treatment access, and behavioral health issues may help identify vulnerable counties before deaths become severe. Previous studies haven't used explainable machine learning with SHAP attribution on 2022 CDC WONDER data to study treatment access gaps and silent risk counties.
AI.method: Combined data from four government sources for 975 U.S. counties: CDC WONDER (2022) overdose mortality data, CDC Social Vulnerability Index (SVI), CDC PLACES health behavior data, and Area Health Resources Files. Used XGBoost model to predict overdose mortality risk using Standardized Mortality Ratio (SMR) with five-fold cross-validation for accuracy testing. Applied SHAP values to identify factors increasing/decreasing risk, and used K-means clustering to identify silent risk counties. Conducted spatial analysis (Moran's I) to detect clustering patterns.
AI.result: XGBoost model performed best (Spearman rho=0.67, R2=0.457, MAE=0.409, high-risk recall=71.1%). Top predictors: disability rate, hypertension, smoking, and lack of vehicle access. Treatment desert counties had 52.6% higher overdose mortality (SMR 1.786 vs 1.170; p<0.0001). K-means identified 143 silent risk counties. Spatial analysis showed significant clustering (Moran's I=0.505, p=0.001) with 75 hotspots and 136 coldspots. Suppressed counties were 58.2% of WONDER counties, mostly rural (72%) and treatment deserts (65%).
AI.conclusion: County-level social determinants of health factors effectively predict overdose deaths, particularly disability, treatment access, and behavioral health burden. MOUD (Medications for Opioid Use Disorder) expansion should prioritize treatment desert counties, and silent risk counties need early intervention before mortality worsens. The methodology provides a framework for identifying vulnerable counties using explainable machine learning approaches.

title: AHD Agent: Agentic Reinforcement Learning for Automatic Heuristic Design
summary: Automatic heuristic design (AHD) has emerged as a promising paradigm for solving NP-hard combinatorial optimization problems (COPs). Recent works show that large language models (LLMs), when integrated into well-designed frameworks (i.e., LLM-AHD), can autonomously discover high-performing heuristics. However, existing LLM-AHD frameworks typically treat LLMs as passive generators within fixed workflows, where the model generates heuristics from manually designed, limited context. Such context may fail to capture state-dependent information (e.g., specific failure modes), leading to inefficient trial-and-error exploration. To overcome these limitations, we propose AHD Agent, a novel tool-integrated, multi-turn framework that empowers LLMs to proactively decide whether to generate heuristics or invoke tools to retrieve targeted evidence from the solving environment. To effectively train such a dynamic decision-making agent, we introduce an agentic reinforcement learning (RL) system, which leverages a novel environment synthesis pipeline to optimize a compact model's generalizable AHD capabilities. Experiments across eight diverse domains, including four held-out tasks, demonstrate that our 4B-parameter agent matches or surpasses state-of-the-art baselines using much larger models, while requiring significantly fewer evaluations. Model and inference scaling analysis further reveals that AHD Agent offers an effective trajectory toward truly autonomous heuristic design.

AI.tldr: AHD Agent is a novel framework that transforms LLMs from passive heuristic generators into proactive agents that can dynamically decide between generating heuristics or using tools to gather evidence, trained via agentic RL to achieve state-of-the-art performance with smaller models and fewer evaluations.
AI.motivation: Existing LLM-AHD frameworks treat LLMs as passive generators within fixed workflows, using manually designed limited context that may fail to capture state-dependent information (like specific failure modes), leading to inefficient trial-and-error exploration in heuristic design for combinatorial optimization problems.
AI.method: Proposes AHD Agent: a tool-integrated, multi-turn framework where LLMs proactively decide between generating heuristics or invoking tools to retrieve targeted evidence from the solving environment. Uses an agentic reinforcement learning system with a novel environment synthesis pipeline to train the model's generalizable AHD capabilities.
AI.result: Experiments across eight diverse domains (including four held-out tasks) show that the 4B-parameter agent matches or surpasses state-of-the-art baselines using much larger models, while requiring significantly fewer evaluations. Scaling analysis indicates AHD Agent offers an effective trajectory toward truly autonomous heuristic design.
AI.conclusion: AHD Agent transforms LLMs from passive heuristic generators into proactive agents with dynamic decision-making capabilities, enabling more efficient and effective automatic heuristic design through tool integration and agentic reinforcement learning, representing a significant advancement toward autonomous heuristic design.

title: TRAM: Training Approximate Multiplier Structures for Low-Power AI Accelerators
summary: Reducing power consumption in AI accelerators is increasingly important. Approximate computing can reduce power consumption while keeping the accuracy loss small. Since multipliers are power-hungry components in AI models, this paper focuses on synthesizing low-power approximate multipliers (AxMs). Unlike prior works that design AxMs separately from AI model training, we present TRAM, which jointly optimizes the AxM structure and AI model parameters to lower power with small accuracy loss. Experiments show that compared to state-of-the-art AxMs, TRAM achieves up to 25.05% AxM power reduction on CNNs with CIFAR-10, and reduces power by up to 27.09% on vision transformers with ImageNet.

AI.tldr: TRAM jointly optimizes approximate multiplier design and AI model training for significant power reduction with minimal accuracy loss.
AI.motivation: Power consumption in AI accelerators is critical, and multipliers are particularly power-hungry components. Existing approaches design approximate multipliers separately from AI model training, missing opportunities for better joint optimization.
AI.method: TRAM introduces a co-design approach that jointly optimizes approximate multiplier structures and AI model parameters during training, enabling adaptive approximation that minimizes power while maintaining accuracy.
AI.result: TRAM achieves up to 25.05% power reduction in approximate multipliers for CNNs on CIFAR-10, and up to 27.09% power reduction for vision transformers on ImageNet, compared to state-of-the-art approximate multipliers.
AI.conclusion: Joint optimization of approximate multiplier design and AI model training is more effective than separate optimization, enabling significant power savings in AI accelerators while maintaining acceptable accuracy.

title: From Holo Pockets to Electron Density: GPT-style Drug Design with Density
comment: Published as a conference paper in ICML 2026
summary: Recent advances in generative modeling have enabled significant progress in structure-based drug design (SBDD). Existing methods typically condition molecule generation on empty binding pockets from holo complexes, overlooking informative components such as the filler (ligands and solvent). Here, we leverage low-resolution electron density (ED) derived from the filler as a physically grounded condition for \textit{de novo} drug design. We consider two types of ED, calculated and cryo-EM/X-ray, obtainable from computational or experimental sources, supporting unified pre-training and experimental integration. Compared with rigid pocket representations, experimental ED naturally captures conformational flexibility and provides a more faithful description of the binding environment. Based on this, we introduce EDMolGPT, a decoder-only autoregressive framework that generates molecules from low-resolution ED point clouds. By grounding generation in physically meaningful density signals, EDMolGPT mitigates structural bias and produces molecules with 3D conformations. Evaluations on 101 biological targets verify the effectiveness. Our project page: https://jiahaochen1.github.io/EDMolGPT_Page/.

AI.tldr: EDMolGPT is an autoregressive framework that generates molecules directly from low-resolution electron density point clouds, using physical density signals from fillers (ligands/solvent) as conditioning to produce 3D conformations for structure-based drug design.
AI.motivation: Current structure-based drug design methods generate molecules conditioned only on empty binding pockets from holo complexes, ignoring informative filler components (ligands and solvent). The authors aim to leverage physically-grounded electron density from fillers as a more informative condition that naturally captures conformational flexibility.
AI.method: Proposed EDMolGPT, a decoder-only autoregressive framework that generates molecules from low-resolution electron density point clouds. Uses two types of electron density: calculated (computational) and cryo-EM/X-ray derived (experimental), enabling unified pre-training and experimental integration.
AI.result: Evaluations on 101 biological targets verify effectiveness. The approach mitigates structural bias and produces molecules with 3D conformations by grounding generation in physically meaningful density signals rather than rigid pocket representations.
AI.conclusion: Leveraging electron density from fillers as a physical condition for molecular generation addresses limitations of current SBDD methods. EDMolGPT provides a unified framework that integrates computational and experimental data sources, offering improved representation of binding environments for de novo drug design.

title: Hierarchical Multi-Fidelity Learning for Predicting Three-Dimensional Flame Wrinkling and Turbulent Burning Velocity
summary: High-fidelity experimental characterization of turbulent premixed flames remains limited by the cost and complexity of advanced diagnostics, particularly under elevated pressures and intense turbulence where measurements of coupled flame morphology and burning dynamics are sparse. Here, we develop a hierarchical multi-fidelity neural network framework (MuFiNNs) to address this challenge by integrating sparse high-fidelity experimental data with structured low-fidelity representations encoding dominant physical trends. The framework combines hierarchical low-fidelity construction with nonlinear multi-fidelity correction to learn coupled geometric and reactive flame behavior while recovering discrepancies that simplified models alone cannot capture. The methodology is applied to expanding turbulent premixed flames to predict three-dimensional flame wrinkling dynamics and turbulent mass burning velocity across varying fuels, pressures, and turbulence intensities. Using experimentally informed low-fidelity trend models with sparse high-fidelity measurements, MuFiNNs accurately reconstruct observed flame behavior, enable interpolation across unseen operating conditions, and demonstrate robust extrapolation beyond the training domain. Importantly, the framework remains effective in noisy, weakly structured, or experimentally inaccessible regimes where conventional data-driven approaches often fail. These results show that hierarchical multi-fidelity learning provides a scalable and physically grounded strategy for predictive combustion modeling in data-limited regimes. More broadly, this work establishes multi-fidelity scientific machine learning as a practical framework for extracting physically meaningful predictive models from sparse experiments, particularly for instability-dominated and turbulence-sensitive reactive flows where high-fidelity data acquisition is demanding.

AI.tldr: MuFiNNs: A hierarchical multi-fidelity neural network framework that integrates sparse high-fidelity experimental data with structured low-fidelity physical models to predict turbulent premixed flame behavior in data-limited regimes.
AI.motivation: High-fidelity experimental characterization of turbulent premixed flames is limited by cost and complexity of advanced diagnostics, especially under elevated pressures and intense turbulence where measurements of coupled flame morphology and burning dynamics are sparse.
AI.method: Develops a hierarchical multi-fidelity neural network framework (MuFiNNs) that combines hierarchical low-fidelity construction with nonlinear multi-fidelity correction to learn coupled geometric and reactive flame behavior, integrating sparse high-fidelity experimental data with structured low-fidelity representations encoding dominant physical trends.
AI.result: MuFiNNs accurately reconstruct observed flame behavior, enable interpolation across unseen operating conditions, and demonstrate robust extrapolation beyond training domains. The framework remains effective in noisy, weakly structured, or experimentally inaccessible regimes where conventional data-driven approaches often fail.
AI.conclusion: Hierarchical multi-fidelity learning provides a scalable and physically grounded strategy for predictive combustion modeling in data-limited regimes. Establishes multi-fidelity scientific machine learning as a practical framework for extracting physically meaningful predictive models from sparse experiments, particularly for instability-dominated and turbulence-sensitive reactive flows.

title: EvoMAS: Learning Execution-Time Workflows for Multi-Agent Systems
summary: Large language model (LLM)-based multi-agent systems have shown strong potential on complex tasks through agent specialization, tool use, and collaborative reasoning. However, most automated multi-agent system design methods still follow a one-shot paradigm: a workflow is optimized or selected before execution and then reused unchanged throughout the task. This static coordination strategy is ill-suited for long-horizon tasks whose subgoals, intermediate evidence, and information needs evolve over multiple execution stages. We propose EvoMAS, a framework for execution-time multi-agent workflow construction. EvoMAS formulates workflow construction as a meta-level sequential decision problem along a single task trajectory. At each stage, it constructs an explicit task state through a Planner-Evaluator-Updater pipeline and uses a learned Workflow Adapter to instantiate a stage-specific layered workflow from a fixed pool of candidate agents. The adapter is trained with policy gradients using sparse, verifiable terminal task success as the main supervision signal, while evaluator-based process reward is analyzed separately under very-hard sparse-reward settings. Experiments on GAIA, HLE, and DeepResearcher show that EvoMAS outperforms single-agent baselines and recent automated multi-agent workflow design methods. Our analyses further show that explicit task-state construction and learned workflow adaptation provide complementary benefits. Additional results indicate that process reward is most useful when terminal success is extremely sparse, and qualitative case studies illustrate that EvoMAS adapts agent coordination as the task state evolves.

AI.tldr: EvoMAS is a dynamic multi-agent workflow construction framework that adapts agent coordination in real-time based on evolving task states, outperforming static workflow methods on complex, long-horizon tasks.
AI.motivation: Current automated multi-agent systems use static, one-shot workflow designs that are unsuitable for long-horizon tasks where subgoals, evidence, and information needs evolve throughout execution. The authors identified a need for execution-time adaptation of multi-agent workflows.
AI.method: EvoMAS treats workflow construction as a meta-level sequential decision problem. It uses a Planner-Evaluator-Updater pipeline to construct explicit task states at each stage, then employs a learned Workflow Adapter to instantiate stage-specific layered workflows from a fixed pool of agents. The adapter is trained with policy gradients using terminal task success as supervision, with separate analysis of process rewards in sparse-reward settings.
AI.result: Experiments on GAIA, HLE, and DeepResearcher benchmarks show EvoMAS outperforms single-agent baselines and recent automated multi-agent workflow design methods. Analyses reveal that explicit task-state construction and learned workflow adaptation provide complementary benefits. Process reward proves most useful in extremely sparse terminal success scenarios, and case studies demonstrate adaptive coordination as task states evolve.
AI.conclusion: EvoMAS successfully addresses the limitations of static multi-agent coordination by enabling dynamic, execution-time workflow construction that adapts to evolving task requirements, representing a significant advancement in automated multi-agent system design for complex, long-horizon tasks.

title: When Does Value-Aware KV Eviction Help? A Fixed-Contract Diagnostic for Non-Monotone Cache Compression
summary: Long-context LLM inference is bottlenecked by the memory and bandwidth cost of reading large KV caches during decoding. KV compression reduces this cost by keeping only part of the cache, but task accuracy alone does not identify why a selector succeeds or fails. A selector can fail at three steps: it may miss the evidence future decoding needs, give high scores to tokens that do not affect the output, or break related evidence when fitting scores into a small cache. We introduce a fixed-contract diagnostic that holds the selector's setup fixed and changes one decision slot at a time. For value ranking, the probe combines a block's attention mass with the estimated output change from removing it. On LongBench across three models and two budgets, the probe is positive on 72.6% of positive-margin cells and 32.4% of nonpositive-margin cells. NeedleBench M-RT at 32k and a RULER 8k check probe support closure under branched retrieval, and a 264-cell sign evaluation separates support recovery and output-value ranking from leverage effects near the boundary. The resulting order is to recover decode-side evidence, rank its output value, and preserve coupled evidence during projection.

AI.tldr: KV cache compression for long-context LLM inference introduces a diagnostic probe to evaluate why selectors succeed/fail at three failure modes: missing evidence, scoring irrelevant tokens, and breaking related evidence.
AI.motivation: Long-context LLM inference is bottlenecked by memory/bandwidth costs of reading large KV caches. While KV compression reduces costs, task accuracy alone doesn't explain why selectors succeed or fail - they can fail in three distinct ways that need systematic diagnosis.
AI.method: Introduces a fixed-contract diagnostic that holds the selector's setup fixed while changing one decision slot at a time. For value ranking, combines a block's attention mass with estimated output change from removal. Tests on LongBench across three models and two budgets, plus NeedleBench M-RT at 32k and RULER 8k checks.
AI.result: The probe is positive on 72.6% of positive-margin cells and 32.4% of nonpositive-margin cells. The diagnostic supports closure under branched retrieval, and a 264-cell sign evaluation separates support recovery/output-value ranking from leverage effects near the boundary.
AI.conclusion: The resulting approach prioritizes: recover decode-side evidence first, rank its output value, and preserve coupled evidence during projection. This provides systematic diagnosis for KV cache selector performance beyond just task accuracy.

title: Reasoning Compression with Mixed-Policy Distillation
summary: Reasoning-centric large language models (LLMs) achieve strong performance by generating intermediate reasoning trajectories, but often incur excessive token usage and high inference-time decoding cost. We observe that, when solving the same problems, larger reasoning models can often produce more concise traces, whereas smaller reasoning models tend to generate longer and more redundant trajectories. This is especially problematic in real-world deployment, where memory, latency, and serving-cost constraints often favor smaller models. Our observations suggest that reasoning compression can be transferred from large models to small ones rather than enforced through explicit length constraints. Based on this insight, we propose Mixed-Policy Distillation (MPD), a reasoning compression framework that transfers concise reasoning behavior from a larger-sized teacher to a smaller student by distilling teacher-compressed student trajectories. Unlike on-policy distillation, which aligns the student with teacher distributions over verbose student trajectories, or off-policy distillation, which relies on teacher-generated trajectories and may suffer from distribution mismatch, MPD combines the strengths of both. Given a student-sampled trajectory, the teacher rewrites it into a more concise reasoning trace, and the student is trained via KL-based alignment on the compressed trajectory. This preserves student-policy exploration while injecting teacher-guided compression. Experiments on Qwen3-1.7B show that MPD reduces token usage by up to 27.1% while improving performance across multiple reasoning benchmarks, demonstrating an effective approach to efficient small-model reasoning.

AI.tldr: MPD transfers concise reasoning behavior from large teacher LLMs to smaller student models by having the teacher compress student-generated reasoning trajectories, reducing token usage by up to 27.1% while improving performance.
AI.motivation: Smaller reasoning LLMs tend to generate verbose, redundant reasoning trajectories compared to larger models, causing excessive token usage and high inference costs. This creates deployment challenges where memory, latency, and cost constraints favor smaller models, necessitating efficient reasoning compression techniques.
AI.method: Mixed-Policy Distillation (MPD): A framework where the teacher model rewrites student-sampled reasoning trajectories into more concise traces, then the student is trained via KL-based alignment on these compressed trajectories. This combines student-policy exploration with teacher-guided compression.
AI.result: Experiments on Qwen3-1.7B show MPD reduces token usage by up to 27.1% while improving performance across multiple reasoning benchmarks, demonstrating effective small-model reasoning compression.
AI.conclusion: MPD provides an effective approach to transferring reasoning compression from large to small models, enabling more efficient small-model reasoning without performance degradation, addressing practical deployment constraints.

title: Distributional Spectral Diagnostics for Localizing Grokking Transitions
summary: In grokking, a model first fits the training data while test accuracy remains low, and only later begins to generalize. We ask whether this transition can be localized from observed training trajectories before the test accuracy rises, and formulate grokking transition localization as a diagnostic problem with an explicit threshold/FPR/lead-time trade-off. Task-dependent observables are summarized as empirical distributions, mapped to Wasserstein/quantile coordinates, and analyzed by Hankel dynamic mode decomposition (DMD); the resulting reconstruction residual, together with spectrum and effective rank, forms the diagnostic output. On held-out modular-addition Transformer runs, the residual achieves AUROC \(\approx \) 0.93 for grokking-vs-non-grokking discrimination at the run level; under a fixed sustained-threshold operating rule, true-positive alarms can precede onset, with lead time reported jointly with false-alarm rate and uncertainty intervals. Perturbation experiments show that, in the tested \(wd=1\) pool, high-residual windows exhibit about \(3\times\) larger short-horizon perturbation deviation than low-residual windows. In a same-data norm-window control, perturbation sensitivity aligns with the residual ordering rather than total-parameter-norm ordering, suggesting that the residual is not merely a total-norm proxy at the window level in the studied \(wd=1\) dynamics. Norm signals remain strong run-level regime indicators, and log-probability performs best among the observables tested under the current protocol. We position the residual as a window-level monitoring and localization signal in the studied modular-arithmetic Transformer settings, not a universal early-warning predictor or an intervention rule.

AI.tldr: This paper develops a diagnostic method to localize grokking transitions in neural networks before test accuracy rises, using Wasserstein/quantile coordinates and Hankel DMD to compute residuals that can detect grokking windows with high AUROC and provide lead-time warnings.
AI.motivation: In grokking phenomena, models first memorize training data with poor generalization, then later suddenly generalize. The authors want to detect these transitions early from training trajectories before test accuracy improves, enabling localization and monitoring of the grokking process.
AI.method: Task observables are summarized as empirical distributions, mapped to Wasserstein/quantile coordinates, and analyzed using Hankel dynamic mode decomposition (DMD). The reconstruction residual, spectrum, and effective rank form the diagnostic output for grokking detection.
AI.result: The method achieves AUROC ≈ 0.93 for grokking vs non-grokking discrimination at run level on modular-addition Transformers. High-residual windows show 3× larger short-horizon perturbation deviation. Residual signals work better than total parameter norms for window-level localization in wd=1 dynamics.
AI.conclusion: The residual serves as an effective window-level monitoring and localization signal for grokking transitions in modular-arithmetic Transformer settings, but is not presented as a universal early-warning predictor or intervention rule.

title: Path-Coupled Bellman Flows for Distributional Reinforcement Learning
comment: Accepted to the 43rd International Conference on Machine Learning (ICML 2026)
summary: Distributional reinforcement learning (DRL) models the full return distribution, but existing finite-support or quantile-based methods rely on projections, while recent flow-based approaches can suffer from \emph{boundary mismatch} at the flow source or from \emph{high-variance} bootstrapping when current and successor noises are independent. We propose Path-Coupled Bellman Flows (PCBF), a continuous-time DRL method that learns return distributions with flow matching using \textbf{source-consistent Bellman-coupled paths}: the current path starts from the required base prior at $t{=}0$, reaches the Bellman target at $t{=}1$, and maintains a pathwise affine relation to the successor flow at intermediate times (without requiring time-$t$ marginals to satisfy a distributional Bellman fixed point for all $t$). PCBF couples current and successor return flows through shared base noise and uses a $λ$-parameterized control-variate target: $λ{=}0$ recovers an unbiased sample Bellman target, while $λ{>}0$ trades controlled bias for variance reduction. Experiments on analytically tractable MRPs, OGBench, and D4RL show improved distributional fidelity and training stability, and competitive offline RL performance.

AI.tldr: PCBF is a continuous-time DRL method that learns return distributions via flow matching with source-consistent Bellman-coupled paths, addressing boundary mismatch and high-variance issues in existing DRL approaches.
AI.motivation: Existing DRL methods face issues: finite-support or quantile-based methods rely on projections, while flow-based approaches suffer from boundary mismatch at flow sources and high-variance bootstrapping when current and successor noises are independent.
AI.method: PCBF learns return distributions using flow matching with source-consistent Bellman-coupled paths. The method couples current and successor return flows through shared base noise and maintains a pathwise affine relation at intermediate times. It uses a λ-parameterized control-variate target that allows trading controlled bias for variance reduction.
AI.result: Experiments on analytically tractable MRPs, OGBench, and D4RL show improved distributional fidelity and training stability, as well as competitive offline RL performance.
AI.conclusion: PCBF provides a principled continuous-time DRL approach that addresses key limitations of existing methods through novel Bellman-coupled paths and variance-reducing techniques, achieving better distributional modeling and practical performance.

title: Mirror, Mirror on the Wall: Can VLM Agents Tell Who They Are at All?
summary: In the animal kingdom, mirror self-recognition is a canonical probe of higher-order cognition, emerging only in some species. We ask whether an analogous functional capability emerges in embodied vision-language model (VLM) agents: can they recognize themselves in a mirror? We introduce a controlled 3D benchmark where a first-person VLM agent must infer a hidden body attribute from its reflection and select the matching target, while avoiding self-other misattribution. To separate mirror-grounded self-identification from shortcuts, we test mirror removal, misleading cues, and occluded reflections. We also evaluate the decision process through mirror seeking, temporal ordering, self-attribution, and reasoning-action consistency. Our experiments show that mirror-based self-identification emerges mainly in stronger VLMs. These models can use reflected evidence for action, whereas weaker models often inspect the mirror but fail to extract self-relevant information or misattribute their reflection. Language-vision conflict further shows that self-referential language alone is not evidence of grounded self-identification. Overall, mirror-based evaluation provides a diagnostic for whether embodied self-grounding is causally rooted in perception and action rather than priors, prompt compliance, or confabulation.

AI.tldr: Embodied vision-language models are tested for mirror self-recognition abilities similar to animals, with results showing stronger models can use mirror reflections for self-identification while weaker models fail or misattribute their reflection.
AI.motivation: To determine whether embodied vision-language model agents can develop analogous functional capabilities to animal mirror self-recognition, and to create a diagnostic tool that evaluates whether embodied self-grounding is causally rooted in perception and action rather than just language priors or prompt compliance.
AI.method: Developed a controlled 3D benchmark where first-person VLM agents must infer hidden body attributes from mirror reflections and select matching targets while avoiding self-other misattribution. Used mirror removal, misleading cues, and occluded reflections to separate mirror-grounded self-identification from shortcuts. Evaluated through mirror seeking, temporal ordering, self-attribution, and reasoning-action consistency tests.
AI.result: Mirror-based self-identification emerges mainly in stronger VLMs, which can use reflected evidence for action. Weaker models often inspect mirrors but fail to extract self-relevant information or misattribute their reflection. Language-vision conflict shows self-referential language alone is not evidence of grounded self-identification.
AI.conclusion: Mirror-based evaluation provides a valuable diagnostic for assessing whether embodied self-grounding in VLMs is causally rooted in perception and action rather than being driven by priors, prompt compliance, or confabulation. The benchmark distinguishes between models with genuine self-identification capabilities and those without.

title: HyperTransport: Amortized Conditioning of T2I Generative Models
summary: As foundation models grow in capability, the ability to efficiently and reliably control their behavior becomes critical. Fine-tuning these models can be costly, and while prompting can be practical for controllability, it remains fragile due to models' high sensitivity to exact prompt wording and structure. This brittleness has driven interest in activation steering techniques that offer more stable and predictable control over model behavior. However, existing activation steering methods require per-concept optimization, which makes them ill-suited to deployment scenarios where the concept set is large, evolving, or only specified at request time: each new concept incurs at least minutes of optimization on the target model. We propose HyperTransport, a hypernetwork framework that amortizes this cost by mapping embeddings from a pretrained encoder (CLIP in our instantiation) directly to intervention parameters, trained end-to-end using an optimal transport loss. Once trained, HyperTransport produces each new intervention in a single hypernetwork forward pass, 3600-7000x faster than per-concept fitting. On concepts unseen during training, it matches the strongest per-concept baselines at inducing the target concept. By decoupling concept representation from intervention prediction, HyperTransport combines three capabilities that no existing approach offers as a set: amortized steering for open-ended concept sets, continuous interpretable strength control, and cross-modal conditioning where reference images can directly steer text-based generation. We validate HyperTransport on DMD2 and Nitro-1-PixArt across 167 held-out test concepts via CLIP-based metrics, a VLM-as-a-judge evaluation, and a user study. In pairwise comparisons, both human and VLM judges prefer HyperTransport over prompting ~2x as often.

AI.tldr: HyperTransport uses hypernetworks to generate activation steering parameters from concept embeddings, enabling fast, amortized control of foundation models with 3600-7000x speedup over per-concept optimization.
AI.motivation: Existing activation steering methods require per-concept optimization, making them impractical for deployment with large, evolving concept sets that change frequently or are specified at runtime. Fine-tuning is expensive, and prompting is fragile due to sensitivity to wording.
AI.method: Propose HyperTransport, a hypernetwork framework that maps embeddings from a pretrained encoder (CLIP) directly to intervention parameters using optimal transport loss. Trains end-to-end to amortize costs, producing interventions in a single forward pass instead of per-concept optimization.
AI.result: Achieves 3600-7000x faster intervention generation than per-concept fitting, matches strongest baselines on unseen concepts, enables continuous strength control and cross-modal conditioning (images steer text generation), and is preferred ~2x more often than prompting in human and VLM evaluations across 167 concepts.
AI.conclusion: HyperTransport provides a practical solution for amortized activation steering that combines open-ended concept support, continuous control, and cross-modal conditioning - capabilities not offered together by existing approaches, making stable model control viable for real-world deployment.

title: How You Begin is How You Reason: Driving Exploration in RLVR via Prefix-Tuned Priors
summary: Reinforcement learning with verifiable rewards (RLVR) recently thrives in large language model (LLM) reasoning tasks. However, the reward sparsity and the long reasoning horizon make effective exploration challenging. In practice, this challenge manifests as the \emph{entropy collapse} phenomenon, where RLVR improves single-rollout accuracy but fails to expand coverage on successful reasoning trajectories. Passive exploration techniques like entropy regularization tend to dismiss generation quality, resulting in noisy rollouts. In response to this issue, we propose an Information-Maximizing Augmented eXploration (IMAX) framework to train a pool of soft prefixes that reshapes the base model's prior over reasoning trajectories. Rather than relying on RL to incentivize exploration on top of the base model, each prefix acts as a trainable control knob that induces a distinct rollout distribution from the same backbone model. To encourage discovery of diverse and task-relevant reasoning behaviors, we derive an Information Maximization (InfoMax) reward to complement the verifiable rewards for RL training. IMAX is in general algorithm-agnostic and can be seamlessly integrated into existing RLVR pipelines. Experiment results have shown that across three backbone scales, IMAX consistently improves reasoning performance over standard RLVR, with gains up to 11.60\% in Pass@4 and 10.57\% in Avg@4.

AI.tldr: RLVR faces exploration challenges in LLM reasoning tasks due to sparse rewards and long horizons, causing entropy collapse. IMAX proposes training soft prefixes with InfoMax rewards to diversify reasoning trajectories and improve performance over standard RLVR.
AI.motivation: Reinforcement Learning with Verifiable Rewards (RLVR) struggles with effective exploration in LLM reasoning tasks due to reward sparsity and long reasoning horizons. This leads to "entropy collapse" where RLVR improves single-rollout accuracy but fails to expand coverage of successful reasoning trajectories. Existing passive exploration techniques like entropy regularization often degrade generation quality with noisy rollouts.
AI.method: Proposes Information-Maximizing Augmented eXploration (IMAX) framework that trains a pool of soft prefixes to reshape the base model's prior over reasoning trajectories. Instead of using RL to incentivize exploration on the base model directly, each prefix acts as a trainable control knob inducing distinct rollout distributions from the same backbone model. Uses Information Maximization (InfoMax) reward alongside verifiable rewards to encourage discovery of diverse, task-relevant reasoning behaviors.
AI.result: Experiments across three backbone scales show IMAX consistently improves reasoning performance over standard RLVR, with gains up to 11.60% in Pass@4 and 10.57% in Avg@4 metrics.
AI.conclusion: IMAX successfully addresses exploration challenges in RLVR by promoting diverse reasoning trajectories through trainable soft prefixes with InfoMax rewards, demonstrating significant performance improvements while maintaining algorithm generality.

title: Can LLMs Predict Polymer Physics Just by Reading Synthesis and Processing Prose?
summary: Can large language models predict physical and mechanical polymer properties simply by reading unstructured scientific prose? Polymer performance is rarely determined by chemical structure alone; identical nominal polymers can exhibit drastically different behaviors depending on their synthesis route, processing history, morphology, and testing conditions. Yet, state-of-the-art polymer property models typically rely on structure-only representations -- such as SMILES or molecular graphs -- which strip away this vital experimental context.
