AI.motivation: Existing circuit discovery methods make two problematic assumptions: (1) LMs implement tasks with single circuits, and (2) datasets adequately represent tasks as humans understand them. These methods produce dataset-specific rather than general task circuits, and fail when multiple distinct computational mechanisms exist.
AI.method: DCD (Data-driven Circuit Discovery) clusters examples by how similarly the model processes them, then discovers separate circuits for each group. This allows distinct mechanisms to appear separately rather than being merged into a single circuit.
AI.result: Experiments across four tasks show DCD discovers multiple circuits per dataset, each more faithful to its group than a single circuit from existing methods. DCD reveals mechanistic structure within LMs that doesn't align with human-defined task boundaries.
AI.conclusion: DCD enables data-driven revelation of LM computational structure by letting example clustering drive circuit discovery, moving beyond human-defined task boundaries to better capture how models actually organize computation.

title: When Attention Beats Fourier: Multi-Scale Transformers for PDE Solving on Irregular Domains
summary: We study the problem of \emph{architecture selection} for deep learning models trained to solve partial differential equations (PDEs), asking when transformer-based architectures with learned attention outperform Fourier-domain neural operators. We introduce the \textbf{Multi-Scale Attention Transformer} (\msat{}), a deep learning architecture that encodes spatiotemporal solution histories as token sequences and trains end-to-end via a composite supervised objective with optional physics-informed regularization terms. We conduct a comprehensive empirical evaluation against nine baselines -- including physics-informed neural networks (PINNs), neural operators (FNO, DeepONet, GNOT), and state-space models (Mamba-NO) -- across five benchmark problems from the PINNacle suite, using identical train/test splits and reference data for all methods. \msat{} achieves state-of-the-art generalization on complex geometry problems ($L^2_\mathrm{rel} = 0.0101$ on Heat2D-CG, a $3.7\times$ improvement over FNO) at $34\,\mathrm{s}$ total inference vs.\ $120{,}812\,\mathrm{s}$ for Mamba-NO. Ablation studies over the physics regularization component reveal a precise inductive bias tradeoff: physics priors reduce test error on diffusion-dominated problems but degrade generalization on chaotic and recirculating-flow regimes, directly characterizing the prior misspecification boundary. Approximation error bounds as a function of domain boundary complexity $κ$ provide a theoretical basis for these empirical findings and a principled rule for architecture selection.

AI.tldr: Transformer-based Multi-Scale Attention Transformer (MSAT) outperforms Fourier neural operators on complex geometry PDE problems, achieving state-of-the-art accuracy while being 3,500x faster than some baselines, with theoretical bounds for architecture selection.
AI.motivation: The paper addresses the problem of architecture selection for deep learning models solving PDEs, specifically investigating when transformer-based architectures with learned attention outperform Fourier-domain neural operators, which is an important but underexplored question in scientific machine learning.
AI.method: Proposes Multi-Scale Attention Transformer (MSAT) that encodes spatiotemporal solution histories as token sequences and trains end-to-end via composite supervised objective with optional physics-informed regularization terms. Compares against 9 baselines (PINNs, neural operators like FNO, DeepONet, GNOT, and Mamba-NO) across 5 PINNacle benchmark problems with identical train/test splits and reference data.
AI.result: MSAT achieves state-of-the-art generalization on complex geometry problems (L²_rel = 0.0101 on Heat2D-CG, 3.7x improvement over FNO) with total inference time of 34s vs. 120,812s for Mamba-NO. Ablation studies reveal physics priors help on diffusion-dominated problems but hurt on chaotic/recirculating-flow regimes, characterizing the prior misspecification boundary.
AI.conclusion: MSAT demonstrates transformer-based architectures can outperform Fourier neural operators, especially on complex geometry problems. The paper provides both empirical evidence and theoretical approximation error bounds as function of domain boundary complexity κ, offering principled rules for architecture selection in PDE learning tasks.

title: BoostAPR: Boosting Automated Program Repair via Execution-Grounded Reinforcement Learning with Dual Reward Models
summary: Reinforcement learning for program repair is hindered by sparse execution feedback and coarse sequence-level rewards that obscure which edits actually fix bugs. We present BoostAPR, a three-stage framework addressing these challenges: (1) supervised fine-tuning on execution-verified demonstrations with reasoning traces, (2) training dual reward models--a sequence-level assessor and a line-level credit allocator--from execution outcomes, and (3) PPO optimization where the line-level model redistributes rewards to critical edit regions. This line-level credit assignment operates at an intermediate granularity naturally suited to code changes. Trained on SWE-Gym and evaluated on four benchmarks, BoostAPR achieves 40.7% on SWE-bench Verified (+22.9pp over base model), 24.8% on Defects4J (Python-to-Java transfer), 84.5% on HumanEval-Java, and 95.0% on QuixBugs, achieving competitive results among open-source models with strong cross-language generalization.

AI.tldr: BoostAPR introduces a three-stage reinforcement learning framework for program repair with line-level credit assignment, achieving strong performance across multiple benchmarks including SWE-bench, Defects4J, HumanEval-Java, and QuixBugs.
AI.motivation: Reinforcement learning for program repair faces challenges with sparse execution feedback and coarse sequence-level rewards that obscure which specific edits actually fix bugs, making it difficult to learn effective repair strategies.
AI.method: Three-stage framework: (1) Supervised fine-tuning on execution-verified demonstrations with reasoning traces, (2) Training dual reward models (sequence-level assessor and line-level credit allocator) from execution outcomes, (3) PPO optimization where line-level model redistributes rewards to critical edit regions for more granular feedback.
AI.result: Achieves 40.7% on SWE-bench Verified (+22.9 percentage points over base model), 24.8% on Defects4J (Python-to-Java transfer), 84.5% on HumanEval-Java, and 95.0% on QuixBugs, demonstrating competitive results among open-source models with strong cross-language generalization.
AI.conclusion: The proposed BoostAPR framework successfully addresses the sparse reward problem in program repair through line-level credit assignment, enabling more effective learning from execution feedback and achieving strong performance across diverse benchmarks while demonstrating robust cross-language transfer capabilities.

title: SDG-MoE: Signed Debate Graph Mixture-of-Experts
summary: Sparse MoE models achieve a good balance between capacity and compute by routing each token to a small subset of experts. However, in most MoE architectures, once a token is routed, the selected experts process it independently and their outputs are combined via a weighted sum. This leaves open whether enabling communication among them could improve performance. While prior work has raised this question, direct interaction among the active routed experts remains underexplored. In this paper, we propose SDG-MoE (Signed Debate Graph Mixture-of-Experts), a novel architecture that adds a lightweight, iterative deliberation step before final aggregation. SDG-MoE introduces three components: (i) two learned interaction matrices over the active experts, a support graph $A^+$ and a critique graph $A^-$, capturing reinforcing and corrective influences; (ii) a signed message-passing step that updates expert representations before aggregation; and (iii) a disagreement-gated Friedkin-Johnsen-style anchoring that controls deliberation strength while preventing expert drift. Together, these enable a structured deliberation process where interaction strength scales with disagreement and specialization is preserved. We also provide a theoretical analysis establishing stability conditions on expert states and showing that deliberation adds only low-order overhead over the active set. In controlled three-seed pretraining experiments, SDG-MoE improves validation perplexity over both an unsigned graph communication baseline and vanilla MoE, outperforming the strongest baseline by 19.8%, and gives the best external perplexity on WikiText-103, C4, and Paloma among the compared systems.

AI.tldr: SDG-MoE introduces a signed debate graph mechanism where routed experts communicate through support and critique interactions before final output aggregation, improving performance over standard MoE architectures.
AI.motivation: Current sparse MoE models process tokens independently after routing, missing potential performance gains from enabling communication among active experts. The paper investigates whether structured deliberation among routed experts can improve model capabilities.
AI.method: Proposes SDG-MoE with three components: (1) two learned interaction matrices (support graph A⁺ and critique graph A⁻) for expert communication, (2) signed message-passing to update expert representations, and (3) disagreement-gated Friedkin-Johnsen-style anchoring to control deliberation strength while preserving specialization.
AI.result: In 3-seed pretraining experiments, SDG-MoE improves validation perplexity by 19.8% over the strongest baseline, achieving best external perplexity on WikiText-103, C4, and Paloma datasets compared to unsigned graph communication and vanilla MoE.
AI.conclusion: Enabling structured deliberation among active experts through signed debate graphs significantly improves MoE performance with low computational overhead, demonstrating the value of expert communication in sparse routing architectures.

title: Do LLMs Experience an Internal Polylogue? Investigating Reasoning through the Lens of Personas
summary: Recent work shows that large language models (LLMs) encode behavioural traits ("personas") as linear directions in activation space, often called "persona vectors". Prior work has used such directions as static handles for behavioural steering. Building on this, we treat them as dynamic signals instead: probes we can monitor and intervene on as reasoning unfolds. We use the term polylogue to denote the time series of alignments between persona vectors and hidden activations over the course of generation. Experiments across four open-weight models show that polylogue features predict correctness on MMLU-Pro competitively with low-dimensional activation baselines, while remaining interpretable through their associated persona directions. They also suggest concrete steering targets, namely which latent directions to modulate at different stages of a response. We instantiate this as a simple paragraph-conditioned intervention that improves accuracy on three of four models, pointing to stage-aware latent steering as a promising direction for reasoning-time control. Together, this positions the polylogue as an interpretable tool for reasoning-time monitoring and intervention.

AI.tldr: Polylogues (persona vector alignments over time) provide interpretable monitoring and intervention targets for improving LLM reasoning through stage-aware latent steering.
AI.motivation: Prior work uses persona vectors as static handles for behavioral steering, but this approach treats them as dynamic signals that can be monitored and intervened on as reasoning unfolds to better control model behavior.
AI.method: Introduces "polylogues" - time series of alignments between persona vectors and hidden activations over generation. Uses these features to predict correctness on MMLU-Pro and identifies specific latent directions to modulate at different reasoning stages through paragraph-conditioned interventions.
AI.result: Polylogue features predict MMLU-Pro correctness competitively with low-dimensional activation baselines while remaining interpretable. A simple paragraph-conditioned intervention improves accuracy on three of four tested models.
AI.conclusion: Polylogues serve as an interpretable tool for reasoning-time monitoring and intervention, pointing to stage-aware latent steering as a promising direction for reasoning-time control in LLMs.

title: The Reciprocity Gradient
summary: Communication is fundamental to sustaining reciprocity and cooperation in strategic interactions. We identify and formulate the influence attribution problem as the central optimization difficulty inherent in such dynamics for a learning agent: any action or signal the agent emits reshapes the reputations of many third parties along combinatorially branching paths before feeding back into its own future rewards, forcing the agent to account for all of these indirect channels at once when choosing every action. To address this, we introduce the reciprocity gradient, which explicitly backpropagates reward gradients through private estimators of opponents' policies trained from public observations. The gradient flows through the reputation chain itself analytically, rather than being estimated from sampled returns. It jointly optimizes actions and evaluative signals without intrinsic rewards or reward shaping. Empirically, the method recovers near-optimal context-sensitive policies, while sample-based baselines collapse into constant-output policies.

AI.tldr: Introduces reciprocity gradient method that backpropagates reward gradients through opponents' policy estimators to solve influence attribution problem in strategic interactions, enabling learning of context-sensitive policies without reward shaping.
AI.motivation: Communication is essential for sustaining reciprocity and cooperation in strategic interactions, but poses an optimization challenge called "influence attribution problem" - each action/signal affects reputations of many third parties along combinatorially branching paths before feeding back to the agent's own rewards.
AI.method: Introduces "reciprocity gradient" that explicitly backpropagates reward gradients through private estimators of opponents' policies trained from public observations. The gradient flows through the reputation chain analytically rather than being estimated from sampled returns, jointly optimizing actions and evaluative signals without intrinsic rewards or reward shaping.
AI.result: Empirically, the method recovers near-optimal context-sensitive policies, while sample-based baselines collapse into constant-output policies that fail to adapt to different contexts.
AI.conclusion: The reciprocity gradient provides an effective solution to the influence attribution problem in strategic learning by analytically tracing how actions affect reputations and future rewards, enabling agents to learn sophisticated communication strategies that sustain cooperation and reciprocity.

title: FORTIS: Benchmarking Over-Privilege in Agent Skills
summary: Large language model agents increasingly operate through an intermediate skill layer that mediates between user intent and concrete task execution. This layer is widely treated as an organizational abstraction, but we argue it is also a privilege boundary that current models routinely exceed. We present \textbf{FORTIS}, a benchmark that evaluates over-privilege in agent skills across two stages: whether a model selects the minimally sufficient skill from a large overlapping library, and whether it executes that skill without expanding into broader tools or actions than the skill permits. Across ten frontier models and three domains, we find that over-privileged behavior is the norm rather than the exception. Models consistently reach for higher-privilege skills and tools than the task requires, failing at both stages at rates that remain high even for the strongest available models. Failure is especially severe under the ordinary conditions of real user interaction: incomplete specification, convenience framing, and proximity to skill boundaries. None of these requires adversarial construction. The results indicate that the skill layer, far from containing agent behavior, is itself a primary source of privilege escalation in current systems.

AI.tldr: LLM agents often over-select high-privilege skills and tools even when minimal privileges would suffice, revealing the skill layer itself as a source of privilege escalation rather than containment.
AI.motivation: Current large language model agents use a skill layer as an organizational abstraction, but this layer is also a privilege boundary that models routinely exceed. The authors aim to demonstrate that over-privilege in agent skills is common, not exceptional.
AI.method: The authors present FORTIS, a benchmark that evaluates over-privilege across two stages: 1) whether models select minimally sufficient skills from overlapping libraries, and 2) whether they execute skills without expanding into broader tools/actions than permitted. They test ten frontier models across three domains under ordinary user interaction conditions.
AI.result: Across ten frontier models and three domains, over-privileged behavior is the norm rather than exception. Models consistently select higher-privilege skills/tools than necessary, failing at both selection and execution stages even for strongest models. Failure is especially severe under ordinary conditions like incomplete specification, convenience framing, and proximity to skill boundaries.
AI.conclusion: The skill layer, far from containing agent behavior, is itself a primary source of privilege escalation in current LLM agent systems. This occurs under normal usage conditions without adversarial construction, highlighting a fundamental security vulnerability in agent architectures.

title: LLM Advertisement based on Neuron Auctions
summary: As Large Language Models (LLMs) transition into conversational agents, generative advertising emerges as a crucial monetization strategy. However, embedding advertisements within unstructured LLM outputs introduces a critical trilemma: balancing advertiser payoffs, platform revenue, and user experience. Existing methods, such as prompt injection or rigid position slots, disrupt semantic coherence and lack a parametric framework for independent control, rendering rigorous mechanism design intractable. To bridge this gap, we introduce Neuron Auctions, a novel paradigm that shifts the auction object from the surface text space to the LLM's internal representations. Leveraging mechanistic interpretability, we identify brand-specific feed-forward network (FFN) neurons and demonstrate that competing brands activate within approximately orthogonal subspaces. This near-perfect independence allows us to define continuous, disentangled intervention budgets (specifically, neuron counts and amplification factors) as auctionable commodities. Building on this computational carrier, we design a continuous menu-based auction mechanism that naturally guarantees strategy-proofness and optimizes revenue for the platform. By explicitly incorporating a user utility penalty into the platform's optimization objective, our framework dynamically prices out overly aggressive interventions. Extensive experiments demonstrate that Neuron Auctions effectively preserve natural discourse quality while achieving an optimal alignment between commercial incentives and user satisfaction.

AI.tldr: Neuron Auctions is a novel framework that auctions access to LLM internal representations (brand-specific neurons) instead of text space, enabling continuous control over advertising interventions while balancing advertiser payoffs, platform revenue, and user experience.
AI.motivation: Existing advertising methods for LLMs (prompt injection or rigid slots) disrupt semantic coherence and lack parametric control, making mechanism design difficult. There's a critical trilemma between advertiser payoffs, platform revenue, and user experience when embedding ads in unstructured LLM outputs.
AI.method: Uses mechanistic interpretability to identify brand-specific feed-forward network (FFN) neurons. Discovers competing brands activate in approximately orthogonal subspaces. Defines continuous, disentangled intervention budgets (neuron counts and amplification factors) as auctionable commodities. Designs a continuous menu-based auction mechanism that guarantees strategy-proofness.
AI.result: Extensive experiments show Neuron Auctions effectively preserve natural discourse quality while achieving optimal alignment between commercial incentives and user satisfaction. The framework dynamically prices out overly aggressive interventions by incorporating user utility penalties.
AI.conclusion: Neuron Auctions represent a paradigm shift from surface text auctioning to internal representation auctioning, enabling rigorous mechanism design that balances the trilemma of advertiser payoffs, platform revenue, and user experience in generative advertising for LLMs.

title: CIVeX: Causal Intervention Verification for Language Agents
comment: 16 pages, 3 figures. Includes Causal-ToolBench, IHDP, ZOZO Open Bandit, and LaLonde NSW evaluations
summary:
A valid tool call is not necessarily a valid intervention. Tool-using language agents are guarded by schema validators, policy filters, provenance checks, state predictors, and self-verification, yet such safeguards do not certify that a state-changing action has an identifiable causal effect. In confounded workflows, the action that looks optimal in observational logs can reduce utility when executed.
  We introduce CIVeX, a causal intervention verifier that maps proposed actions to structural causal queries over a committed action-state graph, checks identifiability, and returns one of four auditable verdicts: EXECUTE, REJECT, EXPERIMENT, or ABSTAIN. Execution requires an assumption-scoped causal certificate carrying graph commitments, an identification argument, a one-sided lower confidence bound (LCB), provenance, and risk limits.
  On Causal-ToolBench (1,890 instances, 7 seeds), CIVeX yields zero observed false executions across moderate and adversarial confounding. Under adversarial confounding it reaches 84.9% accuracy and 81.1% of oracle utility (+2.23 vs +2.76) and is the only non-oracle method whose constrained utility under a zero-false-execution constraint exceeds the AlwaysAbstain floor. On IHDP and ZOZO Open Bandit (real production logs with uniform-random ground truth), CIVeX matches Oracle correct-execution within 0.1pp and cuts per-execute false-execution by >=50x over naive baselines. A chain-of-thought LLM verifier (Claude Opus, Sonnet) cuts false-execution by an order of magnitude over a terse baseline, yet under adversarial confounding Opus's utility falls to 74% of CIVeX's. Intervention identifiability, not action validity, is the missing primitive for reliable tool use.

AI.tldr: CIVeX is a causal intervention verification system for tool-using AI agents that ensures actions have identifiable causal effects before execution, preventing harmful interventions in confounded workflows.
AI.motivation: Current tool-using language agents have various safeguards (schema validators, policy filters, provenance checks, etc.) but these don't certify that state-changing actions have identifiable causal effects. In confounded workflows, actions that appear optimal from observational data can actually reduce utility when executed, creating a reliability gap.
AI.method: CIVeX maps proposed actions to structural causal queries over a committed action-state graph, checks identifiability, and returns one of four auditable verdicts (EXECUTE, REJECT, EXPERIMENT, or ABSTAIN). Execution requires a comprehensive causal certificate with graph commitments, identification arguments, lower confidence bounds, provenance, and risk limits.
AI.result: On Causal-ToolBench (1,890 instances, 7 seeds), CIVeX achieves zero observed false executions across moderate and adversarial confounding. It reaches 84.9% accuracy and 81.1% of oracle utility under adversarial confounding. On real datasets (IHDP and ZOZO Open Bandit), it matches Oracle correct-execution within 0.1 percentage point and reduces false-execution by ≥50x compared to naive baselines.
AI.conclusion: Intervention identifiability, not just action validity, is the critical missing primitive for reliable tool use. CIVeX demonstrates that formal causal reasoning is necessary for safe AI tool execution, significantly outperforming even sophisticated LLM-based verifiers (Claude Opus achieves only 74% of CIVeX's utility under adversarial confounding).

title: Interactive Critique-Revision Training for Reliable Structured LLM Generation
summary: In structured decision-making workflows such as form filling, compliance checking, and maintenance reporting, LLM outputs must be locally correct, globally consistent, and auditable against task-specific rules. Existing refinement methods often rely on heuristic debate, self-play, or LLM-generated supervision, creating a second-order assurance problem. We propose DPA-GRPO (Dual Paired-Action Group-Relative Policy Optimization), a paired-action training method for a two-player generator--verifier game with structured verifier interventions. The generator proposes outputs and may revise them when challenged; the verifier either remains silent or raises a safety assurance case (SAC) containing a claim, argument, and evidence. These SAC/no-SAC and KEEP/REVISE decisions induce paired counterfactual action groups, which DPA-GRPO uses for role-specific KL-regularized GRPO updates. We analyze the unregularized game and show that positive probability on strictly lower-reward intervention or revision actions creates a profitable unilateral deviation. Under standard stochastic-approximation assumptions, DPA-GRPO tracks the corresponding game ODE, whose isolated asymptotically stable limit points are stationary and candidate local equilibria under role-wise local optimality. Experiments on TaxCalcBench TY24 show that DPA-GRPO improves structured decision accuracy over zero-shot generation and generator-only RL baselines across Qwen3-4B and Qwen3-8B. Training increases correct silent acceptance, reduces missed errors, and improves calibrated revision behavior, indicating gains for both generator and verifier.

AI.tldr: DPA-GRPO is a paired-action training method for two-player generator-verifier games that improves structured decision-making accuracy through safety assurance cases and counterfactual action groups.
AI.motivation: Existing LLM refinement methods for structured decision-making workflows often rely on heuristic debate or LLM-generated supervision, creating a "second-order assurance problem" where the verification process itself needs verification. There's a need for methods that ensure local correctness, global consistency, and auditability against task-specific rules.
AI.method: DPA-GRPO (Dual Paired-Action Group-Relative Policy Optimization) is a paired-action training method for a two-player generator-verifier game. The generator proposes outputs and can revise when challenged, while the verifier either remains silent or raises a safety assurance case (SAC) containing claim, argument, and evidence. These decisions create paired counterfactual action groups used for role-specific KL-regularized GRPO updates.
AI.result: Experiments on TaxCalcBench TY24 show DPA-GRPO improves structured decision accuracy over zero-shot generation and generator-only RL baselines across Qwen3-4B and Qwen3-8B. Training increases correct silent acceptance, reduces missed errors, and improves calibrated revision behavior, indicating gains for both generator and verifier.
AI.conclusion: DPA-GRPO provides an effective framework for training generator-verifier pairs in structured decision-making tasks, addressing the assurance problem through formal game-theoretic analysis and practical training methods that lead to improved performance in both roles.

title: Open Ontologies: Tool-Augmented Ontology Engineering with Stable Matching Alignment
summary: We present Open Ontologies, an open-source ontology engineering system implemented in Rust that integrates LLM-driven construction with formal OWL reasoning and ontology alignment via the Model Context Protocol. Our primary finding is that stable 1-to-1 matching is the dominant factor in ontology alignment quality: on the OAEI Anatomy track, it achieves F1 = 0.832 (P = 0.963, R = 0.733), competitive with state-of-the-art systems and exceeding all in precision. Ablation across five weight configurations shows that signal weights are irrelevant when stable matching is applied (F1 varies by less than 0.004), while removing stable matching drops F1 to 0.728. On the Conference track, the same method achieves F1 = 0.438. On tool-augmented ontology interaction, we find a surprising result: an LLM reading a raw OWL file (F1 = 0.323) performs worse than the same LLM with no file at all (F1 = 0.431), while structured MCP tool access achieves F1 = 0.717. This demonstrates that tool structure provides a qualitatively different mode of access that the LLM cannot replicate by reading raw syntax. The system ships as a single binary under the MIT licence.

AI.tldr: Open Ontologies is a Rust-based system combining LLM-driven ontology construction with formal OWL reasoning and Model Context Protocol alignment, finding stable 1-to-1 matching is crucial for alignment quality and that structured tool access outperforms LLMs reading raw OWL files.
AI.motivation: To create an open-source ontology engineering system that integrates modern LLM capabilities with formal ontology reasoning and alignment techniques, addressing the challenge of combining neural and symbolic approaches in ontology engineering.
AI.method: Implemented in Rust as a single binary, integrates LLM-driven construction with formal OWL reasoning and ontology alignment via Model Context Protocol (MCP). Uses stable 1-to-1 matching for ontology alignment and evaluates performance on OAEI Anatomy and Conference tracks with ablation studies.
AI.result: Stable 1-to-1 matching achieves F1 = 0.832 (P = 0.963, R = 0.733) on OAEI Anatomy track, competitive with state-of-the-art. Ablation shows signal weights irrelevant with stable matching (F1 varies <0.004), but removing stable matching drops F1 to 0.728. On Conference track: F1 = 0.438. For LLM interaction: reading raw OWL files yields F1 = 0.323 (worse than no file at F1 = 0.431), while structured MCP access achieves F1 = 0.717.
AI.conclusion: Stable 1-to-1 matching is the dominant factor in ontology alignment quality, and structured tool access provides qualitatively different capabilities that LLMs cannot replicate by reading raw syntax alone, demonstrating the value of integrated symbolic-neural approaches in ontology engineering.

title: P-Flow: Proxy-gradient Flows for Linear Inverse Problems
summary: Generative models based on flow matching have emerged as a powerful paradigm for inverse problems, offering straighter trajectories and faster sampling compared to diffusion models. However, existing approaches often necessitate differentiating through unrolled paths, leading to numerical instability and prohibitive computational overhead. To address this, we propose P-Flow, a framework that stabilizes the reconstruction process by leveraging a proxy gradient to update the source point. This approach effectively circumvents the numerical instability and memory overhead of long-chain differentiation. To ensure consistency with the prior distribution, we employ a Gaussian spherical projection motivated by the concentration of measure phenomenon in high-dimensional spaces. We further provide a theoretical analysis for P-Flow based on Bayesian theory and Lipschitz continuity. Experiments across diverse restoration tasks demonstrate that P-Flow delivers competitive performance, especially under extreme degradations such as severely ill-posed conditions and high measurement noise.

AI.tldr: P-Flow stabilizes flow matching for inverse problems by using proxy gradients and Gaussian spherical projection, avoiding unstable long-chain differentiation while maintaining competitive performance.
AI.motivation: Current flow matching approaches for inverse problems suffer from numerical instability and prohibitive computational overhead due to differentiating through unrolled paths during reconstruction.
AI.method: Proposed P-Flow framework stabilizes reconstruction using proxy gradients to update source points, avoiding long-chain differentiation. Employs Gaussian spherical projection based on concentration of measure phenomenon for prior distribution consistency, with theoretical analysis based on Bayesian theory and Lipschitz continuity.
AI.result: Experiments across diverse restoration tasks show competitive performance, especially under extreme degradations including severely ill-posed conditions and high measurement noise.
AI.conclusion: P-Flow effectively addresses numerical instability and computational overhead in flow matching for inverse problems while maintaining strong performance, particularly in challenging degradation scenarios.

title: Agentic MIP Research: Accelerated Constraint Handler Generation
summary: Mixed-integer programming (MIP) research is both mathematically sophisticated and engineering-intensive: testing an algorithmic hypothesis within a branch-and-cut solver requires substantial implementation, debugging, tuning, and large-scale benchmarking. We propose an agentic MIP research framework that shortens this feedback loop by embedding LLM agents into a solver-aware harness for generating, verifying, and evaluating plugins for the open-source solver SCIP. Propagation methods play a central role in accelerating MIP solving by exploiting global constraints. We instantiate our framework on the semantic lifting of MIP formulations into global constraints and the automatic construction of propagation-only SCIP constraint handlers. On the MIPLIB 2017 benchmark set, the framework successfully recovers global constraint structures from constraint programming and generates executable constraint detectors and propagation-only constraint handlers. Furthermore, the framework naturally extends to in-context learning within a sandboxed environment, enabling agents not only to tune and debug generated constraint handlers on real instances, but also to explore global constraint patterns in MIP problems and discover novel propagation strategies not yet implemented in SCIP. This framework allows us to systematically distinguish meaningful algorithmic improvements from low-value or overly costly candidates: the novel propagation methods successfully solved five additional instances within the explored benchmark. Overall, this framework demonstrates that LLM agents can autonomously navigate the complex MIP research loop, paving the way for a more automated solver development process.

AI.tldr: LLM agents are embedded into SCIP solver to automatically generate, verify, and evaluate constraint propagation plugins, demonstrating they can autonomously navigate MIP research loop and discover novel propagation strategies that solve additional benchmark instances.
AI.motivation: MIP research requires substantial implementation, debugging, tuning, and benchmarking effort, creating a long feedback loop for testing algorithmic hypotheses. The paper aims to shorten this loop by automating parts of the research process.
AI.method: Propose an agentic MIP research framework that embeds LLM agents into a solver-aware harness for SCIP. The framework automatically constructs propagation-only constraint handlers by semantically lifting MIP formulations into global constraints, and enables in-context learning in sandboxed environments for tuning, debugging, and exploring novel propagation strategies.
AI.result: The framework successfully recovers global constraint structures from constraint programming and generates executable constraint detectors and propagation-only constraint handlers. LLM agents discovered novel propagation strategies not yet implemented in SCIP, and the novel methods solved five additional instances within the MIPLIB 2017 benchmark set.
AI.conclusion: LLM agents can autonomously navigate the complex MIP research loop, distinguishing meaningful algorithmic improvements from low-value candidates, and paving the way for more automated solver development processes while demonstrating practical impact on benchmark performance.

title: CDS4RAG: Cyclic Dual-Sequential Hyperparameter Optimization for RAG
comment: Accepted by main track at IJCAI 2026
summary: Retrieval-Augmented Generation (RAG) is sensitive to the vast hyperparameters of the retriever and generator, yet optimizing them using given queries is a challenging task due to the complex interactions and expensive evaluation costs. Existing algorithms are ineffective and slow in convergence, since they often treat RAG as a monolithic black box or only optimize partial hyperparameters. In this paper, we propose CDS4RAG, a framework that optimizes the full RAG hyperparameters using given queries via a new cyclic dual-sequential formulation. CDS4RAG is special in the sense that it distinguishes the hyperparameters of the retriever and generator, cyclically optimizing them in turn. Such a paradigm allows us to design fine-grained within-cycle budget provision and expedite the optimization via cross-cycle seeding when optimizing the generator. CDS4RAG is also an algorithm-agnostic framework that can be paired with diverse general algorithms. Through experiments on four common benchmarks and two backbone LLMs, we reveal that CDS4RAG considerably boosts the vanilla algorithms in 21/24 cases while significantly outperforming state-of-the-art algorithms in all cases with up to 1.54x improvements of generation quality and better speedup.

AI.tldr: CDS4RAG is a framework that optimizes full RAG hyperparameters using given queries via cyclic dual-sequential optimization of retriever and generator parameters separately, achieving significant improvements in generation quality and speed.
AI.motivation: RAG systems are sensitive to many hyperparameters of both retriever and generator components, but optimizing them is challenging due to complex interactions and expensive evaluation costs. Existing algorithms are ineffective and slow because they treat RAG as a monolithic black box or only optimize partial hyperparameters.
AI.method: CDS4RAG uses a cyclic dual-sequential formulation that distinguishes between retriever and generator hyperparameters, optimizing them cyclically in turn. It features fine-grained within-cycle budget provision and expedites optimization via cross-cycle seeding for generator optimization. The framework is algorithm-agnostic and can be paired with diverse general algorithms.
AI.result: Experiments on four common benchmarks and two backbone LLMs show CDS4RAG boosts vanilla algorithms in 21/24 cases and significantly outperforms state-of-the-art algorithms in all cases with up to 1.54x improvements in generation quality and better speedup.
AI.conclusion: CDS4RAG effectively addresses RAG hyperparameter optimization challenges by separating retriever and generator optimization in a cyclic fashion, achieving superior performance and efficiency compared to existing methods.

title: Emergent Semantic Role Understanding in Language Models
summary: Understanding how linguistic structure emerges in language models is central to interpreting what these systems learn from data and how much supervision they truly require. In particular, semantic role understanding ("who did what to whom") is a core component of meaning representation, yet it remains unclear whether it arises from pre-training alone or depends on task-specific fine-tuning. We study whether semantic role understanding emerges during language model pre-training or requires task-specific fine-tuning. We freeze decoder-only transformers and train linear probes to extract semantic roles, using performance to infer whether role information is already encoded in pre-training or learned during adaptation. Across model scales, we find that frozen representations contain substantial semantic role information, with performance improving but not fully matching fine-tuned models. This indicates partial but incomplete emergence from pre-training alone. We show that semantic role structure emerges from language modeling objectives, but its internal implementation shifts toward more distributed representations as model scale increases.

AI.tldr: Frozen transformer representations contain substantial semantic role information that emerges during pre-training alone, though not matching fine-tuned performance, with role structure becoming more distributed at larger scales.
AI.motivation: Understanding whether core semantic role understanding ("who did what to whom") emerges naturally from language model pre-training or requires task-specific fine-tuning is crucial for interpreting what these models learn and how much supervision they truly need.
AI.method: Freeze decoder-only transformers and train linear probes on their frozen representations to extract semantic roles, using performance to infer whether role information is already encoded during pre-training or learned during adaptation.
AI.result: Frozen representations contain substantial semantic role information across model scales, with performance improving but not fully matching fine-tuned models. Semantic role structure emerges from language modeling objectives, but its internal implementation shifts toward more distributed representations as model scale increases.
AI.conclusion: Semantic role understanding partially emerges from pre-training alone, indicating that core meaning representation capabilities develop through language modeling objectives without explicit supervision, though implementation becomes more distributed with scale.

title: Private Vertical Federated Inference for Time-Series
summary: Institutions may benefit from collaborative inference on time-series data. In settings where privacy is necessary, multi-party computation (MPC) is a straightforward approach to providing strong guarantees, yet it remains prohibitively expensive and scales poorly with modern transformer architectures. Vertical Federated Learning (VFL) offers efficiency but suffers from privacy leakage at the embedding level, and securing the entire VFL model head via MPC remains prohibitively slow and communication-heavy for larger models. To enable practical, secure inference at scale, we propose "Public/Private Hybrid Head-VFL" (PPHH-VFL). This hybrid architecture splits the model head into an efficient plaintext public head and a secure, lightweight MPC private head. By applying adversarial training to the public embeddings, we mitigate privacy leakage; concurrently, the small private head securely preserves the flow of sensitive information needed for high downstream utility. Empirical evaluations on models ranging up to 86 million parameters demonstrate that PPHH-VFL accelerates inference by up to six orders of magnitude compared to end-to-end MPC. Compared to a standard VFL+MPC baseline, our approach scales significantly better, achieving a speedup of up to 44.4x in WAN and a 91.2x reduction in communication costs (dropping from 1.7 GB to 19 MB per batch), while simultaneously improving downstream classification accuracy by 2.50% and regression RMSE by 40.7%.

AI.tldr: PPHH-VFL is a hybrid architecture that combines public plaintext and private MPC heads for secure, scalable inference on time-series data, achieving massive speedups over end-to-end MPC while maintaining privacy and utility.
AI.motivation: Current privacy-preserving inference methods face significant trade-offs: MPC provides strong privacy but is prohibitively expensive and scales poorly for large models, while VFL is efficient but suffers from privacy leakage at the embedding level. There's a need for practical, scalable secure inference that maintains both privacy and utility.
AI.method: Proposes Public/Private Hybrid Head-VFL (PPHH-VFL) that splits the model head into two components: an efficient plaintext public head and a secure, lightweight MPC private head. Uses adversarial training on public embeddings to mitigate privacy leakage while the private head securely preserves sensitive information flow for downstream utility.
AI.result: Achieves up to 6 orders of magnitude speedup over end-to-end MPC, 44.4x faster than VFL+MPC baseline in WAN, 91.2x reduction in communication costs (1.7 GB to 19 MB per batch), while improving classification accuracy by 2.50% and regression RMSE by 40.7% on models up to 86M parameters.
AI.conclusion: PPHH-VFL enables practical, scalable secure inference by dramatically improving efficiency while maintaining privacy and utility, making privacy-preserving collaborative inference feasible for real-world applications with large transformer models.

title: Evidence Over Plans: Online Trajectory Verification for Skill Distillation
summary: Agent skills can remarkably improve task success rates by using human-written procedural documents, but their quality is difficult to assess without environment-grounded verification. Existing skill generation methods heavily rely on preference logs rather than direct environment interaction, often yielding negligible or even degraded gains. We identify that it is a fundamental timing bottleneck: robust skills should be posterior-based, distilled from empirical environment interaction rather than prior plans. In this study, we introduce the Posterior Distillation Index (PDI), a trajectory-level metric that quantifies how well a distilled skill is grounded in the task-environment evidence. To operationalize PDI, we present SPARK (Structured Pipelines for Autonomous Runnable tasKs and sKill generation) for preserving task execution evidence towards full trajectory-level analysis. SPARK generates environment-verified trajectories used to compute PDI, and it applies PDI as an online diagnostic and intervention signal to ensure posterior skill formation. Across 86 runnable tasks, SPARK-generated skills consistently surpass no-skill baselines and outperform human-written skills on student models (inference cost up to 1,000x cheaper than teacher models). These findings show that PDI-guided distillation produces efficient and transferable skills grounded in the task-environment interaction. We release our code at https://github.com/EtaYang10th/spark-skills .

AI.tldr: SPARK introduces a posterior distillation approach for generating environment-grounded agent skills using a novel PDI metric, achieving better performance than human-written skills with much lower inference costs.
AI.motivation: Current skill generation methods rely too heavily on preference logs rather than direct environment interaction, often resulting in negligible or degraded performance gains. There's a fundamental timing bottleneck where robust skills should be posterior-based (distilled from empirical environment interaction) rather than prior-based (from human-written plans).
AI.method: Introduces the Posterior Distillation Index (PDI), a trajectory-level metric quantifying how well a skill is grounded in task-environment evidence. Presents SPARK (Structured Pipelines for Autonomous Runnable tasKs and sKill generation) which generates environment-verified trajectories to compute PDI and uses PDI as an online diagnostic and intervention signal for posterior skill formation.
AI.result: Across 86 runnable tasks, SPARK-generated skills consistently surpass no-skill baselines and outperform human-written skills on student models, achieving up to 1,000x cheaper inference costs compared to teacher models.
AI.conclusion: PDI-guided distillation produces efficient and transferable skills that are properly grounded in task-environment interaction, demonstrating the importance of posterior-based skill formation over prior plan-based approaches.

title: What Time Is It? How Data Geometry Makes Time Conditioning Optional for Flow Matching
summary: Recent work has shown that models flow matching models can be trained without explicit time conditioning, challenging the standard view that the interpolation time is needed to disambiguate velocity targets. But why should a time-blind model work at all? Decomposing the time-blind flow matching loss, we identify two sources of irreducible error: a coupling variance, which arises from ambiguous velocity targets induced by how noise and data points are paired, and the time-blindness gap, which is the additional error caused by ignoring time. This gap shows that time-blind training is strictly harder than conventional training, reinforcing the puzzle that time-blind models work so well in practice. We resolve this tension by showing that the geometry of high-dimensional data makes time identifiable directly from noisy observations. When data concentrates near a $k$-dimensional subspace, time can be recovered from the statistical structure of noisy interpolants in directions orthogonal to the data; under a spiked-covariance model, this yields a closed-form estimator that recovers $t$ from a single observation $z$ at rate $O(1/\sqrt{d-k})$ for ambient dimension $d$. As a consequence, we prove that the time-blindness gap is asymptotically negligible relative to the coupling variance. We empirically demonstrate our identifiability result on real-world data and show that changing the coupling has a much larger effect on loss and sample quality than removing time conditioning across CIFAR-10, CelebA-HQ, and FFHQ. These results explain why time-blind flow matching works and show that the main practical lever is the choice of coupling, not explicit time conditioning.

AI.tldr: Time-blind flow matching works because time can be identified from noisy observations in high dimensions, making time conditioning unnecessary in practice.
AI.motivation: To understand why time-blind flow matching models work well despite the theoretical prediction that ignoring time should create additional error, challenging the standard view that explicit time conditioning is essential.
AI.method: Decompose time-blind flow matching loss into coupling variance and time-blindness gap, analyze time identifiability in high dimensions, develop closed-form time estimator under spiked-covariance model, and empirically validate on real datasets.
AI.result: Time can be recovered from noisy interpolants orthogonal to data subspace with rate O(1/√(d-k)), time-blindness gap is asymptotically negligible compared to coupling variance, and coupling choice matters more than time conditioning for performance.
AI.conclusion: Time-blind flow matching works because high-dimensional data geometry makes time identifiable from observations, explaining the empirical success and showing coupling selection is more important than explicit time conditioning.

title: The Geometry of Forgetting: Temporal Knowledge Drift as an Independent Axis in LLM Representations
summary: Large language models confidently produce outdated answers, and no existing method can detect them. We show this is not an engineering failure but a structural one: temporal drift, whether a stored fact has changed since training, is encoded as a direction in the residual stream geometrically orthogonal to both correctness and uncertainty. Any method operating on correctness or uncertainty signals is therefore blind to drift by construction. We verify this across six instruction-tuned models. A linear probe trained directly on drift labels achieves AUROC $0.83$--$0.95$; methods based on token entropy, semantic entropy, CCS, and SAPLMA all remain near chance ($0.49$--$0.57$). Five tests confirm the geometric orthogonality: weight cosines ($|\cos| \leq 0.14$), score correlations ($|r| \leq 0.20$), bidirectional null-space projection ($|Δ| \leq 0.008$), iterative null-space projection with $k{=}10$, and difference-of-means dissociation. Mechanistically, the MLP retrieval circuit produces identical dynamics for stale recall and confabulation ($r > 0.81$, six models), explaining why output confidence cannot separate them. A cross-cutoff experiment holds inputs constant and varies only the model: the probe fires on the model whose training predates the fact's transition and stays silent otherwise ($P(A{>}B) = 0.975$--$0.998$, twelve model pairs), confirming it reads model-internal knowledge state rather than input properties. Our code and datasets will be publicly released.

AI.tldr: Temporal drift in LLMs (outdated facts) is geometrically orthogonal to correctness/uncertainty signals, making current detection methods ineffective; a specialized linear probe can detect drift with high accuracy while traditional methods fail.
AI.motivation: Large language models produce outdated answers, and existing methods (like uncertainty or correctness-based approaches) cannot detect these stale facts. This is a fundamental structural problem rather than just an engineering issue.
AI.method: Show that temporal drift is encoded as an orthogonal direction in the residual stream. Develop a linear probe trained directly on drift labels. Conduct five geometric orthogonality tests (weight cosines, score correlations, null-space projections, iterative null-space projection, difference-of-means dissociation). Analyze MLP retrieval circuit dynamics and conduct cross-cutoff experiments to verify the mechanism.
AI.result: Linear probe achieves AUROC 0.83-0.95 for drift detection, while token entropy, semantic entropy, CCS, and SAPLMA methods remain near chance (0.49-0.57). Geometric orthogonality confirmed across multiple tests. MLP retrieval circuit shows identical dynamics for stale recall and confabulation (r > 0.81). Cross-cutoff experiments confirm probe reads model-internal knowledge state (P(A>B) = 0.975-0.998).
AI.conclusion: Temporal drift detection requires direct modeling of drift as an orthogonal signal; traditional correctness/uncertainty methods are fundamentally limited. The findings reveal a structural limitation in LLMs and provide a practical solution for detecting outdated information.

title: Convergence Analysis of Newton's Method for Neural Networks in the Overparameterized Limit
summary: A convergence analysis is developed for the regularized Newton method for training neural networks (NNs) in the overparameterized limit. As the number of hidden units tends to infinity, the NN training dynamics converge in probability to the solution of a deterministic limit equation involving a ``Newton neural tangent kernel'' (NNTK). Explicit rates characterizing this convergence are provided and, in the infinite-width limit, we prove that the NN converges exponentially fast to the target data (i.e., a global minimizer with zero loss). We show that this convergence is uniform across the frequency spectrum, addressing the spectral bias inherent in gradient descent. The eigenvalues of the NTK for gradient descent accumulate at zero, leading to slow convergence for target data with high-frequency components. In contrast, the NNTK has uniformly lower bounded eigenvalues if the regularization parameter is selected appropriately, allowing Newton's method to converge more quickly for data with high-frequency components. Mathematical challenges that need to be addressed in our analysis include the implicit parameter update of the Newton method with a potentially indefinite Hessian matrix and the fact that the dimension of this linear system of equations tends to infinity as the NN width grows. This complicates deriving the training dynamics in the overparameterized limit as well as proving the convergence of the finite-width dynamics thereto. The analysis identifies a scaling formula for selecting the regularization parameter, which we show can vanish at a suitable rate as the number of hidden units becomes larger. We prove that, for sufficiently large numbers of hidden units, the regularized Hessian remains positive definite during training and the Newton updates for individual NN parameters converge to zero, showing that the model behaves as a linearization around the initialization.

AI.tldr: Regularized Newton method converges exponentially fast to global minimizer for overparameterized neural networks, with uniform spectral convergence overcoming gradient descent's spectral bias.
AI.motivation: Gradient descent suffers from spectral bias where eigenvalues of the NTK accumulate at zero, causing slow convergence for high-frequency components in target data. Newton's method could potentially overcome this, but analyzing it in the overparameterized limit is challenging due to indefinite Hessians and infinite-dimensional linear systems.
AI.method: Develop convergence analysis for regularized Newton method in overparameterized neural networks using a deterministic limit equation involving a "Newton neural tangent kernel" (NNTK). Address challenges with implicit parameter updates, potentially indefinite Hessians, and infinite-dimensional systems as width grows.
AI.result: In the infinite-width limit, neural networks converge exponentially fast to target data (global minimizer with zero loss). NNTK has uniformly lower bounded eigenvalues with proper regularization, enabling faster convergence for high-frequency components. Provides scaling formula for regularization parameter that can vanish as width increases.
AI.conclusion: Regularized Newton method overcomes spectral bias of gradient descent, achieves uniform convergence across frequency spectrum, and behaves as linearization around initialization for sufficiently wide networks.

title: SWE Atlas: Benchmarking Coding Agents Beyond Issue Resolution
summary: We introduce SWE Atlas, a benchmark suite for coding agents spanning three professional software engineering workflows: Codebase Q&A (124 tasks), Test Writing (90 tasks), and Refactoring (70 tasks). SWE Atlas differs from prior SWE benchmarks in three key ways: it targets underrepresented but practically important task categories, uses comprehensive category-specific evaluation protocols, and adopts under-specified, agentic task formulations that better reflect real-world usage. Its evaluation framework combines programmatic checks with rubric-based assessment. This goes beyond functional correctness, evaluating software engineering quality, including test and refactor completeness, maintainability, reusable abstractions, and codebase hygiene. We evaluate a range of frontier and open-weight models on SWE Atlas and find that GPT-5.4 and Opus 4.7 achieve the strongest overall performance, while even the best open-weight models score poorly. Our analysis suggests that top models rely on extensive codebase exploration and runtime-driven reasoning. However, even top models consistently struggle with subtle edge cases, complex runtime analysis, and adherence to software engineering best practices. Overall, SWE Atlas provides a complementary evaluation suite for measuring both correctness and engineering quality in coding agents.

AI.tldr: SWE Atlas is a benchmark suite for coding agents covering three professional software engineering workflows (Codebase Q&A, Test Writing, Refactoring) with comprehensive evaluation protocols that assess both functional correctness and software engineering quality.
AI.motivation: Existing SWE benchmarks don't adequately cover underrepresented but practically important task categories, lack comprehensive evaluation protocols for different task types, and use overly specified task formulations that don't reflect real-world usage scenarios for coding agents.
AI.method: Created a benchmark suite with 284 tasks across three workflows: Codebase Q&A (124 tasks), Test Writing (90 tasks), and Refactoring (70 tasks). Uses category-specific evaluation protocols combining programmatic checks with rubric-based assessment. Features under-specified, agentic task formulations to better simulate real-world usage.
AI.result: GPT-5.4 and Opus 4.7 achieved strongest overall performance, while even the best open-weight models scored poorly. Top models rely on extensive codebase exploration and runtime-driven reasoning but struggle with subtle edge cases, complex runtime analysis, and adherence to software engineering best practices.
AI.conclusion: SWE Atlas provides a complementary evaluation suite that measures both correctness and engineering quality in coding agents, revealing current limitations in handling complex software engineering tasks and highlighting the gap between proprietary and open-weight models.

title: How Much is Brain Data Worth for Machine Learning?
comment: 9 pages main text, 5 figures, 34 pages of appendix with detailed proofs
summary: If a person can solve a task, can measuring their brain make it easier to train a model to solve that task too? Recent NeuroAI work suggests that supplementing task training with neural recordings can modestly improve model performance and robustness. However, it is unclear when there should be a benefit from using neural data and how much benefit to expect. We formulate this question mathematically, and begin to address it theoretically using a simple, analytically tractable linear gaussian model of task targets and neural recordings. For a multimodal estimator trained on both brain data and task labels, we derive scaling laws for how performance scales with the numbers of brain and task samples. From these laws we derive relative value and exchange rates between brain samples and task samples, quantifying how much extra task samples neural data is worth as a function of task-brain alignment, neural and task noise, latent dimension, and brain data sample size. We also analyze test distribution shift, to identify conditions where brain-regularized learning can produce substantial robustness gains through learned invariances. Finally, under a fixed collection budget, we characterize the regimes in which brain data is worth collecting. Our results provide a foundation for understanding how valuable brain data could be for improving machine learning.

AI.tldr: Brain recordings can supplement task training to improve AI model performance, with benefits depending on task-brain alignment, data quality, and collection constraints.
AI.motivation: While NeuroAI research shows neural data can improve model training, it's unclear when and how much benefit to expect from using brain recordings. The paper aims to mathematically quantify the value of neural data for machine learning.
AI.method: The authors use an analytically tractable linear Gaussian model of task targets and neural recordings. They derive scaling laws for multimodal estimators trained on both brain data and task labels, and analyze relative value/exchange rates between brain and task samples.
AI.result: The paper provides mathematical formulas quantifying how much extra task samples neural data is worth, as a function of task-brain alignment, noise levels, latent dimensions, and sample sizes. It also identifies conditions where brain-regularized learning provides robustness gains through learned invariances during distribution shift.
AI.conclusion: Under fixed collection budgets, brain data can be worth collecting in specific regimes. The theoretical framework provides foundation for understanding neural data's potential value for improving machine learning systems.

title: Embedding Dimension Lower Bounds for Universality of Deep Sets and Janossy Pooling
summary: In many practical applications it is important to build symmetries into neural network architectures. Consider the important case of permutation symmetry on point clouds consisting of $n$ points in $d$ dimensions. In this case the network learns a function on a set of $n$ points in $\mathbb{R}^d$, and a natural paradigm for constructing invariant networks is Janossy pooling, which generalizes the popular Deep Sets architecture. We study the universality of this approach, in particular the important question of how large the embedding dimension must be to guarantee universality of this architecture. Specifically, using a novel technique, we prove new lower bounds on the required size of this embedding dimension. For Deep Sets, this gives the correct minimal dimension up to a constant factor for all $d > 1$. For $k$-ary Janossy pooling, we prove the first non-trivial lower bound on the required embedding dimension when $k > 1$.

AI.tldr: Janossy pooling for permutation-invariant neural networks on point clouds requires certain embedding dimensions for universality; new lower bounds are proven for Deep Sets (optimal up to constant factor) and k-ary Janossy pooling (first non-trivial bounds for k>1).
AI.motivation: Building symmetries into neural networks is important for practical applications, especially permutation symmetry on point clouds. While Janossy pooling generalizes Deep Sets to create invariant networks, understanding the required embedding dimension for universality remains an important open question.
AI.method: Using a novel mathematical technique, the authors prove new lower bounds on the required embedding dimension for Janossy pooling architectures to achieve universality. They analyze both Deep Sets (a special case) and k-ary Janossy pooling with k>1.
AI.result: For Deep Sets architecture, they achieve the correct minimal dimension up to a constant factor for all dimensions d>1. For k-ary Janossy pooling with k>1, they prove the first non-trivial lower bound on the required embedding dimension.
AI.conclusion: The paper establishes fundamental limits on how large the embedding dimension must be for Janossy pooling architectures to be universal, providing both optimal results for Deep Sets and pioneering bounds for more general k-ary cases, advancing theoretical understanding of permutation-invariant neural network design.

title: SeePhys Pro: Diagnosing Modality Transfer and Blind-Training Effects in Multimodal RLVR for Physics Reasoning
summary: We introduce SeePhys Pro, a fine-grained modality transfer benchmark that studies whether models preserve the same reasoning capability when critical information is progressively transferred from text to image. Unlike standard vision-essential benchmarks that evaluate a single input form, SeePhys Pro features four semantically aligned variants for each problem with progressively increasing visual elements. Our evaluation shows that current frontier models are far from representation-invariant reasoners: performance degrades on average as information moves from language to diagrams, with visual variable grounding as the most critical bottleneck. Motivated by this inference-time fragility, we further develop large training corpora for multimodal RLVR and use blind training as a diagnostic control, finding that RL with all training images masked can still improve performance on unmasked validation sets. To analyze this effect, text-deletion, image-mask-rate, and format-saturation controls suggest that such gains can arise from residual textual and distributional cues rather than valid visual evidence. Our results highlight the need to evaluate multimodal reasoning not only by final-answer accuracy, but also by robustness under modality transfer and by diagnostics that test whether improvements rely on task-critical visual evidence.

AI.tldr: SeePhys Pro is a fine-grained modality transfer benchmark testing if models maintain reasoning as information shifts from text to images. It reveals current models struggle with visual grounding, and RL training with masked images still improves performance but likely through residual textual cues rather than true visual reasoning.
AI.motivation: Current multimodal reasoning benchmarks often evaluate only single input forms, lacking systematic assessment of whether models maintain consistent reasoning capabilities when the same semantic information is presented through different modality combinations. There's a need to understand if models are truly representation-invariant reasoners and whether performance improvements rely on task-critical visual evidence.
AI.method: Created SeePhys Pro benchmark with four semantically aligned variants for each problem featuring progressively increasing visual elements. Used this to evaluate current frontier models. Developed large multimodal RLVR training corpora and employed blind training as diagnostic control, masking all training images. Conducted text-deletion, image-mask-rate, and format-saturation experiments to analyze performance gains.
AI.result: Current models show significant performance degradation as information moves from language to diagrams, with visual variable grounding identified as the critical bottleneck. Surprisingly, RL training with all training images masked still improves performance on unmasked validation sets, but controls suggest these gains arise from residual textual and distributional cues rather than valid visual evidence.
AI.conclusion: Multimodal reasoning evaluation should consider not just final-answer accuracy but also robustness under modality transfer and diagnostic tests that verify improvements rely on task-critical visual evidence rather than exploiting superficial cues.

title: Reinforcement Learning for Scalable and Trustworthy Intelligent Systems
summary:
Reinforcement learning has become a powerful paradigm for improving the capability of intelligent systems, but its practical deployment faces two central challenges. First, reinforcement learning must scale efficiently in distributed environments where communication bandwidth is limited and computation is heterogeneous across agents. Second, as reinforcement learning is increasingly used in post-training large language models and autonomous agents, the optimized policies must also be aligned with human preferences and satisfy safety requirements such as privacy-aware information disclosure. This dissertation addresses both challenges through four complementary contributions spanning federated optimization, preference alignment, and contextual safety.
  The first part of the dissertation studies scalable reinforcement learning in federated settings. The second part of the dissertation studies trustworthy reinforcement learning for large language models. Together, these contributions advance reinforcement learning along two complementary dimensions. On the one hand, they make reinforcement learning more scalable through communication-efficient and asynchronous federated optimization. On the other hand, they make reinforcement learning more trustworthy by improving alignment with human preferences and by reducing contextually inappropriate information disclosure in language-based intelligent systems. As a whole, this dissertation argues that the next generation of intelligent systems will require both efficient optimization and trustworthy behavior, and that reinforcement learning provides a unifying framework for addressing both goals.

AI.tldr: This dissertation addresses scalability and trustworthiness challenges in RL through federated optimization for distributed settings and preference/safety alignment for LLMs and autonomous agents.
AI.motivation: Two central challenges in practical RL deployment: 1) Scaling efficiently in distributed environments with limited communication and heterogeneous computation, and 2) Ensuring alignment with human preferences and safety requirements like privacy-aware information disclosure for post-training LLMs and autonomous agents.
AI.method: Four complementary contributions spanning federated optimization, preference alignment, and contextual safety. First part focuses on scalable RL in federated settings; second part focuses on trustworthy RL for large language models.
AI.result: Advancements along two dimensions: 1) Making RL more scalable through communication-efficient and asynchronous federated optimization, and 2) Making RL more trustworthy by improving alignment with human preferences and reducing contextually inappropriate information disclosure.
AI.conclusion: Next generation intelligent systems require both efficient optimization and trustworthy behavior, with RL providing a unifying framework for addressing both goals. The dissertation presents an integrated approach to scalable and trustworthy RL.

title: Shaping Schema via Language Representation as the Next Frontier for LLM Intelligence Expanding
summary: Although natural language is the default medium for Large Language Models (LLMs), its limited expressive capacity creates a profound bottleneck for complex problem-solving. While recent advancements in AI have relied heavily on scaling, merely internalizing knowledge does not guarantee its effective application. Defining language representation as the linguistic and symbolic constructs used to map and model the real world, this paper argues that shaping schemas through advanced language representation is the next frontier for expanding LLM intelligence. We posit that an LLM's knowledge activation and organization -- its schema -- depends heavily on the structural and symbolic sophistication of the language used to represent a given task. This paper contributes both a formalization of this claim and the empirical evidence to support it. With a new formalization, we present multiple lines of evidence to support our position: Firstly, we review recent empirical practices and emerging methodologies that demonstrate the substantial performance gains achievable through deliberate language representation design, even without modifying model parameters or scale. Secondly, we conduct controlled experiments showing that LLM performance and its internal feature activations vary under different language representations of the same underlying task. Together, these findings highlight language representation design as a promising direction for future research.

AI.tldr: The paper argues that improving language representation design, not just scaling LLMs, is the key frontier for enhancing their problem-solving capabilities.
AI.motivation: Natural language's limited expressive capacity creates a bottleneck for complex problem-solving in LLMs. While scaling has driven recent AI advancements, merely internalizing knowledge doesn't guarantee effective application. The paper contends that how knowledge is represented and structured (language representation) critically impacts LLM performance.
AI.method: 1. Provides a formalization of the claim that LLM schema depends on language representation sophistication. 2. Reviews recent empirical practices showing performance gains through deliberate language representation design. 3. Conducts controlled experiments measuring LLM performance and internal feature activations under different language representations of the same task.
AI.result: The paper presents empirical evidence supporting its position: 1) Performance gains are achievable through language representation design without modifying model parameters or scale. 2) LLM performance and internal feature activations vary significantly under different language representations of identical tasks.
AI.conclusion: Language representation design represents a promising and underexplored research direction for expanding LLM intelligence, offering an alternative or complement to traditional scaling approaches.

title: The Power of Second Order Methods for Sequence Preconditioning
summary: Sequence prediction methods for dynamical systems with long memory, i.e. marginally stable systems, typically achieve regret that grows polynomially with the hidden dimension of the underlying generative model. Universal Sequence Preconditioning (USP) is a method that compresses any sequence which comes from a linear dynamical system into a "preconditioned" sequence which requires exponentially shorter memory for accurate prediction. However, the preconditioned sequence yields exponentially larger diameters and gradients, hindering USP from unlocking optimal regret bounds. Inspired by the minimum description length principle, we show that the Vovk-Azoury-Warmuth (VAW) algorithm is naturally matched to the USP regime. Indeed, it takes advantage of the memory compression while remaining robust to the exponential explosion of the diameter. We prove that combining USP with VAW achieves astoundingly strong results: for any marginally-stable linear dynamical system, this algorithm achieves polylogarithmic regret $O \left( \log^3 T \right)$ even in the presence of asymmetric hidden transition matrices. Finally, we extend the applicability of USP beyond bounded-spectrum systems by providing new complex-analytic bounds on Chebyshev polynomials, allowing for systems with constant complex arguments.

AI.tldr: USP compresses long-memory linear dynamical systems into sequences with exponentially shorter memory, and combined with VAW algorithm achieves polylogarithmic regret O(log³T) for marginally stable systems.
AI.motivation: Existing sequence prediction methods for dynamical systems with long memory (marginally stable systems) typically suffer from polynomial regret growth with hidden dimension. USP provides memory compression but causes exponential explosion in diameters and gradients, preventing optimal regret bounds.
AI.method: Combine Universal Sequence Preconditioning (USP) with Vovk-Azoury-Warmuth (VAW) algorithm. USP compresses linear dynamical system sequences into preconditioned sequences requiring exponentially shorter memory. VAW algorithm is naturally suited to USP regime as it leverages memory compression while remaining robust to exponential diameter/gradient growth.
AI.result: The combination of USP with VAW achieves polylogarithmic regret O(log³T) for any marginally-stable linear dynamical system, even with asymmetric hidden transition matrices. The paper also extends USP applicability beyond bounded-spectrum systems using new complex-analytic bounds on Chebyshev polynomials.
AI.conclusion: USP+VAW provides a powerful framework for sequence prediction in long-memory dynamical systems, achieving near-optimal regret bounds by overcoming the limitations of USP alone through the robustness properties of the VAW algorithm.

title: Towards Conversational Medical AI with Eyes, Ears and a Voice
comment: Video examples are available on Youtube: https://youtu.be/y5Vaa_SN1t0, https://youtu.be/dC4icb75vLQ, and https://youtu.be/E7iEvWo-E6c
summary: The practice of medicine relies not only upon skillful dialogue but also on the nuanced exchange and interpretation of rich auditory and visual cues between doctors and patients. Building on the low-latency voice and video processing capabilities of Gemini, we introduce AI co-clinician, a first-of-its-kind conversational AI system utilizing continuous streams of audio-visual data from live patient conversations to inform real-time clinical decisions. Its dual-agent architecture balances deep clinical reasoning with the low latency required for natural dialogue. To assess this system, we implemented a video-based interface emulating telemedicine consultations. We crafted 20 standardized outpatient scenarios requiring proactive real-time auditory and visual reasoning and designed "TelePACES" evaluation criteria alongside case-specific rubrics.
