In a randomized, interface-blinded, crossover simulation study (n = 120 encounters) with 10 internal medicine residents as patient actors, we compared AI co-clinician with primary care physicians (PCPs), GPT-Realtime, and a baseline agent. AI co-clinician approached PCPs in key TelePACES dimensions, including management plans and differential diagnosis, while significantly outperforming GPT-Realtime across all general criteria. While our agent demonstrated parity with PCPs in case-specific triage measures, physicians maintained superior overall performance in case-specific assessments. Although AI co-clinician marks a significant advance in real-time telemedical AI, gaps remain in physical examination and disease-specific reasoning. Our work shows that text-only approaches fail to capture the true challenges of medical consultation and suggests that high-stakes real-time diagnostic AI is most safely advanced in collaborative, triadic models where AI can be a supportive co-clinician for doctors and patients.

AI.tldr: AI co-clinician is a real-time conversational AI system using continuous audio-visual data streams from patient conversations to assist clinical decision-making during telemedicine consultations.
AI.motivation: Medical practice relies on rich auditory and visual cues during doctor-patient interactions that text-only AI systems cannot capture, creating a need for AI that can process multimodal data in real-time to support clinical decisions.
AI.method: Developed a dual-agent architecture balancing clinical reasoning with low latency, implemented a video-based telemedicine interface, created 20 standardized outpatient scenarios requiring audio-visual reasoning, designed "TelePACES" evaluation criteria, and conducted a randomized, interface-blinded crossover simulation study with 120 encounters involving internal medicine residents as patient actors.
AI.result: AI co-clinician approached primary care physicians in key TelePACES dimensions (management plans and differential diagnosis) and significantly outperformed GPT-Realtime across all general criteria, though physicians maintained superior overall performance in case-specific assessments.
AI.conclusion: While representing significant progress in real-time telemedical AI, gaps remain in physical examination and disease-specific reasoning; text-only approaches inadequately capture medical consultation challenges, suggesting high-stakes diagnostic AI should advance through collaborative triadic models where AI serves as supportive co-clinician.

title: SACHI: Structured Agent Coordination via Holistic Information Integration in Multi-Agent Reinforcement Learning
summary: Cooperative multi-agent reinforcement learning agents that act on partial local observations face a fundamental information bottleneck: the knowledge needed to select jointly optimal actions is scattered across the team, yet each agent must commit to a decision without access to its teammates' observations, intentions, or chosen actions. Existing methods either ignore this bottleneck, compress it into a scalar mixing signal, or route around it with learned communication channels. Framing action coordination as a problem of structured information integration among agents, we propose \textit{structured agent coordination via holistic information integration}, or SACHI, in which graph transformer convolutions over an inter-agent coordination graph enrich each agent's representation with receiver-sensitive, content-dependent signals from teammates prior to action selection. We evaluate SACHI across five cooperative tasks spanning spatial, communicative, and adversarial coordination challenges against twelve baselines. SACHI consistently matches or outperforms the best baseline on every task, and rigorous aggregate statistical analyses, including normalized metrics with bootstrap confidence intervals, Friedman ranking, and performance profiling, confirm that this advantage is statistically significant, robust across environments, and not attributable to increased model capacity. Parameter-matched ablations further trace the source of the gains to a single architectural property: the degree of content-dependence in the message-passing operator.

AI.tldr: SACHI is a novel multi-agent RL approach that uses graph transformer convolutions over coordination graphs to integrate structured information among agents before action selection, outperforming baselines on cooperative tasks.
AI.motivation: Multi-agent RL agents face a fundamental information bottleneck: needed coordination knowledge is scattered across the team but each agent must decide based only on local observations. Existing methods either ignore this bottleneck, compress it into scalar signals, or use learned communication that may not optimally integrate structured information.
AI.method: SACHI (structured agent coordination via holistic information integration) uses graph transformer convolutions over an inter-agent coordination graph. This enriches each agent's representation with receiver-sensitive, content-dependent signals from teammates prior to action selection, enabling structured information integration.
AI.result: SACHI was evaluated across five cooperative tasks spanning spatial, communicative, and adversarial coordination challenges against twelve baselines. It consistently matched or outperformed the best baseline on every task. Statistical analyses (normalized metrics with bootstrap CIs, Friedman ranking, performance profiling) confirmed statistically significant advantages across environments, not attributable to increased model capacity.
AI.conclusion: The key advantage comes from content-dependent message passing in the coordination graph. Parameter-matched ablations traced the performance gains specifically to this architectural property, showing that structured, holistic information integration via graph transformers effectively addresses the multi-agent coordination bottleneck.

title: EquiMem: Calibrating Shared Memory in Multi-Agent Debate via Game-Theoretic Equilibrium
summary: Multi-agent debate (MAD) systems increasingly rely on shared memory to support long-horizon reasoning, but this convenience opens a critical vulnerability: a single corrupted entry can contaminate the downstream memory-augmented reasoning, and debate alone fails to filter such errors. Existing safeguards filter entries via heuristics or LLM-based validation, yet they rely on AI judgments that share the same failure modes and overlook the cross-agent dynamics of MAD. We address this gap by formulating memory updating in MAD as a zero-trust memory game, in which no agent is assumed honest and the game's equilibrium serves as an indicator of optimal memory trust. Guided by this equilibrium, we propose EquiMem, an inference-time calibration mechanism that quantifies each update algorithmically against the shared memory state, using agents' existing retrieval queries and traversal paths as evidence rather than soliciting any LLM judgment. EquiMem instantiates calibration for both embedding- and graph-based memory, and across diverse benchmarks, MAD frameworks, and memory architectures, it consistently outperforms existing safeguards, remains robust under adversarial agents, and incurs negligible inference overhead.

AI.tldr: EquiMem is a zero-trust memory calibration system for multi-agent debate frameworks that algorithmically evaluates memory updates without LLM validation, outperforming existing safeguards with minimal overhead.
AI.motivation: Multi-agent debate systems use shared memory for long-horizon reasoning, but this creates vulnerability where single corrupted memory entries can contaminate downstream reasoning. Current safeguards rely on AI judgments that share the same failure modes and ignore cross-agent dynamics in multi-agent debate systems.
AI.method: Formulate memory updating as a zero-trust memory game where no agent is assumed honest, and use the game's equilibrium as an indicator of optimal memory trust. EquiMem algorithmically quantifies each update against shared memory state using agents' existing retrieval queries and traversal paths as evidence, without soliciting LLM judgments. It works with both embedding- and graph-based memory architectures.
AI.result: Across diverse benchmarks, MAD frameworks, and memory architectures, EquiMem consistently outperforms existing safeguards, remains robust under adversarial agents, and incurs negligible inference overhead.
AI.conclusion: The zero-trust equilibrium-based approach provides effective memory calibration for multi-agent debate systems without relying on potentially flawed LLM judgments, offering superior protection against memory contamination with minimal computational cost.

title: Geometry-Aware Discretization Error of Diffusion Models
summary: Practical diffusion sampling is a numerical approximation problem: under a fixed inference budget, one must simulate a reverse-time ODE or SDE using only a limited number of denoising steps, so discretization error is often the dominant source of error. Existing non-asymptotic analyses provide convergence guarantees, but are typically too loose and too insensitive to diffusion parameters to guide practical design: broad families of schedules receive the same rates, which depend on coarse worst-case quantities such as the dimension or the drift Lipschitz constant. We take a less ambitious but more informative route. In the exact-score setting, we derive first-order asymptotic expansions of the Euler-Maruyama weak and Fréchet discretization errors. These formulas hold for general smooth reverse diffusions and become fully explicit under Gaussian data. They show how discretization error adapts to the geometry of the data through the covariance spectrum, and how this geometry interacts with key diffusion parameters, including the diffusion schedules and the diffusion-term coefficient. This yields tractable objectives for geometry-aware parameter optimization. Finally, we show that the qualitative predictions of the Gaussian formulas remain robust across diffusion sampling problems with different geometries, including image generation on different datasets and image posterior sampling.

AI.tldr: A theoretical analysis of diffusion model sampling errors showing how discretization errors adapt to data geometry through covariance spectra, with practical implications for parameter optimization.
AI.motivation: Existing non-asymptotic analyses of diffusion sampling are too loose and insensitive to diffusion parameters to guide practical design. They provide convergence guarantees but treat broad schedule families equally with worst-case dependencies on coarse quantities like dimension or drift Lipschitz constant.
AI.method: Derive first-order asymptotic expansions of Euler-Maruyama weak and Fréchet discretization errors in the exact-score setting. These formulas become fully explicit under Gaussian data assumptions and show how errors adapt to data geometry through covariance spectrum interactions with diffusion parameters.
AI.result: The formulas demonstrate how discretization error adapts to data geometry through covariance spectrum, and how this geometry interacts with key diffusion parameters including schedules and diffusion-term coefficients. This yields tractable objectives for geometry-aware parameter optimization.
AI.conclusion: The qualitative predictions from Gaussian formulas remain robust across diverse diffusion sampling problems, including image generation on different datasets and image posterior sampling, suggesting practical utility for optimizing diffusion parameters based on data geometry.

title: A Prompt-Aware Structuring Framework for Reliable Reuse of AI-Generated Content in the Agentic Web
summary: The evolution of Large Language Models (LLMs) and the software agents built on them (AI agents) marks a turning point in the transition from a human-centric Web to an ``Agentic Web'' driven by AI agents. However, for AI-Generated Content (AIGC), which is expected to dominate the Web, there is currently no mechanism for agents to verify its reliability, reproducibility, or license compliance during generation. This lack of transparency risks causing chained hallucinations and compliance violations through the reuse of AIGC. Consequently, a framework to manage the provenance and generation conditions of AIGC is essential. In this paper, we present a framework that automatically attaches structured metadata to AIGC at generation time, including modularized prompts, contexts, thoughts, model information, hyperparameters, and confidence. The metadata is enveloped together with verifiable credentials to support the reliable assessment and reuse of AIGC. This framework enables efficient curation of structured AIGC and facilitates its safe use for applications such as fine-tuning and knowledge distillation.

AI.tldr: A framework for attaching verifiable metadata to AI-Generated Content to address reliability, reproducibility, and licensing issues in the emerging Agentic Web.
AI.motivation: The shift from human-centric Web to AI-driven "Agentic Web" lacks mechanisms for AI agents to verify AIGC reliability, reproducibility, and license compliance, risking chained hallucinations and compliance violations through content reuse.
AI.method: Automatic attachment of structured metadata to AIGC at generation time, including modularized prompts, contexts, thoughts, model info, hyperparameters, and confidence scores, wrapped with verifiable credentials.
AI.result: Framework enables reliable assessment and safe reuse of AIGC, supporting applications like fine-tuning and knowledge distillation through efficient curation of structured AIGC.
AI.conclusion: A provenance management framework is essential for the Agentic Web to ensure transparency, reliability, and compliance in AI-Generated Content, facilitating safe and effective use across various applications.

title: Exploring and Exploiting Stability in Latent Flow Matching
summary: In this work, we show that Latent Flow-Matching (LFM) models are robust to different types of perturbations, including data reduction and model capacity shrinkage. We characterize this stability by their tendency to generate similar outputs under identical noise seeds. We provide a perspective relating this phenomenon to flow matching theory, which indicates that this stability is inherent to the FM objective. We further exploit this stability to derive practical algorithms for more efficient training and inference. Concretely, first, we show that by training LFM models on significantly reduced datasets, the performance does not degrade perceptually or quantitatively. This yields multiple advantages, such as reducing training time by converging faster under limited compute budget, and alleviating annotation effort when training conditional models. Second, LFM stability under architectural shrinkage gives rise to a two-model coarse-to-fine approach, one using a light-weight architecture for the first phase of the FM trajectory, and one with higher capacity for the second, thereby reducing the inference cost substantially. To determine which samples are informative, we introduce three sample-scoring criteria and evaluate them under standard metrics for generative models. Our results are thoroughly evaluated on multiple datasets, demonstrating the practical advantage of this stability, including data saving and a more than two-fold inference speedup while generating comparable outputs.

AI.tldr: LFM models show remarkable stability to data reduction and architectural shrinkage, enabling efficient training through dataset pruning and faster inference via coarse-to-fine modeling.
AI.motivation: The paper aims to characterize and exploit the inherent stability properties of Latent Flow-Matching models to address computational and data efficiency challenges in training and inference.
AI.method: Characterizes LFM stability theoretically via flow matching theory, then develops practical algorithms: 1) training on reduced datasets using three sample-scoring criteria to identify informative samples, and 2) a two-model coarse-to-fine approach with lightweight architecture for early trajectory phases and higher capacity for later phases.
AI.result: LFM models maintain performance despite significant data reduction, enable faster convergence with limited compute, reduce annotation effort for conditional models, and achieve >2x inference speedup via coarse-to-fine approach while generating comparable outputs.
AI.conclusion: The inherent stability of LFM models provides practical advantages for efficient training and inference, enabling data saving and substantial speed improvements without quality degradation, with implications for resource-constrained generative modeling.

title: PiCA: Pivot-Based Credit Assignment for Search Agentic Reinforcement Learning
summary: Large Language Model (LLM)-based search agents trained with reinforcement learning (RL) have significantly improved the performance of knowledge-intensive tasks. However, existing methods encounter critical challenges in long-horizon credit assignment: (i) Reward Sparsity, where models receive only outcome feedback without step-level guidance to differentiate action quality; (ii) Isolated Credit, where credit is assigned to steps independently, failing to capture sequential dependencies; and (iii) Distributional Shift, where rewards are estimated on templates that deviate from the model's natural generative distribution. To address these issues, we propose Pivot-Based Credit Assignment (PiCA), a novel step reward mechanism that reformulates the search trajectory as a sequential process of cumulative search progress. Unlike prior isolated step rewards, PiCA defines process rewards as success probabilities dependent on the historical context based on Potential-Based Reward Shaping (PBRS). This approach identifies pivot steps, which comprise target golden sub-queries and sub-answers derived from historical trajectories, as information peaks that significantly boost the likelihood of a correct final answer. By anchoring these step rewards to the final task objective, PiCA provides dense, pivot-aware and trajectory-dependent guidance while maintaining distributional consistency. Extensive experiments show that PiCA outperforms existing strong baselines across seven knowledge-intensive QA benchmarks, achieving 15.2% and 2.2% improvements for 3B and 7B models. The consistent performance gains across various models show PiCA's robust generalization. The code is available at https://github.com/novdream/PiCA.

AI.tldr: PiCA is a novel credit assignment method for LLM-based search agents that addresses long-horizon credit assignment challenges by using pivot steps and potential-based reward shaping to provide trajectory-dependent guidance.
AI.motivation: Existing RL-trained LLM search agents face three critical challenges: reward sparsity (only outcome feedback), isolated credit assignment (ignoring sequential dependencies), and distributional shift (rewards estimated on templates that don't match natural generation).
AI.method: Proposes Pivot-Based Credit Assignment (PiCA) that reformulates search trajectories as cumulative progress processes. Uses Potential-Based Reward Shaping to define process rewards dependent on historical context, identifies pivot steps (target golden sub-queries/sub-answers) as information peaks that boost final answer likelihood, and anchors step rewards to final task objectives.
AI.result: Outperforms existing baselines across seven knowledge-intensive QA benchmarks, achieving 15.2% and 2.2% improvements for 3B and 7B models. Shows robust generalization across various models.
AI.conclusion: PiCA effectively addresses long-horizon credit assignment challenges by providing dense, pivot-aware, trajectory-dependent guidance while maintaining distributional consistency, leading to significant performance improvements in LLM-based search agents.

title: AdamFLIP: Adaptive Momentum Feedback Linearization Optimization for Hard Constrained PINN Training
summary: Physics-informed neural networks (PINNs) provide a flexible framework for solving forward and inverse problems governed by partial differential equations (PDEs), but standard PINN training typically relies on soft penalty formulations that combine PDE residuals, data mismatch, and initial/boundary conditions using manually chosen weights. This often leads to ill-conditioning, sensitivity to loss weights, and poor constraint satisfaction. In this work, we reformulate PINN training as an equality-constrained optimization problem and propose a novel Adaptive Momentum Feedback Linearization Optimization for Hard Constrained PINN (AdamFLIP). The key idea is to view the constraint residuals as the output of a controlled dynamical system and to compute the Lagrange multiplier as a feedback input that locally drives these residuals toward stable linear contraction dynamics. AdamFLIP then applies Adam-style first- and second-moment adaptation to the resulting feedback-linearized Lagrangian gradient, combining principled constraint handling with the scalability and robustness of adaptive neural-network optimization. We test AdamFLIP on a range of benchmark forward and inverse PDE problem, and it consistently outperforms both the standard soft-constrained PINN and state-of-the-art constrained optimizers. Specifically, on the Navier--Stokes equations benchmark, AdamFLIP \textbf{reduces relative $L_2$ error by more than two thirds} for the predicted solution compared to the next best method. Our AdamFLIP framework provides an effective and computationally scalable hard constraint optimization method for PINN training.

AI.tldr: AdamFLIP: A novel hard-constrained optimization method for PINNs that reformulates training as an equality-constrained problem and uses adaptive momentum feedback linearization, significantly outperforming standard PINN training.
AI.motivation: Standard PINN training uses soft penalty formulations with manually chosen weights, leading to ill-conditioning, sensitivity to loss weights, and poor constraint satisfaction. There's a need for more robust and effective constraint handling methods for PINNs.
AI.method: Reformulates PINN training as an equality-constrained optimization problem. Views constraint residuals as output of a controlled dynamical system and computes Lagrange multiplier as feedback input to drive residuals toward stable linear contraction dynamics. Combines feedback-linearized Lagrangian gradient with Adam-style first- and second-moment adaptation.
AI.result: AdamFLIP consistently outperforms standard soft-constrained PINNs and state-of-the-art constrained optimizers across benchmark forward and inverse PDE problems. On Navier-Stokes equations, reduces relative L2 error by more than two-thirds compared to the next best method.
AI.conclusion: AdamFLIP provides an effective and computationally scalable hard constraint optimization method for PINN training, combining principled constraint handling with the scalability and robustness of adaptive neural network optimization.

title: Beyond Accuracy: Evaluating Strategy Diversity in LLM Mathematical Reasoning
summary: Large language models now achieve high final-answer accuracy on mathematical reasoning benchmarks, but accuracy alone does not capture reasoning flexibility. We introduce a strategy-level evaluation framework instantiated on 80 AMC 10/12 and AIME problems with 217 AoPS-derived reference strategy families. Model outputs are annotated for strategy identity, validity, and correctness using dual-AI coding with human adjudication. Across four frontier models, we find a pronounced decoupling between answer accuracy and strategy diversity. Under a single-solution prompt, all models achieve high accuracy (95%-100%), but under a multiple-strategy prompt they recover substantially fewer strategies than the human reference set. Gemini, DeepSeek, GPT, and Claude generate 184, 152, 151, and 110 distinct valid strategies, respectively, with the largest gaps in Geometry and Number Theory. The models collectively produce 50 benchmark-novel valid strategies, indicating both incomplete coverage of human strategies and some capacity for alternative reasoning. A repeated-run robustness check on 20 problems shows diminishing gains in discovered strategies, with the strongest model recovering only 39 of 55 AoPS-reference strategies (71%) after three runs. These findings position strategy diversity as a complementary dimension for evaluating mathematical reasoning beyond answer correctness.

AI.tldr: LLMs achieve high answer accuracy on math problems but show limited strategy diversity compared to human reference sets, with strategy recovery rates significantly lower than accuracy rates.
AI.motivation: Current mathematical reasoning evaluations focus primarily on final-answer accuracy, but this doesn't capture reasoning flexibility or the ability to generate diverse solution strategies like humans can.
AI.method: Created a strategy-level evaluation framework using 80 AMC 10/12 and AIME problems with 217 AoPS-derived reference strategies. Model outputs were annotated for strategy identity, validity, and correctness using dual-AI coding with human adjudication. Tested four frontier models under both single-solution and multiple-strategy prompts.
AI.result: Models achieve high accuracy (95%-100%) under single-solution prompts, but recover substantially fewer strategies than human references under multiple-strategy prompts: Gemini (184), DeepSeek (152), GPT (151), Claude (110). Models collectively produced 50 benchmark-novel valid strategies. In a robustness check, the strongest model recovered only 71% (39/55) of AoPS-reference strategies after three runs.
AI.conclusion: There's a pronounced decoupling between answer accuracy and strategy diversity in LLMs, indicating that strategy diversity should be considered as a complementary evaluation dimension for mathematical reasoning beyond just answer correctness.

title: Central Limit Theorem for Two-Time-Scale Approximate Distributionally Robust RL
summary: Designing model-free algorithms for distributionally robust reinforcement learning (DRRL) poses fundamental challenges. The robust Bellman operator is nonlinear in the transition kernel, which makes one-sample Bellman updates biased, while the adversarial optimization underlying robustness makes robust evaluation computationally demanding. To address these difficulties, we consider the natural small-ambiguity regime under Kullback--Leibler ambiguity sets and propose an approximate DRRL framework based on a first-order expansion of the relevant robust functional. This yields an approximate robust Bellman equation that removes the adversarial optimization while remaining first-order accurate in the ambiguity radius. To learn the fixed point of this approximate equation, we propose Mean-Variance Stochastic Approximation (MVSA), a model-free algorithm that uses only one-sample updates. This is achieved via a lifted stochastic approximation dynamics and a two-time-scale design. We then prove convergence and a central limit theorem for MVSA: its main iterate satisfies a central limit theorem at the canonical $n^{-1/2}$ scale, with explicitly characterized asymptotic covariances. Finally, we validate our theoretical findings with a numerical experiment.

AI.tldr: Proposes Mean-Variance Stochastic Approximation (MVSA), a model-free algorithm for distributionally robust RL with KL ambiguity sets, using first-order approximations to avoid adversarial optimization while maintaining theoretical guarantees.
AI.motivation: Model-free DRRL faces fundamental challenges: the robust Bellman operator's nonlinearity makes one-sample Bellman updates biased, and adversarial optimization for robustness is computationally demanding.
AI.method: Proposes an approximate DRRL framework using first-order expansion of robust functionals for KL ambiguity sets in small-ambiguity regime. Introduces Mean-Variance Stochastic Approximation (MVSA) with lifted stochastic approximation dynamics, two-time-scale design, and one-sample updates to learn the fixed point of the approximate robust Bellman equation.
AI.result: Developed MVSA algorithm that converges and satisfies a central limit theorem at n^{-1/2} scale with explicitly characterized asymptotic covariances. Theoretical findings were validated with numerical experiments.
AI.conclusion: The proposed approximate DRRL framework and MVSA algorithm effectively address the computational and theoretical challenges of model-free distributionally robust RL by removing adversarial optimization while maintaining first-order accuracy and provable convergence properties.

title: Beyond ESG Scores: Learning Dynamic Constraints for Sequential Portfolio Optimization
summary: ESG-aware portfolio optimization is increasingly important for sustainable capital allocation, yet most learning-based methods still operationalize ESG by appending static scores to the policy observation or reward. This creates a mismatch for sequential control: ESG scores are noisy, provider-dependent, low-frequency, and temporally misaligned with sequential portfolio decisions, while financial evidence suggests that ESG is better treated as a portfolio preference, risk-exposure, or hedge dimension than as a robust alpha factor. We propose to impose ESG constraints without modifying the financial policy's observation or reward, using a Multimodal Action-Conditioned Constraint Field (MACF) that learns mechanism-specific ESG costs from point-in-time multimodal evidence and contemplated portfolio transitions. We then introduce MACF-X, a family of optimizer-specific adapters that converts MACF costs and uncertainties into native constrained-optimization interfaces through a shared slack- and uncertainty-aware pressure layer. Across multiple constraint-integration interfaces, MACF-X reduces tail ESG budget pressure while maintaining competitive financial performance. Ablations show that this improvement depends on dynamic evidence inputs and three-head decomposition, while static ESG-score proxies are nearly indistinguishable from score-shuffled noise baselines.

AI.tldr: ESG-aware portfolio optimization using a novel Multimodal Action-Conditioned Constraint Field (MACF) that learns ESG costs from dynamic evidence without modifying financial policy observations or rewards, implemented through MACF-X adapters that integrate ESG constraints while maintaining financial performance.
AI.motivation: Current learning-based ESG portfolio optimization methods have limitations: they treat ESG as static scores appended to policy observation/reward, but ESG scores are noisy, provider-dependent, low-frequency, and temporally misaligned with sequential portfolio decisions. Financial evidence suggests ESG should be treated as portfolio preference, risk-exposure, or hedge dimension rather than a robust alpha factor.
AI.method: Proposes Multimodal Action-Conditioned Constraint Field (MACF) that learns mechanism-specific ESG costs from point-in-time multimodal evidence and contemplated portfolio transitions. Introduces MACF-X family of optimizer-specific adapters that convert MACF costs and uncertainties into native constrained-optimization interfaces through a shared slack- and uncertainty-aware pressure layer.
AI.result: MACF-X reduces tail ESG budget pressure while maintaining competitive financial performance across multiple constraint-integration interfaces. Ablations show improvement depends on dynamic evidence inputs and three-head decomposition, while static ESG-score proxies are nearly indistinguishable from score-shuffled noise baselines.
AI.conclusion: The proposed MACF framework provides a better approach to ESG-aware portfolio optimization by learning dynamic ESG costs from evidence without disrupting financial policy learning, leading to effective ESG constraint integration with preserved financial performance.

title: Queryable LoRA: Instruction-Regularized Routing Over Shared Low-Rank Update Atoms
summary: We present a data-adaptive method for parameter-efficient fine-tuning of large neural networks. Standard low-rank adaptation methods improve efficiency by restricting each layer update to a fixed low-rank form, but this static parameterization can be too rigid when the appropriate correction depends on the input and on the evolving depth-wise computation of the network. Our approach replaces a purely layer-local adapter with a shared queryable memory of low-rank update atoms. For each block of layers, the model forms a query from the current low-rank state and a running summary of previous blocks, uses this query to retrieve a content-dependent combination of shared update components via attention, and applies the resulting routed operator within the low-rank bottleneck. In this way, the method retains the efficiency and scalability of low-rank adaptation while allowing the effective update to vary across inputs and to share reusable structure across layers. The resulting architecture provides a principled middle ground between static LoRA-style updates and fully generated parameter updates: it remains compact and parameter-efficient while supporting dynamic, context-sensitive adaptation. Further, we incorporate instruction-regularization by augmenting routing logits with a language-induced prior over update atoms, thereby biasing the selection of low-rank transformations toward semantically relevant directions without generating unconstrained parameter updates. Experiments on noisy non-linear regression tasks and LLM fine-tuning suggest that this queryable update-memory formulation can improve final test performance and training stability compared to standard low-rank adaptation, while using a comparable number of trainable parameters.

AI.tldr: A data-adaptive, parameter-efficient fine-tuning method that uses a shared queryable memory of low-rank update atoms, allowing dynamic, context-sensitive adaptation while maintaining efficiency comparable to standard LoRA.
AI.motivation: Standard low-rank adaptation (LoRA) methods are too rigid with fixed low-rank parameterization, unable to adapt to input-dependent or depth-dependent corrections. There's a need for a middle ground between static LoRA updates and fully generated parameter updates that remains efficient while supporting dynamic adaptation.
AI.method: Replaces layer-local adapters with shared queryable memory of low-rank update atoms. For each block, forms a query from current low-rank state and running summary of previous blocks, uses attention to retrieve content-dependent combination of shared update components, and applies routed operator within low-rank bottleneck. Includes instruction-regularization by augmenting routing logits with language-induced prior.
AI.result: Experiments on noisy non-linear regression tasks and LLM fine-tuning show improved final test performance and training stability compared to standard low-rank adaptation, while using comparable number of trainable parameters.
AI.conclusion: The queryable update-memory formulation provides a principled middle ground between static LoRA-style updates and fully generated parameter updates, offering parameter-efficient dynamic adaptation that varies across inputs and shares reusable structure across layers.

title: How LLMs Are Persuaded: A Few Attention Heads, Rerouted
summary: Language models can be persuaded to abandon factual knowledge. This vulnerability is central to AI safety, but its internal mechanism remains poorly understood. We uncover a compact causal mechanism for persuasion-induced factual errors. A small set of mid-layer attention heads almost entirely determines the model's answer. These heads write answer options into a low-dimensional polyhedron, with options occupying distinct vertices. Persuasion does not blur belief or merely reduce confidence; it causes a discrete latent jump from the correct-answer vertex to the persuasion-target vertex. We show that decision heads are not reasoning over evidence. Instead, they copy whichever option token their attention selects. Persuasion works by redirecting attention. We isolate a rank-one evidence-routing feature that controls the route. Directly modifying this feature steers the model's choice, and removing it blocks persuasion. We then trace the feature back to a band of shallower attention heads that build it from persuasive keywords in the input. Every step is validated by intervention. This mechanism appears across open-source LLMs and realistic poisoning scenarios such as Generative Engine Optimization, revealing persuasion as a narrow, monitorable circuit.

AI.tldr: LLMs have a specific internal mechanism where persuasion causes factual errors via attention redirection rather than confidence reduction - a discrete jump between answer options in a low-dimensional space controlled by evidence-routing features.
AI.motivation: Language models can be persuaded to abandon factual knowledge, which is a central vulnerability for AI safety, but the internal mechanism behind this persuasion-induced factual errors remains poorly understood and needs investigation.
AI.method: The researchers uncover a compact causal mechanism where a small set of mid-layer attention heads write answer options into distinct vertices of a low-dimensional polyhedron. They use interventions to trace the mechanism back through the network, identifying specific features and attention heads involved in evidence routing.
AI.result: Persuasion causes a discrete latent jump from correct-answer vertex to persuasion-target vertex, not just confidence reduction. The researchers isolate a rank-one evidence-routing feature that controls attention selection, show that modifying this feature steers model choice, and trace it back to shallower attention heads that build it from persuasive keywords. The mechanism appears across open-source LLMs and realistic poisoning scenarios.
AI.conclusion: Persuasion in LLMs operates through a narrow, monitorable circuit where attention heads copy whichever option token their attention selects, with persuasion working by redirecting attention through specific evidence-routing features, making it a more tractable problem than previously thought.

title: Generalized Wasserstein Flow Matching: Transport Plans, Everywhere, All at Once
summary: Flow matching has recently emerged as a flexible and efficient framework for generative modelling by learning deterministic transport dynamics between probability measures. In this work, we extend flow matching to the space of probability measures over probability measures, introducing a Wasserstein-on-Wasserstein (WoW) formulation. Leveraging the nested Wasserstein geometry, we show that measures over transport plans naturally induce velocity fields that realize metameasure flows. This yields a principled generalization of Wasserstein flow matching via coupled outer and inner transport plans. To address the substantial computational cost of WoW transport, we propose scalable approximations based on sliced and linear Wasserstein distances, enabling efficient training while promoting numerically stable, near-straight trajectories. Our framework unifies and extends existing approaches to point cloud and set generation, providing a practical and theoretically grounded method for generative modelling in WoW spaces.

AI.tldr: Flow matching is extended to probability measures over probability measures using a Wasserstein-on-Wasserstein (WoW) framework, with scalable approximations for computational efficiency.
AI.motivation: Flow matching has shown promise for generative modeling, but existing approaches operate on standard probability measures. The authors aim to extend this to meta-measures (measures over measures) to enable more flexible generative modeling for complex structures like point clouds and sets.
AI.method: Introduces Wasserstein-on-Wasserstein (WoW) formulation by leveraging nested Wasserstein geometry. Shows that measures over transport plans induce velocity fields for metameasure flows. Proposes scalable approximations using sliced and linear Wasserstein distances to reduce computational costs.
AI.result: Develops a principled generalization of Wasserstein flow matching through coupled outer and inner transport plans. Provides theoretically grounded framework that unifies existing approaches to point cloud and set generation.
AI.conclusion: The WoW framework offers a practical, theoretically sound method for generative modeling in WoW spaces, enabling efficient training with numerically stable trajectories while extending flow matching capabilities to meta-measures.

title: Do Self-Evolving Agents Forget? Capability Degradation and Preservation in Lifelong LLM Agent Adaptation
summary:
Recent advances in LLM agents enable systems that autonomously refine workflows, accumulate reusable skills, self-train their underlying models, and maintain persistent memory. However, we show that such self-evolution is often non-monotonic: adapting to new task distributions can progressively degrade previously acquired capabilities across all major evolution channels.
  We identify this phenomenon as \emph{capability erosion under self-evolution} and show that it consistently emerges across workflow, skill, model, and memory evolution. To mitigate this issue, we propose \emph{Capability-Preserving Evolution} (CPE), a general stabilization principle that constrains destructive capability drift during continual adaptation. Across all four evolution dimensions, CPE consistently improves retained capability stability while preserving adaptation performance. For example, in workflow evolution, CPE improves retained simple-task performance from 41.8\% to 52.8\% under GPT-5.1 optimization while simultaneously achieving stronger complex-task adaptation.
  Our findings suggest that stable long-horizon self-evolving agents require not only acquiring new capabilities, but also explicitly preserving previously learned ones during continual adaptation.

AI.tldr: Self-evolving LLM agents suffer from capability erosion where adapting to new tasks degrades previously acquired abilities, so the authors propose Capability-Preserving Evolution (CPE) to stabilize continual adaptation while maintaining performance.
AI.motivation: Recent advances enable LLM agents to autonomously refine workflows, accumulate reusable skills, self-train models, and maintain memory. However, the authors discovered that such self-evolution often leads to non-monotonic degradation where adapting to new task distributions erodes previously acquired capabilities.
AI.method: The authors propose Capability-Preserving Evolution (CPE), a general stabilization principle that constrains destructive capability drift during continual adaptation. CPE is applied across four evolution dimensions: workflow, skill, model, and memory evolution.
AI.result: CPE consistently improves retained capability stability while preserving adaptation performance. In workflow evolution, CPE improved retained simple-task performance from 41.8% to 52.8% under GPT-5.1 optimization while achieving stronger complex-task adaptation.
AI.conclusion: Stable long-horizon self-evolving agents require not only acquiring new capabilities but also explicitly preserving previously learned ones during continual adaptation. CPE provides an effective mitigation strategy for capability erosion in self-evolving systems.

title: A meshfree exterior calculus for generalizable and data-efficient learning of physics from point clouds
summary: We introduce a meshfree exterior calculus (MEEC) for learning structure-preserving descriptions of physics on point clouds, and use it to build MEEC-Net, a data-efficient surrogate that transfers across resolutions, geometries, and physical parameters. MEEC equips an $\varepsilon$-ball graph with virtual node and edge measures via a single sparse Schur complement solve; the resulting complex satisfies discrete conservation exactly, is end-to-end differentiable in the point positions, and exposes a direct geometry-to-physics link without the mesh-generation step required by conventional structure-preserving discretizations. MEEC-Net learns unknown physics as a shared edge-wise flux law in an SO($d$)-invariant local frame, so the same kernel produces compatible fluxes on any point cloud whose features lie in the training range. We prove a solution-error bound that splits into discretization and kernel-approximation terms which is independent of problem geometry, explaining the observed transfer from very few examples. We show that single-solution training transfers to unseen geometries, boundary conditions, and physical parameters. On five canonical PDE benchmarks MEEC-Net achieves 1-2 orders of magnitude lower out-of-distribution error than baseline neural-operator approaches. On the SimJEB structural-bracket benchmark it achieves competitive error while using substantially fewer training geometries.

AI.tldr: MEEC-Net is a meshfree exterior calculus framework for learning structure-preserving physics on point clouds that transfers across resolutions, geometries, and physical parameters with high data efficiency.
AI.motivation: Traditional structure-preserving discretizations require mesh generation, which is computationally expensive and limits flexibility. There's a need for meshfree methods that can learn physics from sparse data while preserving conservation laws and transferring across different problem settings.
AI.method: MEEC equips ε-ball graphs with virtual node and edge measures via a sparse Schur complement solve, creating a differentiable complex that satisfies discrete conservation exactly. MEEC-Net learns unknown physics as shared edge-wise flux laws in SO(d)-invariant local frames, enabling transfer to any point cloud within training feature range.
AI.result: MEEC-Net achieves 1-2 orders of magnitude lower out-of-distribution error than baseline neural operators on five PDE benchmarks. On the SimJEB structural-bracket benchmark, it achieves competitive error with substantially fewer training geometries. The method demonstrates transfer to unseen geometries, boundary conditions, and physical parameters from single-solution training.
AI.conclusion: MEEC provides a mathematically rigorous meshfree framework for structure-preserving physics learning that bridges geometry and physics without meshing. The approach offers superior data efficiency and transfer capabilities compared to existing neural operator methods, with proven error bounds independent of problem geometry.

title: SKG-VLA: Scene Knowledge Graph Priors for Structured Scene Semantics and Multimodal Reasoning for Decision Making
summary: Decision making in large-scale complaint handling systems increasingly relies on heterogeneous evidence, including complaint narratives, screenshots, order metadata, historical interactions, and platform policies. Existing complaint understanding systems mainly perform shallow classification or template matching over isolated modalities, while underutilizing explicit scene structure, rule knowledge, and cross-evidence dependencies. To address this limitation, we present SKG-VLA for multimodal complaint decision making. The core idea is to model each case as a structured complaint scene and represent its decision-relevant semantics with a \emph{Scene Knowledge Graph} (SKG), which organizes complaint entities, evidence items, policy clauses, temporal events, transactional states, and action-relevant relations into a unified graph. Based on SKG, we build a data synthesis pipeline that generates complaint scene descriptions, rule-consistent graph generalizations, question-answer supervision, and decision recommendations. We further construct a large-scale complaint scene dataset with both text-only and multimodal in-domain benchmarks. Finally, we adopt a three-stage training strategy -- domain-adaptive pre-training, task-oriented instruction fine-tuning, and end-to-end multimodal alignment -- to inject structured scene priors into a multimodal decision model. Experiments show that SKG-VLA consistently improves policy-grounded reasoning, complaint decision accuracy, long-tail generalization, and robustness under incomplete evidence.

AI.tldr: SKG-VLA is a multimodal complaint decision making system that models complaints as structured Scene Knowledge Graphs to improve policy-grounded reasoning and decision accuracy by capturing cross-evidence dependencies and rule knowledge.
AI.motivation: Existing complaint handling systems perform shallow classification or template matching over isolated modalities, underutilizing scene structure, rule knowledge, and cross-evidence dependencies in large-scale complaint systems with heterogeneous evidence.
AI.method: Models each case as a structured complaint scene using Scene Knowledge Graphs (SKG) that organize entities, evidence, policy clauses, events, states, and relations. Uses a data synthesis pipeline for training data generation, constructs multimodal complaint datasets, and employs three-stage training: domain-adaptive pre-training, task-oriented instruction fine-tuning, and end-to-end multimodal alignment.
AI.result: SKG-VLA consistently improves policy-grounded reasoning, complaint decision accuracy, long-tail generalization, and robustness under incomplete evidence conditions.
AI.conclusion: The structured scene modeling approach with Scene Knowledge Graphs effectively addresses limitations of existing complaint systems and enhances multimodal decision making by capturing complex dependencies and rule knowledge.

title: TARO: Temporal Adversarial Rectification Optimization Using Diffusion Models as Purifiers
summary: Adversarial purification with diffusion models seeks to project adversarial examples back toward the data manifold, but balancing semantic preservation and robustness against adaptive attacks remains challenging. Recent work shows that standard diffusion purification can fail under adaptive evaluation, while test-time score-based optimization is more resilient. Existing optimization defenses, however, typically rely on a single diffusion noise regime or treat timesteps uniformly, overlooking the distinct roles of coarse and fine denoising scales. We propose Temporal Adversarial Rectification Optimization (TARO), an inference-time purification method that builds a temporally guided score prior from multiple denoising views along the diffusion trajectory. TARO forms a coarse-to-fine residual target: high-noise experts provide globally smoothed structure with reduced adversarial sensitivity, while low-noise experts restore image-specific, class-relevant details. A guidance strength controls this temporal correction, allowing TARO to balance robust global rectification with semantic preservation. Empirically, TARO improves robust accuracy across datasets and adaptive threat models in a zero-shot setting, while remaining compatible with complementary adversarial-likelihood objectives for further robustness gains.

AI.tldr: TARO improves diffusion-based adversarial purification by using temporally-guided score priors from multiple denoising views, balancing global robustness with semantic preservation through coarse-to-fine optimization.
AI.motivation: Existing diffusion purification methods struggle to balance semantic preservation and robustness against adaptive attacks, often failing under adaptive evaluation. Current optimization defenses typically use single noise regimes or treat timesteps uniformly, overlooking the distinct roles of coarse and fine denoising scales.
AI.method: Temporal Adversarial Rectification Optimization (TARO) builds temporally-guided score priors from multiple denoising views along the diffusion trajectory. It forms a coarse-to-fine residual target where high-noise experts provide globally smoothed structure with reduced adversarial sensitivity, while low-noise experts restore image-specific, class-relevant details. A guidance strength controls the temporal correction for balancing robust global rectification with semantic preservation.
AI.result: TARO improves robust accuracy across datasets and adaptive threat models in a zero-shot setting. It remains compatible with complementary adversarial-likelihood objectives for further robustness gains, outperforming standard diffusion purification methods.
AI.conclusion: TARO successfully addresses the limitations of existing diffusion purification by leveraging temporal guidance and coarse-to-fine optimization, achieving better balance between robustness against adaptive attacks and semantic preservation of clean images.

title: Dsat: A Native SAT Solver for Discrete Logic
comment: To Appear at The International Conferences on Theory and Applications of Satisfiability Testing (SAT), 2026
summary: Discrete variables are common in many applications, such as probabilistic reasoning, planning and explainable AI. When symbolic reasoning techniques are brought in to bear on these applications, a standard technique for handling discrete variables is to binarize them into Boolean variables to allow the use of Boolean computational machinery such as SAT solvers. This technique can face both computational and semantical challenges though. In this work, we develop a native SAT solver for discrete logic, which is a direct extension of Boolean logic in which variables can take arbitrary values. Our proposed solver has a similar design to Boolean SAT solvers, with ingredients such as unit resolution and clause learning but ones that operate natively on discrete variables. We illustrate the merits of the developed SAT solver by comparing it empirically to CSP solvers applied to discrete CNFs, to Boolean SAT solver applied to binarized CNFs, and to some hybrid solvers.

AI.tldr: Developed a native SAT solver for discrete logic that extends Boolean SAT solving to handle variables with arbitrary values directly, avoiding the computational and semantic challenges of binarization.
AI.motivation: Many applications like probabilistic reasoning, planning, and explainable AI use discrete variables. Current symbolic reasoning approaches binarize these variables into Boolean form to use SAT solvers, but this faces computational and semantic challenges.
AI.method: Created a direct extension of Boolean SAT solvers for discrete logic with similar design principles (unit resolution, clause learning) but operating natively on discrete variables rather than binarizing them.
AI.result: The paper illustrates the merits of the discrete SAT solver through empirical comparison with CSP solvers on discrete CNFs, Boolean SAT solvers on binarized CNFs, and hybrid solvers.
AI.conclusion: A native discrete SAT solver can overcome limitations of binarization approaches, providing a more efficient and semantically sound method for handling discrete variables in symbolic reasoning applications.

title: DUET: Optimize Token-Budget Allocation for Reinforcement Learning with Verifiable Rewards
summary: Reinforcement learning with verifiable rewards (RLVR) generates hundreds of thousands of tokens per training step, with rollout generation dominating the computational cost. The overall token budget can be controlled along two main dimensions: (i) deciding which prompts to allocate rollouts to, and (ii) deciding how long each rollout should be. Prior work has generally controlled only one of these dimensions at a time. We show that jointly tuning both decisions under a shared compute budget improves both reasoning quality and wall-clock training time. We instantiate this view as \textbf{DU}al-controlled tok\textbf{E}n alloca\textbf{T}ion (DUET), a computationally efficient layer over GRPO that uses a lightweight pre-rollout surrogate of prompt informativeness to set how many rollouts each prompt receives, and a marker-gated abort rule with importance reweighting to set when to stop them. On Qwen3-1.7B trained on MATH, DUET outperforms full-budget GRPO and the other three budget-aware baseline methods. DUET's advantage further generalizes to other benchmarks across math and coding, and is on par with the best baseline on the scientific Q\&A domain, while also achieving a $1.62\times$ wall-clock speedup. More notably, using only 50\% of the token budget, DUET still outperforms all baseline methods at their full budget, achieving an even higher $2.51\times$ speedup over full-budget GRPO. We verify the high performance of DUET on other backbone LLMs, including Qwen3-4B and Llama-3.2-3B-Instruct. Notably, the gap between DUET and the strongest baseline \emph{widens} as the budget tightens, contrary to the usual pattern in which efficient methods trade off quality as compute decreases. More broadly, these results suggest that DUET budget-aware control strategies are valuable not only for accelerating training, but also for improving the quality of the learning signal.

AI.tldr: DUET (DUal-controlled tokEn allocation) is a budget-aware method that jointly controls both which prompts receive rollouts and how long each rollout runs, achieving better reasoning quality and faster training than existing methods with the same or even reduced token budget.
AI.motivation: Reinforcement learning with verifiable rewards (RLVR) generates massive amounts of tokens during training, with rollout generation being computationally expensive. Prior work only controlled either prompt selection or rollout length individually, not both jointly. There's a need for more efficient token allocation strategies to improve training efficiency without sacrificing quality.
AI.method: DUET is a computationally efficient layer over GRPO that: 1) Uses a lightweight pre-rollout surrogate to estimate prompt informativeness and allocate different numbers of rollouts to different prompts, and 2) Implements a marker-gated abort rule with importance reweighting to decide when to stop rollouts early.
AI.result: DUET outperforms full-budget GRPO and three budget-aware baselines on MATH benchmark. It generalizes to other math and coding benchmarks, matches best baseline on scientific Q&A, and achieves 1.62× wall-clock speedup. With only 50% token budget, it still outperforms all baselines at full budget with 2.51× speedup. Performance advantage widens as budget tightens, contrary to typical trade-off patterns.
AI.conclusion: DUET's dual-controlled token allocation strategy not only accelerates training but also improves learning signal quality. The method is effective across different backbone LLMs and demonstrates that jointly controlling both prompt allocation and rollout length dimensions is crucial for efficient RLVR training.

title: CHAINTRIX: A multi-pipeline LLM-augmented framework for automated smart-contract security auditing
summary: Smart-contract exploits have caused billions of USD in cumulative losses, yet audits remain expensive and slow. Automated tools have emerged to close this gap, but each class has a characteristic failure mode. Static analyzers report findings that frequently fail manual triage at high rates, while large language models (LLMs) hallucinate findings that contradict the source code. Thus, we propose Chaintrix, an end-to-end auditing framework whose central architectural commitment is that every LLM-generated claim must be discharged against a deterministic structural contract representation. We introduce a Cross-Contract Interaction Model (CCIM) that parses Solidity into a structured map of function-level reads, writes, modifiers and resolved cross-contract calls. CCIM serves as the substrate against which all 12 of Chaintrix's deterministic signal engines and the parallel LLM audit pipelines operate. A staged false-positive-reduction pipeline, terminating in a Structural Verdict Engine (SVE) that applies deterministic structural checks against parsed code, filters the merged finding set, with selected high-confidence findings further validated through symbolic execution and fuzz testing. We evaluate Chaintrix on EVMbench, the smart-contract security benchmark by OpenAI, Paradigm, OtterSec. Chaintrix detects 86 of 120 high-severity vulnerabilities (71.7% recall), with 25 audits scoring 100% recall, placing Chaintrix 26 percentage points above the strongest frontier-model baseline.

AI.tldr: Chaintrix is an automated smart-contract auditing framework that combines LLM-generated insights with deterministic structural analysis to improve vulnerability detection accuracy while reducing false positives.
AI.motivation: Current smart-contract auditing approaches have significant limitations: manual audits are expensive and slow, static analyzers produce too many false positives, and LLMs frequently hallucinate incorrect findings. There's a need for an automated solution that maintains high accuracy while reducing false positives.
AI.method: The framework uses a Cross-Contract Interaction Model (CCIM) that parses Solidity code into structured function-level representations. It combines 12 deterministic signal engines with parallel LLM audit pipelines. A multi-stage pipeline filters findings through deterministic structural checks via a Structural Verdict Engine (SVE), with high-confidence findings further validated through symbolic execution and fuzz testing.
AI.result: Chaintrix detects 86 of 120 high-severity vulnerabilities (71.7% recall) on EVMbench benchmark, with 25 audits achieving 100% recall. This performance is 26 percentage points above the strongest frontier-model baseline.
AI.conclusion: By combining LLM capabilities with deterministic structural analysis and verification techniques, Chaintrix provides a more reliable and effective automated smart-contract auditing solution that addresses the limitations of existing approaches while maintaining high vulnerability detection rates.

title: Direct Bethe Free Energy Minimization for Bayesian Neural Ne twork
summary: We propose training Bayesian neural networks by directly minimizing the Bethe free energy rather than maximizing a variational lower bound. On tree-structured factor graphs the Bethe free energy is exact; deterministic layers drop out of the objective and are trained by standard backpropagation, so the framework accommodates any mixture of probabilistic and deterministic subgraphs without modification. Restricting the weight posterior to a last-layer Gaussian yields analytically tractable losses: for a Gaussian likelihood the Bethe loss equals the exact marginal likelihood, and for a probit likelihood it reduces to a closed form via the probit-Gaussian convolution. Both objectives sit strictly between MAP and the ELBO ($L_\text{MAP} \leq L_\text{Bethe} \leq L_\text{ELBO}$), removing the structural Jensen gap that no choice of variational family can close. The Z-consistent prior formulation makes the prior precision a differentiable parameter, enabling empirical Bayes - joint optimization of weights, covariance, and hyperparameters - in a single gradient pass, with no cross-validation or outer loop. All variants admit a closed-form predictive at MAP-equivalent inference cost, in contrast to ensemble and sampling-based methods. On 8 UCI regression and 12 UCI classification benchmarks evaluated under a single shared hyperparameter regime, Bethe is competitive with standard reference methods at single-pass cost. Independently, joint single-pass empirical Bayes matches grid-search cross-validation of the prior precision on essentially all dataset-variant combinations, eliminating the outer hyperparameter loop without measurable cost. Isolated optimization gaps on a few datasets reflect numerical rather than principled limitations of the framework.

AI.tldr: Training Bayesian neural networks by minimizing Bethe free energy instead of variational ELBO, enabling exact computation for certain likelihoods, empirical Bayes hyperparameter optimization in a single pass, and competitive performance at MAP-equivalent inference cost.
AI.motivation: Current Bayesian neural networks typically use variational inference with an Evidence Lower Bound (ELBO), which has a structural Jensen gap that cannot be closed by any variational family choice. The authors aim to develop a more principled objective that sits between MAP and ELBO, enabling exact computation for certain likelihoods and eliminating the need for outer-loop hyperparameter optimization.
AI.method: The authors propose training Bayesian neural networks by directly minimizing the Bethe free energy rather than maximizing the ELBO. For tree-structured factor graphs, the Bethe free energy is exact. They restrict the weight posterior to a last-layer Gaussian, yielding analytically tractable losses: for Gaussian likelihood it equals exact marginal likelihood, for probit likelihood it reduces to closed form via probit-Gaussian convolution. The Z-consistent prior formulation makes prior precision a differentiable parameter, enabling empirical Bayes optimization of weights, covariance, and hyperparameters in a single gradient pass.
AI.result: On 8 UCI regression and 12 UCI classification benchmarks with a single shared hyperparameter regime, Bethe training is competitive with standard reference methods at single-pass cost. Joint single-pass empirical Bayes matches grid-search cross-validation of prior precision on essentially all dataset-variant combinations. The method provides closed-form predictive distributions at MAP-equivalent inference cost, unlike ensemble and sampling-based methods.
AI.conclusion: The Bethe free energy provides a principled alternative to ELBO for Bayesian neural networks, eliminating the structural Jensen gap while maintaining computational efficiency. The framework supports exact computation for specific likelihoods, enables joint hyperparameter optimization without outer loops, and delivers competitive performance at inference costs comparable to MAP methods.

title: The Wittgensteinian Representation Hypothesis: Is Language the Attractor of Multimodal Convergence?
summary: Understanding why independently trained neural networks from different modalities converge toward shared representations, and where this convergence leads, remains an open question in representation learning. All existing evidence relies on symmetric similarity measures, which can detect convergence but are structurally blind to its direction. We introduce directional convergence analysis using cycle-kNN, an asymmetric alignment measure, applied across dozens of independently trained unimodal models spanning point clouds, vision, and language. We uncover a consistent directional asymmetry: non-language modalities move toward the neighborhood structure of language significantly more than the reverse, and this pattern holds across all model families and scales--yet is entirely invisible to symmetric measures. Mechanistic analysis traces the directionality to feature density asymmetry, whereby language representations occupy the most compact regions of representational space. The Information Bottleneck framework provides a principled interpretation: optimization under compression drives representations toward discrete, compositional structures characteristic of language. We formalize this as the Wittgensteinian Representation Hypothesis: the semantic structure of language is the asymptotic attractor of multimodal representation convergence.

AI.tldr: Researchers found that when neural networks from different modalities (vision, point clouds, language) are trained independently, they converge toward language's representation structure more than the reverse - a directional asymmetry that symmetric similarity measures miss.
AI.motivation: Understanding why neural networks from different modalities develop shared representations, and what direction this convergence takes, remains an open question in representation learning. Current symmetric similarity measures can detect convergence but cannot identify its directionality.
AI.method: Introduced directional convergence analysis using cycle-kNN (an asymmetric alignment measure) applied across dozens of independently trained unimodal models spanning point clouds, vision, and language. Used mechanistic analysis to trace directionality to feature density asymmetry and interpreted findings through the Information Bottleneck framework.
AI.result: Uncovered a consistent directional asymmetry: non-language modalities move toward the neighborhood structure of language significantly more than the reverse. This pattern holds across all model families and scales but is invisible to symmetric measures. Found that language representations occupy the most compact regions of representational space, suggesting optimization under compression drives representations toward discrete, compositional language-like structures.
AI.conclusion: Proposes the Wittgensteinian Representation Hypothesis: the semantic structure of language serves as the asymptotic attractor of multimodal representation convergence, with compression-driven optimization pushing representations toward language's discrete, compositional structures.

title: Zero-shot Imitation Learning by Latent Topology Mapping
summary: Imitation learning is effective for training agents when expert demonstrations are available, but collecting demonstrations for every complex task in an environment is costly. We study the long-horizon, goal-conditioned setting where a fixed demonstration dataset contains useful behavior, but not complete examples for every task the agent must solve. Existing imitation learning methods can learn strong policies from demonstrations, but when solving long-horizon tasks, small errors accumulate over long primitive-action trajectories and make zero-shot adaptation to new tasks unreliable. We introduce Zero-shot Agents from Latent Topologies (ZALT), an imitation-learning method that solves unseen start-goal tasks beyond those demonstrated during training. ZALT identifies latent hub states where trajectories converge or diverge, learns policies and a dynamics model over hub-to-hub transitions, and plans over the hub topology to complete new tasks. This topology makes demonstrated behaviors explicitly composable while compressing long tasks into shorter sequences of abstract transitions -- combined, these enable ZALT to perform zero-shot adaptation. In a complex 3D maze environment, ZALT achieves 55% zero-shot success on unseen tasks, compared to 6% for the strongest baseline.

AI.tldr: ZALT is a zero-shot imitation learning method that learns latent hub topologies from demonstrations and composes them to solve unseen long-horizon tasks.
AI.motivation: Collecting expert demonstrations for every complex task is expensive, and existing imitation learning methods struggle with zero-shot adaptation to unseen tasks due to error accumulation over long trajectories.
AI.method: ZALT identifies latent hub states (where trajectories converge/diverge), learns policies and dynamics models for hub-to-hub transitions, and plans over the learned hub topology to compose behaviors for new tasks.
AI.result: In complex 3D maze environments, ZALT achieves 55% zero-shot success on unseen tasks, dramatically outperforming the strongest baseline (6%).
AI.conclusion: Learning latent hub topologies makes demonstrated behaviors explicitly composable and enables effective zero-shot adaptation to unseen long-horizon tasks by compressing them into shorter abstract sequences.

title: Position: Avoid Overstretching LLMs for every Enterprise Task
summary: Enterprise workloads are dominated by deterministic, structured, and knowledge-dependent tasks operating under strict cost, latency, and reliability constraints. While these are often addressed through large language model (LLM) deployment or distillation into smaller models, we argue this is inefficient, unreliable, and misaligned with enterprise task structures. Instead, AI systems should treat language models as interfaces rather than monolithic engines, externalizing knowledge and computation into dedicated components for greater reliability, scalability, and transparency. Our theoretical evidences show that finite-capacity models cannot fully capture the breadth of knowledge required for enterprise tasks, creating inherent limits to efficiency and interpretability. Building on this, we take the position that language models should primarily be used for structured extraction in deterministic enterprise workflows, while computation and storage are delegated to knowledge bases and symbolic procedures. We formally demonstrate that such modular architectures are more reliable and maintainable than monolithic frameworks, offering a sustainable foundation for enterprise tasks.

AI.tldr: Enterprise AI should use language models as interfaces for structured extraction, not as monolithic engines, delegating computation and knowledge to external components for better reliability and scalability.
AI.motivation: Current approaches using LLMs or distilling them into smaller models for enterprise tasks are inefficient, unreliable, and misaligned with enterprise task structures that require deterministic operations with strict cost, latency, and reliability constraints.
AI.method: Propose treating language models as interfaces rather than monolithic engines, externalizing knowledge and computation into dedicated components (knowledge bases and symbolic procedures) for enterprise workflows. Formally demonstrate modular architectures' advantages.
AI.result: Theoretical evidence shows finite-capacity models cannot fully capture required enterprise knowledge, creating inherent limits. Modular architectures using language models for structured extraction with delegated computation are formally shown to be more reliable and maintainable.
AI.conclusion: Language models should primarily serve as interfaces for structured extraction in deterministic enterprise workflows, while computation and storage are delegated to knowledge bases and symbolic procedures, providing a more sustainable foundation for enterprise AI systems.

title: RubiConv -- Efficient Boundary-Respecting Convolutions
summary: Convolutional architectures have emerged as powerful alternatives to Transformers for sequence modeling. The primary advantage is that they offer improved theoretical sequence length complexity by leveraging the Fast Fourier Transform (FFT). However, this theoretical improvement does not always meaningfully land in practice. One critical obstacle is that applying standard FFTs is not amenable to the large-scale training pipeline wherein data is packed from different sources into a single sequence for hardware efficiency. Indeed, standard FFT algorithms are not easily amenable to document packing. Existing workarounds suffer from severe inefficiencies, crippling the practical performance of convolutional architectures. We close this gap with RubiConv, a novel algorithm for performing hardware-efficient, boundary-respecting convolutions on packed sequences. Extensive experiments show that RubiConv achieves significant speedups over both attention and standard FFT-based baselines. This work makes the theoretical efficiency of long convolutional models a practical reality for large-scale, real-world data packing.

AI.tldr: RubiConv is a novel algorithm that enables hardware-efficient convolutions on packed sequences, making the theoretical efficiency of long convolutional models practical for real-world data packing scenarios.
AI.motivation: While convolutional architectures offer better theoretical sequence length complexity than Transformers using FFT, standard FFT implementations don't work well with document packing in large-scale training pipelines. Existing workarounds are severely inefficient, preventing convolutional models from achieving their promised practical performance.
AI.method: RubiConv is a new algorithm designed specifically to perform hardware-efficient, boundary-respecting convolutions on packed sequences. It addresses the limitations of standard FFT algorithms when dealing with data that comes from different sources packed into single sequences for training efficiency.
AI.result: Extensive experiments show RubiConv achieves significant speedups over both attention-based models and standard FFT-based convolutional baselines. It successfully closes the gap between theoretical efficiency and practical implementation.
AI.conclusion: RubiConv makes the theoretical efficiency of long convolutional models a practical reality for large-scale, real-world applications with data packing, overcoming previous obstacles that limited the adoption of convolutional architectures for sequence modeling.

title: Towards a Virtual Neuroscientist: Autonomous Neuroimaging Analysis via Multi-Agent Collaboration
summary: Transforming neuroimaging data into clinically actionable biomarkers is a knowledge-intensive and labor-intensive process. Standardized workflows such as fMRIPrep have improved robustness and efficiency, but they are statically configured and cannot reason about downstream objectives, deliberate over alternative strategies, or close the loop between intermediate evidence and subsequent decisions in the way a human researcher would. This lack of closed-loop adaptation often leaves domain experts trapped in a cycle of manual trial-and-error to tune parameters and remediate pipeline failures, severely constraining the scalability of clinical biomarker development. To bridge this gap, we introduce NIAgent, a multi-agent system for autonomous end-to-end neuroimaging analysis. Unlike conventional flat tool-calling agents, NIAgent adopts a code-centric execution paradigm where specialist agents collaboratively synthesize and optimize executable programs over composable domain-specific primitives. This design enables robust, long-horizon workflow construction that adapts dynamically to runtime observations. Furthermore, we propose a hierarchical verification framework for autonomous quality control, integrating cohort-level metric screening with agentic visual inspection to drive evidence-grounded workflow remediation. Experiments on ADHD-200 and ADNI demonstrate that NIAgent outperforms standard workflow-based baselines in predictive performance while exhibiting sophisticated agentic behaviors, including strategy exploration and adaptive refinement.

AI.tldr: NIAgent is a multi-agent system that autonomously conducts neuroimaging analysis through collaborative code synthesis and adaptive workflow construction, outperforming static pipelines like fMRIPrep.
AI.motivation: Current neuroimaging analysis workflows (like fMRIPrep) are statically configured and lack adaptive reasoning capabilities, requiring manual trial-and-error tuning by domain experts, which severely limits the scalability of clinical biomarker development.
AI.method: NIAgent uses a multi-agent system with code-centric execution paradigm where specialist agents collaboratively synthesize and optimize executable programs over composable domain-specific primitives, featuring hierarchical verification framework for autonomous quality control.
AI.result: Experiments on ADHD-200 and ADNI datasets show NIAgent outperforms standard workflow-based baselines in predictive performance while demonstrating sophisticated agentic behaviors including strategy exploration and adaptive refinement.
