AI.conclusion: NIAgent represents an advance in autonomous neuroimaging analysis by enabling dynamic, adaptive workflow construction that can reason about downstream objectives and close the loop between evidence and decisions, addressing scalability limitations of current methods.

title: Sink vs. diagonal patterns as mechanisms for attention switch and oversmoothing prevention
summary: This paper studies the role of sinks and diagonal patterns as attention switch and anti-oversmoothing mechanisms. We analyze geometric conditions under which sinks can be represented, showing a necessary alignment between the embedding of the sink and all other embeddings. Next, we refine the current understanding of the role of sinks in oversmoothing prevention: we specify the conditions under which dense attention provably smooths more than sparse attention, and empirically verify that such conditions are often satisfied in practice. We further prove an equivalence between sinks and hard attention switch, in which the output of the attention is identically 0. Finally, we relax the hard attention switch by allowing token self-communication: we provide a quantitative comparison of the costs of representing sinks vs.\ diagonal patterns, showing why sinks are favored in pretrained transformers. The introduction and analysis of diagonal patterns and the generalization of the attention switch close the gap between what oversmoothing prevention requires and what sinks provide, while also establishing when and why attention layers act like MLPs if token communication is not necessary.

AI.tldr: The paper analyzes sinks and diagonal patterns as mechanisms for attention switching and preventing oversmoothing in transformers, establishing when attention layers behave like MLPs.
AI.motivation: To better understand the role of sinks in attention mechanisms, refine current understanding of oversmoothing prevention, and close the gap between what oversmoothing prevention requires versus what sinks actually provide in practice.
AI.method: Theoretical analysis of geometric conditions for sink representation, comparison of dense vs sparse attention smoothing, empirical verification of practical conditions, proof of equivalence between sinks and hard attention switch, and quantitative cost comparison of sinks vs diagonal patterns with token self-communication.
AI.result: Shows necessary alignment conditions for sink representation, identifies conditions where dense attention smooths more than sparse, proves sinks are equivalent to hard attention switch, demonstrates why sinks are favored over diagonal patterns in pretrained transformers, and establishes when attention layers act like MLPs.
AI.conclusion: The analysis of diagonal patterns and generalized attention switch fills the gap between oversmoothing prevention requirements and sink capabilities, explaining why sinks are preferred in transformers and clarifying when attention layers behave like MLPs when token communication isn't necessary.

title: Explainable Knowledge Tracing via Probabilistic Embeddings and Pattern-based Reasoning
summary: Knowledge Tracing (KT) models students' knowledge states based on learning interactions to predict performance. While deep learning-based KT models have boosted predictive accuracy, most models rely on deterministic vector embeddings and opaque latent state transitions, limiting interpretability regarding how specific past behaviors influence predictions. To address this limitation, we propose Probabilistic Logical Knowledge Tracing (PLKT), an interpretable KT framework that formulates prediction as a goal-conditioned evidence reasoning process over historical learning behaviors. Instead of representing knowledge states as deterministic vector embeddings, PLKT employs robust Beta-distributed probabilistic embeddings to represent student knowledge states. This probabilistic foundation allows us to model the uncertainty of historical behaviors and perform explicit logical operations (e.g., conjunction), constructing transparent reasoning paths that reveal how specific past interactions contribute to the prediction. Extensive experiments show that PLKT outperforms state-of-the-art KT methods while achieving superior interpretability. Our code is available at https://anonymous.4open.science/r/PLKT-D3CE/.

AI.tldr: PLKT introduces an interpretable knowledge tracing framework that replaces deterministic embeddings with probabilistic Beta distributions to model uncertainty and uses logical operations for transparent reasoning paths.
AI.motivation: Most deep learning-based Knowledge Tracing (KT) models rely on opaque latent state transitions and deterministic embeddings, which limits interpretability about how specific past learning behaviors influence performance predictions.
AI.method: PLKT formulates prediction as goal-conditioned evidence reasoning using Beta-distributed probabilistic embeddings to represent student knowledge states, enabling explicit logical operations (like conjunction) and transparent reasoning paths that connect historical behaviors to predictions.
AI.result: Extensive experiments show PLKT outperforms state-of-the-art KT methods while achieving superior interpretability, with code available publicly for verification and further research.
AI.conclusion: PLKT successfully addresses the interpretability limitation in KT by introducing probabilistic logical reasoning, demonstrating that interpretable models can achieve both high predictive accuracy and transparency in showing how past behaviors influence predictions.

title: Recovering Physical Dynamics from Discrete Observations via Intrinsic Differential Consistency
summary: Recovering continuous-time dynamics from discrete observations is difficult because local supervision (e.g., pointwise regression targets, derivative approximations, or equation residuals) loses fidelity as the observation interval grows. We replace local supervision with a global structural constraint: any flow representing autonomous dynamics must satisfy the semi-group property under time translation. We train a time-conditioned secant velocity field whose deviation from this property, which we call Symmetry Rupture, serves two purposes. As a training regularizer, it confines the hypothesis space to flows that compose consistently across temporal scales. As an inference oracle, it lets the solver select the largest step size that preserves internal consistency, replacing the local truncation error that conventional adaptive solvers depend on. On the diffusion-reaction benchmark under time-informed inference, our method reduces rollout RMSE by 87\% while using 5x fewer function evaluations than a Neural ODE baseline. In the more demanding direct auto-regressive setting, where the model must predict distant future frames without intermediate temporal cues, our adaptive solver allocates compute based on local geometric complexity -- maintaining the lowest rollout RMSE on two of three PDE benchmarks while baselines either diverge or require up to an order of magnitude more function evaluations to remain stable.

AI.tldr: Training neural ODEs with a semi-group symmetry constraint instead of local supervision, enabling adaptive step size selection based on internal consistency for more efficient and accurate dynamics prediction.
AI.motivation: Recovering continuous-time dynamics from discrete observations is challenging because local supervision methods (pointwise regression, derivative approximations, equation residuals) lose fidelity as observation intervals grow. Traditional approaches struggle with temporal scaling and consistency.
AI.method: Replaces local supervision with global structural constraint: autonomous dynamics flows must satisfy semi-group property under time translation. Trains time-conditioned secant velocity field and measures deviation from this property (called "Symmetry Rupture"). This serves as both training regularizer and inference oracle for adaptive step size selection.
AI.result: On diffusion-reaction benchmark: 87% reduction in rollout RMSE with 5x fewer function evaluations than Neural ODE baseline. In direct auto-regressive setting (predicting distant future frames without intermediate cues): maintains lowest rollout RMSE on 2 of 3 PDE benchmarks while baselines either diverge or require up to 10x more function evaluations to remain stable.
AI.conclusion: Semi-group symmetry provides a powerful global constraint for learning continuous-time dynamics, enabling more efficient and accurate prediction through adaptive computation allocation based on local geometric complexity, outperforming traditional locally-supervised methods.

title: NEXUS: Continual Learning of Symbolic Constraints for Safe and Robust Embodied Planning
summary: While Large Language Models (LLMs) have catalyzed progress in embodied intelligence, a fundamental gap between their inherent probabilistic uncertainty and the strict determinism and verifiable safety required in the physical world. To mitigate this gap, this paper introduces NEXUS, a modular framework designed for continual learning in embodied agents. Different from prior works that treat symbolic artifacts merely as static interfaces, NEXUS leverages them for symbolic grounding and knowledge evolution. The framework explicitly decouples physical feasibility from safety specifications: capability of agents is improved through closed-loop execution feedback, while probabilistic risk assessments are grounded into deterministic hard constraints to establish a rigorous pre-action defense. Experiments on SafeAgentBench demonstrate that NEXUS achieves superior task success rates while effectively refusing unsafe instructions, exhibiting robust defense against adversarial attacks, and progressively improving planning efficiency through knowledge accumulation.

AI.tldr: NEXUS is a modular framework for embodied agents that decouples physical feasibility from safety constraints, using symbolic artifacts for grounding knowledge and probabilistic risk assessments for deterministic safety guarantees.
AI.motivation: There is a fundamental gap between the probabilistic uncertainty inherent in Large Language Models (LLMs) and the strict determinism and verifiable safety required in the physical world for embodied intelligence.
AI.method: NEXUS leverages symbolic artifacts for symbolic grounding and knowledge evolution, explicitly decouples physical feasibility from safety specifications, improves agent capability through closed-loop execution feedback, and grounds probabilistic risk assessments into deterministic hard constraints for pre-action defense.
AI.result: Experiments on SafeAgentBench show that NEXUS achieves superior task success rates while effectively refusing unsafe instructions, exhibits robust defense against adversarial attacks, and progressively improves planning efficiency through knowledge accumulation.
AI.conclusion: NEXUS successfully bridges the gap between probabilistic LLMs and deterministic physical world requirements by providing a modular framework for continual learning in embodied agents with rigorous safety guarantees.

title: CUDABeaver: Benchmarking LLM-Based Automated CUDA Debugging
summary: Debugging CUDA programs has long been challenging because failures often arise from subtle interactions among hardware behavior, compiler decisions, memory hierarchy, and asynchronous execution. More importantly, with the rapid expansion of GPU usage across scientific computing, machine learning, graphics, and systems workloads, CUDA debugging has become more challenging than ever. Current evaluations of LLM-based CUDA programming largely miss this setting: a model can pass correctness tests with repair by degeneration, simplifying the CUDA code into a safer but slower program that abandons the original optimization structure. We introduce CUDABEAVER, a benchmark for CUDA debugging from real failing workspaces produced during LLM-based CUDA generation. Each task provides the broken candidate, native build/test commands, raw error evidence, and a single editable file. CUDABEAVER evaluates whether a fixer truly repairs the failing CUDA code or merely finds a slower test-passing replacement, reporting results by failure category, debugging trajectory, stagnation mode, and performance preservation. We further propose pass@k(M,C,A), a protocol-conditional CUDA debugging metric by making the fixer M, corpus C, and protocol axes Aexplicit. Using this metric across 213 tasks and seven frontier LLMs, we show that protocol-aware evaluation gives a more faithful view of CUDA debugging ability: when performance-loss tolerance is high, fixers appear much stronger, but even a minor stricter performance requirement can sharply reduce measured success, shifting scores by up to 40 percentage points.

AI.tldr: CUDABEAVER is a new benchmark for evaluating CUDA debugging capabilities in LLMs, focusing on real-world failures from LLM-generated CUDA code, with performance-aware evaluation metrics.
AI.motivation: Current LLM-based CUDA programming evaluations miss real debugging challenges - models can pass tests by degenerating optimized code into slower but safer versions, not truly fixing bugs while preserving performance.
AI.method: Created CUDABEAVER benchmark with 213 real failing CUDA workspaces from LLM generation. Each task includes broken code, build/test commands, error evidence, and an editable file. Introduced protocol-conditional metric pass@k(M,C,A) that explicitly considers fixer model, corpus, and performance requirements.
AI.result: Protocol-aware evaluation reveals performance requirements dramatically affect measured debugging success. When performance-loss tolerance is high, fixers appear strong (up to 40 percentage point difference), but stricter performance requirements sharply reduce success rates across 7 frontier LLMs.
AI.conclusion: CUDA debugging evaluation must account for performance preservation, not just correctness. CUDABEAVER provides realistic benchmarking, showing current LLMs struggle with true performance-preserving fixes for complex CUDA bugs.

title: Do Linear Probes Generalize Better in Persona Coordinates?
summary: It is becoming increasingly necessary to have monitors check for harmful behaviors during language model interactions, but text-only monitoring has not been sufficient. This is because models sometimes exhibit strategic deception and sandbagging, changing their behavior during evaluation. This motivates the use of white-box monitors like linear probes, which can read the model internals directly. Currently, such probes can fail under distribution shift, limiting their usefulness in real settings. We study whether there exists a low-dimensional subspace of the model internals that captures harmful behaviors more robustly, while leaving out spuriously correlative features. Inspired by the Assistant Axis and Persona Selection Model, we construct persona axes for deception and sycophancy using contrastive persona prompts. The first principal components, obtained by unsupervised PCA of the persona-specific vectors, cleanly separate harmful and harmless personas. Across 10 evaluation datasets, we show that persona-derived directions transfer non-trivially and probes trained on persona-PC projections generalize better than probes trained on raw activations. We also find that a unified axis consisting of multiple harmful and harmless behaviors improves generalization across behaviors and datasets. Overall, persona vectors provide a useful inductive bias for building more transferable behavior probes.

AI.tldr: Paper introduces persona axes using contrastive persona prompts and PCA to create robust white-box probes for detecting harmful behaviors in language models that generalize better across distribution shifts.
AI.motivation: Text-only monitoring insufficient for detecting strategic deception and sandbagging in language models. White-box monitors like linear probes can fail under distribution shift, limiting real-world usefulness.
AI.method: Construct persona axes for deception and sycophancy using contrastive persona prompts. Apply PCA to get first principal components that separate harmful/harmless personas. Train probes on persona-PC projections rather than raw activations.
AI.result: Persona-derived directions transfer non-trivially across 10 evaluation datasets. Probes trained on persona-PC projections generalize better than those on raw activations. Unified axis with multiple harmful/harmless behaviors improves generalization.
AI.conclusion: Persona vectors provide useful inductive bias for building more transferable behavior probes, enabling more robust monitoring of harmful model behaviors across distribution shifts.

title: Neurally-plausible radial basis kernels using distributed Fourier embeddings
summary: Coherent, continuous spatial representations are critical for synthesizing physical and perceptual phenomena into a single representational space. Radial basis kernels provide a path forward for this type of distributed representation. In this work, we aim to characterize and analyze common radial basis kernels realizable in the neurally-plausible framework of spatial semantic pointers. Further, we analyze previous radial basis kernel work based on grid cell-like representations and demonstrate that such representations are both capable of and optimal for realizing radial basis kernels.

AI.tldr: The paper analyzes radial basis kernels in spatial semantic pointers, showing grid cell-like representations are optimal for realizing these kernels.
AI.motivation: Coherent, continuous spatial representations are needed to integrate physical and perceptual phenomena into unified representational spaces. Radial basis kernels offer a promising approach for distributed spatial representations.
AI.method: Characterizing and analyzing common radial basis kernels within the neurally-plausible framework of spatial semantic pointers. Analyzing previous work on grid cell-like representations to determine their capabilities and optimality.
AI.result: Demonstrates that grid cell-like representations are both capable of and optimal for realizing radial basis kernels in spatial semantic pointer frameworks.
AI.conclusion: Grid cell-based representations provide an optimal solution for implementing radial basis kernels in neurally-plausible spatial representation systems, supporting the synthesis of physical and perceptual phenomena into coherent spatial frameworks.

title: The Geometric Structure of Models Learning Sparse Data
summary: The manifold hypothesis (MH) is often used to explain how machine learning can overcome the curse of dimensionality. However, the MH is only applicable in regimes where the training data provides a sufficiently dense sample of the underlying low-dimensional data manifold, or where such a low-dimensional manifold is conceivably present. We describe the regimes where the MH is not applicable as sparse. In this paper, we demonstrate that models succeed in the sparse regime by exploiting a highly structured local geometry, a property we formalize as normal alignment. We prove that normal-aligned classifiers -- whose input-output Jacobians are rank-one and align perfectly with the training data -- minimize the training objective under norm constraints and achieve maximal local robustness under a non-zero Jacobian constraint. For continuous piecewise-affine deep networks, normal alignment manifests geometrically as centroid alignment within the network's induced power diagram partition and results from the feature-learning regime. Motivated by these theoretical insights, we introduce GrokAlign, a regularization strategy that actively induces normal alignment. We demonstrate that GrokAlign significantly accelerates the training dynamics of deep networks relevant to the grokking phenomenon. Furthermore, we apply the principle of normal alignment to Recursive Feature Machines (RFMs) to introduce Recursive Feature Alignment Machines (RFAMs). We show that RFAMs exhibit greater adversarial robustness compared to RFMs when trained on tabular data.

AI.tldr: The paper challenges when the manifold hypothesis applies, showing models succeed in sparse regimes via "normal alignment" - a structured local geometry property that enables robust classifiers, which they leverage to accelerate training and improve adversarial robustness.
AI.motivation: The manifold hypothesis (MH) is commonly used to explain success in high-dimensional machine learning, but it only applies when data densely samples low-dimensional manifolds. The paper investigates what enables success in sparse regimes where MH doesn't apply.
AI.method: 1) Formalize "normal alignment" property where classifiers' input-output Jacobians are rank-one and align perfectly with training data. 2) Prove normal-aligned classifiers minimize training objective and achieve maximal local robustness. 3) For deep networks, show normal alignment manifests as centroid alignment in power diagram partitions. 4) Introduce GrokAlign regularization to induce normal alignment. 5) Apply principle to RFMs to create RFAMs with improved robustness.
AI.result: 1) GrokAlign significantly accelerates training dynamics for deep networks experiencing grokking. 2) Recursive Feature Alignment Machines (RFAMs) exhibit greater adversarial robustness than standard RFMs on tabular data. 3) Theoretical results show normal-aligned classifiers achieve optimal properties under constraints.
AI.conclusion: Models succeed in sparse regimes via normal alignment, a structured local geometry property. By actively inducing this property through GrokAlign regularization and extending it to RFMs as RFAMs, the paper demonstrates improved training dynamics and adversarial robustness, providing a principled alternative to the manifold hypothesis for understanding success in high-dimensional sparse settings.

title: Strategic commitments shape collective cybersecurity under AI inequality
summary: The growing integration of AI into cybersecurity is reshaping the balance between attackers and defenders. When access to advanced AI-enabled defence tools is uneven, resource-limited defenders may be unable to adopt effective protection, creating persistent system vulnerabilities. We study the impact of differential AI access using an evolutionary game-theoretic model in a finite population. We first show that when high-capability defence is costly, the population is driven toward low-cost, weak-defence behaviour, sustaining attacks and weakening long-run security. To address this problem, we introduce differential access to AI defence tools by allowing defenders to choose between low- and high-capability protection based on their resources. We then examine the role of a small group of committed defenders who always adopt strong defence and influence others through social learning. Although commitment increases the prevalence of strong defence, it alone cannot stabilise secure outcomes due to high defence costs. We therefore incorporate a targeted subsidy to remove the cost disadvantage from committed defenders. Our analysis shows that subsidised commitment significantly increases strong defence adoption, suppresses successful attacks, and improves overall system resilience. Simulations across a broad parameter space confirm that subsidies consistently outperform commitment alone. In addition, social-welfare analysis shows improved defender outcomes while keeping attacker gains low. These findings suggest that targeted support for key defenders can be an effective mechanism for stabilising cybersecurity in AI-driven environments and provide a theoretical bridge between cybersecurity policy, AI governance, and strategic allocation of defensive AI capabilities.

AI.tldr: Targeted subsidies for committed defenders using high-capability AI tools can stabilize cybersecurity in AI-driven environments by overcoming cost barriers and improving overall system resilience.
AI.motivation: The uneven integration of AI into cybersecurity creates vulnerabilities when resource-limited defenders cannot adopt effective AI-enabled protection, potentially shifting the balance in favor of attackers and creating persistent system vulnerabilities.
AI.method: Using evolutionary game-theoretic modeling in finite populations to study differential AI access, allowing defenders to choose between low- and high-capability protection based on resources, and examining committed defenders with targeted subsidies.
AI.result: Subsidized commitment significantly increases strong defense adoption, suppresses successful attacks, improves system resilience, and consistently outperforms commitment alone across parameter space simulations. Social-welfare analysis shows improved defender outcomes while keeping attacker gains low.
AI.conclusion: Targeted support for key defenders can effectively stabilize cybersecurity in AI-driven environments, providing a theoretical bridge between cybersecurity policy, AI governance, and strategic allocation of defensive AI capabilities.

title: CUDAHercules: Benchmarking Hardware-Aware Expert-level CUDA Optimization for LLMs
summary: Large language models show promise for automated CUDA programming, however even the strongest coding models (e.g., Claude-Opus-4.6) may still fall short of expert-level, architecture-aware optimization. We introduce CUDAHercules, a benchmark that evaluates generated CUDA against end-to-end human-expert SOTA systems. It spans single kernels, module-level operators, full applications, and unsolved challenge tasks across Ampere, Hopper, and Blackwell GPUs, with end-to-end tasks gated by domain-specific semantic validators. Evaluating models such as Claude-Opus-4.6 and GPT-5.4 shows a large gap between runnable CUDA and expert CUDA engineering: models often compile and pass tests, but rarely recover the optimization strategies needed to match expert performance. Application semantics further reduce success, and iterative or tool-augmented feedback can improve correctness while drifting toward slow fallback implementations. These results show that automated CUDA programming remains far from fully solved and requires stronger hardware reasoning, better tool use, and training objectives that connect code understanding to hardware architecture-grounded intelligence.

AI.tldr: CUDAHercules is a benchmark revealing that current LLMs, despite generating runnable CUDA code, fail to achieve expert-level hardware-aware optimizations needed for competitive performance across modern GPU architectures.
AI.motivation: While large language models show promise for automated CUDA programming, they fall short of expert-level, architecture-aware optimization. There's a need to measure the gap between generated CUDA code and human-expert systems across different GPU architectures and task complexities.
AI.method: Introduced CUDAHercules benchmark that evaluates generated CUDA against end-to-end human-expert state-of-the-art systems. It spans multiple levels: single kernels, module-level operators, full applications, and unsolved challenge tasks across Ampere, Hopper, and Blackwell GPUs. The benchmark includes domain-specific semantic validators and evaluates models like Claude-Opus-4.6 and GPT-5.4.
AI.result: Evaluation shows a large gap between runnable CUDA and expert CUDA engineering. Models often compile and pass tests, but rarely recover the optimization strategies needed to match expert performance. Application semantics further reduce success, and iterative or tool-augmented feedback can improve correctness but drifts toward slow fallback implementations.
AI.conclusion: Automated CUDA programming remains far from fully solved and requires stronger hardware reasoning, better tool use, and training objectives that connect code understanding to hardware architecture-grounded intelligence.

title: From Passive Reuse to Active Reasoning: Grounding Large Language Models for Neuro-Symbolic Experience Replay
summary: While experience replay is essential for data efficiency in reinforcement learning (RL), standard methods treat the replay buffer as a passive memory system, prioritizing samples based on numerical prediction errors rather than their semantic significance. This approach stands in contrast to human learning, which accelerates mastery by actively abstracting fragmented experiences into behavioral rules. To bridge this gap, we propose Neuro-Symbolic Experience Replay (NSER), a framework that transforms experience replay from a passive sample reuse mechanism into an active engine for knowledge construction. Specifically, NSER addresses the incompatibility between linguistic reasoning and numerical optimization through a novel neuro-symbolic grounding pipeline. It leverages Large Language Models (LLMs) in a zero-shot manner to induce candidate behavioral rules from accumulated trajectories, grounds these insights into differentiable first-order logic representations, and utilizes the resulting symbolic structures to dynamically reweight the replay distribution. By allowing abstract knowledge to directly shape policy optimization, NSER achieves consistent superior sample efficiency and convergence speed across reactive, rule-based, and procedural benchmarks.

AI.tldr: Neuro-Symbolic Experience Replay (NSER) enhances RL by using LLMs to extract behavioral rules from trajectories and using symbolic logic to actively reweight replay samples, improving sample efficiency.
AI.motivation: Standard experience replay treats buffer as passive memory prioritizing prediction errors, while human learning accelerates by abstracting experiences into behavioral rules. There's a gap between linguistic reasoning and numerical optimization in RL.
AI.method: NSER uses zero-shot LLMs to induce behavioral rules from trajectories, grounds insights into differentiable first-order logic representations, and uses symbolic structures to dynamically reweight replay distribution.
AI.result: NSER achieves consistent superior sample efficiency and convergence speed across reactive, rule-based, and procedural benchmarks compared to standard methods.
AI.conclusion: NSER transforms experience replay from passive sample reuse to active knowledge construction, bridging the gap between linguistic reasoning and numerical optimization for more efficient RL.

title: Transformers Can Implement Preconditioned Richardson Iteration for In-Context Gaussian Kernel Regression
summary: Mechanistic accounts of in-context learning (ICL) have identified iterative algorithms for linear regression and related linear prediction tasks, often using linear or ReLU attention variants. For nonlinear ICL, prior work has related softmax and kernelized attention to functional-gradient-type dynamics, but it remains unclear whether a standard transformer with softmax attention can implement a convergent solver with an end-to-end prediction-error guarantee. In this paper, we study in-context kernel ridge regression (KRR) with Gaussian kernels and show that a standard softmax-attention transformer can approximate the KRR predictor during its forward pass by implementing preconditioned Richardson iteration on the associated kernel linear system. Under bounded-data assumptions, we construct a single-head transformer with $O(\log(1/ε))$ blocks and MLP width $O(\sqrt{N/ε})$ that achieves $ε$-accurate prediction for prompts of length $N$. Our construction reveals a functional decomposition within the transformer architecture: softmax attention produces a row-normalized Gaussian-kernel operator needed for cross-token interactions, while ReLU MLP layers act locally to approximate the intra-token scalar arithmetic required by the update. Empirically, we train GPT-2-style transformers on Gaussian-process regression tasks to further test the preconditioned Richardson interpretation. Through linear probing, we compare the transformer's layer-wise predictions with the step-wise outputs of classical KRR solvers and find that its error profiles align most consistently with preconditioned Richardson iteration. Ablation studies further support this interpretation. Together, our theory and experiments identify preconditioned Richardson iteration as a concrete mechanism that softmax-attention transformers can realize for nonlinear in-context Gaussian-kernel regression.

AI.tldr: Transformers with softmax attention can implement preconditioned Richardson iteration to solve kernel ridge regression, achieving end-to-end prediction error guarantees for nonlinear in-context learning.
AI.motivation: Prior work showed transformers can implement linear algorithms for in-context learning, but it was unclear if standard softmax-attention transformers could implement convergent solvers with prediction-error guarantees for nonlinear tasks.
AI.method: Theoretical construction of single-head transformer with O(log(1/ε)) blocks and MLP width O(√N/ε) to approximate KRR predictor via preconditioned Richardson iteration on kernel linear systems, plus empirical evaluation training GPT-2-style transformers on Gaussian-process regression tasks.
AI.result: Transformers achieve ε-accurate prediction for prompts of length N; linear probing shows error profiles align with preconditioned Richardson iteration; ablation studies support this interpretation.
AI.conclusion: Preconditioned Richardson iteration is a concrete mechanism that softmax-attention transformers can realize for nonlinear in-context Gaussian-kernel regression, revealing functional decomposition where attention handles cross-token interactions and MLPs handle intra-token arithmetic.

title: SimWorld Studio: Automatic Environment Generation with Evolving Coding Agent for Embodied Agent Learning
summary: LLM/VLM-based digital agents have advanced rapidly thanks to scalable sandboxes for coding, web navigation, and computer use, which provide rich interactive training grounds. In contrast, embodied agents still lack abundant, diverse, and automatically generated 3D environments for interactive learning. Existing embodied simulators rely on manually crafted scenes or procedural templates, while recent LLM-based 3D generation systems mainly produce static scenes rather than deployable environments with verifiable tasks and standard learning interfaces. We introduce SimWorld Studio, an open-source platform built on Unreal Engine 5 for generating evolving embodied learning environments. At its core is SimCoder, a tool/skill-augmented coding agent that writes and executes engine-level code to construct physically grounded 3D worlds from language/image instructions. SimCoder self-evolves by using verifier feedback (e.g., compilation errors, physics checks, VLM critiques) to revise environments and autonomously add reusable tools and skills to its library. Generated worlds are exported as Gym-style environments for embodied agent learning. SimWorld Studio further enables co-evolution between environment generation and embodied learning: agent performance feedback guides SimCoder to generate adaptive curricula near the learner's capability frontier, so that environments become increasingly challenging as the embodied agent improves. Three case studies on embodied navigation show that self-evolution improves generation reliability, generated environments substantially improve embodied agent performance that generalizes to unseen benchmarks, and co-evolution yields an 18-point success-rate gain over fixed-environment learning and a 40-point gain over an untrained agent.

AI.tldr: SimWorld Studio is an open-source platform using a tool-augmented coding agent (SimCoder) to generate evolving 3D embodied learning environments from language/image instructions, enabling co-evolution between environment generation and embodied agent learning through adaptive curricula.
AI.motivation: Embodied agents lack abundant, diverse, and automatically generated 3D environments for interactive learning. Existing simulators rely on manually crafted scenes or procedural templates, while recent LLM-based 3D generation systems mainly produce static scenes without deployable environments with verifiable tasks and standard learning interfaces.
AI.method: SimWorld Studio built on Unreal Engine 5 uses SimCoder, a tool/skill-augmented coding agent that writes and executes engine-level code to construct physically grounded 3D worlds from language/image instructions. It self-evolves using verifier feedback (compilation errors, physics checks, VLM critiques) and autonomously adds reusable tools/skills to its library. Generated worlds are exported as Gym-style environments with co-evolution between environment generation and embodied learning via adaptive curricula.
AI.result: Three case studies on embodied navigation show that: 1) self-evolution improves generation reliability, 2) generated environments substantially improve embodied agent performance that generalizes to unseen benchmarks, and 3) co-evolution yields an 18-point success-rate gain over fixed-environment learning and a 40-point gain over an untrained agent.
AI.conclusion: SimWorld Studio provides a scalable platform for generating evolving embodied learning environments, addressing the lack of diverse 3D environments for interactive learning. The co-evolution approach enables adaptive curricula that improve both environment generation quality and embodied agent performance through mutual feedback loops.

title: When Independent Sampling Outperforms Agentic Reasoning
summary: We study how to allocate inference-time compute for competitive programming under fixed budgets. Evaluating 216 Codeforces problems across Divisions 1-3, we compare agent-based reasoning with repeated independent sampling (k-shot) as a function of both cost and number of model calls. Across models and difficulty levels, k-shot consistently achieves a better accuracy-cost and accuracy-query tradeoff. This gap persists despite prompt caching in agent frameworks, indicating lower per-call effectiveness. Our results show that, for self-contained algorithmic tasks, independent exploration can outperform deeper agentic reasoning under realistic resource constraints. We also provide a budget-allocation analysis when the inference budget is fixed, and prove that a cost-optimal solver minimizes the principled metric log failure likelihood per dollar.

AI.tldr: For competitive programming tasks, repeated independent sampling (k-shot) outperforms agent-based reasoning in accuracy-cost tradeoffs under fixed compute budgets.
AI.motivation: There's a need to understand how to best allocate inference-time compute for competitive programming problems under fixed computational budgets, comparing different reasoning approaches.
AI.method: Evaluated 216 Codeforces problems across Divisions 1-3, comparing agent-based reasoning with repeated independent sampling (k-shot) as functions of both cost and number of model calls. Also conducted budget-allocation analysis when inference budget is fixed.
AI.result: k-shot consistently achieves better accuracy-cost and accuracy-query tradeoffs across models and difficulty levels. This gap persists despite prompt caching in agent frameworks, indicating lower per-call effectiveness of agent-based reasoning.
AI.conclusion: For self-contained algorithmic tasks, independent exploration can outperform deeper agentic reasoning under realistic resource constraints. A cost-optimal solver minimizes the principled metric log failure likelihood per dollar.

title: VulTriage: Triple-Path Context Augmentation for LLM-Based Vulnerability Detection
summary: Automated vulnerability detection is a fundamental task in software security, yet existing learning-based methods still struggle to capture the structural dependencies, domain-specific vulnerability knowledge, and complex program semantics required for accurate detection. Recent Large Language Models (LLMs) have shown strong code understanding ability, but directly prompting them with raw source code often leads to missed vulnerabilities or false alarms, especially when vulnerable and benign functions differ only in subtle semantic details. To address this, we propose VulTriage, a triple-path context augmentation framework for LLM-based vulnerability detection. VulTriage enhances the LLM input through three complementary paths: a Control Path that extracts and verbalizes AST, CFG, and DFG information to expose control and data dependencies; a Knowledge Path that retrieves relevant CWE-derived vulnerability patterns and examples through hybrid dense--sparse retrieval; and a Semantic Path that summarizes the functional behavior of the code before the final judgment. These contexts are integrated into a unified instruction to guide the LLM toward more reliable vulnerability reasoning. Experiments on the PrimeVul pair test set show that VulTriage achieves state-of-the-art performance, outperforming existing deep learning and LLM-based baselines on key pair-wise and classification metrics. Further ablation studies verify the effectiveness of each path, and additional experiments on the Kotlin dataset demonstrate the generalization ability of VulTriage under low-resource and class-imbalanced settings. Our code is available at https://github.com/vinsontang1/VulTriage

AI.tldr: VulTriage is a triple-path context augmentation framework that enhances LLM-based vulnerability detection by integrating structural, knowledge, and semantic information to better capture subtle vulnerability patterns.
AI.motivation: Existing learning-based vulnerability detection methods struggle with structural dependencies, domain-specific knowledge, and complex program semantics. LLMs have shown strong code understanding but often miss subtle vulnerabilities when directly prompted with raw source code.
AI.method: Proposes VulTriage, a triple-path context augmentation framework with three complementary paths: Control Path (extracts AST, CFG, DFG information), Knowledge Path (retrieves CWE-derived patterns via hybrid dense-sparse retrieval), and Semantic Path (summarizes functional behavior). These contexts are integrated into unified instructions for LLM reasoning.
AI.result: Achieves state-of-the-art performance on PrimeVul pair test set, outperforming existing deep learning and LLM-based baselines on key metrics. Ablation studies confirm each path's effectiveness, and experiments on Kotlin dataset show generalization ability in low-resource, class-imbalanced settings.
AI.conclusion: VulTriage effectively addresses limitations of existing vulnerability detection approaches by augmenting LLMs with structured context about program dependencies, vulnerability knowledge, and semantic behavior, leading to more accurate and reliable detection.

title: ShifaMind: A Multiplicative Concept Bottleneck for Interpretable ICD-10 Coding
summary: Automated ICD-10 coding from clinical discharge summaries requires models that are both accurate on long-tailed multi-label classification tasks and interpretable to clinicians. Concept Bottleneck Models (CBMs) offer a principled framework for interpretability by routing predictions through human-interpretable concepts, but this transparency often comes at a cost: compressing rich clinical text representations into a narrow concept layer can restrict gradient flow and limit predictive capacity. We present ShifaMind, a concept-grounded architecture built around a Multiplicative Concept Bottleneck (MCB), which changes the form, rather than the width, of the bottleneck. Instead of projecting through a narrow concept layer, ShifaMind uses a learned multiplicative gate over a concept-grounded representation while retaining a scalar concept interface for inspection. On MIMIC-IV top-50 ICD-10 coding, ShifaMind achieves performance competitive with LAAT, the strongest baseline, across F1, AUC, and ranking metrics, while outperforming five additional ICD-coding baselines and providing concept-mediated explanations. Its substantial gains over a capacity-matched Vanilla CBM in both predictive performance and interpretability-oriented metrics highlight the importance of the bottleneck design.

AI.tldr: ShifaMind introduces a Multiplicative Concept Bottleneck architecture for ICD-10 coding that maintains competitive performance while providing interpretable concept-mediated explanations, outperforming traditional Concept Bottleneck Models.
AI.motivation: Automated ICD-10 coding requires both accuracy on long-tailed multi-label classification tasks and interpretability for clinicians. Traditional Concept Bottleneck Models (CBMs) offer interpretability but at the cost of predictive capacity due to compressing rich clinical text representations into narrow concept layers that restrict gradient flow.
AI.method: ShifaMind uses a Multiplicative Concept Bottleneck (MCB) that changes the form rather than width of the bottleneck. Instead of projecting through a narrow concept layer, it employs a learned multiplicative gate over a concept-grounded representation while retaining a scalar concept interface for inspection.
AI.result: On MIMIC-IV top-50 ICD-10 coding, ShifaMind achieves performance competitive with LAAT (the strongest baseline) across F1, AUC, and ranking metrics, while outperforming five additional ICD-coding baselines. It provides concept-mediated explanations and shows substantial gains over capacity-matched Vanilla CBM in both predictive performance and interpretability-oriented metrics.
AI.conclusion: The bottleneck design is crucial for balancing predictive performance and interpretability in clinical coding tasks. ShifaMind demonstrates that changing the bottleneck form (rather than width) via multiplicative gating can maintain competitive performance while preserving interpretability through concept-mediated explanations.

title: Don't Click That: Teaching Web Agents to Resist Deceptive Interfaces
comment: Accepted to ACL 2026 Main Conference. 23 pages, 8 figures, 19 tables
summary: Vision-language model (VLM) based web agents demonstrate impressive autonomous GUI interaction but remain vulnerable to deceptive interface elements. Existing approaches either detect deception without task integration or document attacks without proposing defenses. We formalize deception-aware web agent defense and propose DUDE (Deceptive UI Detector & Evaluator), a two-stage framework combining hybrid-reward learning with asymmetric penalties and experience summarization to distill failure patterns into transferable guidance. We introduce RUC (Real UI Clickboxes), a benchmark of 1,407 scenarios spanning four domains and deception categories. Experiments show DUDE reduces deception susceptibility by 53.8% while maintaining task performance, establishing an effective foundation for robust web agent deployment.

AI.tldr: DUDE is a deception-aware web agent defense framework that reduces vulnerability to deceptive UI elements by 53.8% while maintaining task performance, evaluated on a new benchmark called RUC.
AI.motivation: Vision-language model based web agents show impressive GUI interaction capabilities but are vulnerable to deceptive interface elements (like fake buttons, misleading links, etc.). Existing approaches either detect deception without integrating it into the task execution process or just document attacks without proposing actual defense mechanisms.
AI.method: Proposes DUDE (Deceptive UI Detector & Evaluator), a two-stage framework that combines: 1) Hybrid-reward learning with asymmetric penalties, and 2) Experience summarization to distill failure patterns into transferable guidance. The framework is evaluated on RUC (Real UI Clickboxes), a new benchmark of 1,407 scenarios spanning four domains and deception categories.
AI.result: DUDE reduces deception susceptibility by 53.8% while maintaining task performance. The framework establishes an effective foundation for robust web agent deployment in real-world scenarios with deceptive UI elements.
AI.conclusion: DUDE represents a significant advancement in making web agents more robust against deceptive interfaces by formalizing deception-aware defense, providing both detection and action mechanisms, and validating effectiveness on a comprehensive benchmark.

title: NeuralBench: A Unifying Framework to Benchmark NeuroAI Models
summary: Deep learning and large public datasets have recently catalyzed the proliferation of AI models for processing brain recordings. However, systematically evaluating these models remains a challenge: not only do the preprocessing pipelines, training and finetuning approaches largely vary across studies, but their downstream evaluation is often limited to small sets of tasks and/or datasets. Here, we present NeuralBench: a unified framework for benchmarking AI models of brain activity. We accompany this framework with NeuralBench-EEG v1.0 -- a large EEG benchmark that includes 36 electroencephalography (EEG) tasks and 14 deep learning architectures, and is evaluated on 94 datasets accessed through a standardized interface. This first EEG-focused release already highlights two main findings. First, current foundation models only marginally outperform task-specific models. Second, a large set of tasks (e.g. cognitive decoding, clinical predictions) remain highly challenging, even for the best models. Critically, NeuralBench is designed for the integration of new tasks, datasets, models, and neuroimaging modalities, as illustrated by preliminary extensions to MEG and fMRI datasets and models. Through this white paper, we invite the community to expand this open-source framework and work together toward a unified benchmarking standard for neuroimaging models.

AI.tldr: NeuralBench is a unified benchmarking framework for AI models of brain activity, with an initial EEG benchmark showing that foundation models only slightly outperform task-specific models, and many cognitive/clinical tasks remain challenging.
AI.motivation: The proliferation of AI models for brain recordings lacks systematic evaluation due to varying preprocessing pipelines, training approaches, and limited downstream evaluation across small sets of tasks/datasets.
AI.method: Created NeuralBench framework with NeuralBench-EEG v1.0 benchmark including 36 EEG tasks and 14 deep learning architectures evaluated on 94 datasets through standardized interfaces; designed for extensibility to new modalities.
AI.result: Foundation models only marginally outperform task-specific models; many cognitive decoding and clinical prediction tasks remain highly challenging even for best models; framework demonstrates extensibility to MEG and fMRI.
AI.conclusion: NeuralBench provides an open-source, extensible framework for unified benchmarking of neuroimaging AI models, inviting community collaboration to establish benchmarking standards across modalities.

title: EpiGraph: A Knowledge Graph and Benchmark for Evidence-Intensive Reasoning in Epilepsy
summary: Epilepsy diagnosis and treatment require evidence-intensive reasoning across heterogeneous clinical knowledge, including biosignal patterns, genetic mechanisms, pharmacogenomics, treatment strategies, and patient outcomes. In this work, we present \textsc{EpiGraph}, a large-scale epilepsy knowledge graph and benchmark for evaluating knowledge-augmented clinical reasoning. \textsc{EpiGraph} integrates 48,166 peer-reviewed papers and seven clinical resources into a heterogeneous graph containing 24,324 entities and 32,009 evidence-grounded triplets across five clinical layers. Built upon this graph, \textsc{EpiBench} defines five clinically motivated tasks spanning clinical decision-making, EEG report generation, pharmacogenomic precision medicine, treatment recommendation, and deep research planning. We evaluate six LLMs under both standard and Graph-RAG settings. Results show that integrating \textsc{EpiGraph} consistently improves performance across all tasks, with the largest gains observed in pharmacogenomic reasoning (+30--41\%). Our findings demonstrate that structured epilepsy knowledge substantially enhances evidence-grounded clinical reasoning and provides a practical benchmark framework for evaluating knowledge-augmented LLMs in real-world neurological settings. Our code is available at: https://github.com/LabRAI/EEG-KG.

AI.tldr: EpiGraph is a large-scale epilepsy knowledge graph integrating multiple clinical sources, with EpiBench providing benchmark tasks showing that structured knowledge integration significantly improves LLM performance in epilepsy clinical reasoning tasks.
AI.motivation: Epilepsy diagnosis and treatment require complex reasoning across heterogeneous clinical knowledge domains (biosignals, genetics, pharmacogenomics, treatments, outcomes), necessitating an integrated knowledge framework to support evidence-based clinical decision-making.
AI.method: Built EpiGraph by integrating 48,166 peer-reviewed papers and 7 clinical resources into a heterogeneous graph with 24,324 entities and 32,009 evidence-grounded triplets across 5 clinical layers. Created EpiBench with 5 clinical reasoning tasks and evaluated 6 LLMs in standard vs Graph-RAG settings.
AI.result: Integrating EpiGraph consistently improved LLM performance across all tasks, with largest gains in pharmacogenomic reasoning (+30-41%). Structured epilepsy knowledge substantially enhances evidence-grounded clinical reasoning capabilities.
AI.conclusion: EpiGraph and EpiBench provide a practical benchmark framework for evaluating knowledge-augmented LLMs in real-world neurological settings, demonstrating that structured clinical knowledge significantly improves evidence-based clinical reasoning in epilepsy.

title: MathConstraint: Automated Generation of Verified Combinatorial Reasoning Instances for LLMs
summary: We introduce MathConstraint, a hard, adaptive benchmark for evaluating the combinatorial reasoning capabilities of LLMs. We combine constraint satisfaction problems with rigorous solver-based verification and design an adaptive generator to create instances that remain challenging as the LLMs improve in their reasoning capabilities. Unlike existing benchmarks that quickly saturate on fixed datasets or use LLM-as-a-judge for checking solutions,MathConstraint uses parameterized problem types that enable scalable generation of arbitrarily difficult and automatically verifiable instances. We release MathConstraint-Easy ($266$ instances), on which frontier models achieve between $72.6\%$ (gemini-3.1-flash-lite) and $87.6\%$ (gpt-5.5) accuracy, and MathConstraint ($329$ instances) on which the same models drop to between $18.5\%$ (claude-4.6-sonnet) and $66.9\%$ (gpt-5.5) accuracy, demonstrating the resilience of our benchmark generator against rapid progress in LLM reasoning capabilities. We evaluate 12 frontier and open-weight models with and without access to a sandboxed Python environment that includes generic SAT/SMT solvers. Tool access roughly doubles frontier accuracy on MathConstraint (mean $+28$pp; up to $+52$pp for claude-4.6-sonnet). Further, halving the tool-call budget from $8$ to $4$ rounds erases up to $37$ points -- a sensitivity that most single-budget benchmarks miss. We release the generator, dataset, and evaluation harness as a robust environment for studying combinatorial reasoning and tool-use behavior under adversarially-tunable difficulty.

AI.tldr: MathConstraint is a hard, adaptive benchmark for evaluating combinatorial reasoning in LLMs that combines constraint satisfaction problems with solver-based verification, remaining challenging as models improve.
AI.motivation: Existing benchmarks either saturate quickly on fixed datasets or rely on LLM-as-a-judge for solution checking, failing to provide scalable, automatically verifiable instances that maintain challenge as LLM reasoning capabilities improve.
AI.method: The paper introduces parameterized problem types enabling scalable generation of arbitrarily difficult instances, uses constraint satisfaction problems with rigorous solver-based verification, and includes an adaptive generator that creates challenging instances as LLMs improve.
AI.result: Models achieve 72.6-87.6% accuracy on MathConstraint-Easy but drop to 18.5-66.9% on harder MathConstraint, showing benchmark resilience. Tool access doubles frontier accuracy (mean +28pp), and halving tool-call budget from 8 to 4 rounds erases up to 37 points.
AI.conclusion: MathConstraint provides a robust environment for studying combinatorial reasoning and tool-use behavior with adversarially-tunable difficulty, demonstrating significant performance gaps and sensitivity to tool access that existing benchmarks miss.

title: WindINR: Latent-State INR for Fast Local Wind Query and Correction in Complex Terrain
summary: Many downstream decisions in complex terrain require fast wind estimates at a small number of user-specified locations and heights for a given forecast valid time, rather than another dense forecast field on a fixed grid. We present WindINR, a latent-state implicit neural representation framework for continuous high-resolution local wind query and sparse-observation correction. WindINR maps static terrain descriptors, a low-resolution background field, and continuous query coordinates to a high-resolution wind state through a latent-conditioned decoder. To enable rapid inference-time correction, WindINR separates reusable representation learning from sample-specific latent-state correction. During training, a privileged encoder infers a reference latent state from high-resolution supervision, a deployable latent predictor estimates an initial latent state from inference-time inputs alone, and their discrepancies are summarized into a dataset-adaptive Gaussian prior over latent corrections. At inference time, within the WindINR module, network weights remain fixed and only the latent state is updated by minimizing a regularized correction objective using sparse observations and their uncertainty. In controlled OSSEs over the Senja region, including a UAV-aided approach scenario and random-observation robustness tests, WindINR improves local high-resolution wind estimates by updating only a compact latent state rather than the full network. The corrected representation remains continuously queryable at arbitrary coordinates and, in our CPU benchmark, yields about a $2.6\times$ online-correction speedup over full-network fine-tuning, suggesting a practical interface between kilometer-scale background products, sparse local observations, and wind queries in complex terrain.

AI.tldr: WindINR is an implicit neural representation framework that enables fast, high-resolution local wind estimation and correction using sparse observations, with separate representation learning and sample-specific latent-state updates for efficient inference-time corrections.
AI.motivation: Many downstream applications in complex terrain require fast wind estimates at specific user-specified locations and heights, rather than dense forecast fields on fixed grids. There's a need for efficient interfaces between kilometer-scale background products, sparse local observations, and precise wind queries.
AI.method: WindINR uses a latent-state implicit neural representation with static terrain descriptors, low-resolution background fields, and query coordinates to predict high-resolution wind states. It separates reusable representation learning from sample-specific latent-state correction, using a privileged encoder during training and a deployable latent predictor at inference time. Inference-time corrections update only the latent state using sparse observations and uncertainty.
AI.result: In OSSEs over the Senja region (including UAV-aided scenarios and random-observation tests), WindINR improves local high-resolution wind estimates by updating only a compact latent state rather than full network weights. It achieves about 2.6× faster online correction than full-network fine-tuning while maintaining continuous queryability at arbitrary coordinates.
AI.conclusion: WindINR provides a practical, efficient interface between kilometer-scale background products, sparse local observations, and high-resolution wind queries in complex terrain, enabling rapid inference-time corrections with continuous query capability.

title: Scaling Limits of Long-Context Transformers
summary: We study the long-context limit of softmax self-attention with a fixed query and a random context of $n$ i.i.d. keys on the sphere, viewing the inverse temperature $β_n$ as the scaling parameter that decides whether attention degenerates into uniform averaging or collapses onto the single closest key. We show that the critical scale at which selectivity emerges is determined by the local exponent of the distance-to-query distribution near zero rather than by global features of the context, and scales like $β_n^\ast \asymp n^{2/(d-1)}$ for uniform keys on $\mathbb{S}^{d-1}$. Furthermore, we characterize the limiting laws of the ordered attention weights and of the attention output across all regimes of $β_n$: a subcritical regime in which the output reduces to a local average around $q$ with explicit deterministic bias and Gaussian fluctuations; a critical regime in which a finite collection of nearest keys retains macroscopic mass without single-key collapse; and a supercritical regime in which all mass concentrates on the closest key. Of notable interest is the subcritical case with identity value matrix where the attention map approximately implements a backward heat equation.

AI.tldr: Softmax attention's behavior across temperature regimes: uniform averaging (low β), selective attention to nearest neighbors (critical β), or collapse to single closest key (high β).
AI.motivation: Understand the mathematical properties of softmax attention in long-context limits, specifically how the inverse temperature β affects whether attention becomes uniform averaging or collapses to the closest key, with implications for transformer architectures.
AI.method: Theoretical analysis of softmax self-attention with fixed query and random i.i.d. keys on sphere, using β as scaling parameter. Characterize limiting laws across three regimes: subcritical, critical, and supercritical β scaling.
AI.result: Critical scale for selectivity emerges at β_n* ≈ n^{2/(d-1)} for uniform keys on sphere. Subcritical regime yields local averaging with Gaussian fluctuations; critical regime yields macroscopic mass on finite nearest keys; supercritical regime yields single-key collapse. Notably, subcritical case with identity value matrix approximates backward heat equation.
AI.conclusion: Attention's transition from averaging to selection is determined by local distribution properties near zero distance rather than global features, providing precise mathematical characterization of attention mechanisms across temperature regimes.

title: A Game Theoretic Free Energy Analysis of Higher Order Synergy in Attention Heads of Large Language Models
comment: this manuscript has been submitted to Neural Networks
summary: Large language models rely on multihead attention, but interactions among heads remain poorly understood. We apply the Game Theoretic Free Energy Principle (GTFEP): a framework casting multiagent systems as distributed variational inference to analyze attention heads as bounded rational agents. According to GTFEP, each head minimizes its variational free energy, and collective behavior follows a Gibbs distribution over coalition structures whose energy is decomposed into Harsanyi dividends. Using a tractable approximation (uniform prior, deterministic dynamics), coalition free energy reduces to joint Shannon entropy of discretized head outputs (argmax key index). Pairwise dividends become mutual information (nonnegative), while triple dividends correspond to interaction information and can be negative. On BERT, GPT2, and Llama with GSM8K, triple dividends are consistently negative, revealing higher order redundancy. The Nash FEP correspondence guarantees that stationary points of collective free energy are epsilon Nash equilibria; thus, heads with negligible contribution can be pruned with minimal performance loss. Pruning heads with low marginal contribution reduces computational cost with minimal performance loss: for example, pruning 20% of heads in GPT2 reduces FLOPs by 18%, increases throughput by 22%, and raises perplexity only modestly (from 28.4 to 33.4 on GSM8K). Our work shows GTFEP provides a principled foundation for analyzing and optimizing transformer architectures.

AI.tldr: Multihead attention heads are analyzed as bounded rational agents using Game Theoretic Free Energy Principle, revealing redundancy that enables efficient pruning with minimal performance loss.
AI.motivation: Large language models use multihead attention, but interactions among attention heads remain poorly understood, limiting optimization and interpretation of transformer architectures.
AI.method: Apply Game Theoretic Free Energy Principle (GTFEP) to analyze attention heads as bounded rational agents minimizing variational free energy. Use tractable approximation with uniform prior and deterministic dynamics, reducing coalition free energy to joint Shannon entropy of discretized head outputs. Analyze pairwise dividends (mutual information) and triple dividends (interaction information).
AI.result: On BERT, GPT2, and Llama models on GSM8K: triple dividends are consistently negative, revealing higher order redundancy. Stationary points of collective free energy correspond to epsilon Nash equilibria, enabling principled pruning of heads with negligible contribution. Pruning 20% of heads in GPT2 reduces FLOPs by 18%, increases throughput by 22%, with modest perplexity increase (28.4 to 33.4).
AI.conclusion: GTFEP provides a principled foundation for analyzing and optimizing transformer architectures by revealing head interactions as redundant systems, enabling efficient pruning strategies with minimal performance degradation.

title: Learning Polyhedral Conformal Sets for Robust Optimization
summary: Robust optimization (RO) provides a principled framework for decision-making under uncertainty, but its performance critically depends on the choice of the uncertainty set. While large sets ensure reliability, they often lead to overly conservative decisions, whereas small sets risk excluding the true outcome. Recent data-driven approaches, particularly conformal prediction, offer finite-sample validity guarantees but remain largely task-agnostic, ignoring the downstream decision structure. In this paper, we propose a decision-aware conformal framework that learns uncertainty sets tailored to robust optimization objectives. Our approach parameterizes a flexible family of polyhedral sets via data-driven hyperplanes and learns their geometry by directly minimizing the induced robust loss, while preserving statistical validity through conformal calibration. To correct for data-dependent selection, we incorporate a re-calibration step on an independent dataset to restore coverage. The resulting sets capture directional and anisotropic uncertainty aligned with the decision objective while remaining computationally tractable. We provide finite-sample coverage guarantees and bounds on the sub-optimality gap to an oracle decision. This work bridges the gap between statistical validity and decision optimality, providing a principled framework for data-driven robust optimization.

AI.tldr: Decision-aware conformal framework that learns uncertainty sets for robust optimization, balancing statistical validity with decision optimality
AI.motivation: Standard robust optimization suffers from a trade-off: large uncertainty sets lead to conservative decisions while small sets risk missing true outcomes. Existing data-driven approaches like conformal prediction are task-agnostic and ignore downstream decision structures.
AI.method: Proposes a decision-aware conformal framework that parameterizes flexible polyhedral uncertainty sets via data-driven hyperplanes. Learns set geometry by directly minimizing induced robust loss while preserving statistical validity through conformal calibration. Includes re-calibration step on independent dataset to correct for data-dependent selection.
AI.result: The method produces uncertainty sets that capture directional and anisotropic uncertainty aligned with decision objectives while remaining computationally tractable. Provides finite-sample coverage guarantees and bounds on sub-optimality gap to oracle decisions.
AI.conclusion: Bridges the gap between statistical validity and decision optimality, providing a principled framework for data-driven robust optimization that tailors uncertainty sets to specific decision objectives.

title: Weighted Rules under the Stable Model Semantics
summary: We introduce the concept of weighted rules under the stable model semantics following the log-linear models of Markov Logic. This provides versatile methods to overcome the deterministic nature of the stable model semantics, such as resolving inconsistencies in answer set programs, ranking stable models, associating probability to stable models, and applying statistical inference to computing weighted stable models. We also present formal comparisons with related formalisms, such as answer set programs, Markov Logic, ProbLog, and P-log.

AI.tldr: The paper introduces weighted rules under stable model semantics inspired by Markov Logic's log-linear models, enabling probabilistic reasoning and overcoming deterministic limitations in answer set programming.
AI.motivation: The stable model semantics in answer set programming has deterministic nature, which limits its applicability for handling inconsistencies, ranking models, probabilistic reasoning, and statistical inference.
AI.method: The authors introduce the concept of weighted rules under stable model semantics, drawing inspiration from log-linear models of Markov Logic to create a probabilistic framework.
AI.result: The approach provides versatile methods to resolve inconsistencies, rank stable models, associate probabilities to models, and apply statistical inference to compute weighted stable models.
AI.conclusion: The paper presents formal comparisons with related formalisms (answer set programs, Markov Logic, ProbLog, P-log) and demonstrates that weighted rules under stable model semantics offer a flexible probabilistic extension to traditional answer set programming.

title: MoMo: Conditioned Contrastive Representation Learning for Preference-Modulated Planning
summary: Temporally contrastive representation learning induces a latent structure capable of reducing long-horizon planning to inference in a low-dimensional linear system. However, existing contrastive planning work learns a single latent geometry which cannot distinguish multiple valid behaviors trading task efficiency against risk exposure for the same start-goal query. We introduce MoMo, a preference-conditioned contrastive planner allowing a scalar user preference to continuously modulate plan conservativeness at inference time, without retraining. MoMo learns a joint conditioning of the representation geometry and latent prediction operator via Feature-Wise Linear Modulation and low-rank neural modulation, respectively. We show that our formulation preserves the probability density ratio encoded in the representation space that is required for inference-driven contrastive planning, further retaining its inference-time efficiency. Across six environments, MoMo smoothly adapts plan safety according to user preferences, yielding improved temporal and preferential consistency over state augmentation baselines.

AI.tldr: MoMo is a preference-conditioned contrastive planner that learns to modulate plan conservativeness at inference time using user preferences, without retraining.
AI.motivation: Existing contrastive planning learns a single latent geometry that cannot distinguish between multiple valid behaviors trading task efficiency against risk exposure for the same start-goal query.
AI.method: MoMo uses Feature-Wise Linear Modulation (FiLM) to condition the representation geometry and low-rank neural modulation to condition the latent prediction operator, allowing scalar user preferences to continuously modulate plan conservativeness.
AI.result: Across six environments, MoMo smoothly adapts plan safety according to user preferences, yielding improved temporal and preferential consistency over state augmentation baselines while preserving inference-time efficiency.
AI.conclusion: MoMo enables preference-conditioned contrastive planning that can continuously adjust plan conservativeness at inference time without retraining, while maintaining the theoretical properties and efficiency of contrastive planning approaches.

title: Functional Stable Model Semantics and Answer Set Programming Modulo Theories
summary: Recently there has been an increasing interest in incorporating ``intensional'' functions in answer set programming. Intensional functions are those whose values can be described by other functions and predicates, rather than being pre-defined as in the standard answer set programming. We demonstrate that the functional stable model semantics plays an important role in the framework of ``Answer Set Programming Modulo Theories (ASPMT)'' -- a tight integration of answer set programming and satisfiability modulo theories, under which existing integration approaches can be viewed as special cases where the role of functions is limited. We show that ``tight'' ASPMT programs can be translated into SMT instances, which is similar to the known relationship between ASP and SAT.

AI.tldr: The paper shows how functional stable model semantics enables Answer Set Programming Modulo Theories (ASPMT), allowing intensional functions in ASP, with tight ASPMT programs translatable to SMT instances similar to ASP-to-SAT translation.
AI.motivation: There's growing interest in incorporating intensional functions (whose values are described by other functions/predicates rather than being pre-defined) into answer set programming to enhance its expressiveness and reasoning capabilities.
AI.method: The paper uses functional stable model semantics as the foundation for Answer Set Programming Modulo Theories (ASPMT), showing that it generalizes existing integration approaches and enables translation of tight ASPMT programs into SMT (Satisfiability Modulo Theories) instances.
AI.result: The paper demonstrates that functional stable model semantics is crucial for ASPMT framework, showing that existing integration approaches are special cases with limited functions, and establishes that tight ASPMT programs can be translated to SMT instances, analogous to the ASP-to-SAT relationship.
AI.conclusion: Functional stable model semantics provides a unified framework for ASPMT that generalizes previous approaches and enables practical implementation through translation to SMT, bridging answer set programming with satisfiability modulo theories in a principled way.

title: Quantile-Coupled Flow Matching for Distributional Reinforcement Learning
summary: Unlike standard expected-return Reinforcement Learning (RL), Distributional RL (DRL) models the full return distribution, making it better-suited for uncertainty-aware and risk-sensitive decision-making. Conditional Flow Matching (CFM) critics have recently attracted attention for modelling continuous, multi-modal return distributions. Despite this interest, there remains a substantial metric mismatch: DRL theory relies on the distributional Bellman operator being contractive in the $p$-Wasserstein distance, yet existing CFM critics are trained with arbitrary source-target couplings, so their flow-matching losses are not Wasserstein-aligned surrogates for matching Bellman target return distributions. In this work, we address this mismatch by proposing FlowIQN, a CFM critic that sorts source and Bellman target samples within each mini-batch to approximate the monotone optimal transport coupling, replacing arbitrary pairings with quantile-aligned flow paths. We prove that the loss of our quantile-coupled CFM critic yields a Wasserstein-aligned approximate projection compatible with the foundations of DRL. To our knowledge, FlowIQN is the first flow-matching distributional critic with an explicit Wasserstein-aligned projection guarantee. We further extend FlowIQN with shortcut models for efficient inference. Empirical results show that FlowIQN improves Wasserstein return-distribution accuracy over other CFM critics. It also yields competitive performance on offline RL benchmarks across multiple policy extraction methods, providing a theoretically grounded CFM critic that is readily compatible with DRL pipelines. Code: https://github.com/ori-goals/flowIQN.

AI.tldr: FlowIQN is a novel Conditional Flow Matching (CFM) distributional RL critic that addresses the metric mismatch between arbitrary source-target couplings and Wasserstein distance requirements by using quantile-aligned flow paths, providing theoretical guarantees for Wasserstein-aligned projections.
AI.motivation: There's a metric mismatch in current CFM-based Distributional RL methods: DRL theory requires contractivity in p-Wasserstein distance, but existing CFM critics use arbitrary source-target couplings with flow-matching losses that are not Wasserstein-aligned surrogates for matching Bellman target return distributions.
AI.method: FlowIQN uses quantile-aligned flow paths by sorting source and Bellman target samples within mini-batches to approximate the monotone optimal transport coupling, replacing arbitrary pairings. It also includes shortcut models for efficient inference.
AI.result: Theoretical proof that FlowIQN's loss yields a Wasserstein-aligned approximate projection compatible with DRL foundations. Empirical results show improved Wasserstein return-distribution accuracy over other CFM critics and competitive performance on offline RL benchmarks with multiple policy extraction methods.
AI.conclusion: FlowIQN is the first flow-matching distributional critic with explicit Wasserstein-aligned projection guarantee, providing a theoretically grounded CFM critic that is readily compatible with DRL pipelines while improving distribution accuracy and benchmark performance.

title: Cplus2ASP: Computing Action Language C+ in Answer Set Programming
summary: We present Version 2 of system Cplus2ASP, which implements the definite fragment of action language C+. Its input language is fully compatible with the language of the Causal Calculator Version 2, but the new system is significantly faster thanks to modern answer set solving techniques. The translation implemented in the system is a composition of several recent theoretical results. The system orchestrates a tool chain, consisting of f2lp, clingo, iclingo, and as2transition. Under the incremental execution mode, the system translates a C+ description into the input language of iclingo, exploiting its incremental grounding mechanism. The correctness of this execution is justified by the module theorem extended to programs with nested expressions. In addition, the input language of the system has many useful features, such as external atoms by means of Lua calls and the user interactive mode. The system supports extensible multi-modal translations for other action languages, such as B and BC, as well.
