AI.tldr: Cplus2ASP Version 2 is an upgraded system that implements the definite fragment of action language C+, with full compatibility to Causal Calculator Version 2 input language but significantly faster due to modern answer set solving techniques.
AI.motivation: The authors aim to provide a more efficient implementation of action language C+ by leveraging modern answer set solving techniques to overcome performance limitations of previous systems like the Causal Calculator Version 2.
AI.method: The system composes several recent theoretical results into a translation that orchestrates a tool chain consisting of f2lp, clingo, iclingo, and as2transition. It translates C+ descriptions into iclingo's input language, exploiting incremental grounding mechanisms, and uses the module theorem extended to programs with nested expressions to ensure correctness.
AI.result: The new Cplus2ASP Version 2 system achieves significantly faster performance compared to previous implementations while maintaining full compatibility with the Causal Calculator Version 2 input language. The system also includes useful features like external atoms via Lua calls and user interactive mode.
AI.conclusion: Cplus2ASP Version 2 provides an efficient, feature-rich implementation of action language C+ that leverages modern answer set solving technology while offering extensible multi-modal translations for other action languages like B and BC, making it a practical tool for reasoning about actions and change.

title: A Deep Risk Estimator for Known Operator Learning
summary: We describe an approach for estimating the statistical risk of deep networks that contain a mix of learned and known operators. Building on the maximal training error bounds previously established for known operator learning, we derive a deep risk estimator that connects the expected error of a layered network to the size of the training sample. The estimator decomposes the total risk into a sum over learned layers; every known operator contributes zero to this sum, while every learned layer adds an approximation term inspired by Barron's classic work and an estimation term that decreases with the number of training samples. We are able to show that the bound shrinks whenever a learned layer is replaced by a known operator and that the corresponding sample requirement scales with the number of trainable parameters of the layer that is replaced. As an application, we use computed tomography as an example and compare an operator-aware filtered backprojection network with a fully connected substitute that collapses the entire reconstruction pipeline into a single learned dense matrix. The predicted parameter ratio coincides with the structural sparsity that the analytic decomposition into a circulant filter and a sparse backprojection exposes. We confirm the predicted scaling on CPU at small image scale and on GPU at medium image scale, all on the same scaling law. Beyond CT reconstruction, the estimator applies to physics-informed neural networks that hardcode a known physical operation in its architecture, and we expect the result to be of interest for a broad community working on operator-aware deep learning. Calibrating the per-layer constants on each sweep yields a bound that tracks the empirical test MSE within a factor of two at every training-set size, so the estimator can be inverted to predict how many training samples are required to reach a target error.

AI.tldr: This paper presents a statistical risk estimator for deep networks combining learned and known operators, showing that replacing learned layers with known operators reduces risk and sample requirements, with validation on CT reconstruction tasks.
AI.motivation: The motivation is to provide theoretical understanding of the statistical properties of hybrid deep networks that combine learned parameters with known operators (like physical models). This is important for physics-informed neural networks and operator-aware deep learning where incorporating domain knowledge through known operators is common but lacks theoretical risk analysis.
AI.method: The method builds on maximal training error bounds for known operator learning, deriving a deep risk estimator that connects expected error to training sample size. The estimator decomposes total risk into a sum over learned layers, with known operators contributing zero. Each learned layer adds an approximation term (inspired by Barron's work) and an estimation term decreasing with training samples. The bound shows risk shrinks when learned layers are replaced by known operators.
AI.result: The paper demonstrates that sample requirements scale with the number of trainable parameters of replaced layers. Validated on CT reconstruction, the predicted parameter ratio matches structural sparsity of analytic decomposition. The estimator tracks empirical test MSE within a factor of two across training-set sizes, and can be inverted to predict required training samples for target error.
AI.conclusion: The paper provides a theoretically grounded risk estimator for hybrid neural networks, showing the benefits of incorporating known operators through reduced statistical risk and sample requirements. This work has broad applications in physics-informed neural networks and operator-aware deep learning beyond CT reconstruction.

title: LLM-Guided Monte Carlo Tree Search over Knowledge Graphs: Composing Mechanistic Explanations for Drug-Disease Pairs
comment: Accepted at IJCAI-ECAI 2026. 9 pages (7 content + 2 references), 5 figures, 3 tables. Includes supplementary material (26 pages)
summary: Extracting multi-step explanations from knowledge graphs poses a combinatorial challenge requiring both heuristic guidance (as candidates proliferate with depth) and credit assignment (as path quality emerges over extended sequences). Frontier LLMs, strong on knowledge/reasoning benchmarks, offer a compelling source of such heuristics, yet their knowledge comes sans guarantees and compositional performance degrades as chains lengthen. We thus present TESSERA, a 3-part neuro-symbolic framework that uses LLMs in a circumscribed role: for local discriminative judgement rather than autonomous multi-step generation; the knowledge graph then defines the hypothesis space enforcing hard structural constraints, and MCTS coordinates the long-horizon search with principled credit assignment via backpropagation. LLMs perform dual roles as a prior policy biasing exploration and a comparative state evaluator supplying reward signals. Evaluation on drug mechanism elucidation across two complementary knowledge graphs demonstrates fidelity to curated biology while surfacing coherent alternative mechanisms, with ablations confirming discriminative contribution from both LLM components. Beyond its current application, our framework offers a general paradigm for compositional reasoning over structured knowledge.

AI.tldr: TESSERA: A neuro-symbolic framework combining LLMs for local discrimination, knowledge graphs for structural constraints, and MCTS for multi-step reasoning over structured knowledge.
AI.motivation: Extracting multi-step explanations from knowledge graphs is combinatorially challenging, requiring both heuristic guidance (for candidate proliferation) and credit assignment (for path quality over extended sequences). While frontier LLMs offer strong knowledge/reasoning capabilities, their knowledge lacks guarantees and compositional performance degrades with longer chains.
AI.method: TESSERA is a 3-part neuro-symbolic framework that uses LLMs in a circumscribed role: for local discriminative judgement rather than autonomous multi-step generation. The knowledge graph defines the hypothesis space with hard structural constraints, and Monte Carlo Tree Search (MCTS) coordinates long-horizon search with principled credit assignment via backpropagation. LLMs perform dual roles as a prior policy biasing exploration and a comparative state evaluator supplying reward signals.
AI.result: Evaluation on drug mechanism elucidation across two complementary knowledge graphs demonstrates fidelity to curated biology while surfacing coherent alternative mechanisms. Ablations confirm discriminative contribution from both LLM components (prior policy and state evaluator).
AI.conclusion: The framework offers a general paradigm for compositional reasoning over structured knowledge, successfully combining the strengths of LLMs (knowledge/ discrimination) with knowledge graphs (structural constraints) and MCTS (systematic search) for reliable multi-step explanation generation.

title: SeBA: Semi-supervised few-shot learning via Separated-at-Birth Alignment for tabular data
summary: Learning from scarce labeled data with a larger pool of unlabeled samples, known as semi-supervised few-shot learning (SS-FSL), remains critical for applications involving tabular data in domains like medicine, finance, and science. The existing SS-FSL methods often rely on self-supervised learning (SSL) frameworks developed for vision or language, which assume the availability of a natural form of data augmentations. For tabular data, defining meaningful augmentations is non-trivial and can easily distort semantics, limiting the effectiveness of conventional SSL. In this work, we rethink SSL for tabular data and propose Separated-at-Birth Alignment (SeBA), a joint-embedding framework for SS-FSL that eliminates the dependence on augmentations. Our core idea is to separate the data into two independent, but complementary views and align the representations of one view to mirror the nearest-neighbor correspondence of the data in the second view. Our experimental evaluation supported by a theoretical analysis justifies that SeBA generates an output space, which improves the feature-label relationship. An experimental study conducted in various benchmark datasets demonstrates that SeBA achieves the state-of-the-art performance in the majority of cases, opening a new avenue for SS-FSL paradigm in the domain of tabular data.

AI.tldr: SeBA is a new semi-supervised few-shot learning method for tabular data that eliminates the need for data augmentations by separating data into complementary views and aligning representations based on nearest-neighbor correspondences.
AI.motivation: Tabular data in domains like medicine, finance, and science often suffers from scarce labeled data with abundant unlabeled samples. Existing semi-supervised few-shot learning methods rely on SSL frameworks designed for vision/language data that require natural data augmentations, but tabular data lacks meaningful augmentation methods that don't distort semantics.
AI.method: Proposes Separated-at-Birth Alignment (SeBA), a joint-embedding framework that eliminates dependence on augmentations. The core idea: separate data into two independent but complementary views, then align representations of one view to mirror the nearest-neighbor correspondence of the data in the second view.
AI.result: SeBA generates an output space that improves the feature-label relationship, as justified by theoretical analysis. Experimental evaluation on various benchmark datasets demonstrates state-of-the-art performance in the majority of cases.
AI.conclusion: SeBA opens a new avenue for semi-supervised few-shot learning in tabular data by providing an augmentation-free approach that addresses the unique challenges of tabular domains where traditional SSL methods struggle due to the difficulty of defining meaningful data augmentations.

title: TIDE-Bench: Task-Aware and Diagnostic Evaluation of Tool-Integrated Reasoning
summary: Tool-integrated reasoning has emerged as a promising paradigm for enhancing large language models with external computation, retrieval, and execution capabilities. However, the field still lacks a high-quality and unified evaluation benchmark, and existing TIR evaluations remain limited in dataset quality, task diversity, diagnostic comprehensiveness, and evaluation efficiency. In this work, we introduce TIDE-Bench, a holistic and efficient benchmark for evaluating TIR methods, featuring three key advantages. First, it provides diverse task settings, combining widely used mathematical reasoning and knowledge-intensive QA tasks with two newly designed tasks, namely the tool-grounded experimental design task and the dynamic interactive task, to probe models' abilities in complex tool invocation and multi-tool coordination. Second, TIDE-Bench adopts a comprehensive yet task-aware evaluation protocol, jointly measuring final answer quality, process reliability, tool-use efficiency, and inference cost across heterogeneous task settings. Third, TIDE-Bench constructs high-quality and discriminative evaluation sets by filtering low-discrimination instances from existing datasets, substantially reducing evaluation cost while focusing on more challenging samples. Extensive experiments on multiple foundation models and TIR methods reveal persistent bottlenecks in tool grounding, offering insights for future TIR research.

AI.tldr: TIDE-Bench is a new holistic benchmark for evaluating tool-integrated reasoning methods, featuring diverse tasks, comprehensive evaluation metrics, and high-quality discriminative datasets to address limitations in current TIR evaluation.
AI.motivation: The field of tool-integrated reasoning lacks a high-quality unified evaluation benchmark. Existing TIR evaluations are limited in dataset quality, task diversity, diagnostic comprehensiveness, and evaluation efficiency.
AI.method: TIDE-Bench introduces a comprehensive benchmark with three key components: 1) diverse task settings including mathematical reasoning, knowledge-intensive QA, tool-grounded experimental design, and dynamic interactive tasks; 2) a comprehensive task-aware evaluation protocol measuring answer quality, process reliability, tool-use efficiency, and inference cost; 3) high-quality discriminative evaluation sets created by filtering low-discrimination instances from existing datasets.
AI.result: Extensive experiments on multiple foundation models and TIR methods reveal persistent bottlenecks in tool grounding. The benchmark substantially reduces evaluation cost while focusing on more challenging samples.
AI.conclusion: TIDE-Bench provides a holistic and efficient benchmark for evaluating TIR methods, offering insights for future TIR research by identifying persistent challenges in tool grounding.

title: FlashEvolve: Accelerating Agent Self-Evolution with Asynchronous Stage Orchestration
summary: LLM-based evolution has emerged as a promising way to improve agents by refining non-parametric artifacts, but its wall-clock cost remains a major bottleneck. We identify that this cost comes from synchronized stage execution and imbalance inside each LLM-heavy stage. We present FlashEvolve, an efficient framework that replaces synchronized execution with asynchronous workers and queues, allowing different stages and steps to overlap. To handle data staleness introduced by asynchrony, FlashEvolve tracks artifact versions and applies different policies to update, discard, or patch stale artifacts. Unlike weight-space staleness in asynchronous RL, language-space staleness is inspectable and repairable: a stale artifact is not just delayed work, but readable evidence that the LLM can reflect on, revise, and turn into useful evolution signal. FlashEvolve further improves throughput and token efficiency with speculative stage completion and adaptive workflow control. On GEPA workloads, FlashEvolve improves proposal throughput by $3.5\times$ on local vLLM and $4.9\times$ on API serving over synchronous GEPA. The same design also applies to ACE and Meta-Harness.

AI.tldr: FlashEvolve is an efficient framework that speeds up LLM-based evolution by replacing synchronized execution with asynchronous workers, handling data staleness through version tracking and policies, and improving throughput with speculative completion and adaptive workflow control.
AI.motivation: LLM-based evolution shows promise for improving agents by refining non-parametric artifacts, but suffers from high wall-clock cost due to synchronized stage execution and imbalance within LLM-heavy stages.
AI.method: FlashEvolve replaces synchronized execution with asynchronous workers and queues to allow overlapping of different stages and steps. It tracks artifact versions and applies policies (update, discard, or patch) to handle data staleness. Unlike weight-space staleness in RL, language-space staleness is inspectable and repairable. The framework also uses speculative stage completion and adaptive workflow control.
AI.result: On GEPA workloads, FlashEvolve improves proposal throughput by 3.5× on local vLLM and 4.9× on API serving compared to synchronous GEPA. The same design also applies to ACE and Meta-Harness frameworks.
AI.conclusion: FlashEvolve effectively addresses the efficiency bottleneck in LLM-based evolution through asynchronous execution with intelligent staleness handling, significantly improving throughput while maintaining evolution quality.

title: PDEAgent-Bench: A Multi-Metric, Multi-Library Benchmark for PDE Solver Generation
summary: PDE-to-solver code generation aims to automatically synthesize executable numerical solvers from partial differential equation (PDE) specifications. This task requires not only understanding the mathematical structure of PDEs, but also selecting appropriate discretization schemes and solver configurations, and correctly implementing the resulting formulations in finite-element method (FEM) libraries. Existing code generation benchmarks mainly evaluate syntactic correctness, or success on predefined test cases. To our knowledge, there is currently no publicly available benchmark specifically for PDE-to-solver code generation, and general-purpose code benchmarks do not fully capture the unique challenges of numerical PDE solution, such as ensuring solver accuracy, efficiency, and compatibility with professional FEM libraries. We introduce PDEAgent-Bench, to the best of our knowledge, the first multi-metric, multi-library benchmark for PDE-to-solver code generation. PDEAgent-Bench contains 645 instances across 6 mathematical categories and 11 PDE families, with common FEM libraries for DOLFINx, Firedrake, and deal.II. Each instance provides an agent-facing problem specification, a reference solution on a prescribed evaluation grid, and case-specific accuracy and runtime targets. PDEAgent-Bench adopts a staged evaluation framework in which generated solvers must sequentially pass executability, numerical accuracy, and computational efficiency checks. Experiments with representative LLMs and code agents show that models can often produce runnable code, but their pass rate drops substantially once accuracy and efficiency requirements are enforced. These results indicate that current agents remain limited in producing numerically reliable and efficient PDE solvers, and that PDEAgent-Bench provides a reproducible testbed grounded in the practical requirements of numerical PDE solving.

AI.tldr: PDEAgent-Bench: First multi-metric benchmark for PDE-to-solver code generation, evaluating executability, accuracy, and efficiency across 645 instances and 3 FEM libraries.
AI.motivation: Existing code generation benchmarks mainly evaluate syntactic correctness or predefined test cases, lacking benchmarks for PDE-to-solver code generation that capture unique challenges like ensuring solver accuracy, efficiency, and compatibility with professional FEM libraries.
AI.method: Introduces PDEAgent-Bench with 645 instances across 6 mathematical categories and 11 PDE families, using DOLFINx, Firedrake, and deal.II FEM libraries. Each instance has problem specification, reference solution, and accuracy/runtime targets. Uses staged evaluation framework requiring solvers to pass executability, numerical accuracy, and computational efficiency checks sequentially.
AI.result: Experiments with LLMs and code agents show models often produce runnable code, but pass rates drop substantially when accuracy and efficiency requirements are enforced, indicating current agents remain limited in producing numerically reliable and efficient PDE solvers.
AI.conclusion: PDEAgent-Bench provides the first comprehensive benchmark for PDE-to-solver code generation that addresses practical requirements of numerical PDE solving, revealing limitations in current AI systems for producing high-quality numerical solvers.

title: Skill-CMIB: Multimodal Agent Skill for Consistent Action via Conditional Multimodal Information Bottleneck
summary: While LLM-based agents excel at planning and executing long action sequences, their execution often remains inconsistent across trials, limiting reliability. Consolidating agent consistency requires distilling trial-error trajectories into reusable skills that preserve task-relevant invariants while discarding trajectory-specific noise. However, in multimodal settings, the key challenge is not only that useful invariants are distributed across vision and language information, but that different modalities support different kinds of reusable skill content: while some skills are verbalizable and interpretable, others reside in perceptual evidence beyond text. Text-only skills may lose perceptual cues, whereas storing text and perception naively introduces redundancy and noise. Existing inference-time methods, such as self-consistency, improve reliability through costly multi-sample decoding, while internalization strategies lack a way to separate verbalizable skill content from residual perceptual information. To address this, we introduce Conditional Multimodal Information Bottleneck (CMIB), a method for multimodal skill construction. CMIB begins with a joint bottleneck over multimodal skills and derives an exact sequential decomposition: (1) a text-stage bottleneck distilling interpretable skill cards, and (2) a conditional multimodal bottleneck compressing only residual information in perception that remains predictive beyond text. Unlike naive two-stream formulations, CMIB explicitly conditions the multimodal latent on the text skill, thus structurally reducing cross-modal redundancy and enabling independent control over textual and perceptual compression. We instantiate CMIB with a variational objective that makes its conditional decomposition tractable to optimize, yielding reusable multimodal skills that improve execution stability without incurring multi-sample inference overhead.

AI.tldr: CMIB is a method for distilling multimodal trial-error trajectories into reusable skills that separate verbalizable skill cards from residual perceptual information to improve agent consistency without multi-sample inference overhead.
AI.motivation: LLM-based agents lack consistency across trials. Current skill distillation approaches either lose important perceptual information (text-only skills) or introduce redundancy and noise (naive multimodal storage). Existing inference-time methods like self-consistency require costly multi-sample decoding, while internalization methods can't separate verbalizable from perceptual skill content.
AI.method: Conditional Multimodal Information Bottleneck (CMIB) with joint bottleneck over multimodal skills decomposed into: (1) text-stage bottleneck for interpretable skill cards, and (2) conditional multimodal bottleneck for residual perceptual information. Uses variational objective for tractable optimization, conditioning multimodal latent on text skills to reduce cross-modal redundancy.
AI.result: CMIB creates reusable multimodal skills that improve execution stability without multi-sample inference overhead. The method structurally reduces cross-modal redundancy while preserving both verbalizable and perceptual skill content through the conditional decomposition.
AI.conclusion: CMIB provides a principled approach to multimodal skill construction that addresses the limitations of both text-only and naive multimodal methods, enabling more reliable LLM-based agents through reusable skills that maintain task-relevant invariants across vision and language modalities.

title: Workspace Optimization: How to Train Your Agent
summary: Modern agents built on frontier language models often cannot adapt their weights. What, then, remains trainable? We argue it is the agent's \emph{workspace}, the structured external substrate it reads, writes, and tests; we call its evolution workspace optimization. Workspace optimization targets hard multi-turn environments where a frontier model has strong priors but cannot solve the task in a single shot, so the agent must learn through interaction. We propose a principled way to evolve the workspace, mirroring the structure of weight-space training: artifacts in place of parameters, evidence in place of data, counterexamples in place of losses, and textual feedback in place of gradients. We instantiate the idea in DreamTeam, a multi-agent harness for ARC-AGI-3 whose roles build an executable world model, plan, hypothesize, probe, strategize, and route failures. On the current 25-game ARC-AGI-3 public set under the official scoring protocol and averaged over two independent runs, DreamTeam improves the SOTA protocol-matched agent's score from 36% to 38.4%, while using 31% fewer environment actions per game.

AI.tldr: DreamTeam introduces workspace optimization - training an agent's external structured workspace rather than its internal weights - to improve multi-turn reasoning in challenging environments, achieving state-of-the-art performance on ARC-AGI-3 with fewer actions.
AI.motivation: Modern agents using frontier language models often cannot adapt their internal weights, limiting their ability to learn through interaction in complex multi-turn environments where they need multiple attempts to solve tasks.
AI.method: Workspace optimization treats the agent's external structured workspace as trainable, with artifacts replacing parameters, evidence replacing data, counterexamples replacing losses, and textual feedback replacing gradients. DreamTeam instantiates this with a multi-agent harness featuring specialized roles for world modeling, planning, hypothesizing, probing, strategizing, and failure routing.
AI.result: DreamTeam improves SOTA protocol-matched agent's score on ARC-AGI-3 from 36% to 38.4% (averaged over two runs) while using 31% fewer environment actions per game, demonstrating more efficient reasoning.
AI.conclusion: When internal model weights cannot be updated, optimizing the external workspace provides a viable alternative for agent learning, particularly in hard multi-turn environments where agents need to iteratively refine their approach through structured external reasoning.

title: The Propagation Field: A Geometric Substrate Theory of Deep Learning
comment: Technical notes on exploring the nature of deep learning propagation, Under review by the ICML 4th Workshop on High-dimensional Learning Dynamics (HiLD) 2026
summary: Modern deep learning treats neural networks primarily as endpoint functions from inputs to outputs. Inspired by the shift from force to geometry in physics, we ask whether a network should instead be understood through the geometry of its internal propagation. We define a neural propagation field as the collection of hidden-state trajectories and local Jacobian operators across depth. Endpoint losses constrain only the boundary behavior of this field, leaving its interior geometry underdetermined. We show that endpoint-equivalent models can differ by orders of magnitude in trajectory and Jacobian structure, and introduce observable field metrics such as path sensitivity, solver consistency, and trajectory/Jacobian retention. In controlled teacher-flow and PDE systems, endpoint fitting fails to recover the underlying propagation law. In real multi-path tasks, field-aware objectives improve unseen-path generalization, OOD robustness, and calibration when aligned with the observation structure, but can collapse when over-constrained. In continual learning, field-preservation regularization complements replay and distillation: on Split CIFAR-100, DER++ with field preservation improves average accuracy, backward transfer, and field-retention metrics. These results identify propagation-field quality as a measurable and trainable property of neural networks beyond endpoint performance.

AI.tldr: Neural networks should be understood through the geometry of their internal propagation (neural propagation fields) rather than just as endpoint functions, as endpoint-equivalent models can have vastly different internal structures that impact generalization, robustness, and continual learning performance.
AI.motivation: Current deep learning treats neural networks primarily as endpoint functions from inputs to outputs, inspired by physics' shift from force to geometry. The authors question whether networks should instead be understood through the geometry of their internal propagation, as endpoint losses only constrain boundary behavior while leaving internal geometry underdetermined.
AI.method: Define neural propagation fields as collections of hidden-state trajectories and local Jacobian operators across depth. Introduce observable field metrics: path sensitivity, solver consistency, and trajectory/Jacobian retention. Test in controlled teacher-flow and PDE systems, real multi-path tasks with field-aware objectives, and continual learning with field-preservation regularization (combined with DER++ on Split CIFAR-100).
AI.result: Endpoint-equivalent models differ by orders of magnitude in trajectory and Jacobian structure. Endpoint fitting fails to recover underlying propagation laws in controlled systems. Field-aware objectives improve unseen-path generalization, OOD robustness, and calibration when properly aligned, but can collapse when over-constrained. In continual learning, field-preservation regularization complements replay and distillation: DER++ with field preservation improves average accuracy, backward transfer, and field-retention metrics on Split CIFAR-100.
AI.conclusion: Propagation-field quality is a measurable and trainable property of neural networks beyond endpoint performance. Understanding and optimizing the internal geometry of neural propagation offers benefits for generalization, robustness, and continual learning tasks.

title: Continuity Laws for Sequential Models
summary: Inductive biases influence the behavior and performance of sequential models. In this work, we study an underexplored inductive bias in sequential modeling: continuity in time. We ask a simple question: do models motivated by continuous-time formulations, such as state-space models, actually behave continuously in time, and does this translate into better performance on tasks with continuous temporal structure? To answer this, we formalize model continuity as convergence under temporal refinement, where a model is continuous if its predictions approach an underlying continuous trajectory as the temporal discretization is refined. We show that S4 exhibits stable continuous behavior, whereas S6 (the core of Mamba) can be more sensitive to input amplitude and selective dynamics, despite being derived from a continuous dynamical system. To study whether this distinction matters for learning, we also need a corresponding notion of task continuity. We therefore introduce a metric to quantify the continuity of datasets directly from their temporal structure. Across benchmarks, we find a clear empirical alignment between task continuity, model continuity, and model performance. Beyond an inductive bias, continuity also has practical consequences: we show that it enables a simple temporal subsampling strategy that improves both efficiency and performance.

AI.tldr: The paper investigates whether models derived from continuous-time formulations actually exhibit continuity behavior, and if this continuity bias improves performance on tasks with continuous temporal structure.
AI.motivation: To explore an underexplored inductive bias in sequential modeling: continuity in time, and determine whether models motivated by continuous-time formulations actually behave continuously in time, and whether this translates to better performance on tasks with continuous temporal structure.
AI.method: Formalized model continuity as convergence under temporal refinement (predictions approach continuous trajectory as discretization is refined). Introduced a metric to quantify continuity of datasets from temporal structure. Tested S4 and S6 models across benchmarks, and demonstrated a temporal subsampling strategy enabled by continuity.
AI.result: S4 exhibits stable continuous behavior while S6 can be more sensitive to input amplitude and selective dynamics. Found clear empirical alignment between task continuity, model continuity, and model performance. Continuity enables temporal subsampling strategy that improves both efficiency and performance.
AI.conclusion: Continuity is a meaningful inductive bias that influences model behavior and performance, with practical implications for efficiency and effectiveness in sequential modeling tasks with continuous temporal structure.

title: Absurd World: A Simple Yet Powerful Method to Absurdify the Real-world for Probing LLM Reasoning Capabilities
summary: While extremely powerful and versatile at various tasks, the thinking capabilities of large language models (LLMs) are often put under scrutiny as they sometimes fail to solve problems that humans can systematically solve. However, recent literature focuses on breaking LLM reasoning with increasingly complex problems, and whether an LLM is robust in simple logical reasoning remains underexplored. This paper proposes Absurd World, a benchmarking framework, to test LLMs against altered realism, where scenarios are logically coherent, and humans can easily solve the tasks. Absurd World breaks a real-world model into symbols, actions, sequences, and events, which are automatically altered to create absurd worlds where the logic to solve the tasks remains the same. It evaluates a large collection of models with simple and advanced prompting techniques, and proves that it is an effective tool to determine LLMs' ability to think logically, ignoring the patterns learned from the real world. One can use this framework to extensively test an LLM against a real-world problem to verify whether the LLM's reasoning capability is robust against variations of the task.

AI.tldr: Absurd World is a benchmarking framework that tests LLMs' logical reasoning by creating logically coherent but unrealistic scenarios that humans can easily solve, evaluating models' ability to think logically without relying on real-world patterns.
AI.motivation: While LLMs are powerful, their thinking capabilities are often questioned as they sometimes fail problems that humans systematically solve. Recent literature focuses on breaking LLM reasoning with complex problems, but whether LLMs are robust in simple logical reasoning remains underexplored.
AI.method: Proposes Absurd World framework that breaks real-world models into symbols, actions, sequences, and events, then automatically alters these to create absurd worlds where the logical structure to solve tasks remains the same. Tests a large collection of models with simple and advanced prompting techniques.
AI.result: The framework proves effective at determining LLMs' ability to think logically while ignoring patterns learned from the real world. It provides a tool to extensively test LLMs against real-world problems and verify their reasoning robustness against task variations.
AI.conclusion: Absurd World serves as an effective benchmarking tool to evaluate whether LLMs' reasoning capabilities are truly robust against variations of tasks, testing their ability to apply logical thinking independently of real-world pattern recognition.

title: Tokens-per-Parameter Coverage Is Critical for Robust LLM Scaling Law Extrapolation
summary: Neural scaling laws approximate a language model's loss as a power-law function of parameter count $N$ and token count $D$. Following Chinchilla-style compute-optimal training, many studies fit scaling laws from runs performed under a fixed tokens-per-parameter (TPP) ratio $k$ and set $D = kN$. We show that this collinear design, combined with the empirically common near-equality of the exponents governing $N$ and $D$, induces an inherent ill-conditioning in the Gauss-Newton least-squares problem: the condition number of the design grows as the inverse square of the gap between the $N$ and $D$-exponents. The scale coefficients become practically unidentifiable, with confidence intervals inflating by an order of magnitude or more, yielding a ``sloppy'' model whose extrapolations degrade sharply off the training ray. We prove this for four scaling-law formalisms and derive a closed-form TPP-diversity threshold that is necessary and sufficient for well-conditioned estimation. Empirically, non-collinear designs outperform collinear ones on held-out splits with a 97.3\% win rate across four laws, five corpora, multiple floating point precision modes. We further show the degeneracy is rooted in Jacobian geometry and is not an artifact of the loss function: any smooth estimation objective whose curvature involves the Jacobian inherits the same ill-conditioning.

AI.tldr: Neural scaling laws become ill-conditioned when estimated from collinear training runs (fixed token-per-parameter ratio), making parameters practically unidentifiable and degrading extrapolation performance off the training ray.
AI.motivation: Current practice in neural scaling law estimation involves training models along fixed token-per-parameter (TPP) ratios (collinear design), but this approach may suffer from identifiability issues and poor extrapolation performance.
AI.method: The authors mathematically analyze four scaling-law formalisms to show that collinear designs induce ill-conditioning in the Gauss-Newton least-squares problem when the exponents governing model parameters (N) and tokens (D) are nearly equal. They derive a closed-form TPP-diversity threshold for well-conditioned estimation and empirically validate their findings across multiple corpora and precision modes.
AI.result: The analysis reveals that collinear designs cause condition numbers to grow as the inverse square of the gap between N and D exponents, inflating confidence intervals by an order of magnitude. Non-collinear designs outperform collinear ones with 97.3% win rate across various configurations. The degeneracy is rooted in Jacobian geometry and affects any smooth estimation objective with Jacobian curvature.
AI.conclusion: Standard practices for estimating neural scaling laws using collinear designs are fundamentally flawed due to inherent ill-conditioning when N and D exponents are similar. Researchers should adopt non-collinear experimental designs to ensure well-conditioned parameter estimation and reliable extrapolation, as the issue affects any estimation objective involving Jacobian curvature.

title: Unpredictability dissociates from structured control in language agents
summary: Unpredictable behavior is often taken as evidence of control, yet stochastic dispersion and structured action control need not coincide. This paper tests whether stochastic sampling can substitute for structured mechanisms that couple reasons, memory, self-state and inhibition to action selection in a language-agent implementation whose control components can be selectively disabled. In a seven-dataset baseline lesion matrix comprising 74,352 calls, the high-stochasticity comparator was more unpredictable than the structured-control variant in 7/7 datasets, whereas targeted reason and veto lesions reduced the expected structured-control profiles in 7/7 datasets each. In a matched-interface control spanning 26,946 generations, the structured agent maintained stronger action-field coupling than all stochastic, post-hoc, scrambled and verbosity controls across every dataset. The primary behavioral test removed free-form trace wording from the evaluation: 57,816 scored records showed the structured-control variant exceeding the high-stochasticity comparator or the reason/veto lesions in 7/7 datasets for all predefined behavioral components. Later open-weight runs extended the no-context controls to Qwen2.5 7B, 14B and 32B and to an independent Mistral-7B family across 20 task families and three agent scaffolds; no-fields, scrambled-context and distribution-matched controls failed to recover structured action control. A three-annotator blinded audit over 1,200 overlap items preserved high agreement. Strict entropy matching, strict token/compute matching and a formal counterfactual-flip stress test did not meet their gates and are treated as limitations. Stochastic unpredictability did not reproduce structured, action-coupled control in this implemented agent family.

AI.tldr: Stochastic sampling cannot substitute for structured control mechanisms in language agents; structured control with reasons, memory, self-state and inhibition maintains stronger action-field coupling than high-stochasticity comparators.
AI.motivation: To test whether stochastic unpredictability can substitute for structured control mechanisms (reasons, memory, self-state, inhibition) in action selection for language agents, challenging the assumption that unpredictable behavior inherently indicates control.
AI.method: Implemented language agents with selectively disablable control components, conducted lesion studies across multiple datasets, compared structured control variants against high-stochasticity comparators, post-hoc, scrambled and verbosity controls, with extensive testing including open-weight runs on various model sizes and families.
AI.result: High-stochasticity comparators were more unpredictable but failed to reproduce structured control; structured agents maintained stronger action-field coupling across all tests and datasets; targeted reason and veto lesions reduced structured-control profiles; stochastic sampling did not recover structured action control even with various controls and model variations.
AI.conclusion: Stochastic unpredictability does not substitute for structured, action-coupled control in implemented language agents, supporting the necessity of explicit structured mechanisms rather than relying on behavioral unpredictability as evidence of control.

title: A Call to Lagrangian Action: Learning Population Mechanics from Temporal Snapshots
summary: The population dynamics of molecules, cells, and organisms are governed by a number of unknown forces. In the last decade, population dynamics have predominantly been modeled with Wasserstein gradient flows. However, since gradient flows minimize free energy, they fail to capture important dynamical properties, such as periodicity. In this work, we propose a change in perspective by considering dynamics that minimize a population-level action under a damped Wasserstein Lagrangian. By deriving the corresponding Hamiltonian equations of motion, we formalize Wasserstein Lagrangian Mechanics, a structured class of second-order dynamics that encompasses classical mechanics, quantum mechanics, and gradient flows. We then propose WLM as the first algorithm that learns these second-order dynamics from observed marginals, without specifying the Lagrangian. By directly learning the population mechanics, WLM can both forecast and interpolate unseen marginals, and outperforms existing gradient flow and flow matching methods across a wide range of dynamics, including vortex dynamics, embryonic development, and flocking.

AI.tldr: Wasserstein Lagrangian Mechanics (WLM) introduces second-order dynamics using damped Wasserstein Lagrangians to capture periodicity and other dynamical properties beyond gradient flows, with an algorithm that learns these dynamics from observed marginals.
AI.motivation: Current Wasserstein gradient flow models fail to capture important dynamical properties like periodicity because they only minimize free energy. There's a need for models that can capture a broader class of population dynamics observed in nature.
AI.method: Propose Wasserstein Lagrangian Mechanics using damped Wasserstein Lagrangians, derive Hamiltonian equations of motion, and develop WLM algorithm that learns second-order dynamics from observed marginals without specifying the Lagrangian.
AI.result: WLM outperforms existing gradient flow and flow matching methods across diverse dynamics (vortex dynamics, embryonic development, flocking), can forecast and interpolate unseen marginals, and captures periodicity and other dynamical properties.
AI.conclusion: Wasserstein Lagrangian Mechanics provides a structured framework for modeling second-order population dynamics beyond gradient flows, enabling better learning and prediction of complex biological and physical systems.

title: Ambig-DS: A Benchmark for Task-Framing Ambiguity in Data-Science Agents
summary: As data-science agents shift from co-pilots to auto-pilots, silent misframing becomes a critical failure mode. Agents quietly commit to plausible but unintended task framings, producing clean, executable artifacts that hide their incorrect assessment of the task. Existing benchmarks score whether the pipeline runs, ignoring whether the agent recognized the task was underspecified. We introduce Ambig-DS, two diagnostic suites: one for prediction-target ambiguity (Ambig-DS-Target, 51 tasks built on DSBench, a tabular modeling benchmark) and one for evaluation-objective ambiguity (Ambig-DS-Objective, 61 tasks built on MLE-bench, a Kaggle-style ML competition benchmark), constructed so that scoring uses each source benchmark's original evaluator. For every task we pair the original, fully specified version with an ambiguous variant produced by controlled edits; a human-and-LLM verification pipeline confirms each variant admits multiple plausible interpretations with decision-relevant consequences. The suites are analyzed independently and ambiguity lowers performance in both. Across five agents spanning efficient to frontier-class models, we find in our controlled diagnostic setting: (i) failures are silent commitments: wrong-target submissions on Target, wrong-metric or non-committal baseline submissions on Objective, rather than execution errors; (ii) allowing the agent to ask one clarifying question recovers much of the loss under idealized conditions, suggesting missing framing information drives a substantial part of the observed degradation; but (iii) agents cannot reliably tell when to use it: permissive prompts induce over-asking on clear tasks, while conservative prompts induce silent defaulting on ambiguous ones. Recognizing target and objective underspecification, not pipeline execution, is the bottleneck missing from standard DS-agent evaluations.

AI.tldr: Ambig-DS introduces diagnostic suites to evaluate data-science agents' ability to handle task ambiguity, showing they often produce silent misframings rather than execution errors, and that while clarifying questions help, agents struggle to know when to ask them.
AI.motivation: As data-science agents become autonomous, they often silently commit to plausible but unintended task framings when faced with ambiguity, producing clean but incorrect results. Existing benchmarks only test whether pipelines run, not whether agents recognize underspecified tasks.
AI.method: Created two diagnostic suites: Ambig-DS-Target (51 tasks on DSBench) for prediction-target ambiguity, and Ambig-DS-Objective (61 tasks on MLE-bench) for evaluation-objective ambiguity. Each task pairs an original fully-specified version with an ambiguous variant created through controlled edits, verified by human-and-LLM pipeline to ensure multiple plausible interpretations with decision-relevant consequences.
AI.result: Across five agents from efficient to frontier-class models: (i) failures are silent commitments (wrong targets or metrics) rather than execution errors; (ii) allowing one clarifying question recovers much performance loss; (iii) agents struggle to know when to ask questions - permissive prompts cause over-asking on clear tasks while conservative prompts cause silent defaulting on ambiguous ones.
AI.conclusion: Recognizing target and objective underspecification, not pipeline execution, is the critical bottleneck missing from standard data-science agent evaluations. Agents need better ambiguity detection and clarification mechanisms rather than just technical execution capabilities.

title: Can Revealed Preferences Clarify LLM Alignment and Steering?
summary: LLMs are increasingly used to make or support high-stakes decisions under uncertainty, where alignment depends not only on factual accuracy but on how models weigh tradeoffs between different outcomes. We present an empirical pipeline for estimating the implied preferences that an LLM's observed choices optimize: we elicit the model's probability distribution over unknowns along with the choice it would make for the decision task and then fit a discrete choice model to recover the cost function that best rationalizes the model's decisions. We show how this revealed-preference description allows rigorous evaluation of whether models behave in a consistently goal-directed way, whether they can verbalize a description of their objectives which matches their revealed decision policy, and whether prompting can reliably steer those policies to implement a user-specified cost function. We apply this evaluation across four medical diagnosis domains and multiple frontier and open-source models. We find that while many models have a nontrivial degree of internal coherence, they also have significant weaknesses in faithfully reporting or adopting preferences in response to user direction.

AI.tldr: LLMs' decision-making preferences can be estimated through revealed preference analysis, revealing gaps between their choices and verbalized objectives.
AI.motivation: LLMs are increasingly used for high-stakes decisions under uncertainty, but it's unclear whether their choices align with proper tradeoff considerations between outcomes. There's a need to understand what preferences LLMs actually optimize and whether they can reliably implement user-specified objectives.
AI.method: Developed an empirical pipeline: 1) elicit model's probability distribution over unknowns, 2) observe its decision choices, 3) fit a discrete choice model to recover the cost function that best rationalizes decisions (revealed preference analysis). Applied this to evaluate model coherence, verbalization-match, and steerability across four medical diagnosis domains using frontier and open-source models.
AI.result: Found that many models show nontrivial internal coherence but have significant weaknesses in faithfully reporting their preferences or adopting user-specified cost functions through prompting. Models often cannot verbalize objectives matching their revealed decision policies.
AI.conclusion: Current LLMs have concerning limitations in preference alignment - they don't reliably implement user directions or match their verbalized objectives to actual decision policies, raising important questions about their suitability for high-stakes decision-making.

title: Medical Model Synthesis Architectures: A Case Study
summary: Medicine is rife with high-stakes uncertainty. Doctors routinely make clinical judgments and decisions that juggle many fundamental unknowns, like predictions about what might be causing a patients' symptoms or decisions about what treatment to try next. Despite increasing interest in developing AI systems that aid or even replace doctors in clinical settings, current systems struggle with calibrated reasoning under uncertainty, and are often deeply opaque about their reasoning. We propose a framework for AI systems that can make practically useful but formally transparent clinical predictions under uncertainty. Given a clinical situation, our framework (MedMSA) uses language models to retrieve relevant prior knowledge, but constructs a formal probabilistic model to support calibrated and verifiable inferences under uncertainty. We show how an initial proof-of-concept of this framework can be used for differential diagnosis, producing an uncertainty-weighted list of potential diagnoses that could explain a patients' symptoms, and discuss future applications and directions for applying this framework more generally for safe clinical collaborations.

AI.tldr: MedMSA is a framework that combines language models for knowledge retrieval with formal probabilistic modeling to create transparent, uncertainty-aware AI systems for clinical decision-making like differential diagnosis.
AI.motivation: Medicine involves high-stakes uncertainty in clinical decisions, but current AI systems struggle with calibrated reasoning under uncertainty and are often opaque in their decision-making processes.
AI.method: The MedMSA framework uses language models to retrieve relevant clinical knowledge, then constructs formal probabilistic models to support calibrated and verifiable inferences under uncertainty.
AI.result: A proof-of-concept implementation demonstrates the framework's ability to produce uncertainty-weighted differential diagnosis lists that explain patient symptoms with transparent reasoning.
AI.conclusion: The framework enables transparent clinical predictions under uncertainty and shows promise for enabling safe clinical collaborations between AI systems and human doctors.

title: Beyond Static Bias: Adaptive Multi-Fidelity Bandits with Improving Proxies
summary: As an extension of the classical multi-armed bandit problem, multi-fidelity multi-armed bandits (MF-MAB) enable individual arms to be evaluated using diverse feedback sources that vary in both cost and accuracy. Prior stochastic models typically assume fixed low-to-high fidelity discrepancies, whereas modern proxy sources, such as learning-based simulators and Large Language Models (LLMs), can be improved using additional calibration. We investigate adaptive MF-MAB with improving proxy sources, and focus on the canonical two-fidelity case in which the low-fidelity source becomes more informative with repeated use. To capture this dynamic, we introduce a selected-average mismatch bound that converts dynamic low-fidelity observations into improvement-aware confidence bounds for the high-fidelity target. We propose the Threshold-Based Adaptive Continuation Companion (TACC), an optimistic algorithm that uses a bounded continuation rule to decide when low-fidelity sampling remains cost-effective and when to escalate. We prove an instance-dependent regret bound showing that, for detected intermediate arms, adaptive continuation replaces logarithmic high-fidelity confirmation with bounded low-fidelity continuation. Experiments on synthetic bandits and an LLM-as-a-judge policy-evaluation task examine when continuation improves cost-weighted regret.

AI.tldr: TACC algorithm adaptively balances low-fidelity proxy sampling with high-fidelity confirmation in multi-armed bandits with improving proxy sources, replacing logarithmic high-fidelity costs with bounded low-fidelity continuation.
AI.motivation: Existing multi-fidelity multi-armed bandit models assume fixed fidelity discrepancies, but modern proxy sources like learning-based simulators and LLMs can improve with calibration, requiring adaptive strategies to balance cost and accuracy.
AI.method: Proposes Threshold-Based Adaptive Continuation Companion (TACC), an optimistic algorithm with bounded continuation rule using selected-average mismatch bound to convert dynamic low-fidelity observations into improvement-aware confidence bounds.
AI.result: Proves instance-dependent regret bound showing adaptive continuation replaces logarithmic high-fidelity confirmation costs with bounded low-fidelity continuation for intermediate arms, with experimental validation on synthetic bandits and LLM-as-a-judge tasks.
AI.conclusion: TACC effectively leverages improving proxy sources in multi-fidelity bandits, demonstrating when continuation strategies improve cost-weighted regret through adaptive fidelity escalation based on proxy quality.

title: Primal-Dual Guided Decoding for Constrained Discrete Diffusion
summary: Discrete diffusion models generate structured sequences by progressively unmasking tokens, but enforcing global property constraints during generation remains an open challenge. We propose primal-dual guided decoding, an inference-time method that formulates constrained generation as a KL-regularised optimisation problem and solves it online via adaptive Lagrangian multipliers. At each denoising step, the method modifies token logits through an additive, constraint-dependent bias, with multipliers updated by mirror descent based on constraint violation. The bias arises as the optimal KL-regularised projection of the constraint, so the constrained distribution remains as close as possible to the model's unconstrained distribution while still satisfying the constraint. The method requires no retraining and no additional model evaluations beyond standard sampling, supports multiple simultaneous constraints, and provides formal bounds on constraint violation. We evaluate our approach on topical text generation, molecular design, and music playlist generation, showing that a single algorithm instantiated via domain-specific scoring functions improves constraint satisfaction while preserving relevant domain-specific quality metrics.

AI.tldr: Primal-dual guided decoding enables constraint-aware generation with discrete diffusion models without retraining, using adaptive Lagrangian multipliers to modify token logits at each denoising step.
AI.motivation: While discrete diffusion models can generate structured sequences, they struggle with enforcing global property constraints during generation, which is an important but unsolved challenge for tasks requiring specific property control.
AI.method: Formulates constrained generation as a KL-regularized optimization problem solved online via adaptive Lagrangian multipliers. At each denoising step, adds constraint-dependent bias to token logits, with multipliers updated by mirror descent based on constraint violation. The bias arises from optimal KL-regularized projection to stay close to unconstrained distribution while satisfying constraints.
AI.result: Method requires no retraining or additional model evaluations, supports multiple simultaneous constraints, provides formal bounds on constraint violation, and improves constraint satisfaction while preserving domain-specific quality metrics across topical text generation, molecular design, and music playlist generation tasks.
AI.conclusion: Primal-dual guided decoding offers an effective inference-time solution for constrained generation with discrete diffusion models, enabling control over global properties without compromising generation quality across diverse domains.

title: Finer is Better (with the Right Scaling)
summary: Microscaling is a critical technique for preserving the quality of Large Language Models (LLMs) quantized to ultra-low precision formats. Intuitively, finer block sizes should yield lower quantization error; however, a paradox recently identified in the literature demonstrates that standard abs-max scaling can actually degrade model quality as block sizes shrink. In this work, we investigate the underlying mechanics of this phenomenon. We demonstrate that this degradation is not an inherent limitation of finer granularity, but is primarily driven by heavy-tailed tensor distributions interacting poorly with the coarse upper quantization bins of the FP4 element format. Specifically, we show that i) preventing the scaling factor from underflowing to zero mitigates localized errors, ii) targeted algorithmic interventions like the 4-over-6 methodology effectively correct the quantization geometry for large elements, and iii) a brute-force search establishes an optimal baseline, confirming that the theoretical Mean Squared Error (MSE) strictly improves with finer block sizes. Ultimately, our findings reveal a valuable interchangeability: applying the correct algorithmic recipe allows standard, hardware-compliant formats (like OCP E4M3) to match the performance of custom, wider-exponent formats (like UE5M3). We validate these results across several large language models, fully resolving the block size paradox and achieving robust downstream perplexity improvements.

AI.tldr: The block size paradox in LLM quantization - where finer block sizes surprisingly degrade quality - is solved by understanding that heavy-tailed tensor distributions interact poorly with FP4's coarse upper bins, and specific algorithmic interventions can fix this.
AI.motivation: To resolve the paradoxical finding in quantization literature that standard abs-max scaling actually degrades LLM quality as block sizes shrink during ultra-low precision quantization, despite intuition suggesting finer blocks should reduce error.
AI.method: 1. Analysis of heavy-tailed tensor distributions interacting with FP4 element format's coarse upper quantization bins. 2. Demonstrate preventing scaling factor underflow mitigates localized errors. 3. Implement targeted algorithmic interventions like 4-over-6 methodology to correct quantization geometry for large elements. 4. Conduct brute-force search to establish optimal baseline confirming theoretical MSE strictly improves with finer block sizes.
AI.result: Shows that with proper algorithmic interventions, standard hardware-compliant formats (like OCP E4M3) can match the performance of custom wider-exponent formats (like UE5M3). Resolves the block size paradox and achieves robust downstream perplexity improvements across several large language models.
AI.conclusion: The block size degradation is not inherent to finer granularity but stems from heavy-tailed distributions interacting poorly with FP4's quantization bins. By applying the right algorithmic recipe, standard formats can achieve optimal performance, establishing valuable interchangeability between format types and fully resolving the paradox.

title: UTS at PsyDefDetect: Multi-Agent Councils and Absence-Based Reasoning for Defense Mechanism Classification
summary: This paper describes our system for classifying psychological defense mechanisms in emotional support dialogues using the Defense Mechanism Rating Scales (DMRS), placing second (F1 0.406) among 64 teams.1 A central insight is that defense mechanisms are defined by what is absent: missing affect, blocked cognition, denied reality. We encode this as an affect-cognition integration spectrum in prompt-level clinical rules, which account for the largest single gain (+11.4pp F1). Our architecture is a multi-phase deliberative council of Gemini 2.5 agents where class-specific advocates rate evidence strength rather than voting, achieving F1 0.382 with no fine-tuning - a top-5 result on its own. We find, however, that the council is confidently wrong about minority classes: 59-80% of stable minority predictions are incorrect, driven by a systematic "L7 attractor" in which emotional content defaults to the majority class. A targeted override ensemble from three fine-tuned Qwen3.5 models applies 16 overrides (+2.4pp), selected by a structured multi-agent system (builder, critic, regression guard) that produced a larger F1 gain in one iteration than 8 prior attempts combined.

AI.tldr: A system for classifying psychological defense mechanisms in emotional support dialogues achieved 2nd place (F1 0.406) among 64 teams using a multi-phase deliberative council of LLM agents with clinical rules and targeted override ensemble.
AI.motivation: Psychological defense mechanisms are defined by what is absent (missing affect, blocked cognition, denied reality), which presents a unique challenge for classification in emotional support dialogues. The goal is to accurately identify these mechanisms using the Defense Mechanism Rating Scales (DMRS).
AI.method: Multi-phase architecture with: 1) Prompt-level clinical rules encoding affect-cognition integration spectrum, 2) Deliberative council of Gemini 2.5 agents where class-specific advocates rate evidence strength rather than voting, 3) Targeted override ensemble using three fine-tuned Qwen3.5 models with structured multi-agent system (builder, critic, regression guard) to apply selected overrides.
AI.result: Achieved F1 0.406 (2nd place among 64 teams). The clinical rules provided largest single gain (+11.4pp F1). Council alone achieved F1 0.382 without fine-tuning (top-5 result). Override ensemble added +2.4pp. Key findings: council was confidently wrong about minority classes (59-80% of stable minority predictions incorrect) due to systematic "L7 attractor" where emotional content defaults to majority class.
AI.conclusion: The system demonstrates effective classification of psychological defense mechanisms through combination of clinical rule-based prompting, multi-agent deliberative architecture, and targeted override ensemble. The research highlights challenges with minority class predictions in such systems and shows how structured multi-agent override selection can substantially improve performance.

title: Different Prompts, Different Ranks: Prompt-aware Dynamic Rank Selection for SVD-based LLM Compression
summary: Large language models (LLMs) have rapidly grown in scale, creating substantial memory and computational costs that hinder efficient deployment. Singular value decomposition (SVD) has emerged as an effective post-training compression technique, but existing SVD-based methods rely on static rank truncation, applying a fixed prefix of singular components to all inputs regardless of their diversity. We identify two limitations of this static design: the optimal rank varies across individual prompts, and the selected rank is sensitive to the choice of calibration set, leading to suboptimal performance across diverse inputs. To address these challenges, we propose $\textbf{PARSE}$, a post-training framework for $\textbf{P}$rompt-$\textbf{A}$ware $\textbf{R}$ank $\textbf{S}$election as $\textbf{E}$xperts in SVD-compressed LLMs. PARSE trains a linear router offline to perform prompt-aware rank selection, decoupling it from calibration information by supervising the router against dense-model outputs on a large-scale corpus. We further observe that rank-selection patterns are shared across semantically similar prompts and remain stable across decoding steps, allowing appropriate rank subsets to be served directly from a pattern cache at inference. Complemented by expert memory aggregation and kernel fusion for system-level efficiency, PARSE is orthogonal to existing SVD-based pipelines and consistently improves both model quality and inference efficiency. Integrated with four representative SVD-based methods, PARSE improves average task accuracy by up to 10% at a compression ratio of 0.6 on LLaMA-7B, and achieves up to 2.5 $\times$ prefill and 2.4 $\times$ decode speedup over native SVD execution.

AI.tldr: PARSE: A prompt-aware rank selection framework for SVD-compressed LLMs that dynamically selects optimal singular value ranks per prompt, improving both accuracy and inference efficiency.
AI.motivation: Existing SVD-based compression methods use static rank truncation that applies the same fixed prefix of singular components to all inputs, which is suboptimal because: 1) optimal rank varies across individual prompts, and 2) selected rank is sensitive to calibration set choice, leading to poor performance across diverse inputs.
AI.method: PARSE trains a linear router offline to perform prompt-aware rank selection, decoupling it from calibration information by supervising against dense-model outputs. It observes that rank-selection patterns are shared across semantically similar prompts and stable across decoding steps, enabling caching. The system includes expert memory aggregation and kernel fusion for efficiency.
AI.result: Integrated with four SVD-based methods, PARSE improves average task accuracy by up to 10% at 0.6 compression ratio on LLaMA-7B, and achieves up to 2.5× prefill and 2.4× decode speedup over native SVD execution.
AI.conclusion: PARSE addresses limitations of static SVD compression by enabling dynamic, prompt-aware rank selection, demonstrating significant improvements in both model quality and inference efficiency while being orthogonal to existing SVD pipelines.

title: Marrying Generative Model of Healthcare Events with Digital Twin of Social Determinants of Health for Disease Reasoning
summary: Despite the central role of sensor-derived measurements such as imaging traits and plasma biomarkers in biomedical research and clinical practice, existing generative models for disease prediction largely depend on event-level representations from hospital and registry data. Given the multi-factorial nature of human disease, the absence of explicit modeling of social determinants of health (SDoH), even in the limited form of ICD-coded proxies (chapters Z and V--Y in ICD-10), limits the capacity for personalized disease modeling and clinical decision support. To address this limitation, we propose a generative model with ICD-coded proxies of SDoH for \textit{in silico} modeling of disease reasoning, a conditioned latent diffusion framework that establishes the connection between multi-organ sensor data with tokenized healthcare events. Specifically, we introduce a novel geometric diffusion model to characterize the temporal evolution of complex data representation such as brain networks (region-to-region connectivity encoded in a graph), in parallel with diffusion models for tabular data from other organ systems. Together, we integrate the generative model with digitalized SDoH proxies (coined \modelname{}) for simulated intervention and reasoning of future disease trajectories. We conduct extensive experiments on the UK Biobank (UKB) dataset, which contains organ-specific imaging traits, including brain (44,834), heart (23,987), liver (28,722), and kidney (32,155), along with nearly 500k medical history sequences (age range: 25$\sim$89 years). Our \modelname{} achieves significant improvements over state-of-the-art human disease autoregressive models and imaging trait generative baselines.

AI.tldr: A generative model called ModelName integrates ICD-coded SDoH proxies with multi-organ sensor data using a conditioned latent diffusion framework for disease trajectory simulation and reasoning.
AI.motivation: Existing disease prediction models rely on hospital/registry events without explicit modeling of social determinants of health (SDoH), limiting personalized disease modeling capacity despite disease being multi-factorial.
AI.method: Proposes a conditioned latent diffusion framework connecting multi-organ sensor data with tokenized healthcare events, including a novel geometric diffusion model for brain networks (graph connectivity) and diffusion models for other organ tabular data.
AI.result: Significant improvements over state-of-the-art human disease autoregressive models and imaging trait generative baselines on UK Biobank dataset (44,834 brain, 23,987 heart, 28,722 liver, 32,155 kidney imaging traits plus 500k medical sequences).
AI.conclusion: The ModelName framework successfully integrates digitalized SDoH proxies with multi-organ sensor data for simulated intervention and disease trajectory reasoning, advancing personalized disease modeling.

title: Uncovering Intra-expert Activation Sparsity for Efficient Mixture-of-Expert Model Execution
summary: Mixture of Experts (MoE) architecture has become the standard for state-of-the-art large language models, owing to its computational efficiency through sparse expert activation. However, sparsity through finer expert granularity is becoming increasingly difficult to achieve due to fundamental training challenges such as expert collapse and load imbalance. In this work, we explore and leverage intra-expert activation sparsity as a complementary and underexplored dimension of sparsity in MoE models. Surprisingly, substantial intra-expert sparsity is readily available in existing pre-trained MoE models, without any modification to the activation function or model parameters, providing up to 90% sparsity within each expert without significant accuracy loss. We explore intra-expert activation sparsity across eight off-the-shelf MoE models ranging from 1B to 400B parameters, and extend the MoE execution pipeline of vLLM to leverage intra-expert activation sparsity by skipping the computations of inactive neurons, on top of its existing optimizations, achieving up to 2.5 times speedup in MoE layer execution and 1.2 times end-to-end speedup compared to the original dense vLLM baseline.

AI.tldr: Intra-expert activation sparsity in Mixture of Experts (MoE) models provides up to 90% sparsity within each expert, enabling up to 2.5× speedup in MoE layer execution through optimized skipping of inactive neurons.
AI.motivation: While MoE architecture achieves computational efficiency through sparse expert activation, achieving finer expert granularity is increasingly difficult due to fundamental training challenges like expert collapse and load imbalance. Intra-expert activation sparsity presents a complementary and underexplored dimension of sparsity that could provide additional computational benefits.
AI.method: The researchers explored intra-expert activation sparsity across eight off-the-shelf MoE models (1B to 400B parameters), revealing substantial sparsity already present in pre-trained models without modifications. They extended vLLM's MoE execution pipeline to leverage this sparsity by skipping computations of inactive neurons, building on existing optimizations.
AI.result: The study found up to 90% intra-expert sparsity available in existing MoE models without significant accuracy loss. The optimized vLLM implementation achieved up to 2.5 times speedup in MoE layer execution and 1.2 times end-to-end speedup compared to the original dense vLLM baseline.
AI.conclusion: Intra-expert activation sparsity is a readily available, complementary dimension of sparsity in MoE models that can be effectively leveraged to significantly improve computational efficiency without modifying model parameters or activation functions, offering substantial speedups in large language model execution.

title: Attribution-based Explanations for Markov Decision Processes
summary: Attribution techniques explain the outcome of an AI model by assigning a numerical score to its inputs. So far, these techniques have mainly focused on attributing importance to static input features at a single point in time, and thus fail to generalize to sequential decision-making settings. This paper fills this gap by introducing techniques to generate attribution-based explanations for Markov Decision Processes (MDPs). We give a formal characterization of what attributions should represent in MDPs, focusing on explanations that assign importance scores to both individual states and execution paths. We show how importance scores can be computed by leveraging techniques for strategy synthesis, enabling the efficient computation of these scores despite the non-determinism inherent in an MDP. We evaluate our approach on five case-studies, demonstrating its utility in providing interpretable insights into the logic of sequential decision-making agents.

AI.tldr: The paper introduces attribution techniques for explaining AI decision-making in Markov Decision Processes (MDPs) by assigning importance scores to states and execution paths, addressing the gap in sequential decision-making explanations.
AI.motivation: Current attribution techniques focus on static input features at single points in time and fail to generalize to sequential decision-making settings, creating a need for explanation methods that work with Markov Decision Processes.
AI.method: The authors provide a formal characterization of what attributions should represent in MDPs, focusing on explanations assigning importance scores to individual states and execution paths. They compute these scores by leveraging techniques for strategy synthesis to handle the non-determinism inherent in MDPs.
AI.result: The approach was evaluated on five case-studies and demonstrated utility in providing interpretable insights into the logic of sequential decision-making agents.
AI.conclusion: The paper successfully fills the gap in attribution techniques for sequential decision-making by introducing effective methods for explaining MDP outcomes, making AI decision-making in such settings more interpretable.

title: Probing the Impact of Scale on Data-Efficient, Generalist Transformer World Models for Atari
summary: Developing generalist systems that retain human-like data efficiency is a central challenge. While world models (WMs) offer a promising path, existing research often conflates architectural mechanisms with the independent impact of model \emph{scale}. In this work, we use a minimalist transformer world model to analyze scaling behaviors on the Atari 100k benchmark, using fixed offline datasets derived from a presupposed expert policy. Our results reveal that environments fundamentally fall into distinct scaling regimes, even when constrained by identical offline data budgets and model capacities. For individual tasks, some environments naturally allow models to pass the interpolation threshold, yielding monotonic improvements in the overparameterized regime, while others remain trapped in the classical regime, where larger world models degrade fidelity. In the unified setting, i.e., a single transformer trained on a suite of 26 Atari environments, we uncover that joint training stabilizes scaling dynamics, ensuring monotonic gains across all environments, regardless of their distinct inherent scaling regimes. Finally, we demonstrate that improved fidelity translates directly to downstream control, with policies learned entirely within the simulated dynamics achieving a median expert-random-normalized score of 0.770. Our findings suggest that future progress lies as much in precise scaling strategies as in architectural innovation.

AI.tldr: World model scaling follows different regimes across Atari environments - some benefit from larger models while others degrade, but joint multi-environment training stabilizes scaling and leads to effective policy learning.
AI.motivation: To disentangle the effects of model scale from architectural innovations in world models, and understand how scaling behaviors differ across environments when using fixed data budgets.
AI.method: Used a minimalist transformer world model to analyze scaling on Atari 100k benchmark with fixed offline datasets from expert policies, examining both individual environments and joint training across 26 Atari games.
AI.result: 1) Environments fall into distinct scaling regimes - some allow models to pass interpolation threshold for monotonic gains, others degrade with larger models. 2) Joint training stabilizes scaling, ensuring monotonic improvements across all environments. 3) Improved model fidelity directly translates to better control performance (median score 0.770).
