AI.conclusion: Future progress in world models depends as much on precise scaling strategies as on architectural innovation, and joint multi-environment training offers a stable path for scaling benefits.

title: PRISM: Fast Online LLM Serving via Scheduling-Memory Co-design
summary: Modern online large language model (LLM) services, such as Retrieval-Augmented Generation (RAG) and agent systems, increasingly expose two prominent characteristics: prompt segmentation (e.g., system instructions, retrieved passages, tool outputs) and hotspot skew, where a small set of these segments recurs frequently across user requests. Failing to jointly exploit these patterns could lead to repeated prefill of hot segments and prolonged TTFT, undermining both throughput and user-perceived responsiveness. However, existing work tackles these patterns independently: KV-cache management mainly exploits segment reuse while scheduling reorders requests to improve cache locality, yet neither aligns request admission with KV-cache retention. To address this gap, we first analyze how scheduling and KV-cache management jointly affect TTFT. Guided by this, we present PRISM (Prefix Reuse Optimization Integrated Scheduling and Memory), which co-designs a query-aware scheduler (QAS) with a demand-aware radix tree (DART) to align request admission with exact-prefix KV retention. Our evaluation results show that, versus the strongest baseline, PRISM reduces average per-QPS P99 TTFT by 23.3\% and 37.1\% while increasing exact-prefix KV-cache hit rate by 5.9 and 12.2 percentage points on 4B and 13B models, respectively.

AI.tldr: PRISM co-designs query-aware scheduling with demand-aware radix tree to align request admission with exact-prefix KV retention, reducing TTFT and increasing KV-cache hit rates by exploiting prompt segmentation and hotspot patterns in modern LLM services.
AI.motivation: Modern LLM services exhibit prompt segmentation (system instructions, retrieved passages, tool outputs) and hotspot skew where small sets of segments recur frequently across requests. Existing approaches independently handle segment reuse (KV-cache management) and scheduling for cache locality, but fail to align request admission with KV-cache retention, leading to repeated prefill of hot segments and prolonged Time-To-First-Token (TTFT).
AI.method: PRISM co-designs two components: 1) Query-aware scheduler (QAS) that aligns request admission with KV-cache retention, and 2) Demand-aware radix tree (DART) for exact-prefix KV retention. The system analyzes how scheduling and KV-cache management jointly affect TTFT and integrates these components to exploit prompt segmentation and hotspot patterns.
AI.result: Compared to strongest baselines, PRISM reduces average per-QPS P99 TTFT by 23.3% and 37.1% while increasing exact-prefix KV-cache hit rate by 5.9 and 12.2 percentage points on 4B and 13B models respectively.
AI.conclusion: Jointly exploiting prompt segmentation and hotspot patterns through co-designed scheduling and KV-cache management significantly improves LLM service performance by reducing TTFT and increasing cache hit rates, addressing limitations of existing independent approaches.

title: Yield Curve Forecasting using Machine Learning and Econometrics: A Comparative Analysis
comment: 18 pages, 12 figures, comparative study of econometric, machine learning, and deep learning methods for U.S. Treasury yield curve forecasting
summary: While machine learning has revolutionized many fields such as natural language processing (NLP) and computer vision, its impact on time-series forecasting is still widely disputed, especially in the finance domain. This paper compares forecasting performance on U.S. Treasury yield curve data across econometrics/time-series analysis, classical machine learning, and deep learning methods, using daily data over 47 years. The Treasury yield curve is important because it is widely used by every participant in the bond markets, which are larger than equity markets. We examine a variety of methods that have not been tested on yield curve forecasting, especially deep learning algorithms. The algorithms include the Autoregressive Integrated Moving Average (ARIMA) model and its extensions, naive benchmarks, ensemble methods, Recurrent Neural Networks (RNNs), and multiple transformers built for forecasting. ARIMA and naive econometric models outperform other models overall, except in one time block. Of the machine learning methods, TimeGPT, LGBM and RNNs perform the best. Furthermore, the paper explores whether stationary or nonstationary data are more appropriate as input to deep learning models.

AI.tldr: Classical econometric models (ARIMA and naive approaches) generally outperform machine learning and deep learning methods for U.S. Treasury yield curve forecasting, with TimeGPT, LGBM, and RNNs being the best performing ML methods.
AI.motivation: Despite ML's success in NLP and computer vision, its effectiveness for time-series forecasting in finance remains disputed. The Treasury yield curve is crucial as it's widely used in bond markets (larger than equity markets), and many deep learning methods haven't been tested on yield curve forecasting.
AI.method: Comparative study using 47 years of daily U.S. Treasury yield curve data, evaluating three categories: 1) Econometrics/time-series (ARIMA and extensions, naive benchmarks), 2) Classical ML (ensemble methods), 3) Deep learning (RNNs, multiple forecasting transformers). Also explores whether stationary or nonstationary data is better for deep learning inputs.
AI.result: ARIMA and naive econometric models outperform other models overall across most time blocks, with only one exception. Among ML methods, TimeGPT, LGBM, and RNNs perform best. The study also provides insights on data stationarity considerations for deep learning models.
AI.conclusion: Traditional econometric approaches remain superior for yield curve forecasting despite advances in ML, suggesting domain-specific traditional methods may still outperform modern ML approaches in certain financial forecasting tasks.

title: Kaczmarz Linear Attention
summary: Long-context language modeling remains central to modern sequence modeling, but the quadratic cost of Transformer attention makes scaling computationally prohibitive. Linear recurrent models address this bottleneck by compressing the context into a fixed-size state, making the rule that forgets, writes, and edits information a central design problem. To address state maintenance, Gated DeltaNet (GDN) combines gated state decay with delta-rule residual writes, using a learnable coefficient to balance forgetting and update magnitude. However, this coefficient is learned empirically rather than derived from the underlying objective, which can lead to suboptimal update magnitudes. We revisit the online-regression objective underlying GDN and, inspired by the Kaczmarz projection method, derive the key-norm-normalized dynamic step size $β_t = η_t / (\|k_t\|_2^2 + ε)$ for residual updates. We propose Kaczmarz Linear Attention (KLA), a one-scalar modification of GDN that preserves the state shape, gates, linear recurrence, and chunkwise parallel algorithm. At the 0.4B scale with a 1B-token budget, KLA achieves the lowest validation perplexity among evaluated linear-time baselines, 8.09 versus 8.50 for GDN, and remains stable up to 65K tokens. On controlled tasks, KLA reaches 100% on single-needle-in-a-haystack retrieval, improves 8x multi-query associative recall by 7.03 points over GDN, and delivers 2.1x higher decode throughput at 32K context. These results suggest that the key-norm-normalized Kaczmarz coefficient is a first-order design axis for delta-rule sequence models: it improves accuracy, extrapolation, and decoding efficiency without changing the recurrent state or hardware kernel.

AI.tldr: Kaczmarz Linear Attention (KLA) improves linear recurrent models by deriving an optimal key-norm-normalized dynamic step size for residual updates, achieving better accuracy, stability, and efficiency than previous approaches.
AI.motivation: Linear recurrent models address the quadratic cost of Transformer attention by compressing context into a fixed-size state, but the Gated DeltaNet (GDN) approach uses empirically learned coefficients that can lead to suboptimal update magnitudes and performance.
AI.method: The paper revisits the online-regression objective underlying GDN and, inspired by the Kaczmarz projection method, derives a key-norm-normalized dynamic step size β_t = η_t/(‖k_t‖_2^2 + ε) for residual updates. This leads to Kaczmarz Linear Attention (KLA), a one-scalar modification of GDN that preserves state shape, gates, linear recurrence, and chunkwise parallel algorithm.
AI.result: At 0.4B scale with 1B-token budget, KLA achieves lowest validation perplexity among linear-time baselines (8.09 vs 8.50 for GDN), remains stable up to 65K tokens, reaches 100% on single-needle-in-a-haystack retrieval, improves multi-query associative recall by 7.03 points over GDN, and delivers 2.1x higher decode throughput at 32K context.
AI.conclusion: The key-norm-normalized Kaczmarz coefficient is a first-order design axis for delta-rule sequence models that improves accuracy, extrapolation, and decoding efficiency without changing the recurrent state or hardware kernel, suggesting this principled approach to update balancing is crucial for optimal linear attention performance.

title: The Metacognitive Probe: Five Behavioural Calibration Diagnostics for LLMs
comment: 27 pages, 13 tables. Code, data, prompts, and rubrics released with the paper. OSF deposit pending; DOI in v2
summary:
The Metacognitive Probe is an exploratory five-task, 15-slot diagnostic that decomposes an LLM's confidence behaviour into five behaviourally-distinct dimensions: confidence calibration (T1-CC), epistemic vigilance (T2-EV), knowledge boundary (T3-KB), calibration range (T4-CR), and reasoning-chain validation (T5-RCV). It is evaluated on N=8 frontier models and N=69 humans. The instrument is motivated by Flavell (1979) and Nelson and Narens (1990) but operates on observable confidence-correctness alignment; it is not a validated cross-species metacognition scale, and the pre-specified human developmental hypothesis was falsified.
  Composite benchmarks (MMLU, BIG-Bench, HELM, GPQA) ask whether a model produces a correct response. They are silent on whether the model knows when its response is wrong. A model can score 80 on a composite calibration benchmark and still be wildly overconfident in narrow pockets the aggregate cannot surface. The Metacognitive Probe surfaces those pockets.
  Our headline is a 47-point within-model dissociation in Gemini 2.5 Flash: panel-best within-task calibration (T1-CC = 88; Spearman rho = +0.551, 95% CI [+0.14, +0.80], p = 0.005) and panel-worst cross-task difficulty prediction (T4-CR = 41; sigma_conf = 1.4 across twelve factoids).

AI.tldr: The Metacognitive Probe is a 5-task diagnostic tool that assesses LLMs' confidence calibration across five dimensions, revealing significant within-model calibration inconsistencies that standard benchmarks miss.
AI.motivation: Current composite benchmarks (MMLU, BIG-Bench, HELM, GPQA) only measure whether models produce correct responses, but fail to assess whether models know when their responses are wrong. A model can score well on aggregate calibration metrics while being wildly overconfident in specific areas that standard benchmarks don't surface.
AI.method: Developed a five-task, 15-slot diagnostic instrument called the Metacognitive Probe that decomposes LLM confidence behavior into five distinct dimensions: confidence calibration (T1-CC), epistemic vigilance (T2-EV), knowledge boundary (T3-KB), calibration range (T4-CR), and reasoning-chain validation (T5-RCV). Evaluated on N=8 frontier models and N=69 humans.
AI.result: The tool revealed significant within-model calibration dissociations, most notably a 47-point within-model dissociation in Gemini 2.5 Flash: panel-best within-task calibration (T1-CC = 88; Spearman rho = +0.551) but panel-worst cross-task difficulty prediction (T4-CR = 41; sigma_conf = 1.4 across twelve factoids). The human developmental hypothesis was falsified.
AI.conclusion: The Metacognitive Probe successfully surfaces specific pockets of miscalibration that aggregate benchmarks miss, providing a more nuanced assessment of LLM confidence behavior across multiple dimensions. The instrument operates on observable confidence-correctness alignment but is not a validated cross-species metacognition scale.

title: Lattice Deduction Transformers
summary: We introduce the Lattice Deduction Transformer (LDT), a recurrent transformer that approximates logically sound deduction by projecting its latent state through a lattice between forward passes. We train on-policy in a process that mirrors deduction in a search-based constraint solver and supervise training via a domain-agnostic, abstract-interpretation-based approximation of the set of solution candidates. An $800$K-parameter LDT achieves $100\%$ accuracy on Sudoku-Extreme and Snowflake Sudoku, at a fraction of the training cost of prior small recurrent reasoners, while remaining empirically sound: the model returns a correct answer or abstains. A $1.8$M-parameter variant reaches $99.9\%$ accuracy on Maze-Hard. Frontier LLMs score $0\%$ on all three benchmarks.

AI.tldr: Lattice Deduction Transformer (LDT) is a recurrent transformer that achieves near-perfect accuracy on complex reasoning benchmarks like Sudoku-Extreme and Maze-Hard, outperforming frontier LLMs which score 0%.
AI.motivation: The paper aims to create a model that can perform logically sound deduction, addressing the limitations of current LLMs which struggle with complex reasoning tasks like Sudoku and maze-solving.
AI.method: LDT is a recurrent transformer that projects its latent state through a lattice between forward passes, trained on-policy in a process mirroring deduction in search-based constraint solvers, with supervision via domain-agnostic abstract-interpretation-based approximation of solution candidates.
AI.result: An 800K-parameter LDT achieves 100% accuracy on Sudoku-Extreme and Snowflake Sudoku, while a 1.8M-parameter variant reaches 99.9% accuracy on Maze-Hard. Frontier LLMs score 0% on all three benchmarks.
AI.conclusion: LDT demonstrates that small, specialized recurrent transformers can achieve logically sound deduction on complex reasoning tasks at low training cost, significantly outperforming much larger general-purpose LLMs on these specific benchmarks.

title: Fairness of Explanations in Artificial Intelligence (AI): A Unifying Framework, Axioms, and Future Direction toward Responsible AI
summary:
Machine learning algorithms are being used in high-stakes decisions, including those in criminal justice, healthcare, credit, and employment. The research community has responded with two largely independent research fields: \emph{algorithmic fairness}, which targets equitable outcomes, and \emph{explainable AI} (XAI), which targets interpretable reasoning. This survey identifies and maps a novel blind spot at their intersection, which is a model that can satisfy every standard fairness criterion in its outputs while being profoundly unfair in its \emph{reasoning process}. We refer to this as the procedural bias, and mitigating it requires treating the fairness of explanations as a distinct object of scientific study.
  To our knowledge, we provide the first unified theoretical and literature review of this emerging field and elucidate the drawbacks of post-hoc explainers in certifying explanation fairness. Our central contribution is a \emph{conditional invariance framework} formalizing explanation fairness as the requirement that explanations should be indifferent regardless of the protected attributes $ P(E(X) \in \cdot \mid X_\text{rel} = x_\text{rel},\, A = a) = P(E(X) \in \cdot \mid X_\text{rel} = x_\text{rel},\, A = b)$ for all task-relevant $x$, a single principle from which all existing explanation fairness metrics emerge as partial operationalizations. We introduce a seven-dimensional taxonomy, identify three generative mechanisms of explanation inequity (representation-driven, explanation-model mismatch, actionability-driven), and propose a canonical six-step evaluation workflow for operationalizing explanation fairness audits in practice.

AI.tldr: The paper introduces "procedural bias" - when models satisfy fairness criteria in outputs but use unfair reasoning - and establishes a unified framework for explanation fairness as a distinct research field.
AI.motivation: Machine learning in high-stakes decisions has led to two separate fields: algorithmic fairness (equitable outcomes) and explainable AI (interpretable reasoning). The authors identify a blind spot where models can appear fair in outputs while being unfair in their reasoning process, which they call "procedural bias."
AI.method: The paper provides a unified theoretical and literature review, introducing a conditional invariance framework that formalizes explanation fairness. They develop a seven-dimensional taxonomy, identify three generative mechanisms of explanation inequity, and propose a six-step evaluation workflow for explanation fairness audits.
AI.result: The central contribution is a formal framework showing that all existing explanation fairness metrics emerge as partial operationalizations of a single principle: explanations should be indifferent to protected attributes given task-relevant features. The paper identifies three main sources of explanation inequity and provides practical evaluation methods.
AI.conclusion: Explanation fairness must be treated as a distinct scientific study from outcome fairness. The proposed framework and taxonomy provide a foundation for auditing and mitigating procedural bias in ML systems used for high-stakes decisions.

title: Robust Server Defense Against Unreliable Clients in One-Shot Fair Collaborative Machine Learning
comment: Accepted at the 2nd International Conference on Federated Learning and Intelligent Computing Systems (FLICS 2026)
summary: Collaborative machine learning (CML) enables multiple clients to train a global model jointly in a data-distributed setting. To address data privacy and communication efficiency, one-shot CML has been increasingly adopted, where clients communicate with the server only once by sharing synthetic or processed proxy data. This single-round communication, however, eliminates the possibility of iterative correction at the server, making the learning process particularly vulnerable to client unreliability. In this setting, unreliable clients, whether malicious or non-malicious, may provide biased proxy data that favors certain groups, thereby degrading the fairness of the global model and harming minority or unprivileged groups. In this work, we propose a server-side defense framework based on a bilevel optimization formulation. The proposed approach learns client-level weights to mitigate the influence of biased client proxy data while enforcing fairness constraints by using a very small trusted root dataset available at the server. Experimental results on benchmark datasets show that our method improves fairness with little accuracy loss under biased proxy data contributions from unreliable clients. Moreover, the proposed approach remains effective even when unreliable clients make up a majority of the system, consistently outperforming other existing methods.

AI.tldr: A bilevel optimization defense framework for one-shot collaborative machine learning that protects model fairness against biased proxy data from unreliable clients.
AI.motivation: One-shot CML (single-round communication) is vulnerable to client unreliability where malicious or non-malicious clients can provide biased proxy data that degrades global model fairness and harms minority groups, with no opportunity for iterative correction due to the one-shot nature.
AI.method: Server-side defense framework using bilevel optimization that learns client-level weights to mitigate influence of biased proxy data while enforcing fairness constraints using a small trusted root dataset available at the server.
AI.result: Experimental results on benchmark datasets show the method improves fairness with minimal accuracy loss under biased proxy data contributions, and remains effective even when unreliable clients constitute a majority, consistently outperforming existing methods.
AI.conclusion: The proposed bilevel optimization approach provides an effective defense against fairness degradation in one-shot CML systems, particularly valuable in scenarios with unreliable clients, demonstrating robustness and superiority over existing techniques.

title: When to Re-Commit: Temporal Abstraction Discovery for Long-Horizon Vision-Language Reasoning
summary: Long-horizon reasoning requires deciding not only what actions to take, but how deeply to commit before the next observation. We formalize this as \emph{commitment depth}: the number of primitive actions executed open-loop between replans. Commitment depth induces a trade-off between replanning cost and compounding execution error, yet most existing long-horizon systems fix it as a hand-designed scalar. In this work, we instead treat commitment depth as a learnable, state-conditioned variable of the policy itself. We instantiate this within a model-native vision--language policy that jointly predicts both what to execute and for how long. Across Sliding Puzzle and Sokoban, the resulting adaptive policy Pareto-dominates every non-degenerate fixed-depth baseline, achieving up to 12.5 percentage points higher solve rate while using approximately 25\% fewer primitive actions per episode. Despite using a 7B backbone, our method outperforms GPT-5.5 and Claude Sonnet on both tasks, while every tested open-weight vision--language model achieves 0\% zero-shot success. We further present a theoretical analysis showing that, under the standard commitment-depth surrogate, state-conditioned commitment strictly dominates any fixed depth whenever the locally optimal depth varies across states.

AI.tldr: A method for adaptive commitment depth in long-horizon reasoning that learns state-conditioned commitment depth policies, outperforming fixed-depth baselines and large language models in puzzle-solving tasks.
AI.motivation: Long-horizon reasoning requires deciding not only what actions to take, but also how long to commit to executing actions open-loop before replanning. Current systems typically use hand-designed fixed commitment depths, creating a suboptimal trade-off between replanning cost and compounding execution error.
AI.method: Treat commitment depth as a learnable, state-conditioned variable of the policy itself. Instantiate this within a model-native vision-language policy that jointly predicts both what to execute and for how long. Use Sliding Puzzle and Sokoban as test environments.
AI.result: The adaptive policy Pareto-dominates all non-degenerate fixed-depth baselines, achieving up to 12.5 percentage points higher solve rate while using ~25% fewer primitive actions per episode. Despite using a 7B backbone, outperforms GPT-5.5 and Claude Sonnet, while open-weight vision-language models achieve 0% zero-shot success.
AI.conclusion: State-conditioned commitment depth policies strictly dominate fixed-depth approaches when optimal depth varies across states, demonstrating the value of learning adaptive commitment strategies for long-horizon reasoning tasks.

title: Reasoning-Aware Training for Time Series Forecasting
summary: Time Series Foundation Models (TSFMs) excel at numerical forecasting but operate as black boxes lacking qualitative reasoning. Conversely, applying LLMs directly to temporal data introduces a modality gap: text tokenizers fragment continuous numerical values, degrading mathematical relationships and exploding sequence lengths, leading to computational overhead. To resolve this, we introduce STRIDE (Strategic Time-series Reasoning Injected via Distilled Embeddings), a novel framework natively integrating LLM reasoning into the continuous embedding space of TSFMs. Instead of discrete tokens, STRIDE distills reasoning traces into a lightweight LLM, dynamically projecting its mean-pooled hidden states as a cross-modal prior into the target numerical encoder. The architecture is jointly optimized using cross-entropy and quantile losses. Evaluations demonstrate STRIDE establishes state-of-the-art numerical forecasting on GIFT-Eval (0.674 MASE, 0.454 CRPS) compared to TSFMs and exhibits superior in-domain and out-of-domain numerical as well as reasoning performance on TFRBench. Specifically, STRIDE acts as a plug-and-play enhancement, consistently improving diverse TSFMs (e.g., Chronos-2, Timer-S1) across various LLM configurations. Thus, injecting semantic reasoning as a continuous prior equips TSFMs with human-interpretable reasoning while fundamentally improving predictive accuracy.

AI.tldr: STRIDE is a novel framework that integrates LLM reasoning into Time Series Foundation Models by distilling reasoning traces into a lightweight LLM and projecting its hidden states as cross-modal priors into numerical encoders, improving both forecasting accuracy and interpretability.
AI.motivation: Current Time Series Foundation Models (TSFMs) excel at numerical forecasting but operate as black boxes lacking qualitative reasoning. Meanwhile, applying LLMs directly to temporal data introduces a modality gap where text tokenizers fragment continuous numerical values, degrading mathematical relationships and causing computational inefficiencies due to exploding sequence lengths.
AI.method: STRIDE (Strategic Time-series Reasoning Injected via Distilled Embeddings) integrates LLM reasoning into TSFMs by: 1) distilling reasoning traces into a lightweight LLM, 2) dynamically projecting mean-pooled hidden states as cross-modal priors into target numerical encoders, and 3) using joint optimization with cross-entropy and quantile losses.
AI.result: STRIDE achieves state-of-the-art numerical forecasting on GIFT-Eval (0.674 MASE, 0.454 CRPS) and superior in-domain/out-of-domain numerical and reasoning performance on TFRBench. It acts as plug-and-play enhancement, consistently improving diverse TSFMs like Chronos-2 and Timer-S1 across various LLM configurations.
AI.conclusion: Injecting semantic reasoning as a continuous prior equips TSFMs with human-interpretable reasoning while fundamentally improving predictive accuracy, bridging the gap between black-box numerical models and discrete token-based language models.

title: Cross-Family Universality of Behavioral Axes via Anchor-Projected Representations
summary: Large language models from different families use different hidden dimensions, tokenizers, and training procedures, making behavioral directions difficult to compare or transfer across models. We introduce an anchor-projection framework that maps hidden representations from each model into a shared anchor coordinate space (ACS). Behavioral directions extracted from source models are projected into ACS and averaged into a canonical direction. For a new model, the canonical direction is reconstructed into its native hidden space using only anchor activations, without fine-tuning or target-specific direction extraction. We evaluate five instruction-tuned model families and ten behavioral axes. We find that same-axis directions align tightly across the Llama-Qwen-Mistral-Phi (LQMP) cluster in ACS. This shared structure transfers to downstream tasks. For the aligned LQMP cluster, held-out targets achieve (0.83) ten-way detection accuracy and (0.95) mean binary AUROC, while canonical steering induces refusal-rate shifts of up to +0.46% under distribution shift. Sensitivity analyses show that two source models and small anchor pools already suffice to approximate transferable directions. Overall, ACS provides a novel perspective on cross-family interpretability, revealing that representation-level transfer remains robust across model families.

AI.tldr: Cross-family behavioral direction transfer through anchor-projection framework maps different LLMs into shared coordinate space, enabling transfer of behavioral directions without fine-tuning.
AI.motivation: Different LLM families use different hidden dimensions, tokenizers, and training procedures, making behavioral directions difficult to compare or transfer across models, limiting cross-family interpretability.
AI.method: Introduce anchor-projection framework that maps hidden representations from each model into shared anchor coordinate space (ACS). Extract behavioral directions from source models, project into ACS, average into canonical direction, then reconstruct into target model's native space using only anchor activations.
AI.result: Found that same-axis directions align tightly across Llama-Qwen-Mistral-Phi cluster in ACS. For this cluster: held-out targets achieve 0.83 ten-way detection accuracy and 0.95 mean binary AUROC; canonical steering induces refusal-rate shifts up to +0.46% under distribution shift. Two source models with small anchor pools suffice for transferable directions.
AI.conclusion: ACS provides novel perspective on cross-family interpretability, showing representation-level transfer remains robust across model families, enabling behavioral direction transfer without target-specific fine-tuning.

title: ReLibra: Routing-Replay-Guided Load Balancing for MoE Training in Reinforcement Learning
summary: Load imbalance is a long-standing challenge in Mixture-of-Experts (MoE) training and is exacerbated in reinforcement learning (RL) for LLMs, where hot experts can shift frequently across micro-batches. Existing MoE training systems rely on historical loads to predict future expert demand, making them less effective under sharp fluctuations. We propose ReLibra, an MoE RL training system that exploits a unique opportunity in RL's rollout-training workflow, routing replay, to enable fine-grained load balancing at micro-batch granularity. Because rollout and training process the same tokens with the same MoE parameters, the token-to-expert routing decisions are known before training starts. Leveraging this information, ReLibra places two MoE load-balancing mechanisms at inter- and intra-batch timescales, matching their communication patterns to hierarchical network bandwidths. At the inter-batch timescale, ReLibra performs expert reordering to redistribute experts for batch-level cross-node balancing; at the intra-batch timescale, it dynamically performs expert replication within a node to absorb micro-batch-level load fluctuations. Experiments on diverse MoE LLMs and RL workloads show that ReLibra improves training throughput by up to 1.6$\times$ over Megatron-LM and by up to 1.2$\times$ over EPLB, even when EPLB is given oracle loads. Moreover, ReLibra remains within 6%-10% of the throughput of an idealized balanced baseline.

AI.tldr: ReLibra is a MoE RL training system that uses rollout routing information to achieve fine-grained load balancing, improving throughput significantly over existing systems.
AI.motivation: Load imbalance is a persistent problem in MoE training, especially in RL for LLMs where expert demand fluctuates rapidly across micro-batches. Existing systems that rely on historical load predictions fail under sharp fluctuations, limiting training efficiency.
AI.method: ReLibra exploits the unique rollout-training workflow in RL where the same tokens are processed with the same MoE parameters in both phases. It uses known routing decisions from rollouts to implement two load-balancing mechanisms: inter-batch expert reordering for cross-node balancing, and intra-batch dynamic expert replication for micro-batch-level fluctuations.
AI.result: ReLibra achieves up to 1.6× throughput improvement over Megatron-LM and up to 1.2× over EPLB (even with oracle loads). It maintains within 6%-10% of an idealized balanced baseline throughput across diverse MoE LLMs and RL workloads.
AI.conclusion: ReLibra demonstrates that leveraging RL-specific workflow characteristics (rollout routing information) enables effective load balancing in MoE training, significantly improving training efficiency under dynamic expert demand patterns.

title: M2A: Synergizing Mathematical and Agentic Reasoning in Large Language Models
summary: While reasoning has become a central capability of large language models (LLMs), the reasoning patterns required for different scenarios are often misaligned. Mathematical reasoning typically relies on intrinsic logic to solve closed-world problems in a single response, whereas agentic reasoning requires not only internal reasoning but also multi-turn interaction with external environments, interleaving thought and action. This misalignment prevents mathematical and agentic reasoning from effectively benefiting from each other, often yielding unstable reasoning behavior and only limited performance gains under multi-task learning. In this paper, we propose M2A, a novel paradigm that synergizes mathematical and agentic reasoning via model merging. To avoid overfitting to superficial reasoning patterns under joint training, M2A operates directly in parameter space: it identifies the feature subspace critical for agent behavior, and merges the mathematical reasoning task vector only along its null space, thereby injecting reasoning capability along directions that do not perturb agent behavior. Unlike SFT or RL, M2A requires no additional gradient-update and exposes the merging coefficient as a simple knob for controlling reasoning length. Experiments in a challenging real-world coding agent setting show that our method effectively extends agentic reasoning depth and delivers substantial performance improvements. Applied to a fine-tuned Qwen3-8B, M2A improves its SWE-Bench Verified resolved rate from 44.0% to 51.2% without retraining the model. Code is available at https://github.com/laplucky/M2A.git.

AI.tldr: M2A is a novel model merging paradigm that synergizes mathematical and agentic reasoning by merging mathematical reasoning task vectors only in the null space of agentic features, allowing reasoning capability injection without perturbing agent behavior.
AI.motivation: Mathematical and agentic reasoning are misaligned - mathematical reasoning uses intrinsic logic for closed-world single responses, while agentic reasoning requires multi-turn interaction with external environments. This misalignment prevents effective mutual benefit and yields unstable behavior in multi-task learning.
AI.method: M2A operates in parameter space without gradient updates: identifies feature subspace critical for agent behavior, then merges mathematical reasoning task vectors only along its null space. This injects reasoning capability without perturbing agent behavior and allows control of reasoning length via a simple merging coefficient.
AI.result: Applied to fine-tuned Qwen3-8B, M2A improves SWE-Bench Verified resolved rate from 44.0% to 51.2% without retraining. Effective in extending agentic reasoning depth in challenging real-world coding agent settings.
AI.conclusion: M2A successfully bridges the gap between mathematical and agentic reasoning through parameter-space merging, enabling substantial performance improvements without additional training and providing a tunable mechanism for controlling reasoning depth.

title: PAAC: Privacy-Aware Agentic Device-Cloud Collaboration
summary: Large language model (LLM) agents face a structural tension: cloud agents provide strong reasoning but expose user data, while on-device agents preserve privacy at the cost of overall capability. Existing device-cloud designs treat this boundary as a compute split rather than a trust boundary suited to agentic workloads, and existing sanitizers force a choice between policy flexibility and the structural fidelity tool calls require. In this work, we develop PAAC, a privacy-aware agentic framework that aligns planner--executor decomposition with the device-cloud boundary so that role specialization itself becomes the privacy mechanism. The cloud agent reasons over typed placeholder tokens that preserve each sensitive value's reasoning role while discarding its content, while the on-device agent identifies sensitive spans and distills each step's execution outcome into compact key findings. Sanitization confines the on-device LLM to proposing which spans to mask, while a deterministic registry performs all substitution and reversal, keeping actions directly executable on device. On three agentic benchmarks under strict privacy settings, PAAC dominates the Pareto frontier of privacy and accuracy, improving average accuracy by 15-36\% and reducing average leakage by 2-6$\times$ over state-of-the-art device-cloud baselines, with the largest margins on privacy targets outside fixed entity taxonomies. We find consistent improvements on 17 additional benchmarks spanning 10 domains, including math, science, and finance.

AI.tldr: PAAC is a privacy-aware LLM agent framework that aligns planner-executor decomposition with device-cloud boundaries to maintain privacy while preserving agent capabilities, achieving better privacy-accuracy tradeoffs than existing approaches.
AI.motivation: LLM agents face a fundamental tension: cloud agents offer strong reasoning but expose user data, while on-device agents preserve privacy at the cost of reduced capabilities. Existing approaches treat the device-cloud boundary as a compute split rather than a trust boundary suitable for agentic workloads, and current sanitizers force choices between policy flexibility and the structural fidelity needed for tool calls.
AI.method: PAAC aligns planner-executor decomposition with device-cloud boundaries, using typed placeholder tokens that preserve sensitive values' reasoning roles while discarding their content. The cloud agent reasons over these placeholders, while the on-device agent identifies sensitive spans and distills execution outcomes into compact key findings. Sanitization confines the on-device LLM to proposing which spans to mask, with a deterministic registry handling substitution and reversal to keep actions executable on device.
AI.result: On three agentic benchmarks under strict privacy settings, PAAC dominates the Pareto frontier of privacy and accuracy, improving average accuracy by 15-36% and reducing average leakage by 2-6× over state-of-the-art device-cloud baselines. The largest improvements are on privacy targets outside fixed entity taxonomies. Consistent improvements are also shown on 17 additional benchmarks spanning 10 domains including math, science, and finance.
AI.conclusion: PAAC successfully addresses the privacy-capability tension in LLM agents by structurally aligning role specialization with privacy mechanisms, enabling effective device-cloud collaboration while maintaining strong privacy guarantees and agentic capabilities across diverse domains.

title: The Gordian Knot for VLMs: Diagrammatic Knot Reasoning as a Hard Benchmark
summary: A vision-language model can look at a knot diagram and report what it sees, yet fail to act on that structure. KnotBench pairs an 858,318-image corpus from 1,951 prime-knot prototypes (crossing numbers 3 to 19) with a protocol whose answers are checked against Regina's canonical knot signature. Its 14 tasks span four families, equivalence judgment, move prediction, identification, and cross-modal grounding; an image-versus-symbol split locates failures along the perception-operation gap. We score Claude Opus 4.7 and GPT-5, each with and without thinking, under a 64K output-token budget matched on both vendors. Across 56 (task, model) cases, 15 sit at or below a random baseline and 8 of 14 tasks have a best score under 1.5x random. On diagram-to-symbol transcription, no model produces a strictly correct string, and permissive Regina decoding recovers the knot in 0 to 4 of 100 items. Thinking-mode reasoning lifts overall accuracy by 1.65 points for Claude and 9.25 points for GPT-5, narrowing the gap only modestly. Read together, the four families suggest current vision-language models hold features of a diagram but lack apparatus to simulate moves on those features.

AI.tldr: KnotBench is a new benchmark with 858,318 knot diagram images to evaluate vision-language models' ability to understand and reason about knots, revealing that current models can perceive knot structure but cannot simulate moves or operations on them.
AI.motivation: The paper addresses the gap between perception and operation in vision-language models - while models can describe what they see in knot diagrams, they fail to act on that structure. The authors aim to systematically evaluate this perception-operation gap specifically for knot theory understanding.
AI.method: Created KnotBench: an 858,318-image corpus from 1,951 prime-knot prototypes (crossing numbers 3-19). Includes 14 tasks across four families: equivalence judgment, move prediction, identification, and cross-modal grounding. Tasks split into image-versus-symbol to isolate perception failures. Evaluated Claude Opus 4.7 and GPT-5 with/without thinking under matched 64K token budgets. Answers verified against Regina's canonical knot signature.
AI.result: Poor performance across the board: 15 of 56 (task, model) cases at/below random baseline; 8 of 14 tasks have best score under 1.5x random. On diagram-to-symbol transcription: no model produced strictly correct strings; permissive decoding recovered knots in 0-4 of 100 items. Thinking helped slightly: +1.65 points for Claude, +9.25 for GPT-5, but gap remains large.
AI.conclusion: Current vision-language models can extract features from knot diagrams but lack the apparatus to simulate moves or perform operations on those features. The perception-operation gap is substantial, suggesting models need more than just recognition capabilities to truly understand and reason about mathematical structures like knots.

title: FLUX: Geometry-Aware Longitudinal Flow Matching with Mixture of Experts
summary: Many biological systems evolve through continuous local dynamics while switching between latent regimes defined by learning, stimulus context, internal state, or developmental stage. These processes are often observed only as unpaired longitudinal snapshots: the same cells, neurons, or animals are not tracked as matched trajectories, even though population states are sampled across successive stages. This creates two coupled challenges. First, trajectories must respect curved low-dimensional manifolds embedded in high-dimensional biological measurements. Second, the model must identify when the transport mechanism itself changes. We introduce FLUX (FLow matching for Unpaired longitudinal data with miXture-of-experts), a geometry-aware longitudinal flow-matching framework for joint transport modeling and unsupervised regime discovery. FLUX learns a data-dependent metric from pooled labeled and unlabeled observations, uses that metric to construct geometry-aware conditional paths between adjacent marginals, and decomposes the resulting velocity field into sparse expert vector fields selected by a Straight-Through Gumbel-Softmax router. Across manifold controls, a regime-switching Lorenz system, widefield cortical calcium imaging during associative learning, and embryoid body single-cell differentiation, FLUX reconstructs longitudinal transport while recovering interpretable regime structure. Ablations show that mixture-of-experts routing alone is insufficient: FLUX without geometric learning can fit local transport but fails or weakens regime discovery when regimes are encoded in local dynamics. These results suggest that geometry-aware velocity decomposition provides a general strategy for discovering latent biological state transitions from unpaired longitudinal snapshots.

AI.tldr: FLUX is a geometry-aware longitudinal flow-matching framework that jointly models transport between unpaired longitudinal snapshots and discovers latent regimes using mixture-of-experts decomposition with a data-dependent metric.
AI.motivation: Many biological systems evolve through continuous local dynamics while switching between latent regimes, but are observed only as unpaired longitudinal snapshots. This creates two challenges: trajectories must respect curved low-dimensional manifolds, and models must identify when transport mechanisms themselves change between regimes.
AI.method: FLUX learns a data-dependent metric from pooled labeled/unlabeled observations, constructs geometry-aware conditional paths between adjacent marginals, and decomposes velocity fields into sparse expert vector fields selected by a Straight-Through Gumbel-Softmax router (mixture-of-experts approach).
AI.result: FLUX successfully reconstructs longitudinal transport and recovers interpretable regime structure across diverse applications: manifold controls, regime-switching Lorenz systems, widefield cortical calcium imaging during learning, and embryoid body single-cell differentiation. Ablations show geometry-aware learning is essential for regime discovery.
AI.conclusion: Geometry-aware velocity decomposition provides a general strategy for discovering latent biological state transitions from unpaired longitudinal snapshots, combining geometric learning with mixture-of-experts routing for effective regime discovery.

title: Separate First, Fuse Later: Mitigating Cross-Modal Interference in Audio-Visual LLMs Reasoning with Modality-Specific Chain-of-Thought
summary: Audio and vision provide complementary evidence for audio-visual question answering, yet current audio-visual large language models may suffer from cross-modal interference: information from one modality misguides the interpretation of another, thereby inducing hallucinations. We attribute this issue to uncontrolled cross-modal interactions during intermediate reasoning. To mitigate this, we propose Separate First, Fuse Later (SFFL), an audio-visual reasoning framework designed to reduce cross-modal interference. SFFL enforces modality-specific chain-of-thought reasoning, producing separate audio and visual reasoning traces and integrating evidence for answering. We construct modality-preference labels via a data pipeline under different modality input settings. We use these labels as an auxiliary reward in reinforcement learning to encourage a instance-dependent preference for modality cues when answering. We further introduce a modality-specific reasoning mechanism that preserves modality isolation during the separated reasoning stage while enabling full access to cross-modal information at the evidence fusion stage. Experiments demonstrate consistent improvements in both accuracy and robustness, yielding an average relative gain of 5.16\% on general AVQA benchmarks and 11.17\% on a cross-modal hallucination benchmark.

AI.tldr: SFFL framework reduces cross-modal hallucinations in audio-visual QA by separating modality reasoning before fusion.
AI.motivation: Current audio-visual LLMs suffer from cross-modal interference where information from one modality misguides interpretation of another, causing hallucinations during reasoning.
AI.method: Proposes Separate First, Fuse Later (SFFL) with modality-specific chain-of-thought reasoning, modality-preference labels via data pipeline, auxiliary RL reward for instance-dependent modality preference, and modality isolation during reasoning stage.
AI.result: Achieves average relative gain of 5.16% on general AVQA benchmarks and 11.17% on cross-modal hallucination benchmark, showing improved accuracy and robustness.
AI.conclusion: Separating modality reasoning before fusion effectively reduces cross-modal interference and hallucinations in audio-visual question answering systems.

title: Fitting Multilinear Polynomials for Logic Gate Networks
summary: We study learnable logic gate networks that stack layers of 2-input Boolean gates to build combinational circuits. Every 2-input gate has a unique multilinear polynomial with 4 coefficients, so the 16 Boolean gates form a codebook of prototypes in a 4-dimensional space, reducing training to a vector-quantization problem. The baseline method, Soft-Mix, learns a 16-dimensional softmax over gate identities, but the codebook has rank~4: 11 of 15 simplex directions carry nullspace gradient, and at uniform initialization the backward signal vanishes exactly. We prove that no affine product reparameterization fixes the resulting interaction-coefficient starvation under STE, and show that the covariance Jacobian of soft-VQ selection bypasses it by coupling the starved coefficient to the always-active constant channel. Working in the 4-dimensional polynomial space reduces each neuron from 16 to 4 parameters. On seven datasets, at least one 4-parameter method matches or exceeds Soft-Mix on every dataset; the CovJac advantage over STE grows monotonically with interaction demand across all seven datasets. At depth, Soft-Mix collapses ($-37.3$pp on CIFAR-10 at 12 layers) while CovJac holds ($-0.5$pp on CIFAR-10, stable on MNIST).

AI.tldr: Learnable logic gate networks represent Boolean gates as 4D polynomial coefficients, creating a 4D codebook. Traditional softmax methods suffer from gradient vanishing due to rank deficiency, while Covariance Jacobian (CovJac) maintains stable training by coupling starved coefficients to constant channels, achieving superior performance especially at depth and for high interaction demands.
AI.motivation: Traditional neural networks with softmax-based gate selection suffer from gradient vanishing issues due to rank deficiency in the 16-dimensional gate space, particularly at initialization and during training. This leads to interaction-coefficient starvation and poor performance, especially in deep networks and tasks requiring high interaction complexity.
AI.method: Proposes representing each 2-input Boolean gate as a unique multilinear polynomial with 4 coefficients, forming a 4D codebook. Introduces Covariance Jacobian (CovJac) method that bypasses gradient vanishing by coupling starved interaction coefficients to always-active constant channels through the covariance structure of vector quantization selection. Reduces parameters from 16 to 4 per neuron by working in polynomial space.
AI.result: On seven datasets, 4-parameter methods match or exceed traditional Soft-Mix. CovJac advantage over STE grows monotonically with interaction demand. In deep networks (12 layers), Soft-Mix collapses (-37.3pp on CIFAR-10) while CovJac remains stable (-0.5pp on CIFAR-10, stable on MNIST).
AI.conclusion: Working directly in the 4D polynomial space of Boolean gates is both theoretically sound and practically effective. CovJac's ability to maintain gradient flow through covariance structure solves critical training issues in learnable logic gate networks, enabling stable training even in deep architectures where traditional methods fail catastrophically.

title: Sketch-and-Verify: Structured Inference-Time Scaling via Program Sketching
summary: SKETCHVERIFY is a within-tier cost-performance policy, not a universal accuracy improvement. The operational question: a practitioner stuck with a small, cheap code model (here, Gemini 3.1 Flash Lite) for latency, deployment, or budget reasons -- how should they spend a small amount of extra test-time compute? SKETCHVERIFY factorizes the search space: the LLM enumerates K distinct algorithmic strategies, writes a program sketch for each (a partial program with ?? holes), and fills each sketch M times, producing K x M structurally diverse candidates that are verified by execution and selected by fingerprint clustering. Each extra sketch is guaranteed to explore a different algorithm; each extra flat sample likely duplicates an existing one. Our central evidence is a cost-quality Pareto plot on HumanEval+ across three Gemini tiers (Lite, Flash, Pro), and a reanalysis of the 19 problems where Lite greedy fails. Two findings: (1) Within-tier, sketching dominates flat sampling at matched candidate count. On the hard subset, Lite Sketch K=2, M=5 recovers 11/19 (58%) vs. flat N=10 at 5/19 (26%, +32pp); Lite Sketch K=10, M=10 recovers 15/19 (79%) vs. flat N=100 at 10/19 (53%, +26pp). Flat cannot close the gap even at ~3x the budget: flat N=50 still loses to Sketch K=2, M=5 by +11pp. (2) Cross-tier, sketching does not replace upgrading. Pro greedy (89%) dominates Lite Sketch K=10, M=10 (79%) on both pass@1 and dollar cost. Practitioner rule: if a stronger tier is available, use greedy on it; otherwise sketching is the cost-effective way to spend extra compute. We characterize the K-vs-M trade-off via a Flash Lite scaling sweep, report HumanEval+ saturation on Flash and Pro, and show the method composes cleanly with execution-based selection from the concurrent Semantic Voting line of work.

AI.tldr: SKETCHVERIFY is a within-tier cost-performance policy for code generation that uses algorithmic sketching to generate diverse program variants, outperforming flat sampling when using small, cheap models like Gemini 3.1 Flash Lite for latency/budget reasons.
AI.motivation: The paper addresses practitioners who are constrained to use small, cheap LLMs (like Gemini 3.1 Flash Lite) for deployment, latency, or budget reasons. When such practitioners have a small amount of extra test-time compute to spend, they need guidance on how to best use it to improve code generation quality. Existing approaches like flat sampling tend to generate redundant code variants, while upgrading to more expensive models may not be feasible.
AI.method: SKETCHVERIFY factorizes the search space by having the LLM: 1) enumerate K distinct algorithmic strategies, 2) write a program sketch for each strategy (partial program with placeholder holes), 3) fill each sketch M times to generate K x M structurally diverse candidates. These candidates are then verified by execution and selected through fingerprint clustering. Each additional sketch explores a different algorithm, while additional flat samples tend to duplicate existing ones.
AI.result: Two key findings: (1) Within-tier, sketching dominates flat sampling at matched candidate counts. On hard HumanEval+ problems where Lite greedy fails, Sketch K=2,M=5 recovers 58% vs flat N=10 at 26% (+32pp); Sketch K=10,M=10 recovers 79% vs flat N=100 at 53% (+26pp). Flat sampling cannot close the gap even at ~3x the budget. (2) Cross-tier, sketching doesn't replace upgrading models - Pro greedy (89%) dominates Lite sketching (79%). The method scales well, saturates on HumanEval+, and composes cleanly with execution-based selection techniques.
AI.conclusion: SKETCHVERIFY is not a universal accuracy improvement but a within-tier cost-performance policy. Practitioner rule: if a stronger model tier is available, use greedy sampling on it; otherwise, sketching is the most cost-effective way to spend extra compute when stuck with small, cheap models. The approach effectively explores algorithmic diversity rather than generating redundant variations.

title: expo: Exploration-prioritized policy optimization via adaptive kl regulation and gaussian curriculum sampling
summary: Reinforcement Learning with Verifiable Rewards (RLVR) has become the standard paradigm for LLM mathematical reasoning, where Group Relative Policy Optimization (GRPO) serves as the mainstream algorithm. We point out two understudied inefficiencies existing in GRPO. First, the fixed KL penalty coefficient overly restricts policy exploration at stages where the model requires significant deviation from the reference policy. Second, uniform sampling of training questions ignores that moderately difficult problems provide the most informative gradient signals for optimization. We propose Exploration-Prioritized Policy Optimization (EXPO) with two lightweight plug-in modules. The Accuracy-Conditioned KL Scaling (AKL) dynamically adjusts KL regularization strength through a smooth nonlinear function of batch average accuracy, relaxing the penalty when the model underperforms and strengthening it when the model achieves good results. The Gaussian Curriculum Sampling (GCS) assigns sampling weights to questions following a Gaussian distribution centered at moderate accuracy around 0.5, focusing training on the model's learning frontier. We conduct extensive experiments on DeepSeek-R1-Distill-Qwen-1.5B and Qwen3-8B-Base over six mathematical reasoning benchmarks. The results show EXPO steadily surpasses vanilla GRPO. It obtains an absolute gain of 13.34 on AIME 2025 pass@32, rising from 63.33 percent to 76.67 percent, and achieves an average pass@32 improvement of 2.66 on the 8B model. The much larger performance gains on pass@32 compared with pass@1 demonstrate that EXPO effectively enlarges the model's exploration boundary under a fixed inference cost budget.

AI.tldr: EXPO improves GRPO for LLM math reasoning with adaptive KL scaling and curriculum sampling, achieving significant gains on hard problems.
AI.motivation: The paper identifies two inefficiencies in the mainstream GRPO algorithm for LLM mathematical reasoning: 1) fixed KL penalty restricts policy exploration when large deviation from reference policy is needed, and 2) uniform question sampling ignores that moderately difficult problems provide the most informative gradient signals.
AI.method: Proposes EXPO (Exploration-Prioritized Policy Optimization) with two modules: 1) Accuracy-Conditioned KL Scaling (AKL) that dynamically adjusts KL regularization strength based on batch accuracy (relaxing penalty when model underperforms, strengthening when successful), and 2) Gaussian Curriculum Sampling (GCS) that assigns sampling weights following a Gaussian distribution centered at moderate accuracy (~0.5), focusing training on the model's learning frontier.
AI.result: EXPO consistently outperforms vanilla GRPO across six mathematical reasoning benchmarks. On AIME 2025, achieves 13.34% absolute gain in pass@32 (63.33% to 76.67%). Average pass@32 improvement of 2.66% on 8B models. Larger gains on pass@32 vs pass@1 show EXPO effectively expands exploration boundaries under fixed inference cost.
AI.conclusion: EXPO addresses key inefficiencies in GRPO through adaptive KL regularization and intelligent curriculum sampling, enabling better exploration and learning efficiency for LLM mathematical reasoning, particularly improving performance on challenging problems within computational constraints.

title: Optimised Support Vector Regression for California Housing Price Prediction: The Critical Role of Feature Engineering and Hyperparameter Tuning
summary: In the recent literature, Support Vector Regression (SVR) has been cited as one of the weakest performers on the California Housing benchmark dataset, with Preethi et al. (2025)specifically ranking it last among the algorithms they tested, reporting an R2 of only 0.60. This paper examines whether the previously reported performance reflects experimental configuration choices rather than an inherent algorithmic limitation. A structured experimental workflow is applied: ten domain-motivated derived features are constructed from the eight raw inputs, an exploratory ensemble feature importance analysis identifies the most predictive candidates, and a randomised search over hyperparameter combinations with three-fold cross-validation selects the optimal SVR configuration within a leakage-safe scikit-learn Pipeline. A formal four-stage ablation study isolates the contribution of each component: scaling alone accounts for +0.744 in R2 (from -0.054 to 0.690), feature engineering adds +0.026 (to 0.716), and hyperparameter tuning contributes +0.008 (to 0.723). The resulting tuned SVR achieves a test R2 of 0.723, a 0.123-point absolute improvement over the previously reported SVR result (from 0.60 to 0.723, approximately 20% relative gain). In the ten-model comparison, the tuned SVR ranks fourth with R2 = 0.723, below XGBoost (0.832), Random Forest (0.814) and Gradient Boosting (0.783), while substantially outperforming simpler baselines. Ten-fold cross-validation yields a mean R2 of 0.703 (95% CI: [0.630, 0.775]), confirming robust generalisation. The observed improvement from R2 = 0.60 to R2 = 0.723 is associated primarily with proper feature scaling within a unified preprocessing pipeline, with domain-motivated feature engineering and systematic hyperparameter tuning, providing further incremental gains.

AI.tldr: SVR's poor performance on California Housing dataset (R²=0.60) was due to suboptimal experimental setup. Proper feature scaling (+0.744 R²), domain feature engineering (+0.026), and systematic hyperparameter tuning (+0.008) boosted SVR to R²=0.723, a 20% improvement.
AI.motivation: Recent literature reported SVR as one of the weakest performers on California Housing dataset, ranking last with R²=0.60. The paper investigates whether this poor performance reflects experimental configuration choices rather than inherent algorithmic limitations.
AI.method: A structured experimental workflow: (1) construct ten domain-motivated derived features from eight raw inputs, (2) exploratory ensemble feature importance analysis to identify predictive candidates, (3) randomized search over hyperparameter combinations with three-fold cross-validation in leakage-safe scikit-learn Pipeline. Four-stage ablation study isolates contributions of scaling, feature engineering, and hyperparameter tuning.
AI.result: Proper feature scaling alone improved R² from -0.054 to 0.690 (+0.744). Feature engineering added +0.026 (to 0.716). Hyperparameter tuning contributed +0.008 (to 0.723). Final tuned SVR achieved test R²=0.723, a 0.123 absolute improvement (20% relative gain). In ten-model comparison, tuned SVR ranked fourth (below XGBoost, Random Forest, Gradient Boosting). Ten-fold CV confirmed robust generalization (mean R²=0.703, 95% CI: [0.630, 0.775]).
AI.conclusion: SVR's previously reported poor performance was primarily due to improper feature scaling. With proper preprocessing (scaling in unified pipeline), domain feature engineering, and systematic hyperparameter tuning, SVR performance substantially improves, demonstrating the critical importance of proper experimental methodology over algorithmic comparisons.

title: HAGE: Harnessing Agentic Memory via RL-Driven Weighted Graph Evolution
summary: Memory retrieval in agentic large language model (LLM) systems is often treated as a static lookup problem, relying on flat vector search or fixed binary relational graphs. However, fixed graph structures cannot capture the varying strength, confidence, and query-dependent relevance of relationships between events. In this paper, we propose HAGE, a weighted multi-relational memory framework that reconceptualizes retrieval as sequential, query-conditioned traversal over a unified relational memory graph. Memory is organized as relation-specific graph views over shared memory nodes, where each edge is associated with a trainable relation feature vector encoding multiple relational signals. Given a query, an LLM-based classifier identifies the relational intent, and a routing network dynamically modulates the corresponding dimensions of the edge embedding. Traversal scores are computed via a learned combination of semantic similarity and these query-conditioned edge representations. This allows memory traversal to prioritize high-utility relational paths while softly suppressing noisy or weakly relevant connections. Beyond adaptive traversal, HAGE further introduces a reinforcement learning-based training framework that jointly optimizes routing behavior and edge representations using downstream tasks. Finally, empirical results demonstrate improved long-horizon reasoning accuracy and a favorable accuracy-efficiency trade-off compared to state-of-the-art agentic memory systems. Our code is available at https://github.com/FredJiang0324/HAGE_MVPReview.

AI.tldr: HAGE: A weighted multi-relational memory framework for LLMs that treats memory retrieval as query-conditioned graph traversal with dynamically modulated edge weights.
AI.motivation: Current agentic LLM systems treat memory retrieval as static lookup using flat vector search or fixed binary relational graphs, which cannot capture varying relationship strengths, confidence levels, and query-dependent relevance between events.
AI.method: HAGE organizes memory as relation-specific graph views over shared nodes with trainable relation feature vectors. It uses an LLM-based classifier to identify relational intent, a routing network to dynamically modulate edge embeddings, and computes traversal scores via learned combination of semantic similarity and query-conditioned edge representations.
AI.result: Empirical results show improved long-horizon reasoning accuracy and favorable accuracy-efficiency trade-off compared to state-of-the-art agentic memory systems.
AI.conclusion: HAGE successfully reconceptualizes memory retrieval as adaptive, query-conditioned graph traversal, providing a more flexible and effective memory system for agentic LLMs.

title: The Cancellation Hypothesis in Critic-Free RL: From Outcome Rewards to Token Credits
summary: A commonly accepted explanation of critic-free RL for LLMs, based on sequence-level rewards, is that it reinforces successful rollouts with a positive advantage while penalizing failed ones. In contrast, we study critic-free RL from a token-level perspective, revealing the token-flipping phenomenon: positive and negative rollouts exhibit remarkably similar proportions of tokens whose probabilities are boosted or suppressed during RL training. To explain this phenomenon, we further show that a token's change in probability is not fully determined by its own advantage; coupled gradient interactions with other tokens also play a non-negligible role. Specifically, these token coupling effects occur primarily between identical tokens that are both predicted with low confidence. Building upon this analysis, we propose the cancellation hypothesis: as a result of coupling, opposing signals cancel out for tokens shared by positive and negative rollouts, while tokens more specific to successful rollouts receive stronger reinforcement, thereby inducing hidden token-level credit assignment from rollout-level rewards. We support this hypothesis with complementary empirical evidence. (1) Compared with training on only positive rollouts, critic-free RL shifts updates from template and formatting tokens toward reasoning tokens; (2) Tokens boosted by critic-free RL consistently demonstrate higher value than suppressed tokens, regardless of whether they originate from positive or negative rollouts. Guided by this view, we implement two batching interventions to encourage or preserve cancellation in critic-free RL training: query-preserved mini-batching and reward-balanced batching. Despite their simplicity, these interventions improve RLVR training across multiple model scales, supporting cancellation as both an explanatory principle and a practical design criterion for critic-free RL training.

AI.tldr: Critic-free RL for LLMs works through token-level coupling effects where opposing signals cancel out for common tokens, allowing hidden credit assignment to reasoning-specific tokens.
AI.motivation: The paper challenges the conventional sequence-level reward explanation of critic-free RL, revealing the token-flipping phenomenon where both positive and negative rollouts show similar token probability changes. The authors aim to understand the underlying token-level mechanisms driving critic-free RL.
AI.method: 1) Analyze critic-free RL from token-level perspective to reveal token-flipping phenomenon. 2) Examine token coupling effects in gradient interactions. 3) Propose cancellation hypothesis to explain token-level credit assignment. 4) Provide empirical evidence through token-level analysis of boosted vs. suppressed tokens. 5) Implement two batching interventions: query-preserved mini-batching and reward-balanced batching to test the hypothesis.
AI.result: 1) Token-flipping phenomenon discovered: positive and negative rollouts show remarkably similar token probability changes. 2) Token coupling effects exist, especially between identical low-confidence tokens. 3) Cancellation hypothesis supported: opposing signals cancel for common tokens, reinforcing successful-rollout-specific tokens. 4) Critic-free RL shifts updates from formatting to reasoning tokens. 5) Boosted tokens consistently show higher value than suppressed tokens. 6) Batching interventions improve RLVR training across multiple model scales.
AI.conclusion: Critic-free RL operates through hidden token-level credit assignment via cancellation effects where opposing signals cancel for shared tokens, allowing reinforcement of reasoning-specific tokens. The cancellation principle serves as both an explanatory framework and practical design criterion for improving critic-free RL training.

title: LoopVLA: Learning Sufficiency in Recurrent Refinement for Vision-Language-Action Models
summary: Current Vision-Language-Action (VLA) models typically treat the deepest representation of a vision-language backbone as universally optimal for action prediction. However, robotic manipulation is composed of many frequent closed-loop spatial adjustments, for which excessive abstraction may waste computation and weaken low-level geometric cues essential for precise control. Existing early-exit strategies attempt to reduce computation by stopping at predefined layers or applying heuristic rules such as action consistency, but they do not directly answer when a representation is actually sufficient for action. In this paper, we present LoopVLA, a recurrent VLA architecture that jointly learns representation refinement, action prediction, and sufficiency estimation. LoopVLA iteratively applies a shared Transformer block to refine multimodal tokens, and at each iteration produces both a candidate action and a sufficiency score that estimates whether further refinement is necessary. By sharing parameters across iterations, LoopVLA decouples refinement from absolute layer indices and grounds sufficiency estimation in the evolving representation itself. Since sufficiency has no direct supervision, we introduce a self-supervised distribution alignment objective, where intermediate confidence scores are trained to match the relative action quality across refinement steps, thereby linking sufficiency learning to policy optimization signals. Experiments on LIBERO, LIBERO-Plus, and VLA-Arena show that LoopVLA pushes the efficiency-performance frontier of VLA policies, reducing parameters by 45% and improving inference throughput by up to 1.7 times while matching or outperforming strong baselines in task success.

AI.tldr: LoopVLA is a recurrent VLA architecture that learns when visual representations are sufficient for action prediction through iterative refinement and self-supervised sufficiency estimation, improving efficiency while maintaining performance.
AI.motivation: Current VLA models treat the deepest representation as universally optimal for action prediction, but robotic manipulation often requires frequent closed-loop spatial adjustments where excessive abstraction wastes computation and weakens low-level geometric cues needed for precise control.
AI.method: LoopVLA uses a recurrent architecture with a shared Transformer block applied iteratively to refine multimodal tokens. At each iteration, it produces both a candidate action and a sufficiency score estimating whether further refinement is needed. The method introduces self-supervised distribution alignment where intermediate confidence scores are trained to match relative action quality across refinement steps.
AI.result: Experiments on LIBERO, LIBERO-Plus, and VLA-Arena show LoopVLA reduces parameters by 45% and improves inference throughput by up to 1.7 times while matching or outperforming strong baselines in task success.
AI.conclusion: LoopVLA advances VLA models by learning when representations are sufficient for action through joint refinement and sufficiency estimation, pushing the efficiency-performance frontier and enabling more compute-efficient robotic manipulation policies.

title: PHIDA: Persistence-Guided Node-to-Cluster Mapping for Online Clustering
comment: This paper is currently under review
summary: Online clustering methods that adaptively create and update nodes as data arrive often make node learning explicit, whereas the mapping from the learned node state to output clusters often remains implicit or simplified. Implicit mappings make output clusters sensitive to weak graph bridges or local relations based on distance in the graph over learned nodes, leaving no explicit constraint on which node groups remain intact during mapping. This paper addresses this gap by proposing PHIDA, a persistence-guided node-to-cluster mapping method for online clustering with learned nodes. PHIDA implements this mapping within Adaptive Resonance Theory (ART)-based online clustering by combining Inverse-Distance ART (IDA) node learning with node-to-cluster mapping constrained by Persistent Homology (PH). Experiments on 24 benchmark datasets show that PHIDA achieves the best average ranks in stationary comparisons that include the recent stationary-only clustering methods, while also improving aggregate performance in the nonstationary setting over the evaluated online methods that adaptively create and update nodes. Ablations and comparisons with conventional node-to-cluster mappings indicate that the observed gains are associated with PH-constrained mapping that preserves raw PH components, together with the use of the PH component view during node learning. Source code is available at https://github.com/Masuyama-lab/PHIDA

AI.tldr: PHIDA proposes a persistence-guided node-to-cluster mapping method for online clustering that combines Inverse-Distance ART node learning with Persistent Homology-constrained mapping to improve cluster stability and performance.
AI.motivation: Current online clustering methods with adaptive node creation often have implicit or simplified mappings from learned nodes to output clusters, making clusters sensitive to weak graph bridges or local distance relations without explicit constraints on which node groups should remain intact during mapping.
AI.method: PHIDA combines Inverse-Distance ART (IDA) node learning with Persistent Homology (PH)-constrained node-to-cluster mapping within Adaptive Resonance Theory-based online clustering framework. It preserves raw PH components during mapping and uses PH component view during node learning.
AI.result: Experiments on 24 benchmark datasets show PHIDA achieves best average ranks in stationary comparisons (including recent stationary-only methods) and improves aggregate performance in nonstationary settings over evaluated online methods with adaptive node creation.
AI.conclusion: The performance gains are associated with PH-constrained mapping that preserves raw PH components and the use of PH component view during node learning, addressing the gap in explicit node-to-cluster mapping constraints for online clustering.

title: Learning the Interaction Prior for Protein-Protein Interaction Prediction: A Model-Agnostic Approach
summary: Protein-protein interactions (PPIs) are fundamental to cellular function and disease mechanisms. Current learning-based PPI predictors focus on learning powerful protein representations but neglect designing specialized classification heads. They mainly rely on generic aggregating methods like concatenation or dot products, which lack biological insight. Motivated by the biological "L3 rule", where multiple length-3 paths between a pair of proteins indicate their interaction likelihood, our study addresses this gap by designing a biologically informed PPI classifier. In this paper, we provide empirical evidence that popular PPI datasets strongly support the L3 rule. We propose an L3-path-regularized graph prompt learning method called L3-PPI, which can generate a prompt graph with virtual L3 paths based on protein representations and controls the number of paths. L3-PPI reformulates the classification of protein embedding pairs into a graph-level classification task over the generated prompt graph. This lightweight module seamlessly integrates with PPI predictors as a plug-and-play component, injecting the interaction prior of complementarity to enhance performance. Extensive experiments show that L3-PPI achieves superior performance enhancements over advanced competitors.

AI.tldr: The paper proposes L3-PPI, a biologically-inspired graph prompt learning method that enhances protein-protein interaction prediction by incorporating the "L3 rule" (multiple length-3 paths indicate interaction likelihood) into classification heads.
AI.motivation: Current PPI predictors focus on learning protein representations but neglect specialized classification heads, using generic methods like concatenation or dot products that lack biological insight. The paper addresses this gap by designing biologically informed classifiers based on the "L3 rule" where multiple length-3 paths between proteins indicate interaction likelihood.
AI.method: The authors propose L3-PPI, an L3-path-regularized graph prompt learning method that generates a prompt graph with virtual L3 paths based on protein representations while controlling the number of paths. It reformulates protein embedding pair classification into graph-level classification over the generated prompt graph.
AI.result: Extensive experiments show that L3-PPI achieves superior performance enhancements over advanced competitors. The method provides empirical evidence that popular PPI datasets strongly support the L3 rule.
AI.conclusion: L3-PPI is a lightweight, plug-and-play module that seamlessly integrates with PPI predictors, injecting the biological interaction prior of complementarity to enhance performance without requiring architectural changes to existing models.

title: MLS-Bench: A Holistic and Rigorous Assessment of AI Systems on Building Better AI
summary: Modern AI progress has been driven by ML methods that are generalizable across settings and scalable to larger regimes. As large language models demonstrate advanced capabilities in reasoning, coding, and engineering tasks, it is increasingly important to understand whether they can discover such methods rather than only apply existing ones. We introduce MLS-Bench, a benchmark for evaluating whether AI systems can invent generalizable and scalable ML methods. MLS-Bench contains 140 tasks across 12 domains, each requiring an agent to improve one targeted component of an ML system or algorithm and demonstrate that the improvement generalizes across controlled settings and scales. We find that current agents remain far from reliably surpassing human-designed methods, and that engineering-style tuning is easier for them than genuine method invention. We further study the effects of test-time scaling, adaptive compute allocation, and context provision on agents' discovery performance, together with case studies of their behavior. Our analyses suggest that the bottleneck is not only in proposing new methods, but also in the scientific insight needed to plan, validate, and scale claims about them. More search, compute, or context alone does not remove this bottleneck.
