We build and maintain a community platform for cumulative and comparable iteration, and release the data and code at https://mls-bench.com.

AI.tldr: A benchmark called MLS-Bench evaluates whether AI agents can invent novel, generalizable, and scalable machine learning methods, finding current systems fall short of human-designed methods, especially in genuine method invention versus engineering tuning.
AI.motivation: With large language models showing advanced capabilities, the paper investigates whether AI systems can discover new ML methods rather than just applying existing ones, moving beyond current applications to genuine method invention.
AI.method: The authors introduce MLS-Bench, a benchmark with 140 tasks across 12 domains requiring agents to improve targeted ML system components and demonstrate improvements generalize across settings and scale. They study effects of test-time scaling, adaptive compute allocation, and context provision on discovery performance.
AI.result: Current AI agents remain far from reliably surpassing human-designed methods; engineering-style tuning is easier than genuine method invention. The bottleneck is in scientific insight needed for planning, validation, and scaling claims about new methods - not just proposing them.
AI.conclusion: AI systems still struggle with genuine ML method invention, particularly lacking scientific insight for validation and scaling. More search, compute, or context alone doesn't solve this bottleneck. The authors provide a community platform for ongoing, comparable evaluation in this area.

title: Prospective Compression in Human Abstraction Learning
summary: A core challenge in program synthesis is online library learning: the incremental acquisition of reusable abstractions under uncertainty about future task demands. Existing algorithms treat library learning as retrospective compression over a static task distribution, where the learned library is determined by the corpus of past tasks. However, real-world learning domains are often non-stationary, with tasks arising from a generative process that evolves over time. We propose and test the hypothesis that in non-stationary domains human library learning selects abstractions prospectively: targeting compression of future tasks. We study this question using the Pattern Builder Task, a visual program synthesis paradigm in which participants construct increasingly complex geometric patterns from a small set of primitives, transformations, and custom helpers that carry forward across trials. Using this task, we conduct two experiments with complementary latent curricula, designed to dissociate between behaviors consistent with prospective compression, and alternative library learning accounts. Using six computational models spanning online library learning strategies, we show that human abstraction behavior reflects sensitivity to latent, non-stationary structure in the task-generating process. This behavior is consistent with prospective compression, and cannot be captured by existing retrospective compression-based algorithms, or inductive biases modeled by LLM-based program synthesis.

AI.tldr: Humans learn program abstractions prospectively by targeting future task compression rather than retrospectively compressing past tasks, unlike existing algorithms that fail in non-stationary domains.
AI.motivation: Existing program synthesis algorithms treat library learning as retrospective compression over static task distributions, but real-world learning domains are often non-stationary with evolving task-generating processes. The paper investigates whether human library learning operates prospectively in such environments.
AI.method: The study uses the Pattern Builder Task, a visual program synthesis paradigm where participants build geometric patterns from primitives, transformations, and custom helpers. Two experiments with complementary latent curricula dissociate between prospective compression and alternative library learning accounts. Six computational models spanning online library learning strategies are used to analyze human behavior.
AI.result: Human abstraction behavior shows sensitivity to latent, non-stationary structure in the task-generating process. This behavior is consistent with prospective compression and cannot be captured by existing retrospective compression algorithms or LLM-based program synthesis inductive biases.
AI.conclusion: Human library learning in non-stationary domains operates prospectively by targeting future task compression, a mechanism not captured by current retrospective compression-based algorithms. This has implications for designing more adaptive program synthesis systems.

title: Event Fields: Learning Latent Event Structure for Waveform Foundation Models
summary: We propose a new class of waveform foundation models that departs from conventional sequence based representations by modeling physiological time series as realizations of latent event processes. Rather than treating signals as collections of local tokens or patches, our approach assumes that clinically meaningful structure arises from temporally extended, interacting events whose boundaries and dynamics are not directly observed. To capture this structure, we introduce a self supervised learning framework that enforces consistency across stochastic segmentations and time frequency projections of the same waveform, encouraging representations that are invariant to signal level perturbations while preserving event level organization. The resulting model combines a segmentation aware encoder with a latent interaction operator that captures dependencies among inferred events, and naturally extends to multimodal settings by aligning modalities through shared event representations. Across a range of physiological benchmarks, including arrhythmia classification, hemodynamic prediction, and waveform retrieval, the proposed method improves performance, robustness, and label efficiency relative to strong sequence based baselines. These results suggest that shifting from signal centric to event centric representations provides a more appropriate inductive bias for modeling physiological dynamics and offers a complementary path to scaling foundation models in healthcare.

AI.tldr: The paper proposes a new waveform foundation model that represents physiological time series as latent event processes rather than conventional sequences, achieving better performance, robustness, and label efficiency across healthcare benchmarks.
AI.motivation: Current sequence-based representations of physiological time series treat signals as collections of local tokens or patches, which may not capture the clinically meaningful structure arising from temporally extended, interacting events whose boundaries and dynamics are not directly observed.
AI.method: A self-supervised learning framework that enforces consistency across stochastic segmentations and time-frequency projections of the same waveform, combining a segmentation-aware encoder with a latent interaction operator that captures dependencies among inferred events, with natural extension to multimodal settings through shared event representations.
AI.result: The proposed method improves performance, robustness, and label efficiency across physiological benchmarks including arrhythmia classification, hemodynamic prediction, and waveform retrieval compared to strong sequence-based baselines.
AI.conclusion: Shifting from signal-centric to event-centric representations provides a more appropriate inductive bias for modeling physiological dynamics and offers a complementary path to scaling foundation models in healthcare.

title: Optimizer-Induced Mode Connectivity: From AdamW to Muon
summary: Mode connectivity has been widely studied, yet the role of the optimizer remains underexplored. We revisit it through optimizer-induced implicit regularization, asking how connectivity behaves when restricted to solutions constrained by a given optimizer. For two-layer ReLU networks, we show that solutions from a single optimizer -- AdamW, Muon, or others in the Lion-$\mathcal{K}$ family -- form a connected set at sufficiently large width, a result not implied by prior work. We then characterize how optimizer-induced regions interact: at large width two different regions can be disjoint or overlap depending on regularization, while in our small-width example AdamW and Muon converge to disconnected zero-loss components separated by a provable loss barrier. Empirically, in GPT-2 pretraining, we observe same-optimizer paths preserve each model's spectrum while cross-optimizer paths traverse a smooth transition. Our results reveal optimizer-dependent structure beyond classical mode connectivity literature.

AI.tldr: Optimizer-induced implicit regularization affects mode connectivity - solutions from the same optimizer form connected sets, while different optimizers can produce disconnected components with loss barriers.
AI.motivation: Mode connectivity has been widely studied, but the role of optimizer-induced implicit regularization remains underexplored. The paper aims to understand how connectivity behaves when solutions are constrained by specific optimizers.
AI.method: Theoretical analysis of two-layer ReLU networks: shows solutions from a single optimizer (AdamW, Muon, or Lion-$\mathcal{K}$ family) form connected sets at sufficient width. Characterization of optimizer-induced regions interaction at large/small width. Empirical study in GPT-2 pretraining comparing same-optimizer vs cross-optimizer paths.
AI.result: 1) Same-optimizer solutions form connected sets at large width. 2) Different optimizer regions can be disjoint or overlap depending on regularization. 3) At small width, AdamW and Muon converge to disconnected zero-loss components with provable loss barrier. 4) In GPT-2, same-optimizer paths preserve model spectrum while cross-optimizer paths show smooth transition.
AI.conclusion: Optimizers induce distinct implicit regularization patterns that create optimizer-dependent structure beyond classical mode connectivity, revealing previously unrecognized optimizer-specific solution geometry.

title: Structure-Centric Graph Foundation Model via Geometric Bases
summary: Graph foundation models (GFMs) seek transferable representations across graph domains but are limited by structural heterogeneity and incompatible node feature spaces. We propose Structure-Centric Graph Foundation Models (SCGFM), which treat graph topology as the primary source of transferable knowledge. Modeling graphs as metric measure spaces, SCGFM introduces learnable geometric bases that define a shared structural coordinate system. Graphs are aligned to these bases via Gromov-Wasserstein distances, yielding structure-aligned latent representations that accommodate heterogeneous graph topologies. To address feature incompatibility, SCGFM employs a structure-aware feature re-encoding mechanism that unifies node representations without assuming a fixed feature dimensionality or requiring dataset-specific preprocessing. Experiments on graph- and node-level tasks demonstrate strong in-domain and cross-domain generalization, outperforming existing GFM approaches.

AI.tldr: SCGFM treats graph topology as the primary transferable knowledge source by modeling graphs as metric measure spaces with learnable geometric bases, achieving superior cross-domain generalization without requiring dataset-specific preprocessing.
AI.motivation: Current graph foundation models are limited by structural heterogeneity across graph domains and incompatible node feature spaces, which hinders their ability to learn truly transferable representations.
AI.method: Models graphs as metric measure spaces with learnable geometric bases that define a shared structural coordinate system. Aligns graphs to these bases via Gromov-Wasserstein distances to produce structure-aligned latent representations, and uses structure-aware feature re-encoding to handle feature incompatibility.
AI.result: SCGFM demonstrates strong in-domain and cross-domain generalization on graph- and node-level tasks, outperforming existing graph foundation model approaches.
AI.conclusion: By centering on graph topology as the primary knowledge source and providing mechanisms to handle structural heterogeneity and feature incompatibility, SCGFM represents an effective approach for building truly transferable graph foundation models.

title: From Single-Step Edit Response to Multi-Step Molecular Optimization
summary: Conditional molecular optimization aims to edit a molecule to realize a specified property shift. In practice, structurally similar molecule data is scarce, while decisions are inherently action-level: at each step, the system must select one local structural edit from a candidate set that is strictly filtered by chemical feasibility rules. This level mismatch between supervision and decision makes oracle-in-the-loop search unstable in molecular optimization. Regressing on property differences between molecule pairs improves data efficiency but relies on oracle-in-the-loop search, entangling transformation effects with global context and providing limited guidance for selecting the next feasible edit, often resorting to oracle-in-the-loop search. For this reason, we propose a response-oriented discrete edit optimization approach comprising two tightly coupled components: a single-step molecular edit response predictor (SMER) and a multi-step planner that composes local predictions into optimization trajectories via guided tree search (SMER-Opt). The approach learns a directional evaluation model over edit actions to support constraint-aware planning. It mines weakly related molecule pairs and decomposes their structural differences into minimal edit units, turning endpoint property annotations into process-level supervision and yielding reusable, transferable action primitives. A directional edit evaluator then scores feasible candidate edits by their likelihood of moving the molecule toward the desired property change, substantially reducing dependence on external evaluator queries at decision time. Code is available at https://anonymous.4open.science/r/SMER.

AI.tldr: SMER-Opt is a response-oriented discrete edit optimization approach for conditional molecular optimization that learns directional edit evaluation to reduce dependence on oracle-in-the-loop search.
AI.motivation: Conditional molecular optimization faces challenges with scarce structurally similar molecule data and action-level decisions that require selecting local structural edits filtered by chemical feasibility rules. The mismatch between supervision and decision levels makes oracle-in-the-loop search unstable, while existing approaches that regress on property differences still rely heavily on such searches.
AI.method: Proposes SMER-Opt with two components: (1) SMER (single-step molecular edit response predictor) that learns a directional evaluation model over edit actions from weakly related molecule pairs by decomposing structural differences into minimal edit units, and (2) a multi-step planner that composes local predictions into optimization trajectories via guided tree search.
AI.result: The approach substantially reduces dependence on external evaluator queries at decision time by scoring feasible candidate edits based on their likelihood of moving the molecule toward desired property change, providing constraint-aware planning and reusable action primitives.
AI.conclusion: SMER-Opt provides an effective approach for conditional molecular optimization that addresses the supervision-decision mismatch problem by learning directional edit evaluation from endpoint property annotations, enabling more stable and efficient optimization without heavy reliance on oracle-in-the-loop search.

title: AAAC: Activation-Aware Adaptive Codebooks for 4-bit LLM Weight Quantization
summary: Post-training weight-only quantization to 4 bits is widely used to reduce the memory and compute costs of large language model inference. Existing PTQ methods, such as AWQ and GPTQ, improve how weights are mapped onto a fixed 4-bit grid through scaling, clipping, or error compensation. To further improve accuracy, methods such as OmniQuant and QuIP\# uses gradient-assisted algorithms at the cost of hours of quantization time. In this work, we propose AAAC (Activation-Aware Adaptive Codebooks), a lightweight method for 4-bit LLM weight quantization. AAAC replaces the fixed scalar codebook used in standard quantization with two small learned scalar codebooks (64 bytes) per layer. Each group of weights selects the codebook that minimizes activation-weighted reconstruction error, encoding the choice in the unused sign bit of the group's positive scale and adding zero storage overhead. AAAC completes in 3--30 minutes on a single GPU, and adds no memory beyond the model itself. We evaluate against AWQ, GPTQ, IF4, GPTVQ, OmniQuant, SqueezeLLM, and QuIP\# across model families. AAAC outperforms baselines at orders-of-magnitude less quantization time.

AI.tldr: AAAC is a lightweight 4-bit weight quantization method for LLMs that replaces fixed scalar codebooks with two small learned codebooks per layer, achieving better accuracy with minimal overhead and 3-30 minute quantization time.
AI.motivation: Post-training 4-bit quantization reduces LLM inference costs but existing methods either have fixed codebooks (AWQ, GPTQ) or require hours of gradient-based quantization time (OmniQuant, QuIP#). There's a need for a lightweight method that improves accuracy without heavy computational overhead.
AI.method: AAAC replaces fixed scalar codebooks with two small learned scalar codebooks (64 bytes per layer). Each weight group selects the codebook minimizing activation-weighted reconstruction error, encoding this choice in the unused sign bit of the group's positive scale, adding zero storage overhead.
AI.result: AAAC outperforms baselines (AWQ, GPTQ, IF4, GPTVQ, OmniQuant, SqueezeLLM, QuIP#) across model families while requiring only 3-30 minutes quantization time on a single GPU with no additional memory beyond the model itself.
AI.conclusion: AAAC provides an efficient, lightweight solution for 4-bit LLM weight quantization that achieves superior accuracy with minimal computational and memory overhead, making it practical for deployment.

title: TimeClaw: A Time-Series AI Agent with Exploratory Execution Learning
summary: Time series analysis underpins forecasting, monitoring, and decision making in domains such as finance and weather, where solving a task often requires both numerical accuracy and contextual reasoning. Recent progress has moved from specialized neural predictors to approaches built on LLMs and foundation models that can reason over time series inputs and use external tools. However, most such systems remain execution-centric: they focus on solving the current instance but learn little from exploratory execution. This is especially limiting in verifiable numeric settings, where multiple candidate executions and tool-use procedures may all be task-valid yet differ sharply in quantitative quality, and where early success can trigger tool-prior collapse that suppresses further exploration. To address this limitation, we present TimeClaw, an exploratory execution learning framework that turns exploratory execution into reusable hierarchical distilled experience through a four-stage loop: Explore, Compare, Distill, and Reinject. TimeClaw combines metric-supervised exploratory execution learning, task-aware tool dropout, and hierarchical distilled experience for inference-time reinjection, while keeping the base model frozen and avoiding online test-time adaptation. In an MTBench-aligned evaluation with 17 tasks that span finance and weather prediction and reasoning tasks, TimeClaw delivers consistent gains over the baselines. These results suggest that, for scientific systems, the bottleneck is not only execution-time capability, but how exploratory experience is compared, distilled, and reused.

AI.tldr: TimeClaw is a framework that transforms exploratory execution in time series analysis into reusable hierarchical distilled experience, improving performance without modifying the base model through a four-stage loop: Explore, Compare, Distill, and Reinject.
AI.motivation: Current time series analysis systems using LLMs and foundation models are execution-centric, focusing only on solving current instances without learning from exploratory execution. This is particularly limiting in verifiable numeric settings where multiple valid solutions can have different quantitative quality, and early success can lead to tool-prior collapse that prevents further exploration.
AI.method: TimeClaw uses a four-stage loop: Explore (generate multiple candidate executions), Compare (evaluate solutions quantitatively), Distill (transform exploratory experience into hierarchical distilled experience), and Reinject (reuse distilled experience at inference time). The framework combines metric-supervised exploratory execution learning, task-aware tool dropout, and hierarchical distilled experience while keeping the base model frozen and avoiding online test-time adaptation.
AI.result: In an MTBench-aligned evaluation with 17 tasks spanning finance and weather prediction/reasoning, TimeClaw delivers consistent gains over baselines. The framework improves performance without modifying the base model parameters.
AI.conclusion: For scientific systems in time series analysis, the bottleneck is not only execution-time capability but also how exploratory experience is compared, distilled, and reused. Exploratory execution learning can be effectively turned into reusable hierarchical distilled experience to improve performance.

title: METBRA25Y: Brazil Surface Meteorology Archive with Harmonized Variables and Quality Control
comment: 12 pages, 5 figures. Dataset paper describing METBRA25Y, a harmonized archive of hourly Brazilian surface meteorological observations derived from INMET records. Dataset available at Zenodo: 10.5281/zenodo.19964979
summary: This data paper describes METBRA25Y, a harmonized archive of hourly surface meteorological observations from Brazil derived from public historical records of the Instituto Nacional de Meteorologia (INMET). The dataset was designed to support reproducible environmental, climatological, hydrological, agricultural, urban-risk, and machine-learning studies that require station-level meteorological time series with standardized variable names and explicit quality-control metadata. The processing workflow ingests annual INMET archives, parses station metadata from raw file headers, normalizes heterogeneous Portuguese column names into a canonical schema, constructs hourly timestamps, consolidates observations by city and station, and exports compressed CSV files together with station manifests, per-station quality flags, daily precipitation aggregates, variable-level failure summaries, and missing-data audits. The quality-control protocol follows a two-stage strategy: first, physically implausible values are converted to missing values and flagged; second, temporal and cross-variable consistency checks generate diagnostic flags without necessarily overwriting the original measurements. The resulting package covers observations between 2000 and 2025, with stationspecific temporal coverage, and includes key meteorological variables such as precipitation, air temperature, dew point, relative humidity, atmospheric pressure, wind speed, wind gust, wind direction, and global solar radiation. Based on the summary files included in the current release snapshot, the archive contains 616 unique station codes across variable summaries, of which 605 have coordinates within a broad Brazil plausibility envelope. This paper documents the dataset provenance, file organization, harmonized schema, quality-control rules, technical validation outputs, limitations, and recommended usage practices.

AI.tldr: METBRA25Y is a harmonized hourly surface meteorological dataset from Brazil (2000-2025) created by standardizing INMET's historical records with quality control and metadata.
AI.motivation: To support reproducible environmental, climatological, hydrological, agricultural, urban-risk, and machine-learning studies that require standardized station-level meteorological time series with explicit quality-control metadata from Brazil.
AI.method: Processing workflow that ingests annual INMET archives, parses station metadata, normalizes Portuguese column names into canonical schema, constructs hourly timestamps, consolidates observations, and applies two-stage quality control (physical plausibility checks followed by temporal/cross-variable consistency checks).
AI.result: Dataset contains 616 unique station codes (605 with valid Brazilian coordinates) with observations from 2000-2025, including key meteorological variables like precipitation, temperature, humidity, pressure, wind, and solar radiation, with comprehensive quality flags and metadata.
AI.conclusion: The paper presents a comprehensive, standardized meteorological dataset for Brazil with documented provenance, quality control, and usage guidelines, enabling reproducible research across multiple disciplines.

title: Classification-Head Bias in Class-Level Machine Unlearning: Diagnosis, Mitigation, and Evaluation
summary: Class-level machine unlearning aims to remove the influence of specified classes while preserving model utility on retained classes. Existing methods are commonly evaluated by retain-set accuracy, forget-set accuracy, and unlearning time, but these metrics provide limited insight into how forgetting is achieved internally. In this paper, we reveal a bias-dominated shortcut in class-level unlearning: the prediction of forgotten classes can be suppressed by decreasing the corresponding bias terms in the final classification head. We first analyze the gradient dynamics of classification-head biases under softmax cross-entropy training, explaining why retain-set-only optimization tends to reduce the biases of absent classes. Based on this observation, we introduce BiasShift as a diagnostic baseline, showing that simple bias manipulation can satisfy conventional unlearning metrics while leaving abnormal bias patterns that reveal forgotten labels. To mitigate excessive forgotten-class bias suppression, we propose two bias-aware mechanisms, namely Two-Stage Bias Gradient Reversal Mechanism (TS-BGRM) and Lower-Bound Hinge Regularization (LB-HR). We further introduce three bias-oriented metrics, including Bias Stability Coefficient (BSC), Median Bias Gap (MBG), and Minimal Bias Score (MBS), to quantify bias dependence and potential leakage. Experiments on CIFAR-10, CIFAR-100, and Tiny-ImageNet demonstrate that the proposed methods maintain competitive unlearning performance while producing more stable bias distributions. We have released our code at {https://github.com/zwd2024/Beyond-the-Shadow-of-Bias-From-Classification-Head-Bias-to-Parameter-Redistribution}.

AI.tldr: The paper reveals that class-level machine unlearning often relies on suppressing bias terms in the classification head as a shortcut, proposes bias-aware mechanisms to address this issue, and introduces new bias-oriented metrics for better evaluation.
AI.motivation: Current class-level unlearning methods are evaluated with limited metrics (accuracy and time) that don't reveal internal forgetting mechanisms. The authors discovered a bias-dominated shortcut where forgetting is achieved by simply reducing bias terms for forgotten classes, which leaves abnormal bias patterns that could reveal forgotten information.
AI.method: 1) Analyzed gradient dynamics of classification-head biases under softmax cross-entropy training. 2) Introduced BiasShift as a diagnostic baseline showing bias manipulation can satisfy conventional metrics. 3) Proposed two bias-aware mechanisms: Two-Stage Bias Gradient Reversal Mechanism (TS-BGRM) and Lower-Bound Hinge Regularization (LB-HR) to mitigate excessive bias suppression. 4) Introduced three new bias-oriented metrics: Bias Stability Coefficient (BSC), Median Bias Gap (MBG), and Minimal Bias Score (MBS).
AI.result: Experiments on CIFAR-10, CIFAR-100, and Tiny-ImageNet show the proposed methods maintain competitive unlearning performance while producing more stable bias distributions compared to existing approaches.
AI.conclusion: The paper demonstrates that bias suppression is a problematic shortcut in class-level unlearning, proposes effective bias-aware solutions, and establishes better evaluation metrics that reveal internal forgetting mechanisms and potential information leakage.

title: Strategic Exploitation in LLM Agent Markets: A Simulation Framework for E-Commerce Trust
summary: Agent-based modeling (ABM) has long been used in economics to study human behavior, and large language model (LLM) agents now enable new forms of social and economic simulation. While prior work has discovered strategic deception by LLM agents in financial trading and auction markets, e-commerce remains underexplored despite its distinctive information asymmetry: sellers privately observe product quality, whereas buyers rely on advertised claims and reputation signals. We introduce TruthMarketTwin, a controlled simulation framework for studying LLM-agent behavior in e-commerce markets. The framework is one of the first to model bilateral trade under asymmetric information sharing, where agents make strategic listing, purchasing, rating, and recourse-related decisions to optimize seller profit and buyer utility. We find that LLM agents released into traditional markets autonomously exploit weaknesses in reputation-based governance, while warrant enforcement reduces deception and reshapes strategic reasoning. Our results position LLM-agent simulation as a tool for studying institution-governed autonomous markets.

AI.tldr: LLM agents in e-commerce simulations exploit reputation system weaknesses, but warrant enforcement reduces deception.
AI.motivation: While ABM has been used in economics and LLM agents have shown strategic deception in financial contexts, e-commerce with its distinctive information asymmetry (sellers know quality, buyers rely on ads/reputation) remains underexplored despite being a major economic domain.
AI.method: Developed TruthMarketTwin, a controlled simulation framework for studying LLM-agent behavior in e-commerce markets that models bilateral trade under asymmetric information sharing, where agents make strategic listing, purchasing, rating, and recourse decisions to optimize seller profit and buyer utility.
AI.result: LLM agents released into traditional markets autonomously exploit weaknesses in reputation-based governance systems. However, warrant enforcement (a form of recourse mechanism) reduces deception and reshapes strategic reasoning patterns among agents.
AI.conclusion: LLM-agent simulation can serve as a valuable tool for studying institution-governed autonomous markets, providing insights into how different governance structures affect agent behavior in economic systems with information asymmetry.

title: Generative Actor-Critic with Soft Bridge Policies
summary: Expressive generative policies such as diffusion and flow models are appealing for MaxEnt online reinforcement learning because of their ability to model multimodal and highly non-Gaussian action distributions. However, training effective soft generative policies faces two obstacles that often arise together. First, marginal action densities are often unavailable, so existing methods typically rely on entropy bounds, heuristic proxies or approximations. Second, iterative shared-parameter samplers raise inference cost and require backpropagation through time over repeated network evaluations, increasing memory cost and destabilizing policy optimization. These obstacles motivate us to seek a generative policy that exposes a tractable MaxEnt objective while requiring only a single sampled actor forward pass for action generation. To this end, we propose soft generative actor-critic (SoftGAC), whose actor defines a stochastic bridge from a fixed base latent to a terminal action latent in pre-tanh space. This structured bridge allows us to lift the MaxEnt objective as an analytically tractable path-wise relative-entropy objective against a high-entropy reference process. In practical finite-step implementation, this relative entropy reduces exactly to sampled transition control energy and thus provides principled soft regularization. Moreover, we keep the single-pass actor lightweight by using small step-specific bridge transitions, each evaluated only once per sampled action, while maintaining a parameter budget comparable to strong actor baselines. Extensive experiments on challenging continuous-control benchmarks show that SoftGAC attains higher or competitive returns than strong generative policy baselines, including diffusion and flow-matching policies, while staying in the low-latency regime of one-pass actors and showing considerable improvements in the compute-return tradeoff.

AI.tldr: SoftGAC is a soft generative actor-critic method that uses a stochastic bridge from base to action latent space with tractable MaxEnt objective and single-pass action generation, outperforming diffusion/flow models in efficiency.
AI.motivation: Existing generative policies for MaxEnt RL face two key problems: 1) marginal action densities are often unavailable, requiring approximations, and 2) iterative samplers increase computational cost and destabilize optimization. Need a generative policy with tractable MaxEnt objective and single-pass action generation.
AI.method: SoftGAC defines a stochastic bridge from a fixed base latent to terminal action latent in pre-tanh space, enabling analytically tractable path-wise relative-entropy objective. Uses small step-specific bridge transitions evaluated once per action while maintaining comparable parameter budget to baselines.
AI.result: Extensive experiments on continuous-control benchmarks show SoftGAC achieves higher or competitive returns than diffusion and flow-matching policies while maintaining low-latency single-pass generation and better compute-return tradeoff.
AI.conclusion: SoftGAC provides an effective solution to challenges in soft generative policies by offering tractable MaxEnt objective, single-pass action generation, and superior performance-efficiency tradeoffs compared to existing generative policy approaches.

title: MAGE: Multi-Agent Self-Evolution with Co-Evolutionary Knowledge Graphs
summary: Self-evolving language-model agents must decide what to learn next and how to preserve what they have learned across iterations. Existing systems typically carry this cross-iteration knowledge as natural-language feedback, flat episodic memory, or implicit reinforcement signals, none of which cleanly supports a frozen weak backbone at inference time. This paper introduces MAGE (Multi-Agent Graph-guided Evolution), a framework that externalizes self-knowledge into a four-subgraph co-evolutionary knowledge graph. Its experience subgraph stores both teacher-written failure corrections and the learner's own past correct reasoning traces, which are retrieved as task-conditioned guidance for a frozen execution model. During evolution, the graph, a task-level search bandit, and a skill-level routing bandit are updated from the same reward stream, while the learner's backbone remains unchanged. We further provide structural analysis showing how append-only memory growth, bounded curriculum coverage, and task-filtered retrieval together support stable improvement of the retrieval substrate for frozen-learner evolution. Across nine benchmarks spanning mathematical reasoning, multi-hop and open-domain question answering, spatio-temporal analysis, financial numerical reasoning, medical multiple-choice, an open-world survival game, and web navigation, MAGE achieves strong performance against prompt-based frozen-backbone baselines. Ablations show that self-harvested success traces and teacher-written corrections are complementary, with success memories contributing most on reasoning-template-heavy tasks and corrective memories supporting harder composition and interaction settings.

AI.tldr: MAGE is a framework that externalizes self-knowledge into a co-evolutionary knowledge graph to enable frozen language models to improve across iterations without modifying the backbone model.
AI.motivation: Current self-evolving language model agents struggle to preserve knowledge across iterations when using frozen backbone models at inference time. Existing approaches (natural-language feedback, flat episodic memory, implicit reinforcement signals) don't cleanly support frozen weak backbones.
AI.method: MAGE externalizes self-knowledge into a four-subgraph co-evolutionary knowledge graph. It stores teacher-written failure corrections and learner's past correct reasoning traces, which are retrieved as task-conditioned guidance. The framework uses task-level search bandit and skill-level routing bandit updated from reward streams while keeping the learner's backbone frozen.
AI.result: MAGE achieves strong performance across nine diverse benchmarks including mathematical reasoning, multi-hop QA, open-domain QA, spatio-temporal analysis, financial reasoning, medical MCQs, open-world survival games, and web navigation. Ablations show self-harvested success traces and teacher-written corrections are complementary.
AI.conclusion: MAGE demonstrates that externalizing self-knowledge into a co-evolutionary graph structure enables stable improvement of frozen language models, with structural mechanisms (append-only memory growth, bounded curriculum coverage, task-filtered retrieval) supporting effective knowledge retention and reuse.

title: AdaPreLoRA: Adafactor Preconditioned Low-Rank Adaptation
summary: Low-Rank Adaptation (LoRA) reparameterizes a weight update as a product of two low-rank factors, but the Jacobian $J_{G}$ of the generator mapping the factors to the weight matrix is rank-deficient, so the factor-space preconditioner $J_{G}^* {F}_t J_{G}$ induced by any ${W}$-space preconditioner ${F}_t$ is singular, and consequently the standard chain rule cannot be uniquely inverted to map a preconditioned ${W}$-space direction back to a factor-space update. We cast existing LoRA optimizers in a unified framework parameterized by two choices: (i) which invertible surrogate for $J_{G}^* {F}_t J_{G}$ to use, and (ii) which ${F}_t$ on ${W}$ to use. Existing methods occupy four families along these axes: factor-space adaptive updates, block-diagonal surrogates for $J_{G}^* J_{G}$, Frobenius-residual pseudoinverse methods, and Riemannian manifold constraint. Within this design space, a gradient-statistics-aware ${F}_t$ paired with a closed-form factor-space solve at ${O}((m+n)r)$ memory remains underexplored. We propose \textbf{AdaPreLoRA}, which fills this gap by adopting the Adafactor diagonal Kronecker preconditioner ${H}_t$ on ${W}$ and selecting from the resulting factor-space solution family the element minimizing an ${H}_t$-weighted imbalance between the two factor contributions; by construction, the resulting factor update is the closest LoRA approximation to the preconditioned ${W}$-space direction under the ${H}_t$-weighted norm. Across GPT-2 (E2E), Mistral-7B and Qwen2-7B (GLUE, ARC, GSM8K), and diffusion-model personalization, AdaPreLoRA is competitive with or improves over a representative set of LoRA optimizers while keeping peak GPU memory at the LoRA optimizer level.

AI.tldr: AdaPreLoRA is a new LoRA optimization method that addresses the rank-deficiency problem in LoRA's Jacobian by using an Adafactor preconditioner on weight-space and solving for factor updates that minimize preconditioner-weighted imbalance between factor contributions.
AI.motivation: The motivation is that standard LoRA optimization suffers from a fundamental problem: the Jacobian mapping low-rank factors to weight matrices is rank-deficient, making factor-space preconditioners singular. This prevents unique inversion of the chain rule to map preconditioned weight-space directions back to factor-space updates. Existing methods have limitations in combining gradient-statistics-aware preconditioning with computationally efficient factor-space solutions.
AI.method: The paper proposes AdaPreLoRA, which: 1) Uses the Adafactor diagonal Kronecker preconditioner on weight-space (W), 2) Selects from the resulting factor-space solution family the element that minimizes an H_t-weighted imbalance between the two factor contributions, ensuring the factor update is the closest LoRA approximation to the preconditioned W-space direction under the H_t-weighted norm. The method achieves O((m+n)r) memory complexity.
AI.result: AdaPreLoRA performs competitively or improves over representative LoRA optimizers across various benchmarks including GPT-2 (E2E), Mistral-7B and Qwen2-7B (GLUE, ARC, GSM8K), and diffusion-model personalization, while maintaining peak GPU memory at the LoRA optimizer level.
AI.conclusion: AdaPreLoRA successfully addresses the rank-deficiency problem in LoRA optimization by combining gradient-statistics-aware preconditioning with computationally efficient factor-space solving, filling an underexplored gap in the design space of LoRA optimizers.

title: Active Testing of Large Language Models via Approximate Neyman Allocation
summary: Large language models (LLMs) require reliable evaluation from pre-training to test-time scaling, making evaluation a recurring rather than one-off cost. As model scales grow and target tasks increasingly demand expert annotators, both the compute and labeling costs needed for each evaluation rise rapidly. Active testing aims to alleviate this bottleneck by approximating the evaluation result from a small but informative subset of the evaluation pool. However, existing approaches primarily target classification and break down on generative tasks. We introduce a novel active testing algorithm tailored to generative tasks. Our method leverages semantic entropy from surrogate models to stratify the evaluation pool and then conducts approximate Neyman allocation based on signals extracted from these surrogates. Across multiple language and multimodal benchmarks and a range of surrogate-target model pairs, our method significantly improves on baselines and closely tracks Oracle-Neyman, delivering up to 28\% MSE reduction over Uniform Sampling and an average of 22.9\% budget savings.

AI.tldr: Active testing algorithm for generative tasks that uses semantic entropy and Neyman allocation to significantly reduce evaluation costs while maintaining accuracy.
AI.motivation: As LLMs grow in scale and require expert annotators for evaluation, both compute and labeling costs rise rapidly. Current active testing methods work for classification but fail on generative tasks, creating a bottleneck for reliable evaluation.
AI.method: Proposes an active testing algorithm specifically for generative tasks that leverages semantic entropy from surrogate models to stratify the evaluation pool, then uses approximate Neyman allocation based on signals extracted from these surrogates.
AI.result: The method significantly outperforms baselines across multiple language and multimodal benchmarks with various surrogate-target model pairs, achieving up to 28% MSE reduction over Uniform Sampling and average budget savings of 22.9%.
AI.conclusion: The proposed active testing algorithm effectively addresses the evaluation cost bottleneck for generative tasks by intelligently selecting informative subsets for evaluation, closely tracking optimal performance while substantially reducing resource requirements.

title: The Extrapolation Cliff in On-Policy Distillation of Near-Deterministic Structured Outputs
summary: On-policy distillation (OPD) is widely used for LLM post-training. When pushed with a reward-extrapolation coefficient lambda > 1, the student can lift past the teacher in domain, but past a threshold lambda* the same step violates the output contract on structured-output tasks. In a single-position Bernoulli reduction, we derive a closed-form base-relative clip-safety threshold lambda*(p,b,c) determined by three measurable quantities: the teacher modal probability, the warm-start mass, and the importance-sampling clip strength. Above lambda*, the extrapolated fixed point exits the clip-safe region, changing training from format-preserving to format-collapsing. We extend the rule to calibrated K-ary listwise JSON tasks where a single binding equivalence class dominates the output contract and SFT retains parse headroom. On Amazon Fashion, three pre-registered tests--a fine-grid cliff interval, a budget-extension test, and a small-clip cross-prediction--fall within their locked prediction windows, with the small-clip value matching the closed-form prediction below grid resolution. Operating just below lambda*, ListOPD brings a 1.7B Qwen3 student to in-domain parity with an 8B-SFT baseline at one-fifth the parameters. The gain is driven primarily by format adherence: NDCG@1 on parsed outputs remains flat across lambda, while parse validity sharply changes at the predicted boundary. The cliff diagnostic is rubric-independent, whereas the parity claim uses a Gemini-graded rubric and inherits that evaluator's exposure.

AI.tldr: On-policy distillation with reward extrapolation can boost student LLMs past teachers, but has a threshold λ* beyond which format preservation collapses; a closed-form safety threshold is derived and validated on JSON tasks, enabling 1.7B model to match 8B baseline.
AI.motivation: Current on-policy distillation methods for LLMs face a limitation: pushing reward extrapolation (λ > 1) can improve in-domain performance but risks violating output contracts on structured-output tasks when exceeding a critical threshold λ*, causing format collapse.
AI.method: Derives closed-form safety threshold λ*(p,b,c) based on teacher modal probability, warm-start mass, and importance-sampling clip strength. Extends to K-ary listwise JSON tasks with dominant binding equivalence classes. Validates via three pre-registered tests on Amazon Fashion dataset with fine-grid cliff intervals, budget-extension, and small-clip cross-prediction.
AI.result: The predicted safety threshold λ* is validated experimentally. Operating just below λ*, ListOPD enables a 1.7B Qwen3 student to achieve in-domain parity with an 8B-SFT baseline at one-fifth parameters. Format adherence drives gains: NDCG@1 remains flat across λ, while parse validity sharply changes at predicted boundary.
AI.conclusion: There exists a measurable safety threshold λ* for on-policy distillation with reward extrapolation. Staying below this threshold allows format-preserving performance gains, while exceeding it causes format collapse. The diagnostic is rubric-independent, enabling reliable scaling of student models while preserving structured output contracts.

title: Arcane: An Assertion Reduction Framework through Semantic Clustering and MCTS-Guided Rule Exploring
summary: Assertion-based Verification (ABV) is essential for ensuring that hardware designs conform to their intended specifications. However, existing automated assertion-generation approaches, such as LLM-based frameworks, often generate large numbers of redundant assertions, which significantly degrade simulation efficiency. To mitigate the simulation overhead caused by redundant assertions, this paper proposes Arcane, an efficient assertion reduction framework. It integrates a two-tier assertion clustering approach for accurate semantic classification of large assertion sets, and employs Monte Carlo Tree Search (MCTS) to explore optimal rule-application sequences for efficient assertion reduction. The experimental results on Assertionbench [20] show that Arcane achieves a reduction of up to 76.2% in the assertion count while fully preserving formal coverage and mutation-detection ability. Further simulation studies demonstrate a speedup of 2.6x to 6.1x speedup in simulation time. The proposed framework is released at https://anonymous.4open.science/r/Arcane1-0A6F/.

AI.tldr: Arcane is an efficient assertion reduction framework that uses assertion clustering and Monte Carlo Tree Search to eliminate redundant assertions while preserving verification quality, achieving up to 76.2% reduction and 2.6-6.1x simulation speedup.
AI.motivation: Existing automated assertion-generation approaches, especially LLM-based frameworks, produce large numbers of redundant assertions that significantly degrade simulation efficiency, creating a need for effective assertion reduction without compromising verification quality.
AI.method: Arcane integrates a two-tier assertion clustering approach for semantic classification of large assertion sets and employs Monte Carlo Tree Search (MCTS) to explore optimal rule-application sequences for efficient assertion reduction while preserving formal coverage.
AI.result: On Assertionbench, Arcane achieves up to 76.2% reduction in assertion count while fully preserving formal coverage and mutation-detection ability, with simulation studies showing 2.6x to 6.1x speedup in simulation time.
AI.conclusion: Arcane effectively addresses the simulation overhead caused by redundant assertions in ABV through intelligent clustering and search-based optimization, offering significant efficiency gains without compromising verification thoroughness.

title: SlimQwen: Exploring the Pruning and Distillation in Large MoE Model Pre-training
summary: Structured pruning and knowledge distillation (KD) are typical techniques for compressing large language models, but it remains unclear how they should be applied at pretraining scale, especially to recent mixture-of-experts (MoE) models. In this work, we systematically study MoE compression in large-scale pretraining, focusing on three key questions: whether pruning provides a better initialization than training from scratch, how expert compression choices affect the final model after continued training, and which training strategy is most effective. We have the following findings: First, across depth, width, and expert compression, pruning a pretrained MoE consistently outperforms training the target architecture from scratch under the same training budget. Second, different one-shot expert compression methods converge to similar final performance after large-scale continual pretraining. Motivated by this, we introduce a simple partial-preservation expert merging strategy that improves downstream performance across most benchmarks. Third, combining KD with the language modeling loss outperforms KD alone, particularly on knowledge-intensive tasks. We further propose multi-token prediction (MTP) distillation, which yields consistent gains. Finally, given the same training tokens, progressive pruning schedules outperform one-shot compression, suggesting that gradual architecture transitions lead to better optimization trajectories. Putting it all together, we compress Qwen3-Next-80A3B to a 23A2B model that retains competitive performance. These results offer practical guidance for efficient MoE compression at scale.

AI.tldr: Systematic study of MoE model compression showing that pruning pretrained MoEs beats training from scratch, different expert compression methods converge to similar performance after continued training, and combining KD with language modeling outperforms KD alone.
AI.motivation: While structured pruning and knowledge distillation are established compression techniques, it remains unclear how to effectively apply them at pretraining scale, particularly for modern mixture-of-experts (MoE) models, necessitating systematic investigation.
AI.method: The study systematically examines MoE compression through experiments with depth, width, and expert compression, testing pruning vs training from scratch, different expert compression methods (introducing partial-preservation expert merging), knowledge distillation strategies (including novel multi-token prediction distillation), and progressive vs one-shot pruning schedules.
AI.result: Key findings: 1) Pruning pretrained MoEs consistently outperforms training target architectures from scratch; 2) Different expert compression methods converge to similar performance after continued pretraining; 3) Combining KD with language modeling loss beats KD alone, especially on knowledge tasks; 4) Progressive pruning outperforms one-shot compression; 5) Successfully compressed Qwen3-Next-80A3B to 23A2B with competitive performance.
AI.conclusion: The study provides practical guidance for efficient MoE compression at scale, demonstrating that gradual architecture transitions through progressive pruning, combined with effective distillation strategies, can produce highly compressed models while maintaining strong performance.

title: Rethinking Constraint Awareness for Efficient State Embedding of Neural Routing Solver
summary: Heavy-Encoder-Light-Decoder (HELD) neural routing solvers have emerged as a promising paradigm due to their broad applicability across multiple vehicle routing problems (VRPs). However, they typically struggle with VRP variants with complex constraints. To address this limitation, this paper systematically revisits existing neural solvers from the perspective of the generation mechanism for state embeddings (i.e., query vector prior to compatibility calculation) during decoding. We identify that current mechanisms restrict the observation space during attention computation, introducing a key bottleneck to achieving high-quality solutions. Through detailed empirical analysis, we demonstrate the necessity of preserving a global observation space. To overcome the constraint-agnostic drawback inherent to global observation spaces, we propose a simple yet powerful Constraint-Aware Residual Modulation (CARM) module. By adaptively modulating the context embedding with constraint-relevant variables, CARM effectively enhances constraint awareness, enabling the neural solver to fully leverage the global observation space and generate an efficient state embedding. Extensive experimental results across two single-task and five multi-task neural routing solvers confirm that the CARM module consistently boosts baseline performance. Notably, solvers equipped with our CARM achieve substantial improvements in scaling to large-scale instances and in generalizing to unseen VRP variants. These findings provide valuable insights for the architectural design of neural routing solvers.

AI.tldr: HELD neural routing solvers struggle with complex VRP constraints due to limited observation space. The paper proposes a Constraint-Aware Residual Modulation (CARM) module to enable global observation space while maintaining constraint awareness, improving performance across single-task and multi-task solvers.
AI.motivation: Current Heavy-Encoder-Light-Decoder (HELD) neural routing solvers have broad applicability but struggle with VRP variants with complex constraints. The paper identifies that existing state embedding generation mechanisms restrict the observation space during attention computation, creating a bottleneck for achieving high-quality solutions.
AI.method: The paper systematically analyzes existing neural solvers from the perspective of state embedding generation. It proposes a Constraint-Aware Residual Modulation (CARM) module that adaptively modulates context embedding with constraint-relevant variables to enhance constraint awareness while preserving global observation space.
AI.result: Extensive experiments across two single-task and five multi-task neural routing solvers show that CARM consistently boosts baseline performance. Solvers equipped with CARM achieve substantial improvements in scaling to large-scale instances and generalizing to unseen VRP variants.
AI.conclusion: The CARM module effectively addresses the constraint-agnostic drawback of global observation spaces in neural routing solvers, providing valuable insights for architectural design. The approach enables better handling of complex VRP constraints while maintaining the benefits of global observation.

title: Causal Dimensionality of Transformer Representations: Measurement, Scaling, and Layer Structure
comment: 9 pages, 17 figures, 14 tables (excluding references and appendices). Companion short paper under review at the ICML 2026 Mechanistic Interpretability Workshop. Code: https://anonymous.4open.science/r/NeurIPS-Causal-Capacity-in-SAEs-7D20/
summary: Sparse autoencoders (SAEs) decompose transformer residual streams into interpretable feature dictionaries, yet the relationship between SAE width and causal influence on model output has not been systematically characterised. We introduce causal dimensionality kappa(L, M, T), defined as the effective rank of the expected Jacobian outer product at layer L, and show it can be estimated via the SAE width sweep paired with attribution patching. Across seven SAE widths from 16,384 to 1,048,576 features on Gemma-2-2B layer 12, representational capacity grows 15.6x while causal capacity grows only 4.35x: a robust separation we term the representational-causal wedge. A saturating fit yields kappa-hat approximately 1,990 with kappa-hat / d_model = 0.86 and participation-ratio lower bound kappa_PR approximately 280. Crucially, kappa is invariant to model scaling: Gemma-2-9B and Gemma-2-2B yield identical N_causal = 328 at the same SAE width despite a 3.46x parameter increase (the count is forced to 2% of SAE width by calibration; the substantive empirical claim is shape invariance of the AtP score distribution under matched seq=512 conditions). Across eight network depths kappa is constant while the absolute attribution threshold drops 20x from layer 1 to layer 23. Five controls (architecture invariance, threshold robustness, geometric privilege, synthetic ground-truth recovery, and a four-cell encoder/decoder ablation) pin down what kappa measures and what it does not. Our findings establish kappa as a measurable, model-intrinsic property of transformer layers: sub-linearly recoverable by SAE width, invariant to model scaling, and structured across network depth.

AI.tldr: SAEs reveal a representational-causal wedge: feature dictionaries grow 15.6x in representation but only 4.35x in causal influence. Causal dimensionality kappa measures this intrinsic property, remains invariant to model scaling, and varies sub-linearly with SAE width.
AI.motivation: While sparse autoencoders decompose transformer residual streams into interpretable features, the relationship between SAE width (number of features) and actual causal influence on model outputs remains uncharacterized. Researchers need to understand how representational capacity translates to causal impact.
AI.method: Introduced causal dimensionality kappa(L,M,T) as the effective rank of expected Jacobian outer product at layer L. Estimated via SAE width sweep (7 widths from 16,384 to 1,048,576 features on Gemma-2-2B) paired with attribution patching. Used five controls: architecture invariance, threshold robustness, geometric privilege, synthetic ground-truth recovery, and encoder/decoder ablation.
AI.result: Found representational capacity grows 15.6x while causal capacity grows only 4.35x - a "representational-causal wedge." kappa saturates around 1,990 (kappa/d_model = 0.86). kappa is invariant to model scaling (Gemma-2-9B vs 2B yield identical N_causal). Across eight depths, kappa constant while attribution thresholds drop 20x from layer 1 to 23.
AI.conclusion: kappa is a measurable, model-intrinsic property of transformer layers: sub-linearly recoverable by SAE width, invariant to model scaling, and structured across network depth. This reveals fundamental separation between representational and causal capacities in language models.

title: Useful for Exploration, Risky for Precision: Evaluating AI Tools in Academic Research
summary: Artificial intelligence (AI) tools are being incorporated into scientific research workflows with the potential to enhance efficiency in tasks such as document analysis, question answering (Q and A), and literature search. However, system outputs are often difficult to verify, lack transparency in their generation and remain prone to errors. Suitable benchmarks are needed to document and evaluate arising issues. Nevertheless, existing benchmarking approaches are not adequately capturing human-centered criteria such as usability, interpretability, and integration into research workflows. To address this gap, the present work proposes and applies a benchmarking framework combining human-centered and computer-centered metrics to evaluate AI-based Q&A and literature review tools for research use. The findings suggest that Q and A tools can offer valuable overviews and generally accurate summaries; however, they are not always reliable for precise information extraction. Explainable AI (xAI) accuracy was particularly low, meaning highlighted source passages frequently failed to correspond to generated answers. This shifted the burden of validation back onto the researcher. Literature review tools supported exploratory searches but showed low reproducibility, limited transparency regarding chosen sources and databases, and inconsistent source quality, making them unsuitable for systematic reviews. A comparison of these tool groups reveals a similar pattern: while AI tools can enhance efficiency in the early stages of the research workflow and shallow tasks, their outputs still require human verification. The findings underscore the importance of explainability features to enhance transparency, verification efficiency and careful integration of AI tools into researchers' workflows. Further, human-centered evaluation remains an important concern to ensure practical applicability.

AI.tldr: AI tools for scientific research enhance efficiency but lack reliability for precise tasks, requiring human verification despite their utility for overviews and exploratory searches.
AI.motivation: AI tools are increasingly used in research for tasks like document analysis, Q&A, and literature search, but they often lack transparency, verifiability, and reliability. Existing benchmarks fail to capture human-centered criteria like usability, interpretability, and workflow integration.
AI.method: Proposed and applied a benchmarking framework combining human-centered and computer-centered metrics to evaluate AI-based Q&A and literature review tools for research use.
AI.result: Q&A tools provide valuable overviews but aren't reliable for precise information extraction. xAI accuracy was low (source passages didn't match answers). Literature review tools supported exploratory searches but had low reproducibility, limited transparency, and inconsistent source quality. Both tool types require human verification.
AI.conclusion: AI tools can enhance efficiency in early research stages and shallow tasks, but their outputs need human verification. Explainability features are crucial for transparency and verification efficiency. Human-centered evaluation remains essential for practical applicability.

title: The Global Empirical NTK: Self-Referential Bias and Dimensionality of Gradient Descent Learning
summary: In training a neural network with gradient descent (GD), each iteration induces a linear operator that governs first-order updates to a model's internal state variables. We define this operator as the Global Empirical Neural Tangent Kernel (NTK). In finite-width networks, the NTK is typically intractable to form, leading prior work to focus on restrictive settings such as tracking outputs only or taking infinite-width limits. Here, we study the structure of the NTK for a range of models. Formulating the model state as the solution to a single global implicit constraint, we derive the NTK as a product of two operators: K, accounting for immediate parameter-to-state interactions, and P, describing internal state-to-state dependencies. For a broad class of weight-based models, including RNNs and transformers, we prove a universal Kronecker-core theorem showing that K admits an exact, computable form given by the Gram matrix of weight-site variables. This core structure reveals that the NTK is structurally bottlenecked, constraining its effective rank and giving rise to a self-referential bias whereby GD preferentially learns within dominant modes of joint hidden and input activity. For recurrent models, we examine the spectrum of the NTK and show when it is biased and low-rank in space or time under the proposed decomposition. We further demonstrate that model dynamics at initialization bias the NTK, restricting learning and preventing task components from being learned effectively. Finally, we show that the NTK associated with a self-attention transformer is likewise structurally constrained to be low-rank. Overall, we show that the NTK possesses tractable structure that explains GD bias toward task solutions and the emergence of low-rank representations. To enable use of the NTK as a practical metric, we build kpflow, a library relying on randomized matrix-free numerical linear algebra.

AI.tldr: The paper reveals that the Global Empirical Neural Tangent Kernel (NTK) has a tractable Kronecker-core structure in finite-width networks, showing that gradient descent is biased toward low-rank solutions that align with dominant activity patterns.
AI.motivation: Prior work on NTKs focused on restrictive settings like tracking outputs only or infinite-width limits, making them intractable for finite-width networks. There's a need to understand the actual structure of NTKs in practical neural networks to explain gradient descent behavior and learning biases.
AI.method: Formulate model state as solution to a global implicit constraint, then derive NTK as product of two operators: K (parameter-to-state) and P (state-to-state). Prove a universal Kronecker-core theorem showing K has exact computable form as Gram matrix of weight-site variables. Analyze NTK spectra for recurrent and transformer models. Develop kpflow library using randomized matrix-free linear algebra.
AI.result: NTK has bottlenecked structure constraining its effective rank, creating self-referential bias where GD preferentially learns within dominant modes of joint hidden/input activity. Model dynamics at initialization bias NTK, restricting learning of task components. Self-attention transformers are structurally constrained to be low-rank. NTK explains GD bias toward certain solutions and emergence of low-rank representations.
AI.conclusion: The NTK possesses tractable structure that explains fundamental properties of gradient descent learning in neural networks, including its bias toward task solutions and the emergence of low-rank representations, enabling practical use as an analytical metric.

title: FormalRewardBench: A Benchmark for Formal Theorem Proving Reward Models
summary:
Recent neural theorem provers use reinforcement learning with verifiable rewards (RLVR), where proof assistants provide binary correctness signals. While verifiable rewards are cheap and scalable without reward hacking issues, they suffer from sparse credit assignment: models receive no learning signal from difficult problems where partial progress goes unrewarded. This motivates learned reward models that can evaluate proof quality beyond binary verification. However, comparing reward models is challenging since it typically requires expensive RL training ablations. To address this, we introduce \textbf{FormalRewardBench}, the first benchmark for evaluating reward models in formal theorem proving with Lean 4. Our benchmark consists of 250 preference pairs where correct proofs are paired with incorrect variants generated through five expert curated error injection strategies: forced mistakes, minimal single-point variations, verbose incorrect proofs, natural language justification, and Python code injection. We evaluate frontier LLMs (e.g., Claude Opus 4.5), judge LLMs (e.g., CompassJudger-1-14B), general-purpose LLMs (e.g., Qwen2.5-72B-Instruct), and specialized theorem proving models (e.g., DeepSeek-Prover-V2-7B).
  Our results reveal that frontier LLMs achieve the highest performance (59.8\%) while specialized theorem provers perform the worst (24.4\%), suggesting that theorem proving ability does not transfer to proof evaluation. We provide further insights on various error injection mechanisms, highlighting the challenging nature of most injection mechanisms. We release \textbf{FormalRewardBench} publicly to encourage more research on developing reward models in formal mathematics.

AI.tldr: The paper introduces FormalRewardBench, the first benchmark for evaluating reward models in formal theorem proving, addressing sparse credit assignment in RL-based theorem provers by enabling comparison of reward models without expensive RL training.
AI.motivation: Recent neural theorem provers use RL with verifiable rewards, but these binary rewards suffer from sparse credit assignment - models receive no learning signal from difficult problems where partial progress goes unrewarded. Learned reward models could evaluate proof quality beyond binary verification, but comparing them requires expensive RL training ablations.
AI.method: Created FormalRewardBench with 250 preference pairs where correct proofs are paired with incorrect variants generated through five expert-curated error injection strategies: forced mistakes, minimal single-point variations, verbose incorrect proofs, natural language justification, and Python code injection. Evaluated frontier LLMs, judge LLMs, general-purpose LLMs, and specialized theorem proving models on this benchmark.
AI.result: Frontier LLMs achieve highest performance (59.8%) while specialized theorem provers perform worst (24.4%), suggesting theorem proving ability doesn't transfer to proof evaluation. Most error injection mechanisms proved challenging, with varying difficulty levels across different strategies.
AI.conclusion: The benchmark enables evaluation of reward models without costly RL training, revealing that frontier LLMs outperform specialized theorem provers at proof evaluation. The release of FormalRewardBench aims to encourage more research on developing reward models in formal mathematics.

title: The Wristband Gaussian Loss: Deterministic, Composable Latents via a Sphere-Interval Decomposition
summary: We present the Wristband Gaussian Loss, a deterministic batch loss for Gaussianizing point embeddings without sampling, KL terms, or iterative transport. Each $x \in \mathbb{R}^d$ is mapped to a direction $u = x/\|x\|$ and a CDF-transformed radius $t = F_{χ^2_d}(\|x\|^2)$ on the wristband $S^{d-1} \times [0,1]$. We prove (and machine-verify in Lean~4) that for $d \ge 2$ the pushforward wristband map equals $σ_{d-1} \otimes \mathrm{Unif}[0,1]$ iff the source is $\mathcal{N}(0, I_d)$, and that the Neumann-reflected wristband repulsion energy is uniquely minimized at the uniform target. We compute this reflected-kernel objective in two ways: a nearest three-image pairwise truncation at $O(N^2 d)$, and a spectral Neumann path joining angular and radial Mercer modes (spherical-harmonic and cosine) at $O(N d K)$, with empirically matched gradients. A 1D Wasserstein radial term and a moment penalty serve as finite-sample accelerators with the same optimum, and Monte-Carlo null calibration turns the components into a single standardized statistic. We evaluate direct point-cloud Gaussianization with a calibrated barycentric $W_2$ score: a deterministic Gaussian reference batch is built by recursive Hungarian averaging, with each method reported as a $z$-score against same-size Gaussian batches. On the axis-uniform X benchmark, Wristband is competitive in 2D and gives the best 10D score. On a harder radial--angular-copula impostor whose Gaussian radial and angular marginals are correct but dependent, Wristband gives the best 10D and 128D scores. Coupled with learnable-key Euclidean attention and exact invertible flows, the resulting Deterministic Gaussian Autoencoder delivers a Gaussian-latent interface for counterfactual sampling with independent factors and a context/residual construction for dependent factors.

AI.tldr: Wristband Gaussian Loss is a deterministic method for Gaussianizing point embeddings using a wristband transformation (direction + CDF-transformed radius) that achieves Gaussianization without sampling or KL terms, with mathematically proven optimality.
AI.motivation: To create a deterministic Gaussianization method that avoids sampling, KL divergence terms, and iterative transport processes while providing mathematically guaranteed optimality for point embeddings.
AI.method: Transforms each point x in ℝ^d to a direction u=x/||x|| and a CDF-transformed radius t=F_{χ^2_d}(||x||^2), creating a wristband representation S^{d-1}×[0,1]. Uses Neumann-reflected wristband repulsion energy minimization, computed via nearest three-image pairwise truncation or spectral Neumann path methods.
AI.result: The method achieves competitive performance on 2D axis-uniform benchmarks and best scores on 10D tasks. Outperforms alternatives on harder radial-angular copula impostor tasks in 10D and 128D. When combined with Euclidean attention and invertible flows, creates a Deterministic Gaussian Autoencoder for counterfactual sampling.
AI.conclusion: Wristband Gaussian Loss provides an effective, mathematically sound approach to deterministic Gaussianization with proven optimality properties, enabling reliable Gaussian latent representations for downstream applications like counterfactual sampling with independent and dependent factors.

title: Benchmarking Safety Risks of Knowledge-Intensive Reasoning under Malicious Knowledge Editing
summary: Large language models (LLMs) increasingly rely on knowledge editing to support knowledge-intensive reasoning, but this flexibility also introduces critical safety risks: adversaries can inject malicious or misleading knowledge that corrupts downstream reasoning and leads to harmful outcomes. Existing knowledge editing benchmarks primarily focus on editing efficacy and lack a unified framework for systematically evaluating the safety implications of edited knowledge on reasoning behavior. To address this gap, we present EditRisk-Bench, a benchmark for systematically evaluating safety risks of knowledge-intensive reasoning under malicious knowledge editing. Unlike prior benchmarks that mainly emphasize edit success, generalization, and locality, EditRisk-Bench focuses on how injected knowledge affects downstream reasoning behavior and reliability. It integrates diverse malicious scenarios, including misinformation, bias, and safety violations, together with multi-level knowledge-intensive reasoning tasks and representative editing strategies within a unified evaluation framework measuring attack effectiveness, reasoning correctness, and side effects. Extensive experiments on both open-source and closed-source LLMs show that malicious knowledge editing can reliably induce incorrect or unsafe reasoning while largely preserving general capabilities, making such risks difficult to detect. We further identify several key factors influencing these risks, including edit scale, knowledge characteristics, and reasoning complexity. EditRisk-Bench provides an extensible testbed for understanding and mitigating safety risks in knowledge editing for LLMs.

AI.tldr: EditRisk-Bench: A benchmark for evaluating safety risks in LLM knowledge editing that measures how malicious knowledge injections corrupt downstream reasoning behavior across diverse attack scenarios.
AI.motivation: Existing knowledge editing benchmarks focus primarily on editing efficacy without systematically evaluating safety implications. As LLMs increasingly use knowledge editing for reasoning, adversaries can inject malicious knowledge that corrupts downstream reasoning and leads to harmful outcomes, creating critical safety risks that need assessment.
AI.method: Developed EditRisk-Bench, a unified framework that integrates diverse malicious scenarios (misinformation, bias, safety violations) with multi-level knowledge-intensive reasoning tasks and representative editing strategies. Measures attack effectiveness, reasoning correctness, and side effects across open-source and closed-source LLMs.
AI.result: Malicious knowledge editing can reliably induce incorrect or unsafe reasoning while largely preserving general LLM capabilities, making such risks difficult to detect. Key influencing factors include edit scale, knowledge characteristics, and reasoning complexity.
AI.conclusion: EditRisk-Bench provides an extensible testbed for understanding and mitigating safety risks in knowledge editing for LLMs, addressing the critical gap in current evaluation frameworks that overlook safety implications of edited knowledge on reasoning behavior.

title: Automated Approach for Solving Infinite-state Polynomial Reachability Games
summary: Reachability games are two-player games played on a graph, where the objective of $\texttt{REACH}$ player is to reach the target set whereas the objective of $\texttt{SAFE}$ player is to stay away from the target set. Reachability games have important applications in artificial intelligence and reactive synthesis, and many of these applications give rise to infinite-state reachability games. In this paper, we study turn-based reachability games on infinite-state graphs defined over valuations of a finite set of real variables. We consider the problem of determining the existence of and computing a winning strategy for $\texttt{REACH}$ player. Our contributions are twofold. First, we propose ranking certificates for reachability games, a sound and complete proof rule for proving that $\texttt{REACH}$ player has a winning strategy from the specified initial state. Second, we consider polynomial reachability games, where transitions and objectives are described by polynomial constraints over real variables, and propose a fully automated algorithm for computing a winning strategy for $\texttt{REACH}$ player together with a formal correctness witness in the form of a ranking certificate. The algorithm is sound, semi-complete, and runs in sub-exponential time. Our experiments demonstrate the ability of our method to solve challenging examples from the literature that were out of the reach of existing methods. Specifically, for the classical Cinderella-Stepmother game, we are able to compute an optimal winning strategy for an arbitrary precision parameter for the first time.

AI.tldr: Proposes ranking certificates for reachability games as proof rules and an algorithm for polynomial reachability games with sub-exponential runtime.
AI.motivation: Reachability games have important applications in AI and reactive synthesis, but many applications involve infinite-state graphs defined over real variable valuations. Existing methods struggle with challenging examples like the Cinderella-Stepmother game.
AI.method: Two contributions: (1) Ranking certificates as sound and complete proof rules for proving existence of winning strategies for REACH player; (2) A fully automated algorithm for polynomial reachability games that computes winning strategies with formal correctness witnesses as ranking certificates.
