RareCP learns local calibration representations through a mixture of cosine-attention experts that each capture distinct error regimes, while a compact hypernetwork adapts the kernel parameters to track temporal drift. Given a new forecasting context, RareCP retrieves the top-k most relevant calibration examples, assigns similarity weights, and forms a weighted conformal quantile over their signed residuals, yielding asymmetric prediction intervals. The adaptive kernel is trained using a smooth interval score objective, with a parameter-space anchor to a lightweight teacher kernel to preserve stable local representations. On the GIFT-Eval benchmark, RareCP improves interval efficiency over recent conformal baselines and foundation model uncertainty estimates while maintaining empirical coverage. Ablations confirm that regime-specific experts, drift-adaptive kernels, sparse retrieval, and teacher anchoring each contribute to the final performance.

AI.tldr: RareCP introduces a regime-aware retrieval method for adaptive conformal time series prediction that separates smooth error drift from distinct error regimes using mixture of experts and adaptive kernels.
AI.motivation: Standard conformal prediction methods are inefficient under temporal dependence, drift, and heterogeneous error behavior. Existing methods don't explicitly separate two key sources of nonstationarity: smoothly drifting error distributions and co-existing distinct error regimes.
AI.method: RareCP learns local calibration representations through a mixture of cosine-attention experts capturing distinct error regimes, with a compact hypernetwork adapting kernel parameters to track temporal drift. For new forecasting contexts, it retrieves top-k relevant calibration examples, assigns similarity weights, and forms weighted conformal quantiles over signed residuals to produce asymmetric prediction intervals.
AI.result: On the GIFT-Eval benchmark, RareCP improves interval efficiency over recent conformal baselines and foundation model uncertainty estimates while maintaining empirical coverage. Ablations confirm contributions from regime-specific experts, drift-adaptive kernels, sparse retrieval, and teacher anchoring.
AI.conclusion: RareCP provides an effective approach for adaptive conformal time series prediction by explicitly addressing both error regime heterogeneity and temporal drift, outperforming existing methods in efficiency while maintaining coverage guarantees.

title: TMAS: Scaling Test-Time Compute via Multi-Agent Synergy
summary: Test-time scaling has become an effective paradigm for improving the reasoning ability of large language models by allocating additional computation during inference. Recent structured approaches have further advanced this paradigm by organizing inference across multiple trajectories, refinement rounds, and verification-based feedback. However, existing structured test-time scaling methods either weakly coordinate parallel reasoning trajectories or rely on noisy historical information without explicitly deciding what should be retained and reused, limiting their ability to balance exploration and exploitation. In this work, we propose TMAS, a framework for scaling test-time compute via multi-agent synergy. TMAS organizes inference as a collaborative process among specialized agents, enabling structured information flow across agents, trajectories, and refinement iterations. To support effective cross-trajectory collaboration, TMAS introduces hierarchical memories: the experience bank reuses low-level reliable intermediate conclusions and local feedback, while the guideline bank records previously explored high-level strategies to steer subsequent rollouts away from redundant reasoning patterns. Furthermore, we design a hybrid reward reinforcement learning scheme tailored to TMAS, which jointly preserves basic reasoning capability, enhances experience utilization, and encourages exploration beyond previously attempted solution strategies. Extensive experiments on challenging reasoning benchmarks demonstrate that TMAS achieves stronger iterative scaling than existing test-time scaling baselines, while hybrid reward training further improves scaling effectiveness and stability across iterations. Code and data are available at https://github.com/george-QF/TMAS-code.

AI.tldr: TMAS introduces a multi-agent synergy framework for test-time scaling that organizes inference as collaborative specialized agents using hierarchical memories (experience bank and guideline bank) and hybrid reward RL for better exploration-exploitation balance.
AI.motivation: Existing structured test-time scaling methods have limitations: they either weakly coordinate parallel reasoning trajectories or rely on noisy historical information without explicitly deciding what should be retained and reused, which limits their ability to effectively balance exploration and exploitation during inference.
AI.method: TMAS organizes inference as a collaborative process among specialized agents with structured information flow across agents, trajectories, and refinement iterations. It introduces hierarchical memories: 1) experience bank for reusing low-level reliable intermediate conclusions and local feedback, and 2) guideline bank for recording high-level strategies to steer rollouts away from redundant patterns. Additionally, it implements a hybrid reward reinforcement learning scheme that preserves basic reasoning, enhances experience utilization, and encourages exploration.
AI.result: Extensive experiments on challenging reasoning benchmarks demonstrate that TMAS achieves stronger iterative scaling than existing test-time scaling baselines. The hybrid reward training further improves scaling effectiveness and stability across iterations.
AI.conclusion: TMAS provides an effective framework for scaling test-time computation through multi-agent synergy with hierarchical memory organization, offering improved coordination between exploration and exploitation compared to previous structured test-time scaling approaches.

title: BubbleSpec: Turning Long-Tail Bubbles into Speculative Rollout Drafts for Synchronous Reinforcement Learning
summary: Reinforcement Learning (RL) has become a cornerstone for improving the performance of Large Language Models (LLMs). However, its rollout phase constitutes a significant efficiency bottleneck, mainly arising from the long-tail bubbles across data parallel ranks, particularly in long-context scenarios where faster GPUs remain idle while waiting for stragglers. Existing solutions, such as partial rollout or asynchronous RL, mitigate these bubbles by compromising the algorithm's strict synchronous nature. Instead, we propose BubbleSpec, a novel framework that accelerates RL rollouts while strictly keeping the mathematical exactness. Instead of attempting to eliminate bubbles, BubbleSpec exploits them. We exploit the idle time windows of faster ranks to pre-generate rollout results for subsequent steps, serving as drafts for speculative decoding. Unlike prior speculative methods that rely on historical epoch similarity and warm-ups, BubbleSpec is agnostic to dataset size and provides immediate acceleration from the onset of training. Extensive evaluations demonstrate that BubbleSpec reduces decoding steps by 50% and increases rollout throughput by up to 1.8x. Critically, BubbleSpec is seamlessly compatible with various RL frameworks and strategies as it sustains the strict synchronous property of RL algorithms.

AI.tldr: BubbleSpec: A framework that accelerates reinforcement learning rollouts for LLMs by exploiting idle GPU time (bubbles) to pre-generate speculative drafts, maintaining mathematical exactness while providing immediate acceleration.
AI.motivation: RL rollouts for LLMs face efficiency bottlenecks from long-tail bubbles across data parallel ranks, especially in long-context scenarios where faster GPUs idle while waiting for stragglers. Existing solutions compromise the algorithm's strict synchronous nature.
AI.method: Instead of eliminating bubbles, BubbleSpec exploits idle time windows of faster ranks to pre-generate rollout results for subsequent steps as drafts for speculative decoding. It's agnostic to dataset size and provides immediate acceleration from training onset.
AI.result: Extensive evaluations show BubbleSpec reduces decoding steps by 50% and increases rollout throughput by up to 1.8x while maintaining strict synchronous properties.
AI.conclusion: BubbleSpec accelerates RL rollouts while keeping mathematical exactness, is seamlessly compatible with various RL frameworks and strategies, and provides immediate acceleration without dataset dependencies.

title: How Mobile World Model Guides GUI Agents?
summary: Recent advances in vision-language models have enabled mobile GUI agents to perceive visual interfaces and execute user instructions, but reliable prediction of action consequences remains critical for long-horizon and high-risk interactions. Existing mobile world models provide either text-based or image-based future states, yet it remains unclear which representation is useful, whether generated rollouts can replace real environments, and how test-time guidance helps agents of different strengths. To answer the above questions, we filter and annotate mobile world-model data, then train world models across four modalities: delta text, full text, diffusion-based images, and renderable code. These models achieve SoTA performance on both MobileWorldBench and Code2WorldBench. Furthermore, by evaluating their downstream utility on AITZ, AndroidControl, and AndroidWorld, we obtain three findings. First, renderable code reconstruction achieves high in-distribution fidelity and provides effective multimodal supervision for data construction, while text-based feedback is more robust for online out-of-distribution (OOD) execution. Second, world-model-generated trajectories can provide transferable interaction experience in the training process and improve agents' end-to-end task performance, although these data do not preserve the original distribution. Last, for overconfident mobile agents with low action entropy, posterior self-reflection provides limited gains, suggesting that world models are more effective as prior perception or training supervision than as universal post-hoc verifiers.

AI.tldr: Mobile GUI agents need reliable world models for action prediction. The paper compares different world model representations (text vs. visual), evaluates their utility for training vs. execution, and finds that renderable code works best for supervision while text feedback is better for OOD execution.
AI.motivation: Existing mobile world models provide either text-based or image-based future states, but it's unclear which representation is most useful, whether generated rollouts can replace real environments for training, and how test-time guidance helps agents of different capabilities. These gaps need to be addressed for reliable long-horizon and high-risk mobile GUI interactions.
AI.method: The researchers filter and annotate mobile world-model data, then train world models across four different modalities: delta text (changes), full text descriptions, diffusion-based images, and renderable code. They evaluate these models on MobileWorldBench and Code2WorldBench, and assess downstream utility on AITZ, AndroidControl, and AndroidWorld.
AI.result: 1) Renderable code achieves high in-distribution fidelity and provides effective multimodal supervision for data construction, while text-based feedback is more robust for online out-of-distribution execution. 2) World-model-generated trajectories can provide transferable interaction experience during training and improve agents' end-to-end task performance, despite not preserving the original distribution. 3) For overconfident mobile agents with low action entropy, posterior self-reflection provides limited gains, suggesting world models work better as prior perception or training supervision than as universal post-hoc verifiers.
AI.conclusion: The study provides guidance on when and how to use different world model representations for mobile GUI agents. Renderable code is best for data construction supervision, text feedback excels for OOD execution robustness, and world models are more effective as training aids than as universal verification tools, especially for overconfident agents.

title: Higher-Order Equilibrium Tracking for EM-Compressible Online Estimation
summary: We study online estimation in latent-variable models by recasting the problem as tracking a moving empirical equilibrium. Standard online EM and stochastic approximation analyses primarily study convergence toward the population parameter and typically do not isolate the empirical batch optimum from the online tracking error at finite horizon. Our framework decomposes the online estimate into the frozen batch equilibrium at the current running statistic and a tracking lag that captures the algorithm's delay behind this moving target. We prove a batch-to-online transfer theorem: provided $\lVert e_T \rVert_{L^{2}} = o(T^{-1/2})$, the online estimator inherits the batch central limit theorem and the sharp first-order risk constant. Our key observation is that the empirical optimum evolves on a smooth equilibrium manifold indexed by the running statistic. An $m$-th order equilibrium-jet predictor combined with an order-$ν$ frozen corrector yields localized tracking rates $O(T^{-ν(m+1)})$. We formalize EM-compressibility and EM-jet$^R$-compressibility as the structural conditions that make the equilibrium response and the Newton corrector evaluable from a retained streaming statistic. The theory is instantiated in latent linear Gaussian covariance estimation, where the first-order scheme operates on a compressed $d \times d$ statistic with explicit finite-sample risk envelopes and a certified restart rule.

AI.tldr: A framework for online estimation in latent-variable models that decomposes online tracking error into batch equilibrium and tracking lag, proving batch-to-online transfer with conditions for inheriting batch statistical properties.
AI.motivation: Standard online EM and stochastic approximation analyses focus on convergence to population parameters but don't isolate empirical batch optimum from online tracking error at finite horizon. Existing approaches don't provide explicit tracking error analysis for online estimation in latent-variable models.
AI.method: Decompose online estimate into frozen batch equilibrium at current running statistic and tracking lag. Proves batch-to-online transfer theorem with L² error condition. Uses equilibrium-jet predictors with frozen correctors, formalizes EM-compressibility and EM-jet^R-compressibility structural conditions for streaming implementation.
AI.result: Shows online estimator inherits batch central limit theorem and sharp first-order risk constant when tracking error is o(T^{-1/2}). Provides localized tracking rates O(T^{-ν(m+1)}) for equilibrium-jet predictor with frozen corrector. Instantiated in latent linear Gaussian covariance estimation with explicit finite-sample risk envelopes and restart rule.
AI.conclusion: Proposes a framework that separates empirical equilibrium tracking from population convergence in online estimation, providing finite-horizon statistical guarantees and conditions under which online estimators can match batch performance while operating on compressed streaming statistics.

title: Agent-ValueBench: A Comprehensive Benchmark for Evaluating Agent Values
summary: Autonomous agents have rapidly matured as task executors and seen widespread deployment via harnesses such as OpenClaw. Safety concerns have rightly drawn growing research attention, and beneath them lie the values silently steering agent behavior. Existing value benchmarks, however, remain confined to LLMs, leaving agent values largely uncharted. From intuitive, empirical, and theoretical vantage points, we show that an agent's values diverge from those of its underlying LLM, and the agentic modality further introduces dataset-, evaluation-, and system-level challenges absent from text-only protocols. We close this gap with Agent-ValueBench, the first benchmark dedicated to agent values. It features 394 executable environments across 16 domains, offering 4,335 value-conflict tasks that cover 28 value systems and 332 dimensions. Every instance is co-synthesized through our purpose-built end-to-end pipeline and curated per-instance by professional psychologists. Each task ships with two pole-aligned golden trajectories whose checkpoints anchor a trajectory-level rubric-based judge. Benchmarking 14 frontier proprietary and open-weights models across 4 mainstream harnesses, we uncover three concerted findings. Agent values first manifest as a Value Tide of cross-model homogeneity beneath interpretable counter-currents. This tide bends non-additively under harness pull, and yet more decisively under deliberate steering via embedded skills. Together these results signal that the agent-alignment lever is shifting from classical model alignment and prompt steering toward harness alignment and skill steering.

AI.tldr: Agent-ValueBench is the first benchmark for evaluating autonomous agent values across 4,335 conflict tasks in executable environments, revealing that agent values differ from LLM values and are influenced by harnesses and embedded skills.
AI.motivation: As autonomous agents become widely deployed via harnesses like OpenClaw, safety concerns have grown, but existing value benchmarks only test LLMs, leaving agent values largely unmeasured. There's a need to understand how agents' values differ from their underlying LLMs and address unique agent-specific challenges.
AI.method: Created Agent-ValueBench with 394 executable environments across 16 domains, featuring 4,335 value-conflict tasks covering 28 value systems and 332 dimensions. Used purpose-built end-to-end synthesis pipeline with professional psychologists performing per-instance curation. Each task includes two pole-aligned golden trajectories with checkpoints for trajectory-level rubric-based evaluation.
AI.result: Benchmarked 14 frontier models across 4 mainstream harnesses, revealing: 1) Agent values show cross-model homogeneity with interpretable counter-currents (Value Tide), 2) Harnesses non-additively influence agent values, 3) Embedded skills have decisive steering effects. This shows alignment shifting from model/prompt steering to harness/skill steering.
AI.conclusion: Agent values diverge from LLM values, requiring dedicated evaluation frameworks. The alignment paradigm is shifting from classical model alignment toward harness alignment and skill steering as key leverage points for shaping agent behavior according to human values.

title: TopoGeoScore: A Self-Supervised Source-Only Geometric Framework for OOD Checkpoint Selection
summary: Out-of-distribution (OOD) robustness is difficult to diagnose when target-domain labels are unavailable. We consider a more restrictive source-only variant of unsupervised accuracy estimation: selecting robust checkpoints using only source-domain representations, with no target samples or target labels. We propose \textbf{TopoGeoScore}, a source-only geometric scorer for label-free OOD checkpoint selection. Given a trained checkpoint, we construct class-conditional mutual $k$-nearest-neighbour graphs from source embeddings and extract three interpretable signals: a torsion-inspired reduced Laplacian log-determinant for global class-manifold complexity, Ollivier--Ricci curvature for local neighbourhood regularity, and higher-order topological summaries for fragmented connectivity, loops, and global--local inconsistency. Instead of fixing their weights by hand, TopoGeoScore learns a non-negative linear score through a self-supervised objective that enforces invariance under approximately geometry-preserving embedding views and separation from structure-breaking views. The score remains interpretable and uses no target-domain samples or labels. Results across CIFAR-based corruption and distribution-shift benchmarks, ImageNet-C, MNLI$\to$HANS transfer, and OGBN-Arxiv suggest that source representations contain measurable global--local--topological evidence of robustness, supporting practical checkpoint selection before deployment under distribution shift.

AI.tldr: TopoGeoScore: A source-only geometric scorer for label-free OOD checkpoint selection that analyzes class-conditional manifold complexity, local regularity, and topological connectivity without requiring target-domain samples or labels.
AI.motivation: Out-of-distribution robustness is difficult to diagnose when target-domain labels are unavailable. The paper addresses the challenging problem of selecting robust checkpoints using only source-domain representations, with no access to target samples or target labels.
AI.method: Proposes TopoGeoScore which constructs class-conditional mutual k-nearest-neighbor graphs from source embeddings and extracts three interpretable geometric signals: 1) torsion-inspired reduced Laplacian log-determinant for global class-manifold complexity, 2) Ollivier-Ricci curvature for local neighborhood regularity, and 3) higher-order topological summaries for fragmented connectivity, loops, and global-local inconsistency. Learns a non-negative linear score through self-supervised objective that enforces invariance under geometry-preserving views and separation from structure-breaking views.
AI.result: Results across CIFAR-based corruption and distribution-shift benchmarks, ImageNet-C, MNLI→HANS transfer, and OGBN-Arxiv demonstrate that source representations contain measurable global-local-topological evidence of robustness, supporting practical checkpoint selection before deployment under distribution shift.
AI.conclusion: Source-only geometric analysis through TopoGeoScore provides a practical approach for OOD checkpoint selection without target labels, showing that source representations contain meaningful structural signals about model robustness that can be extracted through topological and geometric analysis.

title: EGL-SCA: Structural Credit Assignment for Co-Evolving Instructions and Tools in Graph Reasoning Agents
summary: Graph reasoning agents operating from natural-language inputs must solve a coupled problem: they must reconstruct a structured graph instance from text, decide whether existing computational assets are sufficient, interact with tools under a strict execution protocol, and satisfy an external verifier that checks structured correctness rather than textual plausibility. Existing approaches usually improve either the instruction side or the tool side in isolation, which leaves unclear what should be updated after failure. We propose EGL-SCA, a verifier-centric dual-space framework that models a graph reasoning agent using two collaborative components: an instruction-side policy space for reasoning strategies, and a tool-side program space for executable algorithmic tools. Our central mechanism is structural credit assignment, which maps trajectory evidence to conditional updates, precisely routing failures to either prompt optimization or tool synthesis and repair. To provide sufficient learning signals for dual-space adaptation, we introduce a training distribution stratified by task family, coupled with a Pareto-style retention strategy to balance success, generality, and parsimony. Experiments on four graph reasoning benchmarks show that EGL-SCA achieves a state-of-the-art 92.0\% average success rate. By effectively co-evolving instructions and tools, our framework significantly outperforms both pure-prompting and fixed-toolbox baselines.

AI.tldr: EGL-SCA is a verifier-centric dual-space framework that improves graph reasoning agents by collaboratively evolving both instruction-side reasoning strategies and tool-side executable algorithms through structural credit assignment.
AI.motivation: Existing graph reasoning approaches improve either instruction side or tool side in isolation, leaving unclear what should be updated after failures. There's a need for a coordinated framework that can precisely route failures to either prompt optimization or tool synthesis/repair.
AI.method: EGL-SCA uses a verifier-centric dual-space framework with two collaborative components: instruction-side policy space for reasoning strategies and tool-side program space for executable algorithmic tools. It employs structural credit assignment to map trajectory evidence to conditional updates. Training uses a stratified distribution by task family with Pareto-style retention strategy to balance success, generality, and parsimony.
AI.result: Achieves state-of-the-art 92.0% average success rate on four graph reasoning benchmarks. Significantly outperforms both pure-prompting and fixed-toolbox baselines by effectively co-evolving instructions and tools.
AI.conclusion: The proposed EGL-SCA framework successfully addresses the coupled problem of graph reasoning by coordinating instruction-side and tool-side improvements through structural credit assignment, enabling precise failure attribution and adaptation.

title: CoDistill-GRPO: A Co-Distillation Recipe for Efficient Group Relative Policy Optimization
summary: Group Relative Policy Optimization (GRPO) has emerged as a powerful algorithm for improving the reasoning capabilities of language models, but often fails to improve small models due to sparse rewards on difficult tasks. Existing works mitigate this issue by leveraging a larger model, either to provide hints for rollouts or to provide dense reward signals through knowledge distillation (KD). However, this assumes the existence of such an oracle, and training one can significantly increase total training time. In this work, we propose CoDistill-GRPO, a co-distillation algorithm that simultaneously trains a large and a small model by maximizing carefully designed GRPO objectives. The two models learn from each other: the small model uses an on-policy KD reward to learn from the large model's distribution, while the large model is updated using rollouts generated by the small model with importance reweighting, reducing the computational overhead of rollout generation. We show that CoDistill-GRPO substantially improves small model performance over standard GRPO on mathematical benchmarks across both Qwen and Llama models. Specifically, with Qwen2.5-Math-1.5B, we observe an accuracy increase of over 11.6 percentage points over the base model and an additional 6.0 percentage points over GRPO on the Minerva dataset. Interestingly, the larger model (Qwen2.5-Math-7B) trained with CoDistill-GRPO nearly matches standard GRPO performance despite training on small-model rollouts. This highlights CoDistill-GRPO as a cost-effective alternative to GRPO for larger models, yielding an approximate 18% speedup, which may be of independent interest.

AI.tldr: CoDistill-GRPO: A co-distillation algorithm that trains large and small models simultaneously using mutual reinforcement learning, improving small model performance while reducing computational costs for large models.
AI.motivation: Standard GRPO often fails to improve small models due to sparse rewards on difficult tasks. Existing solutions require a pre-trained larger "oracle" model for hints or knowledge distillation, which increases training time and computational resources.
AI.method: Co-distillation algorithm where small model uses on-policy knowledge distillation reward to learn from large model's distribution, while large model is updated using rollouts generated by small model with importance reweighting. This enables mutual learning and reduces computational overhead of rollout generation.
AI.result: Substantial improvements in small model performance: Qwen2.5-Math-1.5B showed 11.6 percentage point accuracy increase over base model and additional 6.0 points over standard GRPO on Minerva dataset. Larger model (Qwen2.5-Math-7B) trained with CoDistill-GRPO nearly matches standard GRPO performance despite training on small-model rollouts, achieving ~18% speedup.
AI.conclusion: CoDistill-GRPO provides a cost-effective alternative to standard GRPO, enabling simultaneous training of models at different scales without requiring pre-trained oracle models, with significant performance gains for small models and computational savings for large models.

title: Autonomous FAIR Digital Objects: From Passive Assertions to Active Knowledge
summary: Scientific knowledge on the Web is published as passive assertions and cannot decide when to validate evidence, reconcile contradictions, or update confidence as findings accumulate. Curation depends on centralised middleware and institutional continuity, but when registries close, active stewardship stops even when data remain online. We advance the concept of Autonomous FAIR Digital Objects (aFDOs) from an abstract idea to an operational model, to offer a route from passive scientific publication toward accountable, standards-aligned automation that can outlive its publishing institutions. aFDO augments FDOs with three capabilities anchored in Semantic Web standards, namely 1) a policy layer over RDF-star aligned with PROV-O, SHACL, and ODRL for portable condition-action rules, 2) an announcement layer over ActivityStreams 2.0 that bounds per-announcement evaluation cost, and 3) an agreement layer that resolves multi-source contradictions through reputation and confidence weighted agreement under a bounded adversarial model. We provide a formal definition that distinguishes policy specifications, event handlers, and communication interfaces. We evaluate an open reference implementation on 4,305 FDOs grounded in rare-disease ontologies, namely ClinVar, HPO, and Orphanet, combined with controlled synthetic observations. The consensus mechanism resolves 56.3% of 3,914 naturally occurring ClinVar conflicts where multiple submitters disagree and an expert panel has subsequently adjudicated. Under Sybil, collusion, and poisoning attacks, the mechanism degrades gracefully within its design Byzantine-tolerance bound (f < n/5), and fails as predicted beyond that bound.

AI.tldr: Autonomous FAIR Digital Objects (aFDOs) extend FAIR principles with policy, announcement, and agreement layers to enable autonomous scientific evidence validation, contradiction reconciliation, and confidence updates independent of centralized infrastructure.
AI.motivation: Scientific knowledge on the Web exists as passive assertions without autonomous validation capabilities. Current curation depends on centralized middleware and institutional continuity, leading to stewardship failure when institutions close, even when data remains available online.
AI.method: aFDOs augment FAIR Digital Objects with three Semantic Web-based capabilities: 1) policy layer using RDF-star, PROV-O, SHACL, and ODRL for portable condition-action rules, 2) announcement layer using ActivityStreams 2.0 with bounded evaluation cost, and 3) agreement layer using reputation and confidence weighted agreement with bounded adversarial model. Formal definitions distinguish policy specifications, event handlers, and communication interfaces.
AI.result: Implementation on 4,305 FDOs grounded in rare-disease ontologies (ClinVar, HPO, Orphanet) with synthetic observations. Consensus mechanism resolves 56.3% of 3,914 naturally occurring ClinVar conflicts where multiple submitters disagree with subsequent expert adjudication. Mechanism degrades gracefully within Byzantine-tolerance bound (f < n/5) under Sybil, collusion, and poisoning attacks, and fails as predicted beyond bound.
AI.conclusion: aFDOs operationalize autonomous scientific objects capable of evidence validation, contradiction reconciliation, and confidence updates independent of institutional continuity, providing a path from passive publication to accountable, standards-aligned automation that outlives publishing institutions.

title: OTora: A Unified Red Teaming Framework for Reasoning-Level Denial-of-Service in LLM Agents
summary: Large Language Models (LLMs) are increasingly deployed as autonomous agents that execute tool-augmented, multi-step tasks, where latency is a critical factor for real-world applications. Yet an overlooked threat is Reasoning-Level Denial-of-Service (R-DoS), in which an attacker preserves task correctness but degrades availability by inflating an agent's reasoning depth or tool-use budget. We introduce OTora, the first unified, two-stage red-teaming framework for instantiating R-DoS attacks. Stage I optimizes an adversarial trigger that induces targeted tool invocations using insertion-aware scoring and dynamic target co-evolution, supporting both black-box and white-box settings. Stage II generates agent-aware reasoning payloads via an ICL-guided genetic search that amplifies overthinking while maintaining correct task outcomes. Across WebShop, Email, and OS agents built on multiple backbone models such as LLaMA-70B and GPT-OSS-120B, OTora achieves up to 10 times increases in reasoning tokens and order-of-magnitude latency slowdowns, all while preserving near-baseline task accuracy. Finally, we discuss mitigation strategies for detecting and constraining abnormal reasoning and latency spikes. The code is available at https://github.com/llm2409/OTora.

AI.tldr: OTora is a framework for attacking LLM agents with Reasoning-Level Denial-of-Service (R-DoS) attacks that preserve task correctness but degrade performance by inflating reasoning steps and tool usage.
AI.motivation: LLMs are increasingly used as autonomous agents for real-world applications where latency is critical. There's an overlooked threat of Reasoning-Level Denial-of-Service (R-DoS) attacks where attackers can degrade system availability while preserving task correctness.
AI.method: OTora uses a two-stage red-teaming framework: Stage I optimizes adversarial triggers for targeted tool invocations using insertion-aware scoring and dynamic target co-evolution. Stage II generates agent-aware reasoning payloads via ICL-guided genetic search to amplify overthinking while maintaining correct outcomes.
AI.result: Across WebShop, Email, and OS agents built on models like LLaMA-70B and GPT-OSS-120B, OTora achieves up to 10x increases in reasoning tokens and order-of-magnitude latency slowdowns, while preserving near-baseline task accuracy.
AI.conclusion: R-DoS attacks represent a serious threat to LLM agent deployments, and the paper introduces OTora as an effective framework for demonstrating these vulnerabilities while also discussing mitigation strategies for detecting abnormal reasoning and latency spikes.

title: Agent-X: Full Pipeline Acceleration of On-device AI Agents
summary: LLM-based agents deliver state-of-the-art performance across tasks but incur high end-to-end latency on edge devices. We introduce Agent-X, a software-only, accuracy-preserving framework that accelerates both the prefill and decode stages of on-device agent workloads. Agent-X's two key components rewrite prompts to leverage prefix caching tailored to agent-specific input-token patterns and enable LLM-free speculative decoding for fast token generation with minimal overhead. On representative agentic workloads, Agent-X achieves a 1.61x end-to-end speedup in real systems with no accuracy loss and can be seamlessly integrated into existing on-device AI agents. To the best of our knowledge, ours is the first to systematically characterize and eliminate latency bottlenecks in on-device agents.

AI.tldr: Agent-X is a software framework that accelerates LLM-based agents on edge devices by optimizing both prefill and decode stages through prompt rewriting for prefix caching and LLM-free speculative decoding.
AI.motivation: LLM-based agents achieve state-of-the-art performance but suffer from high end-to-end latency on edge devices, creating a need for acceleration without compromising accuracy.
AI.method: Agent-X uses two key components: (1) prompt rewriting to leverage prefix caching tailored to agent-specific input-token patterns, and (2) LLM-free speculative decoding for fast token generation with minimal overhead.
AI.result: Agent-X achieves 1.61x end-to-end speedup on representative agentic workloads in real systems with no accuracy loss, and can be seamlessly integrated into existing on-device AI agents.
AI.conclusion: Agent-X is the first framework to systematically characterize and eliminate latency bottlenecks in on-device agents, providing significant acceleration while preserving accuracy through software-only optimizations.

title: Discrete Flow Matching: Convergence Guarantees Under Minimal Assumptions
summary: Flow Matching has recently emerged as a popular class of generative models for simulating a target distribution $μ_1$ from samples drawn from a source distribution $μ_0$. This framework relies on a fixed coupling between $μ_0$ and $μ_1$, and on a deterministic or stochastic bridge to define an interpolating process between the two distributions. The time marginals of this process can then be approximately sampled by estimating the transition rates, or more generally the generator, of its Markovian projection. This framework has recently been extended to the case of discrete source and target distributions, under the name Discrete Flow Matching (DFM). However, theoretical guarantees for such models remain scarce. In this paper, we study two DFM models on $\mathbb{Z}_m^d = \{0,\ldots,m-1\}^d$, sampled through time discretization, and derive non-asymptotic associated bounds for both of them. In contrast to previous work, we establish non-asymptotic bounds in Kullback--Leibler divergence for the early-stopped version of the target distribution. We also derive explicit convergence guarantees in total variation distance with respect to the true target distribution. Importantly, these bounds rely only on an approximation error assumption, relaxing standard score assumptions used in earlier works, while also yielding improved dependence on the vocabulary size $m$ and the dimension $d$.

AI.tldr: Paper provides non-asymptotic convergence bounds for Discrete Flow Matching (DFM) models on finite discrete spaces, improving upon previous theoretical work by relaxing score assumptions and achieving better dependence on vocabulary size and dimension.
AI.motivation: Theoretical guarantees for Discrete Flow Matching (DFM) models remain scarce despite their emergence as popular generative models for discrete distributions. While DFM extends Flow Matching to discrete source and target distributions, there's a need for rigorous theoretical foundations and non-asymptotic bounds to understand their convergence properties.
AI.method: The authors study two DFM models on finite discrete spaces ℤ_m^d = {0,...,m-1}^d, sampled through time discretization. They analyze these models without relying on standard score assumptions used in previous works, instead working under a more relaxed approximation error assumption.
AI.result: The paper establishes non-asymptotic bounds in Kullback-Leibler divergence for early-stopped versions of the target distribution, and derives explicit convergence guarantees in total variation distance with respect to the true target distribution. These bounds show improved dependence on vocabulary size m and dimension d compared to previous work.
AI.conclusion: The paper provides rigorous theoretical foundations for Discrete Flow Matching models, offering improved convergence guarantees under more general assumptions than previous work, which enhances the theoretical understanding of discrete generative models and their practical implementation.

title: Agentic Performance at the Edge: Insights from Benchmarking
comment: Accepted to AutoEdge workshop, co-located with MobiSys 2026
summary: Agentic artificial intelligence (AI) is a natural fit for Internet of Things (IoT) and edge systems, but edge deployments are often constrained to models around 8 billion parameters or smaller. An important question is: How much agentic-task quality is lost when model size is constrained by memory, power, and latency budgets? To address this question, in this paper, we provide an initial empirical study considering edge-focused model scaling, general-purpose versus coder-oriented model effects, and tool-enabled execution under a fixed protocol. We introduce a domain-conditioned evaluation methodology, an implementation-grounded analysis of model-tool interactions, practical guidance for model selection under constraints, and an analysis of failure modes that reveals distinct semantic versus execution failure patterns across model families. Our core finding is that edge-agent quality is not a simple function of parameter count. Robust deployment depends on the joint design of model choice and tool workflow. Domain-conditioned analysis reveals Pareto fronts in the accuracy-latency space that can guide strategy selection based on operational priorities.

AI.tldr: Edge AI agent performance depends on integrated model-tool workflow design, not just parameter count, with domain-specific Pareto fronts guiding constrained deployment choices.
AI.motivation: Edge IoT systems face practical constraints (memory, power, latency) limiting models to ~8B parameters, raising the question: how much agentic-task quality is sacrificed when model size is constrained? The paper aims to empirically quantify this trade-off for agentic AI in edge deployments.
AI.method: Empirical study using: 1) edge-focused model scaling, 2) comparison of general-purpose vs. coder-oriented models, 3) tool-enabled execution under fixed protocol, 4) domain-conditioned evaluation methodology, 5) implementation-grounded analysis of model-tool interactions.
AI.result: Key findings: Edge-agent quality is not simple function of parameter count; robust deployment requires joint design of model choice and tool workflow; domain-conditioned analysis reveals Pareto fronts in accuracy-latency space; distinct semantic vs. execution failure patterns across model families.
AI.conclusion: Practical guidance for model selection under constraints emphasizes that successful edge AI agent deployment depends on coordinated model-tool workflow design, not just raw model capabilities, with operational priorities determining optimal strategy from identified Pareto fronts.

title: Compact SO(3) Equivariant Atomistic Foundation Models via Structural Pruning
summary: SO(3) equivariant graph neural networks have become the dominant paradigm for atomistic foundation models, achieving high accuracy and data efficiency by building rotational symmetry directly into the architecture. Yet the computational cost of their higher-order tensor operations creates a tough trade-off between model accuracy and inference efficiency. In this paper, we propose a structural pruning method for SO(3) equivariant atomistic foundation models to bridge this accuracy-efficiency gap. The pruning is applied along the channel and order dimensions, with each irreducible representation kept or removed as a complete block, thereby retaining SO(3) equivariance. Starting from a large checkpoint, the pruned model substantially reduces the inference cost while retaining higher accuracy than an independently trained small model. The pruned MACE-MP model outperforms the official from-scratch trained small model on 7 of 9 metrics on the Matbench Discovery leaderboard. In terms of efficiency, compressed MACE-MP and MACE-OFF models contain 1.5$\times$ to 4$\times$ fewer parameters and require 2.5$\times$ to 4$\times$ less pre-training compute than training a small model from scratch. For downstream applications, fine-tuning the pruned model reduces energy and force errors by 70.1% and 34.4% compared to training task-specific models from scratch across eight representative downstream datasets. We demonstrate that the method generalizes to other SO(3) equivariant architectures (SevenNet, eSCN) and can be combined with quantization and knowledge distillation for further gains.

AI.tldr: Structural pruning method for SO(3) equivariant graph neural networks reduces computational costs while maintaining accuracy, outperforming from-scratch small models on various metrics and datasets.
AI.motivation: SO(3) equivariant graph neural networks offer high accuracy and data efficiency for atomistic foundation models but suffer from high computational costs due to higher-order tensor operations, creating a trade-off between accuracy and inference efficiency.
AI.method: Proposes a structural pruning method applied along channel and order dimensions where each irreducible representation is kept or removed as a complete block, preserving SO(3) equivariance. The approach starts from a large checkpoint and prunes to reduce inference cost while retaining accuracy.
AI.result: Pruned MACE-MP model outperforms official from-scratch trained small model on 7 of 9 metrics on Matbench Discovery leaderboard. Compressed models contain 1.5× to 4× fewer parameters and require 2.5× to 4× less pre-training compute. Fine-tuning pruned models reduces energy and force errors by 70.1% and 34.4% across eight downstream datasets compared to training from scratch.
AI.conclusion: Structural pruning effectively bridges the accuracy-efficiency gap in SO(3) equivariant atomistic foundation models, demonstrating generalization to other architectures (SevenNet, eSCN) and compatibility with quantization and knowledge distillation for further improvements.

title: GuardAD: Safeguarding Autonomous Driving MLLMs via Markovian Safety Logic
summary: Multimodal large language models (MLLMs) are increasingly integrated into autonomous driving (AD) systems; however, they remain vulnerable to diverse safety threats, particularly in accident-prone scenarios. Recent safeguard mechanisms have shown promise by incorporating logical constraints, yet most rely on static formulations that lack temporally grounded safety reasoning over evolving traffic interactions, resulting in limited robustness in dynamic driving environments. To address these limitations, we propose GuardAD, a model-agnostic safeguard that formulates AD safety as an evolving Markovian logical state. GuardAD introduces Neuro-Symbolic Logic Formalization, which represents safety predicates over heterogeneous traffic participants and continuously induces them via n-th order Markovian Logic Induction. This design enables the inference of emerging and latent hazards beyond single-step observations. Rather than simply vetoing unsafe actions, GuardAD performs Logic-Driven Action Revision, where inferred safety states actively guide action refinement without modifying the underlying MLLM. Extensive experiments on multiple benchmarks and AD-MLLMs demonstrate that GuardAD substantially reduces accident rates (-32.07%) while slightly improving task performance (+6.85%). Moreover, closed-loop simulation evaluations, together with physical-world vehicle studies, further validate the effectiveness and potential of GuardAD.

AI.tldr: GuardAD: A model-agnostic safeguard for autonomous driving that uses evolving Markovian logical states for temporally grounded safety reasoning to reduce accident rates in MLLM-based driving systems.
AI.motivation: Current multimodal large language models (MLLMs) in autonomous driving systems are vulnerable to safety threats, especially in accident-prone scenarios. Existing safeguard mechanisms use static logical constraints that lack temporal reasoning over evolving traffic interactions, resulting in limited robustness in dynamic driving environments.
AI.method: GuardAD formulates AD safety as an evolving Markovian logical state using Neuro-Symbolic Logic Formalization. It represents safety predicates over heterogeneous traffic participants and continuously induces them via n-th order Markovian Logic Induction to infer emerging and latent hazards. Rather than vetoing unsafe actions, it performs Logic-Driven Action Revision where inferred safety states guide action refinement without modifying the underlying MLLM.
AI.result: Extensive experiments on multiple benchmarks and AD-MLLMs show GuardAD substantially reduces accident rates (-32.07%) while slightly improving task performance (+6.85%). Closed-loop simulation evaluations and physical-world vehicle studies further validate the effectiveness and potential of the approach.
AI.conclusion: GuardAD provides an effective model-agnostic safeguard for autonomous driving systems that enables temporally grounded safety reasoning through evolving Markovian logical states, significantly improving safety while maintaining task performance, with validation across both simulations and real-world vehicle studies.

title: Machine Learning Research Has Outpaced Its Communication Norms and NeurIPS Should Act
summary: Machine learning research has grown exponentially while its communication norms have not. We argue NeurIPS should adopt explicit, measurable writing standards. We analyze 2.8 million arXiv papers (1991-2025), 24,772 NeurIPS papers (1987-2024), and 24.5 million PubMed papers (1990-2025), applying classical readability scores, the Hohmann writing style suite (including sensational language), acronym density and reuse, an LLM as judge readability protocol, and citations from OpenAlex and Semantic Scholar. Four patterns emerge. First, NeurIPS abstracts score harder to read on every classical readability metric: Flesch Reading Ease falls from about 24 in 1987 to 13 in 2024, and sensational language rises by about 50 percent in NeurIPS abstracts between 2015 and 2024. Second, acronym density in NeurIPS titles has grown from 0.33 per 100 words in 1987 to 3.21 in 2024, and about 89 percent of NeurIPS acronyms are used fewer than ten times, ten points above the science-wide baseline. Third, more readable NeurIPS papers tend to receive more citations, suggesting readability and impact are correlated and that less readable papers risk remaining fragmented. LLM as judge scores rate NeurIPS abstracts as roughly stable from 1987 to 2022, with early signs of improvement thereafter, a pattern that disagrees with every classical readability metric and raises a design question for enforcement: is the target reader a human or an LLM? Lastly, NeurIPS volume has grown roughly 50-fold between 1987 and 2024. Assuming the goal is to optimise for human readers, we propose seven standards NeurIPS could pilot at NeurIPS 2027: an acronym budget with a venue-approved term list, a human readability threshold, stricter citation standards, standalone visual elements, a plain language summary, a pre-registered acronym glossary, and open source audit tooling.

AI.tldr: The paper argues that NeurIPS should adopt explicit writing standards as machine learning research has grown without corresponding communication improvements, analyzing millions of papers to show declining readability, rising sensational language, and excessive acronym use in NeurIPS papers.
AI.motivation: Machine learning research has experienced exponential growth but hasn't developed corresponding communication norms and writing standards, creating challenges for scientific communication and accessibility in the field.
AI.method: Analysis of 2.8 million arXiv papers (1991-2025), 24,772 NeurIPS papers (1987-2024), and 24.5 million PubMed papers using classical readability scores, Hohmann writing style suite (including sensational language detection), acronym density analysis, LLM-based readability assessment, and citation data from OpenAlex and Semantic Scholar.
AI.result: Four key findings: 1) NeurIPS abstracts have become significantly harder to read with Flesch Reading Ease dropping from ~24 to 13 and sensational language rising 50% between 2015-2024; 2) Acronym density in titles increased from 0.33 to 3.21 per 100 words with 89% of acronyms used fewer than 10 times; 3) More readable NeurIPS papers receive more citations; 4) LLM-based readability scores show stable or improving readability while classical metrics show decline, raising questions about target audience.
AI.conclusion: Assuming the goal is to optimize for human readers, the authors propose seven standards for NeurIPS 2027: an acronym budget with approved terms, human readability thresholds, stricter citation standards, standalone visual elements, plain language summaries, pre-registered acronym glossaries, and open source audit tools to improve scientific communication.

title: LLM4Branch: Large Language Model for Discovering Efficient Branching Policies of Integer Programs
comment: ICML2026 preprint, camera ready in progress
summary: Efficient branching policies are essential for accelerating Mixed Integer Linear Programming (MILP) solvers. Their design has long relied on hand-crafted heuristics, and now machine learning has emerged as a promising paradigm to automate this process. However, existing learning-based methods are often hindered by their dependence on expensive expert demonstrations and the gap between training objectives and the solver's end-to-end performance. In this work, we propose LLM4Branch, a novel framework that leverages Large Language Models (LLMs) to automate the discovery of efficient branching policies. Specifically, the discovered policy is an executable program with a program skeleton generated by the LLM and a parameter vector, which is optimized via a zeroth-order method over a few instances with their end-to-end performance feedback. Extensive experiments on standard MILP benchmarks demonstrate that LLM4Branch establishes a new state-of-the-art among CPU-based methods and achieves performance competitive with advanced GPU-based models. Codes are available at https://github.com/hzn18/LLM4Branch.

AI.tldr: LLM4Branch: A framework using Large Language Models to automatically discover efficient branching policies for MILP solvers, achieving state-of-the-art performance on CPU-based methods and competitive results with GPU-based models.
AI.motivation: Existing branching policy design for MILP solvers relies on hand-crafted heuristics or machine learning methods that suffer from expensive expert demonstration requirements and misalignment between training objectives and solver end-to-end performance.
AI.method: Leverages LLMs to generate program skeletons for branching policies, which become executable programs with parameter vectors. These parameters are then optimized using a zeroth-order method based on end-to-end performance feedback from running on a few MILP instances.
AI.result: Extensive experiments on standard MILP benchmarks show that LLM4Branch establishes new state-of-the-art among CPU-based methods and achieves competitive performance with advanced GPU-based models.
AI.conclusion: LLM4Branch provides an effective framework for automatically discovering efficient branching policies, addressing limitations of existing methods and demonstrating strong performance across standard benchmarks.

title: Bilinear autoencoders find interpretable manifolds
summary: Sparse autoencoders have become a standard tool for uncovering interpretable latent representations in neural networks. Yet salient concepts often span manifolds that current linear methods cannot capture without post hoc analysis. This paper uses quadratic latents to close this gap: we implement these with bilinear autoencoders, which decompose activations into low-rank quadratic forms, compose linearly in weight space, and admit input-independent geometric analysis. This qualitative difference in what concepts quadratic latents can detect challenges the standard linear representation hypothesis. Our experiments and visualisations show that multi-dimensional geometries are highly prevalent and that composite latents capture them well, systematically improving reconstruction error in language models. Furthermore, we show that autoencoders with varying geometric priors recover the same input subspace despite their dictionary entries being distinct. Practically, these models serve as an unsupervised tool for manifold discovery, which we demonstrate through an interactive online visualizer for Qwen 3.5. This is a step toward nonlinear but mathematically tractable latent representations whose composition is expressive and interpretable by design.

AI.tldr: Quadratic latents in bilinear autoencoders capture multi-dimensional concepts that linear methods miss, improving reconstruction and enabling unsupervised manifold discovery in language models.
AI.motivation: Current sparse autoencoders use linear methods that cannot capture salient concepts spanning manifolds, requiring post hoc analysis. There's a gap in representing complex, multi-dimensional geometries that linear representations fail to detect.
AI.method: Proposes bilinear autoencoders with quadratic latents that decompose activations into low-rank quadratic forms. These compose linearly in weight space and allow input-independent geometric analysis, enabling discovery of multi-dimensional manifolds.
AI.result: Quadratic latents systematically improve reconstruction error in language models, capture prevalent multi-dimensional geometries, and recover the same input subspace despite varying geometric priors. An interactive online visualizer for Qwen 3.5 demonstrates practical manifold discovery.
AI.conclusion: Quadratic latents challenge the standard linear representation hypothesis by enabling nonlinear but mathematically tractable latent representations that are both expressive and interpretable by design, representing a step toward better unsupervised manifold discovery tools.

title: Can Agent Benchmarks Support Their Scores? Evidence-Supported Bounds for Interactive-Agent Evaluation
summary:
Interactive agent benchmarks map an agent run to a binary outcome through outcome checks. When these checks rely on surface level signals or fail to capture the agent's actual action path, they cannot reliably determine whether the run succeeded. For example, a benchmark task may ask whether Alice's shipping address was changed, while the outcome check only verifies that the agent clicked "Save." This does not guarantee that the intended state change occurred, since the agent may have modified the wrong record. Treating such a run as successful therefore makes the reported score misleading. Benchmark quality thus depends not only on task design, but also on the reliability of outcome detection.
  We address this problem by introducing an outcome evidence reporting layer for existing benchmarks, without modifying their tasks, agents, or evaluators. The layer performs three functions. First, before scoring, it specifies which stored artifacts are required to verify the claimed outcome for each case. Second, it applies a locked checklist to each completed run and assigns one of three evidence labels: Evidence Pass, Evidence Fail, or Unknown. Third, it reports evidence supported score bounds that quantify uncertainty arising from Unknown cases. Rather than silently counting, discarding, or hiding uncertain cases inside a single aggregate success rate, the framework keeps them explicitly visible.
  We evaluate the outcome evidence layer on five public benchmarks: ANDROIDWORLD, AGENTDOJO, APPWORLD, tau3 bench retail, and MINIWOB. The resulting reports separate several empirically distinct failure modes.

AI.tldr: The paper introduces an outcome evidence reporting layer for agent benchmarks that verifies actual task success rather than surface-level actions, providing evidence-supported score bounds to quantify uncertainty.
AI.motivation: Current agent benchmarks often use unreliable outcome checks that verify surface-level actions (like clicking "Save") rather than actual state changes, leading to misleading success scores when agents modify wrong records or fail to achieve intended outcomes.
AI.method: Introduces an outcome evidence reporting layer that operates on existing benchmarks without modifying tasks/agents. It 1) specifies required artifacts for outcome verification, 2) applies a locked checklist to label runs as Evidence Pass/Fail/Unknown, and 3) reports evidence-supported score bounds to quantify uncertainty from Unknown cases.
AI.result: Evaluated on five benchmarks (ANDROIDWORLD, AGENTDOJO, APPWORLD, tau3 bench retail, MINIWOB), successfully separates distinct failure modes by distinguishing between surface-level actions and actual outcomes.
AI.conclusion: The evidence reporting layer improves benchmark reliability by making outcome uncertainty explicit and visible rather than hiding it in aggregate scores, enabling more accurate assessment of agent capabilities.

title: Shapley Regression for Rare Disease Diagnosis Support: a case study on APDS
comment: 21 pages, 4 figures. Accepted to the AI and Health special track at IJCAI 2026; the first two named authors had equal contribution
summary: Activated PI3K8 Syndrome (APDS) is a rare genetic immune disorder caused by variants in PIK3CD or PIK3R1, with highly heterogeneous symptoms that often delay diagnosis. Early recognition is hampered by overlapping clinical presentations and limited clinician awareness, motivating systematic, data-driven approaches to detect APDS-associated phenotypic patterns in routine electronic health records. Traditional linear scoring systems cannot capture complex symptom interactions, while deep learning models, though expressive, often lack interpretability. To bridge this gap, we propose Shapley regression, a novel game-theoretic model replacing the linear predictor with a k-additive cooperative game, explicitly modeling co-occurrence of symptoms while maintaining the transparency and convexity of logistic regression. We carry out an empirical study of our lightweight method on eight public biomedical datasets, showing that a 2-additive model with $l_{2}$ regularization achieves an optimal trade-off between predictive power and noise robustness. We also apply it to a real-world cohort of 222 patients, on which Shapley regression accurately distinguished APDS cases from matched controls, confirming and validating phenotypes known to be associated with APDS, and facilitating the exploration of pairwise interactions between symptoms, validated by clinical experts.

AI.tldr: Shapley regression, a novel game-theoretic model using k-additive cooperative games, bridges the gap between predictive power and interpretability for detecting rare genetic immune disorders like APDS in electronic health records.
AI.motivation: APDS (Activated PI3Kδ Syndrome) is a rare genetic immune disorder with highly heterogeneous symptoms that delay diagnosis. Early recognition is hampered by overlapping clinical presentations and limited clinician awareness. Traditional linear scoring systems fail to capture complex symptom interactions, while deep learning models lack interpretability, motivating a need for systematic, data-driven approaches that maintain transparency.
AI.method: Shapley regression replaces the linear predictor in logistic regression with a k-additive cooperative game based on game theory. This explicitly models co-occurrence of symptoms while maintaining transparency and convexity. The method was tested on eight public biomedical datasets and applied to a real-world cohort of 222 patients.
AI.result: A 2-additive model with L2 regularization achieved optimal trade-off between predictive power and noise robustness. On the real-world cohort of 222 patients, Shapley regression accurately distinguished APDS cases from matched controls, confirmed known APDS-associated phenotypes, and facilitated exploration of pairwise symptom interactions validated by clinical experts.
AI.conclusion: Shapley regression successfully bridges the gap between complex pattern recognition and interpretability for rare disease detection in clinical settings. It provides a transparent, game-theoretic approach that models symptom co-occurrences while maintaining the benefits of convex optimization, making it suitable for real-world clinical decision support where interpretability is crucial.

title: ASIA: an Autonomous System Identification Agent
summary: Over the years, research in system identification has provided a rich set of methods for learning dynamical models, together with well-established theoretical guarantees. In practice, however, the choice of model class, training algorithm, and hyperparameter tuning is still largely left to empirical trial-and-error, requiring substantial expert time and domain experience. Motivated by recent advances in agentic artificial intelligence, we present ASIA, a framework that delegates this iterative search to a large language model acting as an autonomous coding agent. Building on existing agentic platforms, ASIA closes the loop between hypothesis, implementation, and evaluation without human intervention, requiring only a plain-English description of the identification problem. We conduct an empirical study of ASIA on two system identification benchmarks and analyse the agent's search behaviour, the architectures and training strategies it discovers, and the quality of the resulting models. We also discuss the potential of the approach and its current limitations, including implicit test leakage, reduced methodological transparency, and reproducibility concerns.

AI.tldr: ASIA is an autonomous system identification framework that uses large language models as coding agents to automatically search for optimal model architectures, training algorithms, and hyperparameters, eliminating manual trial-and-error in system identification.
AI.motivation: Current system identification requires extensive manual trial-and-error for model class selection, training algorithm choice, and hyperparameter tuning, demanding substantial expert time and domain experience. The goal is to automate this iterative search process.
AI.method: ASIA delegates the iterative search to a large language model acting as an autonomous coding agent. It builds on existing agentic platforms to create a closed loop between hypothesis generation, implementation, and evaluation without human intervention, requiring only plain-English problem descriptions.
AI.result: The authors conducted an empirical study on two system identification benchmarks, analyzing the agent's search behavior, the discovered architectures and training strategies, and the quality of resulting models. The framework successfully automates the identification process.
AI.conclusion: ASIA demonstrates the potential of agentic AI for automating system identification, though it has current limitations including implicit test leakage, reduced methodological transparency, and reproducibility concerns that need to be addressed for wider adoption.

title: Non-Monotonic Latency in Apple MPS Decoding: KV Cache Interactions and Execution Regimes
summary: Autoregressive inference is typically assumed to scale predictably with decoding length, and key-value (KV) caching is widely regarded as a universally beneficial optimization for accelerating decoding. In this work, we identify unexpected non-monotonic latency behavior in the Apple MPS backend, where latency changes abruptly across nearby decoding configurations. Using transformer models from multiple families (GPT-2, BLOOM, and OPT), we observe latency spikes of up to 21x within specific decoding-budget intervals, followed by recovery at neighboring configurations. Controlled experiments show that these anomalies are not explained by memory pressure or prefill cost, but are instead consistent with backend execution dynamics, while CPU and NVIDIA T4 (CUDA) exhibit smooth monotonic scaling under identical conditions. Our findings highlight the importance of hardware-aware evaluation for autoregressive inference and caution against relying on aggregated decoding-budget benchmarks, as performance can vary discontinuously across nearby configurations.

AI.tldr: The paper discovers unexpected non-monotonic latency spikes (up to 21x) in autoregressive inference on Apple MPS backend that occur at specific decoding configurations, while CPU and NVIDIA T4 show smooth performance scaling.
AI.motivation: Autoregressive inference is typically assumed to scale predictably with decoding length, and KV caching is widely regarded as universally beneficial for accelerating decoding. However, there's a need to examine these assumptions across different hardware backends.
AI.method: The researchers conducted controlled experiments using transformer models from multiple families (GPT-2, BLOOM, and OPT) across different hardware backends (Apple MPS, CPU, NVIDIA T4). They systematically measured latency across decoding configurations while controlling for memory pressure and prefill costs to isolate backend execution dynamics.
AI.result: Unexpected non-monotonic latency behavior was identified in Apple MPS backend, with latency spikes of up to 21x within specific decoding-budget intervals that abruptly change across nearby configurations. CPU and NVIDIA T4 exhibited smooth monotonic scaling under identical conditions.
AI.conclusion: The findings highlight the importance of hardware-aware evaluation for autoregressive inference and caution against relying solely on aggregated decoding-budget benchmarks, as performance can vary discontinuously across nearby configurations on certain hardware platforms.

title: SkillEvolver: Skill Learning as a Meta-Skill
summary: Agent skills today are static artifact: authored once -- by human curation or one-shot generation from parametric knowledge -- and then consumed unchanged, with no mechanism to improve from real use. We propose \textbf{SkillEvolver}, a lightweight, plug-and-play solution for online skill learning, in which a single meta-skill iteratively authors, deploys, and refines domain-specific skills. The learning target of SkillEvolver is the skill's prose and code, not model weights, so that the resulting artifact drops into any agent without retraining; and the meta-skill itself is just another skill, loaded through the same interface by any protocol-compliant CLI-agent. Unlike trace-distillation, the meta-skill refines only after deploying the learnt skill, such that the learning signal comes from failures another agent encounters while using it -- not from exploratory traces alone. Refinement iterations are governed by a fresh-agent overfit audit that catches possible leakage as well as deployed-skill-specific failures, including the silent-bypass mode in which a skill appears valid in content but is never invoked at runtime. On $83$ SkillsBench tasks spanning $15^{+}$ domains, SkillEvolver reaches $56.8\%$ accuracy versus $43.6\%$ for curated human skills and $29.9\%$ for the no-skill baseline; on three GPU kernel optimization tasks from KernelBench, it also raises mean speedup from $1.16$ to $1.51$ on average.

AI.tldr: SkillEvolver enables online learning of agent skills through iterative authoring, deployment, and refinement by a meta-skill that improves skills based on real-world failures rather than static curation or one-shot generation.
AI.motivation: Current agent skills are static artifacts that cannot improve from real use, either curated by humans or generated once without mechanisms for continuous refinement based on deployment experience.
AI.method: A lightweight, plug-and-play meta-skill iteratively authors, deploys, and refines domain-specific skills. The learning targets skill prose and code (not model weights), making artifacts compatible with any agent without retraining. Refinement occurs after deploying skills, using failures encountered by other agents as learning signals, with overfit auditing to detect leakage and silent-bypass failures.
AI.result: On 83 SkillsBench tasks across 15+ domains, SkillEvolver achieves 56.8% accuracy vs. 43.6% for curated human skills and 29.9% for no-skill baseline. On three GPU kernel optimization tasks, it raises mean speedup from 1.16 to 1.51 on average.
AI.conclusion: SkillEvolver demonstrates that online skill learning through iterative refinement based on deployment failures significantly outperforms static skill approaches, offering a practical plug-and-play solution for continuous agent skill improvement.

title: Transformer autoencoder with local attention for sparse and irregular time series with application on risk estimation
summary: This paper introduces a framework specifically designed for sparse and irregular time series {risk estimation}. It is based on a Transformer Autoencoder with local attention, which leverages the powerful pattern identification capabilities of transformers complemented by traditional data cleaning and normalization methods. It efficiently captures relevant patterns within irregular sequences suffering from sparse data collection, benefiting from the discriminative ability of the local attention mechanism. The proposed framework is applied to a real-world case study, on the risk estimation of non-technical losses in electrical power systems in a wide area in Greece. Non-technical losses in electrical power systems, primarily stemming from electricity theft, pose significant economic and operational challenges. Detecting these anomalies is particularly challenging due to the inherent sparse and irregular nature of real-world data collection practices. Traditional risk estimation methods struggle with effectively capturing long-range dependencies and robustly handling such data characteristics. We demonstrate that our approach effectively yields highly discriminative latent features, which results in more consistent risk estimation compared with existing state-of-the-art and widely used methods. It achieves high recall and precision, meeting the critical objectives of the problem. As such, our solution offers a robust and effective tool for risk detection in irregular time series datasets.

AI.tldr: A Transformer Autoencoder with local attention framework for sparse/irregular time series risk estimation, specifically applied to detecting non-technical losses (electricity theft) in power systems.
AI.motivation: Non-technical losses in electrical power systems (primarily electricity theft) cause significant economic and operational challenges. Traditional methods struggle with sparse, irregular time series data and capturing long-range dependencies needed for accurate risk estimation.
AI.method: Transformer Autoencoder with local attention mechanism, leveraging transformers' pattern identification capabilities combined with traditional data cleaning/normalization. The framework is specifically designed to handle sparse and irregular time series data by efficiently capturing relevant patterns through local attention.
AI.result: The approach yields highly discriminative latent features resulting in more consistent risk estimation compared to state-of-the-art methods. It achieves high recall and precision, meeting critical objectives for detecting electricity theft.
AI.conclusion: The proposed framework offers a robust and effective tool for risk detection in irregular time series datasets, particularly valuable for real-world applications like non-technical loss detection where data collection is sparse and irregular.

title: SLASH the Sink: Sharpening Structural Attention Inside LLMs
summary: Large Language Models (LLMs) show remarkable semantic understanding but often struggle with structural understanding when processing graph topologies in a serialized format. Existing solutions rely on training external graph-based adapters or fine-tuning, which incur high costs and lost generalizability. In this work, we investigate the internal mechanisms of LLMs and present a critical finding: LLMs spontaneously reconstruct the graph's topology internally, evidenced by a distinct "sawtooth" pattern in their attention maps that structurally aligns with the "token-level adjacency matrix". However, this intrinsic structural understanding is diluted by the attention sink. We theoretically formalize this dilution as a representation bottleneck, stemming from a fundamental conflict: the model's anisotropic bias, essential for language tasks, suppresses the topology-aware local aggregation required for graph reasoning. To address this, we propose a training-free solution, named StructuraL Attention SHarpening (Slash), which amplifies this internal structural understanding via a plug-and-play attention redistribution. Experiments on pure graph tasks and molecular prediction validate Slash delivers significant and consistent performance gains across diverse LLMs.

AI.tldr: LLMs spontaneously reconstruct graph topology internally, but this structural understanding gets diluted by attention sink; Slash is a training-free method that sharpens attention to boost graph reasoning performance.
AI.motivation: LLMs have strong semantic understanding but struggle with structural understanding of graphs in serialized formats. Existing solutions require costly training/fine-tuning of external adapters, which harms generalizability.
AI.method: The authors discovered LLMs spontaneously reconstruct graph topology internally (shown by "sawtooth" patterns in attention maps aligning with token adjacency matrices). They identified the dilution problem stems from attention sink and representation bottleneck from anisotropic bias conflicting with topology-aware aggregation. They propose StructuraL Attention SHarpening (Slash) - a training-free plug-and-play attention redistribution method.
AI.result: Slash delivers significant and consistent performance gains across diverse LLMs on pure graph tasks and molecular prediction, without requiring any training or fine-tuning.
AI.conclusion: LLMs possess intrinsic structural understanding capabilities for graphs, but these are suppressed by attention mechanisms optimized for language; Slash effectively unlocks this latent ability through simple attention redistribution, offering a cost-effective solution for graph reasoning with LLMs.

title: Physics-Informed Neural PDE Solvers via Spatio-Temporal MeanFlow
summary: Deep learning paradigms, such as PINNs and neural operators, have significantly advanced the solving of PDEs. However, they often struggle to capture the continuous integral nature of physical systems, relying either on pointwise residuals that ignore the integral perspective or on pre-discretized temporal grids. Drawing inspiration from MeanFlow, a continuous-time integrator recently developed to efficiently solve generative ODEs, we introduce Spatio-Temporal MeanFlow, which functions as a novel PDE solver learning the finite-interval evolution of physical states. By substituting the generative velocity field with the physical PDE operator, we transform multi-step numerical integration into an efficient prediction with a freely controllable integration length. Crucially, we extend the original MeanFlow constraint from the temporal to the spatio-temporal domain, coupling time evolution with spatial consistency. This yields a unified framework naturally accommodating both time-dependent and stationary PDEs. Comprehensive experiments on benchmarks demonstrate that our approach achieves superior accuracy and inference efficiency over representative baselines. Furthermore, the proposed integral constraint enables excellent generalization to out-of-distribution initial conditions and varying spatial resolutions.

AI.tldr: Spatio-Temporal MeanFlow: A novel PDE solver that learns finite-interval evolution of physical states by extending continuous-time integration to spatio-temporal domains, achieving superior accuracy and generalization.
