AI.motivation: Current deep learning approaches for PDEs (PINNs and neural operators) struggle to capture the continuous integral nature of physical systems. They either rely on pointwise residuals that ignore the integral perspective or require pre-discretized temporal grids, limiting their ability to represent true physical evolution.
AI.method: Extends MeanFlow (a continuous-time integrator for ODEs) to spatio-temporal domains. Replaces generative velocity field with physical PDE operator, enabling efficient prediction with freely controllable integration length. Extends the original MeanFlow constraint from temporal to spatio-temporal domain, coupling time evolution with spatial consistency to handle both time-dependent and stationary PDEs.
AI.result: Comprehensive experiments on benchmarks demonstrate superior accuracy and inference efficiency over representative baselines. The integral constraint enables excellent generalization to out-of-distribution initial conditions and varying spatial resolutions.
AI.conclusion: Spatio-Temporal MeanFlow provides a unified framework for PDE solving that naturally handles both time-dependent and stationary problems while offering better accuracy, efficiency, and generalization than existing deep learning approaches.

title: Consistency as a Testable Property: Statistical Methods to Evaluate AI Agent Reliability
summary: This paper establishes a rigorous measurement science for AI agent reliability, providing a foundational framework for quantifying consistency under semantically preserving perturbations. By leveraging $U$-statistics for output-level reliability and kernel-based metrics for trajectory-level stability, we offer a principled approach to evaluating agents across diverse operating conditions. Our proposal highlights the important distinction between the core capability and execution robustness of an agent, showing that minor task-level variations can induce complete strategy breakdowns despite the agent possessing the requisite knowledge for the task. We validate our framework through extensive experiments on three agentic benchmarks, demonstrating that trajectory-level consistency metrics provide far greater diagnostic sensitivity than traditional pass@1 rates. By providing the mathematical tools to isolate where and why agents deviate, we enable the identification and rectification of architectural concerns that hinder the deployment of agents in high-stakes, real-world environments.

AI.tldr: A rigorous measurement science for AI agent reliability using output-level statistics and trajectory-level metrics to quantify consistency under semantic perturbations.
AI.motivation: Current evaluation methods lack the ability to measure AI agent reliability and consistency under semantically preserving perturbations, which is crucial for deploying agents in high-stakes, real-world environments where minor task variations can cause complete strategy breakdowns despite agents possessing required knowledge.
AI.method: Leverages U-statistics for output-level reliability assessment and kernel-based metrics for trajectory-level stability evaluation. Provides a principled framework for quantifying agent consistency across diverse operating conditions and isolating where and why agents deviate.
AI.result: Extensive experiments on three agentic benchmarks demonstrate that trajectory-level consistency metrics provide significantly greater diagnostic sensitivity than traditional pass@1 rates. The framework effectively distinguishes between core capability and execution robustness of agents.
AI.conclusion: Establishes a foundational measurement science for AI agent reliability, enabling identification and rectification of architectural concerns that hinder deployment in high-stakes environments through rigorous quantification of consistency under semantic perturbations.

title: When and Why Grouping Attention Heads Accelerates Muon Optimization
summary: Muon orthogonalizes matrix updates, but multi-head attention naturally operates at the level of heads. This granularity mismatch raises the question of whether Muon should be applied to the full attention projection, to individual heads, or to intermediate head groups. We study this question through a one-step descent comparison between full-matrix Muon and group-wise Muon. Our analysis reveals a trade-off between the \textbf{group-wise whitening gain} from group-wise updates and the \textbf{grouping-induced norm cost}, an additional update-norm cost caused by replacing full-matrix whitening with group-wise whitening. Motivated by this trade-off, we propose \textbf{Group Muon}, which treats head group size and grouping rule as optimizer hyperparameters. On GPT-2 Small trained on FineWeb, appropriate grouping improves validation loss over both full-QKV Muon and fully head-wise MuonSplit.

AI.tldr: Group Muon applies whitening updates to head groups in multi-head attention, balancing whitening benefits against grouping costs, and outperforms both full-matrix and fully head-wise approaches.
AI.motivation: There's a granularity mismatch between Muon's matrix-level orthogonal updates and multi-head attention's head-level operation. It's unclear whether Muon should apply to the full attention projection, individual heads, or intermediate head groups.
AI.method: The paper studies this through one-step descent comparisons between full-matrix Muon and group-wise Muon. They analyze the trade-off between group-wise whitening gain and grouping-induced norm cost, then propose Group Muon which treats head group size and grouping rules as optimizer hyperparameters.
AI.result: On GPT-2 Small trained on FineWeb, appropriate grouping improves validation loss over both full-QKV Muon and fully head-wise MuonSplit approaches.
AI.conclusion: The granularity of whitening updates in multi-head attention matters, and grouping heads appropriately provides a sweet spot between full-matrix and fully head-wise approaches, achieving better optimization performance.

title: PrimeKG-CL: A Continual Graph Learning Benchmark on Evolving Biomedical Knowledge Graphs
summary: Biomedical knowledge graphs underwrite drug repurposing and clinical decision support, yet the upstream ontologies they depend on update on independent cycles that add millions of edges and deprecate hundreds of thousands more between releases. Yet existing continual graph learning has been studied almost exclusively on synthetic random splits of static, generic KGs, a regime that cannot reproduce the asynchronous, structured evolution real biomedical KGs undergo. To this end, we introduce PrimeKG-CL, a CGL benchmark built from nine authoritative biomedical databases (129K+ nodes, 8.1M+ edges, 10 node types, 30 relation types) with two genuine temporal snapshots (June 2021, July 2023; 5.83M edges added, 889K removed, 7.21M persistent), 10 entity-type-grouped tasks, multimodal node features, and a per-task persistent/added/removed test stratification. On three tasks (biomedical relationship prediction, entity classification, KGQA), we evaluate six CL strategies across four KGE decoders, plus LKGE, an LLM-RAG agent, and CMKL. We find that decoder choice and continual learning strategy interact strongly: no single strategy performs best across all decoders, and mismatched combinations can significantly degrade performance. Moreover, only DistMult exhibits a clear separation between persistent and deprecated knowledge, indicating that standard metrics conflate retention of still-valid facts with failure to forget outdated ones; this effect is absent under RotatE. In addition, multimodal features improve entity-level tasks by up to 60%, and a recent CKGE framework (IncDE) failed to scale to our 5.67M-triple base task across five attempts up to 350GB RAM. Data, pipeline, baselines, and the stratified split are released openly. Dataset:huggingface.co/datasets/yradwan147/PrimeKGCL|Code:github.com/yradwan147/primekg-cl-neurips2026

AI.tldr: PrimeKG-CL is a continual graph learning benchmark for biomedical knowledge graphs featuring genuine temporal snapshots, structured evolution patterns, and stratified testing to evaluate how well models retain valid knowledge while forgetting outdated information.
AI.motivation: Biomedical knowledge graphs evolve asynchronously with millions of edges added/removed between releases, but existing continual graph learning benchmarks use synthetic splits of static KGs that don't capture real-world structured evolution patterns in biomedical domains.
AI.method: Created PrimeKG-CL benchmark from nine authoritative biomedical databases with two genuine temporal snapshots (June 2021 and July 2023), including 10 entity-type-grouped tasks, multimodal node features, and per-task persistent/added/removed test stratification. Evaluated six continual learning strategies across four KGE decoders plus other approaches including LKGE (LLM-RAG agent) and CMKL.
AI.result: Found strong interaction between decoder choice and continual learning strategy - no single strategy works best across all decoders. Only DistMult showed clear separation between persistent and deprecated knowledge, while RotatE didn't. Multimodal features improved entity-level tasks by up to 60%. IncDE framework failed to scale to their 5.67M-triple task despite multiple attempts with up to 350GB RAM.
AI.conclusion: Standard continual learning metrics conflate retention of valid facts with failure to forget outdated ones, highlighting the need for stratified evaluation that separates persistent/added/removed knowledge. The benchmark reveals critical interactions between KG encoders and continual learning strategies in biomedical contexts.

title: From Mechanistic to Compositional Interpretability
summary: Mechanistic interpretability aims to explain neural model behaviour by reverse-engineering learned computational structure into human-understandable components. Without a formal framework, however, mechanistic explanations cannot be objectively verified, compared, or composed. We introduce compositional interpretability, a category-theoretic framework grounded in the principles of compositionality and minimum description length. Compositional interpretations are pairs of syntactic and semantic mappings that must commute to enforce consistency between a model's decomposition and its observed behaviour. We deconstruct explanation quality into measures of faithfulness and complexity to cast interpretability as a constrained optimisation problem, and introduce compressive refinement to systematically restructure models into simpler parts without altering their function. Finally, we prove a parsimony criterion under which syntactic compression theoretically guarantees more concise, human-aligned explanations. Our framework situates prominent mechanistic methods as subclasses of refinement, and clarifies why their compressibility heuristics tend to align with human interpretability. Our work provides a measurable, optimisable foundation for automating the discovery and evaluation of mechanistic explanations.

AI.tldr: The paper introduces compositional interpretability, a category-theoretic framework that formalizes mechanistic interpretability as a constrained optimization problem balancing faithfulness and complexity, with proofs that compression leads to more human-aligned explanations.
AI.motivation: Current mechanistic interpretability lacks a formal framework, preventing objective verification, comparison, and composition of explanations. Without formalization, mechanistic explanations remain subjective and unmeasurable.
AI.method: Proposes compositional interpretability based on category theory, compositionality, and minimum description length. Uses syntactic/semantic mapping pairs that must commute. Introduces compressive refinement to restructure models into simpler parts while preserving function, and establishes measures of faithfulness and complexity.
AI.result: The framework situates existing mechanistic methods as subclasses of refinement and explains why their compressibility heuristics align with human interpretability. Proves a parsimony criterion showing syntactic compression guarantees more concise, human-aligned explanations.
AI.conclusion: Provides a measurable, optimizable foundation for automating the discovery and evaluation of mechanistic explanations, enabling objective verification, comparison, and composition of interpretability methods.

title: A Reflective Storytelling Agent for Older Adults: Integrating Argumentation Schemes and Argument Mining in LLM-Based Personalised Narratives
comment: Submitted to ACM Transactions on Intelligent Systems and Technology (TIST)
summary:
This work investigates whether knowledge-driven large language model (LLM)-based storytelling can support purposeful narrative interaction with a digital companion for older adults. To address known limitations of LLMs, including hallucinations and limited transparency, we present a reflective storytelling agent integrating knowledge graphs, user modelling, argumentation theory, and argument mining to guide and inspect narrative generation.
  The study consisted of two phases. Phase I employed participatory design involving 11 domain experts in a formative evaluation that informed iterative refinement. The resulting system generates narratives grounded in structured user models representing health-promoting activities and motivations. Phase II involved 55 older adults evaluating persona-based narratives across four prompts and two creativity levels. Participants assessed perceived purpose, usefulness, cultural relatability, and inconsistencies. The system additionally computed hallucination-risk indicators to evaluate generated narratives.
  Participants recognised personally relevant purposes in roughly two thirds of narratives, while argument-based purposes were identified in around half of these cases. Cultural recognisability strongly influenced willingness to use the functionality, whereas minor inconsistencies were often tolerated when narratives remained understandable and personally relevant. Narratives with higher hallucination-risk indicators were more often perceived as inconsistent, while higher argument-quality indicators tended to co-occur with higher clarity and meaningfulness ratings.
  Overall, the study positions argument mining as a reflective inspection mechanism for comparing formal grounding signals with human evaluations in health-oriented LLM storytelling for older adults.

AI.tldr: A knowledge-driven LLM storytelling system with reflection mechanisms was developed for older adults' digital companionship, showing that cultural relatability drives adoption while structured user models and argument mining help reduce hallucinations.
AI.motivation: To address limitations of LLMs (hallucinations, lack of transparency) in digital companionship for older adults by creating a purposeful narrative interaction system that integrates knowledge structures for reliable storytelling.
AI.method: Two-phase approach: Phase I used participatory design with 11 domain experts for formative evaluation and iterative refinement. Phase II involved 55 older adults evaluating persona-based narratives across 4 prompts and 2 creativity levels, assessing purpose, usefulness, cultural relatability, and inconsistencies with hallucination-risk indicators.
AI.result: Participants recognized personally relevant purposes in ~2/3 of narratives, with argument-based purposes in ~half. Cultural recognizability strongly influenced willingness to use; minor inconsistencies were tolerated when narratives remained understandable. Higher hallucination-risk correlated with perceived inconsistencies, while higher argument-quality correlated with clarity and meaningfulness ratings.
AI.conclusion: Argument mining serves as an effective reflective inspection mechanism for comparing formal grounding with human evaluations in health-oriented LLM storytelling for older adults, demonstrating that structured knowledge integration can address LLM limitations while maintaining user engagement.

title: A Single Deep Preference-Conditioned Policy for Learning Pareto Coverage Sets
summary: Preference-conditioned multi-objective reinforcement learning aims to learn a single policy that captures trade-offs across preferences, but under nonlinear scalarization the uniqueness and continuity of the preference-to-solution correspondence remain unclear. We study this problem in tabular multi-objective Markov decision processes (MDPs) using smooth Tchebycheff scalarization as a monotone utility. Under mild interior conditions on the preference set, we prove that each preference induces a unique Pareto-optimal return vector and that this vector depends Lipschitz-continuously on the preference, providing a principled foundation for preference sweeping toward dense Pareto-front coverage. To compute these targets, we formulate the problem over occupancy measures and derive Concave Mirror Descent Policy Iteration (CMDPI), which achieves an $O(1/k)$ objective-suboptimality rate. We further show that each update is equivalent to solving a Kullback-Leibler-regularized MDP with the previous policy as reference, yielding a policy-iteration interpretation and finite-iterate policy continuity across preferences. We instantiate the update as a deep actor-critic algorithm preserving previous-policy regularization. On eight MO-Gymnasium tasks, it achieves the best average hypervolume rank among recent baselines and strong expected-utility performance. Continuous-control experiments indicate gains beyond the discrete-action setting.

AI.tldr: The paper establishes theoretical foundations for preference-conditioned multi-objective RL with Tchebycheff scalarization, proves uniqueness and Lipschitz continuity of Pareto-optimal returns, and develops Concave Mirror Descent Policy Iteration with practical deep implementation achieving state-of-the-art performance.
AI.motivation: Preference-conditioned multi-objective reinforcement learning aims to learn a single policy that captures trade-offs across different preferences, but there are unclear theoretical foundations regarding uniqueness and continuity of preference-to-solution correspondence under nonlinear scalarization.
AI.method: Theoretical analysis in tabular multi-objective MDPs using smooth Tchebycheff scalarization; formulation over occupancy measures; derivation of Concave Mirror Descent Policy Iteration (CMDPI) with O(1/k) convergence; interpretation as solving KL-regularized MDPs; instantiation as deep actor-critic algorithm with previous-policy regularization.
AI.result: Proves each preference induces unique Pareto-optimal return vector with Lipschitz continuity; achieves O(1/k) objective-suboptimality rate; shows equivalence to KL-regularized MDP solving; deep implementation achieves best average hypervolume rank on 8 MO-Gymnasium tasks among recent baselines; continuous-control experiments show gains beyond discrete-action setting.
AI.conclusion: The paper provides principled theoretical foundation for preference sweeping toward dense Pareto-front coverage, establishes Lipschitz continuity of preference-to-solution mapping, and develops practical algorithm (CMDPI) that outperforms recent baselines while maintaining theoretical guarantees and implementation efficiency.

title: Bridging Sequence and Graph Structure for Epigenetic Age Prediction
summary: Epigenetic clocks based on DNA methylation have emerged as powerful tools for estimating biological age, with broad applications in aging research, age-related disease studies, and longevity science. Despite advances across machine learning approaches to epigenetic age prediction, spanning penalised linear regression, deep feedforward networks, residual architectures, and graph neural networks, no existing method jointly models co-methylation graph structure and site-specific DNA sequence context within a unified framework. We propose a unified sequence--graph integration framework for epigenetic age prediction that addresses this gap, integrating eight-dimensional DNA sequence statistical features through a lightweight gated modulation mechanism that adaptively scales each site's methylation signal according to its sequence-determined biological relevance prior to graph convolution. Evaluated on 3,707 blood methylation samples against a comprehensive set of baselines, our method achieves a test MAE of 3.149 years, a 12.8\% improvement over the strongest graph-based baseline. Biologically informed statistical features outperform CNN-based sequence encoding, demonstrating that handcrafted sequence features are more effective than end-to-end learned representations in this data regime. Post-hoc interpretability analysis identifies CpG density and local adenine frequency as features with age-dependent importance shifts, consistent with known mechanisms of age-related hypermethylation at CpG-dense promoter regions. Our code is at https://github.com/yaoli2022/graphage-seq.

AI.tldr: A novel unified sequence-graph integration framework for epigenetic age prediction that combines DNA sequence statistical features with co-methylation graph structure, achieving state-of-the-art performance with 12.8% improvement over existing methods.
AI.motivation: Existing epigenetic age prediction methods using machine learning have advanced but fail to jointly model both co-methylation graph structure and DNA sequence context in a unified framework. There's a gap in integrating these two complementary sources of biological information.
AI.method: Proposes a unified sequence-graph integration framework that incorporates eight-dimensional DNA sequence statistical features through a lightweight gated modulation mechanism. This mechanism adaptively scales each site's methylation signal based on its sequence-determined biological relevance before performing graph convolution on the co-methylation network.
AI.result: Achieves test MAE of 3.149 years on 3,707 blood methylation samples, representing a 12.8% improvement over the strongest graph-based baseline. Handcrafted sequence features outperform CNN-based encoding, and interpretability analysis reveals CpG density and local adenine frequency as important features with age-dependent importance shifts.
AI.conclusion: The unified sequence-graph integration approach successfully bridges the gap between sequence context and co-methylation structure modeling, demonstrating superior performance for epigenetic age prediction. Handcrafted biological features remain effective in this domain, and the framework provides interpretable insights into age-related methylation mechanisms.

title: Muon-OGD: Muon-based Spectral Orthogonal Gradient Projection for LLM Continual Learning
summary: A central challenge in continual learning for large language models (LLMs) is catastrophic forgetting, where adapting to new tasks can substantially degrade performance on previously learned ones. Existing projection-based methods mitigate such interference by restricting parameter updates to subspaces that are orthogonal to directions associated with past tasks. However, these methods are typically formulated under Euclidean parameter geometry, with update magnitudes and projections governed by the Frobenius norm. The recent empirical success of the Muon optimizer, which applies orthogonalized matrix updates and admits a spectral-norm interpretation, suggests that Frobenius geometry may not be the most effective choice for matrix-valued LLM parameters. Motivated by this observation, we propose Muon-OGD, a spectral-norm-aware continual learning framework that integrates Muon-style operator-norm geometry with orthogonal projection constraints. Our method formulates each update as a spectral-norm-constrained optimization problem with linear non-interference constraints, and solves it efficiently through dual iterations and Newton--Schulz matrix-sign approximations. By applying orthogonalized momentum updates that avoid protected directions associated with prior tasks, Muon-OGD aims to improve the stability--plasticity trade-off in sequential LLM adaptation. We evaluate the proposed method on standard continual learning benchmarks, TRACE, and domain-specific Coding--Math--Medical curricula using both encoder--decoder and decoder-only architectures. Empirically, Muon-OGD consistently improves over sequential fine-tuning and competitive orthogonal-gradient baselines, while remaining computationally scalable. These results suggest that spectral-norm-aware update geometry provides a practical and effective alternative to Frobenius-norm projection for continual learning in LLMs.

AI.tldr: Muon-OGD: A spectral-norm-aware continual learning framework for LLMs that uses orthogonalized momentum updates to prevent catastrophic forgetting while maintaining computational scalability.
AI.motivation: Existing projection-based continual learning methods for LLMs use Euclidean/Frobenius-norm geometry, but recent empirical success of the Muon optimizer (which uses orthogonalized matrix updates with spectral-norm interpretation) suggests Frobenius geometry may not be optimal for matrix-valued LLM parameters.
AI.method: Muon-OGD integrates Muon-style operator-norm geometry with orthogonal projection constraints, formulating updates as spectral-norm-constrained optimization with linear non-interference constraints, solved efficiently via dual iterations and Newton-Schulz matrix-sign approximations. Uses orthogonalized momentum updates that avoid protected directions from prior tasks.
AI.result: Evaluated on standard continual learning benchmarks (TRACE) and domain-specific curricula (Coding-Math-Medical) using both encoder-decoder and decoder-only architectures. Consistently improves over sequential fine-tuning and competitive orthogonal-gradient baselines while remaining computationally scalable.
AI.conclusion: Spectral-norm-aware update geometry provides a practical and effective alternative to Frobenius-norm projection for continual learning in LLMs, improving the stability-plasticity trade-off in sequential LLM adaptation.

title: Agent-First Tool API: A Semantic Interface Paradigm for Enterprise AI Agent Systems
summary: As AI agents transition from research prototypes to enterprise production systems, the tool interfaces they consume remain rooted in human-oriented CRUD paradigms. This paper identifies five fundamental architectural mismatches between conventional APIs and autonomous agent requirements: exact-identifier dependence, rendering-oriented responses, single-shot interaction assumptions, user-equivalent authorization, and opaque error semantics. We propose the Agent-First Tool API paradigm, comprising three integrated mechanisms: (1) a Six-Verb Semantic Protocol that decomposes tool interactions into search, resolve, preview, execute, verify, and recover phases; (2) a Normalized Tool Contract (NTC) providing structured decision-support metadata including confidence scores, evidence chains, and suggested next actions; and (3) a dual-layer governance pipeline combining static capability policies with dynamic risk escalation. The paradigm is implemented and validated in a production multi-tenant SaaS platform serving 85 registered tools across 6 business domains. Comparative experiments on 50 real operational tasks demonstrate that Agent-First APIs achieve 88% end-to-end task success rate versus 64% for optimized CRUD baselines (+37.5%), while reducing required human interventions by 72.7% and improving autonomous error recovery by 5.8x. We establish that the paradigm is orthogonal and complementary to transport-layer standards such as MCP, operating as the semantic application layer above existing tool discovery and invocation protocols.

AI.tldr: The paper proposes Agent-First Tool APIs - a new paradigm addressing architectural mismatches between human-oriented APIs and autonomous AI agent requirements, achieving 88% task success rate vs 64% for conventional APIs.
AI.motivation: Current AI agents use human-oriented CRUD APIs that create fundamental architectural mismatches: exact-identifier dependence, rendering-oriented responses, single-shot interaction assumptions, user-equivalent authorization, and opaque error semantics. These mismatches hinder agent autonomy and efficiency.
AI.method: Proposes Agent-First Tool API paradigm with three integrated mechanisms: 1) Six-Verb Semantic Protocol (search, resolve, preview, execute, verify, recover), 2) Normalized Tool Contract with structured decision-support metadata, and 3) dual-layer governance pipeline combining static policies with dynamic risk escalation.
AI.result: Implementation in production SaaS platform with 85 tools across 6 domains shows: 88% task success rate vs 64% for optimized CRUD baselines (+37.5%), 72.7% reduction in human interventions, and 5.8x improvement in autonomous error recovery. The paradigm is orthogonal to transport-layer standards like MCP.
AI.conclusion: Agent-First Tool APIs provide a semantic application layer that addresses fundamental mismatches between conventional APIs and autonomous agent requirements, significantly improving task success rates and reducing human interventions while complementing existing tool discovery protocols.

title: MolWorld: Molecule World Models for Actionable Molecular Optimization
summary: Molecular optimization in drug discovery aims to discover molecules with improved target properties, but practical lead optimization often requires more than high predicted scores. A useful candidate should also be actionable: it should be reachable from known molecules through valid local structural transformations, so that it can be interpreted as a plausible revision within an evolving chemical series. Existing de novo and single-molecule optimization methods do not explicitly model such reachability, especially when both the target molecules and the intermediate molecules connecting them to known compounds are unknown. In this work, we formulate actionable molecular optimization as sequential expansion of a molecule-transfer graph, where nodes are molecules and edges encode valid local transformations. We propose MolWorld, a molecule world model-guided framework that treats the current molecule-transfer graph as an evolving search state. At each iteration, MolWorld selects local anchor contexts, generates candidate molecules conditioned on these contexts, evaluates their properties, and uses a learned world model to update the evolving molecule world by retaining admissible candidates and inserting them into the molecule-transfer graph. The expanded molecule world then guides subsequent optimization. Experiments on property optimization and docking-based tasks show that MolWorld discovers high-property molecules while maintaining substantially stronger structural connectivity, supporting actionable and sequential molecular design.

AI.tldr: MolWorld is a framework for actionable molecular optimization that generates molecules reachable from known compounds through valid structural transformations, ensuring practical drug discovery viability.
AI.motivation: Current molecular optimization methods focus on high predicted scores but ignore "actionability" - whether molecules are reachable from known compounds through valid local transformations. This gap makes discovered molecules impractical for lead optimization where molecules need to be plausible revisions within evolving chemical series.
AI.method: Formulates molecular optimization as sequential expansion of a molecule-transfer graph. Uses a world model-guided framework that treats current molecule-transfer graph as evolving search state, iteratively: selects local anchor contexts, generates candidate molecules conditioned on contexts, evaluates properties, uses learned world model to update evolving molecule world by retaining admissible candidates and inserting them into graph.
AI.result: MolWorld discovers high-property molecules while maintaining substantially stronger structural connectivity compared to existing methods, supporting actionable and sequential molecular design as demonstrated in property optimization and docking-based tasks.
AI.conclusion: MolWorld provides an effective framework for actionable molecular optimization that balances property improvement with structural reachability, addressing a critical practical limitation in drug discovery optimization pipelines.

title: Deep Arguing
summary: Deep learning has become the dominant approach for creating high capacity, scalable models across diverse data modalities. However, because these models rely on a large number of learned parameters, tightly couple feature extraction with task objectives, and often lack explicit reasoning mechanisms, it is difficult for humans to understand how they arrive at their predictions. Understanding what representations emerge and why they arise from the training data remains an open challenge. We introduce Deep Arguing, a novel neurosymbolic approach that integrates deep learning with argumentation construction and reasoning for interpretable classification with different data modalities. In our approach deep neural networks construct an argumentation structure wherein data points support their assigned label and attack different ones. Using differentiable argumentation semantics for reasoning, the model is trained end-to-end to jointly learn feature representation and argumentative interactions. This results in argumentation structures providing faithful case-based explanations for predictions. Structure constraints over the argumentation graph guide learning, improving both interpretability and predictive performance. Experiments with tabular and imaging datasets show that Deep Arguing achieves performance competitive with standard baselines whilst offering interpretable argumentative reasoning.

AI.tldr: Deep Arguing combines deep learning with argumentation theory to create interpretable neural networks that generate argumentation structures explaining predictions through support/attack relationships between data points.
AI.motivation: Deep learning models lack interpretability - they have many parameters, couple feature extraction with tasks, and lack explicit reasoning mechanisms, making it hard to understand how they arrive at predictions. Understanding what representations emerge and why remains an open challenge.
AI.method: A neurosymbolic approach integrating deep learning with argumentation construction and reasoning. DNNs construct argumentation structures where data points support their assigned labels and attack different ones. Uses differentiable argumentation semantics for end-to-end training to jointly learn feature representations and argumentative interactions.
AI.result: Deep Arguing achieves performance competitive with standard baselines on tabular and imaging datasets while providing interpretable argumentative reasoning through faithful case-based explanations for predictions.
AI.conclusion: The approach successfully combines deep learning with argumentation theory to create interpretable models that maintain competitive performance while offering transparent reasoning through argumentation structures.

title: Outlier detection for patient monitoring and alerting
summary: We develop and evaluate a data-driven approach for detecting unusual (anomalous) patient-management decisions using past patient cases stored in electronic health records (EHRs). Our hypothesis is that a patient-management decision that is unusual with respect to past patient care may be due to an error and that it is worthwhile to generate an alert if such a decision is encountered. We evaluate this hypothesis using data obtained from EHRs of 4486 post-cardiac surgical patients and a subset of 222 alerts generated from the data. We base the evaluation on the opinions of a panel of experts. The results of the study support our hypothesis that the outlier-based alerting can lead to promising true alert rates. We observed true alert rates that ranged from 25\% to 66\% for a variety of patient-management actions, with 66\% corresponding to the strongest outliers.

AI.tldr: A data-driven method using EHR data to detect anomalous patient-management decisions as potential errors, with expert evaluation showing promising true alert rates of 25-66%.
AI.motivation: The motivation is to detect potential medical errors by identifying unusual patient-management decisions that deviate from established patterns in electronic health records, with the hypothesis that such deviations may indicate errors.
AI.method: Developed an outlier-based detection system using past patient cases from EHRs of 4,486 post-cardiac surgical patients. Generated 222 alerts from this data and evaluated them through expert panel review.
AI.result: The evaluation supported the hypothesis, showing promising true alert rates ranging from 25% to 66% across various patient-management actions, with the strongest outliers achieving 66% true alert rate.
AI.conclusion: Outlier-based alerting for patient-management decisions shows potential as an effective error-detection method in healthcare settings, warranting further investigation and implementation.

title: LLM Jaggedness Unlocks Scientific Creativity
summary: As artificial intelligence advances, models are not improving uniformly. Instead, progress unfolds in a jagged fashion, with capabilities growing unevenly across tasks, domains, and model scales. In this work, we examine this dynamic jaggedness through the lens of scientific idea generation. We introduce SciAidanBench, a benchmark of open-ended scientific questions designed to measure the scientific creativity of large language models (LLMs). Given a scientific question, models are asked to generate as many unique and coherent ideas as possible, with the total number of valid responses serving as a proxy for creative potential. Evaluating 19 base models across 8 providers (30 total variants including reasoning versions), we find that jaggedness manifests both across models and within models. First, in a cross-task comparison between general and scientific creativity, improvements in general creativity do not translate uniformly to scientific creativity, revealing divergent capability profiles across models. Second, at the prompt level, stronger models do not improve uniformly; instead, they exhibit high variability, with bursts of creativity on some questions and limited performance on others. Third, at the domain level, individual models display uneven strengths across scientific subfields, reflecting fragmented internal capability profiles. Finally, we show that this jaggedness can be harnessed. We explore mechanisms of inference-time compute, knowledge pooling, and brainstorming to combine models effectively and construct meta-model ensembles that outperform any single model. Our results position jaggedness not as a limitation, but as a resource, a structural feature of AI progress that, when understood and leveraged, can amplify LLM-driven scientific creativity.

AI.tldr: LLMs show uneven progress in scientific creativity - jaggedness exists across models, within models, and across domains. This jaggedness can be leveraged through ensemble methods to enhance scientific idea generation.
AI.motivation: AI progress is not uniform but occurs in a "jagged" fashion with uneven capability development. There's a need to understand and measure this jaggedness, particularly in scientific creativity where current benchmarks may not capture open-ended creative potential.
AI.method: Introduced SciAidanBench benchmark for evaluating scientific creativity through open-ended questions. Evaluated 19 base models across 8 providers (30 total variants). Analyzed jaggedness across multiple dimensions and explored mechanisms like inference-time compute, knowledge pooling, and brainstorming to create ensemble methods.
AI.result: Found jaggedness manifests across models (general vs. scientific creativity divergence), within models (high variability across prompts), and across domains (uneven strengths in scientific subfields). Showed that ensemble methods combining multiple models can outperform any single model by leveraging this jaggedness.
AI.conclusion: Jaggedness in AI progress is not a limitation but a structural feature that can be harnessed as a resource. By understanding and leveraging uneven capability development, we can amplify LLM-driven scientific creativity through intelligent model combination strategies.

title: Learning predictive models for combinations of heterogeneous proteomic data sources
comment: Published at in AMIA Summit on Translational Bioinformatics (STB 2008
summary: Multiple technologies that measure expression levels of protein mixtures in the human body offer a potential for detection and understanding the disease. The recent increase of these technologies prompts researchers to evaluate the individual and combined utility of data generated by the technologies. In this work, we study two data sources to measure the expression of protein mixtures in the human body: whole-sample MS profiling and multiplexed protein arrays. We investigate the individual and combined utility of these technologies by learning and testing a variety of classification models on the data from a pancreatic cancer study. We show that for the combination of these two (heterogeneous) datasets, classification models that work well on one of them individually fail on the combination of the two datasets. We study and propose a class of model fusion methods that acknowledge the differences and try to reap most of the benefits from their combination.

AI.tldr: The paper examines combining two protein expression measurement technologies (MS profiling and protein arrays) for disease classification, finding that standard models fail on combined datasets, and proposes specialized fusion methods to leverage their complementary information.
AI.motivation: Multiple protein expression measurement technologies offer potential for disease detection, but researchers need to evaluate both individual and combined utility of data from different sources to maximize diagnostic capabilities.
AI.method: Study two protein expression data sources (whole-sample MS profiling and multiplexed protein arrays) using a pancreatic cancer dataset. Test various classification models on individual and combined datasets, then propose and evaluate model fusion methods that acknowledge dataset differences.
AI.result: Classification models that perform well on individual datasets fail when applied to combined heterogeneous datasets. The proposed fusion methods successfully leverage complementary information from both technologies while accounting for their differences.
AI.conclusion: When combining heterogeneous protein expression datasets, specialized model fusion approaches that acknowledge dataset differences are necessary to effectively leverage complementary information for improved disease classification performance.

title: A Resilient Solution for Sewer Overflow Monitoring across Cloud and Edge
comment: 3 pages, 6 figures, accepted at 35th International Joint Conference on Artificial Intelligence 2026 (IJCAI-ECAI 2026), Demonstrations Track. URL: https://riwwer.demo.calgo-lab.de
summary: Aging combined sewer systems in many historical cities are increasingly stressed by extreme rainfall events, which can trigger combined sewer overflows (CSO) with significant environmental and public health impacts. Forecasting the filling dynamics of overflow basins is critical for anticipating capacity exceedance and enabling timely preventive actions for CSO. We present a web-based demonstrator (https://riwwer.demo.calgo-lab.de) that integrates Deep Learning forecasting methods in both cloud and edge settings into an interactive monitoring dashboard for overflow monitoring, resilient to network outages. A video showcase is available online (https://cloud.bht-berlin.de/index.php/s/b9xt4T3SdiLBiFZ).

AI.tldr: Web-based overflow monitoring system using Deep Learning for forecasting combined sewer overflow basin filling dynamics, with resilient cloud-edge architecture and interactive dashboard.
AI.motivation: Aging sewer systems in historical cities face increasing stress from extreme rainfall events, leading to combined sewer overflows (CSO) with significant environmental and public health impacts. There's a need to forecast overflow basin filling dynamics to anticipate capacity exceedance and enable timely preventive actions.
AI.method: Developed a web-based demonstrator that integrates Deep Learning forecasting methods in both cloud and edge computing settings. The system includes an interactive monitoring dashboard for overflow monitoring, designed to be resilient to network outages through the cloud-edge architecture.
AI.result: Created a working demonstrator available at https://riwwer.demo.calgo-lab.de with video showcase at https://cloud.bht-berlin.de/index.php/s/b9xt4T3SdiLBiFZ. The system enables forecasting of overflow basin filling dynamics and provides real-time monitoring capabilities that can operate despite network interruptions.
AI.conclusion: The developed web-based monitoring system with Deep Learning forecasting represents a practical approach to addressing CSO challenges in aging sewer infrastructure, offering resilience through cloud-edge integration and enabling proactive management of overflow events.

title: Trustworthy AI: Ensuring Reliability and Accountability from Models to Agents
summary: In this thesis, we develop algorithms with theoretical guarantees for ensuring reliability and accountability of Machine Learning (ML) systems. As ML systems evolve from predictive models to generative models and autonomous agents, the landscape of trustworthy AI has shifted. This thesis introduces tools grounded in information theory, optimization, and statistical learning to mitigate bias, reduce arbitrary decisions, ensure content provenance, and evaluate LLM-driven agents in autonomous settings. Towards mitigating bias and arbitrariness in traditional ML models, we introduce a kernel-based method to achieve multiaccuracy across complex subpopulations that traditional demographic categories may overlook. We also develop methods to address predictive multiplicity, where equally accurate models yield conflicting individual predictions. We ensure the accountability in generative AI through watermarking large language models (LLMs). We characterize the information-theoretic trade-off between watermark detection and text distortion and derive optimal watermarking strategies by leveraging optimal transport and coding theory. Empirical evaluations show our watermarks achieve a superior detection-quality tradeoff across language generation and coding tasks. Finally, we evaluate autonomous LLM agents in multi-agent environments through the first simulator of a fully LLM-driven supply chain. LLM agents offer significant performance gains, outperforming human teams and reducing costs by up to 67%, but also introduce systemic risks, including costly tail events.

AI.tldr: This thesis develops theoretical algorithms for reliable and accountable ML systems across three areas: bias mitigation in traditional ML via multiaccuracy methods, optimal watermarking for generative AI accountability, and evaluation of autonomous LLM agents in simulated supply chains.
AI.motivation: As ML systems evolve from predictive models to generative models and autonomous agents, there's a need for new tools to ensure reliability and accountability. The landscape of trustworthy AI has shifted, requiring mitigation of bias, reduction of arbitrary decisions, assurance of content provenance, and evaluation of LLM-driven agents in autonomous settings.
AI.method: The thesis develops tools grounded in information theory, optimization, and statistical learning. For traditional ML: kernel-based methods for multiaccuracy across complex subpopulations and methods to address predictive multiplicity. For generative AI: optimal watermarking strategies leveraging optimal transport and coding theory to characterize information-theoretic trade-offs between detection and text distortion. For autonomous agents: first simulator of fully LLM-driven supply chain to evaluate multi-agent environments.
AI.result: For bias mitigation: Achieves multiaccuracy across complex subpopulations overlooked by traditional demographic categories. For watermarking: Empirical evaluations show superior detection-quality tradeoff across language generation and coding tasks. For autonomous agents: LLM agents offer significant performance gains (outperforming human teams, reducing costs by up to 67%) but introduce systemic risks including costly tail events.
AI.conclusion: The thesis successfully develops algorithms with theoretical guarantees for ensuring reliability and accountability across different types of ML systems, addressing key challenges in bias mitigation, generative AI accountability, and autonomous agent evaluation, while highlighting both benefits and risks of advanced ML systems.

title: LLARS: Enabling Domain Expert & Developer Collaboration for LLM Prompting, Generation and Evaluation
comment: Accepted at IJCAI-ECAI 2026 Demonstrations Track. Demo video: https://youtu.be/3QaKouwr4gU
summary: We demonstrate LLARS (LLM Assisted Research System), an open-source platform that bridges the gap between domain experts and developers for building LLM-based systems. It integrates three tightly connected modules into an end-to-end pipeline: Collaborative Prompt Engineering for real-time co-authoring with version control and instant LLM testing, Batch Generation for configurable output production across user-selected prompts $\times$ models $\times$ data with cost control, and Hybrid Evaluation where human and LLM evaluators jointly assess outputs through diverse assessment methods, with live agreement metrics and provenance analysis to identify the best model-prompt combination for a given use case. New prompts and models are automatically available for batch generation and completed batches can be turned into evaluation scenarios with a single click. Interviews with six domain experts and three developers in online counselling confirmed that LLARS feels intuitive, saves considerable time by keeping everything in one place and makes interdisciplinary collaboration seamless.

AI.tldr: LLARS is an open-source platform that enables domain experts and developers to collaboratively build LLM-based systems through an integrated pipeline for prompt engineering, batch generation, and hybrid evaluation.
AI.motivation: The paper addresses the gap between domain experts and developers when building LLM-based systems, as these groups typically have different expertise but need to collaborate effectively.
AI.method: LLARS integrates three tightly connected modules: 1) Collaborative Prompt Engineering with version control and instant LLM testing, 2) Batch Generation for producing outputs across prompts × models × data with cost control, and 3) Hybrid Evaluation where human and LLM evaluators jointly assess outputs using diverse methods with live metrics and provenance analysis.
AI.result: Interviews with six domain experts and three developers in online counseling confirmed that LLARS feels intuitive, saves considerable time by keeping everything in one place, and makes interdisciplinary collaboration seamless. The platform automatically makes new prompts/models available for batch generation and allows completed batches to be turned into evaluation scenarios with one click.
AI.conclusion: LLARS successfully bridges the collaboration gap between domain experts and developers for building LLM-based systems through its integrated end-to-end pipeline that combines collaborative prompt engineering, batch generation, and hybrid evaluation with automated workflows and cost control.

title: VORT: Adaptive Power-Law Memory for NLP Transformers
summary: Standard Transformers impose near-exponential decay on the influence of distant tokens, conflicting with the power-law structure of long-range dependencies in natural language. We introduce the \emph{Variable-Order Retention Transformer} (\VORT{}), a memory architecture in which each ingested token is assigned a learnable fractional order α_i\in[δ,1] that governs a Grünwald--Letnikov power-law retention kernel. Because the fractional weighted sum is non-Markovian, we approximate it through a sum-of-exponentials (SOE) decomposition computed by Gauss--Laguerre quadrature on a Laplace-type integral representation of the kernel weights. Each exponential component admits a one-step Markovian recurrence at O(Sd_v) per step, where S=O(\log(T/\varepsilon)) terms suffice for \varepsilon-uniform accuracy on horizon [1,T]. Retrieval is keyed and associative via a linear-attention accumulator with an exact O(KSd_φd_v) -per-step recurrence. Four results are established: (i) an SOE approximation theorem with geometric convergence rate from the analyticity of the integrand after a log-change of variables; (ii) a quantisation bound valid on [δ,1] with correct analysis near α=0; (iii) a direct L^2 energy argument (Proposition) showing that for α>1/2 any mixture with fixed minimum decay rate Λ>0 incurs L^2([1,T]) error at least N_α(T)-C(Λ)\to\infty, with the Λ-dependence made explicit; and (iv) linear convergence of a gradient plasticity rule under the Polyak--Łojasiewicz condition. Two synthetic experiments confirm the architectural advantage: a Zipf-distributed retrieval benchmark and an entity label-copy task with uniform lag distribution, the latter ruling out prior-matching as an explanation for the power-law kernel's advantage.

AI.tldr: Variable-Order Retention Transformer (VORT) replaces exponential decay with learnable fractional-order power-law retention kernels to better match long-range dependencies in natural language.
AI.motivation: Standard Transformers impose near-exponential decay on distant token influence, which conflicts with the power-law structure of long-range dependencies observed in natural language.
AI.method: Introduces a memory architecture where each token gets a learnable fractional order α_i∈[δ,1] that governs Grünwald-Letnikov power-law retention kernels. Uses sum-of-exponentials decomposition via Gauss-Laguerre quadrature for efficient computation, with O(log(T/ε)) terms for ε-uniform accuracy.
AI.result: Establishes four theoretical results: (1) SOE approximation theorem with geometric convergence, (2) quantization bound analysis, (3) L² energy argument showing error lower bounds, (4) linear convergence of gradient plasticity rule. Synthetic experiments show architectural advantages on Zipf-distributed retrieval and entity label-copy tasks.
AI.conclusion: VORT provides a theoretically grounded architecture that better matches natural language's power-law dependency structure through learnable fractional-order retention kernels, with efficient computation and proven convergence properties.

title: Budget-Efficient Automatic Algorithm Design via Code Graph
summary: Large language models (LLMs) have emerged as powerful tools for automatic algorithm design (AAD). However, existing pipelines remain inefficient. They operate at the granularity of full algorithms, redundantly rewriting recurring substructures and discarding low-fitness candidates that may contain valuable algorithmic features. We formalize budget-efficient automatic algorithm design, wherein the search policy maximizes realized fitness subject to limited computational cost. We propose a directed acyclic graph representation of algorithms and build a search framework that fully exploits the LLM's output. Instead of querying the LLM for full algorithms, we use it to obtain corrections: compact operators that add, replace, or remove code blocks. Each correction augments the graph, yielding new algorithms that compose with prior corrections. This graph structure decomposes algorithms into sets of corrections, enabling correction-level credit assignment that informs subsequent queries. We complement this framework with theoretical insights into the ideal balance between search depth and breadth at different budget levels. We validate our method empirically on three combinatorial optimization problems, demonstrating consistent superiority of our graph-based search over full-algorithm search at equal token budget. Finally, our experiments suggest that rich contexts help only when the LLM's prior knowledge is shallow, and can hinder performance otherwise.

AI.tldr: Graph-based search framework for automatic algorithm design using LLMs, decomposing algorithms into reusable corrections for efficient search within computational budgets.
AI.motivation: Existing LLM-based automatic algorithm design pipelines are inefficient because they operate at the full algorithm level, redundantly rewriting recurring substructures and discarding potentially valuable algorithmic features from low-fitness candidates.
AI.method: Proposes a directed acyclic graph representation of algorithms where LLMs generate compact corrections (add, replace, or remove code blocks) instead of full algorithms. Each correction augments the graph, enabling new algorithms that compose with prior corrections. Features correction-level credit assignment to inform subsequent queries, and theoretical analysis of search depth vs. breadth balance at different budget levels.
AI.result: Empirical validation on three combinatorial optimization problems demonstrates consistent superiority of graph-based search over full-algorithm search at equal token budget. Additional insight shows rich contexts help only when LLM's prior knowledge is shallow, and can hinder performance otherwise.
AI.conclusion: The graph-based decomposition approach enables more efficient automatic algorithm design by maximizing realized fitness subject to computational constraints, leveraging LLMs' capability to generate corrections rather than full algorithms, and providing a framework that balances search exploration and exploitation.

title: Muon Does Not Converge on Convex Lipschitz Functions
summary:
Muon and its variants have shown strong empirical performance in a variety of deep learning tasks. Existing convergence analyses of Muon rely on smoothness assumptions, though arguably the most successful function class for developing deep learning methods (such as AdaGrad, Shampoo, Schedule-Free and more) has been the class of convex and Lipschitz functions. In this paper we question whether the classical convex Lipschitz model is a useful one for understanding Muon.
  Our answer is no. We show that Muon does not converge on the class of convex and Lipschitz functions, regardless of the choice of learning rate schedule. We also show that error feedback restores convergence of Muon and all the non-Euclidean subgradient methods with momentum. However, this theoretical fix using error feedback degrades the performance of Muon in
  two representative settings for image classification (CIFAR-10) and language modeling (nanoGPT on FineWeb-Edu 10B). Our conclusion is that convex Lipschitz theory, despite having a prominent role in the design of practical methods for deep learning, is not the most suited one for Muon. This suggests that Muon's success must come from structure absent from this model, most plausibly related to smoothness.

AI.tldr: Muon (a momentum-based optimization method) doesn't converge on convex Lipschitz functions regardless of learning rate schedule, and while error feedback theoretically fixes this, it hurts practical performance on real tasks, suggesting convex Lipschitz theory isn't suitable for understanding Muon's success.
AI.motivation: Despite strong empirical performance of Muon and its variants in deep learning tasks, existing convergence analyses rely on smoothness assumptions. The authors question whether the classical convex Lipschitz model (successful for understanding AdaGrad, Shampoo, Schedule-Free, etc.) is useful for understanding Muon's behavior and success.
AI.method: The authors show through theoretical analysis that Muon does not converge on the class of convex and Lipschitz functions under any learning rate schedule. They then demonstrate that error feedback can theoretically restore convergence for Muon and non-Euclidean subgradient methods with momentum, and test this in practical settings with CIFAR-10 image classification and nanoGPT on FineWeb-Edu 10B for language modeling.
AI.result: 1) Muon fails to converge on convex Lipschitz functions regardless of learning rate schedule. 2) Error feedback theoretically fixes the convergence issue but degrades practical performance in both image classification (CIFAR-10) and language modeling tasks. 3) Convex Lipschitz theory is not suitable for explaining Muon's empirical success.
AI.conclusion: The convex Lipschitz model, despite its prominent role in designing practical deep learning methods, is not the right framework for understanding Muon. Muon's success must stem from structure absent from this model, most likely related to smoothness properties of real-world optimization problems.

title: The Open-Box Fallacy: Why AI Deployment Needs a Calibrated Verification Regime
summary: AI deployment in sensitive domains such as health care, credit, employment, and criminal justice is often treated as unsafe to authorize until model internals can be explained. This often leads to an excessive reliance on mechanistic interpretability to address a deployment challenge beyond its intended scope. We argue that the gate should instead be calibrated verification: authorization should be domain-scoped, independently checkable, monitored after release, accountable, contestable, and revocable. The reason is twofold. First, model capability is uneven across nearby tasks, so authorization must attach to a specific use rather than to a model in general. Second, societies have long governed opaque expertise through credentials, monitoring, liability, appeal, and revocation rather than mechanism-level explanation. Recent evidence reinforces this distinction between mechanistic understanding and deployment authority: a 53-percentage-point gap between internal representations and output correction shows that understanding may not translate into action, while one scoping review found that only 9.0% of FDA-approved AI/ML device documents contained a prospective post-market surveillance study. We propose Verification Coverage, a six-component reportable standard with a minimum-composition rule, as the metric that should sit beside capability scores in model cards, leaderboards, and regulatory disclosures.

AI.tldr: The paper argues that AI deployment authorization should shift from requiring mechanistic interpretability to using calibrated verification systems (domain-scoped, independently checkable, monitored, accountable, contestable, revocable), proposing "Verification Coverage" as a new standard alongside capability scores.
AI.motivation: Current AI deployment in sensitive domains requires mechanistic interpretability as a safety gate, which is misapplied beyond its intended scope. This creates an excessive reliance on understanding model internals rather than focusing on deployment governance and safety verification.
AI.method: The authors propose a framework of calibrated verification with six components: domain-scoped authorization, independent checkability, post-release monitoring, accountability, contestability, and revocability. They introduce "Verification Coverage" as a reportable standard with minimum-composition rules that should accompany capability scores in model documentation and regulation.
AI.result: The paper presents evidence showing a 53-percentage-point gap between understanding internal representations and correcting outputs, and that only 9.0% of FDA-approved AI/ML devices included post-market surveillance studies, demonstrating that mechanistic understanding doesn't guarantee safe deployment. They propose Verification Coverage as a practical alternative to mechanistic interpretability for authorization.
AI.conclusion: AI deployment authorization should move from mechanistic interpretability to calibrated verification systems that mirror how societies have historically governed opaque expertise. Verification Coverage should become a standard metric alongside capability scores in model documentation and regulatory frameworks to ensure safe, accountable AI deployment in sensitive domains.

title: PMCTS: Particle Monte Carlo Tree Search for Principled Parallelized Inference Time Scaling
summary: Monte Carlo Tree Search (MCTS) is a widely used approach for policy improvement through search with increasing popularity for real world applications. Due to the sequential and deterministic nature of its search, runtime-scaling of MCTS with parallel compute remains a major challenge. We introduce Particle MCTS (PMCTS), to our knowledge the first principled parallel MCTS algorithm which is suited for neural network evaluations and can preserve formal policy improvement guarantees. Empirically, PMCTS scales well with parallel compute and significantly outperforms the popular heuristic-based baselines across domains.

AI.tldr: PMCTS is a novel parallel MCTS algorithm that preserves policy improvement guarantees and scales effectively with parallel computation, outperforming existing heuristic-based approaches.
AI.motivation: Monte Carlo Tree Search (MCTS) faces a major challenge in scaling with parallel compute due to its sequential and deterministic nature, limiting its applicability for real-world problems that could benefit from parallel processing.
AI.method: The authors introduce Particle MCTS (PMCTS), which appears to be a particle-based approach to parallelizing MCTS. The method is designed to work well with neural network evaluations while maintaining formal policy improvement guarantees that are typically lost in heuristic parallel MCTS implementations.
AI.result: Empirical results show that PMCTS scales effectively with parallel compute and significantly outperforms popular heuristic-based parallel MCTS baselines across multiple domains.
AI.conclusion: PMCTS represents the first principled parallel MCTS algorithm that combines effective parallel scaling, compatibility with neural network evaluations, and preservation of formal policy improvement guarantees, making it a promising approach for real-world applications.

title: PRISM: Generation-Time Detection and Mitigation of Secret Leakage in Multi-Agent LLM Pipelines
summary: Multi-agent LLM systems introduce a security risk in which sensitive information accessed by one agent can propagate through shared context and reappear in downstream outputs, even without explicit adversarial intent. We formalise this phenomenon as propagation amplification, where leakage risk increases across agent boundaries as sensitive content is repeatedly exposed to downstream generators. Existing defences, including prompt-based safeguards, static pattern matching, and LLM-as-judge filtering, are not designed for this setting: they either operate after generation, rely primarily on surface-form patterns, or add substantial latency without modelling the generation process itself. To resolve these issues, we propose PRISM, a real-time defence that treats credential leakage as a sequential risk accumulation problem during generation. At each decoding step, PRISM combines 16 signals spanning lexical, structural, information-theoretic, behavioural, and contextual features into a calibrated risk score, enabling per-token intervention through green, yellow, and red risk zones. Our central observation is that credential reproduction is often preceded by a measurable shift in generation dynamics, characterised by entropy collapse and increasing logit concentration. When combined with text-structural cues such as identifier-pattern detection, these temporal signals provide an early warning of leakage before a secret is fully reconstructed. Across a 2,000-task adversarial benchmark covering 13 attack categories and three pressure levels in a heterogeneous four-agent pipeline, PRISM achieves F1 = 0.832 with precision = 1.000 and recall = 0.712, while producing no observed leakage on our benchmark (0.0% task-level leak rate) and preserving output utility of 0.893. It substantially outperforms the strongest baseline, Span Tagger, which achieves F1 = 0.719 with a 15.0% task-level leak rate.

AI.tldr: PRISM is a real-time defense system that prevents credential leakage in multi-agent LLM systems by monitoring generation dynamics with multi-signal risk scoring and per-token intervention.
AI.motivation: Multi-agent LLM systems have a security vulnerability where sensitive information accessed by one agent can propagate through shared context and reappear in downstream outputs, even without adversarial intent. Existing defenses are inadequate as they operate after generation, rely on surface patterns, or add latency without modeling the generation process itself.
AI.method: PRISM treats credential leakage as a sequential risk accumulation problem during generation. At each decoding step, it combines 16 signals spanning lexical, structural, information-theoretic, behavioural, and contextual features into a calibrated risk score, enabling per-token intervention through green, yellow, and red risk zones. The method leverages the observation that credential reproduction is often preceded by measurable shifts in generation dynamics like entropy collapse and increasing logit concentration.
AI.result: Across a 2,000-task adversarial benchmark covering 13 attack categories and three pressure levels in a heterogeneous four-agent pipeline, PRISM achieves F1 = 0.832 with precision = 1.000 and recall = 0.712. It produces no observed leakage on the benchmark (0.0% task-level leak rate) while preserving output utility of 0.893, substantially outperforming the strongest baseline (Span Tagger with F1 = 0.719 and 15.0% task-level leak rate).
AI.conclusion: PRISM effectively addresses the propagation amplification problem in multi-agent LLM systems by providing real-time, early detection of credential leakage through multi-signal monitoring of generation dynamics, significantly improving security while maintaining utility and outperforming existing approaches.

title: Benchmarking Compositional Generalisation for Machine Learning Interatomic Potentials
summary: Machine Learning Interatomic Potentials play a fundamental role in computational chemistry and materials science, enabling applications from molecular dynamics simulations to drug design and materials discovery. While recent approaches can estimate inter-atomic forces with high precision, it remains unclear to what extent they can generalise to previously unseen molecules. Do they learn the compositional structure of chemistry, capturing how molecular fragments and their combinations determine properties, or do they primarily learn to interpolate patterns that are specific to the training examples? To address this question, we propose a benchmark consisting of four tasks that require some form of compositional generalisation. In each task, models are tested on molecules that were unseen during training, but the training data is chosen such that generalisation to the test examples should be feasible for models that learn the underlying physical principles. Our empirical analysis shows that the considered tasks are highly challenging for state-of-the-art models, with errors on out-of-distribution examples often an order of magnitude higher than on in-distribution examples, even when using foundation models that have been pre-trained on millions of molecules.

AI.tldr: ML interatomic potentials struggle with compositional generalization - they fail to properly extrapolate to unseen molecular structures even when trained on millions of examples, showing poor out-of-distribution performance.
AI.motivation: There's a fundamental question about whether machine learning interatomic potentials truly learn the compositional structure of chemistry (how molecular fragments and combinations determine properties) or just interpolate training patterns. This is critical for their reliability in computational chemistry and materials science applications like molecular dynamics and drug design.
AI.method: Created a benchmark with four tasks requiring compositional generalization, where models are tested on molecules unseen during training but with training data structured so that generalization should be possible for models learning physical principles.
AI.result: State-of-the-art models perform poorly on these compositional generalization tasks, with errors on out-of-distribution examples often 10x higher than on in-distribution examples, even for foundation models pre-trained on millions of molecules.
AI.conclusion: Current ML interatomic potentials have significant limitations in compositional generalization, suggesting they may not be learning the underlying physical principles of chemistry as well as hoped, which impacts their reliability for novel molecular discovery applications.

title: Hierarchical Causal Abduction: A Foundation Framework for Explainable Model Predictive Control
summary: Model Predictive Control (MPC) is widely used to operate safety-critical infrastructure by predicting future trajectories and optimizing control actions. However, nonlinear dynamics, hard safety constraints, and numerical optimization often render individual control moves opaque to human operators, undermining trust and hindering deployment. This paper presents Hierarchical Causal Abduction (HCA), which combines (i) physics-informed reasoning via domain knowledge graphs, (ii) optimization evidence from Karush--Kuhn--Tucker (KKT) multipliers, and (iii) temporal causal discovery via the PCMCI algorithm to generate faithful, human-interpretable explanations for control actions computed by nonlinear MPC. Across three diverse control applications (greenhouse climate, building HVAC, chemical process engineering) with expert validation, HCA improves explanation accuracy by 53\% over LIME (0.478 vs. 0.311) using a single set of cross-domain parameters without per-domain tuning; domain-specific KKT-threshold calibration over 2--3 days further increases accuracy to 0.88. Ablation studies confirm that each evidence source is essential, with 32--37\% accuracy degradation when any component is removed, and HCA's ranking-and-validation methodology generalizes beyond MPC to other prediction-based decision systems, including learning-based control and trajectory planning.

AI.tldr: HCA (Hierarchical Causal Abduction) is a new method that generates human-interpretable explanations for Model Predictive Control actions by combining physics-informed reasoning, KKT optimization evidence, and temporal causal discovery.
AI.motivation: MPC is widely used for safety-critical infrastructure but suffers from opacity - nonlinear dynamics, hard constraints, and numerical optimization make individual control moves opaque to human operators, undermining trust and hindering deployment.
AI.method: HCA combines three evidence sources: (1) physics-informed reasoning via domain knowledge graphs, (2) optimization evidence from Karush-Kuhn-Tucker (KKT) multipliers, and (3) temporal causal discovery via the PCMCI algorithm to generate faithful explanations.
AI.result: HCA improves explanation accuracy by 53% over LIME (0.478 vs. 0.311) using a single set of cross-domain parameters without per-domain tuning; domain-specific calibration further increases accuracy to 0.88. Ablation studies show 32-37% accuracy degradation when any component is removed.
AI.conclusion: HCA successfully generates human-interpretable explanations for nonlinear MPC, validating across three diverse applications with expert input. The method generalizes beyond MPC to other prediction-based decision systems including learning-based control and trajectory planning.

title: When More Parameters Hurt: Foundation Model Priors Amplify Worst-Client Disparity Under Extreme Federated Heterogeneity
comment: 7 pages, 5 figures. Submitted to FL@FM-IJCAI 2026 Workshop
summary: Federated learning (FL) is increasingly used to fine-tune foundation models (FMs) on distributed private data. The community largely assumes that large-scale pretraining serves as a 'rising tide that lifts all boats' in federated settings. However, our experiments reveal that these powerful priors can hinder rather than help the most disadvantaged clients under extreme heterogeneity. Through controlled experiments on federated text classification, we compare worst-client accuracy between TextCNN (2.7M parameters) and DistilBERT with Low-Rank Adaptation (LoRA, 66M parameters) across four Non-IID heterogeneity levels. Under extreme label skew (alpha = 0.1), DistilBERT+LoRA produces a worst-client accuracy gap of 50.1% -- 56% larger than TextCNN's 32.2% gap, despite having 25x more parameters and extensive pretraining. Under moderate heterogeneity (alpha >= 0.5), the pattern reverses: the FM nearly eliminates the gap. We call this the FM Fairness Paradox. We further show that an inverse-weighted LoRA aggregation method (FedAvgW) does not resolve the disparity, suggesting aggregation reweighting alone may be insufficient. Our results highlight the need for mechanisms that explicitly protect minority clients before deploying foundation models in high-stakes federated contexts such as healthcare and education.

AI.tldr: Foundation models in federated learning can actually hurt disadvantaged clients under extreme data heterogeneity - a surprising finding called the "FM Fairness Paradox" where bigger models perform worse for minority clients.
AI.motivation: The community assumes that foundation models (large-scale pretraining) benefits all clients in federated learning settings, but the authors question this assumption particularly for disadvantaged clients under extreme data heterogeneity.
AI.method: Conducted controlled experiments on federated text classification comparing TextCNN (2.7M parameters) vs DistilBERT with LoRA (66M parameters) across four Non-IID heterogeneity levels. Also tested inverse-weighted LoRA aggregation method (FedAvgW).
AI.result: Under extreme label skew (alpha=0.1), DistilBERT+LoRA had 50.1% worst-client accuracy gap - 56% larger than TextCNN's 32.2% gap, despite having 25x more parameters. Under moderate heterogeneity (alpha>=0.5), FMs nearly eliminate the gap. FedAvgW aggregation didn't resolve the disparity.
AI.conclusion: Foundation models can exacerbate fairness issues for minority clients in federated settings under extreme heterogeneity. Current approaches like aggregation reweighting are insufficient, and mechanisms are needed to explicitly protect disadvantaged clients before deploying FMs in high-stakes domains like healthcare and education.

title: Teacher-Aware Evolution of Heuristic Programs from Learned Optimization Policies
summary: LLM-based automatic heuristic design has shown promise for generating executable heuristics for combinatorial optimization, but existing methods mainly rely on delayed endpoint performance. We propose a \emph{teacher-aware evolutionary framework} that uses independently trained learned optimization policies as behavioral teachers. Instead of deploying or imitating the teacher, our method queries it on states visited by candidate heuristic programs and uses its action preferences as local feedback for evolution.
