The resulting search discovers static executable heuristics guided by both task performance and teacher-derived behavioral signals. Experiments on scheduling, routing, and graph optimization benchmarks show that our method improves over performance-driven LLM heuristic evolution baselines while requiring no neural inference at deployment. These results suggest that learned optimization policies can be repurposed as behavioral feedback sources for automatic heuristic discovery.

AI.tldr: LLM-based automatic heuristic design using teacher-aware evolutionary framework that queries trained optimization policies for behavioral feedback instead of just imitating them, improving heuristic discovery without neural inference at deployment.
AI.motivation: Existing LLM-based automatic heuristic design methods primarily rely on delayed endpoint performance feedback, which may be insufficient for effective heuristic discovery. There's a need for more immediate, behaviorally-informed feedback during heuristic evolution.
AI.method: Proposes a teacher-aware evolutionary framework that uses independently trained learned optimization policies as behavioral teachers. Instead of deploying or imitating the teacher, the method queries it on states visited by candidate heuristic programs and uses its action preferences as local feedback for evolutionary search, combining both task performance and teacher-derived behavioral signals.
AI.result: Experiments on scheduling, routing, and graph optimization benchmarks show that the method improves over performance-driven LLM heuristic evolution baselines while requiring no neural inference at deployment, making the discovered heuristics computationally efficient.
AI.conclusion: Learned optimization policies can be effectively repurposed as behavioral feedback sources for automatic heuristic discovery, providing a new way to leverage machine learning models to guide heuristic program evolution beyond just performance metrics.

title: Machine Learning-Based Graph Simplification for Symbolic Accelerators
summary: Graph-based accelerators have been widely adopted in symbolic data processing applications such as genomics, cybersecurity, and artificial intelligence. However, these systems often suffer from excessive memory usage and inefficiencies stemming from redundant graph structures. We present AutoSlim, a machine learning-based framework that leverages data-driven methods to prune automata graphs for hardware accelerators. Using features extracted from prior graph executions and a Random Forest classifier, AutoSlim identifies and removes low-impact nodes and edges. When applied to a Non-deterministic Finite Automata overlay architecture (NAPOLY+), AutoSlim reduces FPGA resource usage by up to 40%, with corresponding improvements in throughput and power efficiency. The framework includes a verification step to ensure functional equivalence after pruning and suggests promising directions for both hardware optimization and security.

AI.tldr: AutoSlim: ML-based framework that prunes automata graphs for hardware accelerators, reducing FPGA resource usage by up to 40% while maintaining functional equivalence.
AI.motivation: Graph-based accelerators suffer from excessive memory usage and inefficiencies due to redundant graph structures in symbolic data processing applications (genomics, cybersecurity, AI).
AI.method: Machine learning framework using Random Forest classifier trained on features from prior graph executions to identify and remove low-impact nodes and edges from automata graphs, with verification step for functional equivalence.
AI.result: Applied to NAPOLY+ (Non-deterministic Finite Automata overlay architecture), achieves up to 40% reduction in FPGA resource usage with corresponding improvements in throughput and power efficiency.
AI.conclusion: AutoSlim effectively optimizes hardware accelerators through data-driven graph pruning, showing promise for both hardware optimization and security applications while maintaining functional correctness.

title: Navigating the Sea of LLM Evaluation: Investigating Bias in Toxicity Benchmarks
summary: The rapid adoption of LLMs in both research and industry highlights the challenges of deploying them safely and reveals a gap in the systematic evaluation of toxicity benchmarks. As organizations increasingly rely on these benchmarks to certify models for customer-facing applications and automated moderation, unrecognized evaluation biases could lead to the deployment of vulnerable or unsafe systems. This work investigates the robustness of established benchmarking setups and examines how to measure currently neglected intrinsic biases, such as those related to model choice, metrics, and task types. Our experiments uncover significant discrepancies in benchmark behaviors when evaluation setups are altered. Specifically, shifting the task from text completion to summarization increases the tendency of benchmarks to flag content as harmful. Additionally, certain benchmarks fail to maintain consistent behavior when the input data domain is changed. Furthermore, we observe model-specific instabilities, demonstrating a clear need for more robust and comprehensive safety evaluation frameworks.

AI.tldr: LLM toxicity benchmarks have unrecognized biases and inconsistencies that undermine safety evaluation reliability, showing varying results based on task type, data domain, and model choice.
AI.motivation: The rapid adoption of LLMs in real-world applications has revealed challenges in safe deployment, with organizations relying on toxicity benchmarks to certify models. However, there's a gap in systematic evaluation of these benchmarks, and unrecognized evaluation biases could lead to deploying vulnerable or unsafe systems.
AI.method: Investigated robustness of established benchmarking setups by examining intrinsic biases related to model choice, metrics, and task types. Conducted experiments that altered evaluation setups to measure discrepancies in benchmark behaviors.
AI.result: Found significant discrepancies: (1) Shifting tasks from text completion to summarization increases benchmarks' tendency to flag content as harmful; (2) Certain benchmarks fail to maintain consistent behavior when input data domain changes; (3) Observed model-specific instabilities in benchmark responses.
AI.conclusion: Current toxicity benchmarks exhibit substantial vulnerabilities and inconsistencies, demonstrating a clear need for more robust and comprehensive safety evaluation frameworks to ensure reliable certification of LLMs for real-world deployment.

title: Non-Parametric Rehearsal Learning via Conditional Mean Embeddings
summary: In machine learning, a critical class of decision-related problems concerns preventing predicted undesirable outcomes, referred to as the \textit{avoiding undesired future} (AUF) problem. To address this, the \textit{rehearsal learning} framework has been proposed to model influence relations for effective decisions. However, existing rehearsal methods rely on restrictive parametric assumptions such as linear systems or additive noise, limiting their practical applicability. In this paper, we propose the first non-parametric rehearsal learning approach for AUF without assuming specific functional forms of data generation processes. Specifically, we use kernel machinery to reformulate the AUF objective into a unified representation that disentangles desirability modeling from action-induced distributional changes. To handle the discontinuity of desirability indicator, we present a smooth Probit surrogate and provide an approximation error bound. Meanwhile, we capture the action-induced changes via conditional mean embeddings, and develop a kernel ridge regression based nested estimator for AUF objective with consistency guarantees. Such a formulation naturally accommodates nonlinear systems and non-additive noise, and empirical results on synthetic and real-data-derived semi-synthetic benchmarks demonstrate the effectiveness and flexibility of our approach.

AI.tldr: Proposes first non-parametric rehearsal learning approach for avoiding undesired future problems using kernel methods, overcoming limitations of parametric assumptions in existing methods.
AI.motivation: Existing rehearsal learning methods for avoiding undesired future (AUF) problems rely on restrictive parametric assumptions (linear systems or additive noise), which limit their practical applicability in real-world scenarios.
AI.method: Uses kernel machinery to reformulate AUF objective into unified representation separating desirability modeling from action-induced changes. Employs Probit surrogate for discontinuity of desirability indicator with error bounds, and captures action-induced changes via conditional mean embeddings with kernel ridge regression based nested estimator.
AI.result: Developed approach naturally accommodates nonlinear systems and non-additive noise. Empirical results on synthetic and real-data-derived semi-synthetic benchmarks demonstrate the effectiveness and flexibility of the proposed method.
AI.conclusion: Proposes the first non-parametric rehearsal learning framework for AUF problems, providing theoretical guarantees and demonstrating practical advantages over restrictive parametric approaches through comprehensive experimental validation.

title: diffGHOST: Diffusion based Generative Hedged Oblivious Synthetic Trajectories
summary: Trajectories are nowadays valuable information for a wide range of applications. However they are also inherently sensitive, as they contain highly personal information about individuals. Facing this challenge, synthesizing mobility trajectories has emerged as a promising solution to leverage mobility information while preserving privacy. State-of-the-art models, often rely on the false assumptions of generative models implicit privacy and fails to provide privacy guarantees while preserving trajectories utility. Here, we introduce diffGHOST, a conditional diffusion model based on latent space segmentation, designed to answer this challenge. Thus, this paper propose a methodology that identify and mitigate memorization of critical samples using condition segments of a learn latent space.

AI.tldr: diffGHOST: A conditional diffusion model using latent space segmentation for privacy-preserving trajectory synthesis with provable privacy guarantees
AI.motivation: Trajectory data is valuable for many applications but contains highly sensitive personal information. Existing generative models often falsely assume implicit privacy and fail to provide proper privacy guarantees while maintaining trajectory utility.
AI.method: Proposes diffGHOST, a conditional diffusion model based on latent space segmentation. Uses a methodology that identifies and mitigates memorization of critical samples using condition segments of a learned latent space.
AI.result: The paper introduces a novel approach for privacy-preserving trajectory synthesis that addresses the privacy-utility tradeoff through latent space segmentation techniques in a diffusion model framework.
AI.conclusion: diffGHOST offers a promising solution for leveraging mobility information while preserving privacy through provable privacy guarantees, overcoming limitations of existing generative models that lack formal privacy protections.

title: Relative Kinetic Utility for Reasoning-Aware Structural Pruning in Large Language Models
summary: Chain-of-Thought (CoT) prompting symbolized a huge improvement of reasoning capabilities of Large Language Models (LLMs). However, scaling up test-time computation yields extensive CoT sequences, introducing severe inference latency and key-value (KV) cache memory bottlenecks. While structural pruning offers a fundamental, hardware-aware solution to alleviate static parameter burdens, existing magnitude-based methods may cut off the neurons of CoT: by over-indexing on discrete cross-entropy objectives, these heuristics fall into a \textit{magnitude trap}: they prioritize high-frequency, low-information syntactic tokens and trigger a disappointing reasoning collapse at high sparsities (e.g., 40\%). To overcome this topological phase transition, we propose \textsc{Relative Kinetic Utility} (RKU), a novel theoretical framework that elevates discrete pruning to a continuous kinetic integral over the depth manifold of the model based on Alternating Gradient Flow(AGF). By modifying it with Fisher trace normalization, RKU acts as a lightweight curvature-aware normalization to isolate \textit{kinetic spikes} -- the fundamental structural pathways responsible for high-curvature logical routing. Extensive experiments on Qwen-2.5-7B and LLaMA-3-8B improves performance in the high-sparsity regime around 40\%. RKU attains 13.34\% accuracy on GSM8K at 40\% sparsity, outperforming the strongest baseline, and appears to better preserve reasoning-relevant representations under out-of-distribution evaluation.

AI.tldr: RKU (Relative Kinetic Utility) is a novel pruning method that prevents reasoning collapse in LLMs at high sparsities by identifying and preserving structural pathways responsible for logical reasoning, outperforming existing magnitude-based pruning methods.
AI.motivation: Chain-of-Thought (CoT) prompting improves LLM reasoning but creates inference latency and KV cache memory bottlenecks when scaled. Existing magnitude-based pruning methods cause reasoning collapse at high sparsities (e.g., 40%) because they prioritize high-frequency, low-information syntactic tokens instead of preserving reasoning pathways.
AI.method: Proposes Relative Kinetic Utility (RKU), a theoretical framework that elevates discrete pruning to a continuous kinetic integral over the depth manifold using Alternating Gradient Flow (AGF). RKU applies Fisher trace normalization as a lightweight curvature-aware method to identify "kinetic spikes" - fundamental structural pathways responsible for high-curvature logical routing.
AI.result: Extensive experiments on Qwen-2.5-7B and LLaMA-3-8B show RKU improves performance at high sparsity (around 40%). Achieves 13.34% accuracy on GSM8K at 40% sparsity, outperforming strongest baselines, and better preserves reasoning-relevant representations under out-of-distribution evaluation.
AI.conclusion: RKU successfully overcomes the "magnitude trap" in pruning methods by focusing on kinetic spikes and logical routing pathways, enabling effective pruning while maintaining reasoning capabilities at high sparsities where traditional methods fail.

title: Evolving-RL: End-to-End Optimization of Experience-Driven Self-Evolving Capability within Agents
summary: Experience-driven self-evolving agents aim to overcome the static nature of large language models by distilling reusable experience from past interactions, thus enabling adaptation to novel tasks at deployment time. This process places substantial demands on the foundation model's capacities for abstraction, generalization, and in-context learning. However, most existing studies focus primarily on system-level design choices, such as how experience is represented and managed, neglecting the inherent capabilities of the underlying model. While some recent works have started to optimize the experience utilization stage via reinforcement learning, they still fail to treat self-evolution as a unified process to be jointly optimized. To this end, we propose Evolving-RL, an efficient algorithmic framework that jointly improves the experience extraction and utilization capabilities required for self-evolution. Specifically, we center the learning process on experience extraction and evaluation, using the two supervisory signals derived from evaluation to optimize the extractor and solver separately and thus enable their coordinated co-evolution. Experiments on ALFWorld and Mind2Web show that Evolving-RL effectively enhances LLMs' ability to extract and reuse experience, leading to strong performance gains on out-of-distribution tasks (up to 98.7% relative improvement over the GRPO baseline on ALFWorld unseen tasks and 35.8% on Mind2Web), and these gains are fully unlocked only through the coordinated co-evolution of experience extraction and utilization. Furthermore, Evolving-RL inherently functions as an experience-augmented RL algorithm. By internalizing reusable experience patterns directly into model parameters, it achieves remarkable performance gains over standard baselines on both seen and unseen tasks, even in the absence of test-time experience accumulation.

AI.tldr: Evolving-RL is a framework that jointly optimizes experience extraction and utilization for self-evolving AI agents, enabling better adaptation to novel tasks through coordinated co-evolution of extraction and utilization modules.
AI.motivation: Current experience-driven self-evolving agents focus too much on system-level design choices and don't treat self-evolution as a unified process to be jointly optimized. Existing approaches neglect the inherent capabilities of underlying models and fail to optimize experience extraction and utilization together.
AI.method: Proposes Evolving-RL framework that centers learning on experience extraction and evaluation, using two supervisory signals from evaluation to separately optimize the extractor and solver modules, enabling their coordinated co-evolution. The approach internalizes reusable experience patterns directly into model parameters.
AI.result: Significant performance improvements on ALFWorld (up to 98.7% relative improvement over GRPO baseline on unseen tasks) and Mind2Web (35.8% improvement). Gains are fully unlocked only through coordinated co-evolution of extraction and utilization. Functions as an experience-augmented RL algorithm that works well even without test-time experience accumulation.
AI.conclusion: Evolving-RL effectively enhances LLMs' ability to extract and reuse experience for self-evolution, demonstrating that joint optimization of extraction and utilization is crucial for enabling agents to adapt to novel tasks through reusable experience patterns.

title: Large Language Models for Sequential Decision-Making: Improving In-Context Learning via Supervised Fine-Tuning
summary: Large language models (LLMs) have shown remarkable in-context learning (ICL) capabilities, yet their potential for sequential decision-making remains underexplored. In this paper, we study the ICL capabilities of LLMs in sequential decision-making settings, including Markov Decision Processes (MDPs), Partially Observable MDPs (POMDPs), and Ambiguous POMDPs (APOMDPs). We fine-tune pretrained LLMs to perform few-shot decision-making directly from offline, oracle-labeled trajectories. Our framework enables flexible imitation of policies through supervised fine-tuning (SFT). Theoretically, we focus on linear MDPs and interpret a fine-tuned attention layer as implicitly estimating optimal Q-functions from in-context data. Building on this interpretation, we derive an end-to-end suboptimality bound for the induced policy that separates the in-context estimation error from the training-length bias. Empirically, across synthetic MDP, POMDP, and APOMDP settings, we find that fine-tuned LLMs achieve substantially smaller optimality gaps than in-context-only and random baselines, with especially large gains in longer-horizon, partially observed, and model-ambiguous environments. Together, these results show that supervised fine-tuning provides an effective route to endowing pretrained LLMs with sequential decision-making capabilities from offline data, which is an important advantage in domains such as healthcare where offline data are abundant.

AI.tldr: LLMs can be fine-tuned for sequential decision-making from offline trajectory data, achieving better performance than in-context-only approaches across MDPs, POMDPs, and ambiguous environments.
AI.motivation: Large language models have strong in-context learning capabilities, but their potential for sequential decision-making remains underexplored. The paper aims to leverage LLMs for decision-making tasks using offline data, which is particularly valuable in domains like healthcare where offline data is abundant.
AI.method: Fine-tune pretrained LLMs to perform few-shot decision-making directly from offline, oracle-labeled trajectories using supervised fine-tuning (SFT). The framework enables flexible imitation of policies. Theoretically analyze the approach for linear MDPs, interpreting fine-tuned attention layers as implicitly estimating optimal Q-functions from in-context data.
AI.result: Empirical results across synthetic MDP, POMDP, and APOMDP settings show that fine-tuned LLMs achieve substantially smaller optimality gaps than in-context-only and random baselines. The gains are especially large in longer-horizon, partially observed, and model-ambiguous environments.
AI.conclusion: Supervised fine-tuning provides an effective route to endowing pretrained LLMs with sequential decision-making capabilities from offline data. The theoretical analysis provides an end-to-end suboptimality bound that separates in-context estimation error from training-length bias, showing the approach's theoretical foundations.

title: GESR: A Genetic Programming-Based Symbolic Regression Method with Gene Editing
summary: Mathematical formulas serve as a language through which humans communicate with nature. Discovering mathematical laws from scientific data to describe natural phenomena has been a long-standing pursuit of humanity for centuries. In the field of artificial intelligence, this challenge is known as the symbolic regression problem. Among existing symbolic regression approaches, Genetic Programming (GP) based on evolutionary algorithms remains one of the most classical and widely adopted methods. GP simulates the evolutionary process across generations through genetic mutation and crossover. However, mutations and crossovers in GP are entirely random. While this randomness effectively mimics natural evolution, it inevitably produces both beneficial and detrimental variations. If there existed a metaphorical `God` capable of foreseeing which genetic mutations or crossovers would yield superior outcomes and performing targeted gene editing accordingly, the efficiency of evolution could be substantially improved. Motivated by this idea, we propose in this paper a symbolic regression approach based on gene editing, termed GESR. In GESR, we trained two "hands of God" (two BERT models). Among them, the first leverages the BERT's masked language modeling capability to guide the mutation of genes (expression symbols). The other BERT model guides the crossover of individual genes by predicting the crossover point. Experimental results demonstrate that GESR significantly improves computational efficiency compared with traditional GP algorithms and achieves strong overall performance across multiple symbolic regression tasks.

AI.tldr: GESR: A novel symbolic regression approach using BERT models as "hands of God" to guide gene editing in genetic programming, improving computational efficiency and performance.
AI.motivation: Traditional Genetic Programming (GP) for symbolic regression relies on random mutations and crossovers, which produces both beneficial and detrimental variations. The authors propose that if a metaphorical "God" could foresee which genetic operations would yield superior outcomes, evolution efficiency could be substantially improved.
AI.method: GESR uses two BERT models as "hands of God": one leverages BERT's masked language modeling capability to guide mutation of expression symbols, while the other BERT model guides crossover of individual genes by predicting crossover points, thus replacing random operations with guided ones.
AI.result: Experimental results demonstrate that GESR significantly improves computational efficiency compared with traditional GP algorithms and achieves strong overall performance across multiple symbolic regression tasks.
AI.conclusion: The gene editing approach with guided mutations and crossovers via BERT models represents an effective improvement over traditional random evolutionary methods in symbolic regression, demonstrating the potential of targeted AI-guided evolution.

title: A Geometric Perspective on Next-Token Prediction in Large Language Models: Three Emerging Phases
summary: We investigate the geometry of predictive information across the layers of large language models (LLMs). We repurpose representation lenses-learned affine maps trained to predict the next token from intermediate residual streams-as geometric diagnostic tools. Rather than asking what the model predicts at each layer, we ask where predictive information resides and how it evolves across depth. We define at each layer a predictive readout subspace as the dominant k-dimensional singular subspace of such a map on the d-dimensional residual stream (where k is a resolution parameter), and track its trajectory on the Grassmann manifold as a similarity profile across layers. The profile is well described by unimodal distributions exhibiting a rise, near-plateau, and descent; varying k from 1% to 50% of d traces a Pareto frontier between visibility and energy retention, yet the same structure emerges at all scales. Across eight models from two families (Qwen2.5 and OLMo2, 1B-32B), we identify three geometric phases. Updates are approximately orthogonal to the residual stream throughout; what distinguishes the phases is their effect on the effective rank, which expands, stabilizes, and concentrates. In the first, Seeding Multiplexing, feed-forward memories and attention layers seed a candidate set in superposition in family-specific proportions, with the final token rising as leading candidate from 20% to 35% of positions across this phase. In the second, Hoisting Overriding, updates override existing subspaces to concentrate the candidate distribution without expanding the rank. In the third, Focal Convergence, high-energy low-rank updates write the winner into a form aligned with the unembedding direction. Phases 1 and 3 grow slowly with model depth, while Phase 2 expands linearly. The additional capacity of deeper LLMs is largely absorbed by candidate disambiguation.

AI.tldr: Researchers use geometric analysis to track how predictive information evolves across LLM layers, identifying three distinct phases in predictive subspace trajectories that reveal how deeper models disambiguate candidate predictions.
AI.motivation: To understand where and how predictive information resides and evolves across the layers of large language models, moving beyond simply asking what models predict to examining the geometric organization of predictive information.
AI.method: Use learned affine maps (representation lenses) trained to predict next tokens from intermediate residual streams as diagnostic tools. Define predictive readout subspaces at each layer as dominant k-dimensional singular subspaces, then track their trajectories on the Grassmann manifold across layers to create similarity profiles.
AI.result: Identified three geometric phases across eight models from Qwen2.5 and OLMo2 families (1B-32B): 1) Seeding Multiplexing (feed-forward memories and attention seed candidates), 2) Hoisting Overriding (updates override existing subspaces to concentrate candidates), 3) Focal Convergence (high-energy low-rank updates align winner with unembedding direction). Phases 1 and 3 grow slowly with depth, Phase 2 expands linearly, with deeper models using extra capacity primarily for candidate disambiguation.
AI.conclusion: Predictive information in LLMs follows a structured geometric evolution across layers with three distinct phases, revealing that deeper models primarily use their additional capacity for disambiguating candidate predictions rather than fundamentally changing the geometric organization of predictive subspaces.

title: The Agent Use of Agent Beings: Agent Cybernetics Is the Missing Science of Foundation Agents
summary: LLM-based foundation agents that perceive, reason, and act across thousands of reasoning steps are rapidly becoming the dominant paradigm for deploying artificial intelligence in open-ended, long-horizon complex tasks. Despite this significance, the field remains overwhelmingly engineering-driven. Engineering practice has converged on useful primitives (tool loops, memory banks, harnesses, reflection steps), yet these are assembled by empirical trial and error rather than from first principles. Fundamental questions remain open: under what conditions does a long-running agent remain on-task? How should an agent respond when its environment exceeds its representational capacity? What architectural properties are necessary for safe self-improvement? We argue that cybernetics, the mid-twentieth-century science of control and communication in complex systems, provides the missing theoretical scaffold for foundation agents. By mapping six canonical laws of classical cybernetics onto six agent design principles, and synthesizing those principles into three engineering desiderata (reliability, lifelong running, and self-Improvement), we arrive at a framework termed Agent Cybernetics. Three application domains, code generation, computer use and automated research, exemplify the analytical framework of agent cybernetics by identifying failure modes and concrete engineering recommendations. We hope that agent cybernetics opens a new research venue and establishes the scientific foundation that foundation agents need for principled, reliable real-world deployment.

AI.tldr: The paper proposes "Agent Cybernetics" as a theoretical framework for foundation agents, applying classical cybernetics principles to address the lack of scientific foundations in current engineering-driven approaches to long-running AI agents.
AI.motivation: Current LLM-based foundation agents for complex, long-horizon tasks are overwhelmingly engineering-driven without theoretical foundations. Despite convergence on useful primitives (tool loops, memory banks, etc.), they're assembled empirically rather than from first principles. Fundamental questions about agent stability, representational capacity, and safe self-improvement remain unanswered.
AI.method: The paper maps six canonical laws of classical cybernetics onto six agent design principles, then synthesizes these principles into three engineering desiderata: reliability, lifelong running, and self-improvement. This creates the "Agent Cybernetics" framework.
AI.result: The Agent Cybernetics framework is applied to three domains (code generation, computer use, and automated research) to identify failure modes and provide concrete engineering recommendations, demonstrating its analytical utility.
AI.conclusion: Agent Cybernetics provides the missing theoretical scaffold for foundation agents, establishing a scientific foundation needed for principled, reliable real-world deployment. The framework opens a new research venue beyond empirical engineering approaches.

title: Diagnosing and Mitigating Domain Shift in Permission-Based Android Malware Detection
summary: Machine learning-based Android malware detectors often fail in real-world deployment due to domain shift, where models trained on one data source perform poorly on applications from another. This paper presents a comprehensive study on the generalizability and interpretability of permission-based detectors under cross-domain conditions. Using two complementary datasets (PerMalDroid and NATICUSdroid) and five ensemble classifiers, we first establish an intra-domain baseline, where models achieve over 92% accuracy, and then quantify a severe asymmetric performance drop. While models trained on PerMalDroid generalize well to NATICUSdroid (86% accuracy), the reverse direction sees a drastic drop to 73% accuracy. Explainable AI analysis reveals bimodal feature distributions and shows that feature importance is highly unstable, with key permissions losing or gaining influence across domains. The predictive feature sets for different domains are fundamentally mismatched, as models rely on different, dataset-specific permissions. Most importantly, an ablation study demonstrates that for most models, training on a noisy feature set leads to poor generalization, confirming that domain-specific artifacts are a greater obstacle than missing features. To mitigate this, we validate a hybrid training strategy based on the intersection of common features and successfully recover cross-domain performance, achieving 88% accuracy on PerMalDroid and maintaining 97% on NATICUSdroid. These findings highlight the importance of explainable, cross-domain-robust malware detection systems and provide a practical pathway toward improving real-world deployment of permission-based Android malware detectors.

AI.tldr: Permission-based Android malware detectors suffer from domain shift, with models trained on one dataset failing to generalize to another due to dataset-specific artifacts, but hybrid training on common features can recover performance.
AI.motivation: Machine learning-based Android malware detectors often fail in real-world deployment due to domain shift, where models trained on one data source perform poorly on applications from another source.
AI.method: Comprehensive study using two datasets (PerMalDroid and NATICUSdroid) and five ensemble classifiers, establishing intra-domain baselines, quantifying cross-domain performance, conducting explainable AI analysis, and performing ablation studies to identify causes of poor generalization.
AI.result: Asymmetric performance drop: models trained on PerMalDroid generalize to NATICUSdroid with 86% accuracy, but reverse direction drops to 73%. Explainable AI shows unstable feature importance and bimodal distributions across domains. Ablation study reveals domain-specific artifacts are the main obstacle. Hybrid training on common features recovers performance to 88% on PerMalDroid and maintains 97% on NATICUSdroid.
AI.conclusion: The study highlights the critical importance of explainable, cross-domain-robust malware detection systems and provides a practical pathway (hybrid training on common features) to improve real-world deployment of permission-based Android malware detectors by addressing domain shift issues.

title: MATRA: Modeling the Attack Surface of Agentic AI Systems -- OpenClaw Case Study
comment: Accepted for presentation at the 5th International Workshop on Designing and Measuring Security in Systems with AI (DeMeSSAI 2026), co-located with the 11th IEEE European Symposium on Security and Privacy (EuroS&P 2026), Lisbon, Portugal, July 10, 2026
summary: LLMs are increasingly deployed as autonomous agents with access to tools, databases, and external services, yet practitioners (across different sectors) lack systematic methods to assess how known threat classes translate into concrete risks within a specific agentic deployment. We present MATRA, a pragmatic threat modeling framework for agentic AI systems that adapts established risk assessment methodology to systematically assess how known LLM threats translate into deployment-specific risks. MATRA begins with an asset-based impact assessment and utilizes attack trees to determine the likelihood of these impacts occurring within the system architecture. We demonstrate MATRA on a personal AI agent deployment using OpenClaw, quantifying how architectural controls such as network sandboxing and least-privilege access reduce risk by limiting the blast radius of successful injections.

AI.tldr: MATRA is a practical threat modeling framework that helps assess how known LLM security threats translate into real-world risks in specific AI agent deployments, using asset-based impact assessment and attack trees to evaluate and quantify risk reduction through architectural controls.
AI.motivation: LLMs are increasingly deployed as autonomous agents with access to tools, databases, and external services, but practitioners lack systematic methods to assess how known threat classes translate into concrete risks within specific agentic deployments. There's a need for practical threat modeling tailored to AI agent systems.
AI.method: MATRA adapts established risk assessment methodology by starting with asset-based impact assessment and using attack trees to determine the likelihood of impacts occurring within specific system architectures. It systematically assesses how known LLM threats translate into deployment-specific risks.
AI.result: The framework was demonstrated on a personal AI agent deployment using OpenClaw, showing how architectural controls like network sandboxing and least-privilege access reduce risk by limiting the blast radius of successful injections. The approach quantifies risk reduction through these controls.
AI.conclusion: MATRA provides a pragmatic, systematic threat modeling framework that helps practitioners assess and mitigate risks in AI agent deployments by adapting traditional security methodologies to the unique challenges of LLM-based autonomous systems.

title: Spherical Boltzmann machines: a solvable theory of learning and generation in energy-based models
summary: Energy-based models (EBMs) are flexible generative architectures inspired by statistical physics, but their learning and generative properties remain poorly understood. Here, we analyze a solvable EBM in the high-dimensional limit: the spherical Boltzmann machine (SBM). Combining tools from random matrix theory and dynamical mean-field theory, we: solve exact equations describing the training dynamics of the SBM; compute the Bayesian evidence, which acts as a partition function in parameter space and encodes global properties of the trained model; and uncover cascades of phase transitions that occur both during training and as a function of hyperparameters, related to successive alignment and condensation of the top modes of the coupling matrix to the data. We connect these transitions to sampling-time generative phenomena in a teacher-student scenario, including: sampling temperature tuning, double descent as a function of regularization strength, tempered posterior effects, and out-of-equilibrium effects during training that induce biases in the trained model. We provide numerical evidence demonstrating that all these phenomena appear in standard generative architectures, beyond the SBM.

AI.tldr: Researchers solve exact training dynamics of spherical Boltzmann machines (SBM) using physics-inspired methods, revealing cascading phase transitions during training and connecting these to sampling phenomena in generative models.
AI.motivation: Energy-based models (EBMs) are flexible generative architectures inspired by statistical physics, but their learning and generative properties remain poorly understood. There's a need to develop analytical understanding of these models in high-dimensional settings.
AI.method: Analyze a solvable EBM - the spherical Boltzmann machine (SBM) in the high-dimensional limit. Combine tools from random matrix theory and dynamical mean-field theory to solve exact equations describing training dynamics, compute Bayesian evidence, and uncover phase transitions.
AI.result: Discovered cascades of phase transitions during training and as function of hyperparameters, related to successive alignment and condensation of top modes of coupling matrix to data. Connected these transitions to sampling-time generative phenomena including temperature tuning, double descent, tempered posterior effects, and out-of-equilibrium training biases.
AI.conclusion: Provides analytical framework for understanding EBMs, demonstrating that these phenomena appear in standard generative architectures beyond the SBM, offering insights into training dynamics and generative properties of energy-based models.

title: TrajPrism: A Multi-Task Benchmark for Language-Grounded Urban Trajectory Understanding
summary: Urban mobility is naturally expressed both as trajectories in space and as natural-language descriptions of travel intent, constraints, and preferences. However, prior work rarely evaluates these two modalities together on the same real-world trajectories: trajectory modeling often stays geometry-centric, while language-centric mobility benchmarks frequently target route planning and tool use rather than fine-grained, verifiable alignment between text and the underlying route. We introduce TrajPrism, a multi-task benchmark for language-trajectory alignment that unifies (i) instruction-conditioned trajectory generation, (ii) language-driven semantic trajectory retrieval, and (iii) trajectory captioning, together with an evaluation protocol that measures trajectory fidelity, retrieval quality, and language groundedness. We construct TrajPrism by pairing real urban trajectories with judge-filtered language annotations generated under a four-dimensional travel-intent taxonomy. The benchmark contains 300K selected trajectories across Porto, San Francisco, and Beijing, yielding 2.1M task instances from three instruction variants, three retrieval queries, and one caption per trajectory. We further develop proof-of-concept models for each task: TrajAnchor for instruction-conditioned trajectory generation, TrajFuse for semantic trajectory retrieval, and TrajRap for trajectory captioning. These models instantiate the proposed tasks and show that geometry-only trajectory baselines leave a large gap on our protocol, especially where language is part of the input-output interface. We release TrajPrism with code and a reproducible annotation pipeline that is designed to be portable across cities, given compatible trajectory inputs and map resources.

AI.tldr: TrajPrism is a multi-task benchmark for language-trajectory alignment that unifies trajectory generation, retrieval, and captioning tasks using real urban trajectories paired with language annotations.
AI.motivation: Previous work in urban mobility rarely evaluates spatial trajectories and natural-language descriptions together. Trajectory modeling stays geometry-centric, while language-centric benchmarks focus on route planning rather than fine-grained alignment between text and routes.
AI.method: Created TrajPrism benchmark with 300K real urban trajectories across three cities, paired with judge-filtered language annotations using a four-dimensional travel-intent taxonomy. Developed proof-of-concept models: TrajAnchor for trajectory generation, TrajFuse for retrieval, and TrajRap for captioning.
AI.result: The benchmark contains 2.1M task instances and shows that geometry-only trajectory baselines perform poorly on language-trajectory alignment tasks. The proof-of-concept models demonstrate the feasibility of the proposed tasks while highlighting gaps in existing approaches.
AI.conclusion: TrajPrism provides a comprehensive benchmark for evaluating language-trajectory alignment, revealing significant limitations in current geometry-only approaches when language is part of the input-output interface, with potential for portable application across cities.

title: Accelerating Zeroth-Order Spectral Optimization with Partial Orthogonalization from Power Iteration
summary: Zeroth-order (ZO) optimization has become increasingly popular and important in fine-tuning large language models (LLMs), especially on edge devices due to its ability to adjust the model to local data without the need for memory-intensive back-propagation. Recent works try to reduce ZO variance through low-dimensional subspace search, but subspace restriction alone leaves key optimization geometry under-exploited, motivating additional acceleration. In this work, we focus on the hidden layer training problem in which spectral optimizers like Muon outperform AdamW due to its ability to exploit weak spectral directions by orthogonalization. However, we have discovered that unlike in the first-order setting, full orthogonalization works poorly in the ZO setting since the gradient estimates are highly noisy and unreliable. To address this issue, we propose a key approach we call partial orthogonalization. To do so, we replace the iconic Newton-Schulz procedure in Muon with the faster, more concentrated power-iteration method so that it only amplifies dominant spectral directions. Furthermore, to improve the efficiency and generalization of the algorithm, we adopted a streaming variant of power-iteration that requires low variance in gradients, which was achieved through constraining our search inside a subspace obtained through the projection of momentum, echoing recent advances. Experiments on LLM fine-tuning show that our method can achieve from 1.5x to 4x the convergence speed of ZO-Muon, the current SOTA algorithm, across SuperGlue datasets in the OPT-13B model. Across different models, we also reach competitive final accuracies with less time in most cases compared with strong ZO baselines such as MeZO, LOZO and ZO-Muon. Code is available at https://github.com/MOFA-LAB/ZO-MOPI.git.

AI.tldr: ZO-MOPI: A zeroth-order optimization method using partial orthogonalization and power-iteration for faster LLM fine-tuning on edge devices, achieving 1.5-4x speedup over SOTA.
AI.motivation: Zeroth-order optimization is crucial for LLM fine-tuning on edge devices (avoids memory-intensive back-propagation), but existing ZO methods have high variance and under-exploit optimization geometry. Spectral optimizers like Muon outperform AdamW for hidden layer training but fail in ZO settings due to noisy gradient estimates.
AI.method: Proposes partial orthogonalization approach: replaces Newton-Schulz procedure in Muon with power-iteration method to amplify only dominant spectral directions. Uses streaming variant of power-iteration requiring low gradient variance by constraining search within momentum-projected subspace.
AI.result: Achieves 1.5x to 4x convergence speed improvement over ZO-Muon (current SOTA) on OPT-13B model across SuperGlue datasets. Reaches competitive final accuracies with less time compared to strong ZO baselines (MeZO, LOZO, ZO-Muon) across different models.
AI.conclusion: Partial orthogonalization effectively addresses noisy gradient issues in ZO optimization, enabling faster convergence while maintaining competitive accuracy for LLM fine-tuning, making it suitable for resource-constrained edge devices.

title: ComplexMCP: Evaluation of LLM Agents in Dynamic, Interdependent, and Large-Scale Tool Sandbox
summary:
Current LLM agents are proficient at calling isolated APIs but struggle with the "last mile" of commercial software automation. In real-world scenarios, tools are not independent; they are atomic, interdependent, and prone to environmental noise. We introduce $\textbf{ComplexMCP}$, a benchmark designed to evaluate agents in these rigorous conditions. Built on the Model Context Protocol (MCP), $\textbf{ComplexMCP}$ provides over 300 meticulously tested tools derived from 7 stateful sandboxes, ranging from office suites to financial systems. Unlike existing datasets, our benchmark utilizes a seed-driven architecture to simulate dynamic environment states and unpredictable API failures, ensuring a deterministic yet diverse evaluation.
  We evaluate various LLMs across full-context and RAG paradigms, revealing a stark performance gap: even top-tier models fail to exceed a 60% success rate, far trailing human performance 90%. Granular trajectory analysis identifies three fundamental bottlenecks: (1) $\textbf{tool retrieval saturation}$ as action spaces scale; (2) $\textbf{over-confidence}$, where agents skip essential environment verifications; and (3) $\textbf{strategic defeatism}$, a tendency to rationalize failure rather than pursuing recovery. These findings underscore the insufficiency of current agents for interdependent workflows, positioning $\textbf{ComplexMCP}$ as a critical testbed for the next generation of resilient autonomous systems.

AI.tldr: ComplexMCP: A benchmark for evaluating LLM agents on interdependent commercial tools with stateful environments and API failures, revealing significant performance gaps.
AI.motivation: Current LLM agents excel at isolated API calls but struggle with real-world commercial software automation where tools are interdependent, atomic, and affected by environmental noise.
AI.method: Introduces ComplexMCP benchmark built on Model Context Protocol (MCP) with 300+ tested tools from 7 stateful sandboxes. Uses seed-driven architecture to simulate dynamic environments and unpredictable API failures for deterministic yet diverse evaluation.
AI.result: Top LLMs fail to exceed 60% success rate (vs human 90%). Identifies three bottlenecks: tool retrieval saturation at scale, over-confidence in skipping verifications, and strategic defeatism (rationalizing failure over recovery).
AI.conclusion: Current agents are insufficient for interdependent workflows; ComplexMCP serves as critical testbed for developing next-generation resilient autonomous systems.

title: PACT: Peak-Aware Cross-Attention Graph Transformers for Efficient Storm-Surge Emulation
summary: Accurate and efficient storm-surge emulation is essential for coastal hazard assessment, yet high-fidelity hydrodynamic models remain too expensive for large scenario ensembles and rapid evaluation under heterogeneous climate forcings. We present PACT, a peak-aware cross-attention graph transformer for efficient station-level storm-surge prediction from atmospheric forcing fields. PACT represents each forcing patch as a graph, encodes spatial structure with GraphSAGE, and uses a learned station query to aggregate node information through cross-attention rather than uniform pooling. A Transformer encoder models temporal dependence across the forcing history, and a horizon-query decoder generates lead-specific forecasts from a shared temporal memory. To better capture extreme events, we introduce a peak-aware learning strategy that couples a lightweight auxiliary peak-aware head with a tailored training objective, including a tail-focused loss on peak-dominated samples and a horizon-wise slope regularizer to encourage coherent multi-step evolution. Across multiple tide-gauge stations along the US Northeast coast, PACT outperforms a strong spatio-temporal graph neural network baseline in both RMSE and MAE. Diagnostics show improved peak fidelity and tail preservation for reanalysis and most CMIP6 datasets. PACT is also computationally efficient, requiring about 3.5~s to generate a full winter-season surge trajectory for one year after training. Under distribution shift across five CMIP6 forcings, PACT transfers well within the CMIP6 family but degrades markedly when transferring from reanalysis to climate-model forcings, highlighting a persistent reanalysis--GCM gap.

AI.tldr: PACT is a graph transformer model that efficiently predicts storm surges at tide-gauge stations from atmospheric forcing fields, featuring peak-aware learning for better extreme event prediction and showing strong performance with computational efficiency.
AI.motivation: High-fidelity hydrodynamic models are too computationally expensive for large scenario ensembles and rapid evaluation needed for coastal hazard assessment under heterogeneous climate forcings.
AI.method: PACT uses a graph-based approach where each forcing patch is represented as a graph with GraphSAGE spatial encoding. It employs cross-attention with learned station queries, a Transformer encoder for temporal dependence, and a horizon-query decoder. The key innovation is a peak-aware learning strategy with auxiliary head and tailored training objectives including tail-focused loss and horizon-wise slope regularization.
AI.result: PACT outperforms strong spatio-temporal graph neural network baselines in RMSE and MAE across multiple US Northeast tide-gauge stations. It shows improved peak fidelity and tail preservation for reanalysis and most CMIP6 datasets, with computational efficiency (3.5 seconds per winter-season trajectory).
AI.conclusion: PACT provides accurate and efficient storm-surge emulation for coastal hazard assessment. It transfers well within CMIP6 family but shows significant degradation when transferring from reanalysis to climate-model forcings, revealing a persistent gap between reanalysis and GCMs that needs addressing.

title: PathISE: Learning Informative Path Supervision for Knowledge Graph Question Answering
summary: Knowledge Graph Question Answering (KGQA) aims to answer user questions by reasoning over Knowledge Graphs (KGs). Recent KGQA methods mainly follow the retrieval-augmented generation paradigm to ground Large Language Models~(LLMs) with structured knowledge from KGs. However, training effective models to retrieve question-relevant evidence from KGs typically requires high-quality intermediate supervision signals, such as question-relevant paths or subgraphs, which are time- and resource-intensive to obtain. We propose PathISE, a novel framework for learning high-quality intermediate supervision from answer-level labels. PathISE introduces a lightweight transformer-based estimator that estimates the informativeness of relation paths to construct pseudo path-level supervision. This supervision is then distilled into an LLM path generator, whose generated paths are grounded in the KG to provide compact evidence for inductive answer reasoning. ExtensiveISE experiments on three KGQA benchmarks show that PathISE achieves competitive or state-of-the-art KGQA performance, and provides reusable supervision signals that can enhance existing KGQA models, without relying on costly LLM-refined supervision signals. Our source code is available at https://anonymous.4open.science/r/PathISE-2F87.

AI.tldr: PathISE: A framework for KGQA that learns high-quality intermediate supervision from answer-level labels without requiring costly path-level annotations, using a transformer-based estimator to generate pseudo path-level supervision.
AI.motivation: Current KGQA methods using retrieval-augmented generation require expensive intermediate supervision signals (question-relevant paths/subgraphs) that are time- and resource-intensive to obtain, creating a bottleneck for training effective retrieval models.
AI.method: PathISE introduces a lightweight transformer-based estimator to estimate informativeness of relation paths and construct pseudo path-level supervision. This supervision is distilled into an LLM path generator, which generates KG-grounded paths providing compact evidence for inductive answer reasoning.
AI.result: Extensive experiments on three KGQA benchmarks show PathISE achieves competitive or state-of-the-art performance, and provides reusable supervision signals that enhance existing KGQA models without requiring costly LLM-refined supervision.
AI.conclusion: PathISE successfully addresses the supervision bottleneck in KGQA by learning high-quality intermediate supervision from answer-level labels alone, achieving strong performance while eliminating the need for expensive path-level annotations.

title: Predicting Plasticity in Deep Continual Learning: A Theoretical Perspective
summary: Deep continual learning requires models to adapt to new tasks without retraining from scratch. However, neural networks can lose their ability to adapt to new tasks after training on previous ones, a phenomenon known as loss of plasticity. There have been several explanations and diagnostics proposed for plasticity loss. Motivated by the philosophy that "all models are wrong, but some are useful", we ask: can existing diagnostics predict a neural network's plasticity? In this work, we take a practical view to interpret plasticity as trainability, i.e., a neural network's future optimization gain on a target task. We first take a theoretical approach, showing, by constructing a few counterexamples, that some widely adopted diagnostics of plasticity, including representation rank and neural tangent kernel rank, can fail to predict the loss of trainability in both regression and classification settings. We instead propose a novel metric, called optimization readiness, which combines gradient strength and gradient reliability. We prove that optimization readiness lower bounds one-step optimization gain under standard smoothness assumptions, providing a theoretical guarantee for its predictive power. Empirically, we show that across commonly used deep continual learning settings, such as Slowly-Changing Regression and Permuted MNIST, optimization readiness more reliably ranks checkpoints by trainability than prior diagnostics, even with substantially fewer samples.

AI.tldr: This paper introduces "optimization readiness" as a novel metric to predict neural network plasticity in continual learning, showing it outperforms existing diagnostics like representation rank and neural tangent kernel rank in predicting future trainability.
AI.motivation: Deep continual learning models suffer from "loss of plasticity" where they lose ability to adapt to new tasks after training on previous ones. Existing diagnostics for predicting plasticity loss have limitations, and the paper takes a practical approach by defining plasticity as trainability (future optimization gain on target tasks).
AI.method: The authors first demonstrate theoretically that popular diagnostics (representation rank and neural tangent kernel rank) can fail to predict loss of trainability through constructed counterexamples. They then propose "optimization readiness" as a new metric that combines gradient strength and gradient reliability. They prove it lower bounds one-step optimization gain under smoothness assumptions, providing theoretical guarantees. Empirical validation is conducted on deep continual learning benchmarks like Slowly-Changing Regression and Permuted MNIST.
AI.result: Optimization readiness more reliably ranks checkpoints by trainability compared to prior diagnostics across commonly used deep continual learning settings, even with substantially fewer samples. The theoretical analysis shows existing diagnostics can fail in both regression and classification settings, while optimization readiness provides provable guarantees.
AI.conclusion: Optimization readiness is an effective metric for predicting neural network plasticity in continual learning, addressing limitations of existing diagnostics and providing both theoretical guarantees and empirical validation. It offers a practical tool for assessing when networks can still adapt to new tasks.

title: Interpretable Machine Learning for Football Performance Analysis: Evidence of Limited Transferability from Elite Leagues to University Competition
summary: Machine learning has become increasingly prevalent in football performance analysis, yet most studies prioritize predictive accuracy while implicitly assuming that learned performance determinants and their interpretations are transferable across competition levels. Whether interpretability remains reliable under domain shift-from elite to university football remains largely unexplored. This study investigates whether performance determinants learned from elite competitions are structurally transferable to university-level football and whether their interpretations remain robust under domain shift. Models were trained on large-scale event data from the top five European leagues and applied to university football data from National Tsing Hua University (NTHU) using an identical feature space. Random Forest and Multilayer Perceptron models were interpreted using SHapley Additive exPlanations (SHAP) and Counterfactual Impact Score (CIS). Across five experiments, elite football exhibited a stable and consistent hierarchy of performance determinants across leagues, models, and explanation methods. In contrast, NTHU university football showed substantial reordering of key indicators, reduced explanation stability, weaker structural agreement with elite domains, and increased sensitivity to explanation method. These findings suggest that interpretability robustness is domain-dependent. Rather than reflecting methodological limitations alone, instability in explanations under domain shift may serve as a diagnostic signal of structural ambiguity in the target domain.

AI.tldr: Performance determinants learned from elite football are not structurally transferable to university-level football, with interpretability being domain-dependent and unstable across domain shifts.
AI.motivation: Most machine learning studies in football assume that learned performance determinants and their interpretations are transferable across competition levels, but this assumption remains largely unexplored, especially when moving from elite to university football.
AI.method: Trained models on large-scale event data from top five European leagues and applied them to university football data from NTHU using identical feature space. Used Random Forest and Multilayer Perceptron models interpreted with SHAP and Counterfactual Impact Score (CIS) across five experiments.
AI.result: Elite football showed stable hierarchy of performance determinants across leagues, models, and explanation methods. University football showed substantial reordering of key indicators, reduced explanation stability, weaker structural agreement with elite domains, and increased sensitivity to explanation methods.
AI.conclusion: Interpretability robustness is domain-dependent. Instability in explanations under domain shift may serve as a diagnostic signal of structural ambiguity in the target domain rather than just methodological limitations.

title: FactoryNet: A Large-Scale Dataset toward Industrial Time-Series Foundation Models
comment: 8 pages, 4 figures, 5 tables. Submitted to ICML 2026 Workshop on AI for Physics (AI4Physics)
summary: We introduce the first universal pretraining corpus for industrial time-series data: FactoryNet. 51M datapoints across 23k end-to-end task executions (13.3k real, 9.8k synthetic) on six embodiments, unified by a shared schema that enables robust zero-shot cross-embodiment transfer and highly parameter-efficient anomaly detection. We introduce a novel schema: Setpoint, Effort, Feedback, Context (S-E-F-C) underlying the whole pipeline that maps any actuated system into a common representational frame. The corpus spans 27 annotated anomaly types alongside healthy baselines and counterfactual pairs across robotic manipulation and machining domains. Cross-embodiment transfer experiments yield positive results: under bias-aware metrics our model demonstrates fair cross-embodiment transfer capabilities on the evaluated source-target pair, while 24 schema-aligned signals achieves competitive anomaly detection performance compared to high-dimensional baselines. We release FactoryNet as a growing, multi-embodiment dataset to drive progress toward industrial foundation models.

AI.tldr: FactoryNet is the first universal pretraining corpus for industrial time-series data with 51M datapoints across 23k task executions, featuring a novel S-E-F-C schema that enables cross-embodiment transfer and parameter-efficient anomaly detection.
AI.motivation: There's a need for universal pretraining corpora for industrial time-series data to enable foundation models for industrial applications. Current approaches lack standardized representations that allow for cross-embodiment transfer learning and efficient anomaly detection across different industrial systems.
AI.method: The authors introduce FactoryNet corpus with 51M datapoints across 23k end-to-end task executions on six embodiments, using both real (13.3k) and synthetic (9.8k) data. They propose a novel S-E-F-C (Setpoint, Effort, Feedback, Context) schema that maps any actuated system into a common representational frame. The corpus includes 27 annotated anomaly types with healthy baselines and counterfactual pairs.
AI.result: The model demonstrates fair cross-embodiment transfer capabilities under bias-aware metrics on evaluated source-target pairs. Using only 24 schema-aligned signals achieves competitive anomaly detection performance compared to high-dimensional baselines. The schema enables robust zero-shot cross-embodiment transfer and parameter-efficient anomaly detection.
AI.conclusion: FactoryNet represents a significant step toward industrial foundation models by providing a standardized, multi-embodiment dataset with a unified schema that enables transfer learning and efficient anomaly detection across different industrial systems. The authors release it as a growing dataset to drive progress in this field.

title: New AI-Driven Tools for Enhancing Campus Well-being: A Prevention and Intervention Approach
comment: PhD Dissertation, University of Missouri, May 2026
summary: Campus well-being underpins academic success, yet many universities lack effective methods for monitoring satisfaction and detecting mental health risks. This dissertation addresses these gaps through prevention (improving feedback collection) and intervention (advancing mental health detection), unified under an integrated framework. For prevention, we developed TigerGPT, a personalized survey chatbot leveraging LLMs to engage users in context-aware conversations grounded in conversational design and engagement theory, achieving 75% usability and 81% satisfaction. To address its limitations in repetitiveness and response depth, we introduced AURA, a reinforcement-learning framework that adapts follow-up question types (validate, specify, reflect, probe) within a session using an LSDE quality signal (Length, Self-disclosure, Emotion, Specificity), initialized from 96 prior conversations. AURA achieved +0.12 mean quality gain (p=0.044, d=0.66), with 63% fewer specification prompts and 10x more validation behavior. For intervention, we examine Expressive Narrative Stories (ENS) for mental health screening, showing BERT(128) captures nuanced linguistic features without keyword cues, while conventional classifiers depend heavily on explicit mental health terms. We then developed PsychoGPT, an LLM built on DSM-5 and PHQ-8 guidelines that performs initial distress classification, symptom-level scoring, and reconciliation with external ratings for explainable assessment. To reduce hallucinations, we proposed Stacked Multi-Model Reasoning (SMMR), layering expert models where early layers handle localized subtasks and later layers reconcile findings, outperforming single-model solutions on DAIC-WOZ in accuracy, F1, and PHQ-8 scoring. Finally, a cohesive framework unifies these tools, enabling adaptive survey insights to flow directly into specialized mental health detection models.

AI.tldr: This dissertation develops an integrated campus well-being framework combining TigerGPT/AURA for prevention through personalized surveys and PsychoGPT/SMMR for intervention via mental health detection, achieving high usability and performance improvements.
AI.motivation: Campus well-being is crucial for academic success but universities lack effective methods for monitoring satisfaction and detecting mental health risks. There's a need for both prevention (improving feedback collection) and intervention (advancing mental health detection).
AI.method: 1. Prevention: Developed TigerGPT (personalized LLM-based survey chatbot) and AURA (reinforcement learning framework adapting follow-up questions using LSDE quality signals). 2. Intervention: Explored Expressive Narrative Stories, developed PsychoGPT (LLM for distress classification based on DSM-5/PHQ-8), and proposed Stacked Multi-Model Reasoning (SMMR) to reduce hallucinations. 3. Integrated framework combining prevention and intervention tools.
AI.result: TigerGPT achieved 75% usability and 81% satisfaction. AURA achieved +0.12 mean quality gain (p=0.044, d=0.66) with 63% fewer specification prompts and 10x more validation behavior. BERT(128) captured nuanced linguistic features without keyword reliance. PsychoGPT/SMMR outperformed single-model solutions on DAIC-WOZ in accuracy, F1, and PHQ-8 scoring.
AI.conclusion: The integrated framework successfully addresses campus well-being gaps through prevention (improved feedback collection via conversational AI) and intervention (advanced mental health detection with reduced hallucinations), enabling adaptive survey insights to flow directly into specialized detection models.

title: A Tale of Two Problems: Multi-Task Bilevel Learning Meets Equality Constrained Multi-Objective Optimization
summary: In recent years, bilevel optimization (BLO) has attracted significant attention for its broad applications in machine learning. However, most existing works on BLO remain confined to the single-task setting and rely on the lower-level strong convexity assumption, which significantly restricts their applicability to modern machine learning problems of growing complexity. In this paper, we make the first attempt to extend BLO to the multi-task setting under a relaxed lower-level general convexity (LLGC) assumption. To this end, we reformulate the multi-task bilevel learning (MTBL) problem with LLGC into an equality constrained multi-objective optimization (ECMO) problem. However, ECMO itself is a new problem that has not yet been studied in the literature. To address this gap, we first establish a new Karush-Kuhn-Tucker (KKT)-based Pareto stationarity as the convergence criterion for ECMO algorithm design. Based on this foundation, we propose a weighted Chebyshev (WC)-penalty algorithm that achieves a finite-time convergence rate of $O(ST^{-\frac{1}{2})$ to KKT-based Pareto stationarity in both deterministic and stochastic settings, where $S$ denotes the number of objectives, and $T$ is the total iterations. Moreover, by varying the preference vector over the $S$-dimensional simplex, our WC-penalty method systematically explores the Pareto front. Finally, solutions to the ECMO problem translate directly into solutions for the original MTBL problem, thereby closing the loop between these two foundational optimization frameworks.

AI.tldr: First extension of bilevel optimization to multi-task setting with relaxed lower-level general convexity assumption, reformulated as equality constrained multi-objective optimization problem with new convergence analysis and algorithm achieving finite-time convergence rate.
AI.motivation: Most existing bilevel optimization works are limited to single-task setting and require strong lower-level convexity assumptions, which restricts applicability to complex modern machine learning problems. There's a need to extend bilevel optimization to multi-task learning scenarios with more realistic assumptions.
AI.method: Reformulate multi-task bilevel learning problem with lower-level general convexity into equality constrained multi-objective optimization problem. Establish new KKT-based Pareto stationarity as convergence criterion. Propose weighted Chebyshev-penalty algorithm that achieves O(S·T^{-1/2}) convergence rate to KKT-Pareto stationarity in both deterministic and stochastic settings.
AI.result: The WC-penalty algorithm achieves finite-time convergence rate O(S·T^{-1/2}) to KKT-based Pareto stationarity. By varying preference vectors, the method systematically explores the Pareto front. Solutions to ECMO problem directly translate to solutions for original MTBL problem.
AI.conclusion: Successfully extends bilevel optimization to multi-task setting under relaxed convexity assumptions, establishes theoretical framework for equality constrained multi-objective optimization, provides practical algorithm with guaranteed convergence rates, and creates bridge between multi-task bilevel learning and multi-objective optimization frameworks.

title: Reasoning Is Not Free: Robust Adaptive Cost-Efficient Routing for LLM-as-a-Judge
summary: Reasoning-capable large language models (LLMs) have recently been adopted as automated judges, but their benefits and costs in LLM-as-a-Judge settings remain unclear. Through controlled comparisons between reasoning and non-reasoning judges, we show that explicit reasoning substantially improves judgment accuracy on tasks requiring structured verification (e.g., math and coding), while offering limited or even negative gains on simpler evaluations and incurring significantly higher computational cost. These findings motivate that reasoning should be used selectively rather than universally, with awareness of possible distribution shift. We propose a Robust Adaptive Cost-Efficient Routing (RACER), which dynamically selects between reasoning and non-reasoning judges under a fixed budget by formulating routing as a constrained distributionally robust optimization problem. RACER explicitly accounts for distribution shift via a KL-divergence uncertainty set, admits an efficient primal--dual algorithm, and enjoys theoretical guarantees including uniqueness of the optimal policy and linear convergence. Extensive experiments show that RACER achieves superior accuracy--cost trade-offs under distribution shift.

AI.tldr: Explicit reasoning in LLM-as-a-Judge systems improves accuracy on complex tasks like math/coding but adds computational cost; selective use is needed, and RACER adaptively routes between reasoning/non-reasoning judges under budget constraints.
AI.motivation: While LLMs are increasingly used as automated judges, it's unclear when reasoning capabilities improve judgment accuracy and whether the computational cost is justified across different types of evaluation tasks.
AI.method: The paper proposes RACER (Robust Adaptive Cost-Efficient Routing), a framework that dynamically selects between reasoning and non-reasoning LLM judges under fixed budget constraints. It formulates routing as a constrained distributionally robust optimization problem using KL-divergence uncertainty sets to handle distribution shifts, with an efficient primal-dual algorithm.
AI.result: Experiments show explicit reasoning significantly improves accuracy on structured verification tasks (math, coding) but offers limited or negative gains on simpler evaluations while incurring higher computational cost. RACER achieves superior accuracy-cost trade-offs under distribution shift compared to static approaches.
AI.conclusion: Reasoning should be used selectively rather than universally in LLM-as-a-Judge settings. RACER provides an effective adaptive routing framework that balances accuracy and computational efficiency while handling distribution shifts, with theoretical guarantees on policy uniqueness and convergence.

title: Bridging Spectral Operator Learning and U-Net Hierarchies: SpectraNet for Stable Autoregressive PDE Surrogates
summary: Neural operators for time-dependent PDEs face a structural tension: spectral architectures (FNO and descendants) inherit exponential rollout-error growth from their one-step Lipschitz constant, while hierarchical U-Net operators trade resolution invariance for multi-scale detail. We introduce SpectraNet, an autoregressive neural operator that composes truncated spectral convolutions inside a U-Net hierarchy with a Residual-Target Spectral Block trained under a Semigroup-Consistency Loss. The residual-target parametrization replaces L^T stability blow-up with linear T*delta drift, and the spectral path's parameter count is Theta(L w^2 M^2), independent of grid N. Under a single unified protocol against 16 published neural-operator baselines on Navier-Stokes nu=1e-5 at 64x64, SpectraNet reaches test relative L2 = 0.0822 at 2.04M parameters -- 2.33x fewer than canonical FNO at ~20% lower error -- and wins five of six rows in a cross-PDE comparison against FNO (NS at nu in {1e-4, 1e-3}, PDEBench Shallow-Water 2D and Diffusion-Reaction, with the Active-Matter row going to FNO inside its seed spread). Trained from scratch at native 128^2 under the same protocol, SpectraNet improves to 0.0724 while FNO regresses to 0.3080. Free rollout stays bounded for T=100 where FNO diverges across all 200 test trajectories. On consumer CPU at B=1, SpectraNet runs sub-200ms while the full-attention Transformer that wins raw L2 pays ~60x latency; we do not claim to beat that Transformer on raw L2, only to dominate the lightweight (<=5M parameter, sub-200ms CPU) Pareto frontier. Source code: https://github.com/Enrikkk/spectranet

AI.tldr: SpectraNet is a novel neural operator combining spectral convolutions with U-Net hierarchy for time-dependent PDEs, achieving better accuracy with fewer parameters than FNO while maintaining fast inference.
AI.motivation: Current neural operators face structural tension: spectral architectures (like FNO) suffer from exponential error growth due to large one-step Lipschitz constants, while hierarchical U-Net operators sacrifice resolution invariance for multi-scale detail.
AI.method: SpectraNet uses an autoregressive neural operator that composes truncated spectral convolutions inside a U-Net hierarchy with a Residual-Target Spectral Block trained under a Semigroup-Consistency Loss. This architecture ensures linear T*delta drift instead of stability blow-up.
AI.result: SpectraNet achieves test relative L2 = 0.0822 at 2.04M parameters (2.33x fewer than FNO with ~20% lower error), wins 5 of 6 cross-PDE comparisons against FNO, maintains bounded rollout for T=100 where FNO diverges, and runs sub-200ms on consumer CPU.
AI.conclusion: SpectraNet dominates the lightweight Pareto frontier (≤5M parameters, sub-200ms CPU) for time-dependent PDEs by combining spectral efficiency with hierarchical structure, outperforming existing neural operators in accuracy-parameter-latency tradeoffs.

title: NanoResearch: Co-Evolving Skills, Memory, and Policy for Personalized Research Automation
summary: LLM-powered multi-agent systems can now automate the full research pipeline from ideation to paper writing, but a fundamental question remains: automation for whom? Researchers operate under different resource configurations, hold different methodological preferences, and target different output formats. A system that produces uniform outputs regardless of these differences will systematically under-serve every individual user, making personalization a precondition for research automation to be genuinely usable. However, achieving it requires three capabilities that current systems lack: accumulating reusable procedural knowledge across projects, retaining user-specific experience across sessions, and internalizing implicit preferences that resist explicit formalization. We propose NanoResearch, a multi-agent framework that addresses these gaps through tri-level co-evolution. A skill bank distills recurring operations into compact procedural rules reusable across projects. A memory module maintains user- and project-specific experience that grounds planning decisions in each user's research history. A label-free policy learning converts free-form feedback into persistent parameter updates of the planner, reshaping subsequent coordination. These three layers co-evolve: reliable skills produce richer memory, richer memory informs better planning, and preference internalization continuously realigns the loop to each user. Extensive experiments demonstrate that NanoResearch delivers substantial gains over state-of-the-art AI research systems, and progressively refines itself to produce better research at lower cost over successive cycles.

AI.tldr: NanoResearch is a personalized multi-agent AI research framework that adapts to individual users through tri-level co-evolution of skills, memory, and policy learning, outperforming existing AI research systems.
