DARE demonstrates superior performance by co-evolving difficulty estimation with policy learning and applying tailored strategies across difficulty tiers, achieving better trade-offs between training efficiency, final performance, and inference efficiency.

title: Practical Scaling Laws: Converting Compute into Performance in a Data-Constrained World
summary: The scaling laws guiding modern model training were calibrated for a single regime: data-rich, single-epoch pretraining. The dominant such scaling law form, Chinchilla's $L = E + A/N^α+ B/D^β$, has three structural limitations outside that regime: it diverges as unique data shrinks instead of saturating at the uninformed baseline; it cannot represent overfitting when capacity exceeds the data; and it conflates total examples seen with unique examples available. We propose a closed-form extension, $L(N, D, T) = E + (L_0 - E)\,h/(1+h)$ with $h = a/N^α+ b/T^β+ c\,N^γ/D^δ$, that decomposes loss into undercapacity, undertraining, and overfitting terms. It saturates between the irreducible loss $E$ and an uninformed baseline $L_0$ fixed by the loss type, and reduces to Chinchilla in the data-rich, single-epoch limit. We validate it on four multi-epoch experiments spanning four architecture families (MLPs, ResNets, Fourier neural operators, and transformers) across vision, scientific ML, and language domains, and refit it to five published LLM scaling-law grids. Extrapolating to higher compute and larger unique data than seen at fit time, our form achieves state-of-the-art RMSE on every published LLM grid we evaluate and on most cells of our constructed experiments. Once calibrated, the form admits a cost-aware allocation that recovers Chinchilla's optimum when data is free and shifts toward smaller corpora and more epochs as data grows expensive.

AI.tldr: The paper proposes an improved scaling law formula that addresses limitations of Chinchilla's law by handling multi-epoch training, overfitting, and data scarcity scenarios.
AI.motivation: Chinchilla's scaling law has three limitations outside data-rich, single-epoch pretraining: it diverges as unique data shrinks instead of saturating at baseline, can't represent overfitting when capacity exceeds data, and conflates total examples seen with unique examples available.
AI.method: Proposed a closed-form extension: L(N,D,T) = E + (L₀-E)h/(1+h) with h = a/N^α + b/T^β + c N^γ/D^δ, decomposing loss into undercapacity, undertraining, and overfitting terms.
AI.result: Validated on four multi-epoch experiments across MLPs, ResNets, Fourier neural operators, and transformers in vision, scientific ML, and language domains. Refitted to five published LLM scaling-law grids. Achieved state-of-the-art RMSE on every published LLM grid and most experimental cells.
AI.conclusion: The new scaling law addresses Chinchilla's limitations, reduces to Chinchilla in data-rich single-epoch limit, enables cost-aware allocation that recovers Chinchilla's optimum when data is free but shifts toward smaller corpora and more epochs as data grows expensive.

title: On Characterizing Learnability for Adversarial Noisy Bandits
summary: We study adversarial noisy bandits given a known function class $\mathcal{F}$. In each round, the adversary selects a function $f \in \mathcal{F}$, the learner chooses an arm, and then observes a noisy reward determined by the chosen arm and the function $f$. The goal is to minimize the cumulative regret $R(T)$, defined as the difference between the learner's performance and that of the best fixed arm in hindsight over $T$ rounds. We say that a function class $\mathcal{F}$ is learnable if there exists an algorithm achieving sublinear regret. Our main results concern characterizing learnability. The main quantity appearing in our characterization is a convexified variant of the generalized maximin volume introduced by Hanneke and Wang (2025). For oblivious adversaries, we characterize learnability in terms of this convexified generalized maximin volume. For adaptive adversaries, we show that the same quantity characterizes learnability when the arm space is countable. Our analysis builds on a connection between convexified generalized maximin volume and the existence of simple hitting sets. We further conjecture that the same quantity also characterizes learnability when the arm space is uncountable, via its relation to a new complexity measure, which we call the distribution covering number. This notion can be viewed as a strengthened form of the hitting set that still admits efficient learning via the multiplicative weights algorithm. We also pose a number of relevant open questions regarding this problem.

AI.tldr: The paper characterizes learnability in adversarial noisy bandits through a convexified variant of generalized maximin volume, establishing conditions for sublinear regret against both oblivious and adaptive adversaries.
AI.motivation: To understand when function classes are learnable in adversarial noisy bandit settings, where the adversary selects functions from a known class and the learner observes noisy rewards, with the goal of achieving sublinear cumulative regret.
AI.method: The analysis builds on connecting convexified generalized maximin volume with the existence of simple hitting sets. For adaptive adversaries with countable arm spaces, the same quantity characterizes learnability. The paper also introduces distribution covering numbers as a new complexity measure for handling uncountable arm spaces.
AI.result: For oblivious adversaries, learnability is characterized by convexified generalized maximin volume. For adaptive adversaries, the same characterization holds when the arm space is countable. A conjecture suggests this extends to uncountable arm spaces via distribution covering numbers, which are strengthened hitting sets enabling efficient learning through multiplicative weights algorithms.
AI.conclusion: The convexified generalized maximin volume is the key quantity characterizing learnability in adversarial noisy bandits across different adversary types and arm space cardinalities, with distribution covering numbers proposed as an extension mechanism and several open questions raised for future research.

title: LBI: Parallel Scan Backpropagation via Latent Bounded Interfaces
summary: Backpropagation is inherently sequential across depth, creating an $O(K)$-deep dependency chain that bottlenecks parallel training. While parallel-scan formulations theoretically reduce this depth to $O(\log K)$, they are computationally prohibitive for modern architectures due to the $O(d^3)$ cost of composing full-rank $d\times d$ Jacobians over the entire hidden state. We introduce Latent Bounded Interfaces (LBI), an algorithmic formulation that makes scan-based backpropagation tractable by restricting inter-region communication to a low-dimensional latent interface, $ m_k \in \mathbb{R}^{r}$, where $r \ll d$. This reduces the adjoint recursion to a suffix scan over $r \times r$ Jacobians, cutting per-combine cost from $O(d^3)$ to $O(r^3)$ while preserving exact gradients under the bounded-interface model. We demonstrate that LBI maintains model quality across four architectures (Mamba-2, Mamba-3, Transformer, and a Mamba--Transformer hybrid) at 47--61M block parameters. Interfaces of dimension $r=16$ suffice to preserve training quality within 0.16--0.35 cross entropy of dense baselines. The resulting framework provides an algorithmic foundation for region-parallel training, reducing cross-device backward communication to a single scan over $K$ fixed-size matrices, of approximately 56 KB for our experimental configurations.

AI.tldr: LBI enables efficient parallel backpropagation by restricting inter-region communication to low-dimensional latent interfaces, reducing computation cost from O(d³) to O(r³) while maintaining exact gradients.
AI.motivation: Standard backpropagation has O(K)-deep sequential dependency that bottlenecks parallel training. Existing parallel-scan approaches with O(log K) depth are computationally prohibitive due to O(d³) Jacobian composition costs over full d×d hidden states.
AI.method: Introduces Latent Bounded Interfaces (LBI) that restrict inter-region communication to low-dimensional latent interfaces m_k ∈ ℝ^r where r ≪ d. This reduces adjoint recursion to suffix scan over r×r Jacobians instead of d×d Jacobians.
AI.result: LBI maintains model quality across four architectures (Mamba-2, Mamba-3, Transformer, and hybrid) at 47-61M parameters. Interfaces of dimension r=16 preserve training quality within 0.16-0.35 cross entropy of dense baselines. Reduces cross-device backward communication to single scan over K fixed-size matrices (~56KB).
AI.conclusion: LBI provides tractable algorithmic foundation for region-parallel training by enabling efficient scan-based backpropagation through bounded-dimensional interfaces, overcoming computational bottlenecks of traditional parallel approaches while preserving exact gradients.

title: TSNN: A Non-parametric and Interpretable Framework for Traffic Time Series Forecasting
comment: Accepted by IEEE Transactions on Knowledge and Data Engineering
summary: Although many complex models were proposed to analyze time series data, some studies have demonstrated remarkable performance with simpler structures. A recent study proposed a non-parametric framework for 3D point cloud classification, which has the potential to be adapted for time series forecasting and enable interpretability. Inspired by the previous works, we present TSNN, a non-parametric and interpretable framework for traffic time series forecasting. TSNN consists of multiple layers that decouple the time series by matching the entries in a memory bank, where the memory bank is constructed using a similar matching process within the training set. It leverages the periodicity in traffic data to enhance forecasting accuracy while maintaining a simple model architecture. The proposed model operates without trainable parameters, preserving its inherent interpretability. In the experiments, TSNN achieves competitive performance compared to the typical deep learning models in four real-world traffic flow datasets. We also visualize the decoupling process to show the effectiveness of the components. Finally, we demonstrate the interpretability of the model and illustrate the contribution of each time step within the memory bank.

AI.tldr: TSNN is a non-parametric, interpretable framework for traffic time series forecasting that uses memory bank matching and leverages traffic periodicity without trainable parameters.
AI.motivation: While many complex models exist for time series analysis, simpler structures can perform remarkably well. Inspired by non-parametric 3D point cloud classification that enables interpretability, the authors aim to develop an interpretable framework for traffic forecasting that leverages periodicity patterns in traffic data.
AI.method: TSNN consists of multiple layers that decouple time series by matching entries in a memory bank. The memory bank is constructed using similar matching processes within the training set. The framework operates without trainable parameters, maintaining simplicity and interpretability while leveraging traffic periodicity.
AI.result: TSNN achieves competitive performance compared to typical deep learning models on four real-world traffic flow datasets. Visualization of the decoupling process demonstrates effectiveness, and interpretability analysis shows the contribution of each time step within the memory bank.
AI.conclusion: The proposed non-parametric TSNN framework provides competitive forecasting performance while maintaining interpretability through its memory bank matching approach, making it suitable for traffic time series analysis where understanding model decisions is important.

title: Rethinking Ratio-Based Trust Regions for Policy Optimization in Multi-Agent Reinforcement Learning
summary: Centralized training with decentralized execution (CTDE) is a standard framework for cooperative multi-agent policy-gradient reinforcement learning, allowing agents to learn from joint information while acting from local observations. Ratio-based trust-region methods such as Multi-Agent Proximal Policy Optimization (MAPPO) and Multi-Agent Simple Policy Optimization (MASPO) update decentralized actors using per-agent probability ratios weighted by joint advantage estimates. Teammate non-stationarity increases the variance of these advantages, which in turn increases the variance in the local ratio updates. This exposes two method-specific failure modes: MAPPO's additive clipping removes gradients for outlier samples and weakens recovery from policy drift, while MASPO's soft quadratic penalty can allow probability collapse. We introduce Multi-Agent Ratio Symmetry (MARS), a novel policy optimization objective that replaces these additive ratio-based trust-region mechanisms with a multiplicatively symmetric geometric barrier. MARS preserves corrective gradients while assigning unbounded cost as probability ratios approach zero. Across 47 tasks spanning eight multi-agent environments, including novel JAX benchmarks PaxMen and AeroJAX, MARS matches or exceeds MAPPO and MASPO in aggregate environment-level performance. Ablations show that these gains arise from the geometry of the symmetric barrier rather than from flexible trust-region boundaries alone.

AI.tldr: MARS introduces a novel geometric barrier policy optimization method for multi-agent reinforcement learning that addresses ratio-based trust-region issues in existing methods like MAPPO and MASPO.
AI.motivation: Current CTDE (centralized training with decentralized execution) methods like MAPPO and MASPO suffer from failure modes due to teammate non-stationarity increasing variance in advantage estimates, leading to issues with gradient removal (MAPPO) or probability collapse (MASPO).
AI.method: MARS replaces additive ratio-based trust-region mechanisms with a multiplicatively symmetric geometric barrier that preserves corrective gradients while assigning unbounded cost as probability ratios approach zero.
AI.result: Across 47 tasks in 8 multi-agent environments including new JAX benchmarks PaxMen and AeroJAX, MARS matches or exceeds MAPPO and MASPO in aggregate environment-level performance.
AI.conclusion: The symmetric geometric barrier in MARS effectively addresses trust-region issues in multi-agent policy optimization, with performance gains stemming from the barrier geometry rather than just flexible trust-region boundaries.

title: Fast Rates for Offline Contextual Bandits with Forward-KL Regularization under Single-Policy Concentrability
summary: \emph{Kullback-Leibler} (KL) regularization is ubiquitous in reinforcement learning algorithms in the form of \emph{reverse} or \emph{forward} KL. Recent studies have demonstrated $ε^{-1}$-type fast rates for decision making under reverse KL regularization, in contrast to the standard $ε^{-2}$-type sample complexity. However, for forward-KL-regularized objectives, existing statistical analyses are either not applicable or result in $\tilde{O}(ε^{-2})$ slow rates. We take the first step towards addressing this problem via a streamlined analysis of forward-KL-regularized offline CBs. We give the first $\tilde{O}(ε^{-1})$ upper bounds in tabular and general function approximation settings, both under notions of \emph{single-policy concentrability}. In particular, our convex-analytical pipeline unifies these settings by exploiting the pessimism principle in a novel way and completely bypasses the proof routines in previous works based on the mean value theorem, which might be of independent interest. Moreover, we provide rate-optimal lower bounds, manifesting the tightness of our upper bounds in terms of statistical rates. Our lower bounds also demonstrate that the forward-KL-regularized sample complexity recovers the unregularized slow rate in the low-regularization regime, similarly to the reverse-KL regularization.

AI.tldr: Forward-KL-regularized offline contextual bandits achieve fast $\tilde{O}(ε^{-1})$ sample complexity under single-policy concentrability, matching the rate previously known only for reverse-KL regularization.
AI.motivation: Previous research showed fast $ε^{-1}$ rates for reverse-KL regularization in reinforcement learning, but forward-KL-regularized objectives only had $\tilde{O}(ε^{-2})$ slow rates or no applicable analyses. The paper aims to address this gap by analyzing forward-KL-regularized offline contextual bandits.
AI.method: Uses a streamlined convex-analytical pipeline that exploits the pessimism principle in a novel way, completely bypassing previous proof routines based on the mean value theorem. The analysis works for both tabular and general function approximation settings under single-policy concentrability assumptions.
AI.result: First $\tilde{O}(ε^{-1})$ upper bounds for forward-KL-regularized offline contextual bandits in both tabular and general function approximation settings. Also provides rate-optimal lower bounds demonstrating tightness. Shows forward-KL regularization recovers unregularized slow rate in low-regularization regime.
AI.conclusion: Forward-KL regularization achieves fast $ε^{-1}$ statistical rates similar to reverse-KL regularization, with tight upper and lower bounds. The novel convex-analytical approach unifies different settings and provides new proof techniques that bypass previous methods.

title: The Pokémon Theorem and other Fairness Impossibility Results
summary:
Fairness impossibility results often look like distinct scalar incompatibility statements. We show that several share one RKHS geometry: fairness criteria are linear constraints on conditional mean embeddings, and unequal base rates make the law of total expectation overdetermine those constraints.
  This view yields four results. The Kleinberg--Mullainathan--Raghavan dichotomy needs only group-conditional unbiasedness, not full calibration. The \emph{Pokémon theorem} shows that a distinct group pair satisfying any finite collection of linear mean-fairness criteria leaves a residual violation witnessed by the MMD, decaying at the Kolmogorov $m$-width rate under spectral regularity. The same tools prove an impossibility for fair feature learning: parity and class-conditional separation in representation space force class collapse under unequal base rates. The approximate relaxations yield signal and error frontiers, allowing a trade-off between real-world estimators and fairness goals. Experiments on standard fairness benchmarks are consistent with our bounds.

AI.tldr: Several fairness impossibility results share a unified RKHS geometry perspective where fairness criteria are linear constraints on conditional mean embeddings, revealing fundamental trade-offs under unequal base rates.
AI.motivation: Fairness impossibility results appear as distinct scalar incompatibility statements. The paper aims to unify these under a common RKHS (Reproducing Kernel Hilbert Space) geometry framework, showing they share the same underlying mathematical structure where fairness criteria act as linear constraints on conditional mean embeddings.
AI.method: Uses RKHS theory and conditional mean embeddings to represent fairness criteria as linear constraints. Applies law of total expectation analysis, examines group-conditional unbiasedness, and employs MMD (Maximum Mean Discrepancy) to measure residual fairness violations. Uses spectral regularity conditions and Kolmogorov m-width rates for theoretical analysis.
AI.result: Four main results: 1) Shows Kleinberg-Mullainathan-Raghavan dichotomy only needs group-conditional unbiasedness, not full calibration; 2) "Pokémon theorem" shows any finite collection of linear mean-fairness criteria leaves residual violation measured by MMD; 3) Impossibility for fair feature learning - parity and class-conditional separation force class collapse under unequal base rates; 4) Approximate relaxations yield signal-error trade-off frontiers for practical estimators.
AI.conclusion: The paper establishes a unified RKHS geometry framework that reveals fundamental trade-offs between fairness criteria under unequal base rates. This theoretical perspective explains multiple known fairness impossibilities and provides analytical tools to understand the trade-off between real-world estimators and fairness goals, with experimental validation on standard benchmarks.

title: SMIXAE: Towards Unsupervised Manifold Discovery in Language Models
comment: 20 pages, 10 figures, 11 tables. Submitted to Mechanistic Interpretability Workshop, ICML 2026
summary: Sparse autoencoders (SAEs) have been used widely to decompose and interpret neural network activations, especially those of transformer language models. One key issue with SAEs is their inability to directly model multidimensional features. Instead, SAEs may tile such features by a set of independent directions that must be grouped together after the SAE training phase, impeding discoverability and interpretation of learned feature representations. We begin to address this issue by introducing the Sparse MIXture of Autoencoders (SMIXAE) architecture. Empirically, we provide evidence that SMIXAE models have success both in directly learning previously identified manifold structures, as well as finding novel structures, within the open source Gemma 2 2B and 9B models. Finally, we discuss several limitations and point towards areas for future work.

AI.tldr: Sparse MIXture of Autoencoders (SMIXAE) architecture proposed to directly model multidimensional features in neural networks, addressing limitations of current sparse autoencoders that tile such features across independent directions.
AI.motivation: Current sparse autoencoders (SAEs) cannot directly model multidimensional features, instead tiling them across independent directions that must be grouped post-training, which hinders feature discovery and interpretation in transformer language models.
AI.method: Introducing SMIXAE (Sparse MIXture of Autoencoders) architecture that can directly learn multidimensional features without the post-training grouping required by traditional SAEs.
AI.result: Empirical evidence shows SMIXAE successfully learns previously identified manifold structures and discovers novel structures within Gemma 2 2B and 9B transformer language models.
AI.conclusion: SMIXAE addresses key limitations of SAEs for feature interpretation, though the paper acknowledges limitations and suggests directions for future work to further improve multidimensional feature modeling.

title: ProactBench: Beyond What The User Asked For
summary:
Most LLM benchmarks score how well a model responds to explicit requests. They leave unmeasured a different conversational ability: noticing and acting on needs the user has implied but not said. We call this \emph{conversational proactivity}. ProactBench decomposes it into three phase-tied types: \textsc{Emergent}, inference from a single disclosed anchor; \textsc{Critical}, synthesis across multiple anchors; and \textsc{Recovery}, grounded forward-looking value after task completion.
  We operationalise the benchmark with three agents: a Planner, a User Agent, and an Assistant Model. Their information asymmetries defend against style-confounded scoring, rubric leakage, external-context contamination, and information dumps. The released corpus contains 198 curated dialogues with 624 trigger points across 24 communication styles drawn from a psychometric inventory and audited by an independent LLM judge. Across 16 frontier and open-weight models, \textsc{Recovery} is both difficult and weakly predicted by six standard benchmarks, making it a useful new evaluation signal.

AI.tldr: ProactBench is a new benchmark that evaluates conversational proactivity - when LLMs notice and act on implied user needs rather than just responding to explicit requests, decomposed into Emergent, Critical, and Recovery phases.
AI.motivation: Most LLM benchmarks only measure how well models respond to explicit requests, leaving unmeasured the ability to notice and act on implied user needs - what the authors call "conversational proactivity." Current benchmarks fail to capture this important conversational ability.
AI.method: ProactBench decomposes conversational proactivity into three phase-tied types: Emergent (inference from single disclosed anchor), Critical (synthesis across multiple anchors), and Recovery (grounded forward-looking value after task completion). The benchmark uses three agents (Planner, User Agent, and Assistant Model) with information asymmetries to prevent style-confounded scoring, rubric leakage, external-context contamination, and information dumps. The corpus contains 198 curated dialogues with 624 trigger points across 24 communication styles from psychometric inventory, audited by independent LLM judge.
AI.result: Across 16 frontier and open-weight models, the Recovery phase was found to be particularly difficult and weakly predicted by six standard benchmarks, making it a useful new evaluation signal that captures different capabilities than existing benchmarks.
AI.conclusion: Conversational proactivity is a distinct and important capability that current benchmarks fail to measure, and ProactBench provides a valid approach to evaluate this ability, with Recovery phase serving as a particularly valuable new signal for model evaluation.

title: On Variance Reduction in Learning Mean Flows
summary: One-step generative modeling has emerged as a leading approach to amortize the inference cost of diffusion and flow-matching models. Among distillation-free methods, MeanFlow training is notoriously unstable, with non-decreasing loss and unbounded gradient variance. In this work, we establish a theory that attributes this pathology to a misuse of the conditional velocity field: it plays two distinct statistical roles in the loss, both as an unbiased regression target and as a Monte Carlo control variate inside a Jacobi-vector product, with the original loss assigning the wrong coefficient to the latter. We derive the optimal coefficient in closed form, and show that a family of fixes in concurrent works corresponds to different practical realizations of the same optimum. A controlled sweep of this coefficient on two-dimensional benchmarks and on a latent Diffusion Transformer recovers the predicted bias-variance ordering. The optimal coefficient yields up to a %54 improvement in sample quality on two-dimensional benchmarks and a monotone FID trend at every matched-step DiT checkpoint. Crucially, the same DiT measurement also reveals a quantitative FID-MSE landscape mismatch: although gradient variance is minimized at an interior coefficient value, the coefficient that minimizes FID prefers the direct use of conditional velocity.

AI.tldr: The paper identifies and fixes instability in MeanFlow training for one-step generative models by deriving the optimal coefficient for the control variate term in the loss function, achieving significant sample quality improvements.
AI.motivation: MeanFlow training for one-step generative models is notoriously unstable with non-decreasing loss and unbounded gradient variance, which prevents effective distillation-free training of diffusion and flow-matching models.
AI.method: The authors establish a theory showing that the conditional velocity field is misused in the loss function - it serves both as an unbiased regression target and as a Monte Carlo control variate with incorrect weighting. They derive the optimal coefficient for the control variate term in closed form and show that concurrent fixes correspond to practical realizations of this optimum.
AI.result: Sweeping the coefficient on 2D benchmarks and a latent Diffusion Transformer recovers predicted bias-variance ordering. The optimal coefficient yields up to 54% improvement in sample quality on 2D benchmarks and monotone FID improvements at every DiT checkpoint. However, the coefficient that minimizes FID prefers direct use of conditional velocity, not the one that minimizes gradient variance.
AI.conclusion: The paper successfully identifies the root cause of MeanFlow instability and provides an optimal solution. However, it reveals a mismatch between minimizing MSE (gradient variance) and maximizing sample quality (FID), suggesting that sample quality metrics may favor simpler training objectives over variance-minimizing ones.

title: Intrinsic Muon: Spectral Optimization on Riemannian Matrix Manifolds
summary: Muon and related norm-constrained matrix optimizers have become central to large-scale learning problems. They are formulated as a linear maximization oracle (LMO) over an ambient matrix-norm ball in unconstrained Euclidean space. However, these do not generalize cleanly to manifold-valued parameters such as low-rank factorizations, orthogonality constraints, or symmetric positive definite (SPD) matrices. Naively restricting the Muon LMO to the tangent space (i) breaks quotient symmetries and (ii) couples the tangent-space constraint with an ambient norm bound, thereby obstructing closed-form solutions on various manifolds of interest. We resolve both issues with a single observation: every Riemannian metric canonically lifts a unitarily invariant Euclidean norm to an intrinsic norm on each tangent space, and the resulting intrinsic norm constrained LMO is symmetry preserving. Building on this, we introduce intrinsic Muon (iMuon), a unified framework that yields closed-form updates on the fixed-rank, SPD, Stiefel, and Grassmann manifolds for any unitarily invariant norm, including the spectral, Frobenius, and nuclear norms. We establish convergence guarantees for both deterministic and stochastic iMuon with rate constants that depend only on the manifold dimension. Notably, on the fixed-rank manifold this constant depends only on the rank, making the rate independent of factor conditioning and removing the runtime factor-rescaling required by prior work. Experiments on LoRA finetuning of LLMs, image classification, and subspace learning illustrate the efficacy of the proposed approach.

AI.tldr: The paper introduces iMuon, an intrinsic Muon optimization framework that extends norm-constrained matrix optimization to Riemannian manifolds while preserving quotient symmetries and enabling closed-form updates for various manifolds including fixed-rank, SPD, Stiefel, and Grassmann manifolds.
AI.motivation: Existing Muon optimizers work well for matrix optimization in Euclidean space but don't generalize cleanly to manifold-valued parameters (low-rank factorizations, orthogonality constraints, SPD matrices). Naive tangent-space restrictions break quotient symmetries and couple constraints, preventing closed-form solutions on important manifolds.
AI.method: iMuon uses a key observation: every Riemannian metric canonically lifts unitarily invariant Euclidean norms to intrinsic norms on tangent spaces, making the resulting norm-constrained linear maximization oracle (LMO) symmetry-preserving. This yields a unified framework with closed-form updates for various manifolds under any unitarily invariant norm (spectral, Frobenius, nuclear).
AI.result: The method provides convergence guarantees for deterministic and stochastic iMuon with rate constants that depend only on manifold dimension. On fixed-rank manifolds, the constant depends only on rank, making rates independent of factor conditioning and eliminating runtime factor-rescaling required by prior work.
AI.conclusion: iMuon is an effective framework for manifold optimization that preserves symmetries and enables efficient closed-form updates, demonstrated through applications in LoRA finetuning of LLMs, image classification, and subspace learning.

title: Sub-JEPA: Subspace Gaussian Regularization for Stable End-to-End World Models
summary: Joint-Embedding Predictive Architectures (JEPAs) provide a simpleframework for learning world models by predicting future latent representations.However, JEPA training is subject to a bias-variance tradeoff.Without sufficient structural constraints, excessive representationalvariance causes the model to collapse to trivial solutions.The recent LeWorldModel (LeWM) shows that this issue can be alleviated bysimply constraining latent embeddings with an isotropic Gaussian prior.However, latent representations inherently lie on low-dimensional manifoldswithin a high-dimensional ambient space, and enforcing an isotropic Gaussianprior directly in this ambient space introduces an overly strong bias.In this work, we propose ame, which seeks a favorable operatingpoint on the bias-variance frontier by applying Gaussian constraints inmultiple random subspaces rather than in the originalembedding space.This design relaxes the global constraint while preserving itsanti-collapse effect, leading to a better balance between trainingstability and representation flexibility.Extensive experiments across fourcontinuous-control environments demonstrate that consistentlyoutperforms LeWM with very clear margins.Our method is simple yet effective, and serves as a strong baseline for future JEPA-based world model research.fdefinedeeemodeThe code is available at https://github.com/intcomp/Sub-JEPA.

AI.tldr: Sub-JEPA: A method that applies Gaussian constraints in multiple random subspaces instead of the original embedding space to better balance bias-variance tradeoff in JEPA training, preventing collapse while maintaining representation flexibility.
AI.motivation: JEPA training faces a bias-variance tradeoff where without sufficient constraints, excessive representational variance causes collapse to trivial solutions. LeWM's solution of isotropic Gaussian prior in the original embedding space introduces overly strong bias since latent representations naturally lie on low-dimensional manifolds within high-dimensional spaces.
AI.method: Proposes Sub-JEPA which applies Gaussian constraints in multiple random subspaces rather than directly in the original embedding space. This design relaxes global constraints while preserving anti-collapse effects, seeking a favorable operating point on the bias-variance frontier.
AI.result: Extensive experiments across four continuous-control environments show that Sub-JEPA consistently outperforms LeWM with clear margins. The method provides a better balance between training stability and representation flexibility.
AI.conclusion: Sub-JEPA offers a simple yet effective approach that serves as a strong baseline for future JEPA-based world model research, addressing the bias-variance tradeoff through subspace constraints rather than direct embedding space constraints.

title: Improving Generalization by Permutation Routing Across Model Copies
summary: We introduce a use of the \(M\)-cover (or \(M\)-layer) transform for machine learning. The method replicates a model \(M\) times, but instead of coupling the copies through parameter averaging or an explicit attractive force, as in replicated SGD or Elastic SGD, it rewires the contexts in which local learning messages are computed. Each local loss is evaluated on a routed model whose parameters are drawn from different copies according to permutations sampled from a structured mixing kernel \(Q\). Training then uses the original local update rule, while the resulting learning messages are redistributed across the copies through these routed computational paths. Thus \(Q\) defines a topology for message transport and controls the long-loop structure of the lifted factor graph. We formulate this construction for perceptrons, committee machines, and multilayer perceptrons, showing that the same principle applies from discrete models to differentiable neural networks. The resulting framework provides a mechanism for improving generalization through structured message sharing rather than replica collapse or parameter-space coupling.

AI.tldr: A novel M-cover transform for machine learning that replicates models M times with structured message routing between copies rather than parameter averaging, enabling improved generalization through message sharing.
AI.motivation: To develop an alternative approach to model replication that goes beyond traditional methods like parameter averaging (replicated SGD) or attractive forces (Elastic SGD), focusing instead on structured message sharing between model copies to improve generalization.
AI.method: Replicates model M times and routes learning messages through a structured mixing kernel Q that defines permutations for combining parameters from different copies. Each local loss is evaluated on a "routed model" whose parameters are drawn from different copies according to sampled permutations. The original local update rule is maintained while learning messages are redistributed across copies through these computational paths.
AI.result: The M-cover transform framework provides a mechanism for improving generalization through structured message sharing rather than replica collapse or parameter-space coupling. The construction is formulated for perceptrons, committee machines, and multilayer perceptrons, showing applicability from discrete models to differentiable neural networks.
AI.conclusion: This approach offers a new paradigm for model replication that emphasizes message transport topology and long-loop structure in the lifted factor graph, potentially leading to better generalization through structured message sharing across model copies.

title: Memorize Theorems, Not Instances: Probing SFT Generalization through Mathematical Reasoning
summary: Supervised Fine-Tuning (SFT) is widely used for task-specific adaptation, yet recent work shows it systematically undermines reasoning generalization. We argue the root cause is not memorization itself, but its target: vanilla SFT drives models to exploit and memorize spurious surface correlations in problem-solution pairs, leaving them brittle to superficial input variations. To address this, we propose Theorem-SFT, which reorients supervision toward explicit theorem application by teaching models how rules are invoked rather than what answers look like. Theorem-SFT yields consistent gains across benchmarks and model families: +8.8% on MATH (LLaMA3.2-3B-Instruct) and +20.27% on GeoQA (Qwen2.5-VL-7B-Instruct) without modality-specific re-training. Fine-tuning MLP layers alone matches full-layers performance, implicating feed-forward components as the primary locus of reasoning rules. Our findings reframe the debate: Generalization failures stem not from memorization as a mechanism, but from memorizing the wrong inductive targets.

AI.tldr: Theorem-SFT improves reasoning generalization by teaching models to apply theorems rather than memorize surface patterns, achieving significant gains across benchmarks.
AI.motivation: Supervised Fine-Tuning (SFT) undermines reasoning generalization by causing models to memorize spurious surface correlations in problem-solution pairs, making them brittle to superficial input variations.
AI.method: Propose Theorem-SFT, which reorients supervision toward explicit theorem application by teaching models how rules are invoked rather than what answers look like. Fine-tunes only MLP layers to match full-layer performance.
AI.result: +8.8% improvement on MATH benchmark with LLaMA3.2-3B-Instruct and +20.27% improvement on GeoQA with Qwen2.5-VL-7B-Instruct without modality-specific re-training. Fine-tuning MLP layers alone matches full-layer performance.
AI.conclusion: Generalization failures stem not from memorization as a mechanism, but from memorizing the wrong inductive targets. Theorem-SFT provides a more robust approach by focusing on theorem application rather than surface pattern memorization.

title: Instance-Adaptive Online Multicalibration
summary: We study online multicalibration beyond the worst-case. We give a single, efficient algorithm which dynamically interpolates between benign and worst-case sequences by adaptively refining a dyadic grid of prediction values. Its error is controlled by the number of leaves in the refinement tree. Our analysis recovers the known $\widetilde O(T^{2/3})$ worst-case-optimal rate for online multicalibration, while simultaneously automatically adapting to easier instances: in the marginal stochastic setting it obtains a rate of $\widetilde O(\sqrt T)$, and for piecewise-stationary means with $J$ segments its rate is $\widetilde O(\sqrt{JT})$. More generally, the rate depends on a threshold-complexity measure of the predictable mean process relative to the group family. We show that this dependence is tight up to logarithmic factors.

AI.tldr: A single efficient algorithm for online multicalibration that adapts between worst-case and easier scenarios by dynamically refining a dyadic grid, achieving optimal rates for various settings including stochastic and piecewise-stationary sequences.
AI.motivation: The motivation is to develop an online multicalibration algorithm that goes beyond worst-case analysis and can automatically adapt to easier problem instances, bridging the gap between theoretical worst-case guarantees and practical scenarios where data may be more structured or benign.
AI.method: The method uses a single efficient algorithm that dynamically interpolates between benign and worst-case sequences by adaptively refining a dyadic grid of prediction values. The algorithm's error is controlled by the number of leaves in the refinement tree structure.
AI.result: The algorithm recovers the known $\widetilde O(T^{2/3})$ worst-case-optimal rate, automatically adapts to $\widetilde O(\sqrt T)$ rate in marginal stochastic settings, achieves $\widetilde O(\sqrt{JT})$ for piecewise-stationary means with J segments, and generally depends on a threshold-complexity measure of the predictable mean process. The dependence on threshold-complexity is shown to be tight up to logarithmic factors.
AI.conclusion: The paper presents a unified adaptive algorithm for online multicalibration that achieves optimal rates across diverse settings, with theoretical guarantees that show the dependence on threshold-complexity is nearly tight, providing a comprehensive solution that adapts to problem difficulty.

title: DiffATS: Diffusion in Aligned Tensor Space
summary: Direct diffusion modeling of high-resolution spatiotemporal fields is computationally challenging. Parameter-efficient primitives address this by representing high-dimensional data with a compact set of parameters. In this paper, we construct data-dependent tensor primitives without pretrained compression autoencoders. Our construction starts from Tucker decomposition, which captures low-rank multilinear structure through a core tensor and mode-wise factors. However, Tucker factors are non-unique: the same tensor can be represented by different rotated factors, which complicates generative modeling. We address this issue with orthogonal Procrustes (OP) alignment. Specifically, we select medoid anchor matrices from the data and align the factor matrices to resolve the gauge ambiguity. This yields matrix Grassmannian primitives and tensor Grassmannian primitives that are compact, data-adaptive, and directly decodable by explicit multilinear reconstruction. Theoretically, we prove that the proposed primitive maps are homeomorphisms between low-rank tensors and their corresponding primitive spaces, certifying that the representations are non-degenerate and topologically faithful. Building on these primitives, we propose *Diffusion in Aligned Tensor Space* (DiffATS), a generative framework that trains diffusion models directly on aligned tensor primitives. Across images, videos, and PDE solutions, DiffATS achieves strong unconditional and conditional generation performance while compressing original data by $3.9\times$ to $210\times$, without relying on any pretrained deep compression autoencoders.

AI.tldr: DiffATS: A generative modeling framework that constructs data-adaptive tensor primitives without pretrained autoencoders, using OP alignment to resolve Tucker decomposition ambiguities, enabling efficient diffusion models on high-resolution spatiotemporal fields.
AI.motivation: Direct diffusion modeling of high-resolution spatiotemporal fields is computationally challenging. Existing approaches often rely on pretrained compression autoencoders, which have limitations. The paper aims to develop parameter-efficient primitives for representing high-dimensional data without such dependencies.
AI.method: 1) Start from Tucker decomposition to capture low-rank multilinear structure. 2) Address Tucker factor non-uniqueness (gauge ambiguity) using orthogonal Procrustes (OP) alignment with medoid anchor matrices from data. 3) Construct matrix Grassmannian primitives and tensor Grassmannian primitives that are compact and directly decodable. 4) Propose DiffATS (Diffusion in Aligned Tensor Space) framework that trains diffusion models directly on these aligned tensor primitives.
AI.result: The proposed primitive maps are proven to be homeomorphisms between low-rank tensors and their primitive spaces, ensuring non-degenerate and topologically faithful representations. DiffATS achieves strong unconditional and conditional generation performance across images, videos, and PDE solutions, with data compression ratios from 3.9× to 210×, without relying on pretrained deep compression autoencoders.
AI.conclusion: The paper presents a novel approach to generative modeling of high-dimensional spatiotemporal data through data-adaptive tensor primitives. By addressing Tucker decomposition ambiguities via OP alignment, the method enables efficient diffusion modeling without dependency on pretrained compression networks, offering significant compression benefits while maintaining strong generation performance.

title: Uncertainty-Aware Token Importance Estimation in Spiking Transformers
summary: Spiking transformers have shown strong potential for neuromorphic vision, yet their token processing across multiple spiking steps still introduces substantial redundancy and inference cost. Existing token reduction methods mainly rely on response based cues, such as activation magnitude, firing statistics, or feature similarity. Although effective, these criteria do not explicitly characterize token importance from the perspective of temporally evolving class evidence. In spiking transformers, token representations are progressively formed across multiple spiking steps rather than determined at a single instant, suggesting that token importance should be evaluated not only by instantaneous responses but also by temporal uncertainty patterns. Our key observation is that tokens exhibit heterogeneous uncertainty trajectories over time, and that their temporally aggregated uncertainty statistics provide an effective cue for distinguishing informative tokens from redundant ones. Motivated by this, we propose Uncert, a training free and plug and play token importance estimation framework for spiking transformers. Specifically, Uncert models token wise class evidence with a Dirichlet distribution and summarizes each token temporal uncertainty using its mean and fluctuation across spiking steps, yielding an uncertainty aware importance score for token reduction during inference. Experiments on both static and neuromorphic benchmarks show that Uncert achieves favorable accuracy and efficiency tradeoffs, with the most consistent gains observed under token pruning. Further analysis reveals a clear empirical connection between temporal uncertainty patterns and token contribution, offering new insights into token dynamics in spiking transformers.

AI.tldr: Uncert: A training-free token reduction framework for spiking transformers that uses temporal uncertainty patterns (rather than just response magnitude) to identify important tokens across multiple spiking steps, achieving better accuracy-efficiency tradeoffs.
AI.motivation: Spiking transformers process tokens across multiple spiking steps, creating redundancy and high inference costs. Existing token reduction methods use simple response-based cues (activation magnitude, firing statistics) but don't consider how token importance evolves temporally. Since token representations form progressively across time in spiking transformers, importance should be evaluated using temporal uncertainty patterns, not just instantaneous responses.
AI.method: Proposes Uncert, a training-free framework that models token-wise class evidence with a Dirichlet distribution and computes importance scores based on two temporal uncertainty statistics: mean uncertainty across spiking steps and uncertainty fluctuation over time. This creates uncertainty-aware importance scores for token reduction during inference without retraining.
AI.result: Experiments on static and neuromorphic benchmarks show Uncert achieves favorable accuracy-efficiency tradeoffs, with most consistent gains under token pruning. Analysis reveals clear empirical connection between temporal uncertainty patterns and token contribution, providing new insights into token dynamics in spiking transformers.
AI.conclusion: Uncert successfully addresses spiking transformer redundancy by using temporal uncertainty patterns for token reduction, outperforming response-based methods and offering new understanding of token dynamics through temporal uncertainty analysis.

title: First Worst-Case Regret Bounds for Combinatorial Thompson Sampling in Sleeping Semi-Bandits
summary: We revisit combinatorial Thompson sampling (CTS) for semi-bandits with sleeping arms, where arm availability varies over time and actions must satisfy combinatorial constraints, as in wireless mesh routing with fluctuating link availability. Despite its practical relevance, CTS has been hindered by several long-standing problems: (i) the absence of worst-case regret guarantees in the semi-bandit setting even without sleeping arms, (ii) the lack of theory under adversarially varying availability, and (iii) the consistently weak empirical performance of CTS with Gaussian priors (CTS-G). This paper resolves these long-standing issues by providing the first worst-case regret analysis of CTS-G, proving an upper bound of $\tilde{O}(m\sqrt{NT})$ and a matching lower bound of $\tildeΩ(m\sqrt{NT})$. To bridge the gap between theory and practice, we further propose CL-SG, a simple CTS-G variant that samples a single shared Gaussian seed each round to coordinate exploration across arms. We show that CL-SG achieves an improved regret bound of $\tilde{O}(\sqrt{mNT})$, together with a matching lower bound $Ω(\sqrt{mNT})$. Experiments on real-world datasets demonstrate that CL-SG consistently outperforms strong baselines including CTS-G and CTS-B, and we open-source our implementation for reproducibility.

AI.tldr: The paper resolves long-standing issues with combinatorial Thompson sampling for semi-bandits with sleeping arms, providing worst-case regret guarantees and proposing CL-SG, a variant that achieves improved regret bounds with better empirical performance.
AI.motivation: CTS for semi-bandits with sleeping arms has practical applications like wireless mesh routing but suffers from three main problems: 1) no worst-case regret guarantees even without sleeping arms, 2) lack of theory under adversarially varying arm availability, and 3) weak empirical performance of CTS-G (Gaussian prior version).
AI.method: The paper analyzes CTS-G theoretically and proposes CL-SG, a variant that samples a single shared Gaussian seed per round to coordinate exploration across arms, enabling better correlation between arm estimates.
AI.result: For CTS-G: proved upper bound of $\tilde{O}(m\sqrt{NT})$ and matching lower bound $\tilde{\Omega}(m\sqrt{NT})$. For CL-SG: achieved improved regret bound $\tilde{O}(\sqrt{mNT})$ with matching lower bound $\Omega(\sqrt{mNT})$, plus empirical validation showing CL-SG outperforms CTS-G and CTS-B on real-world datasets.
AI.conclusion: The paper resolves major theoretical gaps in combinatorial Thompson sampling with sleeping arms, providing the first worst-case regret analysis for CTS-G and introducing CL-SG, a practically effective variant that bridges theory-practice gap with improved regret bounds and strong empirical performance.

title: TileQ: Efficient Low-Rank Quantization of Mixture-of-Experts with 2D Tiling
summary: Mixture-of-Experts (MoE) models achieve remarkable performance by sparsely activating specialized experts, yet their massive parameters in experts pose significant challenges for deployment. While low-rank quantization offers a promising route to compress MoE models, existing methods still incur nonnegligible memory overhead and inference latency. To address these limitations, we propose \textsc{TileQ}, a fine-tuning-free post-training quantization (PTQ) method that employs 2D-tiling structured low-rank quantization to share low-rank factors across both input and output dimensions of MoE experts. Furthermore, we introduce an efficient inference technique for \textsc{TileQ} that fuses multiple low-rank expert computations into a single-pass operation, significantly improving hardware utilization. Experiments show that \textsc{TileQ} cuts down additional memory usage up to 10$\times$ and reduces inference latency to $\sim$5\% while preserving state-of-the-art accuracy.

AI.tldr: TileQ is a post-training quantization method that uses 2D-tiling structured low-rank quantization to compress Mixture-of-Experts (MoE) models without fine-tuning, achieving significant memory and latency reductions while maintaining accuracy.
AI.motivation: Mixture-of-Experts (MoE) models achieve excellent performance but have massive parameters that create deployment challenges due to high memory overhead and inference latency. Existing low-rank quantization methods still incur nonnegligible memory and latency costs.
AI.method: TileQ employs a fine-tuning-free post-training quantization approach with 2D-tiling structured low-rank quantization that shares low-rank factors across both input and output dimensions of MoE experts. It also introduces an efficient inference technique that fuses multiple low-rank expert computations into a single-pass operation to improve hardware utilization.
AI.result: TileQ reduces additional memory usage up to 10×, cuts inference latency to approximately 5% of original, while preserving state-of-the-art accuracy compared to existing methods.
AI.conclusion: TileQ provides an effective post-training quantization solution for MoE models that significantly improves deployment efficiency through structured low-rank quantization and fused computation, making MoE models more practical for real-world applications.

title: Semi-Supervised Neural Super-Resolution for Mesh-Based Simulations
comment: International Conference on Machine Learning (ICML 2026) (to appear) (Please cite our conference version.)
summary: Mesh-based simulations provide high-fidelity solutions to partial differential equations (PDEs), but achieving such accuracy typically requires fine meshes, leading to substantial computational overhead. Super-resolution techniques aim to mitigate this cost by reconstructing high-resolution (HR), high-fidelity solutions from low-cost, low-resolution (LR) counterparts. However, training neural networks for super-resolution often demands large amounts of expensive HR supervision data. To address this challenge, we propose SuperMeshNet, an HR data-efficient super-resolution framework for mesh-based simulations aided by message passing neural networks (MPNNs). At its core, SuperMeshNet introduces complementary learning, a semi-supervised approach that effectively leverages both 1) a small amount of paired LR-HR data and 2) abundant unpaired LR data via two jointly trained, complementary MPNN-based models. Additionally, our model is enriched by inductive biases, which are empirically shown to further improve super-resolution performance. Extensive experiments demonstrate that SuperMeshNet requires 90% less HR data to achieve even lower root mean square error (RMSE) than that of the fully supervised benchmark without the inductive biases. The source code and datasets are available at https://github.com/jykim-git/SuperMeshNet.git.

AI.tldr: SuperMeshNet is a semi-supervised neural network framework for super-resolution in mesh-based PDE simulations that achieves accurate HR solutions with 90% less HR training data by leveraging both paired LR-HR data and abundant unpaired LR data.
AI.motivation: Mesh-based PDE simulations require fine meshes for high-fidelity results, which is computationally expensive. Existing super-resolution approaches need large amounts of expensive HR supervision data for training neural networks, creating a data efficiency challenge.
AI.method: Proposes SuperMeshNet with complementary learning - a semi-supervised approach using two jointly trained MPNN-based models that leverage 1) small paired LR-HR data and 2) abundant unpaired LR data. Incorporates inductive biases to further improve performance.
AI.result: SuperMeshNet achieves lower RMSE than fully supervised benchmarks while requiring 90% less HR data. The framework effectively reconstructs high-fidelity HR solutions from low-cost LR counterparts.
AI.conclusion: SuperMeshNet provides an HR data-efficient super-resolution solution for mesh-based simulations, significantly reducing computational training costs while maintaining or improving accuracy through complementary semi-supervised learning with MPNNs.

title: MC$^2$: Monte Carlo Correction for Fast Elliptic PDE Solving
summary: Partial differential equation (PDE) solvers underpin scientific computing, but real-world deployment is bounded by compute. Classical Monte Carlo solvers such as Walk-on-Spheres (WoS) are unbiased and geometry-agnostic but are slow. Learned solvers are fast but biased and brittle under distribution shift. We present \textbf{MC$^2$}, a hybrid WoS-Neural Network (WoS-NN) PDE solver that treats a low-budget Monte Carlo solution as a structured estimator of the true field and learns a single-pass neural correction to recover a high-fidelity solution. MC$^2$ matches the accuracy of solutions using over $1000\times$ more Monte Carlo compute, outperforming all evaluated classical, denoising, and neural-operator baselines. To enable reproducible study of finite-compute PDE solving, we additionally release \textbf{PDEZoo}, the largest standardized elliptic PDE benchmark to date: 2M PDEs spanning five elliptic families and unlimited geometric compositions, with analytic ground truth and multi-budget Monte Carlo trajectories. Together \textbf{MC$^2$} and \textbf{PDEZoo} (1) empirically establish that finite-sample Monte Carlo error is structured, learnable, and correctable in a single forward pass, (2) show that we can solve PDEs $\sim$\textbf{1000x} faster than with just WoS, and (3) provide the evaluation infrastructure the field has so far lacked.

AI.tldr: MC² is a hybrid WoS-neural PDE solver that combines cheap Monte Carlo with neural correction for 1000x faster, high-fidelity solutions; plus PDEZoo benchmark for reproducible evaluation.
AI.motivation: Existing PDE solvers face trade-offs: classical Monte Carlo (Walk-on-Spheres) is unbiased but slow, while learned solvers are fast but biased and brittle to distribution shift. There's a need for high-fidelity, fast PDE solvers and standardized evaluation infrastructure.
AI.method: MC² uses a hybrid approach: it takes a low-budget Monte Carlo solution as a structured estimator, then applies a single-pass neural network correction to recover high-fidelity solutions. Also introduces PDEZoo, a large standardized elliptic PDE benchmark.
AI.result: MC² matches accuracy of solutions using 1000× more Monte Carlo compute, outperforming classical, denoising, and neural-operator baselines. PDEZoo provides 2M PDEs across five elliptic families with analytic ground truth for standardized evaluation.
AI.conclusion: Finite-sample Monte Carlo error is structured, learnable, and correctable in a single forward pass, enabling ~1000× faster PDE solving. MC² and PDEZoo provide both superior solving capability and the evaluation infrastructure needed by the field.

title: From Regression to Inference: Meta-Learning Predictors for Neural Architecture Search
summary: Prediction-based approaches are widely used in neural architecture search (NAS), where a predictor estimates the performance of candidate architectures to guide selection. However, existing predictors are typically trained via supervised regression on limited samples, leading to overfitting and poor generalization to unseen architectures. In this work, we propose a fundamentally different formulation that models performance prediction as a conditional function inference problem using a Convolutional Neural Process (ConvNP) with meta-learning capabilities. Instead of fitting a fixed mapping to limited samples, our approach meta-learns to infer performance from partial observations by training with context-target splits across a group of synthesized tasks, explicitly optimizing for generalization under data scarcity and aligning the training procedure with the deployment setting in NAS. We further design simple yet effective meta-features for cell-based architectures and evaluate our method on NAS-Bench-101 and NAS-Bench-201. Extensive experiments show that our approach consistently improves top-K ranking quality and achieves the state-of-the-art architecture selection using limited samples.

AI.tldr: A novel performance prediction method for neural architecture search using Convolutional Neural Processes with meta-learning, achieving state-of-the-art ranking quality with limited samples.
AI.motivation: Existing NAS predictors suffer from overfitting and poor generalization because they're trained via supervised regression on limited samples, which doesn't align well with the deployment setting where models need to generalize to unseen architectures.
AI.method: Reformulates performance prediction as a conditional function inference problem using Convolutional Neural Process (ConvNP) with meta-learning. Instead of fitting a fixed mapping, it meta-learns to infer performance from partial observations by training with context-target splits across synthesized tasks, optimizing explicitly for generalization under data scarcity.
AI.result: Extensive experiments on NAS-Bench-101 and NAS-Bench-201 show the approach consistently improves top-K ranking quality and achieves state-of-the-art architecture selection using limited samples.
AI.conclusion: The ConvNP-based meta-learning approach provides a fundamentally different and effective formulation for NAS performance prediction that better addresses generalization challenges under data scarcity compared to traditional supervised regression methods.

title: dFlowGRPO: Rate-Aware Policy Optimization for Discrete Flow Models
summary: Discrete flow models (DFMs) are a class of flexible generative models for generating discrete data, and diffusion large language models (dLLMs) can be viewed as a special case with a specific choice of mixture path and a masked source distribution. While several recent works have explored reinforcement learning into dLLMs, its application to more general discrete flow models remains underexplored. In this work, we present discrete Flow-GRPO (dFlowGRPO), a unified reinforcement learning framework for discrete flow models that supports a broad family of probability paths and non-masked source distributions. We derive the full trajectory probability for DFMs and formulate denoising as a Markov decision process, enabling dFlowGRPO to incorporate information from both the associated conditional transition rates and the posterior model during reinforcement learning. We apply dFlowGRPO to FUDOKI, a recent multimodal discrete flow model, and evaluate it on both image generation and multimodal understanding tasks. Empirical results show that dFlowGRPO outperforms existing GRPO-type methods for dLLMs on text-to-image generation tasks and achieves performance competitive with continuous flow-based models trained using FlowGRPO, while also demonstrating strong capabilities on understanding tasks.

AI.tldr: dFlowGRPO is a unified RL framework for discrete flow models that extends beyond dLLMs to support diverse probability paths and non-masked source distributions, improving text-to-image generation and multimodal understanding.
AI.motivation: While RL has been explored for diffusion LLMs (dLLMs), its application to more general discrete flow models remains underexplored. Current methods are limited to specific mixture paths and masked source distributions used in dLLMs.
AI.method: dFlowGRPO derives full trajectory probability for DFMs and formulates denoising as an MDP, allowing incorporation of information from both conditional transition rates and posterior model during RL. The framework supports broad families of probability paths and non-masked source distributions.
AI.result: Applied to FUDOKI multimodal discrete flow model, dFlowGRPO outperforms existing GRPO-type methods for dLLMs on text-to-image generation and achieves competitive performance with continuous flow-based models trained using FlowGRPO. Also demonstrates strong capabilities on understanding tasks.
AI.conclusion: dFlowGRPO successfully extends RL to general discrete flow models beyond dLLMs, providing a unified framework that improves generation quality while maintaining strong multimodal understanding capabilities.

title: Towards Effective Theory of LLMs: A Representation Learning Approach
summary: We propose Representational Effective Theory (RET), a framework for describing large language model computation in terms of learned macrostates rather than microscopic details. RET learns these macrostates from hidden-state trajectories using a BYOL/JEPA-style self-supervised objective, coarse-graining activations into macrovariables that preserve higher-level structure relevant for prediction and interpretation. We evaluate whether these macrovariables are practically relevant for interpretability: RET yields temporally consistent states that reveal "mental-state" trajectories of reasoning, capture high-level semantic structure, support early prediction of behavioral outcomes such as sycophancy, and provide causal handles for steering generations toward interpretable computational phases. Together, these results suggest that LLM computation admits useful effective descriptions via RET: high-level, dynamically meaningful variables that support interpretation, prediction, and intervention.

AI.tldr: RET is a framework that learns high-level macrostates from LLM hidden-state trajectories using self-supervised learning, enabling interpretability through consistent mental-state trajectories that capture semantic structure and allow prediction/intervention.
AI.motivation: The paper aims to address the challenge of interpreting large language model computation by moving away from microscopic details of neural activations toward higher-level, meaningful descriptions that are practically useful for understanding model behavior.
AI.method: The authors propose Representational Effective Theory (RET), which learns macrostates from hidden-state trajectories using a BYOL/JEPA-style self-supervised objective. This approach coarse-grains activations into macrovariables that preserve higher-level structure relevant for prediction and interpretation.
AI.result: RET yields temporally consistent states that reveal "mental-state" trajectories of reasoning, capture high-level semantic structure, support early prediction of behavioral outcomes (e.g., sycophancy), and provide causal handles for steering generations toward interpretable computational phases.
AI.conclusion: LLM computation admits useful effective descriptions via RET: high-level, dynamically meaningful variables that support interpretation, prediction, and intervention. The framework bridges microscopic neural activations to interpretable computational phases.

title: Neural Cluster First, Route Second: One-Shot Capacitated Vehicle Routing via Differentiable Optimal Transport
summary: The Capacitated Vehicle Routing Problem (CVRP) underpins modern last-mile logistics. Current Neural Combinatorial Optimization (NCO) methods construct CVRP solutions autoregressively, inheriting sequential decoding bottlenecks, sensitivity to spatial symmetries, and brittle out-of-distribution behavior. We revisit the classical Cluster-First-Route-Second (CFRS) paradigm -- long known to be asymptotically optimal but largely overlooked by NCO -- and argue that it is structurally aligned with the core strengths of deep learning: similarity and assignment over global context, rather than the construction of long sequential tours. We introduce Neural CFRS, the first purely non-autoregressive one-shot neural CFRS framework for the CVRP. It enforces global fleet-capacity constraints end-to-end via a differentiable entropic Optimal Transport layer, producing a continuous transport plan to sparsify an exact capacitated assignment solver. We provide formal theoretical guarantees that our architecture intrinsically abstracts away $E(2)$ spatial, inter-route permutation, and intra-route traversal symmetries. By equipping the framework with a pre-trained spatial vocabulary, we unlock extreme parameter efficiency and zero-shot scaling. Designed primarily for real-world spatial distributions under a constant capacity setting, Neural CFRS scales robustly to out-of-distribution $N=1000$ instances with a < 4% gap -- retaining an approximate 5% gap at this scale even as an ultra-lightweight, single-layer architecture. Furthermore, when deployed out-of-the-box on standard benchmarks, we achieve a highly competitive 2.73% optimality gap on size-100 problems.

AI.tldr: Neural CFRS introduces a non-autoregressive, one-shot neural framework for the Capacitated Vehicle Routing Problem using a Cluster-First-Route-Second approach with differentiable optimal transport, achieving strong performance with parameter efficiency and symmetry guarantees.
AI.motivation: Current Neural Combinatorial Optimization methods for CVRP suffer from sequential decoding bottlenecks, sensitivity to spatial symmetries, and brittle out-of-distribution behavior. The authors argue that the classical Cluster-First-Route-Second paradigm is better aligned with deep learning's strengths in similarity and assignment over global context.
AI.method: Neural CFRS uses a purely non-autoregressive one-shot framework that enforces global fleet-capacity constraints end-to-end via a differentiable entropic Optimal Transport layer, producing a continuous transport plan to sparsify an exact capacitated assignment solver. The architecture abstracts away spatial, inter-route permutation, and intra-route traversal symmetries.
AI.result: The method achieves < 4% gap on out-of-distribution N=1000 instances and retains ~5% gap as an ultra-lightweight single-layer architecture. On standard benchmarks, it achieves a competitive 2.73% optimality gap on size-100 problems. The framework offers extreme parameter efficiency and zero-shot scaling with pre-trained spatial vocabulary.
AI.conclusion: Neural CFRS demonstrates that revisiting classical combinatorial optimization paradigms through modern deep learning techniques can overcome limitations of autoregressive NCO methods, providing efficient, scalable, and robust solutions for real-world CVRP with strong theoretical guarantees.

title: Discrete Langevin-Inspired Posterior Sampling
summary: We study posterior sampling for inverse problems in discrete state spaces using discrete diffusion models as generative priors. While continuous diffusion models have become widely used for inverse problems, their discrete counterparts remain comparatively underexplored. Existing discrete posterior samplers often rely on continuous relaxations of discrete variables, Gibbs-style updates, or mechanisms specialized to particular corruption processes, which can limit scalability or generality. We propose $Δ$LPS, a Discrete Langevin-Inspired Posterior Sampler that uses gradient information to identify promising discrete moves without leaving the discrete state space. The resulting approach enables efficient parallel updates across all token dimensions and is agnostic to the training paradigm of the discrete diffusion prior, including masked and uniform-state diffusion. We evaluate our method on image restoration tasks across MNIST, CIFAR, and FFHQ, as well as spatial mapping, covering linear, nonlinear, and blind inverse problems. Across these settings, we improve over recent discrete diffusion posterior samplers and are competitive with strong continuous diffusion-based inverse solvers. Our results suggest that fully discrete, gradient-informed posterior samplers offer a scalable and general path toward solving inverse problems over discrete representations.

AI.tldr: A new discrete posterior sampling method called ΔLPS uses gradient information from discrete diffusion models to solve inverse problems efficiently in discrete state spaces, outperforming existing discrete methods and competing with continuous diffusion approaches.
AI.motivation: Discrete diffusion models are underexplored for inverse problems compared to continuous ones. Existing discrete posterior samplers have limitations: they use continuous relaxations (lose discreteness), Gibbs-style updates (slow), or specialized mechanisms (lack generality), which restrict scalability and applicability.
AI.method: The authors propose ΔLPS (Discrete Langevin-Inspired Posterior Sampler), which uses gradient information to identify promising discrete moves without leaving the discrete state space. The method enables efficient parallel updates across all token dimensions and is agnostic to the training paradigm of discrete diffusion priors (masked and uniform-state diffusion).
AI.result: Evaluation on image restoration tasks (MNIST, CIFAR, FFHQ) and spatial mapping, covering linear, nonlinear, and blind inverse problems. The method improves over recent discrete diffusion posterior samplers and is competitive with strong continuous diffusion-based inverse solvers.
AI.conclusion: Fully discrete, gradient-informed posterior samplers like ΔLPS offer a scalable and general path toward solving inverse problems over discrete representations, demonstrating that discrete approaches can be effective alternatives to continuous methods.

title: Path-Dependent Denoising: A Non-Conservative Field Perspective on Order Collapse in Diffusion Language Models
summary:
Diffusion language models (DLMs) offer a structural alternative to autoregressive generation: denoising can update tokens in arbitrary orders or in parallel rather than along a fixed left-to-right chain. In practice, fast DLM decoding remains strongly order-sensitive and often drifts toward autoregressive-like trajectories. We trace this tension to compatibility. At each reverse-time step, a DLM provides local denoising conditionals over the unresolved tokens. Arbitrary-order denoising becomes well defined when these local conditionals compose into order-invariant pseudo-joints.
  We formalize this view by defining order-induced pseudo-joints and a local denoising circulation: the log-ratio between the two pseudo-joints obtained by swapping a pair of unresolved positions. This circulation is zero under compatible conditionals, and global order gaps decompose into sums of local circulations along adjacent swaps. We further separate incompatibility-driven path dependence from conditional-dependence error in parallel updates and from order-specific estimation error. The resulting framework provides inference-only diagnostics for testing when DLM decoding is genuinely order-free.

AI.tldr: DLMs theoretically allow arbitrary-order token generation, but in practice they exhibit order sensitivity due to compatibility issues between local denoising conditionals.
AI.motivation: While diffusion language models offer structural advantages over autoregressive models by enabling parallel or arbitrary-order token generation, in practice they still show strong order sensitivity and tend to drift toward autoregressive-like trajectories, which needs explanation.
AI.method: The authors formalize the concept of order-induced pseudo-joints and introduce a local denoising circulation metric to measure compatibility between conditionals. They separate incompatibility-driven path dependence from conditional-dependence error and order-specific estimation error.
AI.result: The analysis framework provides inference-only diagnostics for testing when DLM decoding is genuinely order-free by decomposing order gaps into sums of local circulations and identifying sources of incompatibility.
AI.conclusion: The tension between DLM's theoretical order-free potential and practical order sensitivity stems from compatibility issues in local denoising conditionals, and the proposed framework offers tools to diagnose when true order-free generation is achievable.

title: Hierarchical Attention-based Graph Neural Network with Relevance-driven Pruning
summary: Graph Neural Networks (GNNs) excel at relational reasoning but face two persistent challenges: the lack of interpretable attribution for heterogeneous node types, and the computational overhead of message passing over large, noisy graphs. We propose the Hierarchical Attention-based Heterogeneous GNN (HA-HeteroGNN), a framework that addresses both issues through a unied explainability-to-pruning pipeline. A two-tier attention mechanism separates sensor-level and context-level computation across 16 node types and 18 edge types, producing per-node relevance scores via an attention-based GNN Explainer without requiring gradient backpropagation. These relevance scores then serve as a principled pruning criterion: removing nodes identied as consistently uninformative yields a 27% reduction in graph edges while simultaneously improving classication accuracy by 2.46.1% across all model variants, challenging the conventional assumption that pruning necessarily trades accuracy for eciency. Experiments on a 50,000-record synthetic dataset spanning 11 report categories demonstrate 97.5% cross-strategy explanation stability and domain consistent sensor attribution, with training-time reductions of up to 43.9% and real-time inference latency of approximately 5860 ms per sample.

AI.tldr: HA-HeteroGNN is a novel GNN framework that integrates explainability and pruning through hierarchical attention, achieving both computational efficiency and improved accuracy across heterogeneous graphs.
AI.motivation: Graph Neural Networks face two key challenges: lack of interpretable attribution for heterogeneous node types (with 16 node types and 18 edge types in this case) and computational overhead from message passing over large, noisy graphs.
AI.method: The authors propose a unified explainability-to-pruning pipeline with a two-tier attention mechanism that separates sensor-level and context-level computation. It uses an attention-based GNN Explainer to produce per-node relevance scores without gradient backpropagation, then uses these scores as a principled pruning criterion to remove uninformative nodes.
AI.result: The approach achieves a 27% reduction in graph edges while improving classification accuracy by 2.4-6.1% across all model variants.
