Second, we benchmark supervised alignment mappings between latent spaces using reconstruction error and downstream task performance. Third, we perform a large-scale regression analysis of how pretraining-data complexity, specialization, transfer learning, augmentation, and model scale relate to geometric and probing properties of embeddings. By coupling representational scale with standardized metadata, SEMASIA provides a reproducible foundation for studying latent geometry, evaluating alignment methods, and developing next-generation heterogeneous and interoperable AI systems.

AI.tldr: SEMASIA is a large-scale benchmark of latent representations from ~1,700 pretrained vision models across eight image-classification datasets, providing structured metadata for studying latent space alignment and embedding properties.
AI.motivation: Existing methods struggle to compare latent spaces across different models due to incompatible geometries caused by variations in architecture, pretraining data, objectives, or random seeds. There's a lack of large-scale, model-diverse benchmarks with rich metadata for studying latent space alignment, which is crucial for interpretability, transfer learning, federated systems, and semantic communication.
AI.method: The authors created SEMASIA by extracting latent representations from approximately 1,700 pretrained vision models across eight standard image-classification benchmarks. They paired embeddings with structured metadata covering architectures, training regimes, pretraining sources, and model scale. They demonstrated three applications: analyzing conceptual organization of latent spaces, benchmarking supervised alignment mappings, and performing large-scale regression analysis of embedding properties.
AI.result: The resource reveals consistent prototype-like clustering and hierarchical semantic neighborhoods across models and datasets. It enables benchmarking alignment methods using reconstruction error and downstream task performance, and provides insights into how factors like pretraining-data complexity, specialization, transfer learning, augmentation, and model scale relate to geometric and probing properties of embeddings.
AI.conclusion: SEMASIA provides a reproducible foundation for studying latent geometry, evaluating alignment methods, and developing next-generation heterogeneous and interoperable AI systems by coupling representational scale with standardized metadata.

title: CTQWformer: A CTQW-based Transformer for Graph Classification
summary: Graph Neural Networks (GNN) and Transformer-based architectures have achieved remarkable progress in graph learning, yet they still struggle to capture both global structural dependencies and model the dynamic information propagation. In this paper, we propose CTQWformer, a hybrid graph learning framework that integrates continuous-time quantum walks (CTQW) with GNN. CTQWformer employs a trainable Hamiltonian that fuses graph topology and node features, enabling physically grounded modeling of quantum walk dynamics that captures rich and intricate graph structure information. The extracted CTQW-based representations are incorporated into two complementary modules:(i) a Graph Transformer module that embeds final-time propagation probabilities as structural biases in the self-attention mechanism, and (ii) a Graph Recurrent Module that captures temporal evolution patterns with bidirectional recurrent networks. Extensive experiments on benchmark graph classification datasets demonstrate that CTQWformer outperforms graph kernel and GNN-based methods, demonstrating the potential of integrating quantum dynamics into trainable deep learning frameworks for graph representation learning. To the best of our knowledge, CTQWformer is the first hybrid CTQW-based Transformer, integrating CTQW-derived structural bias with temporal evolution modeling to advance graph learning.

AI.tldr: CTQWformer is a novel graph learning framework that integrates continuous-time quantum walks with Graph Neural Networks and Transformer architecture to better capture both global structural dependencies and dynamic information propagation in graphs.
AI.motivation: Existing Graph Neural Networks (GNN) and Transformer-based architectures struggle to capture both global structural dependencies and model the dynamic information propagation in graphs effectively.
AI.method: Proposes CTQWformer - a hybrid framework using continuous-time quantum walks (CTQW) with trainable Hamiltonian that fuses graph topology and node features. The CTQW-based representations are incorporated into: (1) a Graph Transformer module that embeds final-time propagation probabilities as structural biases in self-attention, and (2) a Graph Recurrent Module with bidirectional recurrent networks to capture temporal evolution patterns.
AI.result: Extensive experiments on benchmark graph classification datasets show CTQWformer outperforms existing graph kernel and GNN-based methods, demonstrating superior performance in graph representation learning.
AI.conclusion: CTQWformer successfully integrates quantum dynamics into deep learning for graph representation learning, being the first hybrid CTQW-based Transformer that combines CTQW-derived structural bias with temporal evolution modeling to advance graph learning capabilities.

title: Kintsugi: Learning Policies by Repairing Executable Knowledge Bases
summary: Modern embodied agents achieve impressive performance, but their task knowledge is often stored in neural weights, latent state, or prompt-bound memory, making individual policy knowledge difficult to inspect, validate, recombine, and reuse. We introduce \textbf{Kintsugi}, a white-box policy-learning framework that treats embodied policy improvement as verifier-gated construction of a typed executable Knowledge Base (KB). Kintsugi represents task-level policy knowledge as composable typed entries -- predicates, operators, policy schemas, monitors, recovery rules, experience records, and goals -- and improves this artifact through localized typed edits induced from rollout evidence, rather than relying on test-time language-model reasoning. Between rollouts, a tool-constrained agentic editing loop diagnoses trajectory failures, localizes them to editable KB layers, and proposes candidate edits. A deterministic verification gate admits an edit only when the candidate type-checks, the resulting KB executes, and focused validation success or trajectory-health metrics improve without violating protected-regression checks. At inference, the accepted KB is executed by a deterministic symbolic executor with zero LLM calls. Across long-horizon text-agent benchmarks and representative object-centric manipulation settings, Kintsugi achieves strong endpoint performance while preserving inspectability, local editability, and verifier-gated deployment. These results suggest that embodied policy improvement can be organized around executable task knowledge.

AI.tldr: Kintsugi is a white-box policy-learning framework that builds embodied agent policies as typed, executable knowledge bases rather than neural representations, enabling inspectability, validation, and reuse through verifier-gated editing.
AI.motivation: Current embodied agents store task knowledge in neural weights, latent states, or prompt-bound memory, making policy knowledge difficult to inspect, validate, recombine, and reuse. There's a need for more transparent and composable policy representations.
AI.method: Kintsugi treats policy improvement as verifier-gated construction of a typed executable Knowledge Base (KB). It represents policy knowledge as composable typed entries (predicates, operators, schemas, etc.) and improves through localized typed edits from rollout evidence. An agentic editing loop diagnoses failures, proposes edits, and a verification gate admits edits only when they type-check, execute successfully, and improve metrics without regression.
AI.result: Across long-horizon text-agent benchmarks and object-centric manipulation settings, Kintsugi achieves strong endpoint performance while preserving inspectability, local editability, and verifier-gated deployment. Inference uses deterministic symbolic execution with zero LLM calls.
AI.conclusion: Embodied policy improvement can be effectively organized around executable task knowledge in a white-box framework, offering advantages in transparency, validation, and reusability compared to black-box neural approaches.

title: Spectral Transformer Neural Processes
summary: Time series, spatial data, and images are natural applications of Neural Processes. However, when such data exhibit strong periodicity and quasi-periodicity, existing methods often suffer from underfitting and generalise poorly beyond the training distribution. In this work, we propose Spectral Transformer Neural Processes (STNPs), a frequency-aware extension of Transformer Neural Processes (TNPs). STNPs introduce a Spectral Aggregator that estimates an empirical context spectrum, compresses it into a spectral mixture, samples task-adaptive spectral features, and concatenates them with time-domain embeddings, thereby injecting a spectral-mixture-kernel bias into TNPs. This design reshapes the similarity geometry, allowing inputs that are distant in Euclidean space to remain close in an induced periodic manifold while enhancing time-frequency interactions. Extensive experiments on synthetic regression tasks, real-world time-series datasets, and an image dataset demonstrate that STNPs consistently improve predictive performance over existing baselines, extending Neural Processes beyond translation equivariance towards effective modelling of periodicity and quasi-periodicity.

AI.tldr: STNPs enhance Transformer Neural Processes with frequency awareness to better model periodic/quasi-periodic data by injecting spectral mixture biases through a Spectral Aggregator component.
AI.motivation: Existing Neural Processes methods underfit and generalize poorly when data exhibits strong periodicity and quasi-periodicity, limiting their effectiveness on time series, spatial data, and images with these characteristics.
AI.method: Proposes Spectral Transformer Neural Processes (STNPs) with a Spectral Aggregator that estimates empirical context spectrum, compresses it into spectral mixture, samples task-adaptive spectral features, and concatenates them with time-domain embeddings to inject spectral-mixture-kernel bias into TNPs.
AI.result: Extensive experiments on synthetic regression tasks, real-world time-series datasets, and an image dataset demonstrate STNPs consistently improve predictive performance over existing baselines.
AI.conclusion: STNPs extend Neural Processes beyond translation equivariance toward effective modeling of periodicity and quasi-periodicity by reshaping similarity geometry and enhancing time-frequency interactions.

title: Doubly Robust Proxy Causal Learning with Neural Mean Embeddings
summary: Unobserved confounding prevents standard covariate adjustment from identifying causal response functions in observational studies. Proxy causal learning addresses this problem through bridge equations involving treatment- and outcome-inducing proxies, avoiding direct recovery of the latent confounder. Existing doubly robust proxy estimators combine outcome and treatment bridges, but typically rely on fixed kernels, sieves, or low-dimensional semiparametric models; existing neural proxy methods are more flexible, but are largely single-bridge estimators. We develop a neural doubly robust framework for proxy causal learning with continuous and structured treatments. Our method introduces a neural mean-embedding estimator for the treatment bridge, combines it with a neural outcome bridge, and estimates the doubly robust correction through a final regression stage. The framework covers population, heterogeneous, and conditional dose-response functions, yielding full response-curve estimators rather than binary-treatment effects. The algorithms use two stages for each bridge and history-aware updates of the final linear layers to stabilize stochastic multi-stage training. We prove consistency of the algorithms showing that the doubly robust error is controlled by the final averaging and regression errors together with the smaller of the outcome- and treatment-side weak-norm bridge errors. Across synthetic and image-valued benchmarks, the proposed estimators outperform existing baselines and single-bridge neural estimators, showing the benefit of combining learned outcome and treatment bridges in a doubly robust construction. Our implementation is available at https://github.com/BariscanBozkurt/DRPCL-Neural-Mean-Embedding.

AI.tldr: Neural doubly robust framework for proxy causal learning with continuous/structured treatments, combining neural outcome and treatment bridges with stable multi-stage training.
AI.motivation: Unobserved confounding prevents standard covariate adjustment from identifying causal response functions. Existing proxy methods have limitations: doubly robust estimators rely on fixed kernels or low-dimensional models, while neural methods are mostly single-bridge estimators.
AI.method: Develops neural doubly robust framework introducing neural mean-embedding estimator for treatment bridge, combines with neural outcome bridge, estimates doubly robust correction through final regression stage. Uses two stages per bridge and history-aware updates for training stability.
AI.result: Proved consistency showing doubly robust error controlled by final averaging/regression errors plus smaller of outcome- and treatment-side bridge errors. Outperforms existing baselines and single-bridge neural estimators across synthetic and image-valued benchmarks.
AI.conclusion: Combining learned outcome and treatment bridges in doubly robust construction benefits proxy causal learning, providing full response-curve estimators for continuous/structured treatments with better performance than existing approaches.

title: Mixture of Layers with Hybrid Attention
summary: Standard Mixture-of-Experts (MoE) transformers route tokens to expert subnetworks within each layer, but the layer structure itself remains monolithic. We introduce Mixture of Layers (MoL), which replaces full-width transformer blocks (d_model) with K parallel thin blocks at reduced dimensionality (d_thin << d_model), connected via learned down/up projections and composed via top-k block routing. Scaling sparse block routing to many blocks creates an attention coverage problem, as each block sees fewer tokens. We address this by introducing hybrid attention, which pairs one shared softmax block for global context with Gated DeltaNet linear attention in routed blocks.

AI.tldr: MoL replaces standard transformer blocks with parallel thin blocks using learned projections and top-k routing, with hybrid attention combining global context via softmax and efficient routed blocks via linear attention.
AI.motivation: Standard MoE transformers only route tokens to expert subnetworks within each layer, but the layer structure itself remains monolithic. This limits flexibility and efficiency, as full-width transformer blocks may not be necessary for all tokens.
AI.method: Proposes Mixture of Layers (MoL) which replaces full transformer blocks with K parallel thin blocks at reduced dimensionality. Uses learned down/up projections and top-k block routing. Introduces hybrid attention: one shared softmax block for global context plus Gated DeltaNet linear attention in routed blocks to solve attention coverage problem when scaling sparse routing.
AI.result: Enables scaling sparse block routing to many blocks while maintaining attention coverage, as hybrid attention combines global context preservation with efficient processing in routed thin blocks.
AI.conclusion: MoL provides a more flexible and efficient transformer architecture than standard MoE by routing not just tokens but also block structures, with hybrid attention solving the attention coverage problem in sparse routing.

title: LLM-Driven Performance-Space Augmentation for Meta-Learning-Based Algorithm Selection
summary: Meta-learning for algorithm selection relies on a meta-dataset in which each row corresponds to a supervised learning dataset described by meta-features and labelled with a target value that is associated with algorithm choice (typically, some function of algorithm performance). A persistent limitation is that the number of curated real-world datasets is small, resulting in sparse meta-datasets that constrain meta-learner generalisation. In this paper, we address this problem by augmenting the meta-dataset with synthetic regression datasets produced via a large language model (LLM), with generation steered toward target regions of a low-dimensionality performance space. In our experiments, we adopt a two-dimensional geometric setting defined by the cross-validated $R^2$ scores of two anchor algorithms, known as landmarkers. We compare two augmentation strategies: (1) uniform sampling, which distributes synthetic datasets across the performance space; and (2) margin-based sampling, which concentrates them near the decision boundary where landmarker preference is most ambiguous. Across 42 real-world UCI regression datasets and 730 synthetic datasets, both strategies substantially improve meta-learner performance over the unaugmented baseline under regression and multi-label evaluation formulations. However, uniform augmentation consistently outperforms margin-based augmentation, achieving a 17.47% relative reduction in Hamming loss, a 100.41% relative improvement in subset accuracy, and a +6.09% relative gain in pooled out-of-fold $R^2$. These results lead us to postulate a central thesis: the performance of algorithms resides on a low-dimensional performance manifold, whose reconstruction bias may be minimised by user-guided LLMs that seek to maximise uniform $ε$-cover, and consequently, lead to improved meta-learning for algorithm selection.

AI.tldr: Using LLMs to generate synthetic regression datasets for meta-learning algorithm selection, with uniform sampling outperforming margin-based sampling in augmenting sparse meta-datasets.
AI.motivation: The persistent limitation of small, sparse meta-datasets in meta-learning for algorithm selection constrains meta-learner generalization, creating a need for data augmentation.
AI.method: Augment meta-dataset with synthetic regression datasets generated by LLMs, guided toward target regions of a low-dimensional performance space defined by R² scores of two landmarker algorithms, comparing uniform vs margin-based sampling strategies.
AI.result: Both augmentation strategies improve meta-learner performance over unaugmented baseline, but uniform sampling consistently outperforms margin-based sampling with 17.47% reduction in Hamming loss, 100.41% improvement in subset accuracy, and +6.09% gain in pooled R².
AI.conclusion: Algorithm performance resides on a low-dimensional performance manifold, and user-guided LLMs that maximize uniform ε-cover can minimize reconstruction bias, leading to improved meta-learning for algorithm selection.

title: HS-FNO: History-Space Fourier Neural Operator for Non-Markovian Partial Differential Equations
comment: 29 pages, 4 figures, 1 table. Under review. Code at https://github.com/lennonshikhman/hs-fno/
summary: Neural operators provide fast surrogate models for time-dependent partial differential equations, but their standard autoregressive use usually assumes that the instantaneous field $u(t,\cdot)$ is a complete state. This assumption fails for delay equations, distributed-memory systems, and other non-Markovian dynamics: two trajectories may agree at time $t$ and nevertheless have different futures because their histories differ. We introduce the History-Space Fourier Neural Operator (HS-FNO), a neural operator for delay and memory-driven PDEs formulated on the lifted state $u_t(θ,x)=u(t+θ,x)$, $θ\in[-τ,0]$. The key computational step is to decompose one history-state update into a learned predictor for the newly exposed future slice and an exact shift-append transport for the portion of the history window already known from the previous state. This avoids learning deterministic history coordinates, reduces the learned output dimension, and enforces the natural discrete history update. We test HS-FNO on five benchmark families covering delayed reaction--diffusion, spatial epidemiology, nonlocal neural-field dynamics, delayed waves, and distributed-memory closures. Across ten random seeds, HS-FNO attains the lowest aggregate one-step, history-space, and rollout errors among the principal baselines. The largest gain occurs in autoregressive prediction, where aggregate rollout error decreases from $0.241$, $0.188$, and $0.185$ for current-state, lag-stack, and unconstrained history-to-history operators, respectively, to $0.094$. The same model uses fewer parameters than unconstrained history prediction. These results indicate that enforcing the discrete shift structure of history-state evolution is an effective inductive bias for non-Markovian PDE surrogate modeling.

AI.tldr: HS-FNO introduces a neural operator for delay PDEs that works in history-space, using learned predictors for future slices combined with exact shift operations, achieving better accuracy with fewer parameters.
AI.motivation: Standard neural operators assume instantaneous field u(t,·) is a complete state, which fails for delay equations, distributed-memory systems, and other non-Markovian dynamics where history matters for future predictions.
AI.method: HS-FNO operates on lifted state u_t(θ,x)=u(t+θ,x) in history-space [-τ,0]. It decomposes history-state updates into: 1) learned predictor for the newly exposed future slice, and 2) exact shift-append transport for the known portion of history window.
AI.result: HS-FNO achieved lowest aggregate errors (one-step, history-space, and rollout) on five benchmark families. Rollout error decreased from 0.241, 0.188, 0.185 for baselines to 0.094 for HS-FNO, with fewer parameters than unconstrained history prediction.
AI.conclusion: Enforcing the discrete shift structure of history-state evolution is an effective inductive bias for non-Markovian PDE surrogate modeling, as demonstrated by HS-FNO's superior performance on delay and memory-driven PDEs.

title: When Adaptation Fails: A Gradient-Based Diagnosis of Collapsed Gating in Vision-Language Prompt Learning
summary: Adaptive prompting mechanisms have been proposed to enhance vision-language models by dynamically tailoring prompts to inputs. However, in frozen few-shot prompt learning with CLIP-style backbones, we systematically observe that adaptive gates and prompt-selection modules often collapse: they produce nearly constant outputs, contribute negligible gradient signals, and frequently fail to outperform fixed prompts. To further explore this issue, we present a systematic diagnostic study to uncover the underlying causes and conditions of adaptation failure. Through controlled experiments across datasets and multiple prompt learning architectures, we identify two recurring failure modes: gradient magnitude imbalance and gate degradation. Our findings invite a re-examination of indiscriminately adding architectural complexity in parameter-efficient learning and clarify when prompt-level adaptive gating is, and is not, effective in this regime.

AI.tldr: Adaptive prompting mechanisms in frozen few-shot CLIP prompt learning often collapse and fail to outperform fixed prompts due to gradient imbalance and gate degradation.
AI.motivation: While adaptive prompting mechanisms have been proposed to enhance vision-language models by dynamically tailoring prompts to inputs, there's a need to understand why these mechanisms frequently fail in frozen few-shot prompt learning settings with CLIP-style backbones.
AI.method: The authors conduct a systematic diagnostic study through controlled experiments across datasets and multiple prompt learning architectures to uncover the underlying causes and conditions of adaptation failure in adaptive prompting.
AI.result: The study identifies two recurring failure modes: (1) gradient magnitude imbalance, and (2) gate degradation. These cause adaptive gates and prompt-selection modules to produce nearly constant outputs, contribute negligible gradient signals, and frequently fail to outperform fixed prompts.
AI.conclusion: The findings suggest re-examining indiscriminately adding architectural complexity in parameter-efficient learning, and clarify when prompt-level adaptive gating is (and is not) effective in the frozen few-shot prompt learning regime with CLIP-style backbones.

title: Online Set Learning from Precision and Recall Feedback
summary:
We consider the problem of learning an unknown subset $N_\text{target}$ of a domain in an online setting. In each round $t$, the learner predicts a set of items ${N}_t$ and receives one of two types of feedback, each with equal probability: precision feedback, in which a randomly chosen item from the predicted set $N_t$ is revealed and the learner is told whether it belongs to $N_\text{target}$ (incurring a reward if it does), or recall feedback, in which a randomly chosen item from the target set $N_\text{target}$ is revealed and the learner is told whether it belongs to $N_t$ (incurring a reward if it does). The goal is to maximize the cumulative reward over time. This simple online set learning problem abstracts a variety of learning scenarios with precision- and recall-type feedback.
  We show that a hypothesis class (a family of subsets of the domain) is learnable in this setting if and only if it has finite Vapnik-Chervonenkis (VC) dimension, mirroring the classical PAC characterization. However, the resulting algorithmic structure is markedly more intricate: in contrast to standard Probably Approximately Correct (PAC) learning -- where the algorithmic landscape is governed by the simple principle of Empirical Risk Minimization (ERM) -- our partial feedback model can invalidate ERM and even all proper learning rules. We develop algorithms to address the dependencies induced by the feedback, obtaining regret guarantees in both the realizable and agnostic settings. Our results provide a qualitative characterization of learnability in this model, addressing its most basic question, while pointing to a range of natural and intriguing open questions, including the determination of optimal regret rates.

AI.tldr: The paper studies online set learning with precision and recall feedback, showing VC dimension characterizes learnability but with more complex algorithmic requirements than standard PAC learning.
AI.motivation: The paper addresses online learning of unknown subsets with two types of partial feedback: precision feedback (checking if predicted items are in target set) and recall feedback (checking if target items are in predicted set). This abstracts various real-world learning scenarios where only partial feedback about predictions is available.
AI.method: The authors formalize an online learning model where in each round, learners predict a set and receive either precision or recall feedback with equal probability. They analyze learnability through the lens of Vapnik-Chervonenkis (VC) dimension theory and develop algorithms that handle the dependencies induced by this partial feedback structure.
AI.result: The main result shows that a hypothesis class is learnable in this setting if and only if it has finite VC dimension, mirroring classical PAC learning. However, the algorithmic structure is more intricate: ERM (Empirical Risk Minimization) can be invalidated, and even all proper learning rules can fail. The authors develop algorithms with regret guarantees for both realizable and agnostic settings.
AI.conclusion: The paper provides a foundational characterization of learnability in online set learning with precision/recall feedback, establishing VC dimension as the key complexity measure while revealing richer algorithmic challenges than standard learning settings. It opens several natural open questions about optimal regret rates and further extensions.

title: End-to-End Keyword Spotting on FPGA Using Graph Neural Networks with a Neuromorphic Auditory Sensor
summary: With the rapid growth of mobile robotics and embedded intelligence, there is an increasing demand for efficient on-device data processing on edge platforms. A promising research direction is the use of neuromorphic sensors inspired by human sensory systems, which generate sparse, event-based data encoding changes in the environment. In this work, we present the first end-to-end FPGA implementation of a keyword spotting system that integrates a Neuromorphic Auditory Sensor (NAS) and a graph neural network (GNN) on a single FPGA device, enabling real-time processing of raw audio data. The proposed architecture eliminates conventional signal preprocessing and operates directly on event-based audio streams. Leveraging a compute-near-memory network architecture, the system achieves efficient inference with low latency and low power consumption. Experimental results demonstrate an accuracy of 87.43% after quantization on the Google Speech Commands v2 dataset processed through the neuromorphic sensor, with end-to-end latency below 35 us and average power consumption of 1.12 W. The processed datasets, software models, and hardware modules are available at https://github.com/vision-agh/NAS-GNN-KWS.

AI.tldr: First FPGA implementation of keyword spotting combining neuromorphic auditory sensor and graph neural network for real-time edge processing with low power consumption.
AI.motivation: Growing demand for efficient on-device processing in mobile robotics/edge platforms; neuromorphic sensors offer sparse event-based data but need specialized hardware implementations.
AI.method: End-to-end FPGA system integrating Neuromorphic Auditory Sensor (NAS) with graph neural network (GNN) using compute-near-memory architecture to process raw audio without preprocessing.
AI.result: 87.43% accuracy on Google Speech Commands v2 after quantization, with <35 us latency and 1.12 W power consumption. All datasets/models/hardware modules open-sourced.
AI.conclusion: Demonstrates feasibility of integrated neuromorphic sensor + GNN on FPGA for real-time keyword spotting with low latency/power, enabling efficient edge AI applications.

title: Biosignal Fingerprinting: A Cross-Modal PPG-ECG Foundation Model
summary: Cardiovascular disease remains the leading cause of global mortality, yet scalable cardiac monitoring is hindered by the gap between diagnostic-rich ECG and ubiquitous wearable PPG. Bridging this gap requires representations that are compact, transferable across modalities and devices, and deployable without task-specific retraining. Here we introduce biosignal fingerprints: compact latent representations of cardiovascular state derived from a cross-modal foundation model, the Multi-modal Masked Autoencoder (M2AE), trained on over 3.4 million paired ECG and PPG signals. M2AE integrates modality-specific encoders with a shared bottleneck and dual decoders, jointly optimized using reconstruction and cross-modal contrastive objectives, yielding generalizable fingerprints that retain intra- and inter-modality features. Like a biometric fingerprint, these representations uniquely encode an individual's cardiovascular state in a modality-agnostic, privacy-preserving form reusable across clinical tasks without exposing raw waveform data or requiring model retraining. Across 7 downstream tasks, spanning cross-modal reconstruction, cardiovascular disease classification, hypertension detection, mortality prediction, and demographic inference, biosignal fingerprints achieve competitive or superior performance compared to leading domain-specialist foundation models in frozen settings, including an AUROC of 0.974 for five-class CVD classification and 0.877 for hypertension detection, with a maximum improvement of 27.7% in AUROC across 5 classification tasks. Critically, strong performance is maintained with only a single modality, enabling deployment in resource-constrained, single-sensor environments typical of real-world wearable monitoring, with direct implications for continuous cardiovascular monitoring across clinical and consumer health settings.

AI.tldr: Biosignal fingerprints - compact cross-modal representations from a foundation model (M2AE) trained on ECG/PPG data - enable scalable cardiovascular monitoring without task-specific retraining, maintaining strong performance even with single-modality deployment.
AI.motivation: There's a gap between diagnostic-rich ECG monitoring and ubiquitous wearable PPG monitoring. Scalable cardiac monitoring is hindered by the need for task-specific retraining and the lack of compact, transferable representations across modalities and devices.
AI.method: Developed biosignal fingerprints using Multi-modal Masked Autoencoder (M2AE) foundation model trained on 3.4M paired ECG/PPG signals. Uses modality-specific encoders with shared bottleneck and dual decoders, jointly optimized with reconstruction and cross-modal contrastive objectives to create compact, generalizable representations.
AI.result: Achieved competitive/superior performance across 7 downstream tasks without retraining: 0.974 AUROC for five-class CVD classification, 0.877 AUROC for hypertension detection, with up to 27.7% AUROC improvement across 5 classification tasks. Maintains strong performance with single modality only.
AI.conclusion: Biosignal fingerprints provide modality-agnostic, privacy-preserving cardiovascular representations that are reusable across clinical tasks without exposing raw data or requiring retraining. Enables continuous monitoring in resource-constrained wearable environments with broad implications for clinical and consumer health.

title: Geometry Conflict: Explaining and Controlling Forgetting in LLM Continual Post-Training
summary: Continual post-training aims to extend large language models (LLMs) with new knowledge, skills, and behaviors, yet it remains unclear when sequential updates enable capability transfer and when they cause catastrophic forgetting. Existing methods mitigate forgetting through sequential fine-tuning, replay, regularization, or model merging, but offer limited criteria for determining when incorporating new updates is beneficial or harmful. In this work, we study LLM continual post-training through three questions: What drives forgetting? When do sequentially acquired capabilities transfer or interfere? How can compatibility be used to control update integration? We address these questions through task geometry: we represent each post-training task by its parameter update and study the covariance geometry induced by the update. Our central finding is that: forgetting can be considered as a state-relative update-integration failure, it arises when the covariance geometries induced by tasks misalign with the geometry of the evolving model state. Sequential updates transfer when they remain compatible with the model state shaped by previous updates, and interfere when state-relative geometry conflict becomes high. Motivated by this finding, we propose Geometry-Conflict Wasserstein Merging (GCWM), a data-free update-integration method that constructs a shared Wasserstein metric via Gaussian Wasserstein barycenters and uses geometry conflict to gate geometry-aware correction. Across Qwen3 0.6B--14B on domain-continual and capability-continual settings, GCWM consistently outperforms data-free baselines, improving retention and final performance without replay data. These results identify geometry conflict as both an explanatory signal for forgetting and a practical control signal for LLM continual post-training.

AI.tldr: Geometry conflict in LLM continual post-training explains forgetting and enables data-free update integration via Wasserstein barycenters.
AI.motivation: Current continual post-training methods for LLMs lack clear criteria for determining when sequential updates cause catastrophic forgetting versus enable capability transfer, limiting effective knowledge integration.
AI.method: Proposes Geometry-Conflict Wasserstein Merging (GCWM), a data-free method that uses task geometry (parameter update covariance) to detect geometry conflicts between sequential updates, constructing shared Wasserstein metrics via Gaussian Wasserstein barycenters and using geometry conflict to gate geometry-aware correction.
AI.result: GCWM consistently outperforms data-free baselines across Qwen3 models (0.6B-14B) in both domain-continual and capability-continual settings, improving retention and final performance without requiring replay data.
AI.conclusion: Geometry conflict serves as both an explanatory signal for forgetting in LLM continual post-training and a practical control signal for effective update integration, enabling better capability transfer while minimizing catastrophic forgetting.

title: Minimal Filling Architectures of Polynomial Neural Networks: Counterexamples, Frontier Search, and Defects
summary: We provide a counterexample to the minimal unimodal conjecture for polynomial neural networks (PNNs) with power activation functions. Fixing the input and output widths, the conjecture states that any minimal filling architecture has unimodal widths for the hidden layers. We found a counterexample via a frontier search and certified it using recursive dimension bounds and symbolic computation. Notably, several subarchitectures of this example exhibit large defect, in contrast with the predominantly small-defect behavior observed in prior examples.

AI.tldr: The paper provides a counterexample disproving the minimal unimodal conjecture for polynomial neural networks with power activation functions, showing that minimal filling architectures do not always have unimodal hidden layer widths.
AI.motivation: The motivation is to test the validity of the minimal unimodal conjecture for polynomial neural networks (PNNs) with power activation functions, which posited that any minimal filling architecture must have unimodal widths for the hidden layers (first increasing, then decreasing).
AI.method: The authors conducted a frontier search to find potential counterexamples and then certified their findings using recursive dimension bounds and symbolic computation techniques.
AI.result: They successfully found a counterexample to the conjecture. Interestingly, several subarchitectures of this counterexample exhibit large defect, contrasting with the predominantly small-defect behavior observed in prior examples.
AI.conclusion: The minimal unimodal conjecture for polynomial neural networks with power activation functions is false. The discovered counterexample shows that minimal filling architectures can have non-unimodal hidden layer widths, with some subarchitectures displaying unexpectedly large defect behavior.

title: Plan2Cleanse: Test-Time Backdoor Defense via Monte-Carlo Planning in Deep Reinforcement Learning
comment: Published in Transactions on Machine Learning Research (TMLR)
summary: Ensuring the security of reinforcement learning (RL) models is critical, particularly when they are trained by third parties and deployed in real-world systems. Attackers can implant backdoors into these models, causing them to behave normally under typical conditions, but execute malicious behaviors when specific triggers are activated. In this work, we propose Plan2Cleanse, a test-time detection and mitigation framework that adapts Monte Carlo Tree Search to efficiently identify and neutralize RL backdoor attacks without requiring model retraining. Our approach recasts backdoor detection as a planning problem, enabling systematic exploration of temporally extended trigger sequences while maintaining black-box access to the target policy. By leveraging the detection results, Plan2Cleanse can further achieve efficient mitigation through tree-search preventive replanning. We evaluated our method in competitive MuJoCo environments, simulated O-RAN wireless networks, and Atari games. Plan2Cleanse achieves substantial improvements, increasing trigger detection success rates by more than 61.4 percentage points in stealthy O-RAN scenarios and improving win rates from 35\% to 53\% in competitive Humanoid environments. These results demonstrate the effectiveness of our test-time defense approach and highlight the importance of proactive defenses against backdoor threats in RL deployments. Our implementation is publicly available at https://github.com/rl-bandits-lab/RL-Backdoor.

AI.tldr: Plan2Cleanse: A test-time detection and mitigation framework for RL backdoor attacks that uses Monte Carlo Tree Search to identify and neutralize backdoors without model retraining.
AI.motivation: RL models trained by third parties can be vulnerable to backdoor attacks where attackers implant triggers causing malicious behavior when activated, posing security risks in real-world deployments.
AI.method: Adapts Monte Carlo Tree Search to recast backdoor detection as a planning problem, enabling systematic exploration of temporally extended trigger sequences with black-box policy access, followed by tree-search preventive replanning for mitigation.
AI.result: Significant improvements: increased trigger detection success rates by >61.4 percentage points in O-RAN scenarios and improved win rates from 35% to 53% in competitive Humanoid environments.
AI.conclusion: Plan2Cleanse effectively defends against RL backdoor attacks at test-time, demonstrating the importance of proactive defenses and enabling secure RL deployments without requiring model retraining.

title: Make Each Token Count: Towards Improving Long-Context Performance with KV Cache Eviction
comment: A learnable KV eviction method for large language models
summary: The key-value (KV) cache is a major bottleneck in long-context inference, where memory and computation grow with sequence length. Existing KV eviction methods reduce this cost but typically degrade performance relative to full-cache inference. Our key insight is that full-cache attention is not always optimal: in long contexts, irrelevant tokens can dilute attention away from useful evidence, so selective, learnable eviction can improve generation rather than merely approximate the full cache. We introduce a global retention-based KV eviction method that learns each token's future utility under a unified memory budget. Lightweight retention gates assign utility scores to cached KV entries, and a shared final scoring projection calibrates these scores across all layers and heads. This enables a single global eviction policy in which tokens from different layers, heads, and modalities compete directly for cache capacity. We further provide theoretical analysis showing that preferentially retaining useful tokens reduces attention dilution, and we justify geometric retention as a query-agnostic proxy for future utility. Across diverse long-context language and vision-language reasoning, and multi-turn dialogue benchmarks, our method substantially reduces KV memory while matching or surpassing full-cache inference. These results suggest that learned, globally calibrated KV eviction is not only a compression technique, but also a mechanism for improving long-context reasoning.

AI.tldr: A learnable global KV cache eviction method that uses retention gates to score token utility and enables cross-layer/head competition for cache space, reducing memory while improving performance over full-cache inference.
AI.motivation: KV cache memory and computation grow with sequence length in long-context inference, creating a bottleneck. Existing eviction methods reduce cost but degrade performance. The paper argues that full-cache attention isn't always optimal because irrelevant tokens can dilute attention from useful evidence, so selective learnable eviction can improve generation rather than just approximate full cache.
AI.method: Introduces a global retention-based KV eviction method that learns each token's future utility under a unified memory budget. Uses lightweight retention gates to assign utility scores to cached KV entries, with a shared final scoring projection that calibrates scores across all layers and heads. This enables a single global eviction policy where tokens from different layers, heads, and modalities directly compete for cache capacity.
AI.result: Across diverse long-context language and vision-language reasoning, and multi-turn dialogue benchmarks, the method substantially reduces KV memory while matching or surpassing full-cache inference performance.
AI.conclusion: Learned, globally calibrated KV eviction is not just a compression technique, but also a mechanism for improving long-context reasoning by reducing attention dilution and preferentially retaining useful tokens.

title: Causal Parametric Drift Simulation: A Digital Twin Framework for Classifier Robustness Evaluation
summary:
Machine learning classifiers in dynamic environments face concept drift -- changes in the data-generating process that degrade performance. Conventional evaluation via static test sets or noise perturbations fails to preserve causal dependencies in tabular data, often producing causally invalid assessments. Post-hoc tools like SHAP and LIME offer correlational insights that may not reflect the causal mechanisms driving model failure.
  We propose a framework that complements existing drift detection by leveraging Structural Causal Models as "Digital Twins" of data-generating processes, enabling precise causal interventions while preserving structural dependencies. Our technique, Causal Parametric Drift Simulation, stress-tests classifiers to identify vulnerabilities before deployment. Experiments on the Open Sourcing Mental Illness (OSMH) dataset demonstrate that this approach exposes latent vulnerabilities invisible to standard statistical monitors.

AI.tldr: A causal framework for stress-testing ML classifiers by using Structural Causal Models as Digital Twins to simulate concept drift while preserving causal dependencies, revealing vulnerabilities invisible to standard evaluation methods.
AI.motivation: Traditional evaluation methods for ML classifiers in dynamic environments fail to preserve causal dependencies in tabular data when detecting concept drift. Static test sets, noise perturbations, and post-hoc tools like SHAP/LIME provide only correlational insights that don't reflect the true causal mechanisms behind model failures.
AI.method: Proposes a framework using Structural Causal Models (SCMs) as "Digital Twins" of data-generating processes. Introduces Causal Parametric Drift Simulation to perform precise causal interventions while preserving structural dependencies, allowing systematic stress-testing of classifiers before deployment.
AI.result: Experiments on the Open Sourcing Mental Illness (OSMH) dataset demonstrate that this causal approach exposes latent vulnerabilities in classifiers that are invisible to standard statistical drift monitoring methods.
AI.conclusion: The proposed causal framework complements existing drift detection methods by providing causally valid assessments of classifier robustness, enabling better identification of vulnerabilities before deployment in dynamic environments.

title: Learning Multi-Indicator Weights for Data Selection: A Joint Task-Model Adaptation Framework with Efficient Proxies
comment: This work has been accepted at IJCAI 2026
summary: Data selection is a key component of efficient instruction tuning for large language models, as recent work has shown that data quality often matters more than data quantity. Accordingly, prior studies have introduced various multi-dimensional heuristics to evaluate and filter instruction data. However, most existing methods rely on static task-agnostic and model-agnostic weighting schemes, which overlook the varying requirements of specific downstream tasks and the differing pre-existing capabilities of models. In this paper, we propose a framework for learning multi-indicator weights that jointly adapts data selection to both the downstream task and the specific model. Our method identifies optimal weight configurations without full-scale fine-tuning by utilizing in-context learning (ICL) signals on compact tiny-validation sets. These signals serve as efficient performance proxies that ensure high-fidelity evaluation at minimal computational cost. Experiments across multiple benchmarks and model families, including Mistral, Qwen, and Llama, show that the approach achieves performance comparable to or exceeding full-dataset tuning while using only 30\% of the training samples on GSM8K. Furthermore, our analysis reveals a trade-off between semantic diversity and logical complexity in reasoning tasks, highlighting the necessity of joint task-model adaptation.

AI.tldr: A framework that learns adaptive multi-indicator weights for instruction data selection, jointly optimized for specific downstream tasks and models using in-context learning signals on tiny validation sets, achieving comparable performance to full-dataset tuning with 30% of training samples.
AI.motivation: Current data selection methods for instruction tuning rely on static, task-agnostic and model-agnostic weighting schemes, which fail to account for varying downstream task requirements and different pre-existing model capabilities. There's a need for adaptive data selection that considers both task-specific needs and model-specific strengths.
AI.method: Proposes a framework that learns multi-indicator weights adapted to both the downstream task and specific model. Uses in-context learning (ICL) signals on compact tiny-validation sets as performance proxies to identify optimal weight configurations without requiring full-scale fine-tuning, enabling efficient evaluation at minimal computational cost.
AI.result: Experiments across multiple benchmarks and model families (Mistral, Qwen, Llama) show the approach achieves performance comparable to or exceeding full-dataset tuning while using only 30% of training samples on GSM8K. Analysis reveals a trade-off between semantic diversity and logical complexity in reasoning tasks, highlighting the necessity of joint task-model adaptation.
AI.conclusion: Joint adaptation of data selection to both downstream tasks and specific models is essential for efficient instruction tuning. The proposed framework demonstrates that adaptive multi-indicator weighting using ICL signals on tiny validation sets can achieve high performance with significantly reduced training data, addressing limitations of static, one-size-fits-all data selection methods.

title: ChaosNetBench: Benchmarking Spatio-Temporal Graph Neural Networks on Chaotic Lattice Dynamics
summary: Spatio-temporal graph neural networks (STGNNs) are widely used for short-term forecasting in dynamic physical systems such as traffic and weather. However, the prevailing evaluation practice uses real world benchmark data sets in a single domain with a single fixed holdout splits, making it difficult to compare architectures across different dynamical regimes. We introduce ChaosNetBench (CNB), a synthetic benchmark dataset and evaluation framework for studying STGNN performance under controlled multidimensional chaotic dynamics. CNB is built on a lattice of coupled standard maps with independently tunable local chaos ($K$), coupling strength ($\varepsilon$), and system size ($N$), providing known topology and known dynamics across 96 system instances and 9{,}600 trajectories. We introduce chaos indicators, evaluation metrics and a protocol to analyze and compare the capacity of STGNN architectures to deal with different levels of local and global chaos. We illustrate the usage of the framework by analyzing 13 architectures (5 STGNNs and 8 non-graph baselines). The results reveal a regime dependent transition in which non-graph baselines (TCN, N-BEATS, iTransformer) remain competitive when there is low local chaos, while STGNNs (e.g., Graph WaveNet, D2STGNN, STAEformer) are generally more resilient to higher levels of local and global chaos. CNB provides a practical, reusable testbed for systematically comparing and analyzing the capacity of STGNN architectures to handle different levels of local and global chaos.

AI.tldr: Researchers introduce ChaosNetBench, a synthetic benchmark dataset with controlled chaotic dynamics to systematically evaluate and compare spatio-temporal graph neural networks (STGNNs) against baselines.
AI.motivation: Current evaluation practices for STGNNs use real-world benchmark datasets with single fixed holdout splits, making it difficult to compare architectures across different dynamical regimes and understand their capacity to handle varying levels of chaos.
AI.method: Created ChaosNetBench (CNB), a synthetic benchmark dataset built on a lattice of coupled standard maps with tunable parameters (local chaos K, coupling strength ε, system size N), covering 96 system instances and 9,600 trajectories. Introduced chaos indicators, evaluation metrics, and protocols to analyze STGNN performance under controlled chaotic conditions.
AI.result: Analysis of 13 architectures (5 STGNNs, 8 non-graph baselines) revealed a regime-dependent transition: non-graph baselines remain competitive with low local chaos, while STGNNs show greater resilience to higher levels of both local and global chaos.
AI.conclusion: ChaosNetBench provides a practical, reusable testbed for systematic comparison and analysis of STGNN capacity to handle different levels of local and global chaos, addressing limitations of current evaluation practices.

title: Learning Unified Representations of Normalcy for Time Series Anomaly Detection
summary: The core challenge in unsupervised anomaly detection is identifying abnormal patterns without prior knowledge of their characteristics. While existing methods have addressed aspects of this problem, they often struggle to learn a robust representation of the normal data distribution that is distinct from anomalous patterns. In this paper, we present a novel framework, Unified Unsupervised Anomaly Detection ($\text{U}^2\text{AD}$), that comprehensively addresses anomaly detection in multivariate time series. Our approach learns the underlying data distribution of normal samples by utilizing score-based generative modeling. We introduce a novel time-dependent score network and a unified training objective that together delineate the manifold of normal data while considering both local and global temporal contexts. Reconstruction is then performed via a deterministic sampling process using an ordinary differential equation solver. Our extensive experimental evaluations demonstrate that $\text{U}^2\text{AD}$ not only outperforms current state-of-the-art methods in detection accuracy but also identifies anomalies at significantly earlier stages of their occurrence.

AI.tldr: U²AD is a novel unsupervised anomaly detection framework for multivariate time series that uses score-based generative modeling to learn normal data distribution, enabling early anomaly detection with high accuracy.
AI.motivation: Existing unsupervised anomaly detection methods struggle to learn robust representations of normal data that are distinct from anomalous patterns, particularly in multivariate time series where both local and global temporal contexts need to be considered.
AI.method: U²AD uses score-based generative modeling with a novel time-dependent score network and unified training objective to learn the manifold of normal data. It performs reconstruction via deterministic sampling using an ordinary differential equation solver.
AI.result: Extensive experiments show U²AD outperforms current state-of-the-art methods in detection accuracy and identifies anomalies at significantly earlier stages of their occurrence.
AI.conclusion: The proposed U²AD framework successfully addresses the core challenge of unsupervised anomaly detection by learning robust normal data representations through score-based generative modeling, enabling superior detection performance and early anomaly identification.

title: Quantum Circuit Simulation of Compartmental Drug Dynamics: Leveraging Variational Algorithms for Nonlinear Mixed-Effects Population Pharmacokinetics
summary: Population pharmacokinetic/pharmacodynamic (PK/PD) modeling traditionally relies on classical ordinary differential equations to simulate drug dynamics. In this work, we reformulate a compartmental PK/PD model as an open quantum system and implement it using quantum circuits developed in PennyLane. Four pharmacological compartments (central, peripheral, effect-site, and response) are encoded using twelve qubits, with inter-compartmental transitions represented through controlled quantum operations that emulate stochastic dynamics. The framework is evaluated on Phase 1 clinical data using a quantum-enhanced stochastic approximation expectation-maximization (SAEM) approach. Compared with the classical implementation, the quantum model achieves substantially improved log-likelihood values, indicating stronger statistical fit while preserving identical parameter estimates, thereby validating numerical consistency and model interpretability. The quantum-based optimization converges faster in terms of iterations, although total runtime is increased due to current simulation overhead. The study demonstrates stable large-scale simulation performance and establishes a hybrid quantum-classical approach that maintains biological fidelity while improving statistical modeling capacity. The dataset and problem statement originate from the Quantum Innovation Challenge 2025, and additional details are provided via the associated link.

AI.tldr: Quantum circuit implementation of pharmacokinetic models shows improved statistical fit and faster convergence compared to classical ODE approaches, despite current computational overhead.
AI.motivation: Traditional PK/PD modeling relies on classical ordinary differential equations, limiting statistical modeling capacity. The authors aim to leverage quantum computing advantages for improved pharmacological simulations.
AI.method: Reformulated compartmental PK/PD model as open quantum system using PennyLane quantum circuits. Four pharmacological compartments encoded using twelve qubits with inter-compartmental transitions represented through controlled quantum operations. Used quantum-enhanced stochastic approximation expectation-maximization (SAEM) approach on Phase 1 clinical data.
AI.result: Quantum model achieved substantially improved log-likelihood values indicating stronger statistical fit while preserving identical parameter estimates. Quantum-based optimization converged faster in iterations, though total runtime increased due to simulation overhead. Demonstrated stable large-scale simulation performance.
AI.conclusion: Established hybrid quantum-classical approach that maintains biological fidelity while improving statistical modeling capacity. Validated numerical consistency and model interpretability, showing quantum computing's potential for pharmacological applications despite current computational limitations.

title: Discovery of Nonlinear Dynamics with Automated Basis Function Generation
comment: 53 pages, 17 figures. Code available at https://github.com/mabasiri95/AutoSINDy
summary: Discovering governing equations from observational data remains a fundamental challenge in scientific modeling, particularly when the underlying mathematical structure is unknown. Traditional sparse identification methods like SINDy excel at discovering parsimonious models but require researchers to specify candidate basis functions a priori, a limitation that often leads to model failure when critical terms are omitted or when systems exhibit unconventional dynamics. Purely symbolic regression approaches offer unlimited flexibility but struggle with noise sensitivity and frequently produce overly complex, unstable equations. We present AutoSINDy, a hybrid Discovery-then-Solve framework that combines the exploratory power of symbolic regression with the robust sparsity-promoting capabilities of SINDy. Our method operates in three stages: (1) PySR-based symbolic regression discovers candidate functional forms from bootstrapped data chunks; (2) a curation pipeline decomposes, expands, and filters these expressions using collinearity analysis to construct a minimal yet comprehensive library; and (3) SINDy identifies sparse governing equations from this custom-tailored library. Extensive experiments across canonical nonlinear systems demonstrate that AutoSINDy consistently recovers ground-truth equations even under high observational noise, achieving a ground-truth recovery rate of 92.8% across all trials. Compared with standard SINDy using enriched libraries and standalone symbolic regression, AutoSINDy achieves higher predictive accuracy, superior generalization to unseen trajectories, and substantially lower symbolic complexity.

AI.tldr: AutoSINDy is a hybrid framework that combines symbolic regression with SINDy to discover governing equations from observational data, achieving high accuracy even with noise by first exploring candidate functions then selecting sparse models.
AI.motivation: Traditional sparse identification methods like SINDy require specifying candidate basis functions beforehand, which can fail if critical terms are missing. Symbolic regression offers flexibility but struggles with noise and produces overly complex equations. There's a need for a method that combines exploration with robust model selection.
AI.method: AutoSINDy uses a three-stage "Discovery-then-Solve" framework: 1) PySR-based symbolic regression on bootstrapped data chunks to discover candidate functions, 2) a curation pipeline that decomposes, expands, and filters expressions using collinearity analysis to build a minimal comprehensive library, and 3) SINDy to identify sparse governing equations from this custom library.
AI.result: Extensive experiments show AutoSINDy consistently recovers ground-truth equations even under high observational noise, achieving 92.8% recovery rate across all trials. It outperforms standard SINDy with enriched libraries and standalone symbolic regression in predictive accuracy, generalization to unseen trajectories, and produces equations with lower symbolic complexity.
AI.conclusion: AutoSINDy effectively bridges the gap between exploratory symbolic regression and robust sparse identification, providing a powerful framework for discovering governing equations from observational data without requiring prior knowledge of functional forms, especially valuable for noisy or unconventional dynamical systems.

title: Adaptive Data Harvesting for Efficient Neural Network Learning with Universal Constraints
summary: Training neural networks to satisfy universal constraints over continuous domains poses unique challenges. Common examples include Lyapunov Neural Networks (Lyapunov NNs) and Physics-Informed Neural Networks (PINNs), where analytical solutions are generally either unavailable or overly restrictive. Sample-based methods are therefore commonly used to enforce these constraints, and the choice of samples has a substantial impact on convergence speed, stability, and solution quality. Most existing methods rely on fixed heuristics or handcrafted rules, and are suboptimal in practice. In this paper, we aim to improve upon them by learning, from data and experience, how to dynamically and iteratively adjust the samples in response to the model's evolving learning performance. Trained by reinforcement learning, the learned policy improves empirical constraint satisfaction on test problems while significantly improving efficiency. We validate the approach on both Lyapunov NNs and PINNs, and demonstrate its broader applicability to domains where adaptive input selection is essential for effective training.

AI.tldr: A reinforcement learning approach to dynamically adjust training samples for neural networks that must satisfy universal constraints, improving efficiency and constraint satisfaction compared to fixed sampling methods.
AI.motivation: Training neural networks to satisfy universal constraints (like in Lyapunov NNs and PINNs) requires sample-based constraint enforcement, but existing fixed sampling heuristics are suboptimal and impact convergence speed, stability, and solution quality.
AI.method: A reinforcement learning framework that learns to dynamically and iteratively adjust training samples in response to the model's evolving learning performance, replacing fixed sampling heuristics with an adaptive policy.
AI.result: The learned RL policy improves empirical constraint satisfaction on test problems while significantly improving training efficiency. Validated successfully on both Lyapunov Neural Networks and Physics-Informed Neural Networks.
AI.conclusion: The proposed adaptive sampling approach via reinforcement learning offers a superior alternative to fixed sampling methods for training neural networks with universal constraints, demonstrating broader applicability to domains requiring adaptive input selection.

title: Metal-Sci: A Scientific Compute Benchmark for Evolutionary LLM Kernel Search on Apple Silicon
summary: We present Metal-Sci, a 10-task benchmark of scientific Apple Silicon Metal compute kernels spanning six optimization regimes (stencils, all-pairs in $n$-body problems, multi-field Boltzmann, neighbor-list molecular dynamics, multi-kernel PDE, FFT). Each task ships a CPU reference, a roofline-anchored fitness function, and a held-out generalization size. We pair the benchmark with a lightweight harness for automatic kernel search that runtime-compiles each candidate, scores it against the roofline across multiple sizes, and feeds structured compile and per-size correctness diagnostics back to a frozen LLM driving a $(1{+}1)$ evolutionary loop. We report matched single-model sweeps of Claude Opus 4.7, Gemini 3.1 Pro, and GPT 5.5 on M1 Pro: in-distribution self-speedups span $1.00\times$ to $10.7\times$. Beyond raw speedup, our central methodological claim is structural: the held-out gate scoring function $Φ_\mathcal{T}$ (evaluated once at end-of-run on a configuration the agent never sees during search) functions as a cheap mechanical oversight primitive on this automatic search loop, catching e.g. an Opus template <uint D> HMC win that returns wrong samples at unseen dimensions, and a GPT FFT3D best that wins in-distribution at $2.95\times$ speedup but collapses to $0.23\times$ on a $256^3$ held-out cube, a silent regression that the in-distribution score alone cannot see. Code at https://github.com/vicgalle/metal-sci-kernels

AI.tldr: Metal-Sci: A benchmark of 10 scientific Apple Silicon Metal compute kernels with automated kernel search harness and roofline-anchored fitness evaluation, using LLM-driven evolutionary optimization with held-out generalization testing to catch silent regressions.
AI.motivation: Need for systematic benchmarking and optimization of scientific compute kernels on Apple Silicon Metal, with a focus on detecting silent performance regressions that occur only on unseen problem sizes through proper generalization testing.
AI.method: Created 10-task benchmark spanning six optimization regimes with CPU references, roofline-anchored fitness functions, and held-out generalization sizes. Developed lightweight harness for automatic kernel search that runtime-compiles candidates, scores against roofline across multiple sizes, and uses structured diagnostics with frozen LLM-driven (1+1) evolutionary loop.
AI.result: Single-model sweeps show in-distribution self-speedups from 1.00× to 10.7× across Claude Opus 4.7, Gemini 3.1 Pro, and GPT 5.5 on M1 Pro. Held-out scoring function successfully catches silent regressions: Opus template produces wrong samples at unseen dimensions, and GPT FFT3D shows 2.95× speedup in-distribution but collapses to 0.23× on held-out 256³ cube.
AI.conclusion: The held-out gate scoring function Φₜ serves as effective mechanical oversight for automatic kernel search, detecting critical regressions that in-distribution scoring alone misses. This structural approach provides cheap oversight primitive for optimization loops and highlights importance of generalization testing in performance optimization.

title: Benchmarking Transformer and xLSTM for Time-Series Forecasting of Heat Consumption
comment: Submitted version of the paper submitted to IEEE SusTech, 2026
summary: Obtaining an accurate short-term forecasting for heat demand is an essential part of operating district heating networks cost-efficient and reliable. Heat consumption time series at the building level are highly dependent on exogenous variables such as outdoor temperature and individual usage patterns, making forecasting in this context a challenging task. Thus, this paper benchmarks novel Transformer-based and xLSTM architectures for short-term heat-demand forecasting. Using hourly data from 25 German buildings (2017-2025), we compare three-hour and 24-hour forecasting horizons relevant for intraday control and day-ahead scheduling. We establish a multi-building benchmark that tests whether models trained on pooled, heterogeneous building data are able to generalize across diverse building stock. The results show that the xLSTM achieves the lowest RMSE (19.88 kWh for three-hour, 21.47 kWh for 24-hour forecasts), while the Temporal Fusion Transformer attains the best MAE (9.16 kWh for three-hour forecasts). As xLSTMs and Transformers require long training times and have a huge number of trainable parameters, their sustainability remains questionable. Therefore, this paper further investigates the trade-off between predictive accuracy and computational resource demand of the evaluated forecasting models. The findings indicate that also low-parameter models like a traditional fully-connected network achieve good predictive results, highlighting that marginal accuracy gains of the novel prediction models come at substantial resource expense for this use case.

AI.tldr: Transformers and xLSTMs achieve best accuracy for short-term heat demand forecasting but at high computational cost, while simpler models offer good performance with much lower resource requirements.
AI.motivation: Accurate short-term heat demand forecasting is essential for cost-efficient and reliable district heating network operation, but building-level forecasting is challenging due to dependencies on temperature and usage patterns.
AI.method: Benchmarked novel Transformer-based and xLSTM architectures against traditional models using hourly data from 25 German buildings (2017-2025) for 3-hour and 24-hour forecasting horizons, establishing a multi-building benchmark for generalization.
AI.result: xLSTM achieved lowest RMSE (19.88 kWh for 3-hour, 21.47 kWh for 24-hour), while Temporal Fusion Transformer achieved best MAE (9.16 kWh for 3-hour forecasts), but simple fully-connected networks also performed well with much lower computational cost.
AI.conclusion: While advanced architectures provide marginal accuracy gains, their high computational resource demands question their sustainability; simpler models offer good predictive results with much lower resource requirements for heat demand forecasting.

title: Model Capacity Determines Grokking through Competing Memorisation and Generalisation Speeds
summary: Existing accounts of grokking explain the phenomena in terms of mechanistic frameworks such as circuit efficiency or lazy-to-rich transitions. However, despite a known dependence between grokking and model size, how model capacity shapes grokking remains an open question. We give an information-theoretic account of this relationship on the task of modular arithmetic, showing that grokking does not immediately occur when a model becomes large enough to memorise the training set, but rather emerges as the outcome of a competition between two measurable timescales: a memorisation speed $T_{\text{mem}}(P)$ and a generalisation speed $T_{\text{gen}}(P)$, both of which are functions of model parameter count $P$. Adapting the information capacity framework of Morris et al. (2025), we estimate $T_{\text{mem}}(P)$ on random-label data of equivalent complexity and $T_{\text{gen}}(P)$ on the modular task itself, and show that grokking emerges close to the parameter scale where these timescales intersect. The framework also suggests an empirical model for predicting memorisation speed given model capacity and dataset complexity, recovering the previously reported empirical observation that larger models memorise faster. Overall, we motivate the formalisation of different learning timescales as important abstractions to study when explaining how model capacity shapes grokking on algorithmic tasks.

AI.tldr: Grokking emerges when model capacity reaches a point where generalization becomes faster than memorization, with the intersection of these two timescales determining when grokking occurs.
AI.motivation: Despite known dependence between grokking and model size, how model capacity shapes grokking remains an open question. Existing mechanistic frameworks (circuit efficiency, lazy-to-rich transitions) don't fully explain this relationship.
AI.method: Adapt information capacity framework to measure two competing timescales: memorization speed T_mem(P) and generalization speed T_gen(P), both as functions of model parameter count P. Estimate T_mem(P) on random-label data and T_gen(P) on modular arithmetic task.
AI.result: Grokking emerges close to the parameter scale where memorization and generalization timescales intersect, not immediately when models become large enough to memorize training data. Framework provides empirical model for predicting memorization speed given capacity and dataset complexity.
AI.conclusion: Formalizing different learning timescales (memorization vs. generalization) provides important abstractions for understanding how model capacity shapes grokking on algorithmic tasks, recovering the observation that larger models memorize faster.

title: One for All: A Non-Linear Transformer can Enable Cross-Domain Generalization for In-Context Reinforcement Learning
summary: A central challenge in reinforcement learning (RL) is to learn models that generalize beyond the tasks on which they are trained, a goal traditionally pursued through multi-task and meta RL. Recently, transformer architectures have emerged as a promising approach, enabling adaptation to new tasks via in-context learning without explicit parameter updates. From a functional perspective, a transformer can be viewed as a functional operator that maps a context to a task-specific function. It is thus fundamental to understand and design this operator to support stronger generalization in RL. In this work, we address this resulting question of generalization from a kernel-based perspective by establishing a connection between non-linear transformers and kernel-based temporal difference learning. By interpreting the transformer as performing regression in a Reproducing Kernel Hilbert Space (RKHS), we show that value functions from different domains can be represented using a shared set of weights, provided they lie within the same RKHS. Experiments on multiple MetaWorld domains support this interpretation, demonstrating convergence of the temporal-difference objective.

AI.tldr: Transformers can be viewed as functional operators for RL generalization, and this work connects them to kernel-based TD learning, showing that value functions from different domains can share weights when they exist in the same RKHS.
AI.motivation: The paper aims to address the challenge of generalization in RL beyond trained tasks. While transformers show promise for in-context learning without parameter updates, there's a need to fundamentally understand and design these functional operators for stronger generalization. The specific motivation is to investigate generalization from a kernel-based perspective by connecting transformers to kernel methods.
AI.method: The authors establish a theoretical connection between non-linear transformers and kernel-based temporal difference (TD) learning. They interpret the transformer as performing regression in a Reproducing Kernel Hilbert Space (RKHS). This allows value functions from different domains to be represented using shared weights when they exist within the same RKHS. The approach was validated through experiments on multiple MetaWorld domains.
AI.result: Experiments on multiple MetaWorld domains support the kernel-based interpretation, demonstrating convergence of the temporal-difference objective. The results confirm that value functions can indeed be represented using shared weights across domains when they reside in the same RKHS.
AI.conclusion: Transformers in RL can be understood through a kernel-based perspective, connecting them to kernel TD learning. This provides a theoretical foundation for understanding how transformers achieve generalization in RL through their functional operator properties, with practical implications for designing more effective RL systems that can adapt across domains.

title: RubricRefine: Improving Tool-Use Agent Reliability with Training-Free Pre-Execution Refinement
summary: Iterative self-refinement is a popular inference-time reliability technique, but its effectiveness in code-mode tool use depends heavily on the structure of the feedback signal: unstructured critique helps inconsistently across models, and even revision with real execution feedback improves only modestly ($0.75$ vs. $0.65$ baseline). The dominant failures are inter-tool contract violations - wrong output shape, incorrect tool routing, broken argument provenance - that run to completion without raising errors, making runtime feedback insufficient. We introduce RubricRefine, a training-free pre-execution reliability layer that generates task- and registry-specific rubrics, scores candidate code against explicit contract checks, and iteratively repairs failures before any execution occurs. With zero execution attempts, RubricRefine reaches $0.86$ on M3ToolEval averaged across seven models-improving over prior inference-time baselines on every model tested on this benchmark, at $2.6X$ lower latency than the strongest non-iterative alternative - and remains flat on the predominantly single-step API-Bank, consistent with the method's reliance on inter-tool contract structure. A rubric-category ablation and calibration analysis further characterize when and why the method works.

AI.tldr: RubricRefine is a training-free pre-execution reliability layer for code-mode tool use that generates task-specific rubrics to detect and repair inter-tool contract violations before execution, outperforming prior inference-time methods with lower latency.
AI.motivation: Current iterative self-refinement techniques for code-mode tool use are ineffective because they rely on unstructured critique or real execution feedback, which fails to detect inter-tool contract violations (wrong output shape, incorrect tool routing, broken argument provenance) that run to completion without raising errors.
