AI.method: RubricRefine generates task- and registry-specific rubrics, scores candidate code against explicit contract checks, and iteratively repairs failures before any execution occurs. It operates as a training-free pre-execution reliability layer.
AI.result: RubricRefine achieves 0.86 on M3ToolEval across seven models with zero execution attempts, improving over all prior inference-time baselines on every model tested. It achieves 2.6X lower latency than the strongest non-iterative alternative and remains flat on single-step tasks like API-Bank, consistent with its focus on inter-tool contracts.
AI.conclusion: Pre-execution contract checking via rubrics is more effective than runtime feedback for code-mode tool use reliability, particularly for multi-tool workflows where inter-tool contract violations are the dominant failure mode. The method's effectiveness depends on the presence of structured inter-tool contract relationships.

title: CALYREX: Cross-Attention LaYeR EXtended Transformers for System Prompt Anchoring
summary: Modern large language models (LLMs) rely on system prompts to establish behavioral constraints and safety rules. Standard causal self-attention treats privileged instructions and untrusted user content with equal structural priority -- a mismatch that leaves models vulnerable to prompt injection and instruction erosion over extended contexts. We propose CALYREX (Cross-Attention LaYeR EXtended transformers), which utilizes cross-attention between input and system prompt to structurally isolate and anchor the rule. A placement ablation on a 1.5B backbone identifies insertion at the final eighth of layers as optimal, confirmed by mechanistic activation analysis showing behavioral constraints are naturally concentrated there. At 8B scale, controlling for training data, backbone, and parameter budget, CALYREX yields $+7.4\%$ on instruction-following (IFEval) and $+16.3\%$ on multi-turn instruction adherence, while reducing many-shot jailbreaking attack success rate by $13\%$. This advantage appears to widen with model scale, consistent with larger models more effectively utilizing the dedicated routing pathway.

AI.tldr: CALYREX uses cross-attention between input and system prompt to structurally isolate behavioral constraints, improving instruction-following and reducing jailbreaking vulnerability while showing scale benefits.
AI.motivation: Standard causal self-attention treats system prompts and user content equally, leaving models vulnerable to prompt injection and instruction erosion over extended contexts.
AI.method: Proposes CALYREX (Cross-Attention LaYeR EXtended transformers) using cross-attention between input and system prompt to structurally isolate and anchor behavioral rules. Found optimal insertion at final eighth of layers via placement ablation on 1.5B model.
AI.result: At 8B scale, CALYREX yields +7.4% on instruction-following (IFEval) and +16.3% on multi-turn instruction adherence, while reducing many-shot jailbreaking attack success rate by 13%. Advantage widens with model scale.
AI.conclusion: CALYREX provides a more robust architecture for system prompt isolation, showing that dedicated routing pathways improve instruction adherence and safety, with benefits scaling with model size.

title: TIDES: Implicit Time-Awareness in Selective State Space Models
summary: Selective state space models (SSMs), such as Mamba, achieve strong per-token expressivity by making the time discretization step $\TildeΔ$ a learned function of the input. However, in doing so, $\TildeΔ$ ceases to represent a physical sampling interval, limiting its irregular time series modeling capability. Continuous-time SSMs, such as S5, preserve the physical meaning of $\TildeΔ$ and handle irregular timestamps natively ($\TildeΔ\equivΔ)$, but their dynamics remain linear time-invariant (LTI), limiting per-token expressivity. We propose \textbf{TIDES}, a selective SSM variant that reconciles selective and continuous architectures by moving input-dependence off the step size and onto the diagonal state matrix. As a result, $\TildeΔ$ retains its physical meaning, tied to the state discretization, allowing the model to handle irregular timestamps natively without sacrificing the per-token expressivity that makes selective SSMs effective. We show this on a novel \emph{Fading Flash} experimental benchmark, a compact controlled diagnostic for sequence models that jointly tests input-dependence and extrapolation to out-of-distribution $Δ$ values, and isolates the distinct failure modes of current state-of-the-art architectures that TIDES avoids by construction. On large-scale benchmarks, TIDES sets the new state-of-the-art average rank on UEA time-series classification and the Physiome-ODE regression benchmark. Code available at: https://github.com/TaylanSoydan/TIDES.

AI.tldr: TIDES is a selective state space model variant that reconciles input-dependent expressivity with continuous-time irregular timestamp handling by moving input-dependence from step size to state matrix, preserving physical time meaning while maintaining per-token expressivity.
AI.motivation: Current selective SSMs like Mamba lose physical meaning of time discretization when making it input-dependent, limiting irregular time series modeling. Continuous-time SSMs like S5 preserve physical time meaning but lack per-token expressivity due to linear time-invariant dynamics.
AI.method: TIDES moves input-dependence from the time discretization step size to the diagonal state matrix, keeping the step size as physical sampling interval while maintaining selective expressivity through input-dependent state dynamics.
AI.result: TIDES achieves state-of-the-art average rank on UEA time-series classification and Physiome-ODE regression benchmark, and outperforms existing architectures on the novel Fading Flash benchmark for input-dependence and extrapolation to OOD time values.
AI.conclusion: TIDES successfully bridges the gap between selective and continuous SSMs, providing both input-dependent expressivity and native irregular timestamp handling while preserving physical time meaning, addressing fundamental limitations of existing state space architectures.

title: Entropy-informed Decoding: Adaptive Information-Driven Branching
summary: Large language models (LLMs) achieve remarkable generative performance, yet their output quality is dependent on the decoding strategy. While sampling-based methods (e.g., top-k, nucleus) and search-and-select based methods (e.g., beam search, best-of-n, majority voting) can improve upon greedy decoding, both approaches suffer from limitations: sampling generally commits to a single path, while search often expends excessive computation regardless of task complexity. To address these, we introduce Entropy-informed decoding (EDEN), a plug-and-play, model-agnostic decoding framework that adaptively allocates computation based on the model's own uncertainty, approximating higher-width beam search with fewer expansions. At each generation step, EDEN estimates the entropy of the output token distribution and adjusts the branching factor monotonically with the entropy, expanding more candidates in high-entropy regions and following a greedier path in low-entropy regions, improving token efficiency. Experiments across complex tasks, including mathematical reasoning, code generation, and scientific questions, demonstrate that EDEN consistently improves output quality over existing decoding strategies, achieving better accuracy-expansion trade-offs than fixed-width beam search. By treating next-token selection as a noisy maximisation problem, we prove that branching factors monotone in entropy are guaranteed to find better (i.e. more probable) continuations than any fixed branching factor within the same total expansion budget, and derive explicit regret rates characterising the benefit of the adaptive allocation.

AI.tldr: EDEN is a plug-and-play decoding framework that adaptively allocates computation based on LLM uncertainty, expanding more in high-entropy regions and being greedier in low-entropy regions, achieving better accuracy-expansion trade-offs than fixed methods.
AI.motivation: Current LLM decoding strategies have limitations: sampling-based methods commit to single paths, while search-based methods (beam search, best-of-n) use excessive computation regardless of task complexity. There's a need for more efficient, adaptive decoding that allocates computation based on model uncertainty.
AI.method: EDEN (Entropy-informed decoding) estimates the entropy of the output token distribution at each generation step and adjusts the branching factor monotonically with entropy. It expands more candidates in high-entropy regions and follows greedier paths in low-entropy regions, approximating higher-width beam search with fewer expansions.
AI.result: Experiments across mathematical reasoning, code generation, and scientific questions show EDEN consistently improves output quality over existing decoding strategies, achieving better accuracy-expansion trade-offs than fixed-width beam search. Theoretical analysis proves branching factors monotone in entropy guarantee better continuations than fixed branching factors within the same budget.
AI.conclusion: EDEN provides an effective adaptive decoding framework that optimizes computation allocation based on model uncertainty, offering improved efficiency and quality over both sampling and fixed-search methods, with theoretical guarantees for finding better continuations.

title: Sequential Feature Selection for Efficient Landslide Segmentation from Multi-Spectral Data
comment: In Process of Submission to Frontiers in Remote Sensing. Keywords: landslide segmentation, multispectral remote sensing, feature selection, explainability, Landslide4Sense
summary: Landslide detection from satellite imagery has advanced through deep learning, yet most models rely on large, highly correlated spectral-topographic inputs whose contributions remain poorly understood. The question of which channels are actually necessary has received surprisingly little attention. This matters: redundant or correlated inputs obscure physical interpretability, inflate computational overhead, and can actively degrade model performance through the Hughes Phenomenon. We present a systematic, explainable channel-selection framework for the Landslide4Sense benchmark, combining Sentinel-2 multispectral and ALOS PALSAR terrain data with 16 engineered spectral and structural indices. Rather than relying on conventional single-band drop tests, which evaluate channels in isolation and miss interaction effects, we apply Sequential Forward Floating Selection (SFFS) to iteratively build and prune a candidate feature pool using a lightweight U-Net++ proxy model. Beyond identifying a compact 8-channel subset that matches or exceeds the segmentation F1 of configurations using up to 30 channels, we use the selection process itself to interrogate which spectral and topographic features landslide models genuinely rely on, and what this reveals about the physical cues driving their predictions. We argue that SFFS represents a principled feature selection approach to input design in Earth observation, in contrast to the prevailing practice of appending every available band and hoping the model learns what to ignore.

AI.tldr: A systematic channel-selection framework for landslide detection that uses Sequential Forward Floating Selection to identify a compact 8-channel subset from up to 30 spectral/topographic channels, achieving comparable performance while improving interpretability and efficiency.
AI.motivation: Most landslide detection models use large, highly correlated spectral-topographic inputs without understanding which channels are actually necessary. Redundant inputs obscure interpretability, increase computational costs, and can degrade performance through the Hughes Phenomenon (curse of dimensionality). There's surprisingly little research on channel necessity despite its importance for physical interpretability and efficiency.
AI.method: Developed an explainable channel-selection framework for Landslide4Sense benchmark using Sentinel-2 multispectral and ALOS PALSAR terrain data with 16 engineered spectral/structural indices. Used Sequential Forward Floating Selection (SFFS) with a lightweight U-Net++ proxy model to iteratively build and prune candidate features, evaluating interactions rather than isolated single-band drop tests.
AI.result: Identified a compact 8-channel subset that matches or exceeds the segmentation F1 score of configurations using up to 30 channels. The selection process reveals which spectral and topographic features landslide models genuinely rely on and provides insights into physical cues driving predictions.
AI.conclusion: SFFS represents a principled feature selection approach for Earth observation input design, offering a systematic alternative to the prevailing practice of appending all available bands and hoping models learn to ignore irrelevant ones. The framework improves interpretability, reduces computational overhead, and provides insights into what physical features actually matter for landslide detection.

title: On Uniform Error Bounds for Kernel Regression under Non-Gaussian Noise
comment: This paper has been accepted at the 43rd International Conference on Machine Learning (ICML) 2026
summary: Providing non-conservative uncertainty quantification for function estimates derived from noisy observations remains a fundamental challenge in statistical machine learning, particularly for applications in safety-critical domains. In this work, we propose novel non-asymptotic probabilistic uniform error bounds for kernel-based regression. Compared to related bounds in the literature that are restricted to (conditionally) independent sub-Gaussian noise, our bounds allow to consider a broad class of non-Gaussian distributions, such as sub-Gaussian, bounded, sub-exponential, and variance/moment-bounded noise. Moreover, our results apply to correlated and uncorrelated noise. We compare our proposed error bounds with existing results in terms of the induced uncertainty region and their performance in safe control, demonstrating the tightness of the proposed bounds.

AI.tldr: Novel non-asymptotic probabilistic uniform error bounds for kernel-based regression that handle diverse noise distributions (sub-Gaussian, bounded, sub-exponential, moment-bounded) and both correlated/uncorrelated noise, improving uncertainty quantification for safety-critical applications.
AI.motivation: Existing uncertainty quantification methods for function estimates from noisy observations are often conservative or restrictive, particularly lacking for non-Gaussian noise distributions and correlated noise, which limits applicability in safety-critical domains where precise uncertainty bounds are essential.
AI.method: Proposes novel non-asymptotic probabilistic uniform error bounds for kernel-based regression that generalize existing results by accommodating a broader class of noise distributions including sub-Gaussian, bounded, sub-exponential, and variance/moment-bounded noise, and work for both correlated and uncorrelated noise scenarios.
AI.result: The proposed bounds provide tighter uncertainty regions compared to existing results, as demonstrated through comparisons in terms of induced uncertainty region size and performance in safe control applications, showing improved tightness of the uncertainty quantification.
AI.conclusion: The proposed non-asymptotic probabilistic uniform error bounds significantly advance kernel-based regression uncertainty quantification by handling diverse noise distributions and correlation structures, making them more practical for safety-critical applications where accurate uncertainty bounds are essential.

title: WISTERIA: Learning Clinical Representations from Noisy Supervision via Multi-View Consistency in Electronic Health Records
summary: Representation learning in electronic health records (EHR) has largely followed paradigms inherited from natural language processing, relying on sequence modeling and reconstruction based objectives that treat clinical labels as ground truth. However, real world clinical supervision is inherently weak, arising from heterogeneous, noisy, and institution specific labeling processes such as billing codes, heuristic phenotypes, and incomplete annotations. In this work, we propose WISTERIA, a weakly supervised representation learning framework that models labels as stochastic observations of an underlying latent clinical state. Instead of optimizing against a single supervision signal, WISTERIA constructs multiple weak supervision operators and learns representations by enforcing consistency across their induced label distributions. This multi view formulation induces an implicit denoising mechanism, allowing the model to recover clinically meaningful structure by reconciling disagreement between noisy labelers. We further incorporate ontology aware regularization in the label space to impose semantic structure over supervision signals. Empirically, WISTERIA improves predictive performance across standard EHR benchmarks, demonstrates strong robustness to label noise, and exhibits superior cross institutional generalization compared to sequence based pretraining objectives. These results suggest that explicitly modeling the supervision process rather than treating labels as fixed targets provides a more appropriate inductive bias for learning robust and clinically meaningful representations from EHR data.

AI.tldr: WISTERIA is a weakly supervised representation learning framework for EHR data that models clinical labels as noisy observations of latent clinical states, using multiple weak supervision operators and consistency constraints to learn robust representations while handling real-world label noise and heterogeneity.
AI.motivation: Current EHR representation learning treats clinical labels as ground truth, but real-world clinical supervision is inherently weak due to heterogeneous, noisy, and institution-specific labeling processes (billing codes, heuristic phenotypes, incomplete annotations). These approaches fail to account for the noisy nature of clinical labels.
AI.method: WISTERIA models labels as stochastic observations of underlying latent clinical states. Instead of optimizing against single supervision signals, it constructs multiple weak supervision operators and learns representations by enforcing consistency across their induced label distributions. This multi-view formulation provides implicit denoising. The framework also incorporates ontology-aware regularization in the label space to impose semantic structure over supervision signals.
AI.result: WISTERIA improves predictive performance across standard EHR benchmarks, demonstrates strong robustness to label noise, and exhibits superior cross-institutional generalization compared to sequence-based pretraining objectives.
AI.conclusion: Explicitly modeling the supervision process (rather than treating labels as fixed targets) provides a more appropriate inductive bias for learning robust and clinically meaningful representations from EHR data. The weakly supervised approach better handles real-world clinical data characteristics.

title: Bayesian Optimization with Structured Measurements: A Vector-Valued RKHS Framework
summary: Bayesian optimization (BO) is an efficient framework for optimizing expensive black-box functions. However, it is typically formulated as learning an end-to-end mapping from inputs to scalar objectives, thereby discarding the potentially rich information whenever a structured system output is available. In this work, we study Bayesian optimization over a vector-valued operator with structured measurements, where each measurement observes multidimensional or functional outputs, e.g., trajectories or spatial fields, rather than a single scalar value. The objective is then defined as a linear functional of these measurements. This allows each observation to reveal substantially richer information about the underlying system compared to scalar observations. Assuming the unknown operator lies in a vector-valued reproducing kernel Hilbert space (RKHS), we derive high-probability concentration bounds for the kernel ridge regression (KRR) estimator directly in the measurement space, characterizing uncertainty in a general Hilbert space. Building on these results, we propose an algorithm based on the upper confidence bound (UCB) acquisition function with regret guarantees under mild assumptions, recovering sublinear rates for common kernels. Empirically, we demonstrate that leveraging structured measurements leads to improved sample efficiency by enabling efficient transfer of information across objectives and adaptation to time-varying settings.

AI.tldr: Bayesian optimization over vector-valued operators with structured measurements (like trajectories or spatial fields) rather than single scalars, using kernel ridge regression in RKHS with UCB acquisition and regret guarantees.
AI.motivation: Standard Bayesian optimization discards rich information when structured system outputs are available. The authors want to leverage structured measurements (multidimensional or functional outputs like trajectories or spatial fields) that contain substantially richer information about the underlying system compared to single scalar observations.
AI.method: Assume unknown operator lies in vector-valued RKHS. Derive high-probability concentration bounds for kernel ridge regression estimator directly in measurement space. Propose UCB-based acquisition function algorithm with regret guarantees under mild assumptions.
AI.result: Recover sublinear regret rates for common kernels. Empirically demonstrate improved sample efficiency through efficient transfer of information across objectives and adaptation to time-varying settings.
AI.conclusion: Leveraging structured measurements in Bayesian optimization enables richer information extraction and improved performance compared to traditional scalar-based approaches, with theoretical guarantees and practical benefits.

title: Nectar: Neural Estimation of Cached-Token Attention via Regression
summary: Evaluating softmax attention over a fixed long context requires reading every cached key-value pair for each new query token. For a given context (a book, a manual, a legal corpus) the attention output is a deterministic function of the query. We propose Nectar, which fits a compact neural network to this function for queries drawn from a task-relevant distribution. Nectar fits two networks per layer and KV-head: a target network that predicts the attention output and a score network that predicts the log-normalizer. The pair plugs into the standard masked self-attention at inference time, replacing the $O(n)$ attention over the cache with a forward pass whose cost does not depend on $n$. Each module carries on the order of $|θ|$ parameters per layer and KV-head, typically much smaller than the $2nd$ KV-cache footprint at the same granularity. We report experiments on models from 1.7B to 8B parameters across five long-context datasets. The approximation error tracks the next-token accuracy gap to full attention, and allocating capacity non-uniformly across layers reduces that gap in our ablation. Beyond this analysis of metrics, we check that the text generations (following a question prompt) of a model equipped with a Nectar module match in semantic content those obtained by giving the same model access to the full cache.

AI.tldr: Nectar replaces standard O(n) attention computation with compact neural networks that approximate the attention function for long contexts, enabling efficient inference without accessing the full KV cache.
AI.motivation: Standard attention requires reading every cached key-value pair for each query token (O(n) complexity), which is inefficient for long contexts like books, manuals, or legal corpora. The attention output is deterministic for a given context, so this function can be approximated more efficiently.
AI.method: Nectar fits two compact neural networks per layer and KV-head: 1) a target network that predicts attention output, and 2) a score network that predicts the log-normalizer. These networks are trained to approximate the deterministic attention function for queries from task-relevant distributions. The networks plug into standard masked self-attention at inference time, replacing O(n) attention computation with a forward pass whose cost doesn't depend on context length n.
AI.result: Experiments on models from 1.7B to 8B parameters across five long-context datasets show that approximation error tracks the next-token accuracy gap to full attention. Non-uniform capacity allocation across layers reduces this gap in ablation studies. Generated text following question prompts matches semantic content of full cache access.
AI.conclusion: Nectar successfully approximates attention functions for long contexts using compact neural networks, achieving semantic equivalence to full attention while dramatically reducing computational cost from O(n) to constant-time forward passes. This enables efficient long-context processing without accessing the full KV cache.

title: LEAD: Length-Efficient Adaptive and Dynamic Reasoning for Large Language Models
summary: Large reasoning models, such as OpenAI o1 and DeepSeek-R1, tend to become increasingly verbose as their reasoning capabilities improve. These inflated Chain-of-Thought (CoT) trajectories often exceed what the underlying problems require, wasting compute, latency, and context budgets. While introducing length-based efficiency rewards during reinforcement learning offers a natural remedy, existing methods struggle with two fundamental challenges: the optimal balance between correctness and efficiency is non-stationary throughout training, and intrinsic reasoning budgets vary drastically across problems. Relying on static reward weights and global length constraints inevitably forces a compromise between degraded accuracy and unrealized compression. To overcome these limitations, we propose LEAD (Length-Efficient Adaptive and Dynamic reasoning), a method that replaces static heuristics with online, self-adaptive mechanisms. LEAD dynamically calibrates the correctness-efficiency trade-off at each step using a Potential-Scaled Instability, directing optimization capacity to the most informative learning signal. Furthermore, it estimates an adaptive per-problem target length online based on the model's own correct rollouts, applying a symmetric efficiency reward that penalizes both overthinking and over-compression. Evaluated on five mathematical reasoning benchmarks, LEAD achieves the highest accuracy and Accuracy-Efficiency Score among RL-trained efficient-reasoning methods while producing substantially shorter outputs than the base model.

AI.tldr: LEAD introduces adaptive mechanisms to reduce verbose reasoning in large language models while maintaining accuracy, achieving better accuracy-efficiency balance than existing methods.
AI.motivation: Large reasoning models like OpenAI o1 and DeepSeek-R1 produce increasingly verbose Chain-of-Thought trajectories that waste compute, latency, and context budgets. Existing methods using static reward weights and global length constraints compromise between accuracy degradation and insufficient compression.
AI.method: LEAD (Length-Efficient Adaptive and Dynamic reasoning) uses two key innovations: (1) dynamically calibrates correctness-efficiency trade-off using Potential-Scaled Instability to direct optimization to most informative signals, and (2) estimates adaptive per-problem target lengths online based on the model's correct rollouts, applying symmetric efficiency rewards that penalize both overthinking and over-compression.
AI.result: Evaluated on five mathematical reasoning benchmarks, LEAD achieves the highest accuracy and Accuracy-Efficiency Score among RL-trained efficient-reasoning methods while producing substantially shorter outputs than the base model.
AI.conclusion: LEAD successfully addresses the limitations of static reward mechanisms by introducing online, self-adaptive approaches that better balance reasoning quality with efficiency, representing an advancement in efficient reasoning for large language models.

title: Learning to Compress Time-to-Control: A Reinforcement Learning Framework for Chronic Disease Management
summary: Reinforcement learning (RL) in healthcare has had mixed results, with reward sparsity, unreliable off-policy evaluation, and deployment-simulation gap as recurring failure modes. We argue that chronic disease management is structurally a more tractable RL setting than the acute-care problems the field has primarily studied, but only if the problem is formalized to exploit chronic care's properties. We propose such a formalization. The agent's objective is to compress time-to-control (TTC) under a tiered reward calibrated to the CMS ACCESS Model. Two quantities from our companion preference-learning paper [Singh et al. 2026] enter as load-bearing structural elements: the execution intensity εbounds action availability under a constrained Markov Decision Process, and the clinician capability κweights offline-data transitions during RL training. Together they couple preference learning and RL into a two-loop architecture. We present simulation results on synthetic state machines for hypertension and type 2 diabetes. Capability-weighted offline RL outperforms uniform-weighted offline RL and the behavior policy by 15 percentage points on T2D TTC; the uniform-weighted formulation (the standard in existing healthcare RL) underperforms even the heterogeneous behavior policy. \Epsilon-aware policies generalize across deployment regimes while ε-naive policies do not.

AI.tldr: Chronic disease management is more tractable for RL than acute care when formalized with time-to-control compression under CMS ACCESS Model rewards, using execution intensity and clinician capability from preference learning to create a two-loop architecture that outperforms standard approaches.
AI.motivation: Reinforcement learning in healthcare has struggled with reward sparsity, unreliable off-policy evaluation, and deployment-simulation gaps, particularly in acute-care settings. The authors argue that chronic disease management offers a more tractable RL setting if properly formalized to exploit its unique properties.
AI.method: Proposes a formalization where the agent's objective is to compress time-to-control under tiered rewards calibrated to CMS ACCESS Model. Uses two key quantities from companion preference-learning paper: execution intensity (bounds action availability under constrained MDP) and clinician capability (weights offline-data transitions during RL training). These create a two-loop architecture coupling preference learning and RL.
AI.result: Capability-weighted offline RL outperforms uniform-weighted offline RL and behavior policy by 15 percentage points on Type 2 Diabetes time-to-control. Uniform-weighted formulation (standard in healthcare RL) underperforms even heterogeneous behavior policy. ε-aware policies generalize across deployment regimes while ε-naive policies do not.
AI.conclusion: Chronic disease management can be successfully addressed with RL when formalized to exploit its structural properties, particularly through the integration of execution intensity and clinician capability from preference learning into a two-loop architecture that outperforms existing healthcare RL approaches.

title: Dystruct: Dynamically Structured Diffusion Language Model Decoding via Bayesian Inference
summary: Diffusion language models (DLMs) have recently emerged as a promising alternative to autoregressive models, primarily due to their ability to enable parallel decoding. Despite this advantage, most existing DLMs rely on a fixed generation length specified prior to decoding, which restricts their flexibility in real-world applications. While a few recent works attempt to support flexible-length generation, they typically suffer from notable limitations: some require costly retraining to accommodate variable-length outputs, while others depend solely on local confidence signals during decoding. Such local criteria fail to capture the evolving structure of the sequence, often resulting in suboptimal generation quality. In this paper, we propose a training-free, Bayesian structured decoding framework that formulates flexible-length generation as a dynamic structural inference problem. Our approach formulates flexible-length generation as a dynamic structural inference problem, jointly computing the expansion length, the block boundaries, and the decoding schedule. At each window expansion step, the method integrates local uncertainty with structural signals via a unified mechanism that supports dynamic structured generation, including both flexible block expansion and block organization, while maintaining coherence. Extensive experiments across multiple benchmarks demonstrate that our approach significantly improves generation quality and flexibility over existing fixed-length and flexible-length baselines. These results highlight the advantage of Bayesian structured decoding for diffusion language model, providing a principled and efficient solution for structured text generation.

AI.tldr: A training-free Bayesian structured decoding framework for diffusion language models that enables flexible-length generation through dynamic structural inference, outperforming existing fixed-length and flexible-length baselines.
AI.motivation: Current diffusion language models (DLMs) require pre-specified fixed generation lengths, limiting their flexibility in real-world applications. Existing flexible-length generation methods either need costly retraining or rely on local confidence signals that fail to capture sequence structure evolution, leading to suboptimal quality.
AI.method: A training-free Bayesian structured decoding framework that formulates flexible-length generation as a dynamic structural inference problem. The approach jointly computes expansion length, block boundaries, and decoding schedule at each window expansion step, integrating local uncertainty with structural signals through a unified mechanism.
AI.result: Extensive experiments across multiple benchmarks show the approach significantly improves generation quality and flexibility over existing fixed-length and flexible-length baselines, demonstrating superior performance in structured text generation.
AI.conclusion: The Bayesian structured decoding framework provides a principled and efficient solution for structured text generation with diffusion language models, highlighting the advantage of structured inference approaches for flexible-length generation.

title: Pretraining large language models with MXFP4
summary: Why does full-pipeline FP4 training of large language models often diverge, even when forward activations and activation gradients remain stable? We address this question through a controlled study of MXFP4 quantization in transformer training, progressively enabling FP4 across forward propagation (Fprop), activation gradients (Dgrad), and weight gradients (Wgrad) while holding all other factors fixed. In full pretraining of Llama 3.1-8B on the C4 dataset, we observe that quantizing Wgrad is the primary driver of convergence degradation, whereas FP4 in Fprop and Dgrad alone introduces only modest additional token requirements. To interpret this behavior, we evaluate both structured and stochastic interventions under a controlled experimental setting. We find that stochastic rounding and randomized Hadamard rotations fail to stabilize training once Wgrad is quantized, whereas deterministic Hadamard rotations consistently restore stable optimization. These results suggest that FP4 training instability is driven by structured micro-scaling errors along sensitive gradient paths, rather than by insufficient stochasticity. We run experiments with native MXFP4 support on AMD Instinct MI355X GPUs, enabling controlled investigation of these effects without reliance on software emulation.

AI.tldr: Full-pipeline FP4 training divergence is primarily caused by weight gradient quantization, not forward propagation or activation gradient quantization, and can be stabilized with deterministic Hadamard rotations.
AI.motivation: To understand why full-pipeline FP4 training of large language models often diverges despite stable forward activations and activation gradients, investigating the root causes of instability in ultra-low precision training.
AI.method: Controlled study of MXFP4 quantization in transformer training, progressively enabling FP4 across forward propagation, activation gradients, and weight gradients while holding other factors fixed. Experiments include structured and stochastic interventions (stochastic rounding, randomized Hadamard rotations, deterministic Hadamard rotations) under controlled settings.
AI.result: Quantizing weight gradients (Wgrad) is the primary driver of convergence degradation, while FP4 in forward propagation and activation gradients alone introduces only modest additional token requirements. Deterministic Hadamard rotations consistently restore stable optimization, while stochastic rounding and randomized Hadamard rotations fail once Wgrad is quantized.
AI.conclusion: FP4 training instability is driven by structured micro-scaling errors along sensitive gradient paths rather than insufficient stochasticity, suggesting targeted interventions like deterministic Hadamard rotations can stabilize ultra-low precision training.

title: Modeling Atomic Conformational Ensembles of Proteins via Test-Time Supervision of Boltz-2 on Cryo-EM Density Maps
summary: Knowledge of a protein's atomic conformational ensemble is critical to determining its function, yet state-of-the-art ensemble prediction models are limited by lack of high-quality conformational data from simulation or experiment. Recent advances in heterogeneous reconstruction for cryo-electron microscopy (cryo-EM) have enabled scientists to visualize ensembles of density maps for larger proteins and complexes not typically accessible through simulation, but building atomic models into these maps remains a challenge. Traditionally, ensemble prediction models are trained via a two-stage process: experimental density maps are converted into atomic structural ensembles through model building, after which these structures are used to train sequence-to-atomic ensemble predictors. In this work, we propose a new principle for fine-tuning pre-trained static structure prediction models such as Boltz-2 directly on raw cryo-EM maps, bypassing the two-stage process. We apply this technique to the problem of atomic model building by fine-tuning Boltz-2 to generate atomic conformations from an input ensemble of cryo-EM maps, achieving superior model building accuracy compared to prior work. Beyond overfitting to individual map ensembles, our method, CryoSampler, also shows preliminary evidence of in-domain generalization after fine-tuning, sampling diverse atomic conformations for an unseen sequences within the same protein family without requiring cryo-EM data. These capabilities indicate that CryoSampler holds the potential to train next-generation atomic ensemble prediction models directly on raw cryo-EM measurements.

AI.tldr: CryoSampler fine-tunes pre-trained protein structure prediction models directly on raw cryo-EM density maps to generate atomic conformational ensembles, bypassing traditional two-stage model building processes.
AI.motivation: Current ensemble prediction models are limited by lack of high-quality conformational data. Cryo-EM can visualize conformational ensembles for large proteins, but building atomic models into these maps remains challenging. Traditional two-stage approaches (map → structures → training) are inefficient and lose information.
AI.method: Proposes fine-tuning pre-trained static structure prediction models (like Boltz-2) directly on raw cryo-EM maps. The method, CryoSampler, trains models to generate atomic conformations from input cryo-EM map ensembles, eliminating the intermediate structure-building step.
AI.result: Achieves superior model building accuracy compared to prior work. Shows preliminary evidence of in-domain generalization - can sample diverse atomic conformations for unseen sequences within the same protein family without requiring cryo-EM data after fine-tuning.
AI.conclusion: CryoSampler demonstrates potential for training next-generation atomic ensemble prediction models directly on raw cryo-EM measurements, offering a more efficient pathway from experimental data to functional conformational insights.

title: Free Energy Manifold: Score-Based Inference for Hybrid Bayesian Networks
summary:
We introduce the Free Energy Manifold (FEM), a score-trained conditional energy model specialized for inference in hybrid Bayesian networks with discrete and continuous variables. FEM represents each conditional factor as an energy landscape over learned discrete-parent embeddings and continuous observations, enabling posterior evaluation, generative sampling, and compositional inference across multiple continuous leaves by energy addition under conditional independence.
  A central finding is the mode-bridge artifact: standard conditional energy models can create low-energy ridges between separated modes of the same class, producing overconfident posteriors at off-data interior points. We analyze this failure and propose valley regularization, an off-data calibration term that restores near-uniform posteriors in such regions while preserving in-data fit.
  Across synthetic multimodal hybrid-BN benchmarks, FEM substantially reduces KL divergence relative to classical baselines and a vanilla conditional EBM, including large gains at mode-bridge midpoint queries and in multi-leaf evidence composition. We also evaluate high-cardinality discrete-parent settings and a UCI Breast Cancer sanity check, showing that FEM is most useful when multimodal or compositional Bayesian-network inference is required, while discriminative classifiers remain preferable for closed-world classification tasks.

AI.tldr: FEM is a score-trained conditional energy model for hybrid Bayesian networks that addresses mode-bridge artifacts through valley regularization, improving multimodal and compositional inference.
AI.motivation: Traditional methods struggle with inference in hybrid Bayesian networks containing both discrete and continuous variables, particularly with multimodal distributions and compositional inference across multiple continuous leaves.
AI.method: The Free Energy Manifold represents conditional factors as energy landscapes over learned discrete-parent embeddings and continuous observations, enabling posterior evaluation, generative sampling, and compositional inference via energy addition under conditional independence. The key innovation is valley regularization to address mode-bridge artifacts.
AI.result: FEM substantially reduces KL divergence relative to classical baselines and vanilla conditional EBMs, with large gains at mode-bridge midpoint queries and multi-leaf evidence composition. It performs well in high-cardinality discrete-parent settings and UCI Breast Cancer benchmarks.
AI.conclusion: FEM is most useful for multimodal or compositional Bayesian-network inference tasks, while discriminative classifiers remain preferable for closed-world classification. Valley regularization effectively addresses the mode-bridge artifact issue in conditional energy models.

title: Sub-Footprint Effect Correction in FW-LiDAR Point Clouds via Intra-Footprint Target Unmixing
summary: Sub-footprint target mixing within a laser footprint significantly increases LiDAR intensity uncertainty, especially in complex environments where heterogeneous materials inside one footprint cause nonlinear distortions that impair intensity-based applications. However, the forward mixing inherent to the single-pixel detection mode of LiDAR systems blurs sub-footprint contributions, making sub-footprint effects difficult to address effectively in existing studies. To address this issue, we introduce a novel, physics-based framework that explicitly resolves sub-footprint intensity correction in full-waveform LiDAR (FW-LiDAR) point clouds. The key innovation is to make the otherwise implicit intra-footprint mixing process explicit: we first develop a spatiotemporal laser-beam distribution model to physically characterize within-footprint forward mixing of multi-target returns. Building on this formulation, we incorporate ancillary information including waveform parameters and surface geometry as constraints to pose a well-defined inverse unmixing problem and decompose each footprint into fractional contributions from multiple sub-targets. We then recover sub-footprint-corrected intensities by inverting the observed mixtures through a unified combination of parametric and model-driven approaches. To the best of our knowledge, few prior studies explicitly establish sub-footprint inversion and correction within a single laser footprint, and our framework offers a principled, physics-grounded solution. Experiments on both controlled and real-world LiDAR datasets demonstrate that the proposed method significantly enhances semantic separability across heterogeneous targets and intensity consistency across homogeneous targets.

AI.tldr: A physics-based framework that explicitly resolves sub-footprint intensity correction in full-waveform LiDAR by modeling within-footprint mixing and decomposing multiple sub-target contributions.
AI.motivation: Sub-footprint target mixing within a laser footprint significantly increases LiDAR intensity uncertainty, especially in complex environments where heterogeneous materials cause nonlinear distortions that impair intensity-based applications. Existing methods struggle because the forward mixing inherent to LiDAR's single-pixel detection mode blurs sub-footprint contributions.
AI.method: Develop a spatiotemporal laser-beam distribution model to physically characterize within-footprint forward mixing of multi-target returns. Incorporate ancillary information (waveform parameters and surface geometry) as constraints to pose a well-defined inverse unmixing problem and decompose each footprint into fractional contributions from multiple sub-targets. Recover sub-footprint-corrected intensities through a unified combination of parametric and model-driven approaches by inverting observed mixtures.
AI.result: Experiments on both controlled and real-world LiDAR datasets demonstrate that the proposed method significantly enhances semantic separability across heterogeneous targets and intensity consistency across homogeneous targets.
AI.conclusion: The framework offers a principled, physics-grounded solution that explicitly establishes sub-footprint inversion and correction within a single laser footprint - an approach few prior studies have accomplished, addressing a fundamental limitation in LiDAR intensity processing for complex environments.

title: Efficient Neural Architectures for Real-Time ECG Interpretation on Limited Hardware
comment: 9 pages, 6 figures, 3 tables. Published in: 2025 IEEE International Conference on Big Data (BigData), pp. 3275-3284. DOI: 10.1109/BIGDATA66926.2025.11402097
summary: Electrocardiogram (ECG) interpretation is essential for diagnosing a wide range of cardiac abnormalities. While deep learning has shown strong potential for automating ECG classification, many existing models rely on large, computationally intensive architectures that hinder practical deployment. In this paper, we present an empirical study of convolutional neural network (CNN) architectures, exploring tradeoffs between diagnostic accuracy and computational efficiency. We benchmark two established baselines: AttiaNet, a compact model composed of sequential temporal and spatial blocks, and DeepResidualCNN, the winning architecture of the 2021 PhysioNet/Computing in Cardiology Challenge. Building on these, we propose three lightweight models: (i) ParallelCNN, which employs dual temporal and spatial branches for parallel pattern extraction; (ii) ParallelCNNew, a variant with symmetric weight initialization for balanced feature learning; and (iii) SimpleNet, a streamlined architecture that jointly processes temporal and spatial dimensions. Our experiments span three publicly available 12-lead ECG datasets from Germany, China, and the United States, covering binary, multiclass, and multilabel classification tasks across diverse patient populations. We further evaluate the impact of integrating low-cost demographic metadata (age and sex) to improve performance with minimal overhead. To ensure fair comparison, we introduce a unified Efficiency Score that integrates model size, inference speed, memory usage, and AUC performance. By balancing diagnostic performance and efficiency, our models offer a scalable and viable foundation for next-generation AI systems in cardiovascular care.

AI.tldr: A study exploring lightweight CNN architectures for ECG classification that balance diagnostic accuracy with computational efficiency, proposing three new models and a unified efficiency scoring metric.
AI.motivation: Existing deep learning models for ECG interpretation are often large and computationally intensive, hindering practical clinical deployment. There's a need for efficient models that maintain high diagnostic accuracy while being suitable for real-world applications.
AI.method: The paper empirically studies CNN architectures for ECG classification, benchmarking two established models (AttiaNet and DeepResidualCNN) and proposing three new lightweight architectures: ParallelCNN (dual temporal/spatial branches), ParallelCNNew (symmetric weight initialization), and SimpleNet (joint temporal-spatial processing). Experiments use three 12-lead ECG datasets covering different populations and tasks, with evaluation of demographic metadata integration and a proposed unified Efficiency Score.
AI.result: The study demonstrates that lightweight CNN architectures can achieve competitive diagnostic performance while significantly improving computational efficiency. The proposed models show good performance across diverse ECG datasets and tasks, and the inclusion of demographic metadata improves performance with minimal overhead.
AI.conclusion: Lightweight CNN architectures offer a viable path toward practical AI deployment in cardiovascular care by balancing diagnostic accuracy with computational efficiency. The proposed Efficiency Score provides a fair comparison framework, and the models present a scalable foundation for next-generation ECG interpretation systems.

title: Exploration-Driven Optimization for Test-Time Large Language Model Reasoning
summary: Post-training techniques combined with inference-time scaling significantly enhance the reasoning and alignment capabilities of large language models (LLMs). However, a fundamental tension arises: inference-time methods benefit from diverse sampling from a relatively flattened probability distribution, whereas reinforcement learning (RL)-based post-training inherently sharpens these distributions. To address this, we propose Exploration-Driven Optimization (EDO), which extends reward-biasing style exploration objectives to iterative post-training and integrates them into standard RL objectives, encouraging greater diversity in sampled solutions while facilitating more effective inference-time computation. We incorporate EDO into iterative Direct Preference Optimization (iDPO) and Group Relative Policy Optimization (GRPO), resulting in two variants: ED-iDPO and ED-GRPO. Extensive experiments demonstrate that both ED-iDPO and ED-GRPO exhibit greater solution diversity and improved reasoning abilities, particularly when combined with test-time computation techniques like self-consistency. Across three in-distribution reasoning benchmarks, EDO achieves a 1.0-1.3\% improvement over the strongest baselines, and delivers an additional 1.5\% average gain on five out-of-distribution tasks. Beyond accuracy, EDO preserves model entropy and stabilizes RL training dynamics, highlighting its effectiveness in preventing over-optimization collapse. Taken together, these results establish EDO as a practical framework for balancing exploration and exploitation in LLM reasoning, especially in settings that rely on test-time scaling.

AI.tldr: EDO (Exploration-Driven Optimization) is a new post-training method that balances exploration and exploitation for LLMs, enabling better performance with inference-time computation techniques.
AI.motivation: There's a fundamental tension between inference-time methods (which benefit from diverse sampling from flattened probability distributions) and RL-based post-training (which inherently sharpens distributions). This conflict limits the effectiveness of combining post-training with inference-time computation techniques.
AI.method: EDO extends reward-biasing exploration objectives to iterative post-training and integrates them into standard RL objectives. The authors develop two variants: ED-iDPO (based on iterative Direct Preference Optimization) and ED-GRPO (based on Group Relative Policy Optimization). These methods encourage greater diversity in sampled solutions while maintaining effective inference-time computation.
AI.result: ED-iDPO and ED-GRPO show greater solution diversity and improved reasoning abilities, especially when combined with self-consistency techniques. On three in-distribution reasoning benchmarks, EDO achieves 1.0-1.3% improvement over strongest baselines, with an additional 1.5% average gain on five out-of-distribution tasks. EDO also preserves model entropy and stabilizes RL training dynamics, preventing over-optimization collapse.
AI.conclusion: EDO establishes a practical framework for balancing exploration and exploitation in LLM reasoning, particularly in settings that rely on test-time scaling. It effectively addresses the tension between diverse inference-time sampling and distribution-sharpening RL training.

title: Concordia: Self-Improving Synthetic Tables for Federated LLMs
summary: Federated learning (FL) enables training large language models (LLMs) without sharing raw data, but adapting LLMs under strict data isolation and non-IID client distributions remains challenging in practice. Synthetic data offers a natural privacy-preserving surrogate for local training, yet existing federated pipelines typically treat synthetic generation as static or loosely coupled with downstream optimization, leading to rapidly diminishing utility under heterogeneous clients. We study federated adaptation of LLMs on tabular tasks where raw records and validation data cannot be shared, and local training must rely entirely on synthetic tables. We propose Concordia, a tri-level optimization framework that aligns synthetic data generation with federated validation utility despite these constraints. At the client level, models are adapted via parameter-efficient LoRA training on synthetic tables. Clients additionally learn lightweight utility scorers from private validation feedback to reweight synthetic samples during local training. At the outer level, each client refines its own synthetic table generator using group-relative policy optimization (GRPO), guided by an ensemble of heterogeneous scorers shared across clients, without aggregating generator parameters or exposing validation data. Experiments on privacy-sensitive tabular benchmarks from finance and healthcare demonstrate that Concordia consistently improves federated performance, cross-client stability, and robustness to distribution shift compared to static and decoupled synthetic-data baselines.

AI.tldr: Concordia: A tri-level optimization framework for federated LLM adaptation on tabular tasks using synthetic data, featuring client-specific generators trained via GRPO and utility-based sample reweighting.
AI.motivation: Federated learning with LLMs faces challenges under strict data isolation and non-IID distributions; synthetic data preserves privacy but current approaches treat generation as static or loosely coupled, leading to diminished utility with heterogeneous clients.
AI.method: Tri-level optimization framework: 1) Client-level LoRA adaptation on synthetic tables, 2) Client-level utility scorers from private validation feedback to reweight synthetic samples, 3) Outer-level client-specific synthetic table generators refined using group-relative policy optimization (GRPO) guided by ensemble of heterogeneous scorers.
AI.result: Experiments on privacy-sensitive tabular benchmarks from finance and healthcare show Concordia consistently improves federated performance, cross-client stability, and robustness to distribution shift compared to static and decoupled synthetic-data baselines.
AI.conclusion: Concordia effectively aligns synthetic data generation with federated validation utility under strict data isolation constraints, demonstrating a practical approach for privacy-preserving federated LLM adaptation on tabular tasks.

title: Flag Varieties: A Geometric Framework for Deep Network Alignment
summary: Alignment, the tendency of adjacent weight matrices in deep networks to develop compatible subspace orientations, underlies gradient flow, Neural Collapse, and representation similarity across architectures. Despite extensive empirical documentation, these phenomena have resisted unified theoretical treatment: existing explanations are post-hoc, each fitted to a specific observation with whatever mathematics is at hand. We reverse this direction by deriving the mathematical structure that layerwise alignment inherently demands. Using geometric invariant theory, we prove that alignment geometry has a canonical closed, polystable stratum given by a flag variety, and that subspace intersection dimension is its unique reparameterization-invariant observable, establishing that subspace metrics are not empirical conventions but mathematical necessities. This unified framework yields two dynamical consequences: ridge regularization drives subspace alignment at an exponential rate set by weight decay, whereas nonlinear activations induce a commutator obstruction to exact basis alignment, generically present in nonlinear networks and absent in linear ones. Together these give a geometric explanation of the Level-2/3 hierarchy in Neural Collapse from first principles rather than post-hoc analysis. The commutator magnitude and head subspace overlap further serve as weight-space windows into internal alignment structure, requiring no forward passes. Experiments on multilayer perceptrons, residual networks, and pretrained language models support the proposed diagnostics and delineate their scope.

AI.tldr: The paper establishes that layer alignment in deep networks has a canonical geometric structure given by flag varieties, with subspace intersection as the fundamental observable, and shows ridge regularization drives alignment while nonlinear activations prevent exact basis alignment, explaining Neural Collapse hierarchy from first principles.
AI.motivation: Existing explanations for alignment phenomena (gradient flow, Neural Collapse, representation similarity) are post-hoc and fragmented. The paper aims to provide a unified theoretical framework by deriving the inherent mathematical structure of layerwise alignment rather than fitting observations post-hoc.
AI.method: Uses geometric invariant theory to prove alignment geometry has a canonical polystable stratum given by flag varieties. Shows subspace intersection dimension is the unique reparameterization-invariant observable. Analyzes dynamical consequences: ridge regularization drives subspace alignment exponentially, while nonlinear activations create commutator obstructions to exact basis alignment.
AI.result: Derived that alignment structure is fundamentally geometric (flag variety) with subspace intersection as the essential observable. Ridge regularization causes exponential alignment toward this canonical form, while nonlinear activations prevent exact basis alignment, explaining Level-2/3 Neural Collapse hierarchy. Proposed diagnostic tools: commutator magnitude and head subspace overlap as weight-space alignment measures requiring no forward passes.
AI.conclusion: The paper provides a first-principles geometric theory of layer alignment that unifies disparate empirical observations, explains Neural Collapse hierarchy from basic principles rather than post-hoc analysis, and offers practical diagnostic tools validated across architectures including MLPs, ResNets, and pretrained language models.

title: UFO: A Unified Flow-Oriented Framework for Robust Continual Graph Learning
summary: Graph learning research has increasingly shifted toward continual graph learning (CGL), which better reflects real-world scenarios where graphs evolve over time. However, existing CGL methods largely assume clean supervision and overlook a critical challenge: the newly arriving portions of the graph are often noisy, due to annotation errors or adversarial corruption. This mismatch limits their applicability in practice. In this work, we study robust continual graph learning, where models must simultaneously handle catastrophic forgetting and noisy supervision in evolving graph data. We show that label noise introduces a new failure mode, catastrophic remembering, where models persistently reinforce corrupted knowledge across tasks. To address these challenges, we propose a Unified Flow-Oriented framework (UFO). First, UFO models conditional feature distributions via flow-based generative modeling and produces replay representations, mitigating forgetting without storing historical data. Second, UFO estimates instance-level reliability scores to distinguish clean from noisy nodes, reducing the impact of corrupted supervision and alleviating catastrophic remembering. Extensive experiments on four benchmark graph datasets under varying noise ratios demonstrate that UFO consistently outperforms existing methods in both accuracy and forgetting metrics. Code is available at: https://anonymous.4open.science/r/UFO.

AI.tldr: A Unified Flow-Oriented framework (UFO) that addresses both catastrophic forgetting and noisy supervision in continual graph learning by using flow-based generative modeling for replay and instance-level reliability scoring to combat catastrophic remembering.
AI.motivation: Real-world evolving graphs often contain noisy supervision due to annotation errors or adversarial corruption, but existing continual graph learning methods assume clean supervision, limiting their practical applicability.
AI.method: UFO uses flow-based generative modeling to capture conditional feature distributions for replay representations (mitigating forgetting without storing historical data) and estimates instance-level reliability scores to distinguish clean from noisy nodes (reducing impact of corrupted supervision).
AI.result: Extensive experiments on four benchmark graph datasets under varying noise ratios show UFO consistently outperforms existing methods in both accuracy and forgetting metrics.
AI.conclusion: UFO effectively addresses the dual challenges of catastrophic forgetting and catastrophic remembering in robust continual graph learning, providing a practical solution for evolving graph data with noisy supervision.

title: Continuous Latent Contexts Enable Efficient Online Learning in Transformers
summary: Large language models (LLMs) exhibit a strong capacity for in-context learning: Given labeled examples, they can generate good predictions without parameter updates. However, many interactive settings go beyond static prediction to online decision-making, in which effective behavior demands adaptation over long multi-turn horizons in response to feedback, and efficient algorithms in these domains must use compact representations of what they have learned. Recently, continuous transformer architectures with latent chain of thought have shown promise for offline iterative tasks such as directed graph-reachability. Motivated by this, we study whether continuous latent context tokens equip transformers to more effectively realize online learning. We give explicit constructions of constant-depth transformers that implement two foundational online decision-making procedures -- the weighted majority algorithm and $Q$-learning -- by storing their algorithmic state as linear combinations of feature embeddings, using a small number of latent context tokens. We further train a small GPT-2-style transformer with latent contexts using a multi-curriculum objective that does not directly supervise the latent states. On long synthetic online prediction sequences, this model outperforms larger and more complex LLMs, including Qwen-3-14B and DeepSeek-V3. Our results suggest that continuous latent contexts provide a simple and effective persistent state for transformers to implement online learning algorithms.

AI.tldr: Transformers with continuous latent context tokens can implement online learning algorithms like weighted majority and Q-learning, enabling effective multi-turn decision-making without parameter updates.
AI.motivation: While LLMs excel at static in-context learning, online decision-making requires adaptation over long multi-turn horizons with feedback, necessitating compact representations of learned information. The authors want to explore if continuous latent context tokens can equip transformers for effective online learning.
AI.method: Two approaches: 1) Explicit constructions of constant-depth transformers that implement weighted majority algorithm and Q-learning by storing algorithmic state as linear combinations of feature embeddings using few latent context tokens; 2) Training a small GPT-2-style transformer with latent contexts using multi-curriculum objective without direct latent state supervision.
AI.result: On long synthetic online prediction sequences, the trained small transformer outperformed larger, more complex LLMs including Qwen-3-14B and DeepSeek-V3. The continuous latent contexts proved effective for implementing online learning algorithms.
AI.conclusion: Continuous latent contexts provide a simple and effective persistent state mechanism for transformers to implement online learning algorithms, bridging the gap between static in-context learning and dynamic online decision-making.

title: Intervention-Based Time Series Causal Discovery via Simulator-Generated Interventional Distributions
summary: We propose SVAR-FM (Structural VAR with Flow Matching), a framework for time series causal discovery that treats a physics-based simulator as a mechanical realization of Pearl's do operator. Clamping a variable inside the simulator physically severs confounding paths, producing interventional data by construction. Conditional Flow Matching then learns the nonlinear interventional conditionals. Theoretically, we prove that the full structural VAR becomes identifiable under a coverage condition on the simulator-clampable variables, and derive an end-to-end error bound that decomposes into Monte Carlo, simulator fidelity, and Flow Matching terms. A sign-flip corollary predicts that when simulator accuracy falls below a threshold, the estimated causal effect reverses sign. Empirically, a benchmark across four scientific domains confirms that SVAR-FM recovers the correct causal sign where observational methods produce sign-reversed estimates due to confounding. A case study in ultrafast laser physics verifies the sign-flip prediction by physically varying the accuracy level of a first-principles quantum solver: the low-accuracy setting reverses the causal sign, while the high-accuracy setting recovers the correct direction (R-squared = 0.983, zero bias).

AI.tldr: SVAR-FM is a causal discovery framework that uses physics simulators as intervention mechanisms, combines them with Conditional Flow Matching to learn interventional distributions, and achieves structural VAR identifiability with error bounds that predict sign reversals when simulator accuracy is poor.
AI.motivation: Existing observational methods for causal discovery often produce sign-reversed causal estimates due to confounding, particularly in scientific domains where experimental interventions are costly or impossible. The authors aim to leverage available physics-based simulators to create controlled interventions and overcome confounding issues.
AI.method: SVAR-FM treats physics simulators as implementations of Pearl's do-operator by clamping variables to physically sever confounding paths. It combines this with Conditional Flow Matching to learn nonlinear interventional conditionals from the simulated intervention data. The framework includes theoretical identifiability proofs under simulator coverage conditions.
AI.result: Theoretical results show structural VAR identifiability under coverage conditions, with error bounds decomposing into Monte Carlo, simulator fidelity, and Flow Matching terms. Empirical benchmarks across four scientific domains demonstrate SVAR-FM recovers correct causal signs where observational methods produce sign reversals. A case study in laser physics verifies the predicted sign-flip phenomenon (R-squared = 0.983).
AI.conclusion: SVAR-FM successfully bridges physics simulations and causal inference, enabling reliable causal discovery in scientific domains by using simulators as intervention mechanisms. The sign-flip prediction provides practical guidance on simulator accuracy requirements, making the framework robust to imperfect simulations while outperforming observational methods in confounded settings.

title: Key-Value Means
summary: We present Key-Value Means ("KVM"), a novel block-recurrence for attention that can accommodate either fixed-size or growing state. Equipping a strong transformer baseline with fixed-size KVM attention layers yields a strong $O(N)$ chunked RNN, while adding only an insignificant number of new parameters. We train a transformer with a growable KVM cache and show it performs competitively on long-context tests with only subquadratic prefill time and sublinear state growth. KVM is implementable with standard operations and without custom kernels, and supports chunk-wise parallelizable training and prefill. It provides many of the benefits of both traditional transformers (expandable context memory, chunk-wise parallelizable training and prefill) and linear RNNs in a single unified package. It can be used on every layer, saving KV-cache memory, and allowing a continuous range of choices of prefill time complexity between $O(N)$ and $O(N^2)$. It can also be implemented in a hybrid solution in tandem with LRNN layers in place of traditional attention, to supplement the LRNN with improved sublinear memory growth context length usage and long context decoding. We release our code at https://github.com/recursal/KVM-paper and trained models at https://huggingface.co/collections/recursal/key-value-means under the Apache 2.0 license.

AI.tldr: Key-Value Means (KVM) is a novel block-recurrence attention mechanism that combines benefits of transformers and linear RNNs, offering subquadratic prefill time, sublinear state growth, and efficient memory usage with chunk-wise parallelizable training.
AI.motivation: To address the limitations of traditional attention mechanisms which have quadratic prefill time complexity and linear state growth, while also overcoming constraints of linear RNNs that have fixed memory capacity. The goal is to create a unified approach that provides expandable context memory, efficient computation, and flexible memory-time trade-offs.
AI.method: KVM introduces a novel block-recurrence for attention that can work with either fixed-size or growing state. It implements standard operations without custom kernels, supports chunk-wise parallelizable training and prefill, and can be used on every transformer layer to save KV-cache memory. It offers a continuous range of prefill time complexity between O(N) and O(N²), and can be implemented in hybrid solutions with LRNN layers.
AI.result: Equipping transformers with fixed-size KVM attention yields strong O(N) chunked RNNs with minimal new parameters. Trained models with growable KVM cache perform competitively on long-context tests with subquadratic prefill time and sublinear state growth. The approach provides many benefits of both transformers and linear RNNs in a unified package.
AI.conclusion: KVM successfully bridges the gap between transformers and linear RNNs, offering a flexible, efficient attention mechanism that supports various memory-time trade-offs while maintaining competitive performance on long-context tasks, with implementations available as open-source.

title: The Geometric Wall: Manifold Structure Predicts Layerwise Sparse Autoencoder Scaling Laws
summary: Sparse autoencoders (SAEs) operationalise the linear representation hypothesis: they reconstruct model activations as sparse linear combinations of interpretable dictionary atoms, on the implicit assumption that activation space is well approximated by a globally linear structure. Their reconstruction error varies sharply across layers in ways that existing scaling laws, fitted at single layers, do not explain. We argue that this variation is the empirical trace of a geometric mismatch: where the activation manifold is curved and its intrinsic dimension varies across layers, no sparse linear dictionary can match it uniformly, and the SAE's width-sparsity scaling becomes a layer-dependent function of manifold structure rather than a single universal law. We conduct the first cross-layer SAE scaling study, fitting and regressing on 844 residual-stream Gemma Scope SAE checkpoints across 68 layers of Gemma 2 2B and 9B. Stage 1 fits a per-layer scaling-law surface; Stage 2 regresses the fitted parameters and the derived per-layer width exponents on four layerwise geometric summaries. We find that manifold geometry predicts the per-layer width exponent in both models, and that the same regression coefficients learnt on one model predict the other model's per-layer exponents under cross-model transfer, indicating a transferable geometric law. At the showcase layers where richer width grids permit identification of the asymptotic floor, we find that the fitted floor tracks the layerwise geometric ordering: higher curvature and intrinsic dimension correspond to higher floor, consistent with the irreducible second-order residual that any sparse linear approximation of a curved manifold must leave behind. SAEs thus encounter not a finite-resource ceiling but a geometry-dependent wall, set by the manifold they are trying to reconstruct.

AI.tldr: SAE reconstruction error varies across layers due to geometric mismatch between linear sparse dictionary and curved activation manifold, with scaling laws depending on layer-specific manifold geometry rather than being universal.
AI.motivation: Existing SAE scaling laws fitted at single layers cannot explain the sharp variation in reconstruction error across layers, suggesting a deeper geometric explanation is needed.
AI.method: Two-stage cross-layer SAE scaling study: 1) Fit per-layer scaling-law surfaces using 844 SAE checkpoints across 68 layers of Gemma 2 2B and 9B models; 2) Regress fitted parameters and derived per-layer width exponents on four layerwise geometric summaries of the activation manifold.
AI.result: Manifold geometry predicts per-layer width exponents in both models, with regression coefficients from one model successfully transferring to predict the other model's exponents. Higher curvature and intrinsic dimension correspond to higher asymptotic reconstruction floors.
AI.conclusion: SAEs encounter a geometry-dependent performance wall rather than just a finite-resource ceiling, as sparse linear approximations inherently leave irreducible residuals when reconstructing curved manifolds with varying intrinsic dimensions.

title: Rethinking Random Transformers as Adaptive Sequence Smoothers for Sleep Staging
summary: Automatic sleep staging commonly adopts Transformers under the assumption that they learn complex long-range dependencies. We challenge this view by revealing a neglected property of sleep sequences: strong local temporal continuity. We show that a randomly initialized Transformer, without any training, substantially improves sleep staging performance and consistently outperforms heuristic smoothing. We formalize this effect via a Random Attention Prior Kernel (RAPK), showing that random self-attention acts as an adaptive smoother by balancing global averaging and content-based similarity while preserving stage transitions. Using two metrics, the Local Smoothness Influence Index (LSII) and the Weighted Transition Entropy (WTE), we provide evidence that most performance gains in Transformer-based sleep staging arise from architectural inductive bias rather than parameter learning. Our results suggest that sleep staging can be effectively addressed with structure-driven smoothing mechanisms rather than complex dependency modeling, enabling more efficient and edge-deployable healthcare systems for large-scale physiological monitoring.

AI.tldr: Random Transformers without training improve sleep staging by leveraging inherent architectural smoothing biases, challenging the need for complex dependency learning.
AI.motivation: Challenge the assumption that Transformers need to learn complex long-range dependencies for sleep staging by revealing that sleep sequences have strong local temporal continuity properties that can be exploited through architectural inductive biases.
AI.method: Formalize the effect of random self-attention via Random Attention Prior Kernel (RAPK), showing it acts as adaptive smoother. Use two metrics: Local Smoothness Influence Index (LSII) to measure smoothing effects and Weighted Transition Entropy (WTE) to preserve stage transitions.
AI.result: Randomly initialized Transformers without training substantially improve sleep staging performance and consistently outperform heuristic smoothing. Most performance gains come from architectural inductive bias rather than parameter learning.
AI.conclusion: Sleep staging can be effectively addressed with structure-driven smoothing mechanisms rather than complex dependency modeling, enabling more efficient and edge-deployable healthcare systems for large-scale physiological monitoring.

title: Voice Biomarkers for Depression and Anxiety
summary: Current approaches to detecting depression and anxiety from speech primarily rely on machine learning techniques that utilize hand-engineered paralinguistic features and related acoustic descriptors derived from time- and frequency-domain representations of speech signals. Applying deep learning methods directly to raw speech signals has the potential to produce biomarker representations with substantially greater predictive power. However, these approaches typically require large volumes of carefully annotated data to learn robust and clinically meaningful representations of the underlying biomarkers.
