AI.motivation: Most current time series forecasting models treat the task as a fixed mapping from history to future horizons, which causes temporal decoupling across future time points and limits the model's ability to adapt to evolving contexts as forecasting progresses.
AI.method: LeapTS organizes forecasting as a dynamic scheduling process with: 1) hierarchical controller to dynamically select optimal prediction scale and advancement length at each step, and 2) continuous-time state evolution driven by neural controlled differential equations. The controlled update mechanism explicitly couples irregular temporal dynamics with discrete scheduling feedback.
AI.result: Extensive evaluations show LeapTS improves overall forecasting performance by at least 7.4% while achieving 2.6x to 5.3x inference speedup over representative Transformer-based models. The model autonomously adapts to capture non-stationary dynamics through explicit scheduling trajectory tracing.
AI.conclusion: Reformulating time series forecasting as a dynamic scheduling process with hierarchical control and neural differential equations is an effective approach that addresses limitations of fixed-mapping models, providing better performance, faster inference, and adaptive behavior for non-stationary dynamics.

title: BROS: Bias-Corrected Randomized Subspaces for Memory-Efficient Single-Loop Bilevel Optimization
summary: Stochastic bilevel optimization (SBO) has become a standard framework for hyperparameter learning, data reweighting, representation learning, and data-mixture optimization in deep learning. Existing exact single-loop SBO methods and memory-efficient surrogate SBO methods either create severe memory pressure for large lower-level neural networks or lack competitive convergence guarantees under standard assumptions. In this paper, we propose BROS, a memory-efficient single-loop SBO method with the same convergence rate order as exact single-loop SBO methods. BROS performs lower and auxiliary updates in randomized subspaces with a Rademacher bi-probe correction that recovers an unbiased Hessian-action estimator. We prove that BROS preserves the $\mathcal O(\varepsilon^{-2})$ sample complexity of MA-SOBA for finding an $\varepsilon$-stationary point under only standard assumptions. Experiments on hyper-data cleaning, data-mixture learning, hyper-representation learning, and ViT sample reweighting show that BROS reduces peak memory by up to 44.9% while closely matching full-space baseline performance.

AI.tldr: BROS is a memory-efficient single-loop stochastic bilevel optimization method that achieves the same convergence rate as exact methods while reducing memory usage by up to 44.9% through randomized subspace updates and Rademacher bi-probe correction.
AI.motivation: Current SBO methods face limitations: exact single-loop methods create severe memory pressure for large neural networks, while memory-efficient surrogate methods lack competitive convergence guarantees under standard assumptions. There's a need for a method that combines memory efficiency with strong convergence properties.
AI.method: BROS performs lower and auxiliary updates in randomized subspaces with a Rademacher bi-probe correction technique that recovers an unbiased Hessian-action estimator. This enables single-loop optimization while operating in reduced-dimensional subspaces to save memory.
AI.result: BROS maintains O(ε⁻²) sample complexity (same as MA-SOBA) for finding ε-stationary points under standard assumptions, while reducing peak memory by up to 44.9% across applications including hyper-data cleaning, data-mixture learning, hyper-representation learning, and ViT sample reweighting.
AI.conclusion: BROS successfully bridges the gap between memory efficiency and convergence guarantees in stochastic bilevel optimization, providing a practical solution for large-scale deep learning applications without sacrificing theoretical performance.

title: Robust Probabilistic Shielding for Safe Offline Reinforcement Learning
summary: In offline reinforcement learning (RL), we learn policies from fixed datasets without environment interaction. The major challenges are to provide guarantees on the (1) performance and (2) safety of the resulting policy. A technique called safe policy improvement (SPI) provides a performance guarantee: with high probability, the new policy outperforms a given baseline policy, which is assumed to be safe. Orthogonally, in the context of safe RL, a shield provides a safety guarantee by restricting the action space to those actions that are provably safe with respect to a given safety-relevant model. We integrate these paradigms by extending shielding to offline RL, relying solely on the available dataset and knowledge of safe and unsafe states. Then, we shield the policy improvement steps, guaranteeing, with high probability, a safe policy. Experimental results demonstrate that shielded SPI outperforms its unshielded counterpart, improving both average and worst-case performance, particularly in low-data regimes.

AI.tldr: Shielded Safe Policy Improvement (SPI) integrates safety constraints from safe RL shields with performance guarantees from offline RL SPI to produce policies that are both safe and high-performing.
AI.motivation: Offline RL faces dual challenges: (1) providing performance guarantees for learned policies compared to a baseline, and (2) ensuring safety of resulting policies when deployed. While SPI addresses performance guarantees and shields provide safety guarantees in safe RL, these approaches haven't been integrated for offline RL scenarios.
AI.method: The authors integrate shielding into offline RL by extending shielding techniques to work with fixed datasets rather than environment interaction. They rely solely on available offline data and knowledge of safe/unsafe states to create a shield that restricts action spaces to provably safe actions. This shield is then applied to policy improvement steps in the SPI framework.
AI.result: Experimental results show that shielded SPI outperforms unshielded SPI, improving both average and worst-case performance, particularly in low-data regimes. The method successfully guarantees a safe policy with high probability while maintaining performance benefits.
AI.conclusion: Integrating shielding with safe policy improvement provides a promising approach for offline RL that addresses both performance and safety concerns simultaneously, making it especially valuable in low-data scenarios where safety risks are higher.

title: Sample-Mean Anchored Thompson Sampling for Offline-to-Online Learning with Distribution Shift
summary: Offline-to-online learning aims to improve online decision-making by leveraging offline logged data. A central challenge in this setting is the distribution shift between offline and online environments. While some existing works attempt to leverage shifted offline data, they largely rely on UCB-type algorithms. Thompson sampling (TS) represents another canonical class of bandit algorithms, well known for its strong empirical performance and naturally suited to offline-to-online learning through its Bayesian formulation. However, unlike UCB indices, posterior samples in TS are not guaranteed to be optimistic with respect to the true arm means. This makes indices constructed from purely online and hybrid data difficult to compare and complicates their use. To address this issue, we propose sample-mean anchored TS (Anchor-TS), which introduces a novel median-based anchoring rule that defines the arm index as the median of an online posterior sample, a hybrid posterior sample, and the online sample mean. The median anchoring systematically corrects bias induced by distribution shift by mitigating over-estimation for suboptimal arms and under-estimation for optimal arms, while exploiting offline information to obtain more accurate estimates when the shift is small. We establish theoretical guarantees showing that the proposed algorithm safely leverages offline data to accelerate online learning, and quantifying how the degree of distribution shift and the size of offline data affect the resulting regret reduction. Extensive experiments demonstrate consistent improvements of our algorithm over baselines.

AI.tldr: Anchor-TS: A Thompson sampling variant that uses median anchoring to safely leverage offline data for online learning while handling distribution shifts.
AI.motivation: Existing offline-to-online learning approaches largely rely on UCB-type algorithms, while Thompson sampling is naturally suited through Bayesian formulation but lacks optimism guarantees for posterior samples when comparing online and hybrid data.
AI.method: Proposes sample-mean anchored TS (Anchor-TS) with a median-based anchoring rule that defines arm index as median of online posterior sample, hybrid posterior sample, and online sample mean to correct bias from distribution shift.
AI.result: Theoretical guarantees show Anchor-TS safely leverages offline data to accelerate online learning, quantifying how distribution shift degree and offline data size affect regret reduction; extensive experiments demonstrate consistent improvements over baselines.
AI.conclusion: Anchor-TS effectively addresses the offline-to-online learning challenge by enabling safe and efficient use of offline data through median anchoring in Thompson sampling framework.

title: Active Tabular Augmentation via Policy-Guided Diffusion Inpainting
summary: Generative tabular augmentation is appealing in data-scarce domains, yet the prevailing focus on distributional fidelity does not reliably translate into better downstream models. We formalize a fidelity-utility gap: common generative objectives prioritize distributional plausibility, whereas augmentation succeeds only when injected samples reduce the current learner's held-out evaluation loss. This gap motivates learning not just how to generate, but what to generate and when to inject as training evolves. We propose TAP (Tabular Augmentation Policy), which couples diffusion inpainting with a lightweight, learner-conditioned policy to steer generation toward high-utility regions and controls safe injection via explicit gating and conservative windowed commitment. Under severe data scarcity, TAP consistently outperforms strong generative baselines on seven real-world datasets, improving classification accuracy by up to 15.6 percentage points and reducing regression RMSE by up to 32%.

AI.tldr: TAP addresses the fidelity-utility gap in generative tabular data augmentation by using a diffusion inpainting model with a learner-conditioned policy to strategically generate and inject high-utility samples during training.
AI.motivation: There's a gap between distributional fidelity (what current generative models focus on) and actual utility for downstream tasks. While generative augmentation is promising for data-scarce domains, simply generating plausible samples doesn't guarantee better performance - samples must actually reduce the learner's evaluation loss.
AI.method: TAP couples diffusion inpainting with a lightweight, learner-conditioned policy that steers generation toward high-utility regions. It controls injection via explicit gating and conservative windowed commitment to ensure generated samples are beneficial at each training stage.
AI.result: Under severe data scarcity, TAP consistently outperforms strong generative baselines on seven real-world datasets, achieving up to 15.6 percentage point improvement in classification accuracy and up to 32% reduction in regression RMSE.
AI.conclusion: The fidelity-utility gap is real and significant. Successful generative augmentation requires not just learning how to generate, but also what to generate and when to inject samples. TAP demonstrates that learner-aware generation and strategic injection substantially improve downstream task performance in data-scarce settings.

title: Relations Are Channels: Knowledge Graph Embedding via Kraus Decompositions
summary: Knowledge graph embedding (KGE) models typically represent each relation as an operator on entity embeddings. In this work, we identify three structural axioms that any principled relation operator must satisfy, linearity, trace preservation, and complete positivity, and show that they characterize a Kraus channel structure via the Kraus representation theorem. The completeness constraint defining this family is equivalent to these axioms, providing a principled foundation rather than an externally imposed condition. Under this formulation, most existing operator-based KGE models are recoverable as special cases with Kraus rank $κ= 1$ under specific embedding choices. We further generalize this characterization to arbitrary metric geometries by introducing \mbox{w-Kraus} channels, which satisfy completeness by construction within their respective spaces. Building on this theory, we propose \textsc{KrausKGE}, a principled KGE model that naturally handles $1$-to-$N$ and $N$-to-$N$ relations, supports $k$-hop reasoning without requiring explicit path encoders, and eliminates the need for norm constraints on entity embeddings. Additionally, our framework yields the first theoretically grounded per-relation complexity measure in the KGE literature, with a provable lower bound in terms of the empirical relation matrix rank. Empirical evaluation demonstrates that \textsc{KrausKGE} consistently outperforms strong baselines on $N$-to-$N$ relations, with performance gains that increase monotonically with relation fan-out, in alignment with theoretical predictions.

AI.tldr: KrausKGE: A knowledge graph embedding model based on Kraus channels that satisfies principled structural axioms, naturally handles complex N-to-N relations, enables k-hop reasoning without path encoders, and introduces the first theoretically-grounded per-relation complexity measure.
AI.motivation: Most existing operator-based KGE models lack principled foundations for relation operators. The paper identifies that proper relation operators should satisfy three structural axioms: linearity, trace preservation, and complete positivity. Current models impose completeness as an external constraint rather than deriving it from fundamental principles.
AI.method: The authors use the Kraus representation theorem to show that the three axioms characterize a Kraus channel structure. They generalize this to arbitrary metric geometries via w-Kraus channels that satisfy completeness by construction. Based on this theory, they propose KrausKGE, which uses Kraus rank κ≥1 operators that don't require norm constraints on entity embeddings.
AI.result: KrausKGE consistently outperforms strong baselines on N-to-N relations, with performance gains that increase monotonically with relation fan-out. Most existing operator-based KGE models are recoverable as special cases with Kraus rank κ=1. The framework provides the first theoretically grounded per-relation complexity measure with a provable lower bound based on empirical relation matrix rank.
AI.conclusion: The Kraus channel formulation provides a principled foundation for KGE relation operators satisfying fundamental structural axioms. KrausKGE naturally handles complex relations and k-hop reasoning, eliminates embedding constraints, and offers theoretical complexity measures, advancing the theoretical foundations of knowledge graph embedding.

title: PowerStep: Memory-Efficient Adaptive Optimization via $\ell_p$-Norm Steepest Descent
summary: Adaptive optimizers, most notably Adam, have become the default standard for training large-scale neural networks such as Transformers. These methods maintain running estimates of gradient first and second moments, incurring substantial memory overhead. We introduce PowerStep, a memory-efficient optimizer that achieves coordinate-wise adaptivity without storing second-moment statistics. Motivated by steepest descent under an $\ell_p$-norm geometry, we show that applying a nonlinear transform directly to a momentum buffer yields coordinate-wise adaptivity. We prove that PowerStep converges at the optimal $O(1/\sqrt{T})$ rate for non-convex stochastic optimization. Extensive experiments on Transformer models ranging from 124M to 235B parameters demonstrate that PowerStep matches Adam's convergence speed while halving optimizer memory. Furthermore, when combined with aggressive \texttt{int8} quantization, PowerStep remains numerically stable and reduces optimizer memory by $\sim\!8\times$ compared to full-precision Adam. PowerStep thus provides a principled, scalable and resource-efficient alternative for large-scale training. Code is available at https://github.com/yaolubrain/PowerStep.

AI.tldr: PowerStep is a memory-efficient adaptive optimizer that matches Adam's performance without storing second-moment statistics, halving optimizer memory while maintaining optimal convergence rates.
AI.motivation: Adaptive optimizers like Adam require storing running estimates of gradient first and second moments, which incurs substantial memory overhead, especially for large-scale neural networks like Transformers with billions of parameters.
AI.method: PowerStep achieves coordinate-wise adaptivity without second-moment statistics by applying a nonlinear transform directly to a momentum buffer, motivated by steepest descent under an ℓ_p-norm geometry.
AI.result: PowerStep achieves optimal O(1/√T) convergence rate for non-convex stochastic optimization, matches Adam's convergence speed on Transformer models from 124M to 235B parameters while halving optimizer memory, and with int8 quantization reduces memory by ~8× compared to full-precision Adam.
AI.conclusion: PowerStep provides a principled, scalable, and resource-efficient alternative to Adam for large-scale training, offering substantial memory savings without sacrificing convergence performance.

title: Set Prediction for Next-Day Active Fire Forecasting
summary: Accurate next-day active fire forecasts can support early warning, disaster response, forest risk assessment, and downstream estimation of fire-related carbon emissions. Existing machine learning approaches to wildfire forecasting typically predict wildfire danger or fire probability on kilometre-scale daily grids, which is useful for regional warning but does not directly represent localized fire events. We propose Wildfire Ignition Set Predictor (WISP), a query-based model that reformulates next-day active fire forecasting as point-set prediction. From 48 hours of covariates including meteorology, satellite vegetation products, static land, and fire history, WISP predicts a fixed-size ranked set of future active fire cluster centres on a 375 m grid across globally distributed regions. The model is trained end-to-end with Hungarian matching; to address the conflicting roles of the classification score in assignment, ranking, and query activation, we use asymmetric classification-localization weighting in matching and loss. We further construct a globally distributed, hourly, multi-source benchmark for this task. On a held-out test set spanning fire regions worldwide, the best WISP variant achieves 38.2% average precision (AP) for ranked fire-centre detections, covers 53.4% of fire cluster mass weighted by fire radiative power (FRP), and localizes 54.1% of observed clusters within 5 km. These results establish sparse set prediction as a viable formulation for high-resolution wildfire forecasting and provide a benchmark for future work in this regime.

AI.tldr: WISP is a query-based model that reformulates next-day active fire forecasting as point-set prediction, predicting fixed-size ranked sets of future fire cluster centers at 375m resolution globally, achieving 38.2% AP and covering 53.4% of fire cluster mass.
AI.motivation: Accurate next-day fire forecasts are crucial for early warning, disaster response, and carbon emissions estimation. Existing ML approaches predict wildfire danger or probability on kilometer-scale grids, which is useful for regional warning but doesn't directly represent localized fire events, creating a gap in high-resolution fire event forecasting.
AI.method: WISP (Wildfire Ignition Set Predictor) uses a query-based model that reformulates fire forecasting as point-set prediction. It takes 48 hours of covariates including meteorology, satellite vegetation, static land data, and fire history, then predicts fixed-size ranked sets of future active fire cluster centers on a 375m grid. The model is trained end-to-end with Hungarian matching and uses asymmetric classification-localization weighting to address conflicting roles of classification score.
AI.result: On a held-out global test set: 38.2% average precision for ranked fire-center detections, covers 53.4% of fire cluster mass weighted by fire radiative power, and localizes 54.1% of observed clusters within 5km. The study also constructed a globally distributed, hourly, multi-source benchmark for this task.
AI.conclusion: Sparse set prediction is a viable formulation for high-resolution wildfire forecasting. WISP establishes a benchmark for future work in this regime, demonstrating that direct prediction of fire events as point sets can complement existing regional-scale fire danger predictions.

title: Real vs. Semi-Simulated: Rethinking Evaluation for Treatment Effect Estimation
summary: Estimating heterogeneous treatment effects with machine learning has attracted substantial attention in both academic research and industrial practice. However, the two communities often evaluate models under markedly different conditions. Methodological work typically relies on semi-simulated benchmarks and metrics that require counterfactual outcomes, whereas real-world applications rely on observable metrics based on ranking or test outcomes. Despite the well-known gap between methodological progress and practical deployment, the relationship between these evaluation regimes has not been examined systematically. We conduct a large-scale empirical study of treatment effect evaluation across standard semi-simulated benchmark families and real-world datasets. Our benchmark covers meta-learners paired with multiple base learners, as well as specialized causal machine learning models. We evaluate these methods using observable metrics common in application-oriented literature, alongside counterfactual metrics commonly used in methods papers. Our results reveal two complementary gaps. First, counterfactual metrics do not reliably recover the estimators preferred by observable metrics, even on the same semi-simulated benchmarks. Second, rankings obtained on semi-simulated benchmarks do not transfer to real datasets. We further find that simple meta-learners with strong base models are consistently competitive, in contrast to specialized causal models. Overall, our findings suggest that progress in treatment effect estimation research should not be assessed solely through counterfactual metrics and semi-simulated benchmarks, but it would benefit from incorporating observable metrics and real-data validation.

AI.tldr: The paper reveals significant gaps between how causal ML methods are evaluated in academic research (using counterfactual metrics on semi-simulated data) versus real-world applications (using observable metrics), showing these evaluation regimes don't align and simple meta-learners often outperform specialized causal models.
AI.motivation: There's a disconnect between academic methodological research on heterogeneous treatment effect estimation (using counterfactual metrics on semi-simulated benchmarks) and practical industrial applications (using observable metrics on real data), but the relationship between these evaluation regimes hasn't been systematically examined.
AI.method: Conducted a large-scale empirical study comparing treatment effect evaluation across standard semi-simulated benchmark families and real-world datasets. Evaluated meta-learners with various base learners and specialized causal ML models using both observable metrics (common in applications) and counterfactual metrics (common in methods papers).
AI.result: Found two complementary gaps: 1) Counterfactual metrics don't reliably identify estimators preferred by observable metrics, even on the same semi-simulated benchmarks; 2) Rankings from semi-simulated benchmarks don't transfer to real datasets. Also found that simple meta-learners with strong base models consistently compete with specialized causal models.
AI.conclusion: Progress in treatment effect estimation research should not be assessed solely through counterfactual metrics and semi-simulated benchmarks, but should incorporate observable metrics and real-data validation to bridge the gap between methodological research and practical deployment.

title: Nearly-Optimal Algorithm for Adversarial Kernelized Bandits
summary: This paper studies kernelized bandits (also known as Gaussian process bandits) in an adversarial environment, where the reward functions in a known reproducing kernel Hilbert space (RKHS) may be adversarially chosen at each round. We show that the exponential-weight algorithm achieves $\tilde{O}(\sqrt{T γ_T})$ adversarial regret, where $T$ and $γ_T$ denote the number of total rounds and the maximum information gain, respectively. For squared exponential (SE) and $ν$-Matérn kernels, we also show algorithm-independent lower bounds that guarantee the optimality of our algorithm up to polylogarithmic factors. Furthermore, we present a computationally efficient variant of our algorithm using Nyström approximation while maintaining nearly optimal regret guarantees.

AI.tldr: This paper analyzes kernelized bandits in adversarial environments, showing exponential-weight algorithm achieves near-optimal adversarial regret bounds with efficient variants via Nyström approximation.
AI.motivation: Kernelized bandits (Gaussian process bandits) typically assume stochastic environments, but real-world applications often involve adversarial reward functions. There's a need to understand algorithm performance and optimality guarantees when reward functions in RKHS are adversarially chosen at each round.
AI.method: Uses exponential-weight algorithm for adversarial kernelized bandits, with theoretical analysis of adversarial regret bounds. Also develops computationally efficient variant using Nyström approximation to maintain near-optimal performance while reducing computational complexity.
AI.result: Proves exponential-weight algorithm achieves $\tilde{O}(\sqrt{T γ_T})$ adversarial regret, where $T$ is total rounds and $γ_T$ is maximum information gain. Shows algorithm-independent lower bounds for SE and Matérn kernels proving optimality up to polylog factors. Provides efficient algorithm variant with Nyström approximation maintaining similar regret guarantees.
AI.conclusion: The paper establishes that exponential-weight algorithms are near-optimal for adversarial kernelized bandits, with efficient implementations possible through Nyström approximation, bridging stochastic and adversarial kernel bandit theory while maintaining practical computational feasibility.

title: Follow the Mean: Reference-Guided Flow Matching
summary: Existing approaches to controllable generation typically rely on fine-tuning, auxiliary networks, or test-time search. We show that flow matching admits a different control interface: adaptation through examples. For deterministic interpolants, the velocity field is solely governed by a conditional endpoint mean; shifting this mean shifts the flow itself. This yields a simple principle for controllable generation: steer a pretrained model by changing the reference set it follows. We instantiate this idea in two forms. Reference-Mean Guidance is training-free: it computes a closed-form endpoint-mean correction from a reference bank and applies it to a frozen FLUX.2-klein (4B) model, enabling control of color, identity, style, and structure while keeping the prompt, seed, and weights fixed. Semi-Parametric Guidance amortizes the same idea through an explicit mean anchor and learned residual refiner, matching unconditional DiT-B/4 quality on AFHQv2 while allowing the reference set to be swapped at inference time. These results point to a broader direction: generative models that adapt through data, not parameter updates.

AI.tldr: Training-free and semi-parametric control of generative models by adjusting reference sets rather than model parameters, enabling controllable generation through example-based adaptation.
AI.motivation: Existing controllable generation methods typically require fine-tuning, auxiliary networks, or test-time search, which are resource-intensive. The authors seek a simpler, more efficient approach to controlling generative models without modifying model parameters.
AI.method: Two approaches based on flow matching: 1) Reference-Mean Guidance (training-free) computes closed-form endpoint-mean corrections from reference data and applies them to frozen models; 2) Semi-Parametric Guidance uses an explicit mean anchor with learned residual refiner, allowing reference set swapping at inference time.
AI.result: Successfully controls color, identity, style, and structure in FLUX.2-klein (4B) model while keeping prompt, seed, and weights fixed. Semi-Parametric Guidance matches unconditional DiT-B/4 quality on AFHQv2 dataset while maintaining reference flexibility.
AI.conclusion: Flow matching provides a novel control interface through example adaptation rather than parameter updates, pointing toward a broader paradigm of data-adaptive generative models that can be steered by changing reference sets.

title: Formally Verifying Analog Neural Networks Under Process Variations Using Polynomial Zonotopes
summary: Analog neural networks are gaining attention due to their efficiency in terms of power consumption and processing speed. However, since analog neural networks are implemented as physical circuits, they are highly sensitive to manufacturing process variations, which can cause large deviations from the nominal model. We present a polynomial-based model that resembles the performance of the neuron circuit under process variations. Then, we formally verify the behavior of the circuit-level model using reachability analysis with polynomial zonotopes, thus, avoiding conventional, time-consuming Monte Carlo simulations. We evaluate our proposed verification approach on three different datasets, verifying both fully-connected and convolutional analog neural networks. Our experimental results confirm the effectiveness of our verification approach by reducing the verification time from days to seconds while enclosing 99% of the variation samples.

AI.tldr: Formal verification approach using polynomial zonotopes for analog neural networks under process variations, reducing verification time from days to seconds while capturing 99% of variation samples.
AI.motivation: Analog neural networks are efficient but highly sensitive to manufacturing process variations, causing large deviations from nominal models. Traditional verification methods like Monte Carlo simulations are time-consuming.
AI.method: Develop polynomial-based model to capture neuron circuit performance under variations. Use formal verification with reachability analysis using polynomial zonotopes instead of Monte Carlo simulations.
AI.result: Verified approach on three datasets with both fully-connected and convolutional analog neural networks. Reduced verification time from days to seconds while enclosing 99% of variation samples.
AI.conclusion: The polynomial zonotope-based formal verification approach is effective for verifying analog neural networks under process variations, offering dramatic speed improvements while maintaining high coverage.

title: Signature Approach for Contextual Bandits with Nonlinear and Path-dependent Rewards
summary: We study contextual bandits with nonlinear and path-dependent rewards through a novel signature-transform-based approach. Leveraging the universal nonlinearity property of signatures, we approximate continuous path-dependent reward functionals by linear functionals in the signature space. This representation enables the use of efficient linear contextual bandit methods while preserving expressive sequential structure. Building on this framework, we propose \texttt{DisSigUCB}, a signature-based disjoint upper confidence bound (UCB) algorithm. Under boundedness and non-degeneracy assumptions, we prove a high-probability data-dependent sublinear regret bound of order \(\tilde{\mathcal O}(\sqrt{(d+m)KT})\) where \(d\) is the context dimension and \(m\) is the signature feature dimension. Synthetic experiments and numerical applications on temperature sensor monitoring, sleep-stage classification, and hospital nurse staffing demonstrate that \texttt{DisSigUCB} consistently outperforms classical linear and kernelized contextual bandit baselines in nonlinear and path-dependent settings.

AI.tldr: The paper introduces a signature-transform-based approach for contextual bandits with nonlinear and path-dependent rewards, proposing the DisSigUCB algorithm that achieves sublinear regret with proven theoretical guarantees and outperforms baseline methods in practical applications.
AI.motivation: Contextual bandits with nonlinear and path-dependent reward functions present significant challenges because traditional linear contextual bandit methods fail to capture complex sequential dependencies and nonlinear reward patterns commonly found in real-world applications like sensor monitoring, medical classification, and resource allocation.
AI.method: The authors use signature transforms to map continuous path-dependent reward functionals into the signature space, leveraging signatures' universal nonlinearity property to approximate nonlinear rewards as linear functionals in this space. They then propose DisSigUCB (Disjoint Signature Upper Confidence Bound), a novel algorithm that applies linear contextual bandit methods in the signature-transformed space while maintaining sequential structure.
AI.result: Theoretical analysis proves a high-probability data-dependent sublinear regret bound of order Õ(√((d+m)KT)), where d is context dimension, m is signature feature dimension, K is number of arms, and T is time horizon. Empirical results show DisSigUCB consistently outperforms classical linear and kernelized contextual bandit baselines on synthetic experiments and real-world applications including temperature sensor monitoring, sleep-stage classification, and hospital nurse staffing.
AI.conclusion: Signature transforms provide an effective framework for handling nonlinear and path-dependent rewards in contextual bandits, enabling the application of efficient linear methods while maintaining expressive power. The DisSigUCB algorithm offers both theoretical guarantees and practical performance advantages over existing approaches in sequential decision-making problems with complex reward structures.

title: CMKL: Modality-Aware Continual Learning for Evolving Biomedical Knowledge Graphs
summary: Biomedical knowledge graphs are increasingly large, dynamic, and multimodal, driven by rapid advances in biotechnology such as high-throughput sequencing. Machine learning models can infer previously unobserved biomedical relationships and characterize biomedical entities in these graphs, but existing knowledge graph embedding methods and their continual learning extensions either assume static graph structure or fail to exploit multimodal information under evolving data distributions. They also apply uniform regularization across all model parameters, ignoring that different modalities may exhibit distinct forgetting dynamics as the graph evolves. We propose the Continual Multimodal Knowledge Graph Learner (CMKL), a CL framework for biomedical KGs that natively encodes structure, text, and molecules, fuses them through a Mixture-of-Experts (MoE) router, and protects previously learned knowledge with standard EWC regularization and a K-means-diverse multimodal replay buffer. We evaluate CMKL on a 129K-entity biomedical continual benchmark with 10 tasks. On continual biomedical entity classification, CMKL reaches AP 0.591 versus 0.370 for the strongest structural baseline, a 60% gain that is driven by access to multimodal features and preserved across the sequence with near-zero forgetting (AF 0.008). On continual relationship prediction, CMKL reaches AP $0.062$, matching Naive Sequential and EWC (0.058) within seed noise and outperforming Joint Training (0.047, p=0.045) and LKGE (0.039). A frozen-text ablation reaches AP 0.136, more than double any jointly trained model, yet that signal is unreachable by margin-ranking gradients: the greedy-modality asymmetry lives at the representation level, not the fusion level, and MoE routing manages it by suppressing the unreachable modality without forcing it through a learned bottleneck. Code: github.com/yradwan147/cmkl-neurips2026

AI.tldr: CMKL is a continual multimodal knowledge graph learning framework for biomedical KGs that handles evolving structure, text, and molecular data with Mixture-of-Experts fusion and specialized forgetting prevention.
AI.motivation: Biomedical knowledge graphs are becoming large, dynamic, and multimodal, but existing KG embedding methods either assume static graphs or fail to effectively use multimodal information under evolving data distributions. They also use uniform regularization across all parameters, ignoring that different modalities may have different forgetting dynamics.
AI.method: Proposes Continual Multimodal Knowledge Graph Learner (CMKL) framework that: 1) Natively encodes structure, text, and molecules, 2) Fuses them through a Mixture-of-Experts (MoE) router, 3) Uses standard EWC regularization and a K-means-diverse multimodal replay buffer to prevent forgetting, 4) Specifically handles different forgetting dynamics across modalities.
AI.result: On biomedical entity classification (129K-entity benchmark with 10 tasks): AP 0.591 vs 0.370 for best structural baseline (60% gain). Near-zero forgetting (AF 0.008). On relationship prediction: AP 0.062, matching Naive Sequential and EWC, outperforming Joint Training (0.047) and LKGE (0.039). Frozen-text ablation reaches AP 0.136 (double any jointly trained model), showing modality asymmetry at representation level.
AI.conclusion: CMKL effectively handles continual learning on multimodal biomedical KGs by addressing modality-specific forgetting dynamics and using MoE routing to manage modality asymmetry. The framework significantly outperforms baselines and shows that modality asymmetry exists at the representation level, not fusion level.

title: HH-SAE: Discovering and Steering Hierarchical Knowledge of Complex Manifolds
summary: Rare semantic innovations in high-dimensional, mission-critical domains are often obscured by dense background contexts, a challenge we define as \textit{feature density conflict}. We introduce the \textbf{Hybrid Hierarchical SAE (HH-SAE)} to resolve this by factorizing manifolds into a nested hierarchy of \textbf{Contextual} ($L_0$), \textbf{Atomic} ($f_1$), and \textbf{Compository} ($f_2$) tiers. Evaluating across disparate manifolds, HH-SAE demonstrates superior resolution by \textbf{``fracturing'' administrative clinical labels into physiological modes} and achieving a peak \textbf{cross-domain zero-shot AUC of 0.9156 in fraud detection}. Path ablation confirms the architecture's structural necessity, revealing a 13.46\% utility collapse when contextual subtraction is removed. Finally, knowledge-steered synthesis achieves a +9.9\% AUPRC lift over state-of-the-art generators, proving that HH-SAE effectively prioritizes high-order mechanistic innovation over environmental proxies to enable high-precision discovery in high-stakes environments.

AI.tldr: HH-SAE is a hierarchical sparse autoencoder that resolves feature density conflicts by factorizing data manifolds into contextual, atomic, and compository tiers, enabling better detection of rare semantic innovations in high-stakes domains.
AI.motivation: Rare semantic innovations in mission-critical domains are obscured by dense background contexts (feature density conflict), making them hard to detect.
AI.method: Proposed Hybrid Hierarchical SAE (HH-SAE) that factorizes manifolds into nested hierarchy: Contextual (L₀), Atomic (f₁), and Compository (f₂) tiers.
AI.result: Superior resolution in clinical data (fracturing administrative labels into physiological modes), 0.9156 cross-domain zero-shot AUC in fraud detection, and +9.9% AUPRC lift over SOTA generators in knowledge-steered synthesis.
AI.conclusion: HH-SAE effectively prioritizes high-order mechanistic innovation over environmental proxies, enabling high-precision discovery in high-stakes environments by resolving feature density conflicts.

title: Fairness vs Performance: Characterizing the Pareto Frontier of Algorithmic Decision Systems
comment: 23 pages, The 2026 ACM conference on Fairness, Accountability, and Transparency (FAccT'26)
summary:
Designing fair algorithmic decision systems requires balancing model performance with fairness toward affected individuals: More fairness might require sacrificing some performance and vice versa, yet the space of possible trade-offs is still poorly understood. We investigate fairness in binary prediction-based decision problems by conceptualizing decision making as a multi-objective optimization problem that simultaneously considers decision-maker utility and group fairness. We investigate the set of Pareto-optimal decision rules for arbitrary utility functions for decision maker, arbitrary population distributions, and a wide range of group fairness metrics.
  We find that the Pareto frontier consists of deterministic, group-specific threshold rules applied to individuals' success probability. This complements existing optimality theorems from literature which, for specific fairness constraints, posit lower-bound threshold rules only. However we also show that, depending on the used fairness metric, the Pareto frontier may include upper-bound threshold rules, thus preferring individuals with lower success probabilities. We show that the location of the Pareto frontier depends only on population characteristics, utility functions and fairness score, but not on the technical design of the algorithm - our findings hold for pre-, in-, and post-processing approaches alike.
  Our results generalize existing optimality theorems for fairness-constrained classification and extend them to generalized fairness metrics and fairness principles, and to partial fairness regimes. This paper connects formal fairness research with legal and ethical requirements to search for less discriminatory alternatives, offering a principled foundation for evaluating and comparing algorithmic decision systems.

AI.tldr: The paper establishes that for binary decision problems, Pareto-optimal fairness-performance trade-offs are achieved through deterministic, group-specific threshold rules on success probability, with frontiers containing both lower-bound and upper-bound thresholds depending on fairness metrics.
AI.motivation: Current understanding of fairness-performance trade-offs in algorithmic decision systems is limited, despite the need to balance model performance with fairness toward affected individuals. The space of possible trade-offs remains poorly understood, creating challenges for designing systems that meet both technical and ethical requirements.
AI.method: The authors conceptualize decision making as a multi-objective optimization problem considering decision-maker utility and group fairness simultaneously. They investigate Pareto-optimal decision rules for arbitrary utility functions, arbitrary population distributions, and a wide range of group fairness metrics.
AI.result: The Pareto frontier consists of deterministic, group-specific threshold rules applied to individuals' success probabilities. This includes both lower-bound and upper-bound threshold rules depending on the fairness metric used. The location of the Pareto frontier depends only on population characteristics, utility functions, and fairness scores, not on algorithmic design approaches (pre-, in-, or post-processing).
AI.conclusion: The findings generalize existing optimality theorems for fairness-constrained classification to broader fairness metrics, fairness principles, and partial fairness regimes. This provides a principled foundation for evaluating and comparing algorithmic decision systems while connecting formal fairness research with legal and ethical requirements to search for less discriminatory alternatives.

title: A Recursive Decomposition Framework for Causal Structure Learning in the Presence of Latent Variables
summary: Constraint-based causal discovery is widely used for learning causal structures, but heavy reliance on conditional independence (CI) testing makes it computationally expensive in high-dimensional settings. To mitigate this limitation, many divide-and-conquer frameworks have been proposed, but most assume causal sufficiency, i.e., no latent variables. In this paper, we show that divide-and-conquer strategies can be theoretically generalized beyond causal sufficiency to settings with latent variables. Specifically, we propose a recursive decomposition framework, termed DiCoLa, that enables divide-and-conquer causal discovery in the presence of latent variables. It recursively decomposes the global learning task into smaller subproblems and integrates their solutions through a principled reconstruction step to recover the global structure. We theoretically establish the soundness and completeness of the proposed framework. Extensive experiments on synthetic data demonstrate that our approach significantly improves computational efficiency across a range of causal discovery algorithms, while experiments on a real-world dataset further illustrate its practical effectiveness.

AI.tldr: DiCoLa: A recursive decomposition framework that enables divide-and-conquer causal discovery in the presence of latent variables, significantly improving computational efficiency while maintaining theoretical soundness.
AI.motivation: Constraint-based causal discovery methods are computationally expensive in high-dimensional settings due to heavy reliance on conditional independence testing. While divide-and-conquer frameworks exist to mitigate this limitation, most assume causal sufficiency (no latent variables), which limits their practical applicability.
AI.method: Proposes DiCoLa, a recursive decomposition framework that generalizes divide-and-conquer strategies beyond causal sufficiency. It recursively decomposes the global learning task into smaller subproblems and integrates their solutions through a principled reconstruction step to recover the global structure.
AI.result: Theoretical soundness and completeness of the framework are established. Extensive experiments on synthetic data show significant improvements in computational efficiency across various causal discovery algorithms. Real-world dataset experiments further demonstrate practical effectiveness.
AI.conclusion: Divide-and-conquer strategies can be successfully generalized to handle latent variables through the DiCoLa framework, achieving both theoretical guarantees and practical computational efficiency gains in causal discovery.

title: Foundations of Reliable Inference: Reliability-Efficiency Co-Design
summary: Reliable inference requires that artificial intelligence (AI) models provide trustworthy uncertainty estimates, not merely accurate predictions. Recent advances in Bayesian learning have made significant progress toward this goal, and growing concerns about computational overhead have jointly shifted the design criterion from reliability alone to the co-design of reliability and efficiency, i.e., reducing computational overhead while preserving trustworthy uncertainty quantification. This thesis develops a unified framework from two perspectives to address the central question: can we efficiently perform reliable inference?

AI.tldr: Thesis develops a unified framework for co-designing reliable inference (trustworthy uncertainty estimates) with computational efficiency in Bayesian AI models.
AI.motivation: Reliable inference requires AI models to provide trustworthy uncertainty estimates, not just accurate predictions. There's growing concern about computational overhead in Bayesian learning, shifting the design criterion from reliability alone to co-designing reliability and efficiency.
AI.method: Develops a unified framework from two perspectives to address efficient reliable inference. The approach considers both reliability (trustworthy uncertainty quantification) and computational efficiency simultaneously.
AI.result: A unified framework is developed that aims to reduce computational overhead while preserving trustworthy uncertainty quantification in Bayesian AI models.
AI.conclusion: The thesis presents a framework for co-designing reliable and efficient Bayesian inference, addressing the central question of whether efficient reliable inference is achievable.

title: Active Learning for Gaussian Process Regression Under Self-Induced Boltzmann Weights
summary:
We consider the active learning problem where the goal is to learn an unknown function with low prediction error under an unknown Boltzmann distribution induced by the function itself. This self-induced weighting arises naturally in problems such as potential energy surface (PES) modeling in computational chemistry,
  yet poses unique challenges as the target distribution is unknown and its partition function is intractable. We propose \texttt{AB-SID-iVAR}, a Gaussian Process-based acquisition function that approximates the intractable Bayesian target distribution in closed form while avoiding partition function estimation, and is applicable to both discrete and continuous input domains. We also analyze a Thompson sampling alternative (\texttt{TS-SID-iVAR}) as a higher variance Monte Carlo variant. Despite the unknown target, under mild conditions, we establish that the terminal prediction error vanishes with high probability, and provide a tighter average-case guarantee. We demonstrate consistent improvements over existing approaches in this setting on synthetic benchmarks and real-world PES modeling and drug discovery tasks.

AI.tldr: Active learning with unknown self-induced Boltzmann distribution; proposed AB-SID-iVAR acquisition function avoids partition function estimation, works for both discrete/continuous domains; provides theoretical guarantees and empirical improvements.
AI.motivation: The paper addresses active learning problems where the target function induces its own unknown Boltzmann distribution, creating unique challenges. This occurs naturally in applications like potential energy surface modeling in computational chemistry, where the target distribution is unknown and its partition function is intractable.
AI.method: Proposes AB-SID-iVAR, a Gaussian Process-based acquisition function that approximates the intractable Bayesian target distribution in closed form without needing partition function estimation. Also analyzes TS-SID-iVAR as a Thompson sampling alternative. Both methods are applicable to discrete and continuous input domains.
AI.result: Under mild conditions, establishes that terminal prediction error vanishes with high probability and provides tighter average-case guarantees. Demonstrates consistent improvements over existing approaches on synthetic benchmarks and real-world tasks including PES modeling and drug discovery.
AI.conclusion: The proposed methods effectively handle the challenges of active learning with self-induced unknown distributions, providing theoretical guarantees and practical performance improvements across diverse domains, from continuous PES modeling to discrete drug discovery tasks.

title: DeepLévy: Learning Heavy-Tailed Uncertainty in Highly Volatile Time Series
summary: Modeling uncertainty in heavy-tailed time series remains a critical challenge for deep probabilistic forecasting models, which often struggle to capture abrupt, extreme events. While Lévy stable distributions offer a natural framework for modeling such non-Gaussian behaviors, the intractability of their probability density functions severely limits conventional likelihood-based inference. To address this, we introduce DeepLévy, a neural framework that learns mixtures of Lévy stable distributions by minimizing the discrepancy between empirical and parametric characteristic functions. DeepLévy incorporates a mixture mechanism that adaptively learns context-dependent weights and parameters over multiple Lévy components, enabling flexible multi-horizon uncertainty modeling. Evaluations on both real and synthetic datasets demonstrate that DeepLévy outperforms state-of-the-art deep probabilistic forecasting approaches in tail risk metrics, especially under extreme volatility.

AI.tldr: DeepLévy: A neural framework that learns mixtures of Lévy stable distributions via characteristic function matching for heavy-tailed time series forecasting with improved extreme event modeling.
AI.motivation: Deep probabilistic forecasting models struggle to capture abrupt, extreme events in heavy-tailed time series, and conventional likelihood-based inference is limited by the intractability of Lévy stable distribution probability density functions.
AI.method: The framework learns mixtures of Lévy stable distributions by minimizing the discrepancy between empirical and parametric characteristic functions, using a mixture mechanism that adaptively learns context-dependent weights and parameters over multiple Lévy components for multi-horizon uncertainty modeling.
AI.result: DeepLévy outperforms state-of-the-art deep probabilistic forecasting approaches in tail risk metrics, especially under extreme volatility, on both real and synthetic datasets.
AI.conclusion: DeepLévy provides an effective neural framework for modeling uncertainty in heavy-tailed time series by leveraging Lévy stable distributions through characteristic function matching, enabling superior performance in capturing extreme events compared to existing deep probabilistic forecasting methods.

title: Step Rejection Fine-Tuning: A Practical Distillation Recipe
summary: Rejection Fine-Tuning (RFT) is a standard method for training LLM agents, where unsuccessful trajectories are discarded from the training set. In the context of SWE-bench tasks, this corresponds to filtering out runs where the submitted patch does not pass the tests. However, this approach discards unresolved trajectories, even though they form a large portion of all trajectories for hard tasks and even then may be partially correct. In this work, we propose Step Rejection Fine-Tuning (SRFT) - a practical way to leverage these unresolved trajectories. For this, we employ a critic LLM to assess the correctness of each step in a trajectory. Consequently, during training, we mask the loss for erroneous steps while retaining them in the context window. This way we ensure the model learns to recover from errors without reproducing them. Evaluation on SWE-bench Verified shows that while RFT improves the resolution rate by 2.4% by excluding unresolved trajectories, SRFT improves it by 3.7% by filtering them instead of discarding completely, reaching the total resolution rate of 32.2%.

AI.tldr: Step Rejection Fine-Tuning (SRFT) improves LLM agent training by using critic LLMs to filter erroneous steps within unsuccessful trajectories rather than discarding entire failed trajectories like traditional Rejection Fine-Tuning (RFT).
AI.motivation: Traditional RFT discards entire unsuccessful trajectories in SWE-bench tasks when patches fail tests, wasting valuable learning signals from partially correct trajectories that are especially common in hard tasks.
AI.method: SRFT employs a critic LLM to assess correctness of each step in unresolved trajectories. During training, the loss is masked for erroneous steps while retaining them in context, enabling models to learn error recovery without reproducing mistakes.
AI.result: On SWE-bench Verified, while RFT improves resolution rate by 2.4% by excluding unresolved trajectories, SRFT improves by 3.7% by filtering instead of discarding them, achieving 32.2% total resolution rate.
AI.conclusion: SRFT provides a more effective approach than RFT by leveraging partial correctness in failed trajectories, enabling LLM agents to learn from errors and recover rather than simply discarding unsuccessful attempts.

title: Is Data Shapley Not Better than Random in Data Selection? Ask NASH
comment: Accepted to the 43rd International Conference on Machine Learning (ICML-26) as a Spotlight paper
summary: Data selection studies the problem of identifying high-quality subsets of training data. While some existing works have considered selecting the subset of data with top-$m$ Data Shapley or other semivalues as they account for the interaction among every subset of data, other works argue that Data Shapley can sometimes perform ineffectively in practice and select subsets that are no better than random. This raises the questions: (I) Are there certain "Shapley-informative" settings where Data Shapley consistently works well? (II) Can we strategically utilize these settings to select high-quality subsets consistently and efficiently? In this paper, we propose a novel data selection framework, NASH (Non-linear Aggregation of SHapley-informative components), which (I) decomposes the target utility function (e.g., validation accuracy) into simpler, Shapley-informative component functions, and selects data by optimizing an objective that (II) aggregates these components non-linearly. We demonstrate that NASH substantially boosts the effectiveness of Shapley/semivalue-based data selection with minimal additional runtime cost.

AI.tldr: NASH is a novel data selection framework that decomposes target utility functions into Shapley-informative components and aggregates them non-linearly, significantly improving the effectiveness of Shapley/semivalue-based data selection with minimal runtime overhead.
AI.motivation: Current Data Shapley methods for data selection have inconsistent performance - sometimes working well but sometimes performing no better than random selection. This raises two key questions: 1) Are there specific "Shapley-informative" settings where Data Shapley consistently works well? 2) Can we strategically leverage these settings for consistent and efficient high-quality data selection?
AI.method: The NASH framework: 1) Decomposes the target utility function (like validation accuracy) into simpler, Shapley-informative component functions, and 2) Selects data by optimizing an objective that aggregates these components non-linearly, rather than using simple top-m selection based on Data Shapley values.
AI.result: NASH substantially boosts the effectiveness of Shapley/semivalue-based data selection while maintaining minimal additional runtime cost compared to existing methods.
AI.conclusion: The proposed NASH framework provides a solution to the inconsistent performance problem of Data Shapley by identifying and leveraging Shapley-informative settings through decomposition and non-linear aggregation, enabling more reliable and efficient high-quality data selection.

title: The Polynomial Counting Capabilities of Message Passing Neural Networks
summary: The counting power of Message Passing Neural Networks (MPNN) has been the subject of many recent papers, showing that they can express logic that involves counting up to a threshold or more generally satisfy a linear arithmetic constraint. In this paper, we study the counting capabilities of MPNN beyond linear arithmetic, primarily utilising local and global mean aggregations. In particular, our goal is to tease out conditions required to express extensions of graded modal logic with polynomial counting constraints. We show that global polynomial counting constraints in node-labelled graphs can be checked using mean MPNN under mild assumptions. Checking local constraints is also possible, if we consider formulas with no nested modalities and additionally either (i) permit sum/max aggregations, or (ii) only restrict to regular graphs. We also show how formulas with nested modalities can be captured by mean MPNN over graphs with tree-like structures and similar assumptions.

AI.tldr: MPNNs can check global polynomial counting constraints in graphs using mean aggregations, but local constraints require restrictions like no nested modalities, sum/max aggregations, or regular graphs.
AI.motivation: Previous research has shown MPNNs can handle linear arithmetic constraints, but their capabilities for polynomial counting constraints beyond linear arithmetic haven't been fully explored. The paper aims to understand what conditions allow MPNNs to express graded modal logic with polynomial counting constraints.
AI.method: The study examines MPNNs primarily using local and global mean aggregations. The approach analyzes different graph scenarios: global polynomial counting constraints in node-labelled graphs, local constraints with restrictions (no nested modalities + sum/max aggregations OR regular graphs), and formulas with nested modalities in tree-like graph structures.
AI.result: Global polynomial counting constraints in node-labelled graphs can be checked using mean MPNNs under mild assumptions. Local constraints can be checked with restrictions: either (1) no nested modalities plus sum/max aggregations, or (2) no nested modalities and only regular graphs. Formulas with nested modalities can be captured by mean MPNNs over tree-like graph structures with similar assumptions.
AI.conclusion: Mean-based MPNNs have significant counting capabilities beyond linear arithmetic, capable of handling polynomial counting constraints in various graph contexts with specific architectural or structural restrictions. The work provides theoretical foundations for understanding the expressive power of MPNNs in relation to graded modal logic with polynomial constraints.

title: Causal Explanations from the Geometric Properties of ReLU Neural Networks
comment: 7 pages, 0 figures, Accepted for presentation at the Yorkshire Innovation in Science and Engineering Conference
summary: Neural networks have proved an effective means of learning control policies for autonomous systems, but these learned policies are difficult to understand due to the black-box nature of neural networks. This lack of interpretability makes safety assurance for such autonomous systems challenging. The fields of eXplainable Artificial Intelligence (XAI) and eXplainable Reinforcement Learning (XRL) aim to interpret the decision making processes of neural networks and autonomous agents, respectively. In particular, work on causal explanations aims to provide "why" and "why not" explanations for why a model made a given decision. However, most of the work on explainability to date utilises a distilled version of the original model. While this distilled policy is interpretable, it necessarily degrades in performance significantly when compared to the original model, and is not guaranteed to be an accurate reflection of the decision making processes in the original model and as such cannot be used to guarantee its safety. Recent work on understanding the geometry of ReLU neural networks shows that a ReLU network corresponds to a piecewise linear function divided into regions defined by an n-dimensional convex polytope. Through this lens, a neural network can be understood as dividing the input space into distinct regions which apply a single linear function for each output neuron. We show that this geometric representation can be used to generate causal explanations for the network's behaviour similar to previous work, but which extracts rules directly from the geometry of Neural Networks with the ReLU activation function, and is therefore an accurate reflection of the network's behaviour.

AI.tldr: A method for generating causal explanations for ReLU neural network decisions by analyzing their geometric structure as piecewise linear functions, providing accurate safety verification without performance degradation.
AI.motivation: Neural network policies for autonomous systems are black-box and lack interpretability, making safety assurance challenging. Current explainable AI methods use distilled models that degrade performance and don't accurately reflect the original model's decision-making, preventing reliable safety guarantees.
AI.method: Utilizes the geometric understanding of ReLU networks as piecewise linear functions divided into convex polytope regions. Extracts causal explanations directly from the network's geometric structure by analyzing how the input space is partitioned into distinct linear regions for each output neuron.
AI.result: Shows that geometric representation can generate causal explanations (answering "why" and "why not" questions) similar to previous work, but directly from the ReLU network's geometry, making the explanations accurate reflections of the actual network behavior.
AI.conclusion: Geometric analysis of ReLU neural networks provides a foundation for generating accurate causal explanations that can support safety assurance for autonomous systems without the performance degradation inherent in model distillation approaches.

title: Provable Sparse Inversion and Token Relabel Enhanced One-shot Federated Learning with ViTs
summary: One-Shot Federated Learning, where a central server learns a global model in a single communication round, has emerged as a promising paradigm. However, under extremely non-IID settings, existing data-free methods often generate low-quality data that suffers from severe semantic misalignment with ground-truth labels. To overcome these issues, we propose a novel Federated Model Inversion and Token Relabel (FedMITR) framework, which trains the global model by fully exploiting all patches of synthetic images. Specifically, FedMITR employs sparse model inversion during data generation, selectively inverting semantic foregrounds while halting the inversion of uninformative backgrounds. To address semantically meaningless tokens that hinder ViT predictions, we implement a differentiated strategy: patches with high information density utilize generated pseudo-labels, while patches with low information density are relabeled via ensemble models for robust distillation. Theoretically, our analysis based on algorithmic stability reveals that Sparse Model Inversion eliminates gradient instability arising from background noise, while Token Relabel effectively reduces gradient variance, collectively guaranteeing a tighter generalization bound. Empirically, extensive experimental results demonstrate that FedMITR substantially outperforms existing baselines under various settings.

AI.tldr: FedMITR: A one-shot federated learning framework using sparse model inversion for data generation and token relabeling for ViT predictions, achieving better performance in extreme non-IID settings.
AI.motivation: Existing data-free one-shot federated learning methods generate low-quality synthetic data with severe semantic misalignment with ground-truth labels, especially under extremely non-IID settings.
AI.method: FedMITR framework with two key techniques: 1) Sparse model inversion during data generation to selectively invert semantic foregrounds while halting inversion of uninformative backgrounds, and 2) Token relabeling with differentiated strategy - patches with high information density use generated pseudo-labels, while patches with low information density are relabeled via ensemble models for robust distillation.
AI.result: Theoretically, sparse model inversion eliminates gradient instability from background noise while token relabel reduces gradient variance, guaranteeing tighter generalization bound. Empirically, FedMITR substantially outperforms existing baselines under various experimental settings.
AI.conclusion: FedMITR effectively addresses semantic misalignment in one-shot federated learning under non-IID settings by exploiting all patches of synthetic images through sparse model inversion and intelligent token relabeling, with both theoretical and empirical validation.

title: Valid Best-Model Identification for LLM Evaluation via Low-Rank Factorization
summary: Selecting the best large language model (LLM) for a fixed benchmark is often expensive, since exhaustive evaluation requires running every model on every example. Multi-armed bandit (MAB) algorithms can reduce the number of LLM calls by sequentially selecting the next model-example pair to evaluate, thereby avoiding wasted evaluations on clearly underperforming models. Further savings can be achieved by predicting model scores from the partially observed model-example score matrix using low-rank factorization. However, such predictions are not ground truth: they can be biased and may therefore lead to incorrect identification of the best model. In this work, we propose a principled framework that combines MAB with cheap predicted scores without compromising statistical validity. Specifically, we derive doubly robust estimators of each model's performance that use the low-rank predictions to reduce variance. This enables the construction of valid finite-sample confidence intervals in our setting, where models are selected adaptively and examples are sampled without replacement. Empirical results on real-world benchmarks show that our approach reduces the number of required evaluations, yielding meaningful savings in compute and cost while accurately identifying the best-performing model.

AI.tldr: An adaptive bandit algorithm that uses low-rank predictions to reduce LLM evaluation costs while maintaining statistical validity for best-model selection.
AI.motivation: Exhaustive evaluation of LLMs on benchmarks is computationally expensive and wasteful, as many evaluations go to clearly underperforming models. Current approaches that use predicted scores from low-rank factorization can be biased and lead to incorrect model selection.
AI.method: Combines multi-armed bandit algorithms with low-rank predictions using doubly robust estimators of model performance. Constructs valid finite-sample confidence intervals that account for adaptive model selection and sampling without replacement.
AI.result: Empirical results on real-world benchmarks show the approach reduces required evaluations, yielding meaningful compute and cost savings while accurately identifying the best-performing model.
AI.conclusion: The proposed principled framework enables efficient LLM evaluation by combining adaptive bandit algorithms with cheap predicted scores without compromising statistical validity, making large-scale model benchmarking more practical.

title: The Last Word Often Wins: A Format Confound in Chain-of-Thought Corruption Studies
comment: 34 pages, 6 figures, 13 tables. Submitted to NeurIPS 2026. Code and data: https://github.com/Gpgabriel25/LastWordWinsCoT
summary:
Corruption studies, the primary tool for evaluating chain-of-thought (CoT) faithfulness, identify which chain positions are "computationally important" by measuring accuracy when steps are replaced with errors. We identify a systematic confound: for chains with explicit terminal answer statements, the dominant format in standard benchmarks, corruption studies detect where the answer text appears, not where computation occurs.
  A within-dataset format ablation provides the key evidence: on standard GSM8K chains ending with "the answer is X," removing only the answer statement, preserving all reasoning, collapses suffix sensitivity ~19x at 3B (N=300, p=0.022). Conflicting-answer experiments quantify the causal mechanism: at 7B, CC accuracy drops to near-zero (<=0.02) across five architecture families; the followed-wrong rate spans 0.63-1.00 at 3B-7B and attenuates at larger scales (0.300 at Phi-4-14B, ~0.01 at 32B). A within-stable 7B replication (9.3x attenuation, N=76, p=7.8e-3; Qwen3-8B N=299, p=0.004) provides converging evidence, and the pattern replicates on MATH (DeepSeek-R1-7B: 10.9x suffix-survival recovery). On chains without answer suffixes the same protocol identifies the prefix as load-bearing (Delta=-0.77, p<10^-12).
  Generation-time probes confirm a dissociation: the answer is not early-determined during generation (early commitment <5%), yet at consumption time model outputs systematically follow the explicit answer text. The format-determination effect persists through 14B (8.5x ratio, p=0.001) and converges toward zero at 32B. We propose a three-prerequisite protocol (question-only control, format characterization, all-position sweep) as a minimum standard for corruption-based faithfulness studies.

AI.tldr: Corruption studies for CoT faithfulness are confounded by answer statements; the method detects answer text location rather than computation, requiring new protocols for valid evaluation.
AI.motivation: Current corruption studies for evaluating chain-of-thought faithfulness systematically misinterpret results because they detect answer text presence rather than actual computational importance, especially for chains with explicit terminal answer statements common in benchmarks.
AI.method: Conducted format ablation studies on GSM8K, conflicting-answer experiments across five architecture families, within-stable replications, early-determined vs. consumption-time probes, and MATH dataset replication to systematically analyze the confound.
AI.result: Removing answer statements while preserving reasoning collapses suffix sensitivity by ~19x; conflicting-answer experiments show accuracy drops to near-zero; models systematically follow explicit answer text; effect persists through 14B models but attenuates at larger scales (32B).
AI.conclusion: Corruption-based faithfulness studies need a three-prerequisite protocol (question-only control, format characterization, all-position sweep) as minimum standard to avoid format-determination confounds and properly evaluate computational importance.

title: Identified-Set Geometry of Distributional Model Extraction under Top-$K$ Censored API Access
summary: Modern LLM APIs often reveal only top-$K$ logit scores and censor the remaining vocabulary. We study the per-position distribution-recovery limits of this access model. For censoring threshold $τ$, the compatible teacher distributions form an identified set whose total-variation diameter is exactly $U_K=(V-K)\exp(τ)/(Z_A+(V-K)\exp(τ))$, where $Z_A$ is the observed partition function. For KL recovery, we give a computable binary-endpoint lower bound and an asymptotically matching small-ambiguity upper bound, with an extension to reference-aware attackers. Experiments on a Qwen3 math-reasoning teacher reveal a layered extraction hierarchy: on-task top-$K$ distillation recovers 12% of private capability, full-logit distillation recovers 56% despite 99% KL closure, and generation-based extraction recovers 96%. Top-$K$ censoring therefore limits per-position distribution recovery but does not by itself prevent capability extraction, separating fidelity from transfer in prompt-only logit distillation.

AI.tldr: Top-K logit access in LLM APIs creates distribution recovery limits, with experiments showing layered capability extraction despite distribution fidelity constraints.
AI.motivation: Modern LLM APIs often only reveal top-K logit scores while censoring the remaining vocabulary, creating a need to understand the limits of distribution recovery from such restricted access models.
AI.method: Theoretical analysis of per-position distribution-recovery limits under top-K censoring, including total-variation diameter bounds and KL recovery bounds, with experimental validation using a Qwen3 math-reasoning teacher model and various extraction methods.
AI.result: Found exact total-variation diameter U_K for compatible teacher distributions, developed computable KL recovery bounds, and experimentally demonstrated layered extraction hierarchy: top-K distillation recovers 12% capability, full-logit distillation recovers 56%, and generation-based extraction recovers 96% despite 99% KL closure.
AI.conclusion: Top-K censoring limits per-position distribution recovery but doesn't prevent capability extraction, showing a separation between distribution fidelity and capability transfer in prompt-only logit distillation.

title: Policy Gradient Methods for Non-Markovian Reinforcement Learning
summary: We study policy gradient methods for reinforcement learning in non-Markovian decision processes (NMDPs), where observations and rewards depend on the entire interaction history. To handle this dependence, the agent maintains an internal state that is recursively updated to provide a compact summary of past observations and actions. In contrast to approaches that treat the agent state dynamics as fixed or learn it via predictive objectives, we propose a reward-centric formulation that jointly optimizes the agent state dynamics and the control policy to maximize the expected cumulative reward. To this end, we consider a class of Agent State-Markov (ASM) policies, comprising an agent state dynamics and a control policy that maps the agent state to actions. We establish a novel policy gradient theorem for ASM policies, extending the classical policy gradient results from the Markovian setting to episodic and infinite-horizon discounted NMDPs. Building on this gradient expression, we propose the Agent State-Markov Policy Gradient (ASMPG) algorithm, which leverages the recursive structure of the agent state dynamics for efficient optimization. We establish finite-time and almost sure convergence guarantees, and empirically demonstrate that, on a range of non-Markovian tasks, ASMPG outperforms baselines that learn state representations via predictive objectives.

AI.tldr: Proposes Agent State-Markov Policy Gradient (ASMPG) algorithm for non-Markovian decision processes, jointly optimizing agent state dynamics and control policy to maximize reward, outperforming predictive objective baselines.
AI.motivation: Traditional policy gradient methods assume Markovian environments, but many real-world reinforcement learning problems involve non-Markovian decision processes where observations and rewards depend on entire interaction history. Existing approaches either treat agent state dynamics as fixed or learn them via predictive objectives, which may not align with the ultimate goal of maximizing cumulative reward.
AI.method: Proposes a reward-centric formulation that jointly optimizes agent state dynamics and control policy. Introduces Agent State-Markov (ASM) policies comprising recursive agent state dynamics and control policy mapping agent state to actions. Establishes novel policy gradient theorem for ASM policies extending classical results to non-Markovian settings, then develops ASMPG algorithm leveraging recursive structure for efficient optimization.
AI.result: Establishes theoretical convergence guarantees (finite-time and almost sure convergence) for ASMPG. Empirically demonstrates that ASMPG outperforms baselines that learn state representations via predictive objectives on a range of non-Markovian tasks.
AI.conclusion: The reward-centric joint optimization of agent state dynamics and control policy is more effective for non-Markovian RL than predictive objective approaches.
