ASMPG provides a principled framework with convergence guarantees that can handle history dependence in observations and rewards.

title: Equilibrium Residuals Expose Three Regimes of Matrix-Game Strategic Reasoning in Language Models
summary: Large language models can score well on named game-theory benchmarks while failing on the same strategic computation once semantic cues are removed. We show this gap with procedurally generated zero-sum matrix games: a model that recognizes familiar games drops to 34%, 18%, and 2% success on anonymous $2{\times}2$, $3{\times}3$, and $5{\times}5$ payoff matrices. The benchmark separates semantic recall, learned approximate Nash computation, and an output-interface bottleneck that limits scale. Training only on $2{\times}2$ and $3{\times}3$ games, supervised fine-tuning raises unseen $5{\times}5$--$7{\times}7$ success from 2% to 61%, while exploitability-reward training averages 37% with high seed variance. We prove that the exploitability residual is $2$-Lipschitz in payoff perturbations, unlike discontinuous vertex-returning LP equilibrium selectors, explaining why residual training can transfer under payoff shifts even when formatting instability limits mean performance. A dominated-action padding experiment provides causal evidence: trained models solve $3{\times}3$ games embedded in much larger matrices, while random-padded controls fail and dense $12{\times}12$ games remain near failure. Procedural evaluation is therefore necessary for measuring strategic reasoning, and residual rewards expose a real but format-limited route to approximate equilibrium computation.

AI.tldr: LLMs perform well on named game theory tasks but fail on procedurally generated anonymous matrix games, showing they rely on semantic recall rather than strategic reasoning. Training on smaller games enables transfer to larger ones, but success is limited by output format issues.
AI.motivation: The paper aims to reveal that large language models' apparent success on game theory benchmarks may be due to semantic pattern matching rather than genuine strategic reasoning capabilities. There's a need to separate semantic recall from actual equilibrium computation skills in AI systems.
AI.method: Researchers use procedurally generated zero-sum matrix games with anonymous payoff matrices to remove semantic cues. They test models on various matrix sizes (2×2 to 12×12), apply supervised fine-tuning on smaller games, use exploitability-reward training with proven 2-Lipschitz properties, and conduct dominated-action padding experiments for causal analysis.
AI.result: Models drop dramatically on anonymous matrices (34% on 2×2, 18% on 3×3, 2% on 5×5). Training on 2×2 and 3×3 games improves 5×5-7×7 performance to 61% with SFT, 37% with exploitability training. Models solve 3×3 games embedded in larger matrices but fail on dense 12×12 games, showing format limitations rather than computational inability.
AI.conclusion: Procedural evaluation is essential for measuring true strategic reasoning. LLMs can learn approximate equilibrium computation but are bottlenecked by output interface limitations. The exploitability residual's Lipschitz property enables transfer learning under payoff perturbations, distinguishing it from discontinuous equilibrium selectors.

title: SLIM: Sparse Latent Steering for Interpretable and Property-Directed LLM-Based Molecular Editing
summary: Large language models possess strong chemical reasoning capabilities, making them effective molecular editors. However, property-relevant information is implicitly entangled across their dense hidden states, providing no explicit handle for property control: a substantial fraction of edits fail to improve or even degrade target properties. To address these issues, we propose SLIM (Sparse Latent Interpretable Molecular editing), a plug-and-play framework that decomposes the editor's hidden states into sparse, property-aligned features via a Sparse Autoencoder with learnable importance gates. Steering in this sparse feature space precisely activates property-relevant dimensions, improving editing success rate without modifying model parameters. The same sparse basis further supports interpretable analysis of editing behavior. Experiments on the MolEditRL benchmark across four model architectures and eight molecular properties show consistent gains over baselines, with improvements of up to 42.4 points.

AI.tldr: SLIM is a plug-and-play framework that uses a sparse autoencoder to decompose LLM hidden states into property-aligned features, enabling precise property control in molecular editing without modifying model parameters, achieving up to 42.4 point improvements.
AI.motivation: Large language models have strong chemical reasoning capabilities for molecular editing, but property-relevant information is implicitly entangled in their dense hidden states, providing no explicit handle for property control. This leads to many edits failing to improve or even degrading target properties.
AI.method: Proposes SLIM (Sparse Latent Interpretable Molecular editing) - a plug-and-play framework that decomposes the editor's hidden states into sparse, property-aligned features using a Sparse Autoencoder with learnable importance gates. Steering in this sparse feature space activates property-relevant dimensions for precise control.
AI.result: Experiments on the MolEditRL benchmark across four model architectures and eight molecular properties show consistent gains over baselines, with improvements of up to 42.4 points. The framework improves editing success rate without modifying model parameters.
AI.conclusion: SLIM effectively addresses the property control problem in LLM-based molecular editing by decomposing dense hidden states into sparse, interpretable features, enabling precise property manipulation and interpretable analysis while maintaining model integrity.

title: Remember to Forget: Gated Adaptive Positional Encoding
summary: Rotary Positional Encoding (RoPE) is widely used in modern large language models. However, when sequences are extended beyond the range seen during training, rotary phases can enter out-of-distribution regimes, leading to spurious long-range alignments, diffuse attention, and degraded retrieval. Existing remedies only partially address these failures, as they often trade local positional resolution for long-context stability. We propose GAPE (Gated Adaptive Positional Encoding), a drop-in augmentation for positional encodings that introduces a content-aware bias directly into the attention logits while preserving the rotary geometry. GAPE decouples distance-based suppression from token importance through a query-dependent gate that contracts irrelevant context and a key-dependent gate that preserves salient distant tokens. We prove that protected tokens remain accessible, while the attention mass assigned to unprotected distant tokens decays as a function of the query gate. We further show that GAPE can be implemented within standard scaled dot-product attention. We validate these properties empirically, finding that GAPE consistently yields sharper attention and improved long-context robustness over rotary baselines across both synthetic retrieval and long-context benchmarks.

AI.tldr: GAPE is a new positional encoding method that improves long-context handling in LLMs by adding content-aware gates to attention logits while preserving rotary geometry.
AI.motivation: Current Rotary Positional Encoding (RoPE) fails when sequences extend beyond training range, causing spurious alignments, diffuse attention, and degraded retrieval. Existing solutions trade local positional resolution for long-context stability.
AI.method: Proposes GAPE (Gated Adaptive Positional Encoding) as a drop-in augmentation that introduces content-aware bias into attention logits. Uses query-dependent gates to contract irrelevant context and key-dependent gates to preserve salient distant tokens while maintaining rotary geometry.
AI.result: GAPE consistently yields sharper attention and improved long-context robustness over rotary baselines across both synthetic retrieval and long-context benchmarks. Protected tokens remain accessible while attention to unprotected distant tokens decays appropriately.
AI.conclusion: GAPE effectively addresses RoPE's long-context limitations by decoupling distance-based suppression from token importance through content-aware gating, providing better long-context handling without sacrificing local positional resolution.

title: Clin-JEPA: A Multi-Phase Co-Training Framework for Joint-Embedding Predictive Pretraining on EHR Patient Trajectories
summary: We present Clin-JEPA, a multi-phase co-training framework for joint-embedding predictive (JEPA) pretraining on EHR patient trajectories. JEPA architectures have enabled latent-space planning in robotics and high-quality representation learning in vision, but extending the paradigm to EHR data -- to obtain a single backbone that simultaneously forecasts patient trajectories and serves diverse downstream risk-prediction tasks without per-task fine-tuning -- remains an open challenge. Existing JEPA frameworks either discard the predictor after pretraining (I-JEPA, V-JEPA) or train it on a frozen pretrained encoder (V-JEPA 2-AC), leaving the encoder unaware of the rollout signal that the retained predictor must use at inference; co-training the encoder and predictor under a shared JEPA prediction objective would supply this grounding, but naïve co-training is unstable, with representation collapse and online/target drift causing autoregressive rollout to diverge. Clin-JEPA's five-phase pretraining curriculum -- predictor warmup, joint refinement, EMA target alignment, hard sync, and predictor finalization -- addresses each failure mode by phase, stably co-training a Qwen3-8B-based encoder and a 92M-parameter latent trajectory predictor. On MIMIC-IV ICU data, three independent evaluations support the framework: (1) latent $\ell_1$ rollout drift uniquely converges ($-$15.7%) over 48-hour horizons while baselines and ablations diverge (+3% to +4951%); (2) the encoder learns a clinically discriminative latent geometry (deteriorating-patient cohorts displace 4.83$\times$ further than stable patients in latent space, vs $\leq$2.62$\times$ for baseline encoders); (3) a single backbone outperforms strong tabular and sequence baselines on multi-task downstream evaluation. Clin-JEPA achieves mean AUROC 0.851 on ICareFM EEP and 0.883 on 8 binary risk tasks (+0.038 and +0.041 vs baseline average).

AI.tldr: Clin-JEPA: A multi-phase co-training framework that jointly trains an encoder and predictor for EHR patient trajectory forecasting, enabling single-backbone performance on diverse risk-prediction tasks without fine-tuning.
AI.motivation: JEPA architectures have shown success in robotics and vision but adapting them to EHR data remains challenging. Existing approaches either discard the predictor after pretraining or train it on frozen encoders, leaving the encoder unaware of rollout signals needed at inference. Naïve co-training causes instability with representation collapse and rollout divergence.
AI.method: Five-phase pretraining curriculum: predictor warmup, joint refinement, EMA target alignment, hard sync, and predictor finalization. Stably co-trains a Qwen3-8B-based encoder and 92M-parameter latent trajectory predictor on MIMIC-IV ICU data.
AI.result: Three key evaluations: (1) Latent rollout drift converges (-15.7%) while baselines diverge (+3% to +4951%); (2) Encoder learns clinically discriminative geometry (deteriorating patients displace 4.83× further than stable patients vs ≤2.62× for baselines); (3) Single backbone outperforms baselines on multi-task evaluation with mean AUROC 0.851 on ICareFM EEP and 0.883 on 8 binary risk tasks.
AI.conclusion: Clin-JEPA successfully addresses the stability challenges of JEPA co-training for EHR data, enabling joint encoder-predictor optimization that produces both accurate trajectory forecasting and clinically meaningful representations for diverse downstream risk prediction without task-specific fine-tuning.

title: AssayBench: An Assay-Level Virtual Cell Benchmark for LLMs and Agents
summary: Recent advances in machine learning and large-scale biological data collections have revived the prospect of building a virtual cell, a computational model of cellular behavior that could accelerate biological discovery. One of the most compelling promises of this vision is the ability to perform in silico phenotypic screens, in which a model predicts the effects of cellular perturbations in unseen biological contexts. This task combines heterogeneous textual inputs with diverse phenotypic outputs, making it particularly well-suited to LLMs and agentic systems. Yet, no standard benchmark currently exists for this task, as existing efforts focus on narrower molecular readouts that are only indirectly aligned with the phenotypic endpoints driving many real-world drug discovery workflows. In this work, we present AssayBench, a benchmark for phenotypic screen prediction, built from 1,920 publicly available CRISPR screens spanning five broad classes of cellular phenotypes. We formulate the screen prediction task as a gene rank prediction for each screen and introduce the adjusted nDCG, a continuous metric for comparing performance across heterogeneous assays. Our extensive evaluation shows that existing methods remain far from empirically estimated performance ceilings and zero-shot generalist LLMs outperform biology-specific LLMs and trainable baselines. Optimization techniques such as fine-tuning, ensembling, and prompt optimization can further improve LLM performance on this task. Overall, AssayBench offers a practical testbed for measuring progress toward in silico phenotypic screening and, more broadly, virtual cell models.

AI.tldr: AssayBench is a new benchmark for in silico phenotypic screen prediction using CRISPR screens, showing that zero-shot generalist LLMs outperform biology-specific models and that optimization techniques can further improve performance.
AI.motivation: There's growing interest in building virtual cell models for biological discovery, particularly for in silico phenotypic screens that can accelerate drug discovery workflows. However, no standard benchmark exists for this task, as current efforts focus on narrower molecular readouts rather than the phenotypic endpoints that drive real-world drug discovery.
AI.method: The authors created AssayBench from 1,920 publicly available CRISPR screens spanning five broad classes of cellular phenotypes. They formulate screen prediction as a gene rank prediction task and introduce the adjusted nDCG metric for comparing performance across heterogeneous assays.
AI.result: Extensive evaluation shows that existing methods are far from empirically estimated performance ceilings. Surprisingly, zero-shot generalist LLMs outperform biology-specific LLMs and trainable baselines. Optimization techniques like fine-tuning, ensembling, and prompt optimization can further improve LLM performance.
AI.conclusion: AssayBench provides a practical testbed for measuring progress toward in silico phenotypic screening and virtual cell models, highlighting the potential of LLMs and agentic systems for this challenging task that combines heterogeneous textual inputs with diverse phenotypic outputs.

title: Beyond Spatial Compression: Interface-Centric Generative States for Open-World 3D Structure
summary: Current 3D tokenizers largely treat representation as spatial compression: compact codes reconstruct surface geometry, but leave component ownership and attachment validity implicit. In open-world assets with intersecting components, noisy topology, and weak canonical structure, this creates a representation mismatch: local shape, component identity, and assembly relations become entangled in a latent stream and are not natively addressable during decoding. We formulate an alternative view, interface-centric generative states, in which tokenization constructs an operational state rather than a passive compressed code. The state exposes local geometry, component ownership, and attachment validity as variables that can be queried, constrained, and repaired during decoding. We instantiate this formulation with Component-Conditioned Canonical Local Tokens (C2LT-3D), factorizing representation into canonical local geometry, partition-conditioned context, and relational seam variables. Each factor targets a distinct failure mode of compression-centric tokens: pose leakage, cross-component interference, or invalid local attachment. This exposed state supports attachment validation, latent structural repair, targeted intervention, and constrained serialization without a separate post-hoc structure recovery module. Trained on single-object CAD models and evaluated zero-shot on open-world multi-component assets, C2LT-3D improves structural robustness and shows that its latent variables remain actionable under adversarial attachment settings. These results suggest that open-world 3D generative representations should be evaluated not only by reconstruction fidelity, but by whether their discrete states remain operational for assembly-level structural reasoning.

AI.tldr: C2LT-3D introduces an interface-centric 3D tokenizer that treats representations as operational states rather than spatial compression, enabling explicit control over component geometry, ownership, and attachment relationships during decoding.
AI.motivation: Current 3D tokenizers suffer from a representation mismatch where component identity, geometry, and assembly relations become entangled in latent codes, making them difficult to address during decoding. This is especially problematic for open-world assets with intersecting components, noisy topology, and weak canonical structure where assembly relationships are crucial.
AI.method: C2LT-3D factorizes representation into three components: canonical local geometry (for pose invariance), partition-conditioned context (for component ownership), and relational seam variables (for attachment validity). This creates an exposed operational state where geometry, ownership, and attachment validity are queryable variables rather than implicit features.
AI.result: The method improves structural robustness when evaluated zero-shot on open-world multi-component assets, and shows that its latent variables remain actionable under adversarial attachment settings. It supports attachment validation, latent structural repair, targeted intervention, and constrained serialization without separate post-hoc structure recovery.
AI.conclusion: Open-world 3D generative representations should be evaluated not only by reconstruction fidelity but by whether their discrete states remain operational for assembly-level structural reasoning, suggesting a shift from compression-centric to interface-centric tokenization paradigms.

title: LoKA: Low-precision Kernel Applications for Recommendation Models At Scale
summary: Recent GPU generations deliver significantly higher FLOPs using lower-precision arithmetic, such as FP8. While successfully applied to large language models (LLMs), its adoption in large recommendation models (LRMs) has been limited. This is because LRMs are numerically sensitive, dominated by small matrix multiplications (GEMMs) followed by normalization, and trained in communication-intensive environments. Applying FP8 directly to LRMs often degrades model quality and prolongs training time. These challenges are inherent to LRM workloads and cannot be resolved merely by introducing better FP8 kernels. Instead, a system-model co-design approach is needed to successfully integrate FP8. We present LoKA (Low-precision Kernel Applications), a framework that makes FP8 practical for LRMs through three principles: profile under realistic distributions to know where low precision is safe, co-design model components with hardware to expand where it is safe, and orchestrate across kernel libraries to maximize the gains. Concretely, LoKA Probe is a statistically grounded, online benchmarking method that learns activation and weight statistics, and quantifies per-layer errors. This process pinpoints safe and unsafe, fast and slow sites for FP8 adoption. LoKA Mods is a set of reusable model adaptations that improve both numerical stability and execution efficiency with FP8. LoKA Dispatch is a runtime that leverages the statistical insights from LoKA Probe to select the fastest FP8 kernel that satisfies the accuracy requirements.

AI.tldr: LoKA is a framework that enables practical FP8 low-precision training for large recommendation models through statistical profiling, model-hardware co-design, and smart kernel selection, overcoming numerical sensitivity and small GEMM challenges.
AI.motivation: Recent GPUs offer high FLOPs with low-precision arithmetic like FP8, but large recommendation models (LRMs) cannot directly benefit due to their numerical sensitivity, small matrix multiplication workloads, and communication-intensive training environments. Direct FP8 application degrades model quality and prolongs training time.
AI.method: LoKA uses three principles: 1) Profile under realistic distributions to identify safe low-precision regions, 2) Co-design model components with hardware to expand safe regions, and 3) Orchestrate across kernel libraries to maximize gains. Specifically includes: LoKA Probe for online benchmarking and statistical analysis, LoKA Mods for model adaptations to improve stability and efficiency, and LoKA Dispatch for runtime kernel selection.
AI.result: The framework makes FP8 practical for LRMs by systematically addressing numerical sensitivity issues through statistical profiling, model adaptations, and intelligent kernel orchestration, enabling the use of low-precision arithmetic without degrading model quality.
AI.conclusion: A system-model co-design approach is essential for successfully integrating FP8 into large recommendation models, and the LoKA framework provides a comprehensive solution through profiling, adaptation, and intelligent dispatch to overcome inherent LRM workload challenges.

title: Don't Fix the Basis -- Learn It: Spectral Representation with Adaptive Basis Learning for PDEs
summary: Spectral neural operators achieve strong performance for PDE learning, but rely on fixed global bases that limit their ability to represent spatially heterogeneous and multiscale dynamics. We propose Adaptive Basis Learning (ABLE), a framework that learns data-dependent spectral representations instead of relying on predefined bases. ABLE constructs a spatially adaptive Parseval frame via a learned ancillary density, enabling the operator to act in a lifted spectral space while preserving invertibility and maintaining $O(N\log N)$ complexity through FFT-based implementation. This shifts the source of expressivity from spectral coefficients to the representation itself, allowing the model to capture localized structures and non-translation-invariant interactions more efficiently. ABLE integrates seamlessly into existing neural operator architectures as a drop-in replacement for spectral layers. Across a range of benchmarks ABLE improves accuracy over strong baselines, with the largest gains in regimes characterized by sharp gradients and multiscale behavior. Moreover, augmenting existing models (e.g., U-FNO, HPM) with ABLE further enhances their performance, demonstrating its role as a general and complementary spectral refinement. Our results highlight that the data-driven choice of representation, rather than operator complexity alone, is a key bottleneck in neural operator design. By learning the basis itself, ABLE provides a principled and efficient framework for improving spectral methods in PDE learning.

AI.tldr: ABLE (Adaptive Basis Learning) is a framework that learns data-dependent spectral representations for PDE learning, replacing fixed global bases with spatially adaptive Parseval frames to better capture heterogeneous and multiscale dynamics while maintaining computational efficiency.
AI.motivation: Spectral neural operators rely on fixed global bases that limit their ability to represent spatially heterogeneous and multiscale dynamics, creating a bottleneck in expressing complex PDE behavior with localized structures and non-translation-invariant interactions.
AI.method: ABLE constructs a spatially adaptive Parseval frame via a learned ancillary density, enabling the operator to act in a lifted spectral space while preserving invertibility. It maintains O(N log N) complexity through FFT-based implementation and serves as a drop-in replacement for spectral layers in existing neural operator architectures.
AI.result: ABLE improves accuracy over strong baselines across various benchmarks, with largest gains in regimes with sharp gradients and multiscale behavior. Augmenting existing models (U-FNO, HPM) with ABLE further enhances performance, demonstrating its effectiveness as a general spectral refinement.
AI.conclusion: The data-driven choice of representation, rather than operator complexity alone, is a key bottleneck in neural operator design. ABLE provides a principled and efficient framework for improving spectral methods in PDE learning by learning the basis itself, shifting expressivity from spectral coefficients to the representation.

title: Unmasking On-Policy Distillation: Where It Helps, Where It Hurts, and Why
summary: On-policy distillation offers dense, per-token supervision for training reasoning models; however, it remains unclear under which conditions this signal is beneficial and under which it is detrimental. Which teacher model should be used, and in the case of self-distillation, which specific context should serve as the supervisory signal? Does the optimal choice vary from one token to the next? At present, addressing these questions typically requires costly training runs whose aggregate performance metrics obscure the dynamics at the level of individual tokens. We introduce a training-free diagnostic framework that operates at the highest resolution: per token, per question, and per teacher. We derive an ideal per-node gradient defined as the parameter update that maximally increases the student's probability of success. We then develop a scalable targeted-rollout algorithm to estimate this gradient efficiently, even for long chains of intermediate thoughts. The gradient alignment score, defined as the cosine similarity between this ideal gradient and any given distillation gradient, quantifies the extent to which a particular configuration approximates the ideal signal. Across a range of self-distillation settings and external teacher models, we observe that distillation guidance exhibits substantially higher alignment with the ideal on incorrect rollouts than on correct ones, where the student already performs well and the teacher's signal tends to become noisy. Furthermore, we find that the optimal distillation context depends jointly on the student model's capacity and the target task, and that no single universally effective configuration emerges. These findings motivate the use of per-task, per-token diagnostic analyses for distillation.

AI.tldr: Introduces a training-free diagnostic framework to analyze per-token distillation signals, showing optimal distillation guidance varies by token, question, teacher, and student capacity.
AI.motivation: Current on-policy distillation lacks understanding of when per-token supervision is beneficial vs detrimental, with expensive training runs obscuring individual token dynamics and optimal teacher/context selection unclear.
AI.method: Develops a training-free diagnostic framework operating at per-token, per-question, per-teacher resolution. Derives an ideal per-node gradient maximizing student success probability, implements scalable targeted-rollout algorithm for estimation, and uses gradient alignment score to evaluate distillation signals.
AI.result: Distillation guidance shows higher alignment with ideal gradients on incorrect rollouts than correct ones; optimal distillation context depends jointly on student capacity and target task; no universally effective configuration emerges.
AI.conclusion: Per-task, per-token diagnostic analyses are essential for effective distillation, as distillation signal quality varies across contexts and no single configuration works universally.

title: SlimSpec: Low-Rank Draft LM-Head for Accelerated Speculative Decoding
summary: Speculative decoding speeds up autoregressive generation in Large Language Models (LLMs) through a two-step procedure, where a lightweight draft model proposes tokens which the target model then verifies in a single forward pass. Although the drafter network is small in modern architectures, its LM-head still performs projection to a large vocabulary, becoming one of the major computational bottlenecks. In prior work this issue has been predominantly addressed via static or dynamic vocabulary truncation. Yet mitigating the bottleneck, these methods bring in extra complexity, such as special vocabulary curation, sophisticated inference-time logic or modifications of the training setup. In this paper, we propose SlimSpec, a low-rank parameterization of the drafter's LM-head that compresses the inner representation rather than the output, preserving full vocabulary support. We evaluate our method with EAGLE-3 drafter across three target models and diverse benchmarks in both latency- and throughput-bound inference regimes. SlimSpec achieves $4\text{-}5\times$ acceleration over the standard LM-head architecture while maintaining a competitive acceptance length, surpassing existing methods by up to $8\text{-}9\%$ of the end-to-end speedup. Our method requires minimal adjustments of training and inference pipelines. Combined with the aforementioned speedup improvements, it makes SlimSpec a strong alternative across wide variety of draft LM-head architectures.

AI.tldr: SlimSpec is a low-rank parameterization method for drafter LM-heads in speculative decoding that compresses inner representations instead of output vocabulary, achieving 4-5× speedup while maintaining full vocabulary support.
AI.motivation: In speculative decoding for LLMs, the drafter model's LM-head projection to large vocabulary becomes a major computational bottleneck. Existing solutions like vocabulary truncation add complexity with special vocabulary curation, sophisticated inference logic, or training modifications.
AI.method: Proposes SlimSpec - a low-rank parameterization of the drafter's LM-head that compresses the inner representation rather than truncating the output vocabulary. This preserves full vocabulary support while reducing computational overhead.
AI.result: SlimSpec achieves 4-5× acceleration over standard LM-head architecture while maintaining competitive acceptance length. It surpasses existing methods by up to 8-9% in end-to-end speedup. Evaluated with EAGLE-3 drafter across three target models and diverse benchmarks in latency- and throughput-bound regimes.
AI.conclusion: SlimSpec provides a strong alternative to existing drafter LM-head architectures with minimal training and inference pipeline adjustments, offering significant speedup improvements across various draft LM-head architectures.

title: DataMaster: Towards Autonomous Data Engineering for Machine Learning
summary: As model families, training recipes, and compute budgets become increasingly standardized, further gains in machine learning systems depend increasingly on data. Yet data engineering remains largely manual and ad hoc: practitioners repeatedly search for external datasets, adapt them to existing pipelines, validate candidate data through downstream training, and carry forward lessons from prior attempts. We study task-conditioned autonomous data engineering, where an autonomous agent improves a fixed learning algorithm by optimizing only the data side, including external data discovery, data selection and composition, cleaning and transformation. The goal is to obtain a stronger downstream solution while leaving the learning algorithm unchanged. To address the open-ended search space, branch-dependent refinement, and delayed validation inherent in autonomous data engineering, we propose DataMaster, a data-agent framework that integrates tree-structured search, shared candidate data, and cumulative memory. DataMaster consists of three key components: a DataTree that organizes alternative data-engineering branches, a shared Data Pool that stores discovered external data sources for reuse, and a Global Memory that records node outcomes, artifacts, and reusable findings. Together, these components allow the agent to discover candidate data, construct executable training inputs, evaluate them through downstream feedback, and carry useful evidence across branches. We evaluate DataMaster on two types of benchmarks, MLE-Bench Lite and PostTrainBench. On MLE-Bench Lite, it improves medal rate by 32.27% over the initial score; on PostTrainBench, it surpasses the instruct model on GPQA (31.02% vs 30.35%).

AI.tldr: DataMaster is an autonomous agent framework for data engineering that automatically discovers, selects, composes, and optimizes training data to improve downstream ML performance without modifying the learning algorithm.
AI.motivation: As ML models, training recipes, and compute budgets become standardized, data becomes the key differentiator for performance gains. However, data engineering remains manual and ad-hoc, requiring practitioners to repeatedly search for datasets, adapt pipelines, validate through training, and learn from prior attempts.
AI.method: Proposes DataMaster, a data-agent framework with three components: (1) DataTree for organizing alternative data-engineering branches, (2) shared Data Pool for storing discovered external data sources for reuse, and (3) Global Memory for recording node outcomes, artifacts, and reusable findings. The framework handles open-ended search, branch-dependent refinement, and delayed validation inherent in autonomous data engineering.
AI.result: On MLE-Bench Lite, DataMaster improves medal rate by 32.27% over the initial score. On PostTrainBench, it surpasses the instruct model on GPQA (31.02% vs 30.35%). The system effectively discovers candidate data, constructs executable training inputs, evaluates through downstream feedback, and carries useful evidence across branches.
AI.conclusion: DataMaster provides an effective framework for autonomous data engineering that can systematically improve ML performance by optimizing only the data side while keeping learning algorithms fixed. It addresses key challenges in data engineering automation and shows promising results on benchmark tasks.

title: AxiomOcean: Forecasting the Three-Dimensional Structure of the Upper Ocean
summary: Short-term ocean forecast skill depends strongly on the three-dimensional ocean structure of the upper ocean, which governs stratification, subsurface heat storage, and the response of the ocean to atmospheric forcing. However, AI ocean forecasting models often fail to preserve this vertical structure, resulting in over-smoothed subsurface features and weak physical consistency under strong forcing. Here, we present AxiomOcean, a global AI ocean forecasting model that explicitly represents vertical hierarchy and cross-layer dependence within the water column. By combining a fully three-dimensional encoder-backbone-decoder architecture with surface atmospheric forcing, AxiomOcean jointly predicts upper-ocean temperature, salinity, and three-dimensional currents at global 1/12° resolution down to 643 m depth. In 10-day forecasts, AxiomOcean outperforms an advanced AI comparison model across variables and lead times, reducing day-1 RMSE by approximately 20 to 35% while maintaining higher anomaly correlation. The gain is not achieved through excessive smoothing: AxiomOcean better preserves eddy kinetic energy, temperature and salinity variance. Its advantage also extends through the water column and remains evident across the equatorial Pacific, Kuroshio Extension, and Southern Ocean, yielding a more realistic reconstruction of upper-ocean heat content. These results show that explicitly preserving upper-ocean three-dimensional structure can improve both forecast accuracy and physical fidelity in AI ocean prediction.

AI.tldr: AxiomOcean is a 3D AI ocean forecasting model that preserves vertical structure and outperforms existing models in 10-day global forecasts.
AI.motivation: AI ocean forecasting models often fail to preserve vertical ocean structure, leading to over-smoothed subsurface features and weak physical consistency under strong atmospheric forcing, which compromises forecast accuracy.
AI.method: Developed AxiomOcean using a fully 3D encoder-backbone-decoder architecture that explicitly represents vertical hierarchy and cross-layer dependence, incorporating surface atmospheric forcing to jointly predict upper-ocean temperature, salinity, and 3D currents at global 1/12° resolution down to 643 m depth.
AI.result: Outperforms advanced AI comparison model in 10-day forecasts, reducing day-1 RMSE by 20-35% while maintaining higher anomaly correlation. Better preserves eddy kinetic energy, temperature/salinity variance, with advantages extending through water column and across key ocean regions (equatorial Pacific, Kuroshio Extension, Southern Ocean), yielding more realistic reconstruction of upper-ocean heat content.
AI.conclusion: Explicitly preserving upper-ocean three-dimensional structure improves both forecast accuracy and physical fidelity in AI ocean prediction, demonstrating the importance of representing vertical hierarchy and cross-layer dependence.

title: Variational Inference for Lévy Process-Driven SDEs via Neural Tilting
comment: The associated project page which contains the official implementation can be found in https://circle-group.github.io/research/NeuralTilting/
summary: Modelling extreme events and heavy-tailed phenomena is central to building reliable predictive systems in domains such as finance, climate science, and safety-critical AI. While Lévy processes provide a natural mathematical framework for capturing jumps and heavy tails, Bayesian inference for Lévy-driven stochastic differential equations (SDEs) remains intractable with existing methods: Monte Carlo approaches are rigorous but lack scalability, whereas neural variational inference methods are efficient but rely on Gaussian assumptions that fail to capture discontinuities. We address this tension by introducing a neural exponential tilting framework for variational inference in Lévy-driven SDEs. Our approach constructs a flexible variational family by exponentially reweighting the Lévy measure using neural networks. This parametrization preserves the jump structure of the underlying process while remaining computationally tractable. To enable efficient inference, we develop a quadratic neural parametrization that yields closed-form normalization of the tilted measure, a conditional Gaussian representation for stable processes that facilitates simulation, and symmetry-aware Monte Carlo estimators for scalable optimization. Empirically, we demonstrate that the method accurately captures jump dynamics and yields reliable posterior inference in regimes where Gaussian-based variational approaches fail, on both synthetic and real-world datasets.

AI.tldr: Neural exponential tilting framework for variational inference in Lévy-driven SDEs, enabling scalable Bayesian inference while preserving jump structures in heavy-tailed phenomena.
AI.motivation: Bayesian inference for Lévy-driven SDEs is intractable with existing methods: Monte Carlo approaches lack scalability, while neural variational methods rely on Gaussian assumptions that fail to capture discontinuities in extreme events and heavy-tailed phenomena common in finance, climate science, and safety-critical AI.
AI.method: Introduces neural exponential tilting framework that constructs flexible variational family by exponentially reweighting the Lévy measure using neural networks. Includes quadratic neural parametrization for closed-form normalization, conditional Gaussian representation for stable processes to facilitate simulation, and symmetry-aware Monte Carlo estimators for scalable optimization.
AI.result: Method accurately captures jump dynamics and yields reliable posterior inference in regimes where Gaussian-based variational approaches fail, demonstrated on both synthetic and real-world datasets. Preserves jump structure while remaining computationally tractable.
AI.conclusion: The neural exponential tilting framework successfully bridges the gap between rigorous but unscalable Monte Carlo methods and efficient but overly-simplistic Gaussian variational approaches, enabling practical Bayesian inference for Lévy-driven SDEs while properly modeling discontinuities and heavy tails.

title: QT-Net: Rethinking Evaluation of AI Models in Atomic Chemical Space
summary: Atomic properties such as partial charges or multipoles encode chemically meaningful information that can inform downstream molecular property prediction, but their evaluation as machine learning targets has been complicated by the absence of a principled out-of-distribution evaluation protocol at the atomic level. In this work, we propose a held-out evaluation protocol that clusters atomic environments by SOAP descriptors and computes metrics accounting only for cluster labels unseen during training. Following this procedure, we use 5$\times$5 cross-validation and Tukey's HSD to run a statistically rigorous comparison of E(3)-equivariant against non-equivariant, rotationally augmented models for predicting electron populations and multipoles of H, C, N, and O atoms. Building on our results, we introduce the Quantum Topological Neural Network (QT-Net), a rotationally augmented, non-equivariant graph neural network. We show that QT-Net can be used to infer properties of atoms in molecules from QM9 outside our training set, and that these inferred properties can yield improvement when used as input features for downstream molecular property prediction. To further validate the framework, molecular dipole moments computed from QT-Net's per-atom outputs recover the ground-truth values reported in QM9. We release all code and data, including a JAX implementation of QT-Net, to support the broader use of learned QTA properties as inductive biases for atomic-scale molecular machine learning.

AI.tldr: Proposed held-out evaluation protocol for atomic property ML, compared equivariant vs non-equivariant models statistically, introduced QT-Net (non-equivariant GNN) that improves downstream tasks and recovers accurate dipole moments.
AI.motivation: Lack of principled out-of-distribution evaluation protocol for atomic properties like partial charges and multipoles in machine learning applications. Need for statistically rigorous comparison between E(3)-equivariant and non-equivariant models for predicting atomic electron populations and multipoles.
AI.method: A held-out evaluation protocol based on clustering atomic environments with SOAP descriptors, using 5x5 cross-validation and Tukey's HSD for statistical comparison. Introduction of Quantum Topological Neural Network (QT-Net) - a rotationally augmented, non-equivariant graph neural network implemented in JAX.
AI.result: QT-Net can infer properties of atoms outside the training set and these properties improve downstream molecular property prediction when used as input features. QT-Net's per-atom outputs accurately recover ground-truth molecular dipole moments from QM9 dataset.
AI.conclusion: The authors propose a principled evaluation protocol for atomic property prediction and introduce QT-Net, a non-equivariant graph neural network that demonstrates competitive performance. They show QT-Net's learned atomic properties can improve downstream molecular property prediction, validating the framework through accurate dipole moment recovery.

title: Self-Attention as a Covariance Readout: A Unified View of In-Context Learning and Repetition
summary: Large language models (LLMs) exhibit two striking and ostensibly unrelated behaviours: in-context learning (ICL) and repetitive generation. In both, the model behaves as though it had summarised the context into a population-level statistic and discarded token-level detail. We ask whether this ``summarisation and forgetting'' can be derived from the attention mechanism itself, and answer in the affirmative. Under stationary, ergodic and elliptical inputs, the softmax attention output converges almost surely to $Θ_VΣΘ_K^{\top}Θ_Q x_t$, where $Σ$ is the input covariance; the long-context limit is therefore a linear readout of the input's second-order statistics. Two consequences follow. (i) For in-context linear regression, a single softmax head can implement one step of population gradient descent. Stacking such heads with residual connections iterates this update and implements multiple gradient descent steps. (ii) Propagated across an $L$-layer transformer, this readout drives the terminal hidden state at the parametric $1/t$ rate to a deterministic function of the current token alone, so that autoregressive generation collapses asymptotically to a first-order Markov chain whose attracting orbits furnish a structural account of repetition and mode collapse. The two phenomena thus emerge as facets of a single covariance-readout principle.

AI.tldr: LLMs exhibit in-context learning and repetitive generation due to attention mechanism converging to linear readout of input covariance statistics.
AI.motivation: To explain why LLMs show in-context learning and repetitive generation, which appear as unrelated behaviors involving summarization and forgetting of context.
AI.method: Mathematical analysis of softmax attention under stationary, ergodic, elliptical inputs shows convergence to linear readout of covariance; applied to in-context linear regression and transformer propagation.
AI.result: 1. Single softmax head implements one step of population gradient descent for linear regression; stacked heads implement multiple steps. 2. Across transformer layers, terminal hidden state converges to function of current token alone, causing autoregressive generation to collapse to Markov chain with attracting orbits explaining repetition.
AI.conclusion: In-context learning and repetitive generation are unified facets of a single covariance-readout principle inherent to attention mechanism.

title: Can Muon Fine-tune Adam-Pretrained Models?
summary: Muon has emerged as an efficient alternative to Adam for pretraining, yet remains underused for fine-tuning. A key obstacle is that most open models are pretrained with Adam, and naively switching to Muon for fine-tuning leads to degraded performance due to an optimizer mismatch. We investigate this mismatch through controlled experiments and relate it to the distinct implicit biases of Adam and Muon. We provide evidence that the mismatch disrupts pretrained knowledge, and that this disruption scales with update strength. This leads us to hypothesize that constraining updates should mitigate the mismatch. We validate this with LoRA: across language and vision tasks, LoRA reduces the performance gap between Adam and Muon observed under full fine-tuning. Studies on LoRA rank, catastrophic forgetting, and LoRA variants further confirm that mismatch severity correlates with update strength. These results shed light on how optimizer mismatch affects fine-tuning and how it can be mitigated. Our code is available at https://github.com/XingyuQu/muon-finetune.

AI.tldr: Investigates optimizer mismatch when switching from Adam to Muon during fine-tuning, showing it degrades performance due to disruptive implicit biases. Proposes using LoRA to constrain updates as a mitigation strategy.
AI.motivation: Muon is an efficient alternative to Adam for pretraining, but switching to Muon for fine-tuning existing Adam-pretrained models leads to degraded performance due to optimizer mismatch.
AI.method: Conducted controlled experiments to study optimizer mismatch, related it to implicit biases of Adam and Muon, proposed constraining updates via LoRA to mitigate mismatch, and validated approach across language/vision tasks.
AI.result: Found optimizer mismatch disrupts pretrained knowledge and scales with update strength. LoRA reduces performance gap between Adam and Muon in fine-tuning. Studies on LoRA rank, forgetting, and variants confirm mismatch severity correlates with update strength.
AI.conclusion: Optimizer mismatch negatively impacts fine-tuning when switching optimizers, but can be mitigated by constraining updates through techniques like LoRA.

title: ConfoundingSHAP: Quantifying confounding strength in causal inference
summary: In causal inference, confounders are variables that influence both treatment decisions and outcomes. However, unlike as in randomized clinical trials, the treatment assignment mechanism in observational studies is not known, and it is thus unclear which covariates act as confounders. Here, we aim to generate insight for causal inference and answer: which of the observed covariates act as confounders? We introduce ConfoundingSHAP, a Shapley-based method for attributing confounding strength to individual covariates. Our contributions are twofold. First, we propose a Shapley game targeted to infer the confounding strength of the covariates. Our resulting Shapley values differ from the standard applications of SHAP explanations on causal targets, such as understanding treatment effect heterogeneity, which are ill-suited for our task. Second, as our task requires evaluating the value function over many adjustment sets, we provide a scalable TabPFN-based estimation that avoids exhaustive refitting. We demonstrate the practical value across various datasets, where ConfoundingSHAP provides informative explanations of which observed covariates drive confounding and thereby helps to provide more insight for causal inference in practice.

AI.tldr: ConfoundingSHAP is a Shapley-based method that identifies which covariates act as confounders in observational studies, using a specialized game design and scalable estimation to provide practical insights.
AI.motivation: In observational studies, unlike randomized trials, the treatment assignment mechanism is unknown, making it unclear which covariates act as confounders that influence both treatment decisions and outcomes. This creates a need to identify which observed covariates actually drive confounding.
AI.method: Introduces ConfoundingSHAP, a Shapley-based method with two contributions: (1) a specialized Shapley game for confounding attribution that differs from standard SHAP applications, and (2) a scalable TabPFN-based estimation to avoid exhaustive refitting when evaluating value functions over many adjustment sets.
AI.result: ConfoundingSHAP successfully provides informative explanations across various datasets, demonstrating which observed covariates drive confounding and thereby offering practical insights for causal inference.
AI.conclusion: ConfoundingSHAP provides a scalable and effective method for attributing confounding strength to individual covariates, offering practical insights for causal inference in observational studies.

title: Higher Resolution, Better Generalization: Unlocking Visual Scaling in Deep Reinforcement Learning
summary: Pixel-based deep reinforcement learning agents are typically trained on heavily downsampled visual observations, a convention inherited from early benchmarks rather than grounded in principled design. In this work, we show that observation resolution is a critical yet overlooked variable for policy learning: higher-resolution inputs can substantially improve both performance and generalization, provided the network architecture can process them effectively. We find that the widely used Impala encoder, which flattens spatial features into a vector, suffers from quadratic parameter growth as resolution increases and fails to leverage the additional visual detail. Replacing this operation with global average pooling, as in the Impoola architecture, decouples parameter count from resolution and yields consistent improvements across resolutions and network widths - at their respective best conditions, visual scaling unlocks a 28 % performance gain for Impoola over Impala. These gains are strongest in environments that require precise perception of small or distant objects, and gradient saliency analysis confirms that the underlying mechanism is a more spatially localized visual attention of the policy at higher resolutions. Our results challenge the prevailing practice of aggressive input downsampling and position resolution-independent architectures as a simple, effective path toward scalable visual deep RL. To facilitate future research on resolution scaling in deep RL, we publicly release the open-source code for the Procgen-HD benchmark: https://github.com/raphajaner/procgen-hd.

AI.tldr: Higher-resolution visual inputs significantly improve policy learning performance and generalization in RL, but require resolution-independent architectures like Impoola (using global average pooling) instead of traditional Impala (which has quadratic parameter growth)
AI.motivation: Observation resolution has been overlooked as a critical variable in deep RL; current practice of heavy downsampling is inherited from early benchmarks rather than principled design
AI.method: Analyze resolution scaling with Impala vs. Impoola architectures; Impoola replaces spatial feature flattening with global average pooling to decouple parameter count from resolution
AI.result: Higher-resolution inputs provide substantial performance gains (+28% for Impoola over Impala); strongest improvements in environments requiring precise perception of small/distant objects; gradient saliency shows more spatially localized visual attention at higher resolutions
AI.conclusion: Challenges aggressive input downsampling practice; proposes resolution-independent architectures as effective path for scalable visual RL; releases Procgen-HD benchmark for future research

title: PhysEDA: Physics-Aware Learning Framework for Efficient EDA With Manhattan Distance Decay
comment: 9 pages, 4 figures, plus appendix. Code and data to be released upon publication
summary: Electronic design automation (EDA) addresses placement, routing, timing analysis, and power-integrity verification for integrated circuits. Learning methods -- attention (Transformer) and reinforcement learning (RL) -- have recently emerged on EDA tasks, yet face two common bottlenecks: vanilla attention's quadratic complexity limits scaling, and data-scarce models overfit statistical noise and amplify weak long-range correlations against the underlying physics. We observe that EDA tasks share a physical prior -- pairwise electrical and routing interactions decay exponentially along Manhattan distance -- and integrate it as a unified inductive bias into both architecture and training. We propose PhysEDA, comprising two components Physics-Structured Linear Attention (PSLA) folds the separable Manhattan decay into the linear-attention kernel as a multiplicative bias, reducing complexity from quadratic to linear; Potential-Based Reward Shaping (PBRS) constructs a physical potential from the same kernel, providing dense reward signal under sparse RL while preserving the optimal policy via the policy-invariance theorem. Across three EDA scenarios -- decoupling-capacitor placement, macro placement, and IR-drop prediction -- PhysEDA improves zero-shot cross-scale transfer by 56.8% and achieves 14x inference speedup with 98.5% memory savings on 100x100 grids; PBRS adds another 10.8% in sparse-reward DPP.

AI.tldr: PhysEDA integrates physics-based inductive bias into Transformer and RL models for electronic design automation tasks by incorporating Manhattan distance decay into attention kernels and reward shaping
AI.motivation: Current learning methods in EDA (attention and reinforcement learning) face scalability issues due to quadratic complexity of vanilla attention and overfitting problems in data-scarce models that amplify weak long-range correlations against physical principles
AI.method: PhysEDA comprises two components: 1) Physics-Structured Linear Attention (PSLA) that folds separable Manhattan decay into linear-attention kernel as multiplicative bias to reduce complexity from quadratic to linear, 2) Potential-Based Reward Shaping (PBRS) that constructs physical potential from the same kernel to provide dense reward signals in sparse RL while preserving optimal policy via policy-invariance theorem
AI.result: Across three EDA scenarios (decoupling-capacitor placement, macro placement, IR-drop prediction), PhysEDA improves zero-shot cross-scale transfer by 56.8%, achieves 14x inference speedup with 98.5% memory savings on 100x100 grids, and PBRS adds another 10.8% improvement in sparse-reward decoupling capacitor placement
AI.conclusion: Incorporating physical prior of exponential decay of pairwise electrical/routing interactions along Manhattan distance as unified inductive bias in both architecture and training significantly improves performance, efficiency, and generalization in EDA tasks

title: It's All Connected: Topology-Aware Structural Graph Encoding Improves Performance on Polymer Prediction
summary: Graph Neural Networks (GNNs) have achieved strong results in molecular property prediction, but polymers present distinct challenges: labeled datasets are scarce and small (typically in the order of hundreds of polymers) due to the need for expensive experimentation, and complex polymer chain distributions influence polymer properties. Established practice in polymer prediction represents polymers solely by graphs of their repeat units, discarding the chain-scale morphology that governs key properties such as the glass transition temperature ($T_g$). In this work, we propose a principled graph construction that addresses this gap. Given a polymer's molecular mass distribution (MMD), we sample representative chains from the Schulz-Zimm distribution and construct representative sets of large graphs encoding chain-scale topology directly, with atoms and bonds featurized using rich chemical descriptors. We further pretrain GNN encoders via masked graph modeling on 100,000 unlabeled PSMILES strings before fine-tuning on labeled data. On a dataset of 381 polymers (180 homopolymers and 201 copolymers), we show that graph construction and self-supervised pretraining are jointly necessary: without pretraining, the large graph method matches the repeat-unit baseline (28.40 K vs. 28.36 K RMSE); with pretraining, it achieves 24.76 K +/- 3.30 K, a 5.1% reduction in mean error over the pretrained repeat-unit baseline (26.08 K +/- 4.20 K, p < 0.001, 30 runs). An ablation removing chemical features degrades performance to 36.65 K, confirming both components are essential. Results are architecture-agnostic, holding for both GINE and GATv2 encoders.

AI.tldr: Proposes a graph construction method for polymers that captures chain-scale morphology and combines with self-supervised pretraining, achieving significant improvement in glass transition temperature prediction over existing methods.
AI.motivation:
1. Polymer property prediction faces two key challenges: scarce labeled datasets (typically hundreds of samples) due to expensive experimentation, and complex polymer chain distributions that influence properties.
2. Current methods represent polymers solely by repeat-unit graphs, discarding chain-scale morphology that governs key properties like glass transition temperature (T_g).
3. Need to bridge the gap between molecular-level representation and polymer-scale properties.
AI.method:
1. Graph construction: Sample representative polymer chains from molecular mass distribution (Schulz-Zimm distribution), construct large graphs encoding chain-scale topology with rich chemical descriptors for atoms and bonds.
2. Self-supervised pretraining: Masked graph modeling on 100,000 unlabeled PSMILES strings before fine-tuning on labeled data.
3. Evaluation: Tested on dataset of 381 polymers (180 homopolymers and 201 copolymers), comparing with repeat-unit baseline.
AI.result:
1. Without pretraining, the large graph method matches repeat-unit baseline (28.40 K vs. 28.36 K RMSE).
2. With pretraining, achieves 24.76 K +/- 3.30 K RMSE, a 5.1% reduction in mean error over pretrained repeat-unit baseline (26.08 K +/- 4.20 K, p < 0.001).
3. Ablation removing chemical features degrades performance to 36.65 K RMSE.
4. Results are architecture-agnostic, holding for both GINE and GATv2 encoders.
AI.conclusion: A principled graph construction method for polymer representation, combined with self-supervised pretraining on unlabeled PSMILES data, significantly improves GNN performance on polymer property prediction compared to established repeat-unit baselines.

title: Online Sharp-Calibrated Bayesian Optimization
summary: Bayesian optimization (BO) is a widely used framework for optimizing expensive black-box functions, commonly based on Gaussian process (GP) surrogate models. Its effectiveness relies on uncertainty quantification that is both sharp (informative) and well-calibrated along the BO trajectory. In practice, GP kernel hyperparameters are unknown and are refit online from sequentially collected (non-i.i.d.) data, which can yield miscalibrated or overly conservative uncertainty and lies outside the fixed-kernel assumptions of standard BO regret theory. We propose Online Sharp-Calibrated Bayesian Optimization (OSCBO), a BO algorithm that adaptively balances GP sharpness and calibration by casting hyperparameter selection as a constrained online-learning problem. We also show that OSCBO preserves sublinear regret bounds by leveraging the theoretical guarantees of the underlying online learning algorithm. Empirically, OSCBO performs competitively across synthetic and real-world benchmarks, ranking among the strongest methods in final simple regret while maintaining robust cumulative-regret behavior.

AI.tldr: OSCBO balances sharpness and calibration in Bayesian optimization via online learning of GP hyperparameters, achieving competitive performance with theoretical guarantees.
AI.motivation: GP kernel hyperparameters in BO are unknown and refit online from sequentially collected non-i.i.d. data, leading to miscalibrated or overly conservative uncertainty estimates that violate standard BO regret assumptions.
AI.method: Proposes OSCBO which frames kernel hyperparameter selection as a constrained online-learning problem, adaptively balancing GP sharpness and calibration using online learning techniques.
AI.result: Empirically performs competitively across synthetic and real-world benchmarks, ranking among strongest methods in final simple regret while maintaining robust cumulative-regret behavior. Theoretically preserves sublinear regret bounds.
AI.conclusion: OSCBO addresses the fundamental challenge of balancing uncertainty sharpness and calibration in BO by framing kernel hyperparameter selection as an online learning problem, while preserving theoretical regret guarantees.

title: Controllability in preference-conditioned multi-objective reinforcement learning
summary: Multi-objective reinforcement learning (MORL) allows a user to express preference over outcomes in terms of the relative importance of the objectives, but standard metrics cannot capture whether changes in preference reliably change the agent's behavior in the intended way, a property termed controllability. As a result, preference-conditioned agents can score well on standard MORL metrics while being insensitive to the preference input. If the ability to control agents cannot be reliably assessed, the symbolic interface that MORL provides between user intent and agent behavior is broken. Mainstream MORL metrics alone fail to measure the controllability of preference-conditioned agents, motivating a complementary metric specifically designed to that end. We hope the results spur discussion in the community on existing evaluation protocols to consolidate advances in preference adaptation in MORL to larger and more complex problems.

AI.tldr: Standard MORL metrics don't measure whether agents are controllable by preference changes, so the paper proposes a new metric specifically for assessing controllability.
AI.motivation: Standard MORL metrics cannot capture whether changes in preference reliably change agent behavior in the intended way (controllability). This breaks the symbolic interface between user intent and agent behavior that MORL is supposed to provide.
AI.method: The paper critiques existing MORL metrics and proposes a new complementary metric designed specifically to measure controllability of preference-conditioned agents.
AI.result: The analysis reveals that mainstream MORL metrics alone fail to measure the controllability of preference-conditioned agents, and preference-conditioned agents can score well on standard metrics while being insensitive to preference input.
AI.conclusion: The authors propose a new metric specifically for measuring controllability in MORL, arguing that standard metrics fail to properly assess preference-conditioned agents. They hope this will spur discussion and improve evaluation protocols for preference adaptation in MORL.

title: MulTaBench: Benchmarking Multimodal Tabular Learning with Text and Image
summary: Tabular Foundation Models have recently established the state of the art in supervised tabular learning, by leveraging pretraining to learn generalizable representations of numerical and categorical structured data. However, they lack native support for unstructured modalities such as text and image, and rely on frozen, pretrained embeddings to process them. On established Multimodal Tabular Learning benchmarks, we show that tuning the embeddings to the task improves performance. Existing benchmarks, however, often focus on the mere co-occurrence of modalities; this leads to high variance across datasets and masks the benefits of task-specific tuning. To address this gap, we introduce MulTaBench, a benchmark of 40 datasets, split equally between image-tabular and text-tabular tasks. We focus on predictive tasks where the modalities provide complementary predictive signal, and where generic embeddings lose critical information, necessitating Target-Aware Representations that are aligned with the task. Our experimental results demonstrate that the gains from target-aware representation tuning generalize across both text and image modalities, several tabular learners, encoder scales, and embedding dimensions. MulTaBench constitutes the largest image-tabular benchmarking effort to date, spanning high-impact domains such as healthcare and e-commerce. It is designed to enable the research of novel architectures which incorporate joint modeling and target-aware representations, paving the way for the development of novel Multimodal Tabular Foundation Models.

AI.tldr: MulTaBench: A comprehensive benchmark for multimodal tabular learning (40 datasets with image-tabular and text-tabular tasks) demonstrating that task-specific embedding tuning improves performance when modalities provide complementary predictive signal.
AI.motivation: Current tabular foundation models lack native support for unstructured modalities and rely on frozen pretrained embeddings, which may lose critical information for tasks requiring complementary predictive signals from different modalities. Existing benchmarks focus on mere co-occurrence rather than complementary signal.
AI.method: Introduces MulTaBench with 40 datasets split equally between image-tabular and text-tabular tasks, specifically focusing on predictive scenarios where modalities provide complementary predictive signal. Evaluates target-aware representation tuning across both modalities, multiple tabular learners, encoder scales, and embedding dimensions.
AI.result: Task-specific embedding tuning improves performance when modalities provide complementary predictive signal. Gains from target-aware representation tuning generalize across text and image modalities, various tabular learners, encoder scales, and embedding dimensions.
AI.conclusion: MulTaBench enables research on novel architectures with joint modeling and target-aware representations, paving the way for novel multimodal tabular foundation models. It demonstrates the importance of task-specific tuning for modalities with complementary predictive signals.

title: Hierarchical End-to-End Taylor Bounds for Complete Neural Network Verification

AI.tldr: HiTaB is a novel verification framework for neural network safety certification that systematically exploits higher-order information (second-order smoothness) through Hessian analysis and Lipschitz continuity of curvature, providing tighter reachable set bounds than existing methods.
AI.motivation: Existing neural network verification methods for reachability analysis typically use tractable overapproximations but exploit at most second-order information and don't systematically leverage higher-order information. There's a need for tighter safety certificates that use more precise mathematical analysis.
AI.method: HiTaB framework exploits second-order smoothness through both Hessian (∇²f) and its Lipschitz constant (L∇²f). It develops a unified hierarchy of zeroth-, first-, and second-order bounds with precise conditions for improvements. Includes compositional procedure for efficiently bounding L∇²f via layerwise propagation of curvature bounds in deep networks, supporting both ℓ₂- and ℓ∞-constrained input sets and integration into branch-and-bound verification pipelines.
AI.result: HiTaB provides tighter and more informative safety certificates by systematically exploiting Lipschitz continuity of curvature. It's the first practical reachability analysis framework for smooth neural networks that uses higher-order information effectively.
AI.conclusion: HiTaB advances neural network verification by enabling systematic exploitation of higher-order smoothness information, leading to improved reachability bounds and safety certification for learning-enabled physical systems.

title: Composing diffusion priors with explicit physical context via generative Gibbs sampling
summary: Pretrained diffusion models provide powerful learned priors, but in scientific sampling the target distribution often depends on physical context that is not fully represented by one generative model. We introduce Generative Gibbs for Physics-Aware Sampling (GG-PA), a training-free framework that formulates the composition of learned partial priors and explicit physical context as inference over a joint target distribution in an augmented state space. We derive a Gibbs sampler for this joint target, show that it is asymptotically exact as the diffusion time approaches zero, and prove that in settings with quadratic interactions it remains exact at finite diffusion times. We further introduce replica exchange over diffusion time to accelerate mixing. Experiments on a double-well system, a $φ^4$ lattice model, and atomistic peptide systems show that GG-PA recovers context-induced distribution shifts and emergent collective behavior in interacting systems using partial priors without retraining. These results demonstrate GG-PA as a practical approach for combining pretrained generative priors with explicit physical context.

AI.tldr: GG-PA is a training-free framework that combines pretrained diffusion priors with explicit physical context via Gibbs sampling in an augmented state space, enabling accurate sampling of complex scientific systems.
AI.motivation: Pretrained diffusion models provide powerful learned priors, but in scientific sampling the target distribution often depends on physical context not fully captured by a single generative model.
AI.method: GG-PA formulates composition of learned partial priors and physical context as inference over a joint target distribution in an augmented state space, derives a Gibbs sampler for this joint target (asymptotically exact as diffusion time → 0, and exact at finite diffusion times for quadratic interactions), and introduces replica exchange over diffusion time to accelerate mixing.
AI.result: Experiments on a double-well system, a φ⁴ lattice model, and atomistic peptide systems show that GG-PA recovers context-induced distribution shifts and emergent collective behavior in interacting systems using partial priors without retraining.
AI.conclusion: GG-PA is a practical and effective framework for combining pretrained diffusion priors with explicit physical context without retraining, enabling accurate sampling of complex interacting systems.

title: A Random-Matrix Criterion for Initializing Gated Recurrent Neural Networks
summary: Proper weight initialization prior to training has historically been one of the key factors that helped kick off the deep learning revolution. Initialization is even more crucial in "reservoir computing", where the weights of a readout layer are learned linearly while the reservoir weights are fixed and largely determine the richness, stability and memory of the resulting dynamics. In the infinite-width limit it has been shown that meaningful initializations are those sitting at an effective critical point of the randomly initialized model. The phase transition is controlled by the weight variance $g^2$ and separates an ordered phase from a chaotic one where information progressively degrades. Here we derive a simple criterion to estimate the critical $g_c$ for a broad class of recurrent architectures and we show that it closely tracks the gain at which a gated-RNN reservoir achieves peak performance on a chaotic forecasting task. Finally, we argue that our criterion can serve as a design principle for future initialization schemes.

AI.tldr: A simple criterion for estimating the critical weight variance in recurrent architectures is derived and shown to track peak reservoir performance on chaotic forecasting.
AI.motivation: Proper weight initialization is crucial in deep learning and especially in reservoir computing, where reservoir weights are fixed and determine the richness, stability, and memory of dynamics. Meaningful initializations in the infinite-width limit are those at an effective critical point separating ordered and chaotic phases.
AI.method: Derived a simple criterion to estimate the critical weight variance $g_c$ for a broad class of recurrent architectures, and validated it by showing it closely tracks the gain at which a gated-RNN reservoir achieves peak performance on a chaotic forecasting task.
AI.result: The derived criterion accurately tracks the critical gain $g_c$ where a gated-RNN reservoir achieves peak performance on a chaotic forecasting task, demonstrating its practical utility.
AI.conclusion: The proposed criterion for estimating the critical weight variance can serve as a design principle for future initialization schemes in reservoir computing and recurrent neural networks.

title: BCJR-QAT: A Differentiable Relaxation of Trellis-Coded Weight Quantization
comment: 26 pages, 4 figures, 4 tables. Code at https://github.com/Venugopalan2610/quant-2bit. Model weights and trajectory snapshots at https://huggingface.co/Venugopalan2610/BCJR-QAT-Llama-3.2-1B-2bit
summary: Trellis-coded quantization sets the current 2-bit post-training frontier for LLMs (QTIP), but pushing below the PTQ ceiling requires quantization-aware training, and QAT on a trellis is obstructed by the non-differentiable Viterbi argmax. We introduce BCJR-QAT, a relaxation that replaces the argmax with the BCJR forward-backward sum-product algorithm at temperature $T$, producing a soft codeword equal to the Boltzmann expectation over trellis paths, exactly differentiable, recovering the hard QTIP code as $T \to 0$, and mathematically identical to the transfer-matrix computation for a 1D Ising-like spin chain. We contribute (i) a fused Triton kernel making BCJR tractable on a single consumer GPU ($6.57\times$ speedup, fp32 parity); (ii) a quantitative drift-budget theory of when BCJR-QAT can escape the QTIP-PTQ Voronoi basin, verified across four experiments; and (iii) a positive empirical result on Llama-3.2-1B at 2 bpw under end-to-end forward-KL distillation: with the right schedule (skip the high-$T$ phase to avoid an overshoot we diagnose), single-layer BCJR-QAT beats QTIP-PTQ by $\mathbf{-0.084}$ PPL on WikiText-2, and multi-layer compounding is super-additive.

AI.tldr: BCJR-QAT enables quantization-aware training for trellis-coded quantization by replacing non-differentiable Viterbi argmax with differentiable BCJR algorithm, achieving better than 2-bit post-training quantization for LLMs.
AI.motivation: Current trellis-coded quantization hits a PTQ ceiling at 2-bit, and pushing below requires QAT. However, QAT is obstructed by the non-differentiable Viterbi argmax in the trellis framework.
AI.method: Introduces BCJR-QAT: a relaxation replacing Viterbi argmax with BCJR forward-backward sum-product algorithm at temperature T, producing soft codewords as Boltzmann expectations over trellis paths. Includes (i) fused Triton kernel for GPU efficiency, (ii) drift-budget theory, and (iii) schedule optimization.
AI.result: On Llama-3.2-1B at 2 bpw: single-layer BCJR-QAT beats QTIP-PTQ by -0.084 PPL on WikiText-2. Multi-layer compounding shows super-additive benefits. Achieves 6.57× speedup with fp32 parity.
AI.conclusion: BCJR-QAT enables effective quantization-aware training for trellis-coded quantization, breaking the PTQ ceiling and achieving state-of-the-art 2-bit quantization for LLMs through differentiable trellis optimization.

title: Why Zeroth-Order Adaptation May Forget Less: A Randomized Shaping Theory
summary: Continual learning requires new-task adaptation without damaging previously acquired capabilities. Recent forward-pass and zeroth-order (ZO) results show that low-query adaptation may retain better than first-order (FO) descent, but the usual view of ZO as noisy FO estimation does not explain why. We give a local randomized gradient-shaping analysis: finite differences expose a raw shape that is mean-aligned with FO, while the norm-matched comparator fixes the expected squared adaptation norm. Under this controlled comparison, forgetting depends on how the adaptation shape exposes retention curvature. For norm-matched ZO, the expected shaped retention curvature obeys an exact identity that preserves the isotropic retention floor while contracting only the anisotropic component. Projecting this identity onto the incoming gradient yields the observable FO--ZO quadratic forgetting gap: ZO improves mean forgetting precisely when the FO direction has above-average retention curvature, by a query-dependent fraction of that curvature excess. A practical finite-query accounting separates the mean mechanism from one-batch sampling and smoothing perturbations. As an algorithmic transfer, RISE applies the calibrated ZO shape to exact FO gradients inside parameter blocks.
