Its target is a stability--plasticity tradeoff: randomized shaping may reduce the retention exposure paid by FO, exact gradients remove finite-smoothing bias from finite-difference ZO, and blockwise sampling supplies many local shaping directions after one gradient computation. The blockwise analysis separates mean-step damage from centered random exposure, showing how block-diagonal curvature, cross-block coupling, and local shaping diagnostics specify where this exact-gradient transfer is most likely to be visible.

AI.tldr: The paper shows that zeroth-order (ZO) gradient methods in continual learning can preserve past knowledge better than first-order (FO) methods due to gradient shaping effects, not just noisy estimation. It introduces RISE, which applies calibrated ZO shaping to FO gradients within parameter blocks to balance stability and plasticity.
AI.motivation: Understanding why zeroth-order (finite-difference) methods sometimes outperform first-order gradient descent in continual learning, where the usual view of ZO as noisy FO estimation doesn't explain the observed advantage in knowledge retention.
AI.method: Provides a local randomized gradient-shaping analysis showing how ZO gradient estimates expose a "shape" with different retention curvature properties. Introduces RISE (Randomized Shaping for Exact gradients), which applies calibrated ZO shaping to exact FO gradients within parameter blocks while using blockwise sampling to generate multiple shaping directions from a single gradient computation.
AI.result: Reveals an exact identity where norm-matched ZO preserves isotropic retention while contracting only anisotropic components, creating a FO-ZO quadratic forgetting gap. The practical algorithm RISE demonstrates how randomized shaping can reduce retention exposure while exact gradients eliminate finite-smoothing bias from finite-difference ZO methods.
AI.conclusion: ZO gradient shaping offers a mechanism to improve stability-plasticity tradeoffs in continual learning by selectively contracting anisotropic retention curvature components. RISE provides a practical implementation that transfers these benefits to exact gradients through blockwise randomized shaping, with block-diagonal curvature and cross-block coupling specifying where these advantages are most visible.

title: A Spectral Framework for Closed-Form Relative Density Estimation
summary: We propose a closed-form spectral framework for relative log-density estimation in linearly parameterized probabilistic models, including unnormalized and conditional models. This is achieved by representing the Kullback-Leibler (KL) divergence as an integral of weighted chi-squared divergences, converting KL estimation into a family of least-squares problems. We derive an explicit spectral formula based only on first- and second-order feature moments, yielding closed-form estimators of both divergences and log-density potentials for fixed features. The framework extends to a broad class of f-divergences and can be combined with kernelization or feature learning with neural networks. We prove convergence guarantees for the resulting estimators and empirically compare them on synthetic data with optimization-based variational formulations, including logistic and softmax regression for normalized conditional models.

AI.tldr: Closed-form spectral framework for relative log-density estimation using KL divergence decomposition into weighted chi-squared divergences, yielding estimators based on feature moments
AI.motivation: Need for efficient relative log-density estimation in linearly parameterized probabilistic models (including unnormalized and conditional models) without requiring optimization-based approaches
AI.method: Represent KL divergence as integral of weighted chi-squared divergences, convert KL estimation into family of least-squares problems, derive explicit spectral formula using first- and second-order feature moments
AI.result: Closed-form estimators for both divergences and log-density potentials that work with fixed features, extendable to f-divergences, and combinable with kernelization or neural network feature learning
AI.conclusion: Proposed spectral framework provides efficient closed-form alternative to optimization-based methods with proven convergence guarantees and empirical validation against variational formulations

title: Natural Policy Gradient as Doubly Smoothed Policy Iteration: A Bellman-Operator Framework
summary: In this work, we show that natural policy gradient, a core algorithm in reinforcement learning, admits an exact formulation as a smoothed and averaged form of policy iteration. Specifically, we introduce doubly smoothed policy iteration (DSPI), a Bellman-operator framework in which each policy is obtained by applying a regularized greedy step to a weighted average of past $Q$-functions. DSPI includes policy iteration, dual-averaged policy iteration, natural policy gradient, and more general policy dual averaging methods as special cases. Using only monotonicity and contraction of smoothed Bellman operators, we prove distribution-free global geometric convergence of DSPI. Consequently, standard natural policy gradient and policy dual averaging achieve an iteration complexity of $\mathcal{O}((1-γ)^{-1}\log((1-γ)^{-1}ε^{-1}))$ for computing an $ε$-optimal policy, without modifying the MDP, adding regularization beyond the mirror map inherent in the update, or using adaptive, trajectory-dependent stepsizes. For the unregularized greedy case, corresponding to dual-averaged policy iteration, we also prove finite termination. The same Bellman-operator framework further extends to discounted MDPs with linear function approximation and stochastic shortest path problems.

AI.tldr: Natural Policy Gradient (NPG) is reformulated as smoothed policy iteration with geometric convergence guarantees, achieving improved iteration complexity without extra regularization or adaptive stepsizes.
AI.motivation: The paper aims to provide a unified theoretical understanding of natural policy gradient (NPG) and related algorithms by connecting them to policy iteration through a Bellman-operator framework, addressing the need for better convergence guarantees and iteration complexity analysis.
AI.method: Introduces Doubly Smoothed Policy Iteration (DSPI), a framework where policies are obtained by applying regularized greedy steps to weighted averages of past Q-functions. This unifies policy iteration, dual-averaged policy iteration, natural policy gradient, and policy dual averaging methods.
AI.result: Proves distribution-free global geometric convergence of DSPI using monotonicity and contraction properties. Shows that natural policy gradient achieves iteration complexity of O((1-γ)⁻¹log((1-γ)⁻¹ε⁻¹)) for ε-optimal policies. Also proves finite termination for unregularized greedy case (dual-averaged policy iteration).
AI.conclusion: The DSPI framework provides a unified theory connecting natural policy gradient to policy iteration with strong convergence guarantees, and extends to function approximation and stochastic shortest path problems, offering improved theoretical foundations for reinforcement learning algorithms.

title: Compander-Aligned Query Geometry for Quantized Zeroth-Order Optimization
summary: Low-bit forward evaluation is an attractive route to memory-efficient zeroth-order (ZO) adaptation: the optimizer needs only scalar losses, and the model can be queried near deployment precision. The obstacle is that a quantized ZO query is not a continuous finite difference followed by harmless storage rounding. The query chooses endpoints, the low-precision engine rounds them, and the loss difference is measured along the rounded chord. For nonuniform companding quantizers, this makes the codebook insufficient to predict ZO behavior: a fixed weight-space radius can collapse in dense cells, over-span sparse cells, or assign a rounded chord to an unrounded update direction. We identify the missing object as query geometry and model scalar nonuniform quantization as $Q = φ^{-1} \circ U \circ φ$. CAQ-ZO (Compander-Aligned Queries for Zeroth-Order Optimization) forms one-grid-step Rademacher stencils $z \pm Δr$ in $z = φ(x)$, maps endpoints back through $φ^{-1}$, and updates in $z$. Our theory proves the grid-span mismatch, decomposes endpoint-rounding estimator residuals, and gives stationarity bounds in which generic off-grid queries retain a $Δ^2/μ^2$ residual channel while CAQ-ZO makes the query-time residual exactly zero. Synthetic experiments isolate this channel, and matched NF4 Qwen/Llama fine-tuning shows that CAQ-ZO improves the trained NF4 baseline under the same quantizer and evaluation budget.

AI.tldr: CAQ-ZO improves low-precision zeroth-order optimization by addressing geometry mismatches in nonuniform quantization, using compander-aligned queries to eliminate query-time residuals that plague generic off-grid approaches.
AI.motivation: Low-bit forward evaluation offers memory-efficient zeroth-order adaptation, but conventional quantized ZO queries suffer from geometry mismatches where fixed weight-space radii behave unpredictably in nonuniform quantization spaces, leading to inefficiencies.
AI.method: CAQ-ZO models nonuniform quantization as Q = φ^{-1} ∘ U ∘ φ, forms one-grid-step Rademacher stencils in the transformed space z = φ(x), maps endpoints back through φ^{-1}, and updates in the transformed z space to align queries with quantization grid.
AI.result: Theoretical analysis proves CAQ-ZO eliminates query-time residuals that generic off-grid queries retain (Δ²/μ² residual channel), and synthetic experiments validate this. Fine-tuning experiments with NF4 Qwen/Llama show CAQ-ZO outperforms baseline under same quantizer and evaluation budget.
AI.conclusion: CAQ-ZO provides a principled solution to the geometry mismatch problem in low-precision zeroth-order optimization, offering improved performance by aligning queries with nonuniform quantization structure, enabling more effective memory-efficient adaptation.

title: Exact Unlearning from Proxies Induces Closeness Guarantees on Approximate Unlearning
summary: This paper proposes a paradigm shift linking machine unlearning directly to the structure of the data distributions rather than a mere update of the neural network parameters. We show that inferring these distributions with precision enables distilling the exact unlearning signal induced by the modeling. Theoretical bounds on the Kullback-Leibler divergence from the ideal retrained model to our unlearned model, under verifiable admissibility criterion, reveal the soundness of our framework. This method is experimentally validated over three forgetting scenarios as reaching the closest classifier to the ideal retrained model when compared to competitors.

AI.tldr: Machine unlearning method that focuses on data distribution structure rather than parameter updates, providing theoretical guarantees on divergence from ideal retrained models.
AI.motivation: Current machine unlearning approaches primarily focus on updating neural network parameters, which may not fully capture the underlying data distribution structure and may not provide theoretical guarantees about the quality of unlearning.
AI.method: The method links machine unlearning directly to data distribution structure by precisely inferring these distributions, enabling distillation of exact unlearning signals. Uses verifiable admissibility criteria and provides theoretical bounds on Kullback-Leibler divergence from ideal retrained models.
AI.result: Experimental validation across three forgetting scenarios shows the method achieves the closest classifier to the ideal retrained model compared to competitors. Theoretical bounds on KL divergence demonstrate the soundness of the framework.
AI.conclusion: This distribution-focused approach to machine unlearning represents a paradigm shift that provides both theoretical guarantees and practical effectiveness, outperforming existing methods by directly addressing data structure rather than merely updating parameters.

title: The finite expression method for turbulent dynamics with high-order moment recovery
summary: Turbulent dynamical systems are characterized by nonlinear interactions and stochastic effects that generate coupled statistical quantities, such as non-zero higher-order moments, which are difficult to capture from data with accuracy. We propose a two-stage data-driven modeling framework that combines symbolic regression with generative models to jointly identify the governing dynamics and predict their key statistical quantities. In Stage I of the framework, the Finite Expression Method (FEX) is adopted to discover closed-form expressions of the deterministic dynamics, recovering nonlinear interaction terms and external forcing without predefined libraries. In Stage II, generative models are introduced to learn the residual stochastic components as a refined correction to the model error from the Stage I approximation, enabling accurate characterization of higher-order statistics. Theoretical analysis establishes the consistency of the symbolic estimator and quantifies the estimation error in terms of data size and numerical discretization. The model performance is verified through detailed numerical experiments on the stochastic triad models across multiple regimes, demonstrating that the framework successfully recovers interaction terms and forcing expressions, and accurately predicts statistical moments up to order five. These results highlight the potential of integrating interpretable symbolic discovery with data-driven stochastic modeling for complex turbulent systems.

AI.tldr: A two-stage data-driven framework combining symbolic regression (FEX) to discover deterministic dynamics and generative models to capture stochastic residuals, enabling accurate prediction of higher-order statistics in turbulent systems.
AI.motivation: Turbulent systems have coupled statistical quantities (non-zero higher-order moments) that are difficult to capture accurately from data due to nonlinear interactions and stochastic effects.
AI.method: Two-stage framework: Stage I uses Finite Expression Method (FEX) for symbolic regression to discover closed-form deterministic dynamics without predefined libraries. Stage II uses generative models to learn residual stochastic components as model error corrections.
AI.result: Successfully recovers interaction terms and forcing expressions, and accurately predicts statistical moments up to order five in stochastic triad models across multiple regimes.
AI.conclusion: The framework demonstrates the potential of integrating interpretable symbolic discovery with data-driven stochastic modeling for complex turbulent systems, with theoretical consistency guarantees.

title: DANCE: Detect and Classify Events in EEG
summary: Event identification in continuous neural recordings is a critical task in neuroscience. Decoding in EEG is dominated by classifying windows aligned to known event onsets. However, while available in controlled experiments, such onsets are absent in continuous real-world monitoring. Here, we introduce DANCE, a deep learning pipeline that frames neural decoding as a set-prediction problem and jointly detects and classifies events directly from raw, unaligned signals. Evaluated separately on ten datasets curated from the literature with a wide variety of event types (ranging from milliseconds to minutes in duration), our model outperforms existing methods on a broad range of cognitive, clinical and BCI tasks. This single architecture establishes a new state of the art in the competitive task of seizure monitoring and matches the accuracy of onset-informed models for BCI tasks. Overall, our method marks a step towards end-to-end asynchronous neural decoding models

AI.tldr: DANCE is a deep learning pipeline that jointly detects and classifies neural events directly from raw, unaligned EEG signals without requiring known event onsets, outperforming existing methods across diverse cognitive, clinical and BCI tasks.
AI.motivation: Current EEG decoding methods require windows aligned to known event onsets, which are available in controlled experiments but absent in real-world continuous monitoring scenarios. This limits practical applications of neural decoding systems.
AI.method: DANCE frames neural decoding as a set-prediction problem using a deep learning pipeline that jointly performs detection and classification of events directly from raw, unaligned neural signals without requiring onset information.
AI.result: The model outperforms existing methods across ten diverse datasets with event durations ranging from milliseconds to minutes. It establishes a new state-of-the-art for seizure monitoring and matches the accuracy of onset-informed models for BCI tasks.
AI.conclusion: DANCE represents a significant step toward end-to-end asynchronous neural decoding models that can work in real-world continuous monitoring scenarios without requiring event onset information, advancing the field toward more practical applications.

title: RelFlexformer: Efficient Attention 3D-Transformers for Integrable Relative Positional Encodings
summary: We present a new class of efficient attention mechanisms applying universal 3D Relative Positional Encoding (RPE) methods given by arbitrary integrable modulation functions $f$. They lead to the new class of 3D-Transformer models, called \textit{RelFlexformers}, flexibly integrating those RPEs, and characterized by the $O(L \log L)$ time complexity of the attention computation for the $L$-length input sequences. RelFlexformers builds on the theory of the Non-Uniform Fourier Transform (NU-FFT), naturally generalizing several existing efficient RPE-attention methods from structured settings with tokens homogeneously embedded in unweighted grids into general non-structured heterogeneous scenarios, where tokens' positions are arbitrarily distributed in the corresponding 3D spaces. As such, RelFlexformers can be applied in particular to model point clouds. Our extensive empirical evaluation on a large portfolio of 3D datasets confirms quality improvements provided by the NU-FFT-driven attention modulation techniques in the RelFlexformers.

AI.tldr: RelFlexformer: A new class of 3D Transformers with efficient O(L log L) attention using universal 3D Relative Positional Encoding via Non-Uniform Fourier Transform, applicable to arbitrary token distributions like point clouds.
AI.motivation: Existing efficient attention methods with Relative Positional Encoding (RPE) are limited to structured settings where tokens are homogeneously embedded in unweighted grids. There's a need to generalize these methods to handle non-structured, heterogeneous scenarios where tokens are arbitrarily distributed in 3D space.
AI.method: Proposes RelFlexformers based on Non-Uniform Fourier Transform (NU-FFT) theory, using universal 3D RPE methods with arbitrary integrable modulation functions f. This allows efficient O(L log L) attention computation for sequences of length L, generalizing existing methods to arbitrary token distributions.
AI.result: Extensive empirical evaluation on multiple 3D datasets shows quality improvements from NU-FFT-driven attention modulation in RelFlexformers. The method can handle arbitrary token distributions in 3D space, particularly applicable to point clouds.
AI.conclusion: RelFlexformers provide a flexible and efficient framework for 3D attention mechanisms that generalize beyond structured settings, enabling applications to non-homogeneous data like point clouds while maintaining computational efficiency.

title: What should post-training optimize? A test-time scaling law perspective
summary: Large language models are increasingly deployed with test-time strategies: sample $N$ responses, score them with a reward model or verifier, and return the best. This deployment rule exposes a mismatch in post-training: standard objectives optimize the mean reward of a single response, whereas best-of-$N$ performance is governed by the upper tail of the reward distribution. Recent test-time-aware objectives partly address this mismatch, but typically assume that training can use the same per-prompt rollout budget as deployment, which is impractical when post-training must cover many prompts while deployment can allocate much larger per-prompt test-time compute. We study this budget-mismatch regime, where only $m\ll N$ per-prompt rollouts are available during training but the target objective is best-of-$N$ deployment. Under structural assumptions on the reward tails, we show that the policy gradient of the best-of-$N$ objective can be approximated from a much smaller rollout group by extrapolating upper-tail statistics. This yields a family of Tail-Extrapolated estimators for best-of-$N$-oriented post-training: a simple direct estimator, Tail-Extrapolated Advantage (TEA), and a fixed-order debiased Prefix-TEA estimator based on moment cancellation. Experiments on instruction-following tasks show that TEA and Prefix-TEA improve best-of-$N$ performance across different language models, reward models and datasets under various training and test-time budget settings.

AI.tldr: A new training method called Tail-Extrapolated Advantage (TEA) that improves best-of-N deployment performance when training has much smaller per-prompt rollout budgets than deployment.
AI.motivation: There's a mismatch between standard training objectives (optimizing mean reward of single responses) and best-of-N deployment (optimizing upper tail of reward distribution). Existing test-time-aware objectives assume same per-prompt rollout budget during training and deployment, which is impractical when training must cover many prompts while deployment can allocate much larger per-prompt compute.
AI.method: Proposes Tail-Extrapolated Advantage (TEA) estimators that approximate the policy gradient of best-of-N objective from smaller rollout groups by extrapolating upper-tail statistics. Includes a simple direct estimator (TEA) and a debiased Prefix-TEA estimator based on moment cancellation.
AI.result: Experiments on instruction-following tasks show that TEA and Prefix-TEA improve best-of-N performance across different language models, reward models, and datasets under various training and test-time budget settings.
AI.conclusion: The proposed tail-extrapolation approach effectively addresses the budget mismatch problem in best-of-N deployment scenarios, enabling better performance even when training has significantly smaller per-prompt rollout budgets than deployment.

title: Heteroscedastic Diffusion for Multi-Agent Trajectory Modeling
comment: Accepted to IEEE Transactions on Pattern Analysis and Machine Intelligence (TPAMI). Extended version of arXiv:2503.18589 (CVPR 2025)
summary: Multi-agent trajectory modeling traditionally focuses on forecasting, often neglecting more general tasks like trajectory completion, which is essential for real-world applications such as correcting tracking data. Existing methods also generally predict agents' states without offering any state-wise measure of heteroscedastic uncertainty. Moreover, popular multi-modal sampling methods lack error probability estimates for each generated scene under the same prior observations, which makes it difficult to rank the predictions at inference time. We introduce U2Diffine, a unified diffusion model built to perform trajectory completion while simultaneously offering state-wise heteroscedastic uncertainty estimates. This is achieved by augmenting the standard denoising loss with the negative log-likelihood of the predicted noise, and then propagating the latent space uncertainty to the real state space using a first-order Taylor approximation. We also propose U2Diff, a faster baseline that avoids gradient computation during sampling. This approach significantly increases inference speed, making it as efficient as a standard generative-only diffusion model. For post-processing, we integrate a Rank Neural Network (RankNN) that enables error probability estimation for each generated mode, demonstrating strong correlation with ground truth errors. Our method outperforms state-of-the-art solutions in both trajectory completion and forecasting across four challenging sports datasets (NBA, Basketball-U, Football-U, Soccer-U), underscoring the effectiveness of our uncertainty and error probability estimation.

AI.tldr: U2Diffine is a unified diffusion model that performs trajectory completion with state-wise heteroscedastic uncertainty estimates, plus a faster U2Diff baseline and RankNN for error probability estimation, outperforming SOTA on sports datasets.
AI.motivation: Traditional multi-agent trajectory modeling focuses on forecasting but neglects trajectory completion needed for real-world applications like correcting tracking data. Existing methods lack state-wise heteroscedastic uncertainty estimates, and multi-modal sampling methods lack error probability estimates for ranking predictions at inference time.
AI.method: U2Diffine uses diffusion model augmented with negative log-likelihood of predicted noise for uncertainty estimation, propagating latent space uncertainty to real state space via first-order Taylor approximation. U2Diff is a faster baseline avoiding gradient computation during sampling. Rank Neural Network (RankNN) enables error probability estimation for each generated mode.
AI.result: Method outperforms state-of-the-art solutions in both trajectory completion and forecasting across four challenging sports datasets (NBA, Basketball-U, Football-U, Soccer-U). Shows effectiveness of uncertainty and error probability estimation.
AI.conclusion: U2Diffine provides a unified framework for trajectory completion with uncertainty quantification, addressing limitations of existing methods and demonstrating superior performance on sports trajectory datasets with practical error probability estimation capabilities.

title: On Improving Graph Neural Networks for QSAR by Pre-training on Extended-Connectivity Fingerprints
summary: Molecular Graph Neural Networks (GNNs) are increasingly common in drug discovery, particularly for Quantitative Structure-Activity Relationship (QSAR) studies; yet, their superiority compared to classical molecular featurisation approaches is disputed. We report a general strategy for improving GNNs for QSAR by pre-training to predict Extended-Connectivity Fingerprints (ECFP). We validate our approach with statistical tests and challenging out-of-distribution (OOD) splits. Across five out of six Biogen benchmarks, we observed a statistically significant improvement in standard performance metrics over all evaluated baselines when using ECFP pre-trained GNNs. However, for more heterogeneous datasets and more complex endpoints, such as binding affinity prediction, pre-trained GNNs underperformed in OOD settings. Importantly, we investigated the impact of substructure-level data leakage during pre-training on downstream performance. While we identified scenarios where pre-training on ECFPs was less effective, our findings show that ECFP-based pre-training can enhance downstream OOD performance on a diverse set of practically relevant QSAR tasks.

AI.tldr: ECFP pre-training improves GNNs for QSAR tasks, but effectiveness varies with dataset complexity and binding affinity prediction scenarios.
AI.motivation: There is ongoing debate about whether Molecular Graph Neural Networks (GNNs) are superior to classical molecular featurization approaches for QSAR studies, necessitating investigation of effective strategies to improve GNN performance.
AI.method: Developed a general strategy for improving GNNs for QSAR by pre-training them to predict Extended-Connectivity Fingerprints (ECFPs). Validated the approach using statistical tests and challenging out-of-distribution (OOD) splits across Biogen benchmarks.
AI.result: ECFP pre-trained GNNs showed statistically significant improvement in standard performance metrics across 5 out of 6 Biogen benchmarks compared to all baselines. However, they underperformed for more heterogeneous datasets and complex endpoints like binding affinity prediction in OOD settings. Identified substructure-level data leakage during pre-training as a factor affecting downstream performance.
AI.conclusion: While ECFP-based pre-training has limitations in certain scenarios (heterogeneous datasets, complex endpoints), it can effectively enhance downstream OOD performance for diverse, practically relevant QSAR tasks.

title: Kernel-Gradient Drifting Models
summary: We propose kernel-gradient drifting, a one-step generative modeling framework that replaces the fixed Euclidean displacement direction in drifting models with directions induced by the kernel itself. Standard drifting is attractive because it enables fast, high-quality generation without distilling a large pretrained diffusion model, but its theory is currently understood mainly for Gaussian kernels, where the drift coincides with smoothed score matching and is identifiable. Our gradient-based reformulation exposes this score-based structure for general kernels: the resulting drift is the score difference between kernel-smoothed data and model distributions, yielding identifiability for characteristic kernels and a smoothed-KL descent interpretation of the drifting dynamics. Since kernel gradients are intrinsic tangent vectors, the same construction extends naturally to Riemannian manifolds and to discrete data via the Fisher-Rao geometry of the probability simplex. Across spherical geospatial data, promoter DNA and molecule generation, kernel-gradient drifting enables state-of-the-art one-step generation beyond the Euclidean setting without distillation.

AI.tldr: Kernel-gradient drifting: A one-step generative modeling framework that replaces fixed Euclidean displacement with kernel-induced directions, enabling fast high-quality generation across Euclidean spaces, Riemannian manifolds, and discrete data without distillation.
AI.motivation: Standard drifting models enable fast, high-quality generation without distilling large pretrained diffusion models, but their theory is currently limited to Gaussian kernels where drift coincides with smoothed score matching and is identifiable. The authors seek to extend this theoretical understanding to general kernels.
AI.method: Proposes kernel-gradient drifting framework that replaces fixed Euclidean displacement directions with kernel-induced directions. For general kernels, the drift becomes the score difference between kernel-smoothed data and model distributions, making it identifiable for characteristic kernels. The method extends naturally to Riemannian manifolds via kernel gradients as intrinsic tangent vectors, and to discrete data via Fisher-Rao geometry of probability simplex.
AI.result: The kernel-gradient drifting enables state-of-the-art one-step generation across diverse domains: spherical geospatial data, promoter DNA generation, and molecule generation. It achieves superior performance beyond Euclidean settings without requiring distillation of large pretrained diffusion models.
AI.conclusion: Kernel-gradient drifting provides a theoretically sound and practical one-step generative modeling framework that generalizes drifting models beyond Gaussian kernels to work across various data geometries (Euclidean, Riemannian, discrete) while maintaining identifiability and enabling high-quality generation without distillation.

title: XQCfD: Accelerating Fast Actor-Critic Algorithms with Prior Data and Prior Policies
summary: For reinforcement learning in the real world online exploration is expensive A common practice in robotic reinforcement learning is to incorporate additional data to improve sample efficiency Expert demonstration data is often crucial for solving hard exploration tasks with sparse rewards While prior data is used to augment experience and pretrain models we show that the design of existing algorithms fails to achieve the sample efficiency that is possible in this setting due to a failure to use pretrained policies effectively We propose XQCfD which extends the sample-efficient XQC actor-critic to learn from demonstrations using augmented replay buffers pretrained policies and stationary policy architectures designed to avoid rapidly unlearning the strong initial policy like prior works We show our stationary network architecture enables policy improvement out-of-distribution better than standard network architectures due to its higher entropy predictions XQCfD achieves state of the art performance across a range of complex manipulation tasks with sparse rewards from the popular Adroit Robomimic and MimicGen benchmarks -- notably with a low update-to-data ratio and no ensemble networks

AI.tldr: XQCfD extends XQC actor-critic to efficiently learn from demonstrations using stationary network architectures and augmented replay buffers, achieving state-of-the-art performance on sparse-reward manipulation tasks.
AI.motivation: Real-world reinforcement learning requires sample efficiency due to expensive online exploration. While expert demonstration data is crucial for solving hard exploration tasks with sparse rewards, existing algorithms fail to achieve optimal sample efficiency because they don't effectively use pretrained policies.
AI.method: XQCfD extends the sample-efficient XQC actor-critic to learn from demonstrations using three key components: 1) augmented replay buffers, 2) pretrained policies, and 3) stationary policy architectures designed to avoid rapidly unlearning the strong initial policy. The stationary network architecture enables better policy improvement out-of-distribution due to higher entropy predictions.
AI.result: XQCfD achieves state-of-the-art performance across a range of complex manipulation tasks with sparse rewards from Adroit, Robomimic, and MimicGen benchmarks. Notably, it achieves this with a low update-to-data ratio and without requiring ensemble networks.
AI.conclusion: The proposed XQCfD approach effectively addresses the sample efficiency challenge in RL by better leveraging demonstration data through stationary architectures that prevent catastrophic forgetting of pretrained policies, making it a practical solution for real-world robotic applications with sparse rewards.

title: AdaPaD: Adaptive Parallel Deflation for PEFT with Self-Correcting Rank Discovery
summary: Fine-tuning large language models with LoRA requires choosing a rank r before training starts. Existing approaches either extract rank-1 components sequentially, freezing each component's error permanently into every subsequent residual, or optimize the full low-rank factorization jointly with guarantees that describe only the joint update, not individual rank-1 directions. We present AdaPaD (Adaptive Parallel Deflation), which trains all rank-1 components simultaneously: each worker refines its component against a deflation target built from the latest estimates of all predecessors, and as those estimates improve, the targets improve too. We call this property self-correction: deflation errors converge to zero over rounds rather than persisting as fixed residuals. On top of this backbone, AdaPaD adds advance learning (private pre-training before activation) and per-module dynamic rank discovery (importance-based growth until a shared budget is exhausted), making the rank distribution an output rather than an input. We prove that every component's error decays exponentially after a warm-up period, with a generalization bound that splits into a vanishing algorithmic term and an irreducible statistical floor. Empirically, AdaPaD is competitive with adaptive-rank LoRA baselines on GLUE with DeBERTaV3-base at matched parameter budgets, and competitive with fixed-rank LoRA on Qwen3-0.6B SQuAD/SQuAD v2 while deploying an adapter that is on average 30.7% smaller.

AI.tldr: AdaPaD is an adaptive parallel deflation method for LoRA fine-tuning that trains all rank-1 components simultaneously with self-correction, enabling dynamic rank discovery and competitive performance with smaller adapters.
AI.motivation: Existing LoRA rank selection approaches have limitations: sequential extraction freezes errors into residuals permanently, while joint optimization only provides guarantees for the full update, not individual rank-1 directions.
AI.method: AdaPaD trains all rank-1 components simultaneously with self-correction - each worker refines its component against deflation targets built from latest estimates of predecessors. Adds advance learning (private pre-training) and per-module dynamic rank discovery based on importance until budget is exhausted.
AI.result: Proved exponential error decay after warm-up with generalization bound splitting into vanishing algorithmic term and irreducible statistical floor. Competitive with adaptive-rank LoRA on GLUE with DeBERTaV3-base, and with fixed-rank LoRA on Qwen3-0.6B SQuAD/SQuAD v2 while deploying 30.7% smaller adapters on average.
AI.conclusion: AdaPaD provides a theoretically sound approach to dynamic rank discovery in LoRA fine-tuning with self-correction properties, achieving competitive performance with smaller adapters than existing methods.

title: Reinforce Adjoint Matching: Scaling RL Post-Training of Diffusion and Flow-Matching Models
summary: Diffusion and flow-matching models scale because pretraining is supervised regression: a clean sample is noised analytically, and a model regresses against a closed-form target. RL post-training aligns the model with a reward. In image generation, this makes samples compose objects correctly, render text legibly, and match human preferences. Existing methods rely on costly SDE rollouts, reward gradients, or surrogate losses, sacrificing pretraining's regression structure. We show that the structure extends to RL post-training. Under KL-regularized reward maximization, the optimal generative process tilts the clean-endpoint distribution towards samples with higher reward and leaves the noising law unchanged. Combining this with the adjoint-matching optimality condition and a REINFORCE identity, we derive Reinforce Adjoint Matching (RAM): a consistency loss that corrects the pretraining target with the reward. At each step, we draw a clean endpoint from the current model, evaluate its reward, noise it as in pretraining, and regress. No SDE rollouts, backward adjoint sweeps, or reward gradients are required. Like the pretraining objective, RAM is simple and scales. On Stable Diffusion 3.5M, RAM achieves the highest reward on composability, text rendering, and human preference, reaching Flow-GRPO's peak reward in up to $50\times$ fewer training steps.

AI.tldr: Proposes Reinforce Adjoint Matching (RAM), a simple scaling RL post-training method for diffusion models that extends pretraining's regression structure without requiring expensive SDE rollouts or reward gradients.
AI.motivation: Existing RL post-training methods for diffusion models sacrifice the efficient regression structure of pretraining by relying on costly SDE rollouts, reward gradients, or surrogate losses. The authors aim to extend the elegant regression structure of pretraining to RL post-training.
AI.method: Derives Reinforce Adjoint Matching (RAM) by combining insights from KL-regularized reward maximization, adjoint-matching optimality conditions, and REINFORCE identity. RAM is a consistency loss that corrects the pretraining target with reward information, operating by drawing clean endpoints from the current model, evaluating their reward, noising them as in pretraining, and performing regression.
AI.result: On Stable Diffusion 3.5M, RAM achieves the highest reward on composability, text rendering, and human preference tasks, reaching Flow-GRPO's peak reward in up to 50× fewer training steps.
AI.conclusion: RAM successfully extends the simple regression structure of diffusion model pretraining to RL post-training, providing an efficient, scalable method that avoids expensive operations while achieving superior alignment with rewards.

title: DynaMiCS: Fine-tuning LLMs with Performance Constraints using Dynamic Mixtures
summary: Multi-domain fine-tuning of large language models requires improving performance on target domains while preserving performance on constrained domains, such as general knowledge, instruction following, or safety evaluations. Existing data mixing strategies rely on fixed heuristics or adaptive rules that cannot explicitly enforce preservation of such capabilities. We propose DynaMiCS, a dynamic mixture optimizer that casts multi-domain fine-tuning as a constrained optimization problem. At each update, DynaMiCS performs short domain-specific probing runs to estimate a slope matrix of local cross-domain effects, capturing how training on each fine-tuning dataset affects each evaluation domain. These estimates are then used to compute mixture weights through optimization over the probability simplex, with the objective of improving target-domain performance while keeping constrained-domain losses below reference levels. Across multi-domain fine-tuning scenarios with varying numbers of target and constrained domains, DynaMiCS achieves stronger target-domain improvements and higher constraint satisfaction than fixed-mixture baselines, at lower computational cost and without reference models, per-example scoring, or manually tuned mixture weights.

AI.tldr: DynaMiCS: A dynamic mixture optimizer that frames multi-domain fine-tuning as constrained optimization, using domain-specific probing to estimate cross-domain effects and compute optimal mixture weights that improve target domains while preserving constrained domain capabilities.
AI.motivation: Current multi-domain fine-tuning strategies use fixed heuristics or adaptive rules that cannot explicitly enforce preservation of constrained domain capabilities (like general knowledge, instruction following, or safety), leading to potential degradation of these important capabilities when fine-tuning on target domains.
AI.method: DynaMiCS formulates multi-domain fine-tuning as a constrained optimization problem. It performs short domain-specific probing runs to estimate a slope matrix of local cross-domain effects (how training on each fine-tuning dataset affects each evaluation domain). These estimates are then used to compute mixture weights through optimization over the probability simplex, with the objective of improving target-domain performance while keeping constrained-domain losses below reference levels.
AI.result: DynaMiCS achieves stronger target-domain improvements and higher constraint satisfaction than fixed-mixture baselines across various multi-domain fine-tuning scenarios. It achieves this at lower computational cost and without requiring reference models, per-example scoring, or manually tuned mixture weights.
AI.conclusion: DynaMiCS provides an effective and efficient approach to multi-domain fine-tuning that explicitly preserves constrained domain capabilities while improving target domain performance, overcoming limitations of existing fixed-heuristic approaches.

title: Locking Pretrained Weights via Deep Low-Rank Residual Distillation
summary: The quality of open-weight language models has dramatically improved in recent years. Sharing weights greatly facilitates model adoption by enabling their use across diverse hardware and software platforms. They also allow for more open research and testing, to the extent that users can use them as checkpoints, fine-tune them according to their needs, and potentially redistribute them. In some cases, however, concerns on modifying these weights towards unauthorized uses may outweigh the pros of giving users such a freedom. Defending against such adaptation is non-trivial: since an adaptive attacker can observe all weights and architectures by definition, they can reverse simple structural defenses, and use optimization to defeat the simplest locking mechanisms. In this work, we exploit the inference-training asymmetry of automatic differentiation as a novel defense axis. We propose DLR-Lock, a method where the purveyor of the model purposely replaces each pretrained MLP in their model with a deep low-rank residual network (DLR-Net) of comparable parameter count, forcing activation memory that grows linearly with depth during backpropagation. DLR-Nets are efficiently trained via module-wise distillation. We show that, beyond this memory overhead, DLR-Lock results in architectural mismatches that complicate the optimization landscape of standard fine-tuning, and a backward pass that incurs disproportionately more overhead than the forward pass. Our defense succeeds in withstanding adaptive attackers with full knowledge of the defense strategy while preserving the original model's capabilities. Experiments on LLM validate these claims.

AI.tldr: DLR-Lock is a novel defense mechanism that protects open-weight language models from unauthorized fine-tuning by exploiting inference-training asymmetry through deep low-rank residual networks that increase memory overhead and complicate optimization during backpropagation.
AI.motivation: While open-weight language models facilitate adoption and research, concerns about unauthorized modifications and malicious adaptations warrant protection mechanisms. Traditional defenses are insufficient against adaptive attackers who can observe all weights and architectures, necessitating a novel approach that exploits inherent asymmetries between inference and training.
AI.method: DLR-Lock replaces each pretrained MLP in a language model with a deep low-rank residual network (DLR-Net) of comparable parameter count. These DLR-Nets force activation memory to grow linearly with depth during backpropagation, are trained via module-wise distillation, and create architectural mismatches that complicate the optimization landscape for fine-tuning.
AI.result: The defense successfully withstands adaptive attackers with full knowledge of the defense strategy while preserving original model capabilities. Experiments on LLMs validate that DLR-Lock incurs disproportionately more overhead in backward passes than forward passes, creating effective barriers against unauthorized fine-tuning.
AI.conclusion: DLR-Lock provides a practical defense mechanism for protecting open-weight language models from unauthorized adaptations by exploiting inference-training asymmetry, offering a solution that balances model openness with protection against malicious modifications.

title: Rebellious Student: Reversing Teacher Signals for Reasoning Exploration with Self-Distilled RLVR
summary: Self-distillation has emerged as a powerful framework for post-training LLMs, where a teacher conditioned on extra information guides a student without it, both from the same model. While this guidance is useful when the student has failed, on successful rollouts, the same mechanism instead overwrites the student's choices and suppresses it's own reasoning. Therefore, we propose reading the original self-distillation signal in reverse: when the student succeeds along a path the teacher would not have predicted, these tokens reflect its self-driven reasoning. Building on this, we propose RLRT (RLVR with Reversed Teacher), which augments GRPO by reinforcing these tokens on correct rollouts. We interpret this as a new form of exploration in RLVR: not uniform diversity, but valuable exploration grounded in the student's own success. Across base, instruction-tuned, and thinking-tuned Qwen3 checkpoints, RLRT substantially outperforms self-distillation and exploration-based baselines, establishing information asymmetry as a new, principled design axis for RLVR.

AI.tldr: RLRT (RLVR with Reversed Teacher) improves self-distillation for LLMs by reinforcing student's self-driven reasoning tokens instead of suppressing them, leading to better performance than conventional methods.
AI.motivation: Self-distillation can suppress the student's own reasoning when it succeeds, as the teacher's guidance overwrites the student's correct choices. The authors want to leverage the student's successful reasoning that diverges from teacher predictions.
AI.method: RLRT reads self-distillation signals in reverse: when the student succeeds on paths the teacher wouldn't predict, those tokens represent self-driven reasoning. It augments GRPO by reinforcing these tokens on correct rollouts, creating valuable exploration grounded in student success.
AI.result: RLRT substantially outperforms self-distillation and exploration-based baselines across base, instruction-tuned, and thinking-tuned Qwen3 checkpoints.
AI.conclusion: Information asymmetry between teacher and student serves as a new, principled design axis for RLVR, enabling better exploration based on student's successful self-driven reasoning.

title: MASS-DPO: Multi-negative Active Sample Selection for Direct Policy Optimization
summary: Multi-negative preference optimization under the Plackett--Luce (PL) model extends Direct Preference Optimization (DPO) by leveraging comparative signals across one preferred and multiple rejected responses. However, optimizing over large negative pools is costly, and many candidates contribute redundant gradients due to their similar effects on policy updates. We introduce MASS-DPO, a multi-negative active sample selection method that derives a PL-specific Fisher-information objective for selecting compact, informative negative subsets within each prompt. The resulting log-determinant objective selects negatives that contribute complementary information for policy updates, yielding compact subsets that retain the full pool's information while reducing redundancy. In practice, this favors negatives whose gradients cover different update directions, reducing redundant signal from near-duplicate candidates while preserving the most useful training information. Across four benchmarks spanning recommendation and multiple-choice QA and three model families, MASS-DPO consistently exceeds or matches existing methods in accuracy, improves Recall/NDCG and margin-based optimization dynamics, and delivers stronger alignment with substantially fewer negatives.

AI.tldr: MASS-DPO is an active sample selection method for multi-negative preference optimization that efficiently selects compact, informative negative subsets using a PL-specific Fisher-information objective, reducing redundancy while maintaining performance.
AI.motivation: Multi-negative preference optimization under the Plackett-Luce model is computationally costly with large negative pools, and many rejected responses contribute redundant gradients due to similar effects on policy updates, wasting computational resources.
AI.method: Introduces MASS-DPO, which derives a Plackett-Luce-specific Fisher-information objective for selecting compact, informative negative subsets. Uses a log-determinant objective to select negatives that contribute complementary information for policy updates, favoring negatives whose gradients cover different update directions and reducing redundancy from near-duplicate candidates.
AI.result: Across four benchmarks (recommendation and multiple-choice QA) and three model families, MASS-DPO consistently exceeds or matches existing methods in accuracy, improves Recall/NDCG and margin-based optimization dynamics, and delivers stronger alignment with substantially fewer negatives.
AI.conclusion: MASS-DPO provides an effective solution for multi-negative preference optimization by selecting information-rich negative subsets that reduce computational costs while maintaining or improving performance metrics compared to using full negative pools.

title: Elucidating Representation Degradation Problem in Diffusion Model Training
summary: Diffusion models have achieved remarkable success, yet their training remains inefficient due to a severe optimization bottleneck, which we term Representation Degradation. As noise levels increase, the outputs of the trained model exhibit progressive structural distortion, which can destabilize training and impair generation quality. Our analysis suggests that this instability is driven by mismatched target recoverability, which is associated with Neural Tangent Kernel (NTK) spectral weakening and effective low-rank behavior. To address this, we propose Elucidated Representation Diffusion (ERD), a plug-and-play framework that dynamically reallocates optimization effort according to effective recoverability. By stabilizing representation learning without external supervision, ERD accelerates convergence and achieves strong empirical performance across diffusion backbones.

AI.tldr: ERD addresses representation degradation in diffusion models by dynamically reallocating optimization effort based on effective recoverability, stabilizing training and improving efficiency.
AI.motivation: Diffusion models suffer from inefficient training due to "Representation Degradation" - as noise levels increase, model outputs show progressive structural distortion that destabilizes training and impairs generation quality.
AI.method: Propose Elucidated Representation Diffusion (ERD), a plug-and-play framework that dynamically reallocates optimization effort according to effective recoverability, stabilizing representation learning without external supervision.
AI.result: ERD accelerates convergence and achieves strong empirical performance across various diffusion backbones by addressing the optimization bottleneck.
AI.conclusion: The proposed ERD framework successfully mitigates representation degradation in diffusion models, leading to more stable training, faster convergence, and improved generation quality without requiring external supervision.

title: ConQuR: Corner Aligned Activation Quantization via Optimized Rotations for LLMs
summary: Large language models (LLMs) are costly to deploy due to their large memory footprint and high inference cost. Weight-activation quantization can reduce these costs, but low-bit activation quantization remains difficult because activation outliers induce large quantization error. Recent rotation-based methods address this by applying orthogonal transformations that redistribute activation magnitude across dimensions, but existing approaches either require expensive end-to-end rotation training or rely on stored activation corpora, introducing significant compute or storage overhead. We propose a lightweight post-training rotation calibration method for LLM activation quantization. Our method learns orthogonal rotations that align normalized activations with the corners of an inscribed hypercube, encouraging activation energy to be distributed more evenly across dimensions. This objective admits an efficient closed-form update via the orthogonal Procrustes problem, avoiding gradient-based optimization over the orthogonal group. We further introduce an online calibration procedure that updates rotations as calibration samples are processed, eliminating the need to store activations on disk and allowing rotations to adapt to quantized activation distributions during calibration. Experiments on Llama-2 and Llama-3 models from 3B to 70B parameters show that our method achieves competitive or improved performance across perplexity benchmarks and common sense reasoning tasks while avoiding both costly end-to-end training and large offline activation storage.

AI.tldr: A lightweight post-training method for LLM activation quantization that uses orthogonal rotations to reduce quantization error without expensive training or large storage requirements.
AI.motivation: LLMs are costly to deploy due to memory footprint and inference cost. Weight-activation quantization can help but low-bit activation quantization is challenging due to activation outliers causing large quantization errors. Existing rotation-based methods require expensive end-to-end training or large activation storage, creating significant overhead.
AI.method: Proposes a lightweight post-training rotation calibration method that learns orthogonal rotations to align normalized activations with corners of an inscribed hypercube. Uses efficient closed-form updates via the orthogonal Procrustes problem (avoiding gradient-based optimization). Features online calibration that updates rotations as samples are processed, eliminating disk storage needs and allowing adaptation to quantized activation distributions.
AI.result: Experiments on Llama-2 and Llama-3 models (3B to 70B parameters) show competitive or improved performance across perplexity benchmarks and common sense reasoning tasks while avoiding both costly end-to-end training and large offline activation storage.
AI.conclusion: The proposed method provides an effective, lightweight solution for LLM activation quantization that reduces deployment costs without the overhead of existing approaches, balancing performance and efficiency for practical LLM deployment.

title: Muown: Row-Norm Control for Muon Optimization
summary: Muon has emerged as a strong competitor to AdamW for language model pre-training, yet its behavior at scale is sensitive to weight decay. Recent work has observed that, for Muon without decoupled weight decay, the spectral norm of weight matrices drifts upward over training. Through a decomposition of the spectral norm into a row-magnitude factor and a row-coherence factor, we identify the former as the empirical driver of this drift under Muon, while the latter remains well-behaved along the trajectory. Motivated by this diagnosis, we introduce Muown, a drop-in replacement for Muon that treats the row-magnitude vector as an explicit optimizer variable, updating it under the $\ell_\infty$ geometry induced by the decomposition, while applying Muon unchanged to the remaining direction component. We prove that Muown attains the optimal non-convex rates in both deterministic and stochastic regimes under a dual norm aligned with the underlying geometries and with a stochastic noise coefficient that empirically remains below that of Muon throughout training. Across GPT-style pre-training on FineWeb-Edu with model sizes from 124M up to 2.7B parameters, Muown improves perplexity over Muon, SOAP, AdamW, and Lion. It also widens the plateau of near-optimal learning rates across model scales, reduces sensitivity to weight decay, and avoids the spectral norm drift at negligible step-time overhead when appropriately sharded.

AI.tldr: Muown is a new optimizer that addresses spectral norm drift in Muon by treating row-magnitude as an explicit variable with ℓ∞ geometry updates, improving performance across GPT pre-training scales while maintaining computational efficiency.
AI.motivation: Muon optimizer shows sensitivity to weight decay at scale, with observed upward drift in spectral norm of weight matrices during training, which motivates finding a solution to stabilize this behavior while maintaining Muon's performance advantages.
AI.method: Decompose spectral norm into row-magnitude and row-coherence factors, identify row-magnitude as the drift cause. Introduce Muown as a drop-in Muon replacement that treats row-magnitude vector as explicit optimizer variable updated under ℓ∞ geometry, while applying Muon unchanged to remaining direction component.
AI.result: Muown achieves optimal non-convex rates in deterministic/stochastic regimes, improves perplexity over Muon, SOAP, AdamW, and Lion across GPT pre-training (124M to 2.7B parameters), widens near-optimal learning rate plateau, reduces weight decay sensitivity, avoids spectral norm drift with negligible overhead.
AI.conclusion: Muown successfully addresses Muon's spectral norm drift problem through a principled decomposition approach, offering better training stability and performance across model scales while maintaining computational efficiency, making it a promising optimizer for large-scale language model pre-training.

title: Mistake-Bounded Language Generation
summary: We investigate the learning task of language generation in the limit, but shift focus from the traditional time-of-last-mistake metric of a generator's success to a new notion of "mistake-bounded generation." While existing results for language generation in the limit focus on guaranteeing eventual consistency, they are blind to the cumulative error incurred during the learning process. We address this by shifting the goal to minimizing the total number of invalid elements output by a generation algorithm. We establish a formal reduction to the Learning from Correct Demonstrations framework of Joshi et al. (2025), enabling a general recipe for deriving mistake bounds via weighted update rules. For finite classes, we provide an algorithm that simultaneously achieves an optimal last-mistake time of $\mathsf{Cdim}(L)$ and a mistake bound of $\lfloor \log_2 |L| \rfloor$, whereas for the non-uniform setting of countably infinite streams of languages, we prove a fundamental trade-off: achieving logarithmic mistakes $O(\log i)$ necessarily precludes convergence guarantees established in prior work. Finally, we show that our framework can be extended to accommodate noisy adversaries and guarantee mistake bounds that scale with the adversary's suboptimality.

AI.tldr: The paper introduces mistake-bounded generation as a new metric for language learning, focusing on minimizing cumulative errors rather than just eventual consistency, and establishes connections to learning frameworks while proving trade-offs between mistake bounds and convergence guarantees.
AI.motivation: Traditional language generation in the limit focuses on eventual consistency (time of last mistake), but ignores the cumulative errors made during the learning process. The authors aim to address this limitation by introducing a new notion that minimizes total invalid elements output during generation.
AI.method: Established a formal reduction to the Learning from Correct Demonstrations framework (Joshi et al., 2025) to enable deriving mistake bounds via weighted update rules. Developed algorithms for finite classes and analyzed non-uniform settings for countably infinite streams of languages.
AI.result: For finite classes: algorithm achieves optimal last-mistake time of Cdim(L) and mistake bound of ⌊log₂|L|⌋. For countably infinite streams: proved fundamental trade-off - achieving logarithmic mistakes O(log i) precludes convergence guarantees from prior work. Framework extends to noisy adversaries with mistake bounds scaling with adversary's suboptimality.
AI.conclusion: Mistake-bounded generation provides a more nuanced framework for evaluating language learning algorithms by considering cumulative errors, revealing important trade-offs between convergence guarantees and mistake bounds, with applications extending to noisy settings.

title: Likelihood scoring for continuations of mathematical text: a self-supervised benchmark with tests for shortcut vulnerabilities
summary: We introduce an automatically generated benchmark for predicting hidden text in technical papers. A paper supplies visible context $X$ and a hidden continuation $Y$; the evaluated model writes an auxiliary forecast string $Z$, and a separate scorer assigns next-token probability to $Y$ both with and without conditioning on $Z$. This gives a label-free test of whether $Z$ transmits information about the continuation, compared against controls where $Z$ is recent context rather than a forecast. Our main testbed is equation-suffix prediction: the predictor sees context and the first part of a displayed equation, then forecasts the rest. The task mixes surface-level arXiv/TeX text modeling with reasoning-sensitive inference; the suffix is one of many roughly equivalent continuations, so the benchmark is read statistically rather than item-by-item. On 1363 equation continuations from 138 recent physics and mathematics papers, forecasts from GPT-5.5, Opus 4.7, and GPT-5.4 nano all improve clipped likelihood over the context control under both Qwen3-8B and Kimi K2.6 scorers, distinguishing model families and reasoning-effort settings without human labels. To emulate shortcuts where $Z$ further primes the scorer rather than making a useful forecast, we also fine-tune the scorer on context-only prompts and apply it to held-out papers as a stronger control. GPT-5.5 forecasts still beat this fine-tuned control; GPT-5.4 nano forecasts do not. Longer prose/TeX continuations show positive but noisier lift over controls, concentrated near the beginning of the target. These results support cross-model likelihood scoring as a static benchmark and as a setup for probing shortcut vulnerabilities before reinforcement learning or model-selection optimization is applied.

AI.tldr: A novel automated benchmark for evaluating how well models can predict hidden text in technical papers, using equation-suffix prediction as a testbed with statistical rather than item-by-item scoring.
AI.motivation: To create a label-free, automated test for whether model-generated forecasts transmit useful information about hidden continuations in technical papers, specifically addressing the challenge of evaluating reasoning-sensitive inference without human labels.
AI.method: Developed a benchmark where: 1) Models see visible context X and part of an equation, then forecast continuation Z; 2) Separate scorers evaluate next-token probability of hidden continuation Y with/without conditioning on Z; 3) Compare against controls using recent context as Z; 4) Test on 1363 equation continuations from 138 physics/math papers; 5) Use statistical analysis rather than item-by-item scoring; 6) Implement fine-tuned scorers as stronger controls against shortcuts.
AI.result: Forecasts from GPT-5.5, Opus 4.7, and GPT-5.4 nano all improved clipped likelihood over context controls using both Qwen3-8B and Kimi K2.6 scorers. GPT-5.5 forecasts beat fine-tuned controls while GPT-5.4 nano did not. Longer prose/TeX continuations showed positive but noisier improvement concentrated near target beginnings.
AI.conclusion: Cross-model likelihood scoring works as a static benchmark and as a setup for detecting shortcut vulnerabilities before applying reinforcement learning or model-selection optimization, supporting label-free evaluation of reasoning-sensitive text prediction.

title: On periodic distributed representations using Fourier embeddings
summary: Periodic signals are critical for representing physical and perceptual phenomena. Scalar, real angular measures, e.g., radians and degrees, result in difficulty processing and distinguishing nearby angles, especially when their absolute difference exceeds pi. We can avoid this problem by using real-valued, periodic embeddings in high-dimensional space. These representations also allow us to control the nature of their dot product similarities, allowing us to construct a variety of different kernel shapes. In this work, we aim of highlight how these representations can be constructed and focus on the formalization of Dirichlet and periodic Gaussian kernels using the neurally-plausible representation scheme of Spatial Semantic Pointers.

AI.tldr: The paper introduces periodic representations in high-dimensional space to handle angular data better than traditional radians/degrees, focusing on Dirichlet and periodic Gaussian kernels using Spatial Semantic Pointers.
AI.motivation: Traditional scalar angular measures (radians, degrees) create processing difficulties for nearby angles, especially when absolute differences exceed pi. This motivates using periodic embeddings in high-dimensional space to overcome these limitations.
AI.method: The paper uses real-valued, periodic embeddings in high-dimensional space and formalizes Dirichlet and periodic Gaussian kernels using the neurally-plausible representation scheme of Spatial Semantic Pointers.
AI.result: The work demonstrates how to construct periodic representations that can control dot product similarities and create various kernel shapes, specifically formalizing Dirichlet and periodic Gaussian kernels.
AI.conclusion: Periodic representations in high-dimensional space provide superior handling of angular data compared to traditional scalar measures, with Spatial Semantic Pointers offering a neurally-plausible framework for implementing these representations and specialized kernels.

title: Benchmarking Sensor-Fault Robustness in Forecasting
summary: Cyber-physical system (CPS) forecasting models depend on sensor streams with noisy, biased, missing, or temporally misaligned readings, yet standard forecasting evaluation often selects models by nominal error without showing whether they remain robust under such faults. We introduce SensorFault-Bench, a shared CPS-grounded sensor-fault stress-test protocol for evaluating forecasting architectures and robustness-improvement methods, and an operational taxonomy organizing the method comparison. Across four real-world datasets and eight scored scenarios governed by a standardized severity model, it reports worst-scenario degradation, clean mean squared error (MSE), and worst-scenario fault-time MSE, separating relative robustness from absolute error. A disjoint fault-transfer split lets explicit fault-training methods train on adjacent fault families while evaluation uses separate benchmark scenarios. Empirically, forecasting architectures favored by clean MSE can degrade sharply under faults, and clean-MSE rankings can disagree with worst-scenario fault-time error rankings. Chronos-2, the evaluated zero-shot foundation-model representative, matches or trails the last-value naive forecaster in clean MSE on the two single-target datasets and has the largest worst-scenario degradation on ETTh1 and Traffic, where all channels are forecast targets. For the evaluated robustness-improvement method set, paired deltas show selective degradation reductions: projected gradient descent adversarial training and randomized training lead where value faults dominate observed degradation, while fault augmentation leads where availability faults dominate. SensorFault-Bench provides open-source code, documented data access, and reproduction and extension guides, so new datasets, architectures, and robustness-improvement methods can be evaluated under the same CPS sensor-fault robustness protocol.

AI.tldr: SensorFault-Bench introduces a standardized CPS sensor-fault stress-test protocol for evaluating forecasting model robustness against noisy, biased, missing, and misaligned sensor data.
AI.motivation: Current CPS forecasting models rely on imperfect sensor streams but are typically evaluated only by nominal error metrics, failing to assess their robustness under real-world sensor faults like noise, bias, missing data, and temporal misalignment.
AI.method: A shared sensor-fault stress-test protocol with standardized severity model, operational taxonomy for method comparison, and disjoint fault-transfer split that allows training on adjacent fault families while evaluating on separate benchmark scenarios.
AI.result: Models with best clean MSE can degrade sharply under faults; clean-MSE rankings often disagree with worst-scenario fault-time rankings. Chronos-2 performs poorly, matching naive forecasters in clean MSE and showing largest degradation. Different robustness methods work for different fault types.
AI.conclusion: SensorFault-Bench provides an open-source benchmarking framework for systematic evaluation of CPS forecasting model robustness under sensor faults, revealing critical discrepancies between clean and fault-time performance while offering standardized protocols for future research.

title: NoRIN: Backbone-Adaptive Reversible Normalization for Time-Series Forecasting
summary: Reversible instance normalization (RevIN) and its successors (Dish-TS, SAN, FAN) have become the de facto plug-in for time-series forecasting, yet the map they apply to each data point is strictly affine, $x \mapsto ax+b$, so they cannot reshape the underlying distribution -- heavy tails remain heavy and skewness remains uncorrected. We propose NoRIN, a non-linear reversible normalization based on the arcsinh-form Johnson $S_U$ transform with two shape parameters $(δ,\varepsilon)$ that control tailedness and skewness; the linear $Z$-score used by RevIN is recovered only in the limit $δ\to \infty$. Training $(δ,\varepsilon)$ jointly with the backbone via gradient descent reliably pushes them toward this linear limit within a few epochs -- a phenomenon we name the degeneration problem: the forecasting loss is locally indifferent to shape, and the high-capacity backbone compensates for any monotone reparameterization of its input. NoRIN escapes the degeneration by decoupling shape selection from gradient training: $(δ,\varepsilon)$ are initialized by a closed-form Slifker-Shapiro quantile fit and refined by Bayesian optimization on the validation objective, while the inner training loop is identical to standard RevIN-style training. Across six representative backbones x five real-world datasets x three prediction horizons (90 configurations), decoupled shape optimization recovers $(δ^\star,\varepsilon^\star)$ that sit systematically far from the linear limit, with values that vary in a backbone-dependent way. This empirically supports the central thesis: different backbones genuinely require different normalization parameters to reach their best performance.

AI.tldr: NoRIN is a non-linear reversible normalization method that escapes the degeneration problem of affine transforms in time-series forecasting by using decoupled shape optimization via Bayesian optimization.
AI.motivation: Current reversible normalization methods (RevIN, Dish-TS, SAN, FAN) use only affine transforms (x → ax+b) that cannot reshape the underlying data distribution - they can't correct heavy tails or skewness. These methods suffer from a "degeneration problem" where shape parameters drift toward the linear limit during training.
AI.method: Proposes NoRIN (Non-linear Reversible Normalization) based on the arcsinh-form Johnson SU transform with two shape parameters (δ,ε) controlling tailedness and skewness. Uses decoupled shape optimization: (δ,ε) are initialized via closed-form Slifker-Shapiro quantile fit and refined by Bayesian optimization on validation objective, while inner training loop remains standard RevIN-style.
AI.result: Across 90 configurations (6 backbones × 5 datasets × 3 horizons), decoupled shape optimization recovers optimal shape parameters (δ⋆,ε⋆) systematically far from the linear limit, with values varying backbone-dependently. Different backbones require different normalization parameters for optimal performance.
AI.conclusion: NoRIN effectively escapes the degeneration problem through decoupled shape optimization, demonstrating that different forecasting backbones genuinely require different normalization shape parameters to reach their best performance, supporting the central thesis of the paper.

title: Conditional anomaly detection methods for patient-management alert systems
comment: Published at Workshop on Machine Learning in Health Care Applications ICML-2008 - MLHealth
summary: Anomaly detection methods can be very useful in identifying unusual or interesting patterns in data. A recently proposed conditional anomaly detection framework extends anomaly detection to the problem of identifying anomalous patterns on a subset of attributes in the data. The anomaly always depends (is conditioned) on the value of remaining attributes. The work presented in this paper focuses on instance-based methods for detecting conditional anomalies. The methods rely on the distance metric to identify examples in the dataset that are most critical for detecting the anomaly. We investigate various metrics and metric learning methods to optimize the performance of the instance-based anomaly detection methods. We show the benefits of the instance-based methods on two real-world detection problems: detection of unusual admission decisions for patients with the community-acquired pneumonia and detection of unusual orders of an HPF4 test that is used to confirm Heparin induced thrombocytopenia - a life-threatening condition caused by the Heparin therapy.

AI.tldr: Instance-based conditional anomaly detection methods using distance metrics and metric learning for identifying anomalous patterns in healthcare applications.
AI.motivation: Existing anomaly detection methods need extension to conditional anomaly detection where anomalies depend on context/remaining attributes, particularly for healthcare applications where understanding unusual patterns in patient data or medical tests is critical.
AI.method: Instance-based methods that rely on distance metrics to identify critical examples in datasets; investigation of various metrics and metric learning methods to optimize anomaly detection performance.
AI.result: Methods applied to two real-world healthcare problems: detecting unusual admission decisions for pneumonia patients and identifying unusual orders of HPF4 tests for Heparin-induced thrombocytopenia, showing practical benefits of the approach.
AI.conclusion: Instance-based conditional anomaly detection with optimized distance metrics is effective for identifying context-dependent anomalies in real-world healthcare applications, providing valuable tools for detecting unusual patterns in medical decision-making.

title: Compute Where it Counts: Self Optimizing Language Models
summary:
Efficient LLM inference research has largely focused on reducing the cost of each decoding step (e.g., using quantization, pruning, or sparse attention), typically applying a uniform computation budget to every generated token. In practice, token difficulty varies widely, so static compression can over-compute on easy steps and under-compute on hard ones. We study dynamic budget allocation for autoregressive decoding: learning how much computation to spend per token from within a single model.
  Self-Optimizing Language Models (SOL) pair a frozen LLM with a lightweight policy network that reads the LLM hidden state and selects a discrete efficiency action at each decode step. Actions can jointly control (i) token-level attention sparsity, (ii) structured activation pruning in the MLP, and (iii) activation quantization bit-width, while leaving the base model weights unchanged. We train the policy with group-relative policy optimization on teacher-forced episodes: the token sequence is fixed, while we sample multiple compute schedules (i.e., "counterfactual" schedules that vary only the efficiency actions for the same token path) and compare their likelihoods under the same supervision. Our reward trades off language-model quality against soft penalties that encourage episode-average budget usage to match a requested target. Across model variants and compute regimes, SOL improves quality at matched budget over static allocation and strong random schedule search, offering a complementary axis for inference-efficiency optimization. SOL discovers a better quality-efficiency pareto-front across all our experiments and improves MMLU accuracy by up to 7.3% over uniform budget allocation strategies.

AI.tldr: Self-Optimizing Language Models (SOL) dynamically allocate computation per token during inference using a lightweight policy network that controls attention sparsity, activation pruning, and quantization, outperforming static uniform budget allocation.
