AI.motivation: Current efficient LLM inference methods apply uniform computation budgets to all tokens, but token difficulty varies widely, causing over-computation on easy tokens and under-computation on hard ones. Static compression can't adapt to this variability.
AI.method: SOL pairs a frozen LLM with a lightweight policy network that reads the LLM hidden state and selects discrete efficiency actions per decode step. Actions control token-level attention sparsity, structured MLP activation pruning, and activation quantization bit-width. The policy is trained with group-relative policy optimization on teacher-forced episodes, sampling multiple compute schedules and comparing their likelihoods under supervision. The reward balances language-model quality against budget usage penalties.
AI.result: SOL improves quality at matched budget over static allocation and random schedule search across model variants and compute regimes. It discovers better quality-efficiency Pareto fronts in all experiments and improves MMLU accuracy by up to 7.3% over uniform budget allocation strategies.
AI.conclusion: Dynamic budget allocation through SOL offers a complementary axis for inference-efficiency optimization, adapting computation to token difficulty within a single model without modifying base weights, significantly outperforming static uniform allocation methods.

title: Neural Weight Norm = Kolmogorov Complexity
summary: Why does weight decay work? We prove that, in any fixed-precision regime, the smallest weight norm of a looped neural network outputting a binary string equals the Kolmogorov complexity of that string, up to a logarithmic factor. This implies that weight decay induces a prior matching Solomonoff's universal prior, the optimal prior over computable functions, up to a polynomial factor. The result is norm-agnostic: in fixed precision, every weight norm collapses to the non-zero parameter count up to constants, so the same sandwich bound holds for any norm used as a regulariser. The proof has two short reductions: any program for a universal Turing machine can be encoded into neural weights at unit cost per program bit, and any fixed-precision network can be described by enumerating its non-zero parameters with logarithmic addressing overhead. Both bounds are tight up to constants, with the logarithmic factor realised by permutation encodings: a network whose parameters encode a permutation produces a string whose Kolmogorov complexity is the non-zero parameter count times its logarithm. The fixed-precision assumption is essential: with infinite precision, neural networks can encode non-computable functions and the weight norm loses its relevance.

AI.tldr: Weight decay works because in fixed-precision regimes, the smallest weight norm needed for a neural network to output a binary string approximates the string's Kolmogorov complexity, making weight decay induce a prior similar to Solomonoff's universal prior.
AI.motivation: To understand why weight decay regularization works in neural networks by connecting it to fundamental principles of computational theory and optimal priors.
AI.method: Mathematical proof using two reductions: (1) encoding any universal Turing machine program into neural weights at unit cost per bit, and (2) describing any fixed-precision network by enumerating non-zero parameters with logarithmic addressing overhead. The analysis focuses on fixed-precision regimes.
AI.result: In fixed-precision regimes, the smallest weight norm equals Kolmogorov complexity up to a logarithmic factor, making weight decay induce a prior matching Solomonoff's universal prior up to a polynomial factor. This is norm-agnostic (works for any norm). The proof bounds are tight, with permutation encodings realizing the logarithmic factor.
AI.conclusion: Weight decay works because it implements an optimal prior over computable functions (Solomonoff's universal prior) in fixed-precision neural networks. The fixed-precision assumption is essential - infinite precision would allow encoding non-computable functions and break the connection.

title: V4FinBench: Benchmarking Tabular Foundation Models, LLMs, and Standard Methods on Corporate Bankruptcy Prediction
summary: Corporate bankruptcy prediction is a high-stakes financial task characterized by severe class imbalance and multi-horizon forecasting demands. Public datasets supporting it remain scarce and small: widely used free benchmarks contain between 6,000 and 80,000 company-year observations, while larger resources are behind subscription paywalls. To address this gap, we introduce V4FinBench, a benchmark of over one million company-year records from the Visegràd Group (V4) economies (2006-2021), with 131 financial and non-financial features, six prediction horizons, and a composite distress criterion jointly capturing solvency, profitability, and liquidity deterioration. V4FinBench is designed to support the evaluation of tabular and foundation-model methods under realistic class imbalance, with positive rates between 0.19% and 0.36%. We provide reference evaluations of standard tabular baselines, finetuned TabPFN, and QLoRA-finetuned Llama-3-8B. With imbalance-aware finetuning, TabPFN matches or exceeds gradient boosting at longer time horizons on both $F_1$-score and ROC-AUC. In contrast, Llama-3-8B trails gradient boosting on ROC-AUC at every horizon and is generally weaker on $F_1$-score, with the gap widening sharply beyond the immediate horizon. In an external evaluation on the American Bankruptcy Dataset, the V4FinBench-finetuned TabPFN checkpoint improves over vanilla TabPFN, suggesting that adaptation captures transferable financial-distress structure rather than only V4-specific patterns. V4FinBench is publicly released to support further evaluation and development of prediction methods on realistic financial data.

AI.tldr: Researchers introduce V4FinBench, a large-scale public benchmark of over 1 million company-year records for corporate bankruptcy prediction, featuring severe class imbalance and multi-horizon forecasting. They evaluate tabular and foundation models, finding TabPFN performs well while Llama-3-8B lags behind.
AI.motivation: Corporate bankruptcy prediction suffers from scarce public datasets - existing free benchmarks are small (6,000-80,000 records) while larger resources are behind paywalls. This limits development of robust predictive models for this high-stakes financial task with severe class imbalance and multi-horizon forecasting needs.
AI.method: Introduce V4FinBench benchmark containing over 1 million company-year records from Visegrád Group economies (2006-2021) with 131 features, six prediction horizons, and a composite distress criterion. Evaluate standard tabular baselines, finetuned TabPFN, and QLoRA-finetuned Llama-3-8B on this dataset. Perform external validation on American Bankruptcy Dataset.
AI.result: TabPFN with imbalance-aware finetuning matches or exceeds gradient boosting at longer horizons on F1-score and ROC-AUC. Llama-3-8B consistently trails gradient boosting on ROC-AUC and performs worse on F1-score, with performance gap widening beyond immediate horizon. V4FinBench-finetuned TabPFN improves over vanilla TabPFN on external American dataset, indicating transferable financial distress patterns.
AI.conclusion: V4FinBench successfully addresses the data scarcity problem in bankruptcy prediction research. The benchmark enables realistic evaluation of models under severe class imbalance. While foundation models show promise, TabPFN demonstrates better performance than both gradient boosting and language models for this specific financial tabular prediction task. The dataset is publicly released to support further research.

title: Beyond Red-Teaming: Formal Guarantees of LLM Guardrail Classifiers
summary: Guardrail Classifiers defend production language models against harmful behavior, but although results seem promising in testing, they provide no formal guarantees. Providing formal guarantees for such models is hard because "harmful behavior" has no natural specification in a discrete input space: and the standard epsilon-ball properties used in other domains do not carry semantic meaning. We close this gap by shifting verification from the discrete input space to the classifier's pre-activation space, where we define a harmful region as a convex shape enclosing the representations of known harmful prompts. Because the sigmoid classification head is monotonic, certifying the worst-case point is sufficient to certify the entire region, yielding a closed-form soundness proof without approximation in O(d) time. To formally evaluate these classifiers, we propose two constructions of such regions: SVD-aligned hyper-rectangles, which yield exact SAT/UNSAT certificates, and Gaussian Mixture Models, which yield probabilistic certificates over semantically coherent clusters. Applying this framework to three author-trained Guardrail Classifiers on the toxicity domain, every hyper-rectangle configuration returns SAT, exposing verifiable safety holes across all classifiers, despite seemingly high empirical metrics. Probabilistic GMM certificates also expose a divergent structural stability in how these models represent harm. While GPT-2 and Llama-3.1-8B maintain robust coverage of 90% and 80% across varying boundaries, BERT's safety guarantees prove uniquely volatile. This 'coverage collapse' to 55% at the optimal threshold reveals a sparsely populated safety margin in BERT, which only achieves full coverage by adopting an extremely conservative pessimistic threshold. These approaches combined, provide new insights on how effective Guardrail Classifiers really are, beyond traditional red-teaming.

AI.tldr: Researchers propose a formal verification method for Guardrail Classifiers by shifting analysis from discrete input space to pre-activation space, using convex regions to define harm and enabling efficient certification, revealing safety holes in existing classifiers despite high empirical metrics.
AI.motivation: Guardrail Classifiers lack formal guarantees for harmful behavior prevention because "harmful behavior" has no natural specification in discrete input space, and traditional verification approaches (like epsilon-ball properties) don't carry semantic meaning in language models.
AI.method: Shift verification from discrete input space to classifier's pre-activation space, defining harmful region as convex shape enclosing representations of known harmful prompts. Leverage monotonic sigmoid classification to certify worst-case point efficiently (O(d) time). Propose two region constructions: SVD-aligned hyper-rectangles for exact certificates, and Gaussian Mixture Models for probabilistic certificates over semantically coherent clusters.
AI.result: Applied framework to three author-trained Guardrail Classifiers in toxicity domain. Every hyper-rectangle configuration returned SAT, exposing verifiable safety holes across all classifiers despite high empirical metrics. Probabilistic GMM certificates revealed divergent structural stability: GPT-2 (90% coverage), Llama-3.1-8B (80% coverage), BERT showed "coverage collapse" to 55% at optimal threshold, requiring extremely conservative threshold for full coverage.
AI.conclusion: The framework provides formal verification capabilities for Guardrail Classifiers, revealing hidden safety vulnerabilities not detectable through traditional red-teaming. Different models exhibit varying structural stability in representing harm, with BERT showing particular vulnerability. Combined approaches offer deeper insights into classifier effectiveness beyond empirical metrics.

title: Revisiting Policy Gradients for Restricted Policy Classes: Escaping Myopic Local Optima with $k$-step Policy Gradients
summary: This work revisits standard policy gradient methods used on restricted policy classes, which are known to get stuck in suboptimal critical points. We identify an important cause for this phenomenon to be that the policy gradient is itself fundamentally myopic, i.e. it only improves the policy based on the one-step $Q$-function. In this work, we propose a generalized $k$-step policy gradient method that couples the randomness within a $k$-step time window and can escape the myopic local optima in MDPs with restricted policy classes. We show this new method is theoretically guaranteed to converge to a solution that is exponentially close in performance to the optimal deterministic policy with respect to $k$. Further, we show projected gradient descent and mirror descent with this $k$-step policy gradient can achieve this exponential guarantee in $O(\frac{1}{T})$ iterations, despite only assuming smoothness and differentiability of the value function. This will provide near optimal solutions to previously elusive applications like state aggregation and partially observable cooperative multi-agent settings. Moreover, our bounds avoid the ubiquitous distribution mismatch factors $||d_μ^{π^*} / d_μ^π||_\infty$ and $||d_μ^{π^*} / μ||_\infty$ enabling the $k$-step policy gradient method to escape suboptimal critical points that emerge from poor exploration in fully observable settings.

AI.tldr: The paper proposes a k-step policy gradient method that escapes myopic local optima in MDPs with restricted policy classes by coupling randomness within k-step windows, achieving exponential convergence to near-optimal deterministic policies without distribution mismatch factors.
AI.motivation: Standard policy gradient methods get stuck in suboptimal critical points when applied to restricted policy classes (like state aggregation or partially observable settings). The authors identify the fundamental cause: conventional policy gradients are myopic, only improving policies based on one-step Q-functions, which prevents escaping local optima.
AI.method: The paper introduces a generalized k-step policy gradient method that couples randomness within a k-step time window. Unlike standard myopic policy gradients, this approach considers longer-term dependencies. The method can be implemented with projected gradient descent or mirror descent, requiring only smoothness and differentiability of the value function.
AI.result: The k-step policy gradient method converges to solutions exponentially close to the optimal deterministic policy with respect to k, achieving O(1/T) iteration complexity. Crucially, the bounds avoid problematic distribution mismatch factors (||d_μ^{π^*}/d_μ^π||∞ and ||d_μ^{π^*}/μ||∞), enabling escape from suboptimal critical points even with poor exploration.
AI.conclusion: The proposed k-step policy gradient method overcomes fundamental limitations of standard policy gradients in restricted policy classes, providing near-optimal solutions for previously challenging applications like state aggregation and partially observable cooperative multi-agent settings, while eliminating dependence on problematic distribution mismatch factors.

title: Dynamic Skill Lifecycle Management for Agentic Reinforcement Learning
comment: Implementation code is available at https://github.com/ejhshen/SLIM
summary: Large language model agents increasingly rely on external skills to solve complex tasks, where skills act as modular units that extend their capabilities beyond what parametric memory alone supports. Existing methods assume external skills either accumulate as persistent guidance or internalized into the policy, eventually leading to zero-skill inference. We argue this assumption is overly restrictive, since with limited parametric capacity and uneven marginal contribution across skills, the optimal active skill set is non-monotonic, task- and stage-dependent. In this work, we propose SLIM, a framework of dynamic Skill LIfecycle Management for agentic reinforcement learning (RL), which treats the active external skill set as a dynamic optimization variable jointly updated with policy learning. Specifically, SLIM estimates each active skill's marginal external contribution through leave-one-skill-out validation, then applies three lifecycle operations: retaining high-value skills, retiring skills whose contribution becomes negligible after sufficient exposure, and expanding the skill bank when persistent failures reveal missing capability coverage. Experiments show that SLIM outperforms the best baselines by an average of 7.1% points across ALFWorld and SearchQA. Results further indicate that policy learning and external skill retention are not mutually exclusive: some skills are absorbed into the policy, while others continue to provide external value, supporting SLIM as a more general paradigm for skill-based agentic RL.

AI.tldr: SLIM is a dynamic Skill Lifecycle Management framework for reinforcement learning agents that optimizes external skill sets through lifecycle operations (retain, retire, expand) based on marginal contributions, outperforming baselines by 7.1%.
AI.motivation: Current methods assume external skills either accumulate permanently or get internalized into policy for zero-skill inference, which is overly restrictive due to limited parametric capacity and uneven skill contributions. The optimal active skill set is non-monotonic and depends on tasks and stages.
AI.method: SLIM treats active external skill sets as dynamic optimization variables updated jointly with policy learning. It estimates each skill's marginal external contribution via leave-one-skill-out validation, then applies three lifecycle operations: retaining high-value skills, retiring skills with negligible contribution after sufficient exposure, and expanding the skill bank when failures reveal missing capabilities.
AI.result: SLIM outperforms best baselines by an average of 7.1% points across ALFWorld and SearchQA benchmarks. Results show policy learning and external skill retention are not mutually exclusive - some skills get absorbed into policy while others continue providing external value.
AI.conclusion: SLIM provides a more general paradigm for skill-based agentic RL by dynamically managing skill lifecycles based on marginal contributions, challenging the assumption that skills should either persist permanently or be fully internalized for zero-skill inference.

title: DECO: Sparse Mixture-of-Experts with Dense-Comparable Performance on End-Side Devices
summary: While Mixture-of-Experts (MoE) scales model capacity without proportionally increasing computation, its massive total parameter footprint creates significant storage and memory-access bottlenecks, which hinder efficient end-side deployment that simultaneously requires high performance, low computational cost, and small storage overhead. To achieve these properties, we present DECO, a sparse MoE architecture designed to match the performance of dense Transformers under identical total parameter budgets and training tokens. DECO utilizes the differentiable and flexible ReLU-based routing enhanced by learnable expert-wise scaling, which adaptively balances the contributions of routed and shared experts. Furthermore, we introduce NormSiLU, an activation function that normalizes inputs prior to SiLU operators, producing a more stable trend of routed-expert activation ratio and a higher intrinsic sparsity level. We also identify an empirical advantage in using non-gated MLP experts with ReLU-based routing, indicating the possibility of MoE architecture simplification. Experiments demonstrate that DECO, activating only 20% of experts, matches dense performance and outperforms established MoE baselines. Our specialized acceleration kernel delivers a 3.00$\times$ speedup on real hardware compared with dense inference. Codes and checkpoints will be released.

AI.tldr: DECO is a sparse Mixture-of-Experts architecture that matches dense Transformer performance with only 20% expert activation, offering 3× inference speedup while reducing storage/memory bottlenecks for edge deployment.
AI.motivation: Standard MoE models have massive parameter footprints creating storage and memory-access bottlenecks, making them inefficient for edge deployment that requires high performance, low computational cost, and small storage overhead simultaneously.
AI.method: DECO uses differentiable ReLU-based routing with learnable expert-wise scaling to balance routed and shared experts, introduces NormSiLU activation (normalizing inputs before SiLU) for stable activation ratios and higher sparsity, and employs non-gated MLP experts with ReLU-based routing for architectural simplification.
AI.result: DECO matches dense Transformer performance under identical parameter budgets and training tokens while activating only 20% of experts, outperforms established MoE baselines, and achieves 3× inference speedup on real hardware through specialized acceleration kernels.
AI.conclusion: DECO provides an effective sparse MoE architecture that addresses storage/memory bottlenecks for edge deployment, demonstrating that simplified MoE designs with specialized routing and activation functions can achieve dense model performance with significantly reduced computation.
